author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Data can be the source of data journalism, or it can be the tool with which the story is told—or it can be both. Like any source, it should be treated with skepticism; and like any tool, we should be conscious of how it can shape and restrict the stories that are created with it.
themes: data skepticism; framing effects
relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Data analysis can reveal 'a story's shape' (Sarah Cohen), or provides us with a 'new camera' (David McCandless). By using data, the job of journalists shifts its main focus from being the first ones to report to being the ones telling us what a certain development might actually mean.
themes: interpretive journalism; data as lens
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Becoming knowledgeable in searching, cleaning, and visualizing data is transformative for the profession of information gathering. Journalists who master this will experience that building articles on facts and insights is a relief. Less guessing, less looking for quotes; instead, a journalist can build a strong position supported by data.

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: When information was scarce, most of our efforts were devoted to hunting and gathering. Now that information is abundant, processing is more important. We process at two levels: 1) analysis to bring sense and structure out of the never-ending flow of data and 2) presentation to get what's important and relevant into the consumer's head. Like science, data journalism discloses its methods and presents its findings in a way that can be verified by replication.
themes: scientific method; transparency; replicability
relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Figures are more likely to be taken at face value than other facts, as they carry an aura of seriousness even when they are entirely fabricated. author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Ideally, you use the data to pinpoint outliers, areas of interest, or things that are surprising. In this sense, data can act as a lead or a tip off. While numbers can be interesting, just writing about the data is not enough. You still need to do the reporting to explain what it means.

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Precision journalism was envisioned to be practiced in mainstream media institutions by professionals trained in journalism and social sciences. author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: CAR is a technique for gathering and analyzing data as a way of enhancing (usually investigative) reportage, whereas data journalism pays attention to the way that data sits within the whole journalistic workflow. In this sense data journalism pays as much—and sometimes more—attention to the data itself, rather than using data simply as a means to find or enhance stories.

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Any discussion about how to build a great news app should start with a product development mentality: keep a laser focus on the user. author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: Developers in the newsroom are like water in the desert: highly sought-after and in short supply. author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)

author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)
extract: We follow a modified Agile methodology to build our apps. We iterate quickly and show drafts to the other folks in the newsroom. author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)

They're useful because you may want to start mashing datasets together and it's amazing how many different spellings and word arrangements can get in the way.
chapter: 8. How We Do It: The Minute Guide To Data Journalism
themes: Excel paste special; identifier codes; data merging; standardization
relevance_rationale: Paste-special and keeping identifier codes are still useful principles but LLMs can now automate much of this standardization work

author: Simon Rogers
extract: VLOOKUP in Excel allows you to bring datasets together. It looks for the value in one sheet and brings in the results from another. Pivot table reports in Excel also allow users to count the number of times something comes up, aggregate data or work out averages. It's really useful and we used it a lot with the WikiLeaks data.
chapter: 8. How We Do It: The Minute Guide To Data Journalism
themes: Excel VLOOKUP; pivot tables; data aggregation
relevance_rationale: VLOOKUP and pivot tables remain useful but LLMs can now perform these joins and aggregations via natural language prompts

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Next up we can actually start to perform the calculations that will tell us if there's a story or not -- and then sanity check them to see if it just sounds wrong. The most common are the simplest -- percentage change or rate per 100,000 people.
chapter: 8. How We Do It: The Minute Guide To Data Journalism
themes: sanity checking; basic calculations; percentage change; rates
relevance_rationale: Sanity-checking calculations and understanding basic rates and percentages are core numeracy skills that LLMs do not replace

author: Simon Rogers
extract: At the end of that process is the output -- will it be a story or a graphic or a visualisation, and what tools will we use? Our top tools are the free ones that we can produce something quickly with. We commonly use Google charts for small line graphs and pies, or Google Fusion Tables to create maps quickly and easily.
chapter: 8. How We Do It: The Minute Guide To Data Journalism
themes: Google Charts; Google Fusion Tables; free tools; quick output
relevance_rationale: Google Fusion Tables is discontinued; the specific free tool recommendations are entirely outdated

author: Simon Rogers
extract: But the best option of all is to find a designer you can work with. Designing a graphic and analysing data are two different jobs.
chapter: 8. How We Do It: The Minute Guide To Data Journalism
themes: design collaboration; separation of skills; team roles
relevance_rationale: Finding a designer remains valuable but AI tools now bridge the gap between analysis and design for many routine visualizations

author: Simon Rogers
extract: We often use free tools because they are quick and tell the story simply. But when we have the time, nothing beats having a good designer create something beautiful.
themes: tool selection; speed vs quality tradeoff; design
relevance_rationale: Speed-vs-quality tradeoff in visualization persists but AI tools now raise the floor for quick output quality significantly

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Data visualisation is no more inherently neutral than any other form of statistical analysis. The great hope of some data visualisers runs into the buffers of reality. A graph is just another way of telling a story. But whose story?
themes: visualization bias; neutrality; editorial choices
relevance_rationale: The insight that data visualization encodes editorial choices and is never neutral is more important than ever with AI-generated graphics
notes: Quoting Creative Review

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Locally, those big campaigns have translated to thousands of ultra-local journalists -- reporters who might write about an area only a few miles wide. These reporters either hunt down the data they need or demand their local governments give it to them through Freedom of Information legislation. This is the Open Data movement in action.
themes: FOI; local journalism; data acquisition; open data
relevance_rationale: FOI and local data acquisition through persistence and legal rights remains an inherently human journalistic activity

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Our guide to Nato operations in Libya is dynamically fed from a spreadsheet, which updates from the Nato daily action briefing. It looks good because it's been well-designed; it works because it's easy to update every day.
chapter: 10 Things You Will Learn In This Book
themes: live updating; spreadsheet-driven; design-data combination
relevance_rationale: Live-updating spreadsheet-driven presentations are now supplemented by more dynamic LLM-powered data pipelines

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Without knowing the state of society, how can things ever get any better? This was using data to help fight for a decent education system. The tools we have to analyse the data may have changed; that motivation has stayed exactly the same.
themes: social purpose; accountability; data journalism motivation
relevance_rationale: Using data to fight for social improvement is a motivational principle that transcends any particular toolset
notes: Reflecting on 1821 Guardian data journalism

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Patrick Cain works by doggedly pursuing datasets, often from official sources which don't want to know, but bolstered by a powerful freedom of information system. If something is uncontroversial there are often no issues about releasing it. Sometimes there is ineffective resistance. Mostly I get things in the mail as the law demands.
themes: FOI; persistence; data acquisition strategy
relevance_rationale: Dogged FOI pursuit of datasets from resistant agencies is a human persistence skill AI cannot replicate
notes: Quoting Patrick Cain, Toronto

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Making that data more accessible and easier to do stuff with has become part of the data journalism process.
chapter: 10 Things You Will Learn In This Book
themes: data accessibility; public service
relevance_rationale: Making data accessible as part of the journalism process is a public service principle that remains valid

author: Simon Rogers
extract: Good design still really matters. Work because they're designed not by machine but by humans who understand the issues involved.
chapter: 10 Things You Will Learn In This Book
themes: design quality; human understanding; editorial design
relevance_rationale: Good design by humans who understand issues still matters but AI-assisted design tools are changing who can produce quality output

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: I've yet to meet a spreadsheet that didn't have a story to tell. The key to all these stories, as with all sources, is knowing what questions to ask.
themes: question-driven analysis; data as source
relevance_rationale: Knowing what questions to ask of data is a fundamental journalistic skill no LLM replaces

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Please don't approach the book as something to be 'memorised' from beginning to end - it is better to remember what's possible in general with spreadsheets, and play with the techniques that are useful to you.
themes: learning strategy; tool fluency
relevance_rationale: Learning strategy still valid but should now include learning to direct LLMs to perform spreadsheet operations

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One of the most common stories is about change. Change is, almost by definition, newsworthy, and an integral part of storytelling. To calculate how much something has changed: what something is now MINUS what something was before.
chapter: Stories about change, stories in context: basic calculations
themes: change calculation; newsworthiness; subtraction
relevance_rationale: Understanding change as newsworthy and the math behind it remains core journalistic numeracy

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Calculating a proportion is a simple division: dividing a part by the whole. To calculate what proportion a change represents, divide the amount of change by the original figure it has changed from.
chapter: Stories about change, stories in context: basic calculations
themes: proportion; percentage change; division
relevance_rationale: Proportional reasoning is fundamental numeracy journalists must understand regardless of tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Try to express proportions as a ratio such as 'almost one in ten' or 'over a quarter' - often this is easier for the reader to take in and understand than a dry, precise percentage - unless the precision is important.
chapter: Ratios: calculating a proportion as '1 in 10'
themes: ratios; audience comprehension; presentation
relevance_rationale: Expressing proportions as ratios for audience comprehension is a writing skill LLMs do not replace

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When it comes to increases you need to be very careful about your language. You might talk about something being 66% higher, but you can also talk about figures being 166% what they were. Beware also of confusing percent with percentage points.
chapter: Combining both: calculating what proportion a change is
themes: precision of language; percentage points; numeracy
relevance_rationale: Precision about percent vs percentage points is critical numeracy unaffected by AI tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: We need to be careful that we talk about arrests rather than drunkenness, because the two are not the same. Arrests may be down because fewer people are getting drunk, but they could also be down because police resources are deployed elsewhere, or because there are fewer officers due to cuts, or changes in policy, law or strategy.
chapter: The last chapter's story: are there more drunk and disorderly arrests?
themes: proxy measures; causation; data limitations; precision of language
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing proxy measures from underlying phenomena is core analytical thinking

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Be prepared to move your data 'angle' down the story as it becomes less about the data and more about the conversations you have as a result of the data. In a story about a new initiative to tackle drunkenness, the data becomes background, not the lead.
chapter: The last chapter's story: are there more drunk and disorderly arrests?
themes: story structure; data as background; reporting workflow
relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment about when data leads vs supports a story is uniquely human

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you only had arrest numbers for the last two months, it might be influenced by seasonal factors. We want to compare like with like, and November and December don't allow us to do that. Comparing one December with another, however, does.
chapter: The last chapter's story: are there more drunk and disorderly arrests?
themes: seasonality; like-for-like comparison; temporal context
relevance_rationale: Understanding seasonality and like-for-like comparisons is fundamental analytical reasoning

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can start to search for useful functions whenever you come across a task or problem which you think others may have already solved, adding the word 'function'. For example, 'Excel function extract day from date' or 'Google spreadsheets function count blank cells'.
chapter: How much did it cost? How many people were affected? The first function: adding a series of cells with SUM
themes: self-directed learning; function discovery; search strategy
relevance_rationale: LLMs now serve as the primary way to discover spreadsheet functions rather than searching with keywords

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Typing functions is often quicker than using buttons. The placement and availability of buttons varies widely between spreadsheet software and versions, so knowing functions means you can use the same techniques whatever version you end up on.
themes: functions vs GUI; transferable skills; spreadsheet software
relevance_rationale: Functions vs GUI still valid but LLMs now let users describe intent in natural language instead of either

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you are going to specify a column or row range such as A:A, check: the heading is not numerical (years or dates will be included in the sum); there is no grand total at the bottom (giving you a total twice as large); there are no further tables beneath.
chapter: How much did it cost? How many people were affected? The first function: adding a series of cells with SUM
themes: data validation; SUM pitfalls; column ranges
relevance_rationale: Checking for header inclusion and double-counting in SUM ranges is a verification skill LLMs cannot reliably do

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Using Freedom of Information to find out the costs of things is a well-worn approach. Financial totals can make attention-grabbing headlines.
chapter: How much did it cost? How many people were affected? The first function: adding a series of cells with SUM
themes: FOI; financial data; story angles
relevance_rationale: FOI as a data acquisition strategy is entirely independent of LLM capabilities

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Use the Sort buttons to bring the biggest and smallest values to the top. Use the Sort option in the Data menu to sort on more than one column at the same time. The MAX function gives the biggest number and MIN the smallest in a specified range.
chapter: Who's top, who's bottom? MAX, MIN, and sorting
themes: sorting; MAX; MIN; ranking
relevance_rationale: Sorting and MAX/MIN concepts endure but LLMs can now perform these operations via natural language

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can calculate what proportion the biggest fundraiser was responsible for by dividing the result of MAX by the result of SUM. So we might report that just one fundraiser was responsible for raising 43% of money, or 'over two fifths', or that four in every ten pounds were donated to one particular fundraiser.
chapter: Who's top, who's bottom? MAX, MIN, and sorting
themes: proportion of total; MAX/SUM; contextualizing extremes
relevance_rationale: Contextualizing extremes as proportion of total is core analytical reasoning

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It's worth knowing how to explore your spreadsheet quickly whenever you first open it, in order to check for common problems and make sure your data is fit for your purposes. Pitfalls to watch out for: empty rows in the middle, empty columns, grand totals at the end, rows before your header row, data which looks like one thing but Excel thinks is another, merged cells.
chapter: Detour: getting to know the spreadsheet - useful shortcuts and tips to avoid mistakes
themes: data quality; initial inspection; common pitfalls
relevance_rationale: Initial data inspection for structural problems remains essential regardless of tools used

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Use the CTRL+cursor key keyboard shortcuts to explore the edges of your data at the start of any project. Hold down CTRL and the down arrow key to take you to the cell before the first empty cell. This helps you check for empty rows, grand totals, and multiple tables.
chapter: Detour: getting to know the spreadsheet - useful shortcuts and tips to avoid mistakes
themes: keyboard shortcuts; data exploration; Excel
relevance_rationale: Keyboard shortcuts still useful but LLMs can now perform data boundary checks programmatically

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Before you make any changes to a spreadsheet, make a copy of it. You can then always go back to it if you make a mistake, or check back against this copy. Select File > Save As to save the working version under a different name. You may want to make more than one copy at different stages.
chapter: Detour: getting to know the spreadsheet - useful shortcuts and tips to avoid mistakes
themes: version control; data preservation; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Preserving original data before modifications is a fundamental data management principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Some official data will come with several rows of descriptions before the actual headings. To fix this, delete rows before the headings so the first row contains your headings. Right-click on the row number and select Delete.
chapter: Detour: getting to know the spreadsheet - useful shortcuts and tips to avoid mistakes
themes: header rows; data cleaning; official data
relevance_rationale: Header row cleanup is now easily handled by LLM-assisted data cleaning scripts

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Merged cells are a particularly awkward problem. The difficult part is making sure that unmerging does not put values in the wrong place or result in values missing. In cases like this it is best to explore specific data cleaning processes and tools like Open Refine.
chapter: Detour: getting to know the spreadsheet - useful shortcuts and tips to avoid mistakes
themes: merged cells; OpenRefine; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Merged cell problems persist but LLMs can now generate cleanup scripts more easily than learning OpenRefine

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: There's a quick way to see what type of data Excel thinks it is dealing with: if it thinks you have text, it will align it to the left. If it thinks it is a number it will put it to the right. If your spreadsheet gets the data type wrong, numbers treated as text cannot be calculated with, and text treated as numbers might strip out leading zeros.
chapter: Hitting the deadline: understanding and formatting the data - number or text
themes: data types; text vs number; alignment; type detection
relevance_rationale: Recognizing data type mismatches via alignment cues requires human visual inspection of data

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you have numbers that Excel thinks is text, try to remove the things causing this. Currency signs can be problematic - use Find and Replace to remove them. Extra spaces can also be problematic. Copy the problematic character and paste it into the Find box so you know you're replacing exactly what you've found.
chapter: Hitting the deadline: understanding and formatting the data - number or text
themes: find and replace; type conversion; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Find-and-replace for type conversion still valid but LLMs can now script bulk type fixes more efficiently

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If an individual hospital, school or police force is performing consistently well below average, we may want to investigate further. Causes may or may not be under the institution's control: local deprivation, understaffing or even poor data collection may all be factors.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: averages as leads; investigation triggers; confounding factors
relevance_rationale: Using averages to identify below-average performers as investigation leads is core analytical reasoning

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One of the roles that journalism plays is to ensure we have an accurate self-image - as a nation, as a region, a community, or an industry. We assume most people are 'like us', except when it flatters our egos. A 'journalism of averages' is an essential corrective to those assumptions.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: journalism of averages; public interest; self-image correction
relevance_rationale: The civic role of a journalism of averages is an enduring epistemological principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: There isn't just one function to calculate an average: there are three. AVERAGE calculates a mean; MEDIAN calculates the middle value; MODE calculates the most common value. People often assume average means half the values are higher and half lower, but that's not always the case with a mean.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: mean; median; mode; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Understanding mean vs median vs mode is fundamental statistical literacy no tool replaces

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: MODE is the best sort of average to use when looking at ratings such as 1 to 5 scales, because ratings are actually categories not numbers: a rating of 2 is not twice as good as a rating of 1. If dealing with ratings it is much better to show the proportion of people who gave each rating.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: MODE; ordinal data; ratings; categorical data
relevance_rationale: Understanding ordinal data requires MODE not MEAN is statistical reasoning LLMs cannot substitute

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When dealing with data about money we have to remember that while there may be unusually large values, it is unlikely that there are correspondingly small values to counterbalance them. A mean average can be skewed by very large values. For that reason it is often better to use a median.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: skewed distributions; median vs mean; financial data
relevance_rationale: Understanding skewed distributions and when to use median is core statistical literacy

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You're probably safest working out both the mean and median for your numbers and seeing how different they are. If the difference is significant, that may be the start of a story itself. An organisation where the median wage is significantly lower than the mean may be one where the bosses take home large wages.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: mean-median gap; inequality detection; story discovery
relevance_rationale: Comparing mean and median to detect inequality is a durable analytical technique

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can use this knowledge to avoid being misled. An organisation may use an artificially inflated average to gloss over low pay: knowing the difference means you can ask 'Is that a mean or a median?' Further enquiries may lead to a story fact-checking and debunking the boast.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: critical numeracy; misleading statistics; accountability
relevance_rationale: Critical numeracy to challenge misleading average claims is a core journalistic skill

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Counting is different to adding up. If we were looking at a football club's wage bill, we might add up all the wages but count how many employees there were. A count allows us to put totals into perspective.
chapter: How many payments? How many people? Counting, not adding up: COUNT
themes: counting vs summing; contextualization; COUNT
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing counting from summing is fundamental analytical reasoning

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When writing about what you've counted, make sure you are accurate in how you describe it. FiveThirtyEight's story about kidnappings in Nigeria was based on counting media reports of kidnappings, but mistakenly described them as simply kidnappings. The two are not the same.
chapter: How many payments? How many people? Counting, not adding up: COUNT
themes: precision of language; proxy measures; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Accuracy in describing what was counted vs what it represents is a permanent editorial standard

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sometimes the story is what's missing. COUNTBLANK can reveal where information is absent. If 10% of expense claim cells are blank, one in ten claims the politician didn't declare what they were claiming for. In any dataset where you expect full disclosure, COUNTBLANK gives a quick overview.
chapter: The story is what's missing: COUNTBLANK
themes: missing data; COUNTBLANK; transparency; story discovery
relevance_rationale: Using missing data as a story lead is an enduring investigative technique

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: COUNTIF allows us to count how many cells in a range meet a specific criteria. You can add 'greater than' or 'less than' symbols before the value. Once we know how many cells are above average, we can divide by the COUNT to find what percentage that represents.
chapter: Only count if... setting criteria for a formula: COUNTIF
themes: COUNTIF; conditional counting; criteria-based analysis
relevance_rationale: COUNTIF mechanics still valid but LLMs can generate these formulas from natural language descriptions

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Using text strings with COUNTIF only works properly if your data is very consistently entered - and text entries often aren't. The BBC was described as 'BBC', 'B.B.C.', and 'British Broadcasting Corporation'. A simple COUNTIF would only pick up one of those three. The only situations where text is entered consistently are where the person is selecting from a drop-down list.
chapter: Only count if... setting criteria for a formula: COUNTIF
themes: data consistency; text matching; COUNTIF limitations
relevance_rationale: Understanding data consistency limitations in text matching is essential regardless of tool

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can broaden COUNTIF criteria using wildcards. The asterisk means 'none or any characters'. Place asterisks both before and after specific characters to find them anywhere in a cell. You can also use spaces intelligently to exclude wider matches than you want.
chapter: Only count if... setting criteria for a formula: COUNTIF
themes: wildcards; pattern matching; COUNTIF
relevance_rationale: Wildcard syntax still valid but LLMs now handle pattern matching via natural language more accessibly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Using wildcards can be a process of trial and error. One useful technique is to create a series of COUNTIF formulae which each count a single cell, giving 1 or 0 for each row. By sorting to bring positive results to the top, you can quickly identify matches and see if any don't match what you're looking for.
chapter: Trial and error: the single-cell test
themes: validation technique; single-cell test; COUNTIF verification
relevance_rationale: Validating formula results with single-cell tests is a verification principle that transcends tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: As part of one investigation into Olympic torchbearers I used COUNTIF to find corporate sponsors mentioned in 6,000 nomination stories. The formula COUNTIF(A1,'*adidas*') told me if each cell contained 'adidas'. Copying down the column and sorting brought 21 matches to the top - a quick way to find leads in a large dataset.
chapter: Only count if... setting criteria for a formula: COUNTIF
themes: COUNTIF; investigation technique; pattern detection; case study
relevance_rationale: COUNTIF for pattern detection in large datasets now more easily done with LLM-assisted code or fuzzy matching

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Combining data is often a great way of telling new stories about spreadsheets. You may have one table showing pass rates for each school and another showing addresses. Combining these would allow you to identify geographical patterns. You could combine addresses with poverty rates or unemployment to see if there's a possible relationship - remembering that correlation does not equal causation.
chapter: Putting the story into context: merging data using VLOOKUP
themes: data merging; correlation vs causation; contextualization; VLOOKUP
relevance_rationale: Merging datasets to find geographic or contextual patterns is an enduring analytical strategy

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: To merge information from two tables with VLOOKUP: each table must have a column in common with the same data; that column must be to the left of anything you want to grab; always specify FALSE for the match type to require exact matches.
chapter: Putting the story into context: merging data using VLOOKUP
themes: VLOOKUP; data merging; join requirements
relevance_rationale: VLOOKUP mechanics are increasingly handled by LLM-generated code but join logic concepts still matter

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you get N/A errors in VLOOKUP, the name may appear but be typed slightly differently - 'Devon and Cornwall' vs 'Devon & Cornwall'. Names are often inconsistently used and entered. But codes tend to be much more consistent. If you have the option, use codes to match separate sets of data.
chapter: Putting the story into context: merging data using VLOOKUP
themes: data matching; identifier codes; name inconsistency; VLOOKUP errors
relevance_rationale: Using codes instead of names for reliable data matching is a permanent data management principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Even a few N/A results is good news: it means you've got a much smaller manual workload than if you matched them yourself. Sort your data to bring N/A results to the top and check each one manually using Find (CTRL+F).
chapter: Putting the story into context: merging data using VLOOKUP
themes: error handling; manual verification; VLOOKUP workflow
relevance_rationale: Handling N/A results via manual inspection is a verification step that persists regardless of tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The VLOOKUP formula is always working, so if values change the results will change. If you want to keep results as they were, copy them and then paste using Edit > Paste special > Values only. This converts the live formula into static results.
chapter: Putting the story into context: merging data using VLOOKUP
themes: paste values; static results; VLOOKUP; data preservation
relevance_rationale: Paste-values to freeze formulas is tool-specific; LLM-assisted workflows often export results directly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: TRIM removes extra spaces before and after data. CLEAN removes non-printing characters like carriage returns. SUBSTITUTE replaces particular characters or strings - useful when one dataset uses '&' and another uses 'and'. You can combine them: =CLEAN(TRIM(A2)).
chapter: My data is dirty! Basic cleaning using TRIM, CLEAN and SUBSTITUTE
themes: TRIM; CLEAN; SUBSTITUTE; text cleaning
relevance_rationale: TRIM/CLEAN/SUBSTITUTE still valid but LLMs can now generate cleaning scripts from descriptions of dirty data

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One investigation involved hundreds of company names. Rather than type each name manually into a company search website, we used SUBSTITUTE to generate a URL for each company by replacing spaces with %20 or +. Combined with TRIM: =SUBSTITUTE(TRIM(A2)," ","+"). A repetitive process like this is a prime candidate for some computer assistance.
chapter: My data is dirty! Basic cleaning using TRIM, CLEAN and SUBSTITUTE
themes: URL generation; SUBSTITUTE; automation; investigation technique
relevance_rationale: URL generation via SUBSTITUTE is now more easily accomplished with LLM-generated scripts

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Two years' data isn't a lot to draw conclusions - a 16% drop could be significant, or typical: we don't know. More historical data would help provide more context. You might also look for national rates to see if this data bucks the trend, or data on neighbouring areas, or add population data to draw conclusions on arrests per person.
chapter: The last chapter's story: drunk and disorderly arrest totals
themes: contextual data; trend analysis; per capita rates; benchmarking
relevance_rationale: Need for historical context and benchmarking to validate trends is fundamental analytical reasoning

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can make the total more meaningful and concrete by converting into a daily or weekly rate, e.g. '46 people a week arrested for being drunk and disorderly' rather than '2400 arrested last year'.
chapter: The last chapter's story: drunk and disorderly arrest totals
themes: rate conversion; concreteness; audience comprehension
relevance_rationale: Converting annual totals to daily rates for concreteness is an enduring presentation technique

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When nesting functions, it can be helpful to first write each part in its own cell, then write a formula using those cell references. To create a single nested formula, replace the cell references with the formulae within each. Sometimes it makes more sense not to nest: if you want to see each stage separately for checking or to look for unusual results.
chapter: Using more than one function at a time: nested functions
themes: nested functions; formula transparency; debugging; auditability
relevance_rationale: Breaking complex formulas into visible intermediate steps for auditability is a permanent best practice

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A headline that screams 'Half of hospitals below average' is statistically illiterate, or at least pretending to be. By their very nature, some things will always be above average or lower than average - that's not news. But being consistently so may be an interesting lead to follow - if you pick up the phone and ask questions.
chapter: Best sellers and averages: MEDIAN, AVERAGE and MODE
themes: statistical literacy; misleading headlines; averages
relevance_rationale: Statistical illiteracy in headlines about averages is a permanent problem requiring human judgment

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Dates are enormously useful bits of data. They can help us to find stories about peaks and troughs: whether things are getting better or worse; what times of the year or week are or were best or worst for a particular event.
chapter: Extracting days, months and years from full dates
themes: date analysis; temporal patterns; story discovery
relevance_rationale: Understanding dates as analytically rich dimensions for story discovery is enduring

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: To find a story about trends in dates, you first need to extract the month, day or year from each date. The simple functions DAY, MONTH and YEAR will extract just the day, month or year from any cell. The TEXT function gives you much greater control over what date information you extract and how it is shown, including the ability to show days or months as words.
chapter: Extracting days, months and years from full dates
themes: date extraction; TEXT function; temporal decomposition
relevance_rationale: Date extraction functions still valid but LLMs can now parse and extract date components via natural language

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Make sure you know whether the dates use UK or US date formatting - and that the formulae are interpreting them correctly. UK dates use day/month/year; US dating uses month/day/year.
chapter: Extracting days, months and years from full dates
themes: date formatting; regional conventions; data validation
relevance_rationale: Knowing to check UK vs US date formatting is a data quality issue that persists across all tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is best to extract parts of dates into a separate column so you can still see the original dates in full. Using the TEXT function to fill extra columns with the specific data you want to work with, while leaving the full dates alone to be checked alongside that.
chapter: Using the Format Cells Custom option
themes: data preservation; column design; audit trail
relevance_rationale: Preserving original dates while extracting components is a permanent data integrity principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you have written a formula to extract part of a date and get a whole date as a result (and an odd one at that), it is probably because the cell you are writing in is formatted as date itself. To solve this problem just fix your formatting: select the cells and open the Format cells menu.
chapter: Dealing with errors in date functions
themes: error diagnosis; cell formatting; troubleshooting
relevance_rationale: Cell formatting troubleshooting for dates is tool-specific and LLMs can now diagnose these issues

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If a date is stored as a series of characters instead of as a number, date functions will return a VALUE error. In these situations you can try to convert the series of characters into a date using the DATEVALUE function; or use other functions to extract the relevant part of the date.
chapter: Dealing with errors in date functions
themes: DATEVALUE; data type conversion; error handling
relevance_rationale: DATEVALUE conversion is tool-specific; LLMs can now handle date parsing more flexibly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you are drawing any conclusions about the worst month, year or day, you need to take account of the possibility that the year or month with the most failing inspections might simply be the year or month with the most inspections full stop. You need to make sure you look at proportions rather than absolute numbers.
chapter: Finding the story: worst years and months for hygiene inspections
themes: proportional reasoning; base rate fallacy; verification
relevance_rationale: Checking proportions not just absolute numbers for temporal patterns is fundamental analytical reasoning

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: At this point you should be pausing and thinking: I am not seeing the full picture here. Just as the changing inspection numbers might raise questions, these proportions should make you pick up the phone to ask them. Your story might not be about those early years, but they are a clue to a wider problem.
chapter: Finding the story: worst years and months for hygiene inspections
themes: critical thinking; data skepticism; follow-up reporting
relevance_rationale: Data skepticism and recognizing when you are not seeing the full picture is core journalistic judgment

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If your spreadsheet problem is not solvable with a single function, the best approach is often to break it down into smaller problems which, individually, can. The key question to ask is: does this formula get me any closer to the result I need?
chapter: How old is someone? Ages and using TODAY
themes: problem decomposition; iterative approach; computational thinking
relevance_rationale: Problem decomposition into solvable sub-problems is a permanent computational thinking skill

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Once you have identified a possible error like this, do not write it off immediately. You may be able to add an error checking formula to correct it. A simple subtraction formula will get us either the correct result, or one which is only one year out. All we need is a way of identifying which result it is, and correcting the initial age accordingly.
chapter: How old is someone? Ages and using TODAY
themes: error correction; Boolean arithmetic; systematic checking
relevance_rationale: Building error-checking formulas into analysis is a verification principle that transcends tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: There is no point combining multiple calculations into one powerful formula if the formula is not going to make sense to you later, or if your data is going to be used by colleagues or people who come after you. It is often better to have columns with each stage of the calculation clearly labelled.

themes: reproducibility; documentation; spreadsheet design; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Prioritizing formula clarity and reproducibility for colleagues is a permanent organizational principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Ages can be particularly interesting angles to explore in any dataset: the oldest, youngest, or general demographic makeup of people in a dataset can all be newsworthy.
chapter: How old is someone? Ages and using TODAY
themes: demographic analysis; story angles; newsworthiness
relevance_rationale: Demographic analysis as a story angle is an enduring journalistic technique

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The leap year point is really important: remember that Excel stores dates as the number of days since 1900 - so years with 366 days will have those extra days added into the total. This is another example of identifying possible problems in calculations and building in corrections.
themes: edge cases; date arithmetic; error prevention
relevance_rationale: Leap year edge cases in date arithmetic require awareness regardless of tool used

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The DATE function is a useful way of making sure that your date is understood correctly by Excel. It takes three ingredients: a year, a month, and a day of the month - in that order. If you have a date which has been formatted unusually, this ensures that they are interpreted correctly.
themes: DATE function; data type enforcement; Excel
relevance_rationale: DATE function specifics are tool-bound; LLMs can now handle date construction across platforms

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The functions RIGHT, LEFT and MID are tailor-made for extracting parts of data such as postcodes, zip codes or telephone numbers. These will respectively grab a specified number of characters from the end, beginning, or middle of a cell. They work particularly well where you always want the same number of characters.
chapter: Grabbing or checking parts of information: RIGHT, LEFT and MID
themes: string extraction; data parsing; text functions
relevance_rationale: RIGHT/LEFT/MID string extraction is now more easily done via LLM-generated regex or code

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If your data already has a particular character that marks one part from another - such as a space between first name and surname, or slashes between day, month and year - then you can use the menu option Text to columns to split it, rather than needing to use a function. Note that if you use this, the original column will no longer exist, so you might want to duplicate it first.
chapter: Splitting names, dates, addresses: text to columns
themes: text to columns; data splitting; data preservation
relevance_rationale: Text-to-columns is tool-specific; LLMs can now split fields via natural language instructions

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The LEN function will tell you the number of characters in a given cell, which can then be used to calculate the start or end position for a MID, LEFT or RIGHT function. You can use LEN within a LEFT, RIGHT or MID function as a nested function, e.g. =LEFT(A2,LEN(A2)-3) to grab a variable number of characters.
themes: LEN function; nested functions; variable-length extraction
relevance_rationale: LEN for dynamic extraction is a spreadsheet-specific technique now more easily handled by LLM code generation

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The SEARCH function tells us at what position a specified character or characters occur in a cell. It is not case sensitive, so if you want to be a little fussier you can use the FIND function which has exactly the same parameters but is case sensitive.
themes: SEARCH function; FIND function; pattern matching
relevance_rationale: SEARCH/FIND functions are spreadsheet-specific; LLMs can now generate equivalent regex or code

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You could check that the date of birth is being treated as a number by Excel in a number of ways. Firstly, by looking at the alignment: aligned right is how Excel aligns numbers. Secondly, you could reformat it as number or general. Thirdly, you could use the function ISNUMBER to check if a specified cell contains a number.
chapter: Finding the story: how old are Guantanamo prisoners
themes: data type validation; ISNUMBER; alignment checks
relevance_rationale: Multiple methods of verifying data types is a robust verification practice that persists

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: There are also rows which generate VALUE errors when you attempt to calculate an age. This is because the date of birth field does not contain a date, but a string of text such as '26 yrs old in 07', 'UNDISCLOSED', 'UNKNOWN' or 'c. 1974'. Some further research might yield more information, but more likely we will have to be clear that a certain proportion of our figures are unknown.
chapter: Finding the story: how old are Guantanamo prisoners
themes: data quality; missing data; transparency; limitations
relevance_rationale: Recognizing messy real-world data entries and being transparent about unknowns is permanent

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The first thing to do in any situation like this is break down the task into its constituent problems/challenges. We need to: identify where the number of years is stated, extract that number, identify where the months are stated, extract those months, identify other context like 'suspended' or 'minimum', convert years and months to a number, convert to a common measure.
chapter: Case study: extracting numbers from phrases
themes: problem decomposition; task planning; data extraction
relevance_rationale: Task decomposition for complex data extraction is a permanent computational thinking skill

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Note that this process involves a lot of trial and error: our initial formula works for most cells, but we then focus on error handling with the cells where it does not. You can adapt and repeat the above processes for months, weeks and days to get the numbers you need.
chapter: Case study: extracting numbers from phrases
themes: trial and error; iterative refinement; error handling
relevance_rationale: Trial-and-error approach to data cleaning with iterative error handling is an enduring methodology

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The simple way to convert all measures to a common measure is to convert to the lowest common denominator. Multiply the years figures by 365 to get days, multiply the months by 31, multiply the weeks by 7. Once done, you can add up all figures for each sentence to get a total, then calculate the difference between the two sentences.
chapter: Case study: extracting numbers from phrases
themes: unit conversion; common denominator; comparison
relevance_rationale: Unit conversion to common denominators is now easily handled by LLM-generated code

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is worth adding a column which just measures the length of the description cell, so you can sort it and pick out outliers. The longest cell on that basis may contain more than one mention of the word being searched for or extra caveats which are important to factor in.
themes: outlier detection; LEN function; data quality
relevance_rationale: Using cell length to detect outliers for manual review is a durable data quality technique

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can use COUNTIF as a checking formula. COUNTIF(I2,"*year*") will count 1 if 'year' is in that cell, or 0 if not. Any 0 value should also match any VALUE error in your earlier SEARCH formula. You can use filters to check that they all do, and investigate any that do not.
themes: COUNTIF; cross-validation; checking formulae
relevance_rationale: Cross-validation using checking formulae is a permanent verification practice

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Despite all this work to extract the numbers from the data, in the end it was decided to leave this dimension out of the eventual story, as it became clear that the article was going to focus on the proportion of requests which were not eligible for review. Often in journalism you have to be prepared to leave out material because the focus of your story has changed, regardless of the work that went into it.
chapter: Sometimes hard work ends up left out of the story
themes: editorial judgement; story focus; sunk cost
relevance_rationale: Willingness to discard analysis when the story focus shifts is core editorial judgment

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Consistent formatting can be important both in the presentation of information and in trying to combine it. A map with 'PARIS' for one marker and 'new york' for another is going to look pretty unprofessional. Because computers sometimes treat a lower case letter and an upper case letter as separate things, you might come across errors when trying to merge datasets.
chapter: Formatting text consistently with UPPER, LOWER, PROPER
themes: case normalisation; data merging; presentation quality
relevance_rationale: Case normalization with UPPER/LOWER/PROPER now easily handled by LLMs but concept of consistent formatting endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: As with TRIM and SUBSTITUTE it is a good idea to create a new column for your cleaned data, write a formula using PROPER, UPPER or LOWER to grab and format the contents of the first cell, and then copy it down the entire column.
chapter: Formatting text consistently with UPPER, LOWER, PROPER
themes: cleaning workflow; column design; non-destructive editing
relevance_rationale: Non-destructive cleaning workflow is valid but LLMs now streamline the formula generation

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: FIXED and ROUND do not just create the effect of rounding numbers up or down: the resulting number is indeed fixed. If you add 1 to that number and format it to show four decimal places it is 4.1500, not 4.1457. This is distinct from using the Format cells menu which only changes how numbers are displayed.
chapter: Rounding and formatting numbers: FIXED and ROUND
themes: FIXED; ROUND; data precision; formatting vs transformation
relevance_rationale: FIXED/ROUND distinction is tool-specific; LLMs can explain and apply rounding correctly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Transposing is the process of switching rows into columns and vice versa. If we wanted to sort the data to see the longest wait time for a particular hospital, we could not - because sorting works by columns. Transposing the data solves this problem: when rows become columns we can pick the column for one hospital, and sort it.
themes: TRANSPOSE; data restructuring; sorting
relevance_rationale: TRANSPOSE as a concept endures but the operation is now trivially performed via LLM-generated code

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: As always, you should save a copy of your data before altering it. You may want to copy data from one sheet, and paste-and-transpose into a new sheet, so as to keep the two versions separate.
chapter: The Paste Special option: the one-off transpose
themes: data preservation; backup; non-destructive editing
relevance_rationale: Saving copies before altering data is a permanent data preservation principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Although array formulae are very impressive and powerful, most of the time you may decide for the purposes of clarity and simplicity to avoid them. It may be clearer to split the calculation into two stages: a column showing the change for each month, then a cell calculating the average for that column.
themes: array formulae; clarity vs power; spreadsheet design
relevance_rationale: Preferring clarity over cleverness in spreadsheet design is an enduring organizational principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The most likely reason for using an array formula is the smaller and faster file sizes that are generated as a result: a spreadsheet using a single array formula is smaller and quicker to use than one performing thousands of calculations separately. However, complex array formulae can actually slow down the performance of the spreadsheet.
themes: array formulae; performance; file size; Excel
relevance_rationale: Array formula performance tradeoffs are tool-specific; LLMs shift analysis to code-based approaches

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: In terms of considerations you need to be clear when this data was collected: waiting time data can be over a year old by the time it is released. You also need to be clear that these are average waiting times - many people will be waiting far longer. There are many ways that waiting time statistics can be massaged.
chapter: The last chapter's story: worst time at hospital
themes: data timeliness; averages; statistical literacy; caveats
relevance_rationale: Caveats about data timeliness and how statistics can be massaged are permanent verification concerns

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data in Google Sheets is not as secure as data that only sits on your own computer. So if you are dealing with sensitive data and are concerned about others either hacking into your account or accessing it through legal avenues, then you may want to avoid such web-based services.
themes: data security; Google Sheets; sensitive data; tool selection
relevance_rationale: Data security concerns about cloud-based tools apply equally to LLM services

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You only need to use IMPORT functions if you expect the data to change, because it will always import the latest data. If you do not expect it to change then it will be quicker and easier to simply make a copy of the data. IMPORT functions will stop working if the source data is taken offline, so it is always a good idea to make a static copy of the data.
themes: IMPORT functions; live data; data archiving; link rot
relevance_rationale: Decision about when to use live vs static data imports is a permanent data management principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: IMPORTHTML can grab information stored in a HTML table or a HTML list. Information might look like it is in a table or list, but if it is not contained in particular tags in the raw HTML, then this function will not work. There is no need to check the raw HTML before you use this function - it is only worth checking as part of troubleshooting if your formula does not work first time.
chapter: Grabbing data from webpage tables and lists: IMPORTHTML
themes: IMPORTHTML; web scraping; Google Sheets; HTML structure
relevance_rationale: IMPORTHTML is tool-specific; LLMs can now generate web scraping code more flexibly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Using trial and error - starting with the first table, then the second, and so on - is invariably much quicker than trying to identify the position of a table from the raw HTML. For that reason it is often better to use cell references for each parameter rather than have all the information in the formula, so you can easily change the number.
chapter: Grabbing data from webpage tables and lists: IMPORTHTML
themes: trial and error; parameterised queries; scraping workflow
relevance_rationale: Trial-and-error approach to finding the right table index is a general scraping workflow that persists

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: IMPORTXML is the most powerful of the IMPORT functions. Its primary purpose is to enable you to pull in XML documents that are hosted online, but it can also be adapted to grab data from HTML webpages too. IMPORTXML allows you to craft any query you like using XPath. At its core all you need to know is: take the name of the tag containing your data, and put two slashes in front of it.
themes: IMPORTXML; XPath; web scraping; XML
relevance_rationale: IMPORTXML and XPath are increasingly replaced by LLM-generated scraping code using CSS selectors or APIs

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Trial and error is an important part of the process. It is not about getting the formula right first time, but trying different queries and seeing how useful the results are, triangulating between queries that are too narrow and queries that are too broad until you get the Goldilocks query that is just right.
themes: trial and error; iterative refinement; query design
relevance_rationale: Iterative refinement of queries between too-narrow and too-broad is a permanent search methodology

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: GOOGLETRANSLATE is particularly useful if you are dealing with data in other languages, or where the language is mixed, and want to see it in your own. If you are searching data for terms in your own language, these functions help you spot references using other languages. They also help you translate searches into a variety of other languages.
themes: GOOGLETRANSLATE; multilingual data; Google Sheets
relevance_rationale: GOOGLETRANSLATE in Sheets is useful but LLMs now handle multilingual data analysis natively and more flexibly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: DETECTLANGUAGE is particularly useful as a way to error-check Google Translate. Not always because it actually does get the language right: but more because it tells you what language Google thinks the cell is in, and therefore what language it is translating from if you have not specified that.

relevance_rationale: DETECTLANGUAGE for error-checking translation is now handled more naturally by multilingual LLMs

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Always generate an example URL for the search you want before adapting it. A quick bit of googling on the query parameter throws up a Search Protocol Reference document which confirms the URL structure. This process of testing, re-testing, googling, refining, tweaking and adapting are all integral elements of spreadsheet work and data journalism more generally.
chapter: Advanced: combining translations into one search
themes: URL construction; iterative testing; research methodology
relevance_rationale: Testing URL construction and iterative refinement is a permanent research methodology

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can use GOOGLEFINANCE to convert from one currency to another. Converting currency really means grabbing the exchange rate between any two currencies which you can then apply to an amount. Financial formulae often use the TODAY function to grab data in relation to today's date, such as the last 30 days numbers.
themes: GOOGLEFINANCE; currency conversion; financial data
relevance_rationale: GOOGLEFINANCE for currency conversion is tool-specific; LLMs can now assist with financial data retrieval

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You only have to use GOOGLEFINANCE once - but the data will change every day. You only have to create and publish a chart once - but it will also change every day. You can use functions that fetch live data to feed live charts to get the best out of both. Those functions include GOOGLEFINANCE, IMPORTHTML, IMPORTXML, IMPORTFEED, and IMPORTDATA.

themes: live charts; automation; data visualisation; Google Sheets
relevance_rationale: Live data to live charts pipeline concept endures but modern tools and LLMs offer more flexible approaches

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: What sparklines allow you to is at a glance see how a series of values are behaving. Sparklines are tiny line or bar charts drawn within a cell in your spreadsheet. Unlike data visualisation more generally, their purpose is entirely personal: a way for you to, at a glance, interpret the data in each row or column.
chapter: Comparing change visually by generating sparkline charts
themes: exploratory analysis; visual inspection
relevance_rationale: Sparklines for at-a-glance pattern detection in exploratory analysis remain useful regardless of AI tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Specifying the minimum and/or maximum values for the sparkline is a good way to correct this if you want all your sparklines to be using the same base or maximum. Two sparkline charts may have different bases and peaks, unless the maximum and minimum are specified in the formula.
chapter: Keeping it relative: specifying minimum and maximum scale
themes: comparability; normalisation; visual inspection
relevance_rationale: Standardizing scales across sparklines for valid comparison is a permanent visualization principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: QUERY allows you to form questions of your data. There are two major advantages: firstly, it allows you to quickly perform a number of calculations and filters all at once. And secondly, it allows you to dynamically generate versions of your data which answer particular questions.
themes: data querying; filtering; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: QUERY function concept endures but LLMs now enable SQL-like querying via natural language across tools

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If your data is published, QUERY allows you to create as many different URLs as the number of questions you can come up with. Each URL will show a different view on your data which answers that question. The user writes the query, not you.
chapter: Generating hackable URLs for users to see the data their own way
themes: transparency; data publishing; user engagement
relevance_rationale: Hackable URLs for data transparency concept endures but modern data publishing tools offer better alternatives

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you wanted to show the density of torchbearers in different areas you could scatter the markers slightly so people can get a visual impression of the distribution. Clearly this brings up issues around misleading users, so you must state clearly that positions are indicative and not exact.
chapter: Adding some randomness: spreading out locations using RAND
themes: mapping; visualisation ethics; transparency
relevance_rationale: Ethical concerns about misleading map visualizations and need for transparency labels are permanent

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Ranking is particularly useful if you want to compare ranks. You can use RANK to find out what rank each percentage would have placed in for each year. Once you have both ranks, you can calculate if they have moved up or down, and which have moved the most.
chapter: Is this value ranked high or low: RANK, LARGE and SMALL
themes: ranking; comparability; change over time
relevance_rationale: RANK function mechanics are tool-specific but the concept of comparing ranks over time endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Both Kent and Birmingham are large authorities, with large populations. It highlights a problem with any dataset that only gives you absolute numbers rather than proportions or per capita figures: the biggest places will invariably come top; the smallest will come near the bottom. What you need to do is make sure you are comparing per capita figures.
chapter: Is this value ranked high or low: RANK, LARGE and SMALL
themes: normalisation; comparability; data quality
relevance_rationale: Understanding that absolute numbers mislead without per-capita normalization is permanent statistical literacy

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is quite useful in reporting data stories to be able to say what percentage of values a number is above or below. PERCENTRANK allows you to see what percentage of numbers in a range are below a specified value. PERCENTILE gives you the value at a specified percentile, useful for reporting key benchmarks.
chapter: What percentile is this at or above: PERCENTRANK and PERCENTILE
themes: statistical context; benchmarking; audience communication
relevance_rationale: PERCENTRANK/PERCENTILE for contextualizing values within distributions is permanent statistical communication

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A good habit is to always test your formula on a result you should be able to predict. In this case, let us test PERCENTRANK on the top performing runners. The results are already sorted by finishing order so we already know who finished first.

themes: verification; testing; error detection
relevance_rationale: Testing formulas on predictable results is a permanent verification practice

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Using quartiles allows you to get an accurate overview of the distribution of values. The more categories you create, the more complicated things become and the less clear the story is. Keep things simple and clear wherever you can unless there is a good reason to do otherwise.
themes: classification; distribution analysis; simplicity
relevance_rationale: Keeping classification simple for clarity is a permanent analytical and presentation principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: PFEye decided to colour code each contract in categories of contract value. These are meaningful categories but arbitrary: the contracts are not evenly distributed between these five categories. Two of the five categories have been allocated to the top fifth, while the bottom two fifths are squeezed into just one category. Perhaps the large amounts skew our perception of the data.
themes: classification; data distribution; bias awareness
relevance_rationale: Recognizing arbitrary classification boundaries that distort perception is permanent critical thinking

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Patrick used the LARGE function to find the second biggest number, combined it with MATCH to identify which column that was in, and then used that with INDEX to grab the authority at the same position. To do this manually would take forever: we would have to look across 200-ish columns for each of the 200-ish rows.
themes: cross-referencing; scalability; automation
relevance_rationale: INDEX/MATCH cross-referencing is tool-specific; LLMs can now generate equivalent code or formulas

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Correlation does not mean causation. In many ways for journalists a weak correlation is more useful than a strong one: it can help rule out a potential avenue of enquiry, while a strong correlation proves nothing. Correlation also helps with factchecking: if a politician is bandying around the phrase strong correlation then you can confidently say there is no strong correlation.
themes: statistical literacy; causation; factchecking
relevance_rationale: Understanding correlation vs causation and using weak correlations to rule out hypotheses is permanent

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: This is sometimes called the multiple comparisons problem: the more variables you can look at, the more likely it is that you find a correlation between any two of them. This problem is particularly compounded by the rise of big data: large datasets with lots of different variables.
themes: statistical literacy; big data; spurious correlation
relevance_rationale: Multiple comparisons problem becomes more acute with big data and AI-driven analysis

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You should have a broader understanding of computational thinking: the ability to break down a problem into logical steps, which can then be addressed through programming. It is not about memorising formulae and being able to reproduce them on demand, but rather to know what is possible and be able to access examples.
themes: computational thinking; problem decomposition; skill development
relevance_rationale: Computational thinking as problem decomposition rather than formula memorization is a permanent meta-skill

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You do not have to write code from scratch: most of the time you will be able to find example macros that you can copy or adapt. A good path: follow a tutorial where you copy and paste, try to understand what one part is doing, then change that part so it does something slightly different. It is always useful to start with a problem which applies to you.
themes: skill development; learning strategy; computational thinking
relevance_rationale: Copy-adapt-modify learning path still valid but LLMs now accelerate this cycle dramatically

author: David Herzog
extract: We consider data to be any computerized file that uses columns and rows (a tabular structure) to organize data that are represented as text, numbers, dates and the like. These files can be manipulated using programs like spreadsheets and database managers, and can be transmitted over computer networks.
themes: data definition; tabular data
relevance_rationale: Tabular-only data definition is too narrow; LLMs work with unstructured text, images, and multimodal data that expand what counts as data for journalism.

author: David Herzog
extract: We usually prefer to get raw data. Raw data afford us more flexibility when it comes to analysis. We can make our own decisions about how to summarize. Raw data make it easier for us to examine our data and test the integrity—or quality—of our data.
themes: raw data; data quality; analytical flexibility
relevance_rationale: Preference for raw data over pre-summarized data remains essential; LLMs can hallucinate summaries, making raw-data access even more important for verification.

author: David Herzog
extract: Working effectively with data requires more than computing. It requires thought and reflection on our own parts. As we climb the pyramid, we rely less on computers and more on our own experiences and thoughts.
themes: DIKW hierarchy; critical thinking; data literacy
relevance_rationale: The DIKW insight that higher-order thinking requires human judgment, not just computation, is reinforced by the LLM era where automated outputs still need human sense-making.
notes: References Ackoff DIKW hierarchy

author: David Herzog
extract: One of the big challenges in becoming data literate is being able to quickly identify data and determine which government agency keeps them. If the data are posted on the Internet, we can find and download them. If data are not posted online, we request them from the agency. To meet that challenge we need to develop a data state of mind, one that opens our eyes to the proliferation of data online, in government agency mainframes and servers, and in university libraries.
themes: data state of mind; source identification; government data
relevance_rationale: Developing a data state of mind to identify which agencies hold relevant datasets is a foundational investigative skill unaffected by LLMs.

author: David Herzog
extract: Agencies collect, analyze and publish data for one or more of three primary reasons: (1) they are required to do so by law, (2) agencies believe the data will help them execute their missions or (3) agencies are participating in an open-government effort.
chapter: How and Why Agencies Collect, Analyze and Publish Data
themes: government data; data provenance; open government
relevance_rationale: Understanding why agencies collect data (legal mandate, mission, open-government) is enduring institutional knowledge that LLMs cannot replace.

author: David Herzog
extract: Our goals for working with the data can differ greatly from the goals of the people at the agencies that collect them. For instance, we may be interested in determining which industry group's employees contributed the most money to candidates, but our state probably records the occupation and employer—not industry category—of each contributor. That means we won't be able to answer that question without a lot of additional research and changes to our data.
chapter: How and Why Agencies Collect, Analyze and Publish Data
themes: question scoping; data limitations; fitness for purpose
relevance_rationale: Recognizing the gap between your analytical goals and agency data collection categories is a critical framing skill that endures regardless of tooling.

author: David Herzog
extract: If you're interested in a database, get a copy of the form that's used to feed data into it. Forms tell a lot about what you can expect to find in databases. You should assume that all of the data collected on the form will be entered. You may be incorrect, but you should start with the assumption that more data are available.
themes: data discovery; government forms; source identification
relevance_rationale: Examining data-entry forms to understand what fields a database contains remains a solid investigative technique regardless of AI tools.

author: David Herzog
extract: Web form URLs with a .php extension are a clue to the presence of a database. PHP is a programming language that lets Web forms pass data to databases. Also be on the lookout for .cfm (Adobe ColdFusion) and .asp (Microsoft Active Server Pages), which can also pass data from a Web form to a database.
themes: data discovery; web forms; technical clues
relevance_rationale: URL extension heuristics (.php, .cfm, .asp) are outdated; modern web apps use diverse frameworks and APIs, and LLMs can help identify database-backed sites more efficiently.

author: David Herzog
extract: Use Google Advanced Search to find data files. The trick is to think of words that appear on webpages that link to downloadable data. Try 'downloadable data site:.gov' to look for pages with those terms on government websites. You could narrow further by adding 'filetype:xls' for Excel files.
themes: search techniques; Google Advanced Search; data discovery
relevance_rationale: Google Advanced Search filetype tricks still work but LLMs and AI-powered search tools can now discover datasets more effectively across open-data portals.

author: David Herzog
extract: Before we download any data, we need to practice safe computing and document our work. Create a word processing document or file with the name Data_Notebook; this is going to be where we take notes about the data we get and what we do with them. Enter the date, then the dataset name and URL. Don't forget to include details about the data, such as the number of rows, time span, update frequency, and source.
themes: documentation; data notebook; provenance tracking
relevance_rationale: Documenting data provenance in a data notebook before analysis is a timeless good practice that becomes even more important when AI assists the workflow.

author: David Herzog
extract: When downloading data from a government website, make sure you also download a copy of any documentation. The data documentation, often stored as a Word, PDF or other document file, usually is essential in helping understand what's in the data file. There's no standard name for the documentation, so it might be called record layout, file layout, data dictionary or something entirely different.
themes: data documentation; data dictionary; record layout
relevance_rationale: Downloading and reviewing data documentation (record layouts, data dictionaries) is fundamental due diligence that no AI tool replaces.

author: David Herzog
extract: The documentation usually provides some key pieces of information about the data set: table names along with record counts for each table; column or field names in each table along with a field description, type of data (character, number, date, etc.) and width; and codes and their meanings.
themes: data documentation; metadata; data dictionary
relevance_rationale: Understanding metadata structure (field names, types, widths, codes) is essential for working with any dataset and endures across all tooling eras.

author: David Herzog
extract: Before using data from a nongovernmental source, ask: How did the NGO obtain the data? What did the NGO do to process or change the data? What interest does the NGO have in making the data available? Can you get the data directly from the official government source instead?
themes: source verification; NGO data; data provenance
relevance_rationale: Questioning NGO data provenance and potential bias is a core verification principle that applies equally when AI intermediaries add another layer of transformation.

author: David Herzog
extract: Government data (at least in the United States) are considered to be free of licensing rules that restrict distribution and use. For journalists, official documents and data provide an additional benefit: protection against libel suits. The fair report privilege provides a shield to journalists who base their news accounts on fair and accurate use of official sources.
themes: government data; fair report privilege; legal protections
relevance_rationale: Government data licensing freedom and fair report privilege are legal principles that endure regardless of technology.

author: David Herzog
extract: Records retention schedules, audit reports, federal agency major information system listings and nongovernmental organization sites are tools particularly well suited for helping identify offline data. Records retention schedules provide details about records held by state and local government agencies.
themes: offline data; records retention; audit reports; data discovery
relevance_rationale: Using retention schedules, audit reports, and agency listings to discover offline databases is institutional knowledge that LLMs cannot supplant.

author: David Herzog
extract: GAO audit reports can easily run dozens of pages, but you can get to the meat quickly by reading the Summary of Findings or What GAO Found section, as well as the Scope and Methodology. If a GAO auditor reviewed databases for the report, he or she will list those in the Scope and Methodology.
themes: audit reports; GAO; data discovery; methodology
