relevance_rationale: Strategic reading of GAO audit reports to identify databases is a durable investigative research skill independent of AI tools.

author: David Herzog
extract: Once you've identified an offline database, the agency employees who create or maintain the data can be helpful because they are the ones who are most familiar with the system. As a negotiating tactic, you can always ask to speak to the data specialist directly, in the interests of making things easier for everyone.
themes: source cultivation; data specialists; negotiation
relevance_rationale: Cultivating relationships with agency data specialists for source negotiation is a human-relational skill that LLMs cannot replace.

author: David Herzog
extract: A good open-records request letter is clear, concise and includes enough detail about what you are seeking: the name of the database as known inside the agency; the names of the data columns if known; the time frame of the data; the format in which you'd like to receive the data; request for the documentation; request for written explanation in case of denial; fee waiver request or limitation.
themes: FOI requests; open records; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Crafting clear, specific FOI request letters with proper elements is a durable legal-procedural skill that LLMs may assist with but not replace.

author: David Herzog
extract: Ron Nixon estimated that he files two or three FOIA requests a month, trying to obtain data for stories on his to-do list. Getting data pursuant to a FOIA request can take a while, so Nixon says he tries to give himself plenty of time. When he requests data, Nixon says he tries to be as specific as possible; he avoids making requests for everything in the database because FOIA officers in agencies usually kick back those requests.
themes: FOI requests; specificity; newsroom workflow
relevance_rationale: Filing frequent, specific FOIA requests as part of a sustained investigative workflow is a practice-based discipline that endures in the LLM era.
notes: Based on Ron Nixon interview

author: David Herzog
extract: Nixon recommends that requesters do a lot of leg work before filing requests. Sometimes he bases his requests on the information collected on federal forms. Sometimes he looks at online federal agency FOIA logs to see what data others have already requested. If someone has already requested and received data, the agency should be able to easily provide a copy.
themes: FOI requests; preparation; FOIA logs
relevance_rationale: Pre-filing research using federal forms and FOIA logs to sharpen requests is smart preparation that remains essential regardless of AI tools.
notes: Based on Ron Nixon interview

author: David Herzog
extract: When government agencies tell you data are private, ask them to cite the law in writing so you have a copy of the agency's legal reasoning on the record. If some of the data truly are private, you can probably get the public portion, because under FOIA and state open-records laws, agencies are supposed to disclose what's public.
themes: FOI requests; negotiation; privacy exemptions; redaction
relevance_rationale: Demanding written legal citations for data denial and requesting partial disclosure are FOI negotiation tactics that endure across all technology eras.

author: David Herzog
extract: When we get a data file—by downloading it or through a public records request—it's tempting to want to start analyzing it right away. But at this point, we need to pause and take time to understand our data better. What are the data's strengths? What are their weaknesses? Is anything missing? How important are those missing data? Can we use the data to accurately answer any questions that we might have?
themes: data quality; verification before analysis; critical assessment
relevance_rationale: The discipline of pausing to understand data strengths, weaknesses, and fitness before analysis is a timeless verification principle reinforced by AI speed temptations.

author: David Herzog
extract: As analysts, we have a duty to know our data inside and out. We need to have an intimate understanding of their shortcomings. We owe the consumers of our data analysis—whether that's other researchers, business clients or the public—the most accurate reading of our data that we can possibly deliver.
themes: data ethics; analytical responsibility; transparency
relevance_rationale: The ethical duty to know data intimately and deliver accurate analysis to the public is a foundational professional standard that only grows more important with AI.

author: David Herzog
extract: Blindly trusting that government agencies and others will collect and report data accurately is a neglect of duty on our part. Veteran data users will tell you that government data can run the gamut from slightly messy to so flawed that the data are unusable. Government agencies often have poor data quality control, if they have any at all.
themes: data skepticism; government data quality; verification
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward government data quality is a permanent verification stance; LLMs may even amplify dirty-data risks by processing flawed inputs uncritically.

author: David Herzog
extract: All data are dirty. Dirty data thrive where there are poor controls during the data entry process—the old GIGO, garbage in, garbage out, effect. Data-entry clerks are low paid and perform this work for hours-long stretches. It's easy to get distracted and make mistakes, and easy to enter data inconsistently.
themes: dirty data; data entry errors; data quality
relevance_rationale: The GIGO principle and understanding that all data have entry errors is foundational data literacy that endures across every tool generation.

author: David Herzog
extract: Changing rules for collecting data can hurt your data quality, or at least limit your analysis. Federal government agencies often tweak or even radically change their data collection methods and rules. Any comparisons across rule changes would prove inaccurate.
themes: data comparability; methodology changes; temporal analysis
relevance_rationale: Awareness that changing data collection rules break temporal comparisons is essential analytical knowledge that no AI tool can compensate for automatically.
notes: Examples: BLS mass layoffs scope change 2004, bankruptcy law 2005, MEPS survey hold 2007

author: David Herzog
extract: FAPRI analysts look over the data in tables and create charts that help them spot data that are inconsistent or improbable. Analysts examine supply and use data to see if they are in balance—making sure the sum of production and imports equals the sum of domestic use, exports and ending stocks.
themes: data validation; balance checks; visualization for verification
relevance_rationale: Using charts and balance checks to spot inconsistent or improbable data is a durable verification approach that applies regardless of whether LLMs assist.
notes: Based on Patrick Westhoff interview

author: David Herzog
extract: Immediately after we get a file, we want to answer a few big-picture questions before moving on to more-detailed data-integrity checks: What is the file format? How many rows do we have? Are all of the columns present with proper headers?
themes: data integrity; initial checks; file inspection
relevance_rationale: Initial big-picture checks on file format, row count, and column presence are fundamental intake procedures that endure with any data tool.

author: David Herzog
extract: For detailed integrity checks, generate pivot tables that list each data value and the number of times that value appears. These checks let us take a good look at what we have to work with. Check each column against the metadata to verify values match expected formats, ranges, and patterns.
themes: pivot tables; data integrity; frequency analysis
relevance_rationale: Pivot-table integrity checks are sound in principle but LLMs and code assistants can now automate frequency profiling and metadata validation much faster.

author: David Herzog
extract: When you run data integrity checks you'll need to have your data documentation at your side. Practice safe computing by making a copy of the metadata for yourself, so you always have a copy somewhere.
themes: data documentation; safe computing; backup
relevance_rationale: Keeping data documentation at hand during integrity checks is a basic due-diligence practice that endures in any workflow.

author: David Herzog
extract: Look at the column headers in Row 1 and make sure they are the same as in the metadata. It's tempting to change the headers to something that's more meaningful to us. However, it's best to practice safe computing by keeping the original names. That way, anyone who works with the data can easily refer to the metadata.
themes: column headers; metadata; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Preserving original column headers for reproducibility and documentation alignment is a timeless data management principle.

author: David Herzog
extract: Practice safe computing: We've made changes to our sheet, so let's preserve the original and create a new, working version. Save a working version of the file. After working with data for a while you will no doubt adopt a file-naming convention that fits your workflow.
themes: version control; safe computing; file management
relevance_rationale: Version control via Save As is the right principle but the method needs updating; modern workflows use git, computational notebooks, or cloud versioning rather than manual file copies.

author: David Herzog
extract: Look for suspect values—outliers, or numbers that are so extreme that they might be incorrect. Suspect values pop up in columns that contain dates or numbers. For dates, check that all values fall within the expected time frame. For amounts, look at both extremes.
themes: outlier detection; data validation; suspect values
relevance_rationale: Checking for outliers and suspect values in dates and amounts is a universal data quality practice that endures across all tools.

author: David Herzog
extract: We can use pivot tables to combine cities and states in our results, testing location data more rigorously than checking each field alone. This allows us to spot errors like a city listed in the wrong state.
themes: cross-field validation; pivot tables; location data
relevance_rationale: Cross-field validation logic endures but pivot tables are no longer the primary method; LLM-assisted code or automated profiling tools perform this more efficiently.

author: David Herzog
extract: When importing text files into Excel, we should make all of the columns text to avoid any import flubs that may delete data. Excel's General format will treat zip codes with leading zeros as numbers and lop off the leading zero. We can always change the format of the columns in Excel later.
chapter: Downloading, Unzipping and Inspecting Data Files
themes: data import; text format; leading zeros; Excel
relevance_rationale: The leading-zeros import trap is a real hazard but Excel-specific text-import workarounds are less relevant when Python/pandas or LLM-assisted workflows handle imports natively.

author: David Herzog
extract: Excel and other spreadsheet programs have built-in features and functions for data cleaning: Text to Columns to parse data stored in one column into multiple columns; Remove Duplicates to remove repeated rows; and formulas to carve out and reassemble data. OpenRefine is an open-source program from Google that lets users quickly run data integrity checks, then clean flawed values.
themes: Excel; OpenRefine; data cleaning tools
relevance_rationale: Excel and OpenRefine cleaning features still work but LLMs can now generate cleaning scripts on demand, shifting the toolkit toward code-generation workflows.

author: David Herzog
extract: More-advanced data users will use database managers, such as Microsoft Access or MySQL, to clean data using string functions inserted into SQL queries. Data users who are even more advanced write scripts using programming languages like Python, Ruby, PHP or Perl to clean and transform data sets, processing huge amounts of data more quickly than traditional programs.
themes: SQL; Python; programming; advanced cleaning
relevance_rationale: SQL and Python for advanced cleaning endure as skills but LLM code assistants dramatically lower the barrier, making these accessible to less-technical users.

author: David Herzog
extract: Use Excel's TRIM function to remove extraneous leading and trailing spaces, as well as duplicate spaces inside a text entry. Spaces technically are data (one of the basic ASCII characters) and can throw off any analysis.
themes: TRIM function; whitespace; Excel; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: TRIM for whitespace cleaning is a valid concept but the Excel-specific procedural detail is less relevant when LLMs can generate cleaning code in any language.

author: David Herzog
extract: OpenRefine's Clustering feature uses algorithms designed to find text values that might be the same. It employs two methods—key collision and nearest neighbor—each with multiple keying functions. This allows cleaning of misspelled or inconsistent entries en masse rather than one at a time.
themes: OpenRefine; clustering; standardization; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine clustering algorithms remain useful but LLMs can now perform fuzzy matching and entity resolution directly, offering an alternative approach to standardization.

author: David Herzog
extract: Text files are perhaps the most useful format of all because they can be read, processed and exported by all computers and data analysis programs. These text files come in two different flavors: fixed-width and delimited. Delimited data are easier to import than fixed-width, because computer programs can read the delimiters and automatically determine where the column breaks go.
themes: file formats; text files; CSV; delimited data; interoperability
relevance_rationale: Understanding text file formats (delimited vs fixed-width) and their interoperability properties is foundational data literacy that persists regardless of tools.

author: David Herzog
extract: Use a text editor such as Notepad++ (Windows) or TextWrangler (Mac) to inspect data files before importing them into a spreadsheet. Text editors can handle large files and let you see hidden characters like tabs and carriage returns that serve as delimiters.
chapter: Downloading, Unzipping and Inspecting Data Files
themes: text editors; file inspection; Notepad++; data verification
relevance_rationale: Notepad++/TextWrangler are outdated tool recommendations; modern editors (VS Code) and LLM-assisted inspection tools have largely superseded them.

author: David Herzog
extract: If agencies are unable to produce Excel files, they should be able to create ASCII text (delimited or fixed-width), because that is the format common to all computers. Under FOIA, federal agencies are supposed to provide data in the format that's requested if the agency is able to do so.
themes: data formats; FOIA; ASCII text; negotiation
relevance_rationale: FOIA format-request rights and ASCII text as a universal fallback format remain legally and practically relevant.

author: David Herzog
extract: Use website navigation tabs to look for words like Data, Open Data, Transparency and Statistics when searching agency sites for data. Try typing www.agencyname.gov/data in your browser. You can also try typing data instead of www in a URL, such as data.mo.gov for a state portal.
themes: data discovery; agency websites; URL patterns
relevance_rationale: URL pattern guessing for data discovery is still useful but LLMs and AI search can now surface open-data portals more efficiently than manual URL hacking.

author: David Herzog
extract: When someone creates a data file and stores columns with date data as text, it limits our ability to analyze the file. We won't be able to run date formulas, calculate elapsed time between dates, or figure out the day of the week. Another database design problem is run-on data—when the person who created the file stuffs more data than we'd like into one column.
themes: data structure; dates as text; run-on data; schema design
relevance_rationale: Understanding schema design problems like dates-as-text and run-on columns is essential data literacy that applies regardless of whether humans or LLMs do the cleaning.

author: David Herzog
extract: Let's practice safe computing and make sure we downloaded all of the data: check that we downloaded all columns, then use Ctrl-End to navigate to the bottom right corner of the spreadsheet to verify the row count matches what the source indicated.
themes: download verification; row count; safe computing
relevance_rationale: Verifying download completeness by checking row and column counts against source documentation is a basic but timeless data intake practice.

author: David Herzog
extract: OpenRefine's clustering function uses algorithms to identify similar text values and standardize them. After clicking Merge Selected & Re-Cluster, Refine standardizes all of those variations. We'd go through each of these clusters, then apply all of the other methods and keying functions.
themes: OpenRefine; clustering; standardization; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine clustering workflow is still valid but LLMs now offer alternative entity-resolution approaches that can handle similar standardization tasks conversationally.

author: David Herzog
extract: Many government agencies publish data tables inside PDFs. In order to work with the tables, we must first liberate them. Cometdocs and Zamzar are two proven services. Sometimes one does better than the other, depending on how the PDF is structured. So try both in real-life situations. Note that conversions will not work with PDFs that were created by scanning other documents.
themes: PDF extraction; data liberation; format conversion
relevance_rationale: PDF table extraction tools have been revolutionized by LLMs and vision models that can now parse complex PDF layouts far more reliably than Cometdocs or Zamzar.

author: David Herzog
extract: For students and professionals working with data, cleaning is an essential part of the process. This chapter showed how you can get started to solve some of the most vexing problems with Excel, OpenRefine and online PDF converters.
themes: data cleaning; workflow; essential practice
relevance_rationale: Data cleaning as essential practice endures, but the specific toolchain of Excel plus OpenRefine plus PDF converters needs updating for the LLM-assisted era.

author: David Herzog
extract: When we work with data, we will often compare numbers, because raw numbers hardly ever are meaningful by themselves. We create meaning when we compare them to other numbers or benchmarks.
themes: comparison; context; meaning-making
relevance_rationale: The principle that raw numbers need comparison to be meaningful is foundational numeracy that endures across all technology eras.

author: David Herzog
extract: When we work with data, we often generate what are called summary statistics. Some examples are counts, sums and averages. These numbers are used to take snapshots or descriptions of our data. We can generate these easily and they will tell us much about our data.
themes: summary statistics; counts; sums; averages
relevance_rationale: Understanding summary statistics (counts, sums, averages) as descriptive tools is foundational statistical literacy that endures regardless of who or what computes them.

author: David Herzog
extract: Outliers, or numbers that are notably low or high, can skew the mean. In those instances, the median is a more accurate reflection of the central tendency. Numbers with an unlimited cap—such as incomes or home sale prices—are more prone to having outliers.
themes: outliers; median; mean; central tendency; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Understanding when mean vs median is appropriate and how outliers skew averages is permanent statistical literacy essential for interpreting any AI-generated analysis.

author: David Herzog
extract: In our analytical toolbox we have a number of comparisons that are easy to generate: amount change or raw change, percent change, percent of total, ratios and rates. The amount change doesn't say much because we lack context. That's why we always want to compare the raw change to the starting point.
themes: percent change; ratios; rates; comparison; context
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Rates are helpful tools because they allow us to more fairly compare numbers from areas with dissimilar populations. When we calculate rates, it's a two-step process. First, we calculate a per capita rate. Then, we multiply that by a standard number to normalize our results. Per capita rates are usually so small that they're fractions and don't make much sense. So we normally multiply them by a standard number.
themes: rates; per capita; normalization; population comparison
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: We can have internal and external benchmarks. Internal benchmarks are numbers that are contained within our data. Percent change could be considered an external benchmark because we're comparing a number from one year to that number in the previous year. Reports created by government agencies, or nongovernmental organizations using data, can be a benchmarking tool.
themes: benchmarking; internal comparison; external comparison
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Ratios are helpful when we want to calculate proportions that show differences between numbers. To generate a ratio we take our number and divide it by another number to which we are drawing a comparison.
themes: ratios; proportions; comparison
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Sorting in Excel can be tricky because you first need to highlight all of the data you want to sort, including the headers but excluding any summary functions calculated at the bottom of the sheet. If you fail to highlight all of your data, Excel may sort some of your columns and leave others unsorted. This is a big problem, because you'll have mismatched data.
themes: sorting; Excel; data integrity; verification
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

author: David Herzog
extract: We used Excel's pivot table function to run simple integrity checks that gave us a better understanding of the values in each of our data columns. Here, we use pivot tables to answer 'How much?' or 'How many?' questions about categories that we find in our data.
themes: pivot tables; integrity checks; grouping; summarizing
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

author: David Herzog
extract: A filter is a temporary view of data in Excel. If we want to create a permanent copy we can copy our filtered results and then paste them into a new sheet.
themes: filtering; Excel; data subsetting
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

author: David Herzog
extract: Visualization is simply the act of creating charts based on data. Data analysts will often create many different visualizations that can help show the data from different perspectives, or they will show a subset of data. The techniques of exploratory data analysis, as promoted by statistician Tukey, use different types of graphs to get a better understanding.
themes: visualization; exploratory data analysis; Tukey; perspective
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: The first goal of an infographic is not to be beautiful just for the sake of eye appeal, but above all, to be understandable first, and beautiful after that; or to be beautiful because of its exquisite functionality. Graphics reveal data.
themes: visualization; design principles; Cairo; Tufte
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Quoting Cairo (2013) and Tufte (1983)

author: David Herzog
extract: Tufte's theory of graphical excellence instructs us to: show the data; induce the viewer to think about the substance rather than methodology; avoid distorting what the data have to say; present many numbers in a small space; make large data sets coherent; encourage the eye to compare different pieces of data; reveal the data at several levels of detail; serve a reasonably clear purpose; be closely integrated with statistical and verbal descriptions.
themes: visualization; Tufte; graphical excellence; design principles
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Give your chart a title. Label your chart elements including axes and legends. Include a source line—it shows others where you got the data, because you want to give others the ability to try to replicate or even challenge your results. Display enough data to provide context; showing only three years of unemployment data might mask the fact that the rate had been rising in earlier years.
themes: visualization; labeling; source attribution; replicability
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Which chart will be the most appropriate and communicate the best? The answer depends on the kind of data that we want to visualize. Pie charts are best for showing proportions of the whole. Vertical column charts are ideal for showing change over time with discrete time-series data. Line charts are a great choice for continuous time-series data. Scatterplots help us show whether we might have a relationship between two variables.
themes: chart selection; pie chart; column chart; line chart; scatterplot
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Avoid the temptation to use 3-D pie charts because they can distort results and make some slices appear bigger than they ought to be.
themes: visualization; pie chart; distortion; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: A map is often the best way to visualize geographic data. Excel is unable to create maps but can do so using plug-in programs. We can use geographic information system programs, such as ArcGIS or Quantum GIS, to create data maps. We can also use online programs, such as Google Fusion Tables.
themes: mapping; GIS; ArcGIS; Fusion Tables; geographic data
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

author: David Herzog
extract: We prefer to use data based on real dollars because doing so takes into account the effects of inflation. The title also signals to viewers they are looking at data spanning the years 2000 to 2013.
themes: inflation adjustment; real dollars; chart labeling; context
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: The scatterplot includes a linear trend line that shows the central tendency of the data. Any point that's well above the trend line represents a city whose violent crime numbers are higher than expected. Excel uses a statistical calculation called linear regression to determine the position of the linear trend line.
themes: scatterplot; trend line; linear regression; outlier detection
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

author: David Herzog
extract: Sparklines are intense, simple, word-sized graphics that are placed inside individual cells. A sparkline is an excellent tool for helping compare patterns in large data sets.
themes: sparklines; Tufte; pattern detection; Excel
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: Quoting Tufte

author: David Herzog
extract: When evaluating Web-based data visualization platforms, consider that you often surrender control of your data when you make it available on these sites. Make sure that you read the terms of service and the privacy statement. A related risk is that the visualization platform could be taken offline completely.
themes: web tools; data control; terms of service; platform risk
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

author: David Herzog
extract: Database managers, such as Microsoft Access and the open source MySQL, are designed to work with large files. Some database programs can easily work with millions of rows of data, something that's impossible with Excel's default installation settings. Database managers are especially powerful because they allow us to extract specific pieces of data that interest us.
themes: database managers; Access; MySQL; scalability
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

author: David Herzog
extract: Anyone who learns SQL on one database manager can apply that knowledge to another. Anyone who learns SQL can talk intelligently to database administrators at government agencies, making it easier to negotiate for offline data. Learning SQL forces database users to think logically about their data and how they are structured.
themes: SQL; logical thinking; data negotiation; transferable skills
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Database managers allow us to link tables together through a process called joining. If we get a database from a government agency that's stored in more than one table, we can stitch the data together to better make sense of it.
themes: database joins; relational data; government data
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Most advanced data analysts prefer to use more robust standalone statistical programs, such as IBM's SPSS, SAS or the open source R. Statistical programs can generate descriptive statistics and run tests that allow us to see whether there's any statistical relationship between variables. These are called inferential statistics.
themes: SPSS; SAS; R; inferential statistics; statistical software
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

author: David Herzog
extract: In medical and social science research, analysts will often run descriptive statistics first to understand their data. Then they will run inferential statistics to test their hypotheses.
themes: descriptive statistics; inferential statistics; workflow; hypothesis testing
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Refine can also split and merge values in columns. It even has its own scripting language called GREL, which allows you to write code for cleaning data.
themes: OpenRefine; GREL; scripting; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

author: David Herzog
extract: Anyone who wants to work with data needs to have some basic tools: spreadsheets, text editors and data cleaner. OpenRefine should work comfortably with data files of up to 10 columns by 100,000 rows.
themes: toolkit; spreadsheets; text editors; OpenRefine; tool selection
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

author: David Herzog
extract: Practice safe computing by saving a working copy of the file using File Save As, using a new file name.
themes: data preservation; backup; workflow discipline
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: David Herzog
extract: Experienced data users typically use database manager programs, such as Microsoft Access and MySQL, to create groups and summaries. However, we can perform many of the same functions using spreadsheets.
themes: spreadsheets; database managers; tool progression
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Data is not something that exists in nature, and unemployed people are a very different thing than unemployment data. Data is created. It is a record, a document, an artifact, dripping with meaning and circumstance.
themes: data ontology; quantification
relevance_rationale: The ontological distinction between data and reality is a foundational epistemological insight unaffected by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Data journalism is different from pure statistical analysis because we need culture, law, and politics to tell us what data matters and how. The data alone doesn't determine the story. But the story still has to be true, and hopefully also thorough and fair.
themes: journalism epistemology; subjectivity; truth
relevance_rationale: The need for cultural and political context to determine what data matters cannot be automated away

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Journalists need to understand the methods of any field they touch, or they will be unable to tell good work from bad. They won't know which analyses are worth repeating. Even worse, they will not understand which data matters.
themes: methodological literacy; evaluation
relevance_rationale: Methodological literacy for evaluating work quality remains essential even when LLMs assist analysis

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Data cannot tell us what to do, but it can sometimes tell us about consequences. Where prediction is possible, there is convincing evidence that data is essential. Purely qualitative methods, no matter how sophisticated, just don't seem to be as accurate.
themes: prediction; quantitative vs qualitative
relevance_rationale: The epistemological claim about data's predictive power vs qualitative methods remains valid

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The use of statistics in journalism, like the use of statistics in general, will always involve artistry. Questions of action can hone and refine data work, but actual action—making a choice and doing—requires practical knowledge, wisdom, and creativity.
themes: judgment; limits of data
relevance_rationale: Judgment, wisdom, and creativity in applying statistics to action are inherently human capacities LLMs cannot replace

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Quantification is the process that creates data. You can only measure what you can conceive. That's the first challenge of quantification. The next challenge is actually measuring it, and knowing that you measured it accurately.
themes: quantification; measurement; data creation
relevance_rationale: The conceptual challenges of quantification and measurement validity are unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Many words have quantitative aspects. Words like 'all,' 'every,' 'none,' and 'some' are so explicitly quantitative that they're called quantifiers. Comparisons like 'more' and 'fewer' are clearly about counting. The relation between words and numbers is of fundamental importance to the pursuit of truth. It tells you when you should be counting something.
themes: quantitative reasoning; language; verification
relevance_rationale: Understanding quantitative aspects of language and when to count is a reasoning skill LLMs support but do not replace

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Do not guess; try to count. And if you cannot count, admit that you are guessing.
themes: counting; evidence; intellectual honesty
relevance_rationale: The imperative to count rather than guess and to admit uncertainty is a timeless epistemic principle
notes: Quoting historian G. Kitson Clark

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Asking why the data was collected does not answer how it was collected, but it's often a big hint. The census is not an 'objective' count but a bureaucratic process that generates data for specific purposes.
themes: data provenance; institutional context; bias
relevance_rationale: Questioning data provenance and institutional context remains essential regardless of AI tools

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: If you want to know what the data really measures, the only thing that matters is how it was collected. The census up to 1950 counts something different than the census from 1960 onward, even though both call it 'race.' If the collection method is obscure, so is the data.
themes: data collection; measurement validity; provenance
relevance_rationale: Understanding that collection method defines what data truly measures is a foundational verification principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: What you should count depends on what you care about, that is, it depends on the story you are attempting to tell. And after looking at the data you may realize that you want to count something else. Your initial story may turn out to be uninteresting, unfair, or just plain wrong.
chapter: The Problem of What to Count
themes: operationalization; framing; iteration
relevance_rationale: The iterative nature of defining what to count based on story needs is an editorial judgment LLMs cannot automate

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: In practice we end up replacing rich concepts with much simpler proxies. We get 'test scores' instead of 'educational attainment.' In experimental science this is called operationalizing a variable, a fancy name for picking a definition that's both analytically useful and practical enough to create data.
chapter: The Problem of What to Count
themes: operationalization; proxy measures; abstraction
relevance_rationale: The concept of operationalization and proxy measures is epistemological knowledge unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: You should be skeptical of any headline that says the number of jobs in the United States has changed by fewer than about 105,000 since last month. That's because the monthly jobs growth estimate has a margin of error of about plus or minus 105,000.
themes: margin of error; statistical significance; skepticism
relevance_rationale: Skepticism about claims within margins of error is a statistical literacy principle that endures

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The error of a measurement is a necessary part of understanding what that measurement means. A reputable source will report the margin of error along with the results.
themes: margin of error; uncertainty; data quality
relevance_rationale: Understanding measurement error as essential to data meaning is a foundational principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Randomness is the key to sampling, because it makes over-representation by any one group extremely unlikely. Randomness is not subject to selection bias precisely because it has no relation to anything else.
themes: random sampling; selection bias; representativeness
relevance_rationale: The role of randomness in sampling and selection bias prevention is unchanged statistical knowledge

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Reliable data includes measures of error: how much the reported information is expected to differ from the reality it represents. If you do not know and cannot reasonably guess the sources and magnitudes of possible error, then you don't really know what the data means.
themes: measurement error; uncertainty; data quality
relevance_rationale: Understanding error sources and magnitudes as prerequisite to understanding data meaning endures

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The idea of measurement error is the idea of quantified uncertainty. This is one of the tremendous achievements of modern thought—the recognition that knowing how much we don't know has great value.
themes: uncertainty; epistemology; measurement error
relevance_rationale: The epistemological value of quantified uncertainty is a timeless insight

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: All quantification throws out information. It has to. That's the point of abstraction: to strip away enough detail that it's possible to use powerful general-purpose reasoning tools. Quantification represents the world through the systematic creation of data, a limited but powerful way to gather and summarize information.
themes: abstraction; representation; limits of data
relevance_rationale: The principle that abstraction necessarily discards information is a philosophical constant

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: It's been said that data speaks for itself. This is nonsense. The data didn't tell a story, you did. You saw a story that connects the data to the world. All data analysis is really data interpretation, and relies on combining data with something else, such as previously known facts or cultural knowledge. Data, on its own, has no meaning at all.
themes: interpretation; subjectivity; context
relevance_rationale: Data never speaks for itself; interpretation requires combining data with external knowledge, even more relevant with LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Context is where subjectivity enters into data interpretation. Actually the context comes before the data; it tells us what data is relevant, even what questions are relevant. Subjectivity is at the core of journalism, because there is no objective theory that tells us which true stories are the best.
themes: subjectivity; framing; context
relevance_rationale: Subjectivity in context and story selection is an irreducible human editorial function

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Finding a story in the data will always be an act of cultural creation. But those stories must still be true! The first big idea is the effect of chance, which can obscure the real relation between variables or create the appearance of a connection where none exists. The second is the nature of cause. Above all is the idea of considering multiple explanations for the same data.
themes: alternative explanations; chance; causation
relevance_rationale: Considering multiple explanations for data, including chance and causation, is timeless analytical reasoning

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Our very first questions have to be about the source of the data, the quantification process. Who recorded this and how? The first step in data analysis is seeing the frame: the assumptions about how the data was collected and what it means.
themes: data provenance; framing; verification
relevance_rationale: Questioning data provenance and collection framing is a verification first principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: If you can't express the core of your analysis in plain, non-technical language, you probably don't understand what you're doing.
themes: plain language; analytical clarity
relevance_rationale: Expressing analysis in plain language as test of understanding endures and is even more important when LLMs generate jargon

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The key skill in being right: Consider other explanations. There are common alternative explanations that are always worth considering: First, chance—sheer luck could be fooling us. Second, correlation—the decrease could be related without being caused. Third, everything else—the change could be caused by something that has never occurred to us.
themes: alternative explanations; causation; chance; correlation
relevance_rationale: The checklist of alternative explanations (chance, correlation, confounders) is timeless analytical method

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The less likely it is that something can occur by chance, the more likely it is that something other than chance is the right explanation. The probability that you would see data like yours purely by chance is known as the p-value in statistics.
themes: p-value; statistical significance; chance
relevance_rationale: Understanding p-values and statistical significance is foundational statistical literacy

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: No mathematical procedure can turn uncertainty into truth\! We can only find different ways of talking about the strength of the evidence. The right threshold to declare something 'significant' depends on how you feel about the relative risks of false negatives and false positives for your particular case.
themes: statistical significance; uncertainty; limits of statistics
relevance_rationale: The limits of mathematical procedures in establishing truth and the role of judgment in thresholds endures

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Rough estimates can be incredibly useful for checking your story, and there's a trove of practical lore devoted to them. We're asking whether or not we should believe that chance is a good explanation for seeing the data, and we only need an order-of-magnitude estimate for that.
themes: estimation; verification; back-of-envelope
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now generate rough order-of-magnitude estimates quickly, but the principle of using estimates for verification still endures

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Bayesian statistics works by asking: What hypothetical world is most likely to produce the data we have? And how much more likely is it to do so than the alternatives? The possible 'worlds' are captured by statistical models, little simulations of hypothetical realities that produce fake data. Then we compare the fake data to the real data to decide which model most closely matches reality.
themes: Bayesian statistics; modeling; hypothesis testing
relevance_rationale: Bayesian reasoning about which model best fits data is a conceptual framework unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: A better story than 'stoplight worked' or 'chance decline' would be 'stoplight probably worked.' Journalists tend to be uncomfortable with intermediate probability values. But if that's your best knowledge, it's what you must say.
themes: uncertainty; intellectual honesty; probability
relevance_rationale: Communicating intermediate probability honestly rather than false certainty remains an editorial imperative

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: There is no escape from judgment. But even very rough estimates may be usefully combined. If nothing else, the existence of the prior in statistical formulas helpfully reminds us to consult all other sources!
themes: Bayesian reasoning; prior knowledge; judgment
relevance_rationale: The necessity of judgment and consulting all sources when combining evidence is inherently human

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Every statement about cause is really a statement about the way the world would have been without that cause, a counterfactual statement. Causation is so tricky because it requires reasoning about imaginary worlds that we can never observe directly.
themes: causation; counterfactual reasoning
relevance_rationale: Counterfactual reasoning as the foundation of causal claims is a philosophical principle unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The correct comparison is not 'up or down,' but 'what would have happened otherwise?' Any claim of cause is implicitly a claim about data from a world we don't ever get to see. It's worth thinking about how to approximate this world through comparisons or modeling.
themes: counterfactual reasoning; comparison; causation
relevance_rationale: Comparing to what would have happened otherwise rather than simple up/down is timeless causal reasoning

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Cause cannot usually be read directly from the data. Usually, the data alone cannot tell you which causal pattern produced your correlation. We have to use other information to figure out the correct causal structure.
themes: causation; correlation; limits of data
relevance_rationale: The principle that causation cannot be read directly from data requires human domain knowledge

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The third variable in a three-way causal structure is called a confounder. Confounding variables appear frequently in real world analyses. The key is to look for another variable that causes both of the variables you see as related. You can only find a confounder by thinking about the broader context of the data.
themes: confounding variables; causation; context
relevance_rationale: Understanding confounding variables requires contextual knowledge that LLMs can assist with but not replace

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The best way to figure out causation is to do an experiment. Journalists don't normally get to do experiments. Sometimes we can evaluate other people's experiments, but usually we are reduced to dealing with observational data. Causal models are a way of expressing the possible causal relationships between variables to clarify our thinking.
themes: experiments; observational data; causal models
relevance_rationale: The distinction between experimental and observational data and limits of each is foundational

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The data alone cannot tell us that a story is true, because there could be many other stories that produce the same data. In journalism work, a narrative extracted from the data—'the story'—is morally equivalent to a hypothesis.
themes: hypothesis testing; alternative explanations; journalism epistemology
relevance_rationale: The principle that many stories can produce the same data and stories are hypotheses is timeless

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Your list of hypotheses should include definitional problems, quantification troubles, errors in the data, random chance, and as many confounding variables as you can think of. The basic rule is this: you have to imagine it before you can prove that it's true.
themes: hypothesis generation; checklist; alternative explanations
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now help generate lists of alternative hypotheses systematically, enhancing but not replacing this checklist approach

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Good data analysis is more about ruling out many false interpretations, rather than trying to prove a single interpretation is correct. Falsification is a much more robust practice than verification. There are many reasons why proving a story wrong is a better goal than proving a story right.
themes: falsification; Popper; verification; confirmation bias
relevance_rationale: Falsification as superior to verification is a foundational epistemological principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: It's useful to inquire against your hopes. Your critics certainly will. We know from landmark cognitive psychology experiments that there are biases in human cognition that lead us to reject, discredit, and selectively forget information that doesn't fit with what we already believe.
themes: confirmation bias; cognitive bias; self-criticism
relevance_rationale: Inquiring against your hopes and awareness of confirmation bias is more critical than ever with LLM-generated analysis

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: At the beginning of data analysis work, dream up all sorts of possible interpretations, all sorts of possible stories. The available data will rule some of them out. The stories which survive that test are the ones you have to choose between. The remaining set of hypotheses will tell you which information you need to rule each of them out.
themes: hypothesis testing; method of elimination; investigation
relevance_rationale: LLMs can help systematically generate rival hypotheses, but the human discipline of elimination-based reasoning still essential

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The concept of triangulation in the social sciences captures the idea that a true hypothesis should be supported by many different kinds of evidence, including qualitative evidence and theoretical arguments. What you see in the data cannot contradict what you see in the street, so you always need to look in the street.
themes: triangulation; mixed methods; verification
relevance_rationale: Triangulation across qualitative and quantitative evidence is a core verification method unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The story you run is the story that survives your best attempts to discredit it.
themes: falsification; editorial judgment; rigor
relevance_rationale: Publishing only stories that survive attempts to discredit them is a timeless editorial standard

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: A journalist is responsible for the ideas that end up in their reader's head. There are two parts: ensuring that the data and the story clearly and accurately represents the reality, and ensuring that this accurate representation is what the reader actually comes away with.
themes: communication; audience responsibility; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Journalist responsibility for what ends up in readers heads is an ethical principle unaffected by AI

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Data visualization can be helpful because it transfers some of the cognitive work of understanding data to the enormously powerful human visual system. With a well-designed visualization, you don't need to think about it to see a trend or a cluster.
themes: visualization; cognition; perception
relevance_rationale: The cognitive science of visualization leveraging human visual perception is unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Our unconscious ability to compare lengths is why you should generally start the Y axis at zero. Otherwise, the relative lengths won't correspond to the relative values, and we'll perceive incorrect relationships. Ignoring visual perception when creating data visualizations is like ignoring the consensus meanings of words when writing.
themes: visualization; chart design; perception
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now generate charts and suggest Y-axis settings, but understanding perceptual principles behind design choices still matters

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Information comes in two flavors: examples and statistics. You can collect a small amount of specific information from many people and summarize it with statistics. Or you can collect rich, open-ended information from just a few people and present each as an in-depth example. In the best case, a story combines numbers and narratives.
themes: quantitative vs qualitative; storytelling; mixed methods
relevance_rationale: The complementary roles of examples and statistics in storytelling is a timeless communication principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Generally, examples are much more persuasive than statistics—even when they shouldn't be. The statistical information simply didn't figure into the way readers formed their impressions. People chose bypass surgery twice as often when the anecdotes favored it, completely ignoring the stated odds of a cure.
themes: cognitive bias; anecdotes vs data; persuasion
relevance_rationale: The cognitive finding that anecdotes overpower statistics in persuasion is a human cognition constant

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: If you want the reader to walk away with a fair and representative idea of what the data means, then your examples should be average. They should be typical. Well-chosen examples bring clarity, accuracy, and life to a story.
themes: representative examples; storytelling; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Choosing representative rather than dramatic examples for accuracy is a timeless editorial principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Not everyone is on Twitter, not everyone is Tweeting, and even fewer are speaking on the topic of your story. This data has a bias toward certain types of people. Any representation of public sentiment created from social media data will be biased in an unknown way—the results will be skewed relative to a random sample, and the worst part is you won't know how skewed they are.
themes: selection bias; social media data; representativeness
relevance_rationale: Social media bias critique now extends to LLM training data bias; the principle endures but needs to encompass AI-generated data too

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: The most important principle of communicating uncertainty is this: Communicate it. Don't let someone come away from your story with a warped sense of the risk, or too certain about something subtle.
themes: uncertainty; communication; intellectual honesty
relevance_rationale: Communicating uncertainty to audiences is a timeless editorial obligation

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: One way to communicate a probability is to talk about its frequency interpretation—as a count of some number of things out of some larger number. Frequencies work particularly well if you can compare the denominator to familiar units of population. Get your measurements in units of people whenever possible—it's a unit that everyone understands.
themes: probability communication; frequency framing; audience
relevance_rationale: Frequency framing for communicating probabilities leverages human cognition, unaffected by LLMs

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Humans have a nonlinear perception of numerical probabilities. People exaggerate the probability of very rare events (like plane crashes) while underappreciating the probability of very likely events (like heart disease). Telling people the actual numbers does not change this perception, because their perception is not based on numbers.
themes: probability perception; cognitive bias; risk communication
relevance_rationale: Nonlinear human perception of probability is a cognitive constant no technology changes

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Prediction is important because action is important. What use is journalism that doesn't help you decide what to do? Prediction also has close links to truth. Falsification is one of the strongest truth-finding methods, and it's prediction that allows us to compare our ideas with the world to see if they hold up.
themes: prediction; falsification; actionable journalism
relevance_rationale: The connection between prediction, action, and falsification is a timeless epistemological framework

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Expert political predictions were consistently no better than guessing. A 'dart-throwing chimp' would do just as well. Leaning on experts doesn't excuse the journalist from disseminating bad predictions unchallenged.
themes: expert prediction; skepticism; accountability
relevance_rationale: LLM-based forecasting tools now outperform some expert predictions, making the critique of expert prediction even more potent but requiring updated framing
notes: Based on Tetlock's Expert Political Judgment

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: What does help is keeping track of your predictions. The simplest way is to write down each prediction you make and, when the time comes, tally it as right or wrong. If you really do care about making correct predictions, the very best thing you can do is track your accuracy.
themes: prediction tracking; calibration; accountability
relevance_rationale: Tracking prediction accuracy as calibration practice is timeless and applies to LLM outputs too

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Simple statistical methods are almost always better than humans at combining information to predict behavior. A review of 136 studies showed statistical prediction was as good or better than clinical prediction about 90 percent of the time.
themes: statistical prediction; human judgment; modeling
relevance_rationale: LLMs and ML now extend statistical prediction capabilities far beyond simple models, strengthening the point but changing the methods
notes: Based on Meehl's Clinical Versus Statistical Prediction

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Three lessons for journalism: First, prediction is really hard, and almost everyone who does it is doing no better than chance. Second, it pays to use the best available method of combining information, and that method is often simple statistical prediction. Third, if you really do care about making correct predictions, the very best thing you can do is track your accuracy.
themes: prediction; statistical methods; accountability
relevance_rationale: LLMs now offer additional prediction methods beyond simple statistics, but the three lessons framework needs updating to include AI tools

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Standards of journalistic accuracy apply to descriptions of the future at least as much as they apply to descriptions of the present, if not more so. In the case of predictions it's especially important to be clear about uncertainty, about the limitations of what can be known.
themes: prediction; uncertainty; journalistic standards
relevance_rationale: Standards of accuracy applying to predictions and communicating uncertainty about the future is a permanent editorial principle

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: Data can demolish or reinforce stereotypes, so it's important for the journalist to be aware that these stereotypes are in play. It's not enough for data to be presented 'accurately.' We have to ask what the recipient will end up believing about the world, and about the people represented by the data.
themes: stereotypes; audience impact; representation
relevance_rationale: Awareness of how data can reinforce or demolish stereotypes is more relevant than ever with AI-generated content

source_title: The Curious Journalist's Guide to Data
author: Jonathan Stray
extract: My advice is to look always for the underlying sense of the thing, the plain-language explanation. Keep searching until you find an answer that makes sense. It's not knowing everything that makes a technical professional, but being willing to find out.
themes: learning; plain language; professional development
relevance_rationale: Seeking the underlying plain-language explanation and willingness to learn endures as professional disposition

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Investigative journalists should have a 'documents state of mind' — knowing that documents exist somewhere that can explore, contradict or confirm each point of an investigation.
themes: document trails; source identification; verification mindset
relevance_rationale: The documents state of mind is a foundational investigative epistemology unaffected by AI tools.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The 'working from the outside in' technique uses concentric circles: secondary sources on the outermost ring provide the most information and are easiest to find but may not be reliable; primary documents in the middle ring reflect a direct picture of events; human sources at the center provide eyewitness accounts.
themes: investigative method; source hierarchy; research design
relevance_rationale: The outside-in source hierarchy remains a sound investigative framework regardless of tools used.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Beginning with a hypothesis is not the same as starting with an angle to prove. The best investigative journalists collect both supporting and contradictory evidence for a story, then weigh carefully what the evidence shows.
themes: hypothesis testing; evidence weighing; objectivity
relevance_rationale: Hypothesis testing and weighing contradictory evidence is a core epistemological principle immune to LLM disruption.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The possible questions to ask on the way to launching a project are: How is the system supposed to work? How well is the system working? Who is benefiting and who is suffering because of the way the system works?
themes: hypothesis testing; systems analysis; scoping
relevance_rationale: Systems-analysis scoping questions are foundational to investigative design and unchanged by AI.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Secondary sources are most useful when they lead to primary documents or databases. Documents and databases can lie and err just like human sources can, because documents and databases are prepared by humans. However, unlike humans, documents do not claim to have been misquoted.
themes: source hierarchy; document trails; verification
relevance_rationale: The epistemology of document reliability vs human sources remains essential regardless of technology.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Too many investigators contact only the obvious sources — those in current positions — while ignoring 'formers' (the former spouse, former professional colleague, former accountant, former neighbor). The 'formers' often have scores to settle or outdated knowledge, but they can be candid, have had time to reflect and may have kept relevant documents.
themes: source identification; human sources; interviewing
relevance_rationale: Cultivating former associates as sources is a human-relationship skill AI cannot replace.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Human sources are crucial because documents take on new meaning when explained or discussed by insiders or experts. This is one reason to refrain from key interviews until the document trail has been firmly established. Information in documents often serves as the basis for questions during the interview; because a journalist might get only one interview with a source, it is best to wait until most documents are in hand.
themes: interview sequencing; document trails; human sources
relevance_rationale: Interview sequencing strategy -- documents before key interviews -- is a timeless investigative principle.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Parallel backgrounding: when the subject is connected to an institution, the organization will leave its own trails. By following those trails, investigators might locate new information about the subject. Indirect backgrounding is parallel backgrounding on a grand scale — analyzing the larger economic or political environment to understand the subject's relationships.
themes: backgrounding; document trails; research techniques
relevance_rationale: Parallel backgrounding concept endures but LLMs can now rapidly surface organizational connections that required manual tracing.
notes: Dennis King's techniques from 'Get the Facts on Anyone'

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: As a journalist collects primary documents and human sources, prepare digital files and folders to catch the information as it comes in and then evaluate the information often (perhaps weekly). The potentially valuable material should be entered into a chronology. Creating a chronology does not mean writing the story chronologically — the chronology is meant to organize large amounts of information in a linear way to help illuminate links among factors that might otherwise seem unrelated.
themes: data organization; chronology; information management
relevance_rationale: Chronology building remains essential but LLMs can now assist with organizing and linking large volumes of information.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Information excluded from the chronology early in an investigation may become relevant later. As a result, all information collected must be reevaluated from time to time during the reporting stage.
themes: data organization; iterative analysis; chronology
relevance_rationale: The principle of re-evaluating previously excluded information during iterative analysis is a timeless analytical discipline.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Hannah-Jones stresses that reporters often try to prove that an official acted with prejudicial intent. But it can be difficult or impossible to prove what someone was thinking. If a journalist uses documents, data and interviews to prove that the resulting system is inequitable and that people are suffering as a result, proving intent becomes unnecessary. The outcome of people suffering is the point to focus on.
themes: outcomes vs intent; systems analysis; social justice
relevance_rationale: Focusing on outcomes rather than intent is a durable investigative framing principle independent of tools.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The Star Tribune team grounded its investigation in accepted principles of sound social science research, such as studying a sample of adequate size. Previous studies had been flawed by short follow-up periods, faulty study groups that lumped different types of offenders together, poor counting techniques that only noted convictions rather than arrests, and erroneous time calculations.
themes: research methodology; social science; sample design; data quality
relevance_rationale: Grounding investigations in sound social science methodology is a permanent epistemological standard.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Storyboarding template for investigative feasibility: Key question (state your goal in one or two sentences), New ground (will it tell your audience something new?), Impact on real people, Room for reform, Potential victims, Key people sources (who must you talk to and how realistic is access?), Data (what data do you need and is it obtainable?), Documents and records (how many open records requests?), Timeline (projected timeframe), and visual/interactive components.
themes: project planning; storyboarding; feasibility; scoping
relevance_rationale: Storyboarding and feasibility assessment frameworks for investigations remain essential planning tools.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: After conceiving of an investigation, a journalist has to make a realistic assessment including: accessibility of records and sources, access to buildings and sites, the time it will take, the possibility of public attacks and threats of lawsuits, computer security and physical security, and who might be a good collaborator. Lists of documents needed, people to interview and places to visit should be made.
themes: feasibility assessment; project planning; risk assessment
relevance_rationale: Realistic assessment of access, risk, and collaboration needs is an enduring project-planning discipline.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Periodically, an assessment of how the investigation is going should be done. Decisions on whether to stop, postpone or publish should be calmly considered. The investigative journalist should ensure that all those accused have had a chance to respond. An assessment also allows the journalists to look for inconsistencies in interviews, documents or data. Journalists should begin writing parts of the story as early as possible — it is through writing and editing that gaps in reporting are found earlier.
themes: quality assurance; iterative review; fairness; gap analysis
relevance_rationale: Periodic assessment, fairness checks, and early writing to find reporting gaps are timeless quality practices.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Bogdanich discovered numerous accidents that had been reported and, by comparing the data to news articles, he also discovered those that were not reported. By interviewing plaintiffs' attorneys and victims and victims' families to fill in the gaps, he collected evidence of accident sites being tampered with by railroad companies.
themes: data comparison; gap filling; cross-referencing
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing data with news articles to find unreported events is a sound method that LLMs can now accelerate.
notes: NYT 'Death on the Tracks' example

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Journalists should independently verify all secondary source information. A journalist must check later issues of the publication for corrections, comments on stories, letters to the editor and clarifying follow-up articles, and then confirm the accuracy of the information by seeking out the original sources, if possible.
themes: verification; secondary sources; fact-checking
relevance_rationale: Independent verification of secondary sources is a core journalistic principle undiminished by AI.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: One of the first places to go on any organization's website is the 'About' section, to look for a statement of purpose and to evaluate the people behind the site. If it is a nonprofit, look for a list of financial supporters. Also check to see if the site is part of a larger organization and look into that group.
themes: source evaluation; web research; credibility assessment
relevance_rationale: Evaluating organizational credibility through About pages and financial supporters remains essential due diligence.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: A large amount of information — known as the Invisible or Deep Web — resides in layers below the information that traditional search engines see. Google and other search engines mine the 'surface web' but in most cases do not pull results from many of the databases available on individual websites. Using a traditional search engine is essential but it is important to supplement your search by going to specific sites.
themes: deep web; search strategy; data discovery
relevance_rationale: The deep web concept endures but LLMs and AI-powered search tools are changing how hidden databases are discovered and queried.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Google Advanced Search allows a journalist to point all the power of Google toward one specific web domain, often finding many more results than a website's own search engine. It also allows specifying the type of information sought, from specific types of data files to PDFs, PowerPoints and text files. This is a great way to find reports and databases.
themes: Google Advanced Search; data discovery; search strategy
relevance_rationale: Google Advanced Search techniques still work but AI-powered search tools offer more sophisticated domain-specific discovery.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Almost any project should begin with a review of what's already been reported; there's no point in devoting time to a story if new ground can't be broken. Previously reported stories can offer a goldmine of information unavailable elsewhere — comments a politician made years ago can provide examples of hypocrisy. Reviewing past stories is also a great way to find experts, skeptics, victims and advocates.
themes: literature review; secondary sources; source identification
relevance_rationale: Reviewing prior coverage endures as a principle but LLMs can now rapidly synthesize past reporting on any topic.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Editors of subject-specific magazines, newsletters and websites will have years of experience overseeing coverage of a topic. They are often generous with their knowledge, sharing industry norms as well as specific tips. Trade publications reveal how insiders talk to each other, in the secure belief that no prying outsider would have access — the gap between public and trade communications can be revealing.
themes: trade publications; source identification; industry intelligence
relevance_rationale: Cultivating trade publication editors and exploiting public-vs-trade communication gaps requires human relationships.
notes: Jessica Mitford insight on trade vs public-facing publications

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Doctoral and master's students often receive unparalleled access to institutions because they are seen as nonthreatening. They may obtain access to records or sources that working journalists may not. A journalist should assume a thesis or dissertation has been composed on the project at hand, and then find it through online databases.
themes: dissertations; academic sources; secondary sources
relevance_rationale: Searching dissertations and academic sources endures but LLMs can now surface and summarize relevant theses much faster.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: When a journalist locates a useful book, it is wise to contact the author to request any relevant information that may not have found its way between covers and resides unpublished in a file drawer.
themes: source cultivation; books; unpublished data
relevance_rationale: Contacting authors for unpublished information is a human-source cultivation skill AI cannot replace.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Full-text databases are usually preferable to those providing citations or abstracts only.
