A passing reference to your investigative subject might hold the key to the investigation — journalists have found subjects' names in footnotes to judges' opinions and near the bottom of feature stories that became starting points for entire new sections of investigative projects.
themes: database search; full-text search; secondary sources
relevance_rationale: Full-text database searching remains valuable but LLMs can now parse and surface passing references across massive corpora far more efficiently.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Good reporters will find primary documents pertinent to any investigative topic, obtain those documents and make sure they have an overview and details of a person or an institution before the first formal interview. The best interviewers often compose a dossier — a collection of documents about a particular person, event or subject — on the subject or topic before they set up the interview.
themes: document trails; interview preparation; dossier building
relevance_rationale: Building a dossier from primary documents before key interviews is a permanent investigative discipline.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: It would be a mistake to assume that everything gathered through research is complete and accurate. Nor should investigative journalists assume they can forego checking for hard-copy documents that existed before computers. A database may be composed of only parts of primary documents because they often are a summary of all the information actually filed.
themes: data quality; completeness; verification; analog records
relevance_rationale: Skepticism about database completeness and checking analog originals is even more important when AI generates plausible-seeming summaries.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: MuckRock provides free summaries and links to public records guides throughout the U.S. It also collates Freedom of Information requests and the documents resulting from those requests. DocumentCloud allows thousands of journalists to post millions of searchable public documents. MuckRock and DocumentCloud are an ongoing tutorial in the effective use of open-records laws.
themes: MuckRock; DocumentCloud; FOI; public records
relevance_rationale: MuckRock and DocumentCloud remain active, essential FOI infrastructure for investigative journalists.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: During an investigation of an individual, a journalist should try to examine every public record concerning the individual, including property records, loans, property taxes, licenses, voter's registration, liens and lawsuits, including any divorce or probate court proceedings.
themes: public records; document trails; people investigation
relevance_rationale: Systematic examination of every public record on an individual is an enduring investigative checklist.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Investigative journalists must try to verify all information in primary documents. As with all other documents, the information contained in vital records might be misleading — doctors may list incorrect causes of death, military veterans may falsify service records, and driver's licenses can be fraudulently obtained.
themes: verification; document authenticity; data quality
relevance_rationale: Verification of primary documents against potential fraud or error is a permanent epistemological requirement.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Journalists can use UCC (Uniform Commercial Code) filings to document who is doing business with whom. UCC forms have revealed the address of a borrower unlisted in telephone directories, names of a borrower's spouse or business partners, dates of transactions, and property owned as collateral. They have been used to track airplanes, yachts, art fraud and misuse of government funds.
themes: UCC filings; business investigation; financial records
relevance_rationale: UCC filings as investigative tools for tracing business relationships remain a permanent public records resource.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: A journalist should consider which level of government records (federal, state, county, city or special district) to examine first. Having done that, an investigator should search all branches of that level. Every level has three branches: legislative, executive and judiciary.
themes: government records; systematic search; institutional structure
relevance_rationale: The systematic approach of checking all government levels and branches for records is a permanent investigative framework.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Following a document trail for tracking a political donor: (1) Campaign finance reports for name, occupation, address, donation amount; (2) News articles for background; (3) Social media for statements; (4) Property records for home value and vehicles; (5) Secretary of State for corporate records; (6) SEC records for affiliations; (7) Professional regulation records for licensing/discipline; (8) Court records for criminal or civil cases.
themes: document trails; political investigation; systematic search
relevance_rationale: The document-trail checklist for tracking donors endures but LLMs can now automate cross-referencing across these multiple record types.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Database analysis — also known as computer-assisted reporting (CAR) or data journalism — is an integral part of investigative reporting and has improved the accuracy, credibility and breadth of journalism. But investigative journalists must still use other primary methods — like interviewing and getting out into the field — to judge how accurate the data are and to bring the human stories into their reporting.
themes: data journalism; CAR; mixed methods; human-data balance
relevance_rationale: The principle that data journalism must be complemented by interviews and field reporting is even more relevant in the AI era.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: If online data is not easily available, journalists have become adept at 'scraping' the data — running a program that downloads all of the data and automatically puts the information into a database. When no database yet exists, reporters can build databases themselves by getting paper or digital records and entering the information manually into a spreadsheet.
themes: web scraping; data acquisition; database building
relevance_rationale: Web scraping and manual database building still occur but LLM-powered tools and code generation have transformed the techniques used.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Three basic tools of CAR: (1) Finding and downloading data — searching using combinations of key words, web domains like .gov, and file types like xls. (2) Spreadsheets like Excel or Google Sheets — adept at filtering and summarizing data. (3) Database managers like Access, DB Browser SQLite or MySQL — which allow sorting, filtering, summarizing and joining hundreds of thousands of records, especially when two or more files need to be compared based on a common ID.
themes: spreadsheets; database managers; CAR tools; data analysis
relevance_rationale: Spreadsheets and SQL remain relevant but LLMs now enable natural-language querying and code-free data analysis as alternatives.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Advanced tools for data journalism: (1) Data visualization — charts and maps using Google Maps, Tableau, or ArcGIS Online. (2) Statistical software like R for significant patterns and possible causes. (3) Social network analysis software like NodeXL for visualizing connections among people or entities. (4) Coding with Python for downloading, cleaning, restructuring, analyzing and visualizing data.
themes: data visualization; statistical software; network analysis; Python; R
relevance_rationale: These tools remain useful but LLMs can now generate visualization code, statistical analysis, and network diagrams from natural language prompts.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: How to find if a relevant government database exists: Check the IRE Resource Center with keywords; check the NICAR database library; check audit reports (auditors cite which databases they reviewed in appendices); check the GIJN weekly data updates; check the government agency website's data section; check the agency's record-retention schedule for an inventory of databases; check IRE tip sheets on specific topics.
themes: data discovery; government data; IRE; NICAR
relevance_rationale: Knowing where to find government databases through IRE, NICAR, and audit reports is enduring institutional knowledge.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Before acquiring a database: (1) Ask for the record layout (what columns are included), the code sheet (translating any codes), and the form that is filled out for each record. (2) Ask to see the actual data — just because columns are listed doesn't mean data was entered. (3) Determine the format and size — format tells you how difficult it will be for software to read, size helps check whether records are missing.
themes: data acquisition; record layout; data quality; metadata
relevance_rationale: Due diligence on record layouts, code sheets, and data quality before acquiring databases is a permanent professional practice.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: After receiving a database, always do an 'outer integrity' check: Compare electronic records against a sample of hard-copy records or other studies using the data, and compare the totals of amounts in the database to totals in other reports. Other journalists or experts who have worked with the same database can explain its potential flaws. Remember that every database is incomplete and imperfect — just like human sources.
themes: data validation; integrity checking; cross-referencing; data quality
relevance_rationale: Outer integrity checks and cross-validation of databases remain essential regardless of AI analysis capabilities.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Principles for managing newsroom databases: Community databases come from more than just local agencies — federal and commercial databases can be sliced to local level. A database should not be used for just one story — archive it for years. Have a plan for periodic updating. Keep up with online databases that can augment in-house ones. When a database doesn't exist, consider building your own from hard copy if it can produce a minimum story.
themes: database management; newsroom workflow; data archiving
relevance_rationale: Newsroom database management principles endure but cloud-based and AI-augmented data platforms are changing archiving and updating workflows.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Elliot Jaspin's strategy for gaining access to databases: (1) Get a hard-copy record or digital form so you know it exists and what it contains. (2) Request and obtain one electronic record. (3) Then request a copy of the full database since the release of one record has established it is public information.
themes: FOI strategy; data access; public records
relevance_rationale: Jaspins incremental FOI strategy for establishing data as public information is a permanent legal/tactical approach.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Reporters have matched lottery ticket sales to income data to show how the poor gamble the most, house mortgage denials against racial data to show racial disparity, toxic waste sites against income data to show chemicals dumped in poor areas, and disallowed voter ballots against racial data to show the oldest election technology used in minority areas.
themes: data joining; demographic analysis; social justice; cross-referencing
relevance_rationale: Data joining for demographic disparity analysis endures as a method but LLMs can now assist with matching, cleaning, and interpreting joined datasets.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: DocumentCloud allows journalists to upload public and business documents and run them through software that identifies patterns and key entities — people, places and things — repeated throughout the documents. Adobe Acrobat Pro DC is useful for optical character recognition tools, which index words on each page allowing journalists to search for key words.
themes: DocumentCloud; OCR; unstructured data; text analysis
relevance_rationale: DocumentCloud entity extraction and OCR remain useful but LLMs now offer far more powerful document analysis, entity recognition, and pattern identification.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: When beginning an investigation, divide potential sources into 'currents' and 'formers.' Currents are easy to find with online directories, phone calls, email and social media. Formers are much more likely to talk freely about their previous place of work. Finding formers: check newspaper archives for comings and goings, government paper trails, press releases, industry magazines, LinkedIn and social media.
themes: source identification; human sources; currents and formers
relevance_rationale: The currents-and-formers framework endures but LLMs and LinkedIn-scraping tools have changed how formers are located.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Many whistleblowers are sincere and correct, having become whistleblowers reluctantly. Some are correct but seeking publicity or payback. Others are sincere but ultimately incorrect. Some may simply want money. A journalist must evaluate the motivation of each whistleblower, whether contacted directly or found through unions, government offices, or online forums.
themes: whistleblowers; source evaluation; motivation assessment
relevance_rationale: Evaluating whistleblower motivation and reliability is a human judgment skill unaffected by AI.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: It is important to background outside experts to see if their knowledge is up-to-date and if they are respected in the field. Check résumés, articles, and social media postings. When experts from think tanks are used, it is critical to identify the think tank's ideology or politics.
themes: expert evaluation; source credibility; bias detection
relevance_rationale: Backgrounding experts for credibility and identifying think tank ideology is a permanent verification requirement.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Whether investigating an agency, business or institution, it is often clear who has ultimate authority. But sometimes titles and organization charts can be deceptive. Some journalists have used social network analysis — an accepted method of social science — to diagram networks of power.
themes: network analysis; power structures; social science methods
relevance_rationale: Social network analysis to map power structures endures as a concept but AI tools now automate network visualization and entity extraction.

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: For covering marginalized communities: Don't practice 'parachute journalism.' Do go to lunches, meetings, rallies. Consider doing smaller stories to show you value what is important to the community. Seek out solutions-based stories. Ask 'Who else should I know?' and 'Who doesn't represent you?'
themes: marginalized communities; source cultivation; community engagement
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Maria Polletta tips for IRE

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The interviewer needs to know as much as possible going into the interview. Look for points of commonality with a source: a school or church connection, following the same sports teams. Be genuine — 'Folks smell fake a mile away.' Documents discovered during preparation come in handy: reluctant witnesses are more willing to talk if asked about documents and what they mean.
themes: interview preparation; source cultivation; document-based interviewing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Mike McGraw's three-phase interview approach: Phase one is the 'time to lie' interview — ask softball questions and let the subject claim everything is fine. Phase two is the 'interim' interview — return with harder questions, adopt the subject's angle of vision. Phase three is the 'come to Jesus' interview — present all research results and ask 'Have you told me everything?'
themes: interview technique; confrontation interview; phased interviewing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Some of the best interviewers use silence to their advantage. Most people feel impelled to fill a silence. It is important to figure out what is not being said as well as what is. An investigative reporter should leave no easy out — rather than ask whether the subject fired the whistleblower, ask why. That puts the subject in the position of explaining rather than issuing a simple denial.
themes: interview technique; silence; questioning strategy
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Conducting interviews by email, text or direct message instead of one-on-one conversation is a bad idea except for the most basic information. It's difficult to ask follow-up questions, impossible to read body language or seize a moment and take an interview in a new direction, and with top officials, a journalist doesn't even know who is typing the responses.
themes: interview technique; communication channels
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Start writing as soon as the reporting begins, whether that means jotting down a weekly memo or composing small sections of the story. Constantly work on summarizing your story in as few words as possible — be able to tell an editor what the story is about in 25 words or less. If you have worked primarily in print, start thinking visually from the day you start investigating.
themes: writing process; story focus; early writing; visual thinking
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Investigative stories must show the impact of the issue on people. It is almost impossible to make readers care about an injustice without showing the impact of the misdeeds on an individual or group. Journalists must weave human experience throughout stories — the heavier and harder a story is, the more humanity it needs.
themes: human impact; storytelling; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Two rules for graphics in investigative journalism: (1) Each graphic has to make its own point, sharp enough to be conveyed in a short declarative sentence — not 'Patterns of Crime' but 'Sex Criminals Commit Multiple Offenses.' (2) Graphics must stand on their own — they exist to make their own point, not to say in pictures what has already been said in words.
themes: data visualization; graphics; storytelling
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Sarah Cohen's advice on numbers: Readers want people, not numbers. Choose only numbers with meaning for readers — effective writing comes from selection, not compression. Consider charting numbers instead of writing them. Pepper your story with just the right number in just the right place. Recast numbers in simple terms — ratios, rates and rounding can help simplify.
themes: numbers in journalism; data presentation; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Get feedback on a draft before publication. Ask a half-dozen uninitiated readers to look it over and react. You can use people from around the newsroom or people from outside who can be trusted. Odds are they will show you things that could be done better.
themes: peer review; audience testing; quality assurance
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Ken Armstrong's writing advice: If circumstances allow, write a story, not an expose — create suspense, use dialogue, shift perspective. If you reach a bold conclusion, state it boldly — but you must earn the right through your reporting. Put sourcing and how-we-did-it elements in a 'nerd box' — provides authority and transparency without getting in the story's way.
themes: writing technique; transparency; nerd box; storytelling
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: An experienced journalist will start building a chronology from the first week. After each interview, decide what to include. A chronology should include revealing direct quotations and relevant descriptions. Experienced journalists use the chronology as a basic text as they write first drafts to check the original thesis, identify gaps in reporting and look for new angles.
themes: chronology; writing process; information management
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: T. Christian Miller on story structure: 'My favorite organization structure is the chronology. If you can unspool a story in more or less chronological order, it helps the reader understand what's happening, makes clear how one event is linked to another, and makes for easier reading.' Generally write a top to summarize story and main points, a compelling scene, nut graphs, key findings and responses — about 10-15 paragraphs. Then the story unwinds along the timeline.
themes: story structure; chronology; narrative technique
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Brant Houston calls it 'investigation by annotation' — publicly posting documents obtained under FOI and highlighting misleading or outdated information, annotating with comments about what was wrong and what the actual facts were. This approach tells a second, more detailed story that allows readers to follow along in the investigation.
themes: transparency; document annotation; audience engagement; FOI
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Carole Rich's six story structures: inverted pyramid (most to least important), high fives formula (News/Context/Scope/Edge/Impact), Wall Street Journal formula (specific to general with soft lead), hourglass (most important news then chronological), pyramid (lead/foreshadowing/chronological/climax), and sections structure (book-like chapters each with lead/body/kicker).
themes: story structure; narrative technique; writing formats
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Investigative reporting demands the highest standards of accuracy and delivers more ethical dilemmas than almost any other form of journalism. Journalists must investigate fairly, with factual accuracy, with contextual accuracy and without any ethical breaches. As trust in the media has dropped, the need for bulletproof reporting and strong ethical guidelines has only increased.
themes: ethics; accuracy; credibility; trust
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Journalists at the Post and Courier built a database from police and judicial records, newspaper articles and published obituaries, because no single organization had investigated or kept track of all domestic violence cases. All of the information went into a database built by the journalists. This continues a long tradition of searching both hard copy and digital news articles to identify patterns.
themes: database building; secondary sources; pattern identification
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Till Death Do Us Part project example

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The best resource for finding old information still online is archive.org, the Wayback Machine. The site has been methodically collecting and saving webpages for decades, and often documents posted years ago are still obtainable through old versions of an organization's website.
themes: Wayback Machine; archival research; web archives
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Whenever a journalist begins an investigation, get the in-house directory, whether for a business, government agency, church, college or social organization. It is worth using an advanced search function to search for the place of work, the word 'directory' and the file type for a spreadsheet. It is startling how many internal directories are posted in publicly accessible spreadsheets.
themes: directories; source identification; search technique
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: If reporters build their own databases, they should carefully consider what information is needed for the story and do test analyses as the database is being constructed. You don't want to spend hours entering data and then discover you left out a crucial column of information.
themes: database building; schema design; test analysis
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Requesting a breakdown of the costs of obtaining information can help break through an agency's reluctance to release records. If the charges are too high that can be a story in itself. Most open-records laws provide an exemption for a journalist using data in the public interest — so always request a cost waiver.
themes: FOI strategy; cost waivers; data access
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: When considering undercover or surveillance work, journalists should ask: Is it possible to get convincing evidence through documents and interviews instead? What significant points will undercover work or surveillance add to the story in terms of accuracy and credibility? Will the results of the story be important and alarming enough to overshadow the deceptive practices?
themes: covert methods; source acquisition; ethical decision-making
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The attempts to contact a person should be thoroughly documented. A letter can be sent by registered mail, with a return receipt requested. An email can be sent with preferences set to see if the recipient has opened it. Direct messages can be sent via social media. Going to a person's office or home and keeping careful records on the time and date also help show that every effort was made to reach him or her.
themes: source contact; documentation; audit trail
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Journalists can take three steps to lessen the controversy of using anonymous sources: First, try hard to obtain the information from on-the-record human sources and from documents. Second, try hard to persuade the original off-the-record source to go public. Third, when nothing else works, explain fully why the source has requested and been granted anonymity.
themes: anonymous sources; transparency; source management
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Since newsrooms can now be bypassed by leakers on the internet, the investigative reporter is not an arbiter of what should be made public, but a journalist who provides accuracy, context and standards of fairness is ensuring as little harm is done as possible.
themes: verification; leaks; journalistic role
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: The reporter goes back to the original documentation to check out each fact and that it is supported by documents and/or interviews. The reporter checks quotes from the story with transcribed interviews to ascertain accuracy and context. The line-by-line procedure is also meant to spot logical inconsistencies or information gaps.
themes: fact-checking; verification; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Ullmann procedure from Star Tribune

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: When doing line-by-line checking, it is important to check individual facts like names, titles and addresses against third-party sources, not against a reporter's own notes.
themes: verification; triangulation; fact-checking
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Brant Houston, Mark Horvit, IRE
extract: Good reporters highlight every single fact in a story — names, numbers, quotes — and meticulously check each. Check the spelling of every name against a reliable source such as the subject's social media site or biography page on their workplace. author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Make sure you go back in time, too. In some databases, only the current information is available. But if you look at old records, you might find the connection you are looking for. Archival data is often available. Get the company's whole history.
chapter: Following the Money Trail: Investigative Data Journalism
themes: historical records; archival research; corporate history
relevance_rationale: Checking archival and historical records for hidden connections is investigative persistence no AI replaces

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Get financial reports and audits. In most countries, these are public documents. They will contain information about connected companies, transactions with related parties, including owners, and if you know how to read them or can find someone to show you, they will tell you all you need to know about business activities.
chapter: Following the Money Trail: Investigative Data Journalism
themes: financial reports; audits; corporate records; financial literacy
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now help parse and interpret financial reports, though knowing to request them is still human judgment

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Tracking offshores is not a Mission Impossible. You do not know unless you systematically track through available databases. It is not easy and it takes patience and determination, but sometimes you get lucky. Some jurisdictions, including Panama, Cyprus, and the Isle of Man, have opened up websites where records are obtainable for free or cheap.
chapter: Following the Money Trail: Investigative Data Journalism
themes: offshore tracking; company registries; persistence
relevance_rationale: Systematic persistence in tracking offshore structures through multiple databases remains essential human work

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: New knowledge management databases can help to figure out all the places where a particular politician or company has been mentioned across many different databases, automatically generating maps of asset ownership. Machine learning can help separate common names and give more accurate results. Artificial intelligence can use past patterns of criminal structures to find yet undiscovered criminal activity.
chapter: Following the Money Trail: Investigative Data Journalism
themes: machine learning; knowledge management; entity resolution; AI
relevance_rationale: Knowledge management databases and ML for entity separation are now supercharged by LLM-based tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Journalists should carefully consider how a drone can enlighten a story and captivate audiences. Simply because a drone is accessible does not mean it is the best tool for the job, especially when considering how the novelty of technology can be a distraction from the focus of the story. Always ask: Why use a drone for this story?
chapter: Drone Journalism: Storytelling from a New Perspective
themes: drones; tool selection; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Critical evaluation of whether a technology tool serves the story applies equally to drones, VR, and AI

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: While drones can add to a story by revealing a radically different perspective than what's observed on the ground, they can also distort reality for the audience, sometimes in unexpected ways. Drone visuals alone fail to convey the human story. If the only visuals were captured by drone, I would have reconsidered using them given the lack of ground context.
chapter: Drone Journalism: Storytelling from a New Perspective
themes: drones; visual distortion; ground truth; context
relevance_rationale: Warning that aerial visuals can distort reality and fail to convey human stories is a timeless editorial principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Do research before flying, not only to check for legal and spatial information (airspace, nearby airports, et cetera) and ground hazards but also to get a sense of the story you are going to tell with the drone. Look at satellite imagery, photos, 3D buildings on Google Earth. Still, when flying, expect visual surprises and be prepared to take advantage of them.
chapter: Drone Journalism: Storytelling from a New Perspective
themes: drones; pre-flight research; satellite imagery; safety
relevance_rationale: Pre-flight research and story planning before deploying technology is a permanent workflow principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Do not do something in the air with a drone that you would not do the equivalent of when reporting on the ground. For example, if you would not climb a fence to take photographs over the top then do not use a drone to do the same.
chapter: Drone Journalism: Storytelling from a New Perspective
themes: drones; ethics; privacy; journalism ethics
relevance_rationale: Ethical boundaries for newsgathering apply regardless of whether the tool is a drone or an AI

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: It is important to adopt a flight operations manual, including pre-flight and post-flight checklists, and maintenance logs, to ensure safe operations. Responsible and safe drone use will likely lead to more friendly rules and regulations and a bright future for drone journalism.
chapter: Drone Journalism: Storytelling from a New Perspective
themes: drones; safety protocols; operations manual
relevance_rationale: Flight safety protocols and operations manuals are physical-world requirements unaffected by LLMs

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: One way for journalists to find innovative ways to tell stories with drones is to observe how other industries and professions are applying the technology. This kind of interdisciplinary observation and learning is crucial for understanding the capabilities of the technology. Journalists, farmers, and archaeologists are using the same drones and sensors, yet the way each industry leverages the technology differs.
chapter: Drone Journalism: Storytelling from a New Perspective
themes: interdisciplinary; innovation; drones; photogrammetry
relevance_rationale: Cross-disciplinary observation for storytelling innovation applies to AI tools just as it did to drones

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: A basic understanding of the process of programming, the most important html terms and constantly working with them have enriched my job and saved time. I suddenly understand programmers. And perhaps most importantly, our Visual Stories are getting better—from one line of code to the next.
chapter: Visual Storytelling: Show, Not Tell? Show AND Tell
themes: coding literacy; HTML; multimedia production
relevance_rationale: LLMs and no-code tools now reduce the need for journalists to learn HTML/programming for visual stories

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The most effective way to carry the reader through the story was to apply Aristotle's rules for drama: introduction, build tension, (happy) end. We had high reader exit rates with non-linear stories and photo-centric narratives without dramatic structure.
chapter: Visual Storytelling: Show, Not Tell? Show AND Tell
themes: narrative structure; dramaturgy; reader retention
relevance_rationale: Aristotelian narrative structure for reader engagement is a storytelling principle independent of technology

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: We need a planning tool for central topic management. Whether you use Trello, Slack or Desknet—a list of projects that are in process and status management (In which phase is the project? What is the next step? Who is involved?) facilitate the work of the entire editorial staff, but especially that of multimedia journalists who work in multiple areas at the same time.
chapter: Visual Storytelling: Show, Not Tell? Show AND Tell
themes: project management; editorial planning; Trello; Slack
relevance_rationale: Project management tools have evolved significantly; LLM assistants can now help with status tracking and coordination

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The only way to ensure that multimedia storytelling still remains affordable is to use networks. Present Visual Stories on all platforms and in all existing media and channels. Video journalists who accompany authors, photographers who also shoot on research trips, and motion designers who are also thinking about web and TV. Make outstanding research shine on as many channels as possible.
chapter: Visual Storytelling: Show, Not Tell? Show AND Tell
themes: cross-platform; multimedia production; cost management; synergies
relevance_rationale: Cross-platform distribution strategy now must account for AI-generated content and algorithmic distribution

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: When we design Visual Stories, we now structure them for 10 to no more than 15 minutes of user time. Videos no longer than a minute; text segments that fit on a smartphone screen. Do not overload the user with interactive graphics, and keep them simple.
chapter: Visual Storytelling: Show, Not Tell? Show AND Tell
themes: multimedia length; user attention; mobile design
relevance_rationale: Short-form design principles for mobile consumption remain valid regardless of AI tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: When measuring the interaction rates on The New York Times graphics, only about 10-15% of readers click the buttons the creators intend to be clicked—no matter how salient they are or how many arrows point towards them. As Archie Tse put it: nobody is interacting with news graphics.
themes: interactivity; user engagement; data visualisation; design
relevance_rationale: Low interactivity rates (10-15% click) are an empirical finding about user behavior that persists

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Core innovation in VR storytelling is the fact that users are at the centre of the narrative. New kinds of questions have to be answered by journalists: Who is the user within that story? How do you design the presence of the viewer? To what extent does that have an impact on the story? Producers have to think in two dimensions: the role of the users (Participant vs Observant) and the question of influence within the narration.
chapter: Immersive Journalism: How Virtual Reality Impacts Investigative Storytelling
themes: VR; immersive journalism; user design; narrative structure
relevance_rationale: VR storytelling questions about user presence are being reshaped by AI-generated immersive environments

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Within technology-driven projects, we can observe highly differentiated teams that include, amongst others, technologists, computer scientists, architects, designers, and visual journalists. Journalists need to enrich their professional repertoire to meet the specific requirements of digital investigation.
themes: team composition; interdisciplinary; skills development
relevance_rationale: Team composition now needs AI/ML specialists alongside traditional roles; the interdisciplinary principle endures but roles shift
notes: Part II introduction

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: ICIJ created its Data & Research Unit, a multidisciplinary team of data journalists, database engineers and developers, to work on developing technologies to facilitate the sharing and exploration of documents and to perform data analysis. The increased size of leaks highlights the importance of the relationship with and the protection of whistleblowers.
chapter: Uncovering International Stories with Data and Collaboration
themes: team structure; data unit; multidisciplinary; whistleblower protection
relevance_rationale: Data units now need AI/ML expertise alongside database engineering; the multidisciplinary model endures but skill mix changes

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Obtaining hard copies of company records is important—they can hide treasures. You can find all sorts of documents—from loans, to partnership agreements, to court verdicts. These, looked at by too few reporters who are intimidated by their complexity, can yield copies of IDs, passports, or registration records. Always get everything you can. You never know when a piece of information will prove valuable.
chapter: Following the Money Trail: Investigative Data Journalism
themes: company records; hard copies; document collection; thoroughness
relevance_rationale: Obtaining and reading hard-copy company records for hidden details is physical investigative work LLMs cannot do

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The Trinity Mirror data team looks for investigation ideas and data sets that can provide multiple stories for its different local and national publications (such as crime, schools or hospitals), a model which has also been adopted by a number of other organisations.
chapter: Zeroes and Ones: Investigating with Data
themes: scalable stories; local-national; data team; efficiency
relevance_rationale: Scalable investigation models producing multiple stories from shared datasets is an organizational principle that endures

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: If you make a tooltip or rollover assume that no one will ever see it. You cannot expect the reader to interact with your graphic. All critical information must be visible in the very first layer and not hidden behind interactivity.
themes: data visualization; interactivity; user experience; information design
relevance_rationale: Making critical information visible without requiring interaction is a permanent UX design principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Mirko Lorenz describes the workflow for data journalism as: Data Acquisition (scrape it, cleanse it, structure it, and start digging into it), Data Filtering (mine it for nuggets of information), Visualisation (create charts, graphics, or any kind of multimedia), Storytelling (tell the story around it, to vamp up the dry statistics and point out the interesting parts). The further along you move in that process, the more value you create for the reader.
themes: data journalism workflow; data acquisition; visualization; storytelling
relevance_rationale: The data journalism workflow steps remain valid but LLMs now accelerate scraping, cleaning, and initial analysis

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Topics best suited for interactive treatment include 'Big data' stories concerning stories buried in large data sets, non-statistical process visualisations that help the audience understand an issue, and personalised information generators such as calculators. Outliers often make for good stories, as do stories which involve processes or personalisable information.
themes: data visualization; interactivity; story selection; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Topics suited for interactive treatment now include AI-personalized experiences; big data stories are LLM-accelerated

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Whether interactives are used is less dependent on the subject but due in part to the availability of 'newsworthy' data, and in part to newsroom dynamics. Since producing those interactives takes a lot of resources, 'important' stories are more likely to be selected for coverage.
themes: resource allocation; newsroom dynamics; interactivity; editorial decisions
relevance_rationale: Resource constraints for interactives have shifted as AI tools reduce production costs for data visualizations

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Not all newsrooms have dozens of people in an interactive lab; they often rely on the eagerness of individuals willing to invest extra time in side projects, and in learning to work with available tools on their own. This can potentially lead to the journalistic output to be shaped less by what could be considered the best way of representing the data and more by what can be done and is available for free.
themes: newsroom resources; tool constraints; data visualization; skills gap
relevance_rationale: LLM coding assistants now help individual journalists build interactives without full interactive labs

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Data Visualisation is there to clarify—not to simplify. While it is necessary to remove unnecessary information and simplify the message, we must be extremely careful in ensuring that nothing essential is removed. Sometimes, clarity can require additional data to provide the appropriate context—a task extremely well suited for interactivity.
themes: data visualization; clarity; simplification; context
relevance_rationale: Clarify not simplify principle for data visualization is a permanent design epistemology

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The decision to use interactivity has to be deliberate, conscious, and considered. Does this particular piece really need to show more data than we can fit in a static graphic? Interactivity is an extra layer for visualisations that will make it more complex and therefore more time-consuming.
themes: interactivity; design decisions; data visualization; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Deliberate decision-making about when interactivity adds value is a permanent design principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Every visual decision generates meaning. Our eye assigns meaning to every detail that is there. We assign significance to it at least subliminally. So be careful not to generate the wrong cues. Disable interactivity if it does not have a purpose.
chapter: Rules of Thumb: No Arbitrary Interactivity
themes: visual meaning; interactivity; design principles; user cognition
relevance_rationale: Every visual decision generating meaning is a semiotic principle unaffected by AI tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Make the decision who is driving: author or reader. An author-driven approach uses linear storytelling and the author guides the reader through the visualisations. A reader-driven approach gives the reader many more options to interact and explore. The BBC Specials team found that when taking a prescriptive narrative approach, more readers are likely to complete all stages of the interactive.
chapter: Rules of Thumb: Lean Forward or Lean Back
themes: narrative design; author-driven; reader-driven; interactivity
relevance_rationale: Author-driven vs reader-driven narrative design is a structural choice that persists across technologies

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: A graphic should work on different comprehension levels (the 'Bart and Lisa approach'). Bart, with his low-attention span, should be able to get the message without having to move the mouse at all; whereas Lisa can dig way deeper to satisfy her curiosity. The important parts have to be there even without interacting.
chapter: Rules of Thumb: Bart and Lisa Approach
themes: information design; layered storytelling; accessibility; audience levels
relevance_rationale: Designing for multiple comprehension levels is a universal accessibility principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: If we decide to use the latest web technologies there has to be a fallback solution. Rather than showing a message like 'Please go to your desktop computer', it would be good to have a static fallback image that shows as much as possible. Consider all the platforms and devices and offer at least a fallback solution.
chapter: Rules of Thumb: Have a Fallback Solution
themes: responsive design; mobile; fallback; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Fallback solutions for different devices and browsers remain necessary regardless of AI

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Interactive features often remain undiscovered by the audience. If you are deciding to use an interactive graphic, make it clear how this interactivity is supposed to work. Include annotations, instructions, directions, or suggest the interactivity with visual means. Do not assume the reader will intuitively know what to do.
themes: user experience; affordances; interactivity; design guidance
relevance_rationale: Making interactive affordances discoverable is a permanent UX challenge

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Geolocation involves using all available material to confirm the location of an image. Often this involves satellite imagery available on services such as Bing Maps, Google Earth, Google Maps, and Terraserver. On the ground imagery whose location has already been established is also frequently used, including Panoramio and Google Street View.
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: geolocation; satellite imagery; verification; image analysis
relevance_rationale: AI-powered geolocation tools now automate much of the satellite imagery comparison process

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: What must first be established is the original source of the image. Reverse image search functions on Google Image Search or TinEye is the first tool to use in the search for the original source. One pitfall is that dynamically generated pages can lead to misleading results—the date given in search results may be when the webpage was created, not when the image was posted.
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: reverse image search; source verification; provenance; pitfalls
relevance_rationale: AI reverse image search has advanced far beyond Google/TinEye with multimodal LLMs capable of image analysis

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Videos can be harder to find. Unlike photographs, there is currently no reverse search platform for videos, but Amnesty International's YouTube Dataviewer will extract the preview frames from a YouTube video, allowing them to be easily reverse image searched. However, it will not be accurate if the video is edited, shortened, or extended with additional footage.
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: video verification; YouTube; reverse image search; Amnesty International
relevance_rationale: AI video analysis and reverse video search tools now exist, making the no-reverse-search-for-video limitation obsolete

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: If you are able to find the original source of the image, look at whatever was posted along with it. An Instagram image or Facebook post might have the location listed in the description or discussed in the comments. The next step would be to look at the profile it was posted on. Does it usually post about one location? Is the person who posted it talking about their movements?
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: social media analysis; source investigation; geolocation; context clues
relevance_rationale: Checking metadata, descriptions, and comments from original social media posts remains essential verification

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Begin first by identifying large features and distinct landmarks. These two larger features take you to the same location, but next the video should be re-examined to identify other smaller features. The position of the curb, the location of utility poles (visible by the shadows they cast), the position of buildings and trees. While these features would not have led to the location, they allowed confirmation of the location and precise position of the camera.
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: geolocation; landmark identification; satellite imagery; verification technique
relevance_rationale: AI can now assist with landmark identification and geolocation feature matching, though human judgment still validates

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Understanding how information is organised and shared online can also be useful. A bus visible in an image would suggest the location is on a bus route. In many countries bus routes and tramlines are available online and often include maps. On Google Earth and Yandex Maps tram and bus routes are sometimes visible, which can dramatically reduce the number of areas needing to be looked at.
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: geolocation; open data; transport infrastructure; online databases
relevance_rationale: AI can now identify bus routes, street features, and infrastructure from images, augmenting human geolocation skills

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The only way to develop geolocation skills is simply just to do it. When I began verifying images, I found it extremely helpful to write out the steps I had taken to verify the image. This allowed me to really understand the process in my mind, and to develop my own approaches. It also pays to revisit old cases, reviewing images you may have looked at months or years ago, as often you will spot new details.
chapter: Visual Analysis: Verification via Geolocation and Photographs
themes: skill development; geolocation; practice; reflective learning
relevance_rationale: Learning by doing and documenting verification steps is a skill-building approach independent of tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Investigative journalists adopt a distinctly quasi-scientific approach, setting out to prove or disprove a hypothesis through triangulation of information from various sources. Traditional methods of authentication include assessing the reliability of individual sources and cross-checking with two or more independent sources to corroborate facts.
chapter: eyeWitness to Atrocities: Verifying Images with Metadata
themes: triangulation; hypothesis testing; source reliability; corroboration
relevance_rationale: Quasi-scientific hypothesis testing through triangulation is a fundamental verification epistemology

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: When images are shared via Facebook, Twitter, YouTube, or WhatsApp, key information about the source and context are often stripped or misattributed. Smartphone apps designed to collect key metadata information such as time, date and location at the point of capture include eyeWitness to Atrocities, Truepic, Whistler, the Guardian Project's CameraV and Proofmode.
chapter: eyeWitness to Atrocities: Verifying Images with Metadata
themes: metadata; image verification; social media stripping; evidence tools
relevance_rationale: Metadata collection apps must now contend with AI-generated synthetic media that can fabricate metadata

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Courts require images to be reliable. In assessing reliability, courts consider whether images are original and unedited from the source, the chain of custody record, and information about the time, date and location at the point of capture. The chain of custody means information to establish the integrity of the image by tracing where it has been, who has had access and how susceptible to tampering it has been.
themes: chain of custody; evidence standards; legal admissibility; metadata
relevance_rationale: Legal chain-of-custody requirements for court evidence are institutional standards that persist

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Technology tools are best deployed through collaborative, reciprocal partnerships where assumptions and design are open to consultative re-evaluation. Technology tools designed for human rights investigations are not suited to all contexts. Investigators should consider physical and digital security risks, availability of equipment, level of technology skills, access to Wi-Fi and cell tower coverage.
chapter: eyeWitness to Atrocities: Challenges to Consider
themes: technology deployment; security; partnerships; context assessment
relevance_rationale: Collaborative technology deployment with consultative partnerships is an organizational principle that persists

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Human rights investigators should adhere to the 'do no harm' principle, which is the ethical obligation not to jeopardise the safety of victims and witnesses. The person collecting the data, the victims and bystanders appearing in images should have the opportunity to consent to use of the data where possible and appropriate.
chapter: eyeWitness to Atrocities: Challenges to Consider
themes: do no harm; informed consent; ethics; source protection
relevance_rationale: Do-no-harm principle for data collection protecting victims and witnesses is a permanent ethical obligation

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: A more targeted approach to data collection like crowdseeding may ameliorate concerns about anonymous, inexperienced documenters, as it allows for selection and training of the documenters. It is important that technology tools for human rights investigations complement, rather than replace, other documentation and verification methods.
chapter: eyeWitness to Atrocities: Challenges to Consider
themes: crowdseeding; crowdsourcing; training; complementary methods
relevance_rationale: Targeted crowdseeding with trained documenters addresses quality concerns that persist regardless of AI

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: There is a vast gulf between facts and truth, and history is strewn with factual images that have been used to tell lies. Photography's relationship with truth is little more than 'belief' imposed by the viewer. The photographer's own methodology, personal ethics and politics all lead to the actual act of framing—the taking of the photograph—and then a second act of framing occurs in how publications use and contextualise a particular image.
chapter: A Matter of Perspective: Truth, Evidence and the Role of Photography
themes: photography; truth; framing; editorial context; subjectivity
relevance_rationale: AI-generated images now make the gap between facts and truth even wider; photography truth claims need AI-era update

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Photography can assist in helping us to understand on an emotional level, but it can't explain. In the digital arena one cannot with any certainty look at a photograph and say, 'So that is how it was.' A photograph becomes a starting point in a wider dialogue rather than an end, a static piece of evidence.
chapter: A Matter of Perspective: Truth, Evidence and the Role of Photography
themes: photography; evidence; digital age; truth claims; limitations
relevance_rationale: AI-generated imagery makes the starting-point-not-proof principle even more critical and requires new verification methods

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: A forensic approach builds up a more complete sense of depth of coverage and evidence. The similarities between the role that the photograph plays in journalistic discourse and in the courtroom is striking: in both arenas the photograph serves to help the audience grasp complex issues by simplifying them and by giving a sense of place and orientation that textual descriptions alone cannot deliver.
chapter: A Matter of Perspective: Truth, Evidence and the Role of Photography
themes: forensic photography; evidence; visual journalism; courtroom parallels
relevance_rationale: Forensic approach to building evidence through multiple sources is a permanent investigative principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Three-dimensional scanning creates an enormous data set of a particular environment which can be recreated as a three-dimensional model. This is 'pure documentary' where a data set is collected free from the hindrance of any bias or perspective, free from the limitations of a two-dimensional camera. Everything is collected, and it is only later that this moment is recreated so that it can be interrogated from multiple perspectives.
chapter: A Matter of Perspective: Truth, Evidence and the Role of Photography
themes: 3D scanning; forensic architecture; evidence collection; digital documentation
relevance_rationale: 3D scanning as pure documentary now competes with AI-generated 3D models; bias-free claims need reassessment

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Step 1 Source: Journalists should always prefer sources of particularly high credibility such as news agencies and known professional photographers. If the origin of a photograph cannot be clarified, it should not be spread. Social networking sites should be regarded as a toxic environment for image research, as captions are often deliberately or mistakenly falsified.
chapter: The Context Verification of Digital Photographs
themes: source assessment; credibility; social media; image verification
relevance_rationale: Source credibility hierarchy endures but now must account for AI-generated content from seemingly credible sources

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Step 2 Seriality: When assessing the authenticity of a possibly faked photograph, it is a good strategy to ask the source for further material from the same series. Additional aspects of the situation become visible which allow a better assessment. If the source cannot or will not provide further material, the credibility of the justification is a further hint for authenticity.
chapter: The Context Verification of Digital Photographs
themes: photo series; authenticity; source cooperation; verification technique
relevance_rationale: Requesting photo series for authentication is complicated by AI ability to generate consistent synthetic series

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Step 3 Quality of Data: Digital manipulations are much more difficult to apply to RAW files than to JPEGs. Editorial teams should request access to any RAW file related to a photograph of questionable authenticity. The accompanying metadata should be included in the assessment—GPS coordinates, focal length, shutter speed should be compared with the caption. Metadata readers available only online should be avoided since sensitive image material should not be uploaded without further need.
chapter: The Context Verification of Digital Photographs
themes: RAW files; metadata; EXIF data; image forensics; data quality
relevance_rationale: RAW file analysis now must consider AI-generated RAW files; metadata verification needs AI-era forensic tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Step 4 External Validation: If the source is credible, the photo is part of a larger series, and data is high quality, fair journalistic care has been provided. If not, the search radius should extend to the Web. Use monitoring pages such as snopes.com to search for keywords or URLs. Step 5 Conspicuous Features: Examine attire for cultural context, religious traditions, or climatic conditions. Search for anything containing writing—road signs, number plates, advertisements. Even power sockets or floor coverings can indicate which country.
chapter: The Context Verification of Digital Photographs
themes: external validation; visual clues; cultural indicators; debunking
relevance_rationale: External validation via web search now must include AI content detection tools alongside traditional fact-check sites

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Step 6 Similar Pictures: Use image search images.google.com to insert the URL or upload the image directly to find it elsewhere. The context published can be helpful in assessing authenticity. Step 7 Plausibility: Compare visible phenomena such as weather with databases. Wolframalpha.com makes it possible to search historical weather data. If there is sunshine in a photo and the database indicates heavy rain, you might be confronted with a fake.
chapter: The Context Verification of Digital Photographs
themes: reverse image search; plausibility check; weather data; verification checklist
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search now enhanced by AI multimodal models; plausibility checks must account for deepfakes

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: A fuzzy or perceptual hash is a fingerprint of a file derived from various features from its content. Unlike cryptographic hash functions, it can efficiently help identify images that contain a high percentage of similarities. An investigator can then take the files with the highest percentage of similarities and manually review those individual images. Tools like SSDeep and pHash compute these fuzzy hashes.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: perceptual hashing; image forensics; duplicate detection; SSDeep
relevance_rationale: Perceptual hashing is now supplemented by AI-based image similarity and deepfake detection tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Metadata can contain relevant footprints: GPS coordinates, camera model, serial number, timestamps, lens focal length, whether flash was used. Photo editing software often adds metadata about manipulation when saving. It is suggested to examine all tags and their contents—a correlation between metadata and other evidence could quickly close the investigation. Since metadata are easy to read, write and delete, it is not possible to completely rely on them.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: metadata analysis; EXIF; image forensics; GPS; camera fingerprinting
relevance_rationale: Metadata forensics endures but AI can now fabricate or strip metadata; new AI detection tools are needed

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: In rare cases, if an image is edited with software that saves alteration to the image but misses updating the preview inside metadata, by extracting the preview thumbnail you can spot the difference and discover the original image before manipulation.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: thumbnail forensics; metadata; image manipulation detection
relevance_rationale: Thumbnail extraction trick is now less reliable as AI tools can generate consistent thumbnails; new forensic methods needed

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Error Level Analysis (ELA) is one of the dominant image tampering localisation methods. It is based on the idea that each re-saving of a JPEG file compresses it with lower quality. An area that was edited would have a different compression level, so under ELA it would be brighter than other areas. Each technique aims at detecting different tampering traces; there is no silver bullet, and not all algorithms should be expected to work on all images. It is suggested to use different algorithms and correlate the traces found.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: error level analysis; image forensics; JPEG compression; tampering detection
relevance_rationale: Error Level Analysis is less reliable against AI-generated images which have uniform compression; new AI forensic tools needed

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Most social media platforms tend to strip metadata from uploaded multimedia to protect users' privacy. Sometimes images from social networks or messaging applications are also resized or cropped. Investigation on images coming from social media should take care of this sanitisation and focus on forensic techniques not based on metadata.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: social media; metadata stripping; image forensics; platform limitations
relevance_rationale: Social media metadata stripping as a verification challenge persists and applies equally to AI-manipulated content

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: When evaluating an image forensic tool it should meet requirements: easy integration in verification workflows, easy to use with little training, graphical interface and visual analysis, summary and exhaustive results with clear indicators, support metadata extraction and duplicate detection, access to source code or explanation of techniques used—never trust 'black box' tools as an investigator should understand the methods used.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: tool evaluation; image forensics; transparency; workflow integration
relevance_rationale: Image forensic tool requirements now must include AI-generated content detection capabilities

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Image forensics is not a matter of tools only, it is also about processes and people. Workflows should take care of image validation, and people should be trained to understand the problem of image authenticity and to know how much trust to put in digital media.
chapter: Photo Manipulation: Software to Unmask Tampering
themes: image forensics; training; workflow design; digital literacy
relevance_rationale: Image forensics processes and training now must incorporate AI-generated content awareness and detection workflows

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Fact-checking is the most popular method of debunking and an important tool for protecting the truth. Common techniques include checking the source (or even double-checking), checking geolocation, and verification of the information. Tools include Google for investigating origins of rumours, and tools for checking pictures to authenticate user-generated photos. The most common methods are verifying information about a website and its owner, geolocation verification, using metadata, and asking witnesses.
chapter: Fact-Checking as Defence Against Propaganda in the Digital Age
themes: fact-checking; debunking; source verification; geolocation; propaganda
relevance_rationale: Fact-checking tools and techniques now include AI-powered claim detection and automated verification assistants

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Open source research groups differ from other investigators in their decentralized nature with contributors flung across the world and no central newsroom. They use materials openly available to anyone with an internet connection. While some use non-digital research, the vast majority is built off of open source materials, which bring challenges in verification and contextualization.
chapter: Crowdsourced and Patriotic Digital Forensics in the Ukrainian Conflict
themes: open source intelligence; crowdsourcing; decentralized research; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Open source research methods now include AI-assisted analysis of satellite imagery and social media at scale

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: A frequent type of investigation will be to uncover the social network profiles of soldiers and cross-reference the materials on these pages to establish which military units have had active participation. Cross-referencing photographs and videos from citizens, soldiers, media outlets and government sources provides a timeline tracking equipment from one country to another and to major battles.
chapter: Crowdsourced and Patriotic Digital Forensics in the Ukrainian Conflict
themes: social media analysis; cross-referencing; OSINT; military tracking
relevance_rationale: Social media cross-referencing of military units is now augmented by AI facial recognition and geolocation tools

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Crowdsourcing research involves a call for readers to share the burden of data collection and verification, in hopes of increasing the quantity and quality of analysis for large amounts of information. Bellingcat's Vehicle Tracking Project used the free platform Check to verify photographs and videos, where volunteers gathered over 350 pieces of evidence with exact times and locations established for most materials.
chapter: Crowdsourced and Patriotic Digital Forensics in the Ukrainian Conflict
themes: crowdsourced verification; volunteer research; evidence gathering; Bellingcat
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourced verification can now be augmented by AI pre-processing to reduce human labor in large dataset analysis

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Cooperating with major media outlets is beneficial in increasing the quality of the investigation and generating exposure. If you work together with a large publication, all Russian publications will write about your work. But there are issues: staffs at these outlets often have hard deadlines and are not able to wait while you gather facts. Independent publication offers more flexibility with updating an investigation with newly discovered facts.
chapter: Crowdsourced and Patriotic Digital Forensics in the Ukrainian Conflict
themes: media partnerships; publication strategy; editorial flexibility; amplification
relevance_rationale: Benefits and tensions of cooperating with major media outlets for exposure and credibility persist

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Digital research can be just as trustworthy and appealing to audiences as shoe leather journalism if it is properly presented and contextualized. With user-generated content and internet access expanding globally, the practices of open source research and verification will only become more important over time.
chapter: Crowdsourced and Patriotic Digital Forensics in the Ukrainian Conflict
themes: open source intelligence; credibility; digital research; contextualization
relevance_rationale: Digital open-source research trustworthiness through proper presentation and contextualization is a permanent principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The internet gave us an opportunity to create digital scrapbooks, where each individual story can be told in wider context, allowing us to follow characters, events and trends over a period of time. Digital formats now allow us to show how stories relate to each other; they empower journalists to place intimate, engaging, on-the-ground storytelling into a much wider context of crises and events.
themes: digital storytelling; context; longitudinal reporting; narrative structure
relevance_rationale: Digital storytelling formats now include AI-assisted personalization and dynamic narrative generation

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Coda's operational formula: deploy like everyone else after a crisis, but go in with a long-lens view and commit to a story for an extended period. The core editorial team defines trends or 'currents' that run through the crisis and works with local and international journalists. Each individual piece of content, regardless of format, is placed in one of the currents allowing the audience to follow a narrative line and see wider context.
themes: crisis journalism; thematic curation; longform; narrative architecture
relevance_rationale: Long-term editorial commitment to crisis coverage with local correspondents is an organizational model independent of AI

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: When selected carefully, currents could greatly aid the process of journalistic discovery. Thematic rather than chronological curation of the content did not just allow following trends and individual characters as stories developed, but crucially generated stories that had an infinitely longer shelf life than those reported in reaction to news events.
themes: thematic analysis; editorial framing; story longevity; discovery
relevance_rationale: Thematic curation enabling discovery of cross-cutting patterns is an editorial strategy that persists

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The president deliberately changed the measurement category without indication—he chose statistics that fit his narrative while completely breaking with previously established use of statistical comparisons. This inconsistent use of data reveals a systematic use of numbers that favour a certain agenda. Politicians have leaned heavily on statistics to buttress their authority, often stretching evidence too far, interpreting data too loosely.
chapter: Truth Corrupted: The Role of Fact-Based Journalism in a Post-Truth Society
themes: statistical manipulation; data literacy; fact-checking; political claims
relevance_rationale: Detecting deliberate statistical manipulation by officials requires human domain knowledge and skepticism

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Data sources—be it that they are provided by governmental bodies, non-governmental organisations or whistleblowers—allow citizens and journalists to compare claims and statements with actual underlying data, ultimately holding elites and governments accountable. As Segnini puts it: 'Data can help a reporter fill in the missing pieces, and it also allows journalists to expose a universe beyond the one sources are willing to share.'
chapter: Truth Corrupted: The Role of Fact-Based Journalism in a Post-Truth Society
themes: data journalism; accountability; evidence-based reporting; post-truth
relevance_rationale: Data enabling citizens and journalists to compare claims with evidence for accountability is a permanent democratic function

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: If a data reporter doesn't go out into the field and report, they are only doing a third of their job. Data journalism could greatly benefit from enhancing stories and findings drawn from quantitative data with a qualitative narrative—appending personal accounts with rows of data.
