Giving these numbers voices and faces would make data journalism more tangible and relatable.
chapter: Truth Corrupted: The Role of Fact-Based Journalism in a Post-Truth Society
themes: data journalism; field reporting; qualitative narrative; mixed methods
relevance_rationale: Field reporting to add qualitative narrative and human voices to data journalism is a permanent editorial principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Data journalism needs to fulfil two tasks: First, educating readers by explaining calculations, modelling, and statistical indicators—creating transparency regarding employed methods while dispelling fears of over-complicated stories. Data journalists need to become educators. Secondly, developing appealing and intelligible storytelling formats. A more visual journalism can create empathy and lead to more inclusivity in contrast to fabricating exclusivity through the use of jargon.
chapter: Truth Corrupted: The Role of Fact-Based Journalism in a Post-Truth Society
themes: data literacy; transparency; storytelling; education; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Data journalists as educators who explain methods and create accessible storytelling is a permanent role

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: We need to distinguish predictive and descriptive data journalism. Forecasts based on advanced statistical modelling and likelihoods have a margin of error. After failing predictions, negative repercussions harm not only predictive data journalism but data journalism in general. Descriptive, explanatory and analytical data-driven journalism applies more basic statistical calculations and should not be conflated with predictive models.
chapter: Truth Corrupted: The Role of Fact-Based Journalism in a Post-Truth Society
themes: predictive journalism; descriptive journalism; statistical methods; credibility
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing predictive from descriptive data journalism to protect credibility is a permanent epistemological concern

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Assume you are being watched. Do not put things in writing, do not do certain things if you do not want them to come out afterwards. You have to assume that everything you do is being recorded or traced. A change of practice in managing digital communications is required in response—at both the personal and professional levels.
chapter: Just Assume You Are Being Watched
themes: operational security; source protection; risk assessment; surveillance awareness
relevance_rationale: Assume-you-are-watched principle endures but threat model now includes AI-powered metadata analysis and LLM inference attacks

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Going back to analogue basics: the only safe thing is face-to-face contact, brown envelopes, meetings in parks. Journalists revert to traditional methods—contract phones thrown away weekly, meeting sources in pubs, writing and hiding physical notes. Investigative reporters drove hours for 15-minute conversations rather than use digital communication.
themes: source protection; analogue methods; operational security; confidential sources
relevance_rationale: Analogue tradecraft remains valid but AI surveillance capabilities mean even analogue methods need updated threat assessment

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Encryption was the default requirement of ICIJ during the Panama Papers investigation which saw 400 journalists from 80 countries working collaboratively for 12 months. All members were required to use two-factor authentication and encryption. After the collaboration using a secure interactive platform (Global I-Hub), over 60% of surveyed journalists reported the encryption requirements were easy to use.
themes: encryption; collaboration infrastructure; Panama Papers; digital security training; secure platforms
relevance_rationale: Encryption as default for cross-border collaboration endures but secure infrastructure must now account for AI-era threats

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: The more layers you can put between you and the source the better, and a lot of that is time. If someone gives you some really hot information, the temptation is to publish right away, but that is also when your source is potentially at most risk. Stretching the timeline between contact with a source and publication can disguise connections and minimise the chance a source will be caught.
themes: source protection; publication timing; risk mitigation; operational security
relevance_rationale: Adding time layers between source contact and publication to protect sources is a permanent operational security principle

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: There is a new trend: journalists are beginning to train their sources in digital security to help them ensure their anonymity. If we want journalism to survive and flourish in the 21st century, there is no other option than give our reporters, and sources, the tools necessary to do their jobs. Even the best training cannot keep up with global intelligence services, but encryption may buy time and keep other hostile actors at bay.
chapter: Journalists Need Training in Digital Security, But so Do Their Sources
themes: source training; digital security; encryption; capacity building; source protection
relevance_rationale: Training sources in digital security now must include AI-era threats like LLM data leakage and metadata inference

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: ICIJ essentially becomes the source in global investigations: by taking all the responsibility of source protection and putting the responsibility on each organisation to publish according to their own laws. International news organisations use secure drop boxes—AfriLeaks, Mexicoleaks, Sourcesure, Balkanleaks—to securely receive digital information from confidential sources and whistleblowers.
themes: source protection; intermediaries; secure dropboxes; collaboration infrastructure; whistleblowers
relevance_rationale: ICIJ model of outsourcing source protection and secure dropbox infrastructure is a permanent organizational pattern

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Digital security expertise gives investigative teams an edge in journalism based on confidential sources. Very few journalists use encryption and know how to use it—it is not in their toolbox and that is a major problem. When you come into contact with sources you get confronted with serious questions: how do you protect this source, will you store information on the company server, what level of encryption do you use?
themes: digital security literacy; encryption; source protection; competitive advantage; newsroom capability
relevance_rationale: Digital security as competitive edge endures but encryption toolbox must now address AI-powered deanonymization risks

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Even face-to-face meetings can be compromised by the presence of geolocatable mobile devices and security cameras. The risk of exposure travels with journalists heading to face-to-face meetings if the route they take is subject to security camera surveillance, or they travel with traceable mobile devices that deliver geolocation data.
themes: operational security; geolocation risk; analogue methods; surveillance; source protection
relevance_rationale: Geolocation risks from devices and cameras now amplified by AI-powered facial recognition and movement pattern analysis

author: Oliver Hahn, Florian Stalph (eds.)
extract: Seven tips for journalists: do not grow unnecessarily paranoid—act smarter and get properly equipped; be aware face-to-face meetings can be compromised by geolocatable devices; assume you are being watched; encrypt your data; be aware that using Tor, PGP and other encryption can red-flag digital communications making you a bigger target; recognise your ethical responsibility to protect and train sources in digital safety; recognise it may no longer be possible to guarantee protection for confidential sources.
chapter: Seven Tips for Journalists to Help Make Communications with Sources and Whistleblowers More Secure
themes: operational security checklist; encryption; source protection; risk awareness; digital safety
relevance_rationale: Seven security tips are valid but need updating for AI surveillance capabilities including pattern analysis and encryption red-flagging

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data can no longer be taken for granted, and nor can data journalism. Data does not just provide neutral and straightforward representations of the world, but is rather entangled with politics and culture, money and power.
themes: critical data practice; data politics; epistemology
relevance_rationale: Critical awareness that data is entangled with politics and power becomes even more important when LLMs can generate and manipulate data at scale.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data journalism can be viewed not just in terms of how things are represented, but in terms of how it organizes relations—such that it is not just a matter of producing data stories but also attending to who and what these stories bring together, including audiences, sources, methods, institutions and social media platforms.
themes: relational thinking; audience; stakeholders
relevance_rationale: Relational thinking about who data stories bring together remains essential regardless of tooling.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: How can data journalism projects tell stories both with and about data including the various actors, processes, institutions, infrastructures and forms of knowledge through which data is made?
chapter: Introduction: Twelve Challenges for Critical Data Practice
themes: critical data practice; transparency; data provenance
relevance_rationale: Telling stories about how data is made is a critical literacy that persists in the LLM era.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: How can data journalism projects tell stories about big issues at scale while also affirming the provisionality and acknowledging the models, assumptions and uncertainty involved in the production of numbers?
chapter: Introduction: Twelve Challenges for Critical Data Practice
themes: uncertainty; methodology transparency; scale
relevance_rationale: Communicating uncertainty and model assumptions is even more important with AI-generated analysis.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: How can data journalism projects cultivate their own ways of making things intelligible, meaningful and relatable through data, without simply uncritically advancing the ways of knowing baked into data from dominant institutions, infrastructures and practices?
chapter: Introduction: Twelve Challenges for Critical Data Practice
themes: critical data practice; institutional power; epistemic independence
relevance_rationale: Epistemic independence from dominant institutional framings remains vital when LLMs are trained on those same framings.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: As a data-driven investigation, we used data to get from pattern to story. The choice of crops and countries was made based on a previous data analysis of 68 million records from the United Nations' World Trade Database.
themes: pattern detection; data-driven inquiry; international databases
relevance_rationale: Pattern-to-story approach endures but LLMs can now assist with exploring large trade databases far more quickly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We compiled the land use map of the country and overlaid the surface of palm plantations with protected areas. We found that 7,000 palm oil hectares were illegally planted in protected areas.
themes: geospatial analysis; overlay analysis; environmental data
relevance_rationale: Geospatial overlay analysis endures as a method but LLMs and AI tools can now automate parts of the spatial analysis workflow.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We created a database of all the sugar mills in the country. We dived into the local company registry to find out the owners and directors. Next we used public business records to link these individuals and entities with offshore companies.
themes: corporate registries; entity linking; offshore finance
relevance_rationale: Entity linking across corporate registries endures but LLMs can now accelerate name matching and network discovery.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: This work was undertaken not only by journalists, but also by editors, photographers, designers and developers who participated in the development and production process to develop an integrated web product.
themes: cross-functional teams; web production; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Cross-functional team collaboration for integrated web products remains essential regardless of AI tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: I looked for ways to measure segregation in an academic sense and base my reporting from there. I interviewed an associate professor of sociology who showed me the Multigroup Entropy Index (or Theil Index), a statistical measure that determines the spatial distribution of multiple racial groups simultaneously.
chapter: 2. Repurposing Census Data to Measure Segregation in the United States
themes: academic methods; statistical measures; expert consultation
relevance_rationale: Consulting domain experts and grounding analysis in academic methods remains essential for rigorous data journalism.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: There is no single measure that will prove or not prove segregation, but the measures can work together to explain how a community is comprised. I read a lot of research on census demographics and tried to mirror my categories to existing literature on the topic.
chapter: 2. Repurposing Census Data to Measure Segregation in the United States
themes: measurement validity; academic grounding; multiple measures
relevance_rationale: Using multiple complementary measures and grounding in existing literature remains fundamental to valid analysis.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: I found JavaScript and the node.js ecosystem to provide a rich set of tools to work with data and then display it on the web. One challenge was that I had to write several of my analysis functions by hand, but in return I was able to understand every step of my analysis and use the same functions on the web.
chapter: 2. Repurposing Census Data to Measure Segregation in the United States
themes: JavaScript; reproducibility; web publishing
relevance_rationale: Writing custom analysis functions in JavaScript endures in principle but LLMs now generate and debug such code rapidly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The Botanical Garden refused to give us the full data even after we submitted multiple freedom of information requests. Our solution? Reach out to other organizations that had helped the Botanical Garden collect the data. They gave us the whole data set in no time.
chapter: 3. Multiplying Memories while Discovering Trees in Bogota
themes: FOI; alternative data sources; persistence
relevance_rationale: Creative persistence in obtaining data through alternative sources when FOI fails is a timeless investigative skill.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We involved citizens by inviting them to help us build a collaborative tree catalogue and to share their own stories about the trees we had mapped. We shared a WhatsApp number where people could send voice notes with their stories about trees.
chapter: 3. Multiplying Memories while Discovering Trees in Bogota
themes: crowdsourcing; citizen engagement; participatory journalism
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing and citizen engagement through participatory methods remain valuable community journalism practices.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Taking a data-driven approach to a story doesn't need to be technical or expensive. Sometimes simply following and counting occurrences of an event over time tells you a lot about the scale of a problem. As long as your data-gathering methodology remains consistent, there are many stories you can tell using data.
chapter: 4. Behind the Numbers: Home Demolitions in Occupied East Jerusalem
themes: low-tech methods; consistency; longitudinal tracking
relevance_rationale: Low-tech consistent data-gathering methodology for longitudinal tracking remains valid and accessible.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The most important thing is to thoroughly storyboard exactly what data you need before sending any reporters out into the field.
chapter: 4. Behind the Numbers: Home Demolitions in Occupied East Jerusalem
themes: planning; storyboarding; project management
relevance_rationale: Storyboarding and planning data needs before fieldwork is a timeless project management practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We cross-checked these with news reports and other local information about home demolitions. We then selected a case to highlight each month, as a way of showing different facets of the Israeli policy.
chapter: 4. Behind the Numbers: Home Demolitions in Occupied East Jerusalem
themes: cross-checking; case selection; verification
relevance_rationale: Cross-checking data against news reports and selecting representative cases for verification remain essential.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data-driven reporting need not end when the editor clicks publish. Combining data journalism with online and offline community engagement can lead to positive policy and social change.
chapter: 5. Mapping Crash Incidents to Advocate for Road Safety in the Philippines
themes: impact; community engagement; policy change
relevance_rationale: Combining data journalism with community engagement for policy impact is a purpose-driven practice that transcends tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: With no comprehensive national database on road crash numbers, we knocked on doors and gathered data from over a dozen national and local government units. Data acquired from these repositories are not standardized. A significant part of the work involved cleaning the data for analysis.
chapter: 5. Mapping Crash Incidents to Advocate for Road Safety in the Philippines
themes: data acquisition; government data; data cleaning; standardization
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning from non-standardized government sources endures but LLMs can now accelerate normalization tasks.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Using the open-source data-cleaning application OpenRefine, we were able to come up with a normalized database of information acquired from the different government agencies.
chapter: 5. Mapping Crash Incidents to Advocate for Road Safety in the Philippines
themes: OpenRefine; data normalization; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine remains useful but LLMs can now handle many data normalization and cleaning tasks more flexibly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We started by submitting freedom of information requests and collecting data from trusted NGOs that were extracting data from various sources. The first challenge was that it was not easy to get open data through FOI requests. Sometimes it took two weeks, sometimes four months.
themes: FOI; NGO data; data access challenges
relevance_rationale: FOI requests and persistence in obtaining data from reluctant institutions remain core investigative skills.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We ended up publishing monthly worker deaths by comparing official data gathered through FOI requests with the data that we collected through our own monitoring of these cases.
themes: triangulation; media monitoring; official data comparison
relevance_rationale: Triangulating official data against independently collected monitoring data is a timeless verification practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data journalism needn't be solely based on existing data sets. There is a great case for making your own data. There is a wealth of information in data that is not routinely published or in some cases not even collected. In building your own data set you create a unique set of information with which to explore your story.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: data creation; original data; investigative advantage
relevance_rationale: Creating original datasets where none exist remains a distinctive and essential data journalism practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The basis for using data in journalism is structured thinking. In order to use data to its full potential, at the outset of a project the journalist needs to think structurally: What is the story I want to be able to tell and what do I need to be able to tell it?
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: structured thinking; planning; story design
relevance_rationale: Structured thinking about what story you want to tell and what data you need is foundational regardless of tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The first step to making or gathering data for your analysis is assessing what is required and if it can be obtained. At the outset of any project it's worth making a story memo which sketches out what you expect the story will attempt to tell, where you think the data is, how long it will take to find it and where the potential pitfalls are.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: story memo; planning; feasibility assessment
relevance_rationale: Writing a story memo to assess feasibility and plan data acquisition is a timeless project management practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Research will help you clarify what exists, so comb through all of the sources of information that refer to the issue of interest and talk to academics, researchers and statisticians who gather or work with the data. This will help you identify shortcomings and possible pitfalls in using the data.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: source identification; expert consultation; due diligence
relevance_rationale: Consulting experts and combing existing literature to identify data shortcomings remains essential due diligence.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: When building a data set we need to consider if the source and method we're using to collect the information is the most accurate and complete possible. Examine the information from multiple angles and don't torture the data to get it to say something that is not a fair reflection of the reality. Be prepared to be transparent about the sourcing, analysis and limitations of the data.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: ethics; transparency; data limitations; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Transparency about sourcing, analysis limitations, and avoiding data torture are timeless ethical principles.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Get the data. This may be done manually through data entry into a spreadsheet, transforming information locked in PDFs into structured data, procuring documents through a human source or the Freedom of Information Act, programming to scrape data from web pages or automating data capture through an API. Don't sacrifice simplicity for the sake of it. Seek to find the most straightforward way of getting the information into a data set you can analyze.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: data acquisition; scraping; FOI; simplicity
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition methods like scraping and PDF extraction endure but LLMs now dramatically simplify these tasks.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Think of what the data set should look like at the end of the process. Consider the columns or variables you would want to be able to analyze. Look for inspiration in the methodology and structure underlying other similar data sets. Cast the net wide to begin with, then pare it back by assessing what you need for the story.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: data structure; schema design; methodology
relevance_rationale: Schema design thinking—envisioning the final dataset structure and variables—remains essential planning work.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Keep notes of the sources you have used to create your data set and always keep a copy of the original documents. Write up a methodology and a data dictionary to keep track of your sources, how the data has been processed and what each column contains. Assume nothing and check all your findings with further reporting.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: documentation; data dictionary; methodology; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Keeping source documentation, data dictionaries, and methodology notes is even more important with AI-assisted workflows.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We brainstormed what we wanted to know—name, age and date of the incident were all things we definitely wanted to record. We discussed what we thought we already knew about knife crime—it was mostly male with a disproportionate number of Black male victims. To check our assumptions we added columns for sex and ethnicity.
chapter: 7. Building Your Own Data Set: Documenting Knife Crime in the United Kingdom
themes: variable selection; assumption testing; schema design
relevance_rationale: Testing assumptions by adding variables for sex and ethnicity is a timeless research design practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: When you are faced with an untroubled surface, where no hint of moral tension is to be found, but still something lurks, then attune yourself to numbers and algorithms in situ; work out how to think with a number that catches at you. Find ways to dilate the peepholes that number names cover. Recognize that numbers have pre-established natures and special abilities that emerge in encounter; that the actualities of series of practices by which they come to be, matter.
chapter: 8. Narrating a Number and Staying With the Trouble of Value
themes: number provenance; critical numeracy; sociology of quantification
relevance_rationale: Critical numeracy—interrogating the provenance and politics of numbers—becomes more important when LLMs generate statistics.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: There are two basic speaking positions in mixing narratives and visuals. One might proceed as if the visual is embedded within the narrative, speaking to the visual which represents something in the story. Or you can proceed as if the narrative is embedded in the visual, speaking from within diagram. Switching between these speaking positions within a single piece is perhaps the most effective strategy.
chapter: 8. Narrating a Number and Staying With the Trouble of Value
themes: narrative design; visualization; storytelling modes
relevance_rationale: Narrative design principles for mixing text and visuals remain core storytelling craft regardless of tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Indigenous data sovereignty is concerned with the rights of Indigenous peoples to own, control, access and possess data that derive from them, and which pertain to their members, knowledge systems, customs or territories. ID-SOV begets questions such as: Who owns the data? Who has the power to make decisions about how data is accessed and under what circumstances? Who are the intended beneficiaries?
chapter: 9. Indigenous Data Sovereignty: Implications for Data Journalism
themes: data sovereignty; Indigenous rights; data ownership; power
relevance_rationale: Indigenous data sovereignty principles are ethical imperatives that transcend any particular technology.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data journalists have ample opportunities to rethink how they use data to represent Indigenous peoples and stories, and to expose the complex ways in which Indigenous data is produced, controlled, disseminated and put to work by government and industry. Data journalists ought not to rely on non-Indigenous data producers and users; the rise of ID-SOV networks means there are a growing number of Indigenous data experts to call on.
chapter: 9. Indigenous Data Sovereignty: Implications for Data Journalism
themes: data sovereignty; source diversity; representation; marginalized communities
relevance_rationale: Rethinking how data represents Indigenous peoples and consulting Indigenous data experts are enduring ethical obligations.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: In China, instead of data being the driver of stories, stories or particular issues drove the production of data. Citizens, government, civil society and the media may interact at multiple stages in this process. Data, instead of just serving as the starting point for stories, can also come into the picture at a later stage.
themes: issue-driven data; citizen data; alternative workflows
relevance_rationale: Issue-driven data production and citizen involvement in data creation are workflow approaches independent of technology.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Start with government websites, both central ministries and local agencies. You would need to know which department is the right one for the data you are looking for. Once you find some data that could be useful online, make sure to download a local copy. If the data is not released by the government, check if any user-generated content is available.
themes: government data; data acquisition; archiving; user-generated content
relevance_rationale: Government website navigation for data acquisition endures but LLMs can now assist with discovery and archiving at scale.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We developed a documentation methodology in consultation with academics working on land governance. We put together a network of researchers and journalists, who live across the country, to document the conflicts in their regions following this methodology.
chapter: 11. Making a Database to Document Land Conflicts Across India
themes: methodology design; distributed networks; academic collaboration
relevance_rationale: Designing documentation methodology in consultation with academics and distributed researcher networks is timeless organizational practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Researchers file all the data into reporting-and-review software built into the website. Data is examined and verified by dedicated reviewers. The software allows to-and-fro workflow between the researchers and the reviewers before the data is published.
chapter: 11. Making a Database to Document Land Conflicts Across India
themes: review workflow; quality control; collaborative verification
relevance_rationale: Review workflows for data verification endure in principle but LLMs can now assist with initial data checks and flagging.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We should not settle for existing published databases and we should not make too many early assumptions about what is and is not possible. Journalism students approached the Ministry of Labour and asked for information. In a few days the students had a database in their hands. We had wrongly assumed that the data was not intended for the public, whereas the ministry simply did not have an up-to-date Internet portal.
themes: data access; assumptions; direct request; persistence
relevance_rationale: Not assuming data is inaccessible and directly asking institutions remains a timeless data acquisition lesson.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: To support our analysis and investigations, we have to create databases ourselves based on information that is public but not properly structured. For example, to report on Cuban elections, we had to build databases by combining information from different sources—data from the Cuban Parliament, press reports and Communist Party websites.
themes: database construction; multi-source compilation; structuring
relevance_rationale: Building databases from unstructured public sources endures but LLMs can now automate structuring from multiple heterogeneous sources.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Our advice for journalists who want to involve readers in data investigations is to appoint a dedicated community manager. Large collections of documents can be a good place to start: The learning curve is fast and participants feel part of something bigger. Support collaborators with video tutorials or contextual introductions.
chapter: 13. Making Data With Readers at La Nacion
themes: crowdsourcing; community management; reader collaboration
relevance_rationale: Community management for crowdsourced investigations endures but LLMs could assist with document triage and onboarding.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: When publishing outputs and stories, acknowledge the collaboration process and participant organizations in every platform. Emphasizing the collective character of such projects can send a stronger message to those who we want to hold accountable.
chapter: 13. Making Data With Readers at La Nacion
themes: attribution; collaboration; accountability; transparency
relevance_rationale: Acknowledging collaboration and emphasizing collective character of projects is a timeless transparency practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Respondents cannot be handpicked—a representative pool would include people from all social categories. The selection of respondents needs to be randomised. The number of people taking a survey must reach a certain threshold for it to be representative. Keep the confidence level at 95% and the margin of error no bigger than 5%.
themes: survey methodology; sampling; statistical significance; representativeness
relevance_rationale: Survey methodology principles like representative sampling and statistical significance are fundamental and tool-independent.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Always describe your efforts accurately. If you polled three advisers on a yes-no question, say so. If you run a statistically significant study, have the courtesy to release its methodology. No methodology, no trust. Do not use facts to cover up a lack of certainty over what the truth is.
themes: methodology transparency; honesty; limitations disclosure
relevance_rationale: Transparency about methodology and honest disclosure of limitations are timeless trust-building practices.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: If you run a survey, field-test your findings and protect your reporting against potentially problematic claims. Visit the places that you compare and contrast; talk to people on the streets, in shops, banks, pubs and schools. Such ground truthing efforts will allow you to evaluate your data and decide to what extent you can trust the results.
themes: ground truthing; field verification; data validation
relevance_rationale: Ground truthing survey findings through field visits and interviews is essential verification that no AI can replace.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Where does your story lie? In a pattern? In an outlier? Decide what data you need to collect based on this answer. Figure out where and how the data can be obtained before you decide on the most appropriate methods. The method is never the point, the story is.
themes: story-driven method; patterns; outliers; research design
relevance_rationale: Story-driven method selection—starting with what the story needs rather than the tool—is a timeless principle.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Structural inequality shows up in data collection in two ways. Specific bodies are overcounted through surveillance by those in power. The second way is undercounting or not counting at all. In cases of undercounting, data journalists can count it themselves, typically through a combination of crowdsourcing, story collection and statistical inference.
chapter: 15. Data Journalism: What's Feminism Got to Do With I.T.?
themes: structural bias; overcounting; undercounting; feminist methods
relevance_rationale: Understanding structural inequality in data collection—overcounting and undercounting—is essential critical data literacy.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The pitfall is that journalists take numbers downloaded from the web at face value without understanding the nuances of the collection environment, including power differentials, social stigma and cultural norms. The opportunity is that there are many more data context stories to be told—using the numbers to interrogate the collection environment, point out flawed practices and shift counting practices.
chapter: 15. Data Journalism: What's Feminism Got to Do With I.T.?
themes: data context; collection bias; power dynamics; critical analysis
relevance_rationale: Critically interrogating the data collection environment rather than taking numbers at face value is a timeless skill.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: There are many cases—usually having to do with women and other marginalized groups—in which a neutral title actually does harm to the group depicted by the data, precisely because it is not naming the structural forces at work.
chapter: 15. Data Journalism: What's Feminism Got to Do With I.T.?
themes: framing; visualization ethics; structural forces; marginalization
relevance_rationale: Recognizing that neutral framing can harm marginalized groups is an ethical insight that persists regardless of tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We call our philosophy radical sharing: ICIJ's partners communicate their findings as they are working, not only with their immediate co-workers, but also with journalists halfway around the world. Tax stories are like puzzles with missing pieces: A reporter in Estonia might understand one part of the story; a Brazilian reporter might come across another part.
chapter: 16. Infrastructuring Collaborations Around the Panama and Paradise Papers
themes: radical sharing; cross-border collaboration; distributed investigation
relevance_rationale: Radical sharing and cross-border collaboration principles are organizational practices that transcend any particular technology.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: ICIJ's engineers put together a framework: using Apache Tika to extract metadata and text, Apache Solr to build search engines, and Tesseract to turn images into text. The team built an open-source software called Extract with the single mission of turning unstructured documents into searchable, machine-readable content.
chapter: 16. Infrastructuring Collaborations Around the Panama and Paradise Papers
themes: text extraction; OCR; search infrastructure; open source
relevance_rationale: Text extraction with Tika/Solr/Tesseract endures in concept but LLMs now handle OCR, extraction, and search far more capably.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: By using network graph tools such as Neo4J and Linkurious, ICIJ was able to allow partners to quickly explore connections between people and offshore entities. No coding skills were needed to explore the data. Graph databases and technologies powered ICIJ's radical sharing model.
chapter: 16. Infrastructuring Collaborations Around the Panama and Paradise Papers
themes: graph databases; Neo4J; Linkurious; network analysis; entity connections
relevance_rationale: Graph databases for entity exploration endure but LLMs can now assist with natural-language querying of relationship networks.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: It is mandatory when joining an ICIJ investigation to setup a PGP key pair to encrypt emails. ICIJ implements two-factor authentication on all of its platforms. On some sensitive platforms, we even add third-factor authentication via client certificates.
chapter: 16. Infrastructuring Collaborations Around the Panama and Paradise Papers
themes: security; encryption; PGP; operational security
relevance_rationale: Operational security practices like PGP encryption and multi-factor authentication remain essential for sensitive investigations.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Five ways journalists can use text analysis: length (how much they wrote or spoke), mentions (who said what, when and how many times), comparisons (how similar documents are), classification (categorizing text with machine learning), and sentiment (classifying as positive, negative or neutral).
chapter: 17. Text as Data: Finding Stories in Text Collections
themes: text analysis; NLP; computational journalism; text mining
relevance_rationale: The five text analysis approaches endure but LLMs now perform all of them far more powerfully than the pre-LLM methods described.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: A low number or absence of mentions may be newsworthy as well. These omissions can be analyzed over time, but also based on the expectation that a person or organization mentions something in a given context.
chapter: 17. Text as Data: Finding Stories in Text Collections
themes: omission analysis; absence as signal; text analysis
relevance_rationale: Recognizing absences and omissions as newsworthy signals is a critical analytical insight independent of technology.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The LA Times analyzed more than 400,000 police reports and revealed that an estimated 14,000 serious assaults had been misclassified by the LAPD as minor offenses. The reporters used machine learning classifiers (SVM and MaxEnt) to re-classify eight years' worth of data in half the time needed for the first investigation, which covered one year only.
chapter: 17. Text as Data: Finding Stories in Text Collections
themes: machine learning; classification; police data; scalability
relevance_rationale: ML classification of documents endures as a concept but LLMs now perform this far more flexibly without custom SVM/MaxEnt training.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Using off-the-shelf text mining software is a good starting point. Platforms designed for journalists such as DocumentCloud and Overview include text analysis features. A good command of regular expressions and the tools needed to collect texts (web scraping, API querying, FOIA requests) and process them (OCR, file format conversion) are must-haves.
chapter: 17. Text as Data: Finding Stories in Text Collections
themes: DocumentCloud; Overview; text mining tools; OCR; regex
relevance_rationale: Text mining platforms and regex skills still matter but LLMs now handle many text analysis tasks without specialized tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data journalists often jump between techniques as they need: Scraping data with Python notebooks, throwing the result into a spreadsheet, copying it for cleaning in Refine before pasting it back again.
chapter: Coding With Data in the Newsroom
themes: workflow; tool switching; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Tool-switching workflow between Python, spreadsheets, and Refine endures but LLMs can now mediate between steps more fluidly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: When setting off to do something new, particularly in the beginning of your learning, make sure you leave yourself enough time to be able to complete your task with a tool you know (e.g., spreadsheet). If you are just starting to learn and strapped for time, you may want to use a familiar tool and wait until you have more time to experiment.
chapter: Coding With Data in the Newsroom
themes: deadline management; skill development; newsroom workflow
relevance_rationale: The advice to fall back to familiar tools under deadline still applies but LLMs now dramatically flatten the learning curve for new tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: We prioritized displaying numbers for the reader's local area. Once this was done and there was a bit of extra time, we did the next item on the list: A visualization comparing the reader's local area to other areas, and the national average. The project could have gone to publication at any point thanks to how we worked. This iterative workflow helps you focus and manage expectations at the same time.
chapter: Coding With Data in the Newsroom
themes: iterative development; prioritization; deadline management
relevance_rationale: Iterative development with prioritization so a project can ship at any point is timeless deadline management.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: 40% of responding data teams were three to five members and 30% of them counted only one or two members. These small numbers pose a challenge to internal code reviewing practices. Data journalists thus often work on their own, either because they don't have colleagues, because there are no peer-review systems in place or because there is no one with the right skills to review their code.
chapter: Coding With Data in the Newsroom
themes: code review; team size; quality control
relevance_rationale: Small team code review challenges endure but LLMs can now serve as first-pass code reviewers for solo data journalists.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data, despite its aura of hard objective truth, can be distorted and misrepresented. There are many ways in which data journalists can introduce error into their interpretation of a data set and publish a misleading story. There could be issues at the point of data collection which prevent general inferences being made to a broader population.
chapter: Accounting for Methods: Spreadsheets, Scripts and Programming Notebooks
themes: data quality; error; bias; epistemology
relevance_rationale: Awareness that data can be distorted and that errors can enter at collection is a timeless epistemological concern.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Excel hides much of the process of working with data by design. Formulas are only visible when clicking on a cell. This means that it is harder to review the actual steps taken to reaching a given conclusion. Excel-based workflows generally encourage the removal of the steps taken to arrive at a conclusion. Values rather than formulas are often copied across to other sheets or columns, leaving the 'undo' key as the only route back.
chapter: Accounting for Methods: Spreadsheets, Scripts and Programming Notebooks
themes: reproducibility; transparency; spreadsheets; methodology
relevance_rationale: Excel hiding process steps is still true but LLMs and notebooks now provide better alternatives for transparent reproducible workflows.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Literate programming environments like Jupyter Notebooks produce human-readable documents that mix plain English, visualizations and code in a single document that can be rendered in HTML and published on the web. Original data can be linked to explicitly, and any other technical dependencies such as third-party libraries will be clearly identified.
chapter: Accounting for Methods: Spreadsheets, Scripts and Programming Notebooks
themes: Jupyter Notebooks; reproducibility; literate programming; transparency
relevance_rationale: Literate programming principles for reproducibility and transparency remain essential and are complemented, not replaced, by LLMs.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: A Jupyter Notebook was able to clearly capture how we had performed this type of deduplication by presenting a table at the relevant step that set out the fields that were used to assert the identity of individuals. These same processes have been used at Global Witness for fact-checking purposes as well.
chapter: Accounting for Methods: Spreadsheets, Scripts and Programming Notebooks
themes: entity resolution; deduplication; fact-checking; Jupyter Notebooks
relevance_rationale: Using notebooks to document deduplication steps for fact-checking remains a valuable reproducibility practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Jupyter Notebooks have also proven particularly useful when there is need to monitor a specific data set over time. We acquired a new snapshot from the land registry and reused and published a notebook we had developed for the same purpose two years previously. This yielded comparable results with minimal overheads. The notebook has an additional advantage: It allowed Global Witness to show its methodology in the absence of being able to republish the underlying source data.
chapter: Accounting for Methods: Spreadsheets, Scripts and Programming Notebooks
themes: longitudinal analysis; reproducibility; methodology; Jupyter Notebooks
relevance_rationale: Reusing notebooks for longitudinal monitoring and methodology transparency remains a strong reproducibility practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Technologies that make a reporter's assumptions explicit and their methods reproducible are valuable. They aid collaboration and open up an increasingly technical discipline to scrutiny from various publics. Given the current crisis of trust in journalism, a wider embrace of reproducible approaches may be one important way in which data teams can maintain their credibility.
chapter: Accounting for Methods: Spreadsheets, Scripts and Programming Notebooks
themes: reproducibility; trust; transparency; credibility
relevance_rationale: Making assumptions explicit and methods reproducible for trust and credibility is even more important in the LLM era.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: When information is abundant, not sharing the how-we-got-there may deprive the reader of the means to understand and make sense of a story.
themes: transparency; methodology; open source; trust
relevance_rationale: Sharing the how-we-got-there is more important than ever when LLMs can generate plausible but unverifiable analyses.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Working openly and making various data sets, tools, code, methods and processes transparent and available can help data journalists improve the quality of their work. Documenting processes can encourage journalists to be more organized, more accurate and less likely to miss errors. It can also lighten the burden of editing and reviewing complex stories, enabling readers to report issues.
themes: open source; transparency; quality control; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Working openly with transparent data, tools, code and methods improves quality and is an enduring practice.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: One common approach to transparency is through the methodology section, also known as the 'nerd box.' If your intention is to reach a wider audience, a box inside the article or even a footnote with a succinct explanation of your methods may be sufficient. It is important to avoid jargon, explain how data was obtained and used, ensure readers don't miss important caveats, and explain in the most clear and direct way how you reached your conclusion.
themes: methodology box; transparency; audience; plain language
relevance_rationale: Methodology boxes and plain-language explanations of methods for transparency remain essential communication practices.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The BuzzFeed News team has an index of all its open-source data, analysis, libraries, tools and guides. They release not only the methodology behind their reporting, but also the scripts used to extract, clean, analyze and present data. This practice makes their work reproducible as well as enabling interested readers to explore the data for themselves.
themes: open source; reproducibility; code sharing; GitHub
relevance_rationale: Open-sourcing data, scripts, and methodology for reproducibility is an enduring best practice amplified by the LLM era.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The dirty secret of computational journalism is that the 'algorithmic' part of a story is not the part that takes all of the time and effort. Getting the data, cleaning it and following up algorithmic leads is the hard part. Surveys of data scientists consistently show that most of their work is data wrangling and cleaning—often up to 80%—and journalism is no different.
themes: data wrangling; data cleaning; workflow; effort allocation
relevance_rationale: Data wrangling consuming 80% of effort endures as a challenge but LLMs can now dramatically reduce cleaning time.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Reporters analyzed over 100,000 doctor disciplinary records from every US state. Rather than reading every report, they first drastically reduced this pile by applying machine learning to find reports that were likely to concern sexual abuse. They were able to cut down their pile more than 10 times, to just 6,000 documents, which they then read and reviewed manually.
themes: machine learning; document classification; investigative journalism
relevance_rationale: Using ML to reduce document piles for manual review endures but LLMs now do this far more flexibly without custom training.
notes: Atlanta Journal-Constitution License to Betray

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Just getting the data was a huge challenge. There is no national database of doctor disciplinary reports, just a series of state-level databases. Many of these databases do not contain a field indicating why a doctor was disciplined. At first, the team tried to get the reports through freedom of information requests. This proved to be prohibitively expensive. So, the team turned to scraping documents from state medical board websites. These documents had to be OCR'd and loaded into a custom web-based application for collaborative tagging and review.
themes: data acquisition; scraping; FOI; OCR; collaboration tools
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition challenges via scraping and OCR endure but LLMs now handle OCR, extraction and structuring far more capably.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: OpenRefine is a long-standing hero for all sorts of cleaning tasks. Dedupe.io is machine learning applied to the problem of merging near-duplicate names in a database. Classic text-wrangling methods like regular expressions should be a part of every data journalist's education.
themes: OpenRefine; deduplication; regular expressions; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine, regex and deduplication remain useful but LLMs now handle many cleaning and entity resolution tasks more flexibly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Computational thinking is the reflexive process through which a set of programmatic steps are taken to solve a problem. The infusion of computational thinking into professional journalism challenges the fundamental thought system in journalism from descriptive storytelling to abstract reasoning, autonomous research and visualization of quantitative facts.
chapter: Journalism With Machines? From Computational Thinking to Distributed Cognition
themes: computational thinking; abstraction; journalism epistemology
relevance_rationale: Computational thinking endures as a concept but LLMs shift the emphasis from programming steps to prompt-based reasoning.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: As journalists use networked software and hardware to augment their capacity to produce news at scale and speed, their cognition becomes distributed across the range of platforms and tools they use. Who owns and manages journalists' access to that wealth of knowledge and free analytical power? We experienced this when Google shut down its online data visualization tool Google Fusion Tables. After the closure, dozens of data journalism projects became unavailable.
chapter: Journalism With Machines? From Computational Thinking to Distributed Cognition
themes: platform dependency; distributed cognition; tool sustainability
relevance_rationale: Platform dependency and distributed cognition risks are even more relevant with journalist reliance on LLM platforms.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: These reporters are almost pure newsgatherers—their goal is not to produce a visualization nor to tell stories with data. Instead, they use records to explore a potential story. Their work is integral to the reporting project, often driving the development of an investigation.
themes: empirical journalism; investigation; computer-assisted reporting
relevance_rationale: Using records to explore potential stories as pure newsgathering remains a core investigative journalism practice.
notes: On empirical journalism tradition

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Visual and open-source investigations expand our notion of 'data' into videos, crowdsourced social media and other digital artefacts. While it's less dependent on coding, it fits solidly in the tradition of data journalism by uncovering—through original research—what others would like to hold secret.
themes: OSINT; open-source investigation; digital artefacts; verification
relevance_rationale: OSINT and visual investigations using crowdsourced social media data remain essential and complement LLM capabilities.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Linking a dataviz to a data source, providing access to the raw data and explaining methodologies are seen by some participants as ethical practices which create transparency and counterbalance the subjectivity of selection and interpretation which is an inevitable aspect of visualizing data.
chapter: Data Visualizations: Newsroom Trends and Everyday Engagements
themes: data visualization; transparency; subjectivity; methodology
relevance_rationale: Linking dataviz to data sources and explaining methodology for transparency are enduring ethical practices.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Widespread recognition that audiences increasingly consume news on small, mobile screens has led to a turn away from elaborate and interactive visualizations to greater simplicity and linearity, or simple visual forms with low levels of interactivity. This has led to a predominance of certain chart types, such as bar charts and line charts, and to the advent of scrollytelling.
chapter: Data Visualizations: Newsroom Trends and Everyday Engagements
themes: mobile first; data visualization; scrollytelling; design
relevance_rationale: Mobile-first design and simplicity in visualization remain important presentation principles regardless of AI tools.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Journalists increasingly need 'soft knowledge of Internet culture,' including an understanding of how data visualizations may be more likely to circulate online than text, floating free of their original contexts. This requires understanding of strategies such as embedding explanatory text into a visualization file so that the image cannot be circulated without the explanation.
chapter: Data Visualizations: Newsroom Trends and Everyday Engagements
themes: data visualization; context; misinformation; social media
relevance_rationale: Understanding how visualizations circulate without context on social media is even more relevant in the LLM misinformation era.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Audiences need to feel that they have the necessary skills to decode visualizations, and many participants indicated a lack of confidence in this regard. A broad range of emotions emerged in relation to engagements with dataviz. Whether a visualization is effective depends in large part on how, by whom, when and where it is accessed.
chapter: Data Visualizations: Newsroom Trends and Everyday Engagements
themes: data literacy; audience engagement; emotions; visualization effectiveness
relevance_rationale: Audience data literacy gaps and emotional engagement with visualizations are enduring human factors.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: I don't have really advanced statistical skills. The way that I often start analyzing data is through relatively simple calculations that other people can replicate. At a certain point with other more advanced statistical approaches you present readers with an ultimatum: Either you trust the journalist's analysis or you don't. There is a certain benefit to doing things with simple calculations.
themes: simplicity; replicability; trust; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Simple replicable calculations that build reader trust remain valuable even when more complex analysis is possible.
notes: Mona Chalabi interview

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data can be used as an opportunity to do two different things: To 'zoom in' or 'zoom out.' My responsibility as a data journalist is to zoom out from that one specific incident and give readers context using data. On the other hand, we can zoom in: We can say to readers, here is the national employment rate but also this is what it looks like for women, for different age groups, for different racial and ethnic groups.
themes: context; disaggregation; zoom in zoom out; audience
relevance_rationale: Zoom-in and zoom-out as analytical and storytelling strategies for providing context remain essential.
notes: Mona Chalabi interview

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: I think good data visualizations should communicate uncertainty. So few data journalists take the time to communicate uncertainty. So few data journalists take the time to reach out to communities that aren't geeks. My approach to data journalism is based on the idea that you don't necessarily need specific vocabularies or expertise to be smart.
themes: uncertainty; accessibility; data literacy; inclusion
relevance_rationale: Communicating uncertainty and reaching non-expert communities are enduring principles of inclusive data journalism.
notes: Mona Chalabi interview

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Part of the beauty of data visualization is that it can make things feel more visceral. You don't say 'bankers in London earn this much,' you say 'bankers in London earn 7,000 times what a social worker earns.' All of those analogies really help people. My illustrations are also transparent about their shortcomings. Readers have access to that critique in a way that they don't with computer generated graphics.
themes: data visualization; analogy; comparability; transparency
relevance_rationale: Using analogy and visceral comparisons to make data meaningful is a timeless storytelling technique.
notes: Mona Chalabi interview

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: A now-common format begins with a single chart and then proceeds to manipulate it—zooming in and out, travelling through time, switching out data—in order to fully explore the data set. This pairs exceptionally well with scrollytelling and is especially valuable on mobile, where there may not be enough space to show all elements of a chart at once.
chapter: The Web as Medium for Data Visualization
themes: scrollytelling; data visualization; mobile; interactive graphics
relevance_rationale: Scrollytelling and progressive chart revelation for mobile-first storytelling remain effective presentation techniques.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Another twist on the 'huge data sets' idea—and one that I've found to be incredibly compelling to readers—is to show the reader where they fall in a data set, usually by asking for a couple of personal details.
chapter: The Web as Medium for Data Visualization
themes: personalization; audience engagement; interactive graphics
relevance_rationale: Showing readers where they fall in a dataset through personalization remains a compelling engagement technique.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: ai2html, a script open-sourced by The New York Times, generates a responsive html snippet from Adobe Illustrator files. Maps built with ai2html can leverage a traditional cartographer's skill set and still have sharp, machine-readable text. The lack of interactivity in such maps is often a blessing. Data journalists should be thoughtful in their use of the web's unique features.
chapter: The Web as Medium for Data Visualization
themes: ai2html; maps; responsive design; interactivity
relevance_rationale: ai2html and Illustrator-to-web workflows endure but AI tools can now generate responsive visualizations more directly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Fewer people interact with charts than previously assumed. Most people will miss content if it's hidden behind interactivity, which led many graphic reporters to decide not to hide anything in the first place. Instead of adding to a story, interactivity becomes the story. We've seen great examples of explorable explanations where readers can enter their personal data to then see how they fit into the greater scheme.
chapter: Four Recent Developments in News Graphics
themes: interactivity; audience engagement; design decisions; news graphics
relevance_rationale: The insight that most people miss content hidden behind interactivity is an enduring audience behavior finding.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: A newsroom has two options when it comes to charting tools: Use an external charting tool such as Datawrapper or Infogram, or build an in-house charting tool. Although the second option sounds like a great idea, many newsrooms will find that it uses more resources than expected. External charting tools are built by dedicated teams that will maintain the tool and offer training.
chapter: Four Recent Developments in News Graphics
themes: charting tools; Datawrapper; newsroom resources; tool selection
relevance_rationale: Charting tool selection decisions endure but LLMs now enable faster in-house tool building and can generate charts directly.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Although it has become good practice to make raw data available after a data-driven investigation, the step of building a searchable interface for that data is considerably less common. A database on its own contains many human decisions; what was collected and what was left out, and how it was categorized, sorted or analyzed. No piece of data is objective.
chapter: Searchable Databases as a Journalistic Product
themes: databases; objectivity; data decisions; news applications
relevance_rationale: Recognizing that databases embed human decisions and are never objective is an enduring epistemological insight.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Providing an online database allows readers to search for their own city, politician or doctor and connects the story to their own life. Provided there are analytics running on these search queries, this also gives the newsroom more data on what their readers are interested in—potentially providing more leads for future work.
chapter: Searchable Databases as a Journalistic Product
themes: databases; audience engagement; personalization; lead generation

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Best practices for journalistic databases: Develop a process for how to fix mistakes ahead of publication. Build in a feedback channel. Either keep the database up to date, or clearly mark that it is no longer maintained. Allocate enough resources for maintenance over time. Observe how readers are using the database. Be transparent: Make the choices built into every database visible so readers know what they're looking at.
chapter: Searchable Databases as a Journalistic Product
themes: databases; maintenance; transparency; best practices; error correction
relevance_rationale: Best practices for database maintenance, error correction, and transparency are timeless operational principles.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Responsible data practices are ongoing approaches rather than checklists to be considered at specific points. Approaches which prioritize the rights of people reflected in the data throughout the entire investigation, from data gathering to publication, are a core part of optimizing journalism for trust.
chapter: Searchable Databases as a Journalistic Product
themes: responsible data; ethics; privacy; trust
relevance_rationale: Responsible data practices prioritizing rights of people in the data are ethical imperatives that transcend technology.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Like all other facts, data needs to be verified. Before trying to create known knowns, ask yourself: Is the data true? What does each number actually mean? What is the source? Why was the data collected? Who made the data set? How was the data table created? Are there outliers in the data? Do they make sense? And, often forgotten: What does the source not say?

themes: verification; data quality; critical questions; source assessment
relevance_rationale: Critical questions about data truth, source, purpose and omissions are foundational verification practices.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: You should be precise while doing data analysis. But in order to keep your story 'efficiently graspable,' there needs to be a limit on precision in the final publication. Using '4 out of 10 people' is probably better than '41.8612%.' The best data journalism might hardly be recognizable as such—making data journalism an invisible craft. Form facilitates data, facilitates story. Story is king.
chapter: Data Journalism Should Focus on People and Stories
themes: storytelling; precision; accessibility; audience
relevance_rationale: Balancing analytical precision with accessible storytelling is a timeless craft principle.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Algorithmic accountability reporting is a re-orientation of the traditional watchdog function of journalism towards the power wielded through algorithms. Despite their ostensible objectivity, algorithms can and do make mistakes and embed biases that warrant closer scrutiny. What makes an algorithm newsworthy is when it somehow makes a 'bad' decision—doing something it was not supposed to do, or not doing something it was supposed to do.
chapter: The Algorithms Beat: Angles and Methods for Investigation
themes: algorithmic accountability; bias; watchdog journalism; algorithms
relevance_rationale: Algorithmic accountability reporting is even more important in the LLM era as algorithms wield greater power.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Four driving forces underlie many algorithmic accountability stories: (a) discrimination and unfairness, (b) errors or mistakes in predictions or classifications, (c) legal or social norm violations, and (d) misuse of algorithms by people either intentionally or inadvertently.
chapter: The Algorithms Beat: Angles and Methods for Investigation
themes: algorithmic accountability; story angles; investigation framework
relevance_rationale: The four driving forces of algorithmic accountability stories apply directly to LLMs: discrimination, errors, norm violations, misuse.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The basic idea of auditing is that if the inputs to algorithms are varied in enough different ways, and the outputs are monitored, then inputs and outputs can be correlated to build a theory for how the algorithm may be functioning. When algorithms can be accessed via APIs or online web pages, output data can be collected automatically. For personalized algorithms, auditing techniques have been married to crowdsourcing in order to gather data from a range of people who may each have a unique view of the algorithm.
chapter: The Algorithms Beat: Angles and Methods for Investigation
themes: algorithm auditing; reverse engineering; crowdsourcing; APIs
relevance_rationale: Algorithm auditing via input-output analysis endures but needs updating for LLM black-box systems where APIs and outputs differ.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Focus on the outcomes and impacts of algorithms rather than trying to explain the exact mechanism of their decision making. Identifying algorithmic discrimination (i.e., an output) oftentimes has more value to society as an initial step than explaining exactly how that discrimination came about. By focusing on outcomes, journalists can provide a first-order diagnostic and signal an alarm which other stakeholders can then dig into.
chapter: The Algorithms Beat: Angles and Methods for Investigation
themes: algorithmic accountability; outcomes focus; investigation strategy
relevance_rationale: Focusing on outcomes and impacts rather than mechanisms is even more practical advice for opaque LLM systems.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Since algorithms necessarily rely on a quantified version of reality that only incorporates what is measurable as data they can miss a lot of the social and legal context. By understanding what a particular algorithm actually quantifies about the world—how it 'sees' things—journalists can inform critique by illuminating the missing bits that would support a decision in the richness of its full context.
chapter: The Algorithms Beat: Angles and Methods for Investigation
themes: algorithms; quantification; context; critical data studies
relevance_rationale: Understanding what algorithms quantify and miss about social reality applies directly to LLM limitations.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Social media can be used as a proxy to better understand individuals and their actions. Alternatively, the web can be seen as an ecosystem in its own right in which stories take place on social platforms. chapter: Telling Stories With the Social Web
themes: social media; data sources; misinformation; digital ecosystem
relevance_rationale: Social media as both proxy and ecosystem for stories remains relevant though platforms continue to evolve.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: There are broadly three different ways to harvest data from the social web: APIs, personal archives and scraping. The kind of data that official channels like API data streams provide is very limited. Social media companies only provide a sliver of their data through APIs. Extracting social media data through scraping is often against the terms of service and can result in a lawsuit.
chapter: Telling Stories With the Social Web
themes: social media data; APIs; scraping; data access; legal constraints

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: One of the biggest issues with social media data is that we cannot assume that the people we hear on Twitter or Facebook are representative samples of broader populations offline. Not every user necessarily represents a person—there are bots and cyborgs. A vast amount of people often choose to remain silent, and it is only the extreme reactions that are recorded and fed back into algorithms that disproportionately amplify the loudest.
chapter: Telling Stories With the Social Web
themes: representativeness; social media bias; bots; selection bias

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Which websites share the same Google Analytics ID? If the websites share the same ID, it follows that they are operated by the same registrant. Viewing the source code of the web page, he found a Google Analytics ID, which he inserted into reverse lookup software that furnishes a list of other websites using the same ID. He found a network of websites sharing a similar anti-Ukraine narrative.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Journalists make and follow links in code, public records, databases and leaks, piecing it all together for an account of 'who's behind' particular operations. chapter: Digital Forensics: Repurposing Google Analytics IDs
themes: OSINT; digital forensics; investigation; source identification
relevance_rationale: Digital forensics approach of following links across records and databases to discover who is behind operations is timeless.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Digital methods are defined by the methodological principle of 'following the medium,' responding to and interfacing methods with developments in the digital environment. chapter: 34. Apps and Their Affordances for Data Investigations

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Research affordances attune to the action possibilities within software from the perspective of the researcher. author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Good algorithmic accountability investigations are only possible by joining forces with others and creating teams with diverse skill sets. author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

If your company has a data protection officer, involve them in the project early on.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Raising awareness of the operating principles of algorithms can be a first strong goal. Projects should also try to achieve as much transparency as possible. Checking whether algorithms have a discriminatory effect is a more challenging goal that requires extensive data sets and resources.

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Civic technologists and data journalists complement each other around two core practices: facilitating (enabling others to take actions themselves) and gatekeeping (proactively pushing relevant information). author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
