extract: Award-worthy data journalism is dominated by newspapers and investigative journalism organizations, produced by teams averaging five contributors, with about a third collaborating with external partners. Award-winning projects are produced by larger teams than those only nominated. Data journalism above a certain level appears to be an undertaking for larger organizations with resources for cross-disciplinary teams.
themes: team composition; resources; collaboration; awards
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The more data is out there, the more work is done with it, the greater pressure there is for more data to be released—a virtuous circle of data, easy processing, data visualization, and more data. Free tools combined with access to free public data facilitated the production of more public-facing visualizations and data projects.
chapter: 38. From The Guardian to Google News Lab
themes: open data; virtuous circle; tools; data journalism growth
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Whenever a new technique is introduced in reporting, data would not only be a key part of it but data journalists would be right there in the middle of it. In less than three years, crowdsourcing became established, journalists found data, used databases to manage huge document dumps, published data sets and applied data-driven analytical techniques to complex news stories.
chapter: 38. From The Guardian to Google News Lab
themes: innovation; crowdsourcing; data journalism evolution
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: A high-impact strategy for data journalism might follow the following five steps: Set goals. What might happen as a result of your project? Who or what has the power and/or incentive to address any wrongdoing? Who should have access to the information you are bringing to light? Ask yourself these questions to decide what type or types of impact are reasonable for your project.
chapter: Beyond Clicks and Shares: How and Why to Measure the Impact of Data Journalism Projects
themes: impact strategy; project planning; goal-setting; audience analysis
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Once you have goals for your project, identify the important target audiences for the work. What source of news and information do these audiences trust? How might they best access the information? Do they need an interactive, or will a linear story be more effective?
chapter: Beyond Clicks and Shares: How and Why to Measure the Impact of Data Journalism Projects
themes: audience analysis; project planning; presentation format; impact strategy
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: While media often refer to 'measuring' the impact of their work, I prefer the term 'strategic research,' as both qualitative and quantitative research methods should be considered. The sooner you can identify research methods and indicators, the better your information will be.
chapter: Beyond Clicks and Shares: How and Why to Measure the Impact of Data Journalism Projects
themes: impact measurement; research methods; mixed methods; evaluation
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: If a data journalism project has as its goal to spur institutional change, unique page views are not an appropriate metric of success; mentions of the data by public officials in documents would be a better indicator.
chapter: Beyond Clicks and Shares: How and Why to Measure the Impact of Data Journalism Projects
themes: impact measurement; metrics; institutional change; evaluation
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Rigorous content analysis would identify key words, data and/or phrases in a project, then analyze their prevalence pre- and post-publication in a finite corpus of text to document change. Computational text analysis goes a step further and infers shifts in discourse by advanced counting and analysis techniques.
chapter: Beyond Clicks and Shares: How and Why to Measure the Impact of Data Journalism Projects
themes: content analysis; impact measurement; text analysis; computational methods
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: The questions of 'in whose interest and why' must always be asked --- and in some cases, research and/or data mining analyses are worth refusing because meaning-making processes are predicated on entrenched notions of race, gender and class.
themes: ethics; power; reflexivity; data collection; critical data practice
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Ericson's critique of police statistics as not reflecting the social reality of crime but rather the 'cultural, legal, and social constructs produced ... for organizational purposes' is relevant for journalists focused solely on data wrangling.
themes: data quality; official statistics; critical data practice; institutional bias
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Potential harm could and should take priority over norms such as 'news you can use' and technologically fuelled experimentations. The way journalists cover crime news from a data perspective requires deep understanding of the consequences as well as problems of considering intentions that are only internal to journalism, evidence of success and rationales of innovation.
themes: ethics; harm reduction; critical data practice; crime reporting
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: On a weekly basis Bureau reporters have turned 'free text' reports into a structured data set that can be analyzed, searched and queried. That data --- complemented by interviews with sources --- has been used by NGOs and the Bureau has submitted written evidence to the UK Parliament's Defence Committee.
themes: data structuring; persistence; longitudinal tracking; impact
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Referring to Bureau of Investigative Journalism Drone Warfare project

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Counting the uncounted is a particularly important way that data journalism can make an impact --- indeed, it is probably fair to say that it is data journalism's equivalent of 'giving a voice to the voiceless.'
themes: data gaps; impact; building datasets; social justice
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Even when a government appears to be counting something, it can be worth investigating. While working with the BBC England Data Unit on an investigation into the scale of library cuts, for example, I experienced a moment of panic when I saw that a question was being asked in Parliament for data about the issue. In fact, it didn't --- instead, it established that the government itself knew less than we did about the true scale of those cuts, because they hadn't undertaken the depth of investigation that we had.
themes: official data limitations; verification; government data; original research
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Innovation is getting data journalism on a front page. Delivery is getting it on the front page day after day. Innovation is building a snazzy interactive that allows readers to explore and understand an important issue. Delivery is doing that, and getting large numbers of people to actually use it; then building another one the next day, and another the day after that.
themes: sustainability; workflow; impact; newsroom practice
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Quote from David Ottewell

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Data journalism is not just for churning out new visualizations of data gathered by someone else. At best, churning out someone else's data makes the data providers' assumptions visible; at worst, data journalism becomes as stenographic as a press release for the data provider.
chapter: What Is Data Journalism For? Cash, Clicks, and Cut and Trys
themes: original data collection; data provenance; critical data practice; assumptions
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Embedded in government data are assumptions and decisions about what to collect that need sufficient inquiry and consideration. The data is not inert, but filled with presumptions about what facts matter. Journalists seeking to take control over the domain of facticity need to be able to explain why the facts are what they are, and, in fact, the systematic production of fact is how journalists have claimed their epistemic authority for most of modern journalism.
chapter: What Is Data Journalism For? Cash, Clicks, and Cut and Trys
themes: data provenance; critical data practice; epistemology; government data; assumptions
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Liliana Bounegru, Jonathan Gray (eds.)
extract: Before mapping yet another social problem or academic study, data journalists need to ask: To what end are we mapping and charting (or charticle-ing for that matter)?
chapter: What Is Data Journalism For? Cash, Clicks, and Cut and Trys
themes: visualization ethics; reflexivity; purpose; critical data practice
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Data stories are intentional communicative artefacts that present data in an interesting, evocative, and informative way. Our aim is to focus on the human elements of what is in a dataset in order to be able to more clearly pinpoint what is at stake, and to communicate it effectively and empathetically to our audiences.
themes: data humanism; audience engagement; storytelling definition
relevance_rationale: Human-centered data storytelling focused on empathy and audience engagement is a fundamentally human editorial judgment LLMs cannot replace

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Every dataset contains many possible stories. Our aim in this workbook is not to teach you how to create one true story, or even how to develop the best story. Rather, the aim of this workbook is to cultivate the mind-sets needed for you to become a better data storyteller.
themes: multiple narratives; reflexivity; storytelling
relevance_rationale: Cultivating storytelling mindsets over rote technique remains essential even when LLMs can draft narratives

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: At the heart of developing data storytelling education is the task of balancing truth, accuracy, and transparency with engaging narratives. At the same time, it is crucial that we educate ourselves and the next generation not just to tell data stories, but to tell them responsibly.
themes: ethics; accuracy; transparency; responsibility
relevance_rationale: Balancing truth, accuracy, and transparency with engaging narratives is a core ethical commitment unaffected by AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Even when people are able to attend training classes online and offline, skill development and retention is difficult without regular engagement. Like learning a language, without the ability to practise everyday conversation, skills get lost.
themes: skills retention; training; professional development
relevance_rationale: Skill retention through regular practice applies to all learning regardless of tools available

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Rather than focus on specific software or tools, this workbook focuses on the resources, skills, and mind-sets needed to turn ideas into communicative artefacts.
chapter: A Guide to Using this Workbook
themes: tool-agnostic; mindsets; skills
relevance_rationale: Focusing on transferable mindsets rather than specific tools is precisely the philosophy that survives tool disruption

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: An on-going commitment to collaboration is essential for successful data storytelling. This means committing to curiosity-driven work, clarity in mutual expectations, coordination of working practices, and care for how we treat each other.
themes: collaboration; Four Cs; teamwork
relevance_rationale: Collaboration, curiosity, and mutual care in teamwork are human relational skills that persist regardless of technology

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: We come to believe in this myth that data is untouched and pure. We act as if data can exist in a realm apart from humans. But just as a photograph is not an objective representation of reality, neither is data. Data require human participation. Just like photography, it needs to be understood as framed and framing.
themes: raw data myth; framing; data ontology
relevance_rationale: Critical awareness that data is constructed and framed, not objective, becomes even more important when LLMs can generate plausible-sounding analysis
notes: Citing Gitelman and Jackson 2013

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Working with codes, themes, and other labelling systems can turn qualitative data into structured data, making it ripe for visual storytelling using charts and graphs.
themes: qualitative data; structured data; coding
relevance_rationale: Coding qualitative data into structured data is now significantly accelerated by LLMs, though human judgment on coding schemes endures

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The more we zoom in on a data point, the more we see its complexity, while the more we zoom out, the more we see its context. When presenting data, the goal is to zoom in enough to realistically relate complexity, while zooming out enough to give the context the audience needs to understand its potential meanings and significance.
themes: context; complexity; zoom; audience
relevance_rationale: The zoom-in/zoom-out editorial judgment for balancing complexity and context is a storytelling principle that transcends tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Before starting any data storytelling project, we always think first about audience. The task 'Think, Feel, Do' asks two questions: 1. What do we want our audience to think, feel, or do? 2. Thinking backwards from this objective: what key messages does our audience need to receive, understand and believe in order to perform these actions?
themes: audience analysis; Think Feel Do; objectives
relevance_rationale: Audience-first thinking via Think/Feel/Do framework is a strategic communication principle unchanged by AI
notes: Adapted from Susan O'Halloran 2014

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: There is no such thing as a general public. Understanding your audience segments involves asking questions about who you are writing for: How much detail is too much? When is it better to use technical terms versus translating your findings into everyday language? What elements of your data's backstory should you include?
themes: audience segmentation; specificity; data literacy levels
relevance_rationale: Audience segmentation and adjusting register/detail level is editorial judgment that persists regardless of AI tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: A good data storyteller needs to know their audience and adjust accordingly. It might be that you need to present the same finding in five different ways. As long as you are clear on the key messages, this kind of tailoring becomes easier to do over time.
themes: audience adaptation; key messages; tailoring
relevance_rationale: Tailoring the same finding for multiple audiences is now partly automatable with LLMs, though the strategic choices still require human judgment

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: In order to close the gap between what you would like your audience to think, feel, or do, and what they currently think, feel, or do, you need to understand them. Whatever strategy for gathering insights on audiences you employ, it is important to identify gaps between current mind-sets and the mind-set your work aims to achieve.
themes: audience research; Mind the Gap; information gaps
relevance_rationale: Understanding audience mindsets and closing perception gaps is a strategic communication skill unchanged by AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Finding a balance between author-driven and reader-driven approaches. Where the goal is for your audience to do something quite directed, an author-driven approach is more common. When creating a data story you need to meet your audience half-way, providing a structure for your audience to follow while leaving them with enough room to explore and reflect for themselves.
themes: author-driven; reader-driven; interactivity; narrative structure
relevance_rationale: Balancing author-driven and reader-driven narrative approaches is a design philosophy that transcends specific tools
notes: Drawing on Segel and Heer 2010

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Instead of building a data story around your process of data analysis, by starting with your research question or hypothesis, a data storyteller should be focused on key insights. The exposition and inciting incident should backup the climax of the main insight, rather than explain how you got to your research question.
themes: narrative arc; key insight; Freytag pyramid
relevance_rationale: Leading with insight rather than methodology in data storytelling is a narrative strategy that endures regardless of production tools
notes: Adapting Brent Dykes' data narrative arc

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: In example 1, the character of the data story is the researcher and the narrative arc is about research methodology. In example 2, the characters are students. Instead of taking centre stage, the data analyst fades into the background, and the data points become humanised. The narrative represents the emotional journey, rather than the research journey.
themes: humanising data; narrative perspective; data humanism
relevance_rationale: Humanizing data points and choosing between researcher-centered vs subject-centered narratives is an editorial choice AI cannot make alone
notes: Belonging at BU case study

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Action timelines, plot types, and emotional arcs can be used as templates to help you construct a data story. While the basic plot types can help to imagine overall stories, emotional arcs can assist in how a story is structured for emotional engagement with the audience.
themes: emotional arcs; plot types; narrative structure
relevance_rationale: Using plot archetypes and emotional arcs for data stories is a narrative craft principle that persists across eras

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: News stories are often a good place to look out for how journalists use different types of characters to create narrative with data: Event as Character, Person as Character, Place as Character. Composite characters help reduce complexity for audiences and can provide a more diverse range of traits, but carry the danger of collapsing differences.
themes: characterisation; narrative technique; composite characters
relevance_rationale: Character types in data narratives (event, person, place) and composite character trade-offs are enduring storytelling concepts

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: All data comes from somewhere. The more you know about the backstory of the data in front of you, the more able you are to tell effective data stories with it. Many data journalists and data visualisers have taken up the practice of keeping a data diary that records where they got their figures and what they did to collect, store, further investigate, or statistically analyse them.
themes: provenance; data diary; transparency; documentation
relevance_rationale: Data provenance tracking via data diaries is even more critical when LLMs can fabricate plausible data lineages

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: When writing nonfiction, conflict needs to be understood loosely with a focus on sparking curiosity, connecting with core values, or igniting compassion from your audience. Narrative tension between familiar entities can help the reader navigate a story and provide background context beyond the content of the data story itself.
chapter: What is Conflict in Data Storytelling?
themes: conflict; narrative tension; curiosity; nonfiction
relevance_rationale: Using narrative tension and conflict in nonfiction data storytelling is a timeless writing craft principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: In order to justify its existence, every passage of description should do two or more of the following: provide background information on context, add further evidence to the main data insight, add depth to the broader context, show something unique about the data, add conflict or complicate the data, provide data backstory. Follow a two-ticks minimum principle.
themes: detail selection; writing craft; Details Tick List
relevance_rationale: The two-ticks minimum principle for description passages is a writing craft heuristic that transcends AI tools
notes: Adapted from Marcy Kennedy 2016

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The challenge is getting the right balance between movement and detail. If a writer gets too caught up in the details of description, the reader can quickly get bored. Too much detail can lose your audience and muddle your main insights. The trick is finding what details to include and when to give description the axe.
themes: detail balance; writing craft; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Balancing movement and detail in narrative pacing is a timeless editorial judgment
notes: Drawing on Flaherty 2009 boat metaphor

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Visualizing Impact developed a brief format that helps everyone involved in a project align on communication objectives, audience, potential stories, and information hierarchy. They also allocated more time to exploratory analysis, where researchers and designers work together to identify potential stories and visual concepts, sharing the analytical and creative load.
themes: brief format; collaboration; exploratory analysis
relevance_rationale: Collaborative briefs aligning stakeholders on objectives still endure, but LLMs can now draft and iterate briefs and exploratory analysis faster

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The role of challenger is integral in our process. A challenger is a person who comes in at various points in the development of a data story to offer a fresh and experienced eye, capturing red flags, gaps, or missed opportunities. Creating space for challengers allows us to access the wealth of experience that might be at the disposal of a significantly larger team.
themes: peer review; challenger role; quality assurance
relevance_rationale: The challenger role for fresh-eye review of data stories is a quality assurance practice that remains essential regardless of AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: By the time we reach the review stage, our focus is on final fact checking and verification of sources. Every visual we publish contains sources in the footer. It is critical to reference our sources directly on the visual to maintain credibility and trust.
themes: fact-checking; source attribution; credibility; trust
relevance_rationale: Final fact-checking, source verification, and crediting sources on published visuals are core accountability practices

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The Bureau set out to count homeless deaths. McClenaghan searched out information on how and when people were dying: sat quietly in funerals, talked to family members, collected coroners reports, travelled to doctors surgeries, shadowed homeless outreach teams, contacted soup kitchens and hostels, and compiled scores of Freedom of Information requests.
themes: data collection; FOI; investigative methods; counting the uncounted
relevance_rationale: Ground-level shoe-leather data gathering through funerals, coroners, FOI requests etc. is irreplaceable human investigative work

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Bureau Local was set up with the belief that regional journalism is a crucial part of a healthy society and that collective reporting produces broader and deeper investigations than would be possible by any individual newsroom. We asked collaborators to submit all their findings through an online form, which we worked to cross-check and verify.
themes: collaborative journalism; crowdsourcing; verification; Bureau Local
relevance_rationale: Collaborative regional journalism networks with cross-verification are organizational models that persist regardless of AI tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Subject matter, source or media location, beliefs and opinions, time and confidence, confidence and skills, and emotions are all factors that affect how people engage with data visualisations. Whether a visualisation is effective depends in large part on how, by whom, when and where it is accessed.
themes: audience engagement; visualisation effectiveness; reception factors
relevance_rationale: Understanding contextual factors affecting audience engagement with visualizations is a user-research insight unchanged by AI
notes: Kennedy et al. 2016 research findings

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: A major finding from our research was the important role that emotions play in peoples engagements with data visualisations. A broad range of emotions emerged including pleasure, anger, sadness, guilt, shame, relief, worry, love, empathy, excitement, offence.
themes: emotion; audience response; visualisation; affect
relevance_rationale: The role of emotions in data visualization engagement is a fundamental human perception insight that transcends technology

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: To become a data storyteller dedicated to transparency and accountability, one must be equipped to ask difficult questions about where data comes from, who can access it, and who can engage with the tools and skills needed to analyse and visualise it.
themes: data divide; access; transparency; accountability
relevance_rationale: Critical questioning of data provenance, access, and power is an ethical foundation that becomes more important with AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The data divide is growing for three reasons: accessing, analysing, and visualising data requires time and resources that smaller organisations lack; sensitive subjects often have no straightforward data source; and there is an asymmetry of data power between those who collect and mine data and those whom data collection targets.
themes: data divide; data literacy; data power; access inequality
relevance_rationale: The data divide in resources, sensitive-data gaps, and power asymmetry remains structural and is not solved by LLMs

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Data literacy researchers argue that we must go beyond just making data public. We need participatory data architectures, infrastructures that can enable people to interpret and apply data in beneficial ways. Better data skills can also help get around barriers by embedding understandings of data types and file compatibility into workplace practice.
themes: open data; data literacy; participatory infrastructure
relevance_rationale: Participatory data architectures and data literacy remain vital, but LLMs now lower some technical barriers to data interpretation

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Complex database systems, jargon heavy taxonomies, PDF locked documents, and scattered datasets can make open data inaccessible, even when it has officially been made open. Supplementary data acquisition skills, such as successful navigation of legal mechanisms like Freedom of Information must also be integrated into training.
themes: open data barriers; FOI; data formats; accessibility
relevance_rationale: PDF extraction and navigating complex databases are now significantly easier with LLM-powered tools, though FOI skills remain essential

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: In efforts to counter the problem of no data, many people turn to participatory data collection. This citizen-generated data can be used as an advocacy tool, putting pressure on organisations and governments to establish better public data protocols, and at times, shape new practices and policies.
themes: citizen-generated data; participatory data collection; advocacy
relevance_rationale: Participatory data collection as advocacy to pressure governments for better data protocols is a civic function unaffected by AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: As data storytellers it is crucial to ask who is counting the uncounted and why. The uncounted are most often children, ethnic and sexual minorities, those living in poverty and those living in urban, overcrowded housing. Being uncounted has effects for political participation, allocation of public resources, and gathering of data needed for evidence-based policy reform.
themes: missing data; data gaps; marginalised populations; ethics
relevance_rationale: Asking who is counted and uncounted is a critical equity question that persists and intensifies with algorithmic systems

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The collection and interpretation of data always rests in human hands. Data becomes biased when researchers fail to account for the way that our assumptions can implicitly shape how we gather and interpret data. Whenever we develop a research question, we do so with certain assumptions.
themes: bias; assumptions; objectivity; data fundamentalism
relevance_rationale: Awareness that human assumptions shape data collection and interpretation is foundational and becomes more urgent with AI-generated analysis

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Standpoint theory asks us to think about how our identities and experiences matter for how we go about gathering, analysing, and storytelling with data. Asking which perspectives are present or absent on a project, and how this may or may not affect the data storytelling process, will lead to stronger stories and more accurate evidence-based knowledge claims.
themes: standpoint theory; positionality; bias reduction; reflexivity
relevance_rationale: Standpoint theory applied to data work is an enduring epistemological framework relevant to any era of tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: In order to prevent data discrimination, greater transparency is clearly needed, though transparency in itself is not enough. What is needed is more empirical research into how discrimination emerges in data, more comprehensive law and policy around data and automation, and further collaboration between decision-makers and citizens.
themes: data discrimination; algorithmic bias; transparency; accountability
relevance_rationale: The call for empirical research, policy, and citizen collaboration to prevent data discrimination persists and is amplified by AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Unlike other forms of discrimination, data discrimination is uniquely dangerous because it is based on automated processes that carry the authoritative veneer of objectivity, making data-driven outcomes appear rational and unchallengeable. Data tools are embedded within processes and technologies that are highly secretive and subtle.
themes: algorithmic bias; automation; objectivity veneer; opacity
relevance_rationale: The unique danger of automated discrimination appearing objective is directly applicable to LLM-era algorithmic systems

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Amnesty Decoders uses micro-tasking to engage digital activists in ground-breaking human rights research. The process involves five steps: 1) Define the task and methodology, 2) Get data, archive and pre-process, 3) Design and develop, 4) Engage volunteers, 5) Verify, analyse, publish. Each task is completed by multiple Decoders as the main verification mechanism.
themes: crowdsourcing; micro-tasking; verification; workflow
relevance_rationale: Micro-tasking with human verification for rights research is a methodology that addresses scale while maintaining human oversight

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Visualisation libraries like D3 and Gephi, as well as software like Tableau, embed 19th century conventions into contemporary design practice. Templates make it increasingly easy to visualise data without needing to participate in other parts of the data process. But templates can limit creative potential and when the same graphic symbols tell every story, the individuality of the data gets lost.
themes: templates; D3; Gephi; Tableau; visual conventions
relevance_rationale: Critique of template-driven tools like D3/Tableau limiting creativity still applies but LLMs now offer new code-generation paths beyond templates

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: We integrate visualisation into the process of our data storytelling. Rather than treat visualisation as a separate design step that comes after all the data analysis is complete, it can function as part of a two-way communication system. Often the most important questions emerge during the process of visualising: What standpoints are being privileged? Who or what is missing?
themes: iterative visualisation; exploratory analysis; reflexivity
relevance_rationale: Integrating visualization into the analysis process rather than treating it as a post-hoc step is an enduring workflow principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: When the process of designing a visualisation is detached from the process of data collection, preparation, and analysis, it can be hard to create a meaningful data story. Understanding the context, backstory, or potential biases of the data you are working with is important for adding meaning.
themes: context; design process; meaning-making
relevance_rationale: Understanding data context and backstory is essential for meaningful visualization regardless of the tool used

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Feminist data visualisation addresses how biases in perception and representation can become embedded into design practice. If data being visualised does not include demographic information about gender, then gender cannot be visualised at all. It was only after we noticed this pattern when reading through recap summaries that we decided to add additional codes to the dataset.
themes: feminist data viz; gender bias; missing variables; coding
relevance_rationale: Feminist data visualization principles about embedded bias in design practice are directly relevant to AI-era data representation
notes: CATO police misconduct example

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: D'Ignazio and Klein offer six core principles of Feminist Data Visualisation: rethink binaries with emphasis on multiplicity; embrace multiple perspectives rather than a single view from nowhere; consider communities not just individual users; recognise knowledge as situated; legitimise embodiment and affect; make the labour of the visualisation process more visible.
themes: feminist data viz; design principles; situated knowledge; multiplicity
relevance_rationale: D'Ignazio and Klein's feminist data visualization principles are enduring epistemological commitments independent of technology
notes: D'Ignazio and Klein 2016

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Three key areas where current data visualisation conventions face their biggest challenges: Exclusivity - people believe dataviz requires advanced skills; Sanitisation and Impersonalisation - templates prioritise formal structure over emotion and empathy; Inability to Capture Complex Personhood - visualisations struggle to account for the idea that people are always beset by contradictions.
themes: visualisation limitations; exclusivity; empathy; complex personhood
relevance_rationale: Exclusivity barrier is lowered by LLMs that can generate visualizations from natural language, though sanitization and complexity concerns endure

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Before you can begin to visualise data, it is often useful to transform it into something more structured. Moving information from a Word document table into a spreadsheet requires careful planning. Each record needs its own row and everything that describes it in columns. Create data validations in each column to generate dropdown lists ensuring consistency.
chapter: A Quick Guide to Structuring Your Data
themes: data structuring; spreadsheets; data validation; data entry
relevance_rationale: Manual data structuring from Word to spreadsheet is now heavily assisted by LLMs that can parse and structure unstructured data

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: One of the biggest challenges when creating or modifying datasets is the tedious task of cleaning data. The more structure you give to your data early on, the easier it makes your life later. This means creating well thought-out spreadsheets in advance, using data validation functions to ensure consistency, and becoming familiar with cleaning software like OpenRefine.
chapter: A Quick Guide to Structuring Your Data
themes: data cleaning; OpenRefine; data structure; planning
relevance_rationale: Data cleaning is now substantially accelerated by LLMs, though the principle of early structure and validation endures

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: As we become more and more reliant on computers to do the work of structuring and visualising data for us, we become less and less mindful of the importance of digging into the data. When we cut and paste from dashboards without understanding how the graphs and numbers got there, we are limiting our capacity to critically question data.
chapter: A Quick Guide to Structuring Your Data
themes: critical data literacy; automation dependence; dashboards
relevance_rationale: Warning against over-reliance on computers without understanding underlying data is even more urgent with LLM-generated dashboards

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Learning how to read denotation, connotation, and myth provides insight into how the simplest of images can be loaded with layers of meaning. Thinking like a mythologist can help improve our decision-making around colour, shape, and emotion. This is important when visually representing sensitive subjects or creating icons that will travel across languages and cultures.
themes: semiotics; visual literacy; connotation; cultural meaning
relevance_rationale: Semiotic analysis of denotation, connotation, and myth in visual design is an enduring analytical framework
notes: Drawing on Barthes

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: We have organised universal design principles into Four Pillars: Symbol, Colour, Caption, and Editorial. Symbols should be resonant (the audience quickly gets what you are trying to represent) and relevant (the right match for your audience). Colours must also be resonant and relevant, with cultural considerations essential.
themes: Four Pillars; symbol design; colour; visual design principles
relevance_rationale: Universal design principles of Symbol, Colour, Caption, and Editorial are enduring visual communication fundamentals

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Pictograms should be developed in accordance with the culture, beliefs, attitude and expectations of the target population. When communicating specific health and medication information with patients, pictograms may be interpreted differently depending on the individual's country of residence, education level, and gender.
themes: pictograms; cultural context; cross-cultural design; health communication
relevance_rationale: Cultural sensitivity in pictogram design for diverse audiences is a human-centered principle unchanged by AI
notes: Citing Barros et al. 2014 and Richler et al. 2012

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Our interpretations of a color's meaning are highly influenced by culture. For clear communication and unambiguous information delivery, it is essential to understand the needs, customs, and attitudes of the end user. Investigations into cultural perceptions of color are essential whenever designing for a new audience, market or global campaign.
themes: colour; cultural context; audience; visual design
relevance_rationale: Understanding cultural color perceptions when designing for diverse audiences is an enduring design principle
notes: Citing Visocky O'Grady 2008

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: I don't attempt to be objective in the representations I make. What I do is, I make a representation of events that's consistent with the facts, but I make it as emotionally engaging as possible to the reader. Evans pushes back against claims to objectivity that rely on a non-locatable standpoint.
themes: objectivity; emotion; situated perspective; visual storytelling
relevance_rationale: Editorial transparency about positionality while remaining factual is an enduring journalism ethic

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Data Justice Lab used a multi-methods approach: scraping government websites, filing freedom of information requests, holding workshops to enable discussions between people across sectors, and interviews with practitioners and civil society organizations. This enabled first systematic mapping of data analytics systems across local authorities in the UK.
themes: multi-methods; FOI; web scraping; participatory research
relevance_rationale: Web scraping and FOI methods endure, but LLMs now assist with scraping code generation and data processing at scale

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Forensic Architecture combines digital technologies with investigative journalism to produce detailed inquiries into incidents of human rights violations. They cross-referenced satellite imagery with first-hand testimony from survivors and witnesses to produce a timeline of events. Data is not only visualized, but used as evidence in legal cases to establish culpability.
themes: cross-referencing; satellite imagery; testimony; forensic methods
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing satellite imagery with testimony for human rights investigations is a forensic methodology that endures

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The datasets needed for housing justice work are difficult to integrate, aggregate, and cross-reference. The poor state of data on housing makes evidence-based analysis and advocacy challenging. Key insights included: Lack of standardisation in recordings of data; Cleanliness of data does not correlate to usefulness; Classifying data by relevance to stories is more beneficial than dismissing data as dirty.
themes: data integration; dirty data; standardisation; housing data
relevance_rationale: Data integration and cross-referencing challenges persist but LLMs can now assist with standardization and entity resolution across messy datasets

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: How can data journalism projects tell stories about big issues at scale while also affirming the provisionality and acknowledging the models, assumptions and uncertainty involved in the production of numbers? How can data journalism support marginalised communities to use data to tell their own stories on their own terms, rather than telling their stories for them?
themes: critical data practice; uncertainty; community voice; scale
relevance_rationale: Questions about scale, provisionality, and community agency in data journalism are enduring epistemological challenges
notes: Gray and Bounegru twelve challenges

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Linking data together helps generate data literacy, open information, and combat the fragmentation and lack of accountability that results from scattered data. The prototype focused on individual councils organised around different types of data that emerge throughout the regeneration process, reflecting existing ways that organisations use data in research and advocacy.
themes: participatory data infrastructure; data linking; advocacy
relevance_rationale: Linking fragmented data for accountability and literacy is an organizational infrastructure challenge unchanged by AI

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: It was the process of visually organising this information that revealed the data stories that mattered. Reality is simply too messy and too complex to capture. But the process of visualising entanglement helped to highlight which things were most entangled and to identify dependencies in ways that were both generative and constraining.
themes: visual analysis; complexity; entanglement; emergent insight
relevance_rationale: Visual organization of information as a method for discovering stories is an enduring analytical insight
notes: Hodder 2012 tanglegram example

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: When selecting colours, keep in mind how contrast can be used to highlight significance or show difference. Contrast can be created through your choices of colour hue (type of colour), colour value (lightness or darkness of a colour), and colour saturation (purity of a colour).
themes: colour design; visual contrast; chart design
relevance_rationale: Color theory principles of hue, value, and saturation for highlighting significance are enduring design fundamentals

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: If you want a particular category standout as the subject of your data story, you may choose to create a contrast in its saturation or value. This is particularly useful when the point you are trying to make is not that this category is the largest or smallest, but where it falls in relation to the others.
themes: colour design; visual emphasis; narrative framing
relevance_rationale: Using saturation contrast to show relative position rather than magnitude is an enduring design technique

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: You may also want to use contrast to highlight variables in your data if you are trying to point out a pattern that is not necessarily going to stand out on its own. We wanted to highlight the pattern we found around gendered violence and violence against women. This led us to make the decision to use a bold colour only for those categories that related to women and gendered violence, colouring the other categories in a dull grey.
themes: colour design; pattern highlighting; visual emphasis; editorial decisions
relevance_rationale: Strategic color choices to highlight editorial emphasis like gendered violence patterns is enduring visual rhetoric

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Taking colour blindness and aging into account, the American Disability Act suggests that best practice is to provide a 70 percent contrast between type and background. Visocky O'Grady suggest making sure that the contrast you select works well in greyscale and avoids eye strain.
themes: accessibility; colour design; inclusive design
relevance_rationale: Accessibility standards for color contrast and color blindness are enduring inclusive design requirements

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Colour metaphors refer to the use of different colours that carry particular meanings or connotations in a shared cultural context (i.e. green for go, red for stop). A colour gradient refers to a range of colour transitions that is often gradual and blends together as it progresses. When you have ordinal data, a colour metaphor may be useful for telling your data story. For interval data, colour gradients are a common choice to represent change, rankings, or intensity.
themes: colour design; data types; visual encoding; metaphor
relevance_rationale: Color metaphors and gradients for ordinal vs interval data are enduring visualization grammar principles

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Arcia and her team found that colour analogies worked well when paired with a human figure to help represent gradations of scale. While it comes with additional costs, working directly with target audience communities through participatory infographic design is the best way to create graphics that can enhance health literacy.
themes: participatory design; audience research; health communication; cultural relevance
relevance_rationale: Participatory infographic design with target communities for health literacy is an enduring co-design principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: When colour was attributed to symbols that were not metaphorically resonant, the image failed to make a connection. Colour scales also failed to connect with users when the colour metaphor was unfamiliar. These findings further support other researchers around the importance of the cultural relevance.
themes: cultural relevance; visual encoding; audience comprehension; metaphor
relevance_rationale: Cultural resonance of color-symbol pairings in data visualization is an enduring perception research finding

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Andy Kirk addresses colour blindness design: using red/green variations, opting for a non red-green variation, using a second encoding such as shapes or text to help with comprehension, or (when budget allows) offering two versions of a colour scale for the user to choose between.
themes: accessibility; colour design; inclusive design; redundant encoding
relevance_rationale: Accessible color design strategies for color blindness are enduring inclusive design requirements

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: As the same visual image can mean different things to different people, we need to guide viewers to the intended message of our data story—our Think, Feel, and Do. This means that we need to limit the possible meanings a visual image can have. Captions work to guide audiences toward your Think, Feel, and Do. By crafting carefully worded text that anchors the images in your data story, you can stop viewers' interpretations drifting away from your intended messages.
themes: captions; audience guidance; polysemy; narrative framing
relevance_rationale: Using captions to anchor visual meaning and guide audience interpretation is an enduring editorial technique

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: When creating your captions think about the other possible interpretations of your visual images—including charts, graphs and other illustrated elements of your data story. Then brainstorm how best to use captions to steer the viewer along the narrative of your data story. Captions can also be used to highlight details and spotlight elements essential to your data story.
themes: captions; editorial decisions; narrative framing
relevance_rationale: Brainstorming alternative interpretations of visuals and using captions to steer narrative is enduring editorial practice

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Editorial layout refers to how the different visual elements of your data story are strategically arranged onto a page, screen, poster or whatever platform you are using in order to effectively and efficiently reach your target audience. The layout you choose should work well to get across your intended message—the Think, Feel, Do of your data story.
themes: editorial layout; platform design; audience targeting
relevance_rationale: Editorial layout strategy for reaching target audiences is an enduring design planning principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Layout should come at the start of the planning process. Before you even begin to select symbols, fonts or chart types, ask: What different screen sizes does this layout need to work for? Today many design experts suggest that you begin by planning for the smallest display size and then increase the scale and level of detail for larger versions. This is what people working in user experience and web design refer to as optimisation.
themes: editorial layout; responsive design; planning; mobile-first
relevance_rationale: Mobile-first responsive design planning is an enduring UX principle that transcends AI tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Working with restricted layout templates can help guide your design process. The '6 card design' challenge gives each group a set of index cards and the brief that they must design a 6 element visual data story that can work on a smartphone screen (either by swiping through the story or as stand-alone mini-narratives), and be fitted together to form a printable poster.
themes: design constraints; modular design; teaching method; responsive design
relevance_rationale: Constrained layout exercises like the 6-card design challenge are enduring pedagogical techniques for design thinking

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: While these design principles are a good starting place, it is also important to embrace the experimental and break out of these proscriptive structures. A spin on this same activity asks participants to take their modular 6 card design and then redesign it, throwing out all the rules of grids and order to see what else they can create.
themes: design experimentation; creative process; teaching method
relevance_rationale: Embracing experimentation and breaking design rules is an enduring creative principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Segel and Heer found that there were seven commonly employed genres of narrative visualisation: magazine style, annotated chart, partitioned poster, flow chart, comic strip, slide show, and film/video/animation. These genres can be mixed. What matters is how each of these different styles of layout supports some narrative structures, while limiting others.
themes: narrative visualisation; genre; editorial layout; chart selection
relevance_rationale: Segel and Heer's seven genres of narrative visualization are an enduring taxonomy for data storytelling formats

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Segel and Heer also found across their research sample an 'under-utilization of common narrative messaging techniques such as repetition of key points, introductory texts, and final summaries and syntheses.'
themes: narrative technique; data storytelling; editorial layout
relevance_rationale: The research finding of underutilized narrative techniques in data viz remains relevant regardless of production tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: A final important part of creating effective and efficient designs is to avoid unnecessary features. Making good editorial decisions requires learning how to differentiate between functional flourishes and ornamental embellishments.
themes: chartjunk; editorial decisions; visual simplicity
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing functional elements from ornamental embellishments is an enduring design editing principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: If a grid is needed in order to follow how information is plotted on a graph, then the lines should be muted, ideally in a very pale grey. It is useful to always ask oneself if gridlines are necessary. If so, can they be further faded into the background? This principle can also be applied to extra numbers or variables in a graph. If there seems to be too many interval figures, consider what might be removed or condensed without skewing the information you want to present.
themes: chartjunk; gridlines; visual simplicity; chart design
relevance_rationale: Guidelines for muting gridlines and reducing visual clutter are enduring visualization best practices

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Embellishments can at times, when properly chosen and designed, represent information redundantly in useful ways, and even when they aren't information in and of themselves, can meaningfully support the display of information. Few notes the potential effectiveness of properly designed embellishments that work memorably or metaphorically, immersing readers in the data presented.
themes: chartjunk; embellishment; memorability; visual design
relevance_rationale: The nuanced case for well-designed embellishments that aid memorability is an enduring design research finding
notes: Stephen Few's counter-argument to Tufte

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Used as a tool for visually editing one's work, 'chartjunk' can help differentiate between those visual elements that are needed to tell your data story, and those that are embellishments. But eliminating chartjunk does not have to mean excluding illustrative elements from your visual data stories. Introducing extra information to a graph or chart can also, at times, help create context and humanise data.
themes: chartjunk; humanising data; editorial decisions; visual editing
relevance_rationale: Using chartjunk as an analytical lens while recognizing illustrative elements can add context is enduring design wisdom

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: If you are working with a low data literacy audience, you may want to select a basic chart that is more familiar to lay readers, or that incorporates symbols rather than just numbers. Understanding the principles of good visual design—and the seductive allure of fancy software—can help you engage with charts more confidently, expanding your literacy of their potentials and their pitfalls.
themes: audience literacy; chart selection; data literacy; visual design
relevance_rationale: Matching chart complexity to audience data literacy and resisting software-driven complexity is enduring editorial judgment

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Andy Kirk's CHRTS provides a taxonomy for thinking about what kind of chart is best for your data: C for Categorical (comparing categories and distributions), H for Hierarchical (part-to-whole relationships), R for Relational (correlations and connections), T for Temporal (trends over time), S for Spatial (mapping spatial patterns). Within each cluster the decision of what particular graph to use will often depend on the number of variables you have in the data you are trying to visually represent.
themes: chart selection; taxonomy; data types; visual encoding
relevance_rationale: Kirk's CHRTS taxonomy for chart selection based on data type is an enduring visualization grammar framework

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The samples in these datasets are not the same, so you cannot combine them together directly. Each one can inform your story, but any visualisation must account for the different context and backstory of each dataset. Depending on what data stories you want to tell, different aspects of these datasets can be visualised using a wide variety of graphs.
themes: data provenance; dataset context; methodological rigour
relevance_rationale: Warning against combining incompatible datasets in visualizations is an enduring data integrity principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Tree structures are effective for two reasons. First, they use the symbol of a tree to help the viewer quickly grasp the concept. Second, they represent the information in a hierarchical format, providing the viewer with an ordering system that shows how different pieces of information relate to each other.
themes: tree diagrams; hierarchical data; visual metaphor; chart selection
relevance_rationale: Tree structure effectiveness through metaphor and hierarchy is an enduring information design principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Clarity was the most important consideration in the design of this RiotID guide. The team knew that the infographic would be translated into multiple languages and used by people from a range of cultures, with varying technical literacy. Faced with these challenges, the designers decided to use a tree diagram.
themes: clarity; cross-cultural design; audience analysis; chart selection
relevance_rationale: Prioritizing clarity for multilingual, cross-cultural, variable-literacy audiences is an enduring design principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Treemaps display information hierarchies by representing data categories as nested rectangles. The area size of the rectangle is generally proportional to its quantity or value. Treemaps are space-constrained, which means that they are more compact than other visualisations. Treemaps are a good way to display a large quantity of information. They are especially useful if you want to show how a system or entity is divided.
themes: treemaps; hierarchical data; chart selection; compact design
relevance_rationale: Treemap design principles for hierarchical data display are enduring visualization grammar

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: By hovering over the treemap's rectangles, the user can view more specific information about that data point. While the treemap lets the user quickly and easily grasp the basic contours of information, the interactive functions make the level of detail scalable according to the user's needs. These features make treemaps powerful narrative tools for the data storyteller.
themes: interactivity; treemaps; scalable detail; user experience
relevance_rationale: Interactive treemap features for scalable detail are enduring interaction design principles

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Network graphs are used to illuminate associations or relationships between data points. In a network graph, relationships are displayed as nodes connected by links. The thickness of the links can indicate the strength of an association between nodes. Variables such as node size and link thickness can be powerful indicators of information; the position of nodes, their colour, and whether or not the edges are directional are other important ways that researchers can use network graphs to construct meaning.
themes: network graphs; relational data; visual encoding; social network analysis
relevance_rationale: Network graph design principles using nodes, links, and visual encodings are enduring visualization grammar

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: We call this 'interviewing your dataset.' The same questions police officers would ask a suspect in person, they can ask of their dataset: Who are the individuals most linked together in the network? How are they linked? Who may be vulnerable? Who is a gatekeeper?
themes: data interrogation; analytical questions; network analysis; investigative method
relevance_rationale: Interviewing your dataset through investigative questions is an enduring analytical mindset regardless of tools

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Investigative journalists, NGOs, think tanks, investors, activist groups, and academic researchers commonly use 'bottom-up' social network analysis. Software tools like Oligrapher and Graph Commons offer easy to use platforms that do not require coding and can be used for researching, recording and visualising networks.
themes: network analysis; social network analysis; no-code tools
relevance_rationale: Social network analysis tools like Oligrapher and Graph Commons are now complemented by LLM-assisted network construction and analysis
notes: Also mentions Kumu

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Bounegru and her colleagues point out that the text accompanying the graphs—headlines, labels, captions, and articles—are a key part of constructing narrative meaning. These pieces of written information help frame the data story and cue the reader toward what to look for and pay attention to. Accompanying text can also help make network graphs more accessible to less statistically minded readers.
themes: narrative framing; captions; network graphs; accessibility
relevance_rationale: The role of accompanying text in framing network graph narratives is an enduring editorial design principle

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: When network graphs are used to analyse very large data sets, they are often presented in an interactive format that allows the user to manipulate the graphical display, zoom in on individual nodes, or otherwise 'move' through the visualisation. Data visualisers can also introduce a temporal dimension to a network graph in order to show how associations change over time. Gephi and Cytoscape are two open-source programs that are useful for visualising these kinds of larger or more complex networks.
themes: network analysis; interactivity; temporal data; open-source tools
relevance_rationale: Interactive network visualization tools like Gephi and Cytoscape remain relevant but LLMs can now generate network visualizations and assist with analysis code

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: While timelines provide an excellent means of reducing complex information and stripping down details into easy to process chunks, there are also limits to what these linear representations can do. Timelines are full of absences. Deciding what events, people, or moments in time to include on a chronological map involves a process of elevating some things, while eliminating others. What gets left off the timeline becomes visually absent from the history being presented.
themes: timelines; absence; editorial decisions; selection bias
relevance_rationale: Critical awareness of timeline absences and editorial selection bias is an enduring epistemological concern

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Timelines force data storytellers to make difficult decisions about what data points should be emphasised and why. Having a clear approach to how you are making your selections can help negotiate biases and standpoints.
themes: timelines; editorial decisions; selection criteria; bias
relevance_rationale: Transparent selection criteria for timeline data points to negotiate biases is enduring editorial discipline

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Linear, chronological representations generally can only depict forward movement. It is difficult to use them to compare and contrast information or accounts of events, to show multiple perspectives, or tell stories about entanglements or networks. For this reason, timelines often work well as an accompaniment to other forms of data storytelling.
themes: timelines; limitations; multi-format storytelling
relevance_rationale: Limitations of linear chronological representations for complex, multi-perspective stories are enduring design constraints

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: The visual presentation of life courses is often disconnected from the underlying data, leading to a separation of evidence-based information and personal narratives. From a data storytelling perspective, bringing together broader, quantitative or statistical data with personal stories can maximise the impacts of evidence and interventions by displaying data points chronologically, situated in the larger context of what was going on in someone's life.
themes: life course; personal narrative; mixed methods; humanising data
relevance_rationale: Integrating quantitative data with personal life narratives for evidence-based storytelling is an enduring approach

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: If you work with data over a long enough period, it's inevitable that you will come across data sets that contain missing values. The task of the data storyteller is not to throw up her hands and say 'Oh well' to missing data, but to find ways to include it in the narratives she tells.
themes: missing data; data quality; narrative responsibility
relevance_rationale: The imperative to narratively account for missing data rather than ignore it is enduring data ethics

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Andy Kirk outlines three different ways that 'nothing' can appear in data. First, data sets often contain zero values—the data is available, it's just a zero amount. Second, cases where parts of the data simply aren't available—the data isn't zero, it's non-existent or 'null.' Third, blank spaces can be deliberately introduced into a data visualisation in order to tell a more compelling story. Blank spaces can be used to heighten tension, call attention to an issue, or to show gaps between two or more amounts.
themes: missing data; null values; zero values; visual absence
relevance_rationale: Kirk's taxonomy of nothing in data (zero, null, deliberate blank) is an enduring analytical framework for data storytelling

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Measures of gender inequality, such as wage gap data, don't tell the whole story of women's lives if data about, say, Indigenous women's experiences are missing. We don't have a good sense of what is happening at the community level because there are few reliable, consistent or public data sources available for neighbourhoods or municipalities which disaggregate the population by gender identity, disability, racialized group or Indigenous status.
themes: missing data; disaggregation; equity; data gaps; intersectionality
relevance_rationale: Intersectional data gaps where marginalized groups are missing from datasets is an enduring equity concern
notes: Katherine Scott quote

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Comics are: approachable, accessible, and relatable. Comics combine words and images, using sequential story panels to take the reader through the narrative. Making use of panels can help break complex processes into less complex units, helping guide the reader through transitions. Panels might be used in a data-driven comic story to move from detail to broader context, or as a way of drilling-down from broader picture to smaller detail.
themes: comics; sequential art; accessibility; data storytelling
relevance_rationale: Comics as approachable, sequential data storytelling format is an enduring presentation method

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: In data comics, this layered storytelling style can help capture the uncertainty of data—both as a claim to truth, as well as in relation to the experience or issue it captures. The plurality of meaning in comics can work to convey a diversity of experiences, as promoted by feminist designers calling for a more humanising data visualisation.
themes: uncertainty; data comics; feminist design; humanising data
relevance_rationale: Data comics capturing uncertainty and plurality of meaning is an enduring storytelling philosophy

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Graphic medicine works reclaim the human side of health experiences from the clinical lexicon upheld by healthcare systems. Including quantitative data in graphic medicine often is a way of connecting the clinical definition of what the artist is experiencing and juxtaposing it to the actual lived experience with the illness or disability.
themes: graphic medicine; humanising data; health communication; lived experience
relevance_rationale: Graphic medicine reclaiming human experience from clinical data is an enduring approach to humanizing quantitative information

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: With its greater emphasis on narrative, structural flow, and the use of iconography to capture sensitive subjects, graphic social science can enhance the ways that we communicate quantitative data and embed this data in human stories. As numbers are given narratives, stats come to tell complicated—and contested—stories.
themes: graphic social science; humanising data; narrative; contested knowledge
relevance_rationale: Graphic social science embedding quantitative data in human narratives is an enduring storytelling approach

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: Multisensory approaches are concerned with bringing new insights to traditional research practices, which often assume a singular, universal subject position. Rather than assume that everyone experiences and inhabits the world in the same ways, multisensory methods are reflexive and experimental; they stress that the creation and representation of knowledge is shaped by the experiences and position of the person doing, or participating in, the research.
themes: multisensory methods; positionality; reflexivity; knowledge production
relevance_rationale: Multisensory research methods challenging universal subject positions are enduring epistemological commitments

author: Anna Feigenbaum, Aria Alamalhodaei
extract: A simple way to introduce multisensory experience into your data story is by incorporating interactivity: inviting the user to play or manipulate with the data in real time. This allows the user to compare different trends in data, explore how variables interact, or simply allow for free and open exploration.
