themes: scientific-method; methodology; rigour
relevance_rationale: Need for systematic methods in journalism is amplified not diminished by AI
notes: Paraphrasing Phil Meyer

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The predominant way that data journalism gets done in a newsroom is through simple tools like Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets. Those simple tools, on their own, lack significant logging functions that automatically keep track of steps a data journalist took to reach a given conclusion. That means journalists using those tools have to maintain separate, detailed logs of what they did so any analysis can be replicated.
chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
themes: reproducibility; tooling; workflow; documentation
relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet logging limitations are partly solved by LLM-assisted notebook workflows that auto-document steps

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: If the AP's replication rules are to be followed, journalism needs to become much more serious about the tools and techniques used to do data journalism. The days of 'point and click' tools to do 'quick and dirty' analysis that get published are dying. The days of formal methods using documented steps are here.
chapter: Data journalism in the age of replication
themes: reproducibility; rigour; methodology; tooling
relevance_rationale: Formal documented methods remain vital but LLMs can now generate documentation from point-and-click workflows

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The Stylebook says at a minimum, today's data journalists should keep a log that details: The source of the data, making sure to work on a copy of the data and not the original file. Data dictionaries or any other supporting documentation of the data. Description of all steps required to transform the data and perform the analysis.
themes: reproducibility; documentation; data-management; methodology
relevance_rationale: Logging standards endure but LLMs can auto-generate much of the documentation described here
notes: AP Stylebook minimum standards

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: An increasing number of data journalists are switching to tools like analysis notebooks, which use languages like Python and R, to document their work. The notebooks, generally speaking, allow a data journalist to mix code and explanation in the same document. Combined with online sharing tools like GitHub, analysis notebooks seem to solve the problem of replication.
themes: reproducibility; tooling; documentation; open-source
relevance_rationale: Notebooks remain relevant but LLM coding assistants now offer an additional path to documented, reproducible work

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Why should someone reading one of my articles believe my conclusions, if I don't provide the evidence that explains how I reached them? Simply saying what you've done is not as powerful as providing the means for others to repeat and build on your work.
themes: transparency; reproducibility; trust; verification
relevance_rationale: Transparency and evidence-sharing as trust-building principles are tool-independent
notes: Quoting Peter Aldous, BuzzFeed

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: If at all possible, an editor or another reporter should attempt to reproduce the results of the analysis and confirm all findings before publication.
themes: reproducibility; verification; editorial-process; quality-control
relevance_rationale: Internal replication is still vital but LLMs can now serve as a second pair of eyes for under-resourced newsrooms
notes: AP Stylebook internal replication standard

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Journalism, unlike science, has no history of replication. There is no scientific method for stories. There is no standard research methods class taught at every journalism school. And journalism school isn't a requirement to get into the news business. In other words, journalism lacks the standards other disciplines have.
themes: reproducibility; methodology; training; rigour
relevance_rationale: Journalism still lacks systematic replication standards and LLMs do not change this structural deficit

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Except for the largest newsrooms, most news organizations lack editors who could replicate the analysis. Many don't have a second person who would know what to do. Not having a second set of eyes in a newsroom is a problem. Having a data journalism team is an incredible luxury.
themes: reproducibility; newsroom-capacity; verification; staffing
relevance_rationale: Staffing gaps for replication persist but LLMs can partially fill the role of a second analyst
notes: Quoting Troy Thibodeaux, AP

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The more critical the analysis is to the story, and the more complex that analysis is, that's going to push the bar on what is a sufficient methodology statement. And it could end up being a whole code repo in order to just say, this isn't black magic, here's how we got it if you're so interested.
themes: reproducibility; transparency; methodology; documentation
relevance_rationale: Proportional transparency still applies but code repos can now be LLM-generated with better documentation

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Replication requires two things from you: What and why. What is this piece of code doing, and why are you doing that here and now? That you can copy and paste code from this book or the internet is not impressive. What is necessary for learning is that you know what a piece of code is doing and why you want to do that thing here.
themes: reproducibility; documentation; literacy; methodology
relevance_rationale: Understanding what code does and why remains critical even when LLMs generate the code

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Replication is a theoretical standard, not a requirement that every investigator duplicate his or her own work for every project. Giving enough information in the report to enable another investigator to follow in your footsteps is enough. Just telling enough to make replication possible will build confidence.
themes: reproducibility; transparency; methodology; trust
relevance_rationale: Replication as theoretical standard with sufficient documentation is tool-agnostic
notes: Quoting Phil Meyer

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: One of the critical components of data analysis, especially for beginners, is having a mental picture of your data. What does each row represent? What does each column in each row signify? How many rows do you have? How many columns?
themes: data-literacy; data-quality; methodology
relevance_rationale: Mental model of data structure remains essential even when LLMs can describe datasets

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Is a zip code a number? When we think of data types, numbers are things we do math on. If the thing you want is not something you're going to do math on -- can you add two phone numbers together? -- then make it a character type. If you don't, most every software system on the planet will drop leading zeros.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: A massive amount of data analysis involves grouping like things together and then doing simple things like counting them, or averaging them together. The pattern you'll use many times is: data, then group_by a column, then summarize with an aggregate function.
themes: aggregation; methodology; data-literacy
relevance_rationale: Group-by-summarize pattern endures but LLMs can now generate these aggregations from natural language

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The mean loan amount is much higher than the median loan amount in every county. There are some very large loans skewing the mean higher. source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: You should never assume that missing values won't appear in a dataset. When classifying data with case_when or if_else, always account for blank or NA values as a separate category rather than letting them fall silently into another group.

relevance_rationale: Defensive handling of missing values is a universal data principle that LLMs may overlook if not prompted

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: One of the most frustrating things in data is working with dates. Everyone has a different opinion on how to record them, and every software package on the planet has to sort it out. Consider using a dedicated date library (lubridate) to solve common problems before they start.
themes: date parsing; data types; lubridate
relevance_rationale: Date parsing frustrations are substantially reduced by LLMs that can infer and convert date formats

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Filtering creates a subset of the data based on criteria. All records where the amount is greater than 150,000. All records that match 'College Park.' Something like that. Filtering works with rows -- when we filter, we get fewer rows back than we start with. Selecting simply returns only the fields named. So if you only want to see city and amount, you select those fields. Selecting works with columns. You will have the same number of records when you are done, but fewer columns of data to work with.
themes: filtering; subsetting; column selection; dplyr
relevance_rationale: Filter vs select concepts endure but LLMs can now execute these operations from plain-language requests

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: A general tip about using filter: it's easier to work your way towards the filter syntax you need rather than try and write it once and trust the result. Each time you modify your filter, check the results to see if they make sense. This adds a little time to your process but you'll thank yourself for doing it because it helps avoid mistakes.
themes: filtering; iterative refinement; error prevention; verification
relevance_rationale: Iterative filter refinement with verification is a thinking discipline that applies even with LLM assistance

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Any time you are given a dataset from anyone, you should immediately be suspicious. Is this data what I think it is? Does it include what I expect? Is there anything I need to know about it? Will it produce the information I expect? One of the first things you should do is give it the smell test. Failure to give data the smell test can lead you to miss stories and get your butt kicked on a competitive story.
chapter: Data Cleaning Part I: Data smells
themes: data skepticism; verification; data quality; smell test
relevance_rationale: Data skepticism and the smell test are fundamental journalistic instincts no AI replaces

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Some common data smells are: Missing data or missing values, Gaps in data, Wrong type of data, Outliers, Sharp curves, Conflicting information within a dataset, Conflicting information across datasets, Wrongly derived data, Internal inconsistency, External inconsistency, Wrong spatial data, Unusable data, including non-standard abbreviations, ambiguous data, extraneous data, inconsistent data.
chapter: Data Cleaning Part I: Data smells
themes: data smells; data quality checklist; error detection
relevance_rationale: Data smell checklist endures but LLMs can now automate detection of many of these issues

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Not all of these data smells are detectable in code. You may have to ask people about the data. You may have to compare it to another dataset yourself. Does the agency that uses the data produce reports from the data? Does your analysis match those reports? That will expose wrongly derived data, or wrong units, or mistakes you made with inclusion or exclusion.
chapter: Data Cleaning Part I: Data smells
themes: human verification; cross-referencing; data custodian; institutional knowledge
relevance_rationale: Human verification through source calls and cross-referencing cannot be automated by LLMs

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: We're also going to introduce a new argument to our function that reads in the data, read_csv(), called 'guess_max.' As R reads in the csv file, it will attempt to make some calls on what 'data type' to assign to each field: number, character, date, and so on. The 'guess_max' argument says: look at the values in the whatever number of rows we specify before deciding which data type to assign.
themes: data types; type inference; readr; guess_max; R
relevance_rationale: Type inference control is R-specific; LLMs can now handle type detection and conversion more fluidly
notes: R/tidyverse-specific but the principle of controlling type inference applies broadly

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Things that should be characters -- like state, city, name -- are characters (chr). Things that should be numbers (dbl) -- like amount -- are numbers. Date columns -- like date_approved -- are stored as dates. The id column is a good example. It read in as a number (dbl), which makes sense, because it really is just a string of numbers. But we'd never need to do math on these values; it wouldn't make sense to add two ids together. So it is probably best stored as a character. The opposite would be more problematic. If something that should be stored as a number we want to do math on was stored as a character, we couldn't actually use it to do math.
themes: data types; semantic correctness; identifiers vs numbers
relevance_rationale: Understanding semantic correctness of data types requires human judgment about what numbers mean

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Do the 135,073 NAs represent loans that weren't forgiven? Do they represent loans that might have been forgiven, but the data is simply missing the amount of money? Before we decide to base a publishable finding on this column, we should call the custodian of the data to confirm our hypothesis of NA values in these columns, that they represent loans that have not been forgiven.
themes: missing data; NA values; data custodian; confirmation; documentation
relevance_rationale: Confirming meaning of NA values with data custodians is human reporting work LLMs cannot do

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Those are all the same value, so at least this number is internally consistent. But is it correct? Did someone mistype it when entering the data? A call to the bank -- Bank of America in both cases -- or the companies could help resolve this mystery. Or a call to the SBA -- the owner of the data -- to ask about small values generally may help us understand if small values like this are suspicious or not.

relevance_rationale: Internal consistency does not prove correctness; calling sources to verify remains irreplaceable

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Cleaning data is the process of fixing issues in your data so you can answer the questions you want to answer. Data cleaning is a critical step that you can't skip past. A standard metric is that 80 percent of the time working with data will be spent cleaning and verifying data, and 20 percent the more exciting parts like analysis and visualization.
themes: data cleaning; time allocation; 80/20 rule; workflow
relevance_rationale: The 80/20 cleaning-to-analysis ratio is shifting as LLMs dramatically accelerate data cleaning tasks

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Every developer, every data system, every agency, the all have opinions about how data gets collected. Some decisions make sense from the outside. Some decisions are based entirely on internal politics: who is creating the data, how they are creating it, why they are creating it. Is it automated? Is it manual? Are data normalized? Are there free form fields where users can just type into or does the system restrict them to choices? Your journalistic questions -- what you want the data to tell you -- is almost never part of that equation.
themes: data provenance; institutional context; data collection; journalist vs data producer
relevance_rationale: Understanding data provenance and institutional context of collection is human interpretive work

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: One of the most difficult problems to fix in data is duplicate records in the data. They can creep in with bad joins, bad data entry practices, mistakes -- all kinds of reasons. A duplicated record isn't always there because of an error, but you need to know if it's there before making that determination.
themes: duplicates; deduplication; data quality
relevance_rationale: Duplicate detection endures as a concern but LLMs can now identify and flag duplicates more efficiently

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide

A good rule of thumb is that you should only spend time cleaning fields that are critical to the specific analysis you want to do.

relevance_rationale: Question-driven scoping of cleaning work is editorial judgment that LLMs support but do not replace

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide

author: Rob Wells

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Often, as data journalists, we're looking at data across time or at data stored in multiple tables. And to do that, we need to often need to merge that data together. Depending on what we have, we may just need to stack data on top of each other to make new data. If we have 2019 data and 2018 data and we want that to be one file, we stack them. If we have a dataset of cows in counties and a dataset of populations in county, we're going to join those two together on the county -- the common element.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Sometimes, governments put data online on a page or in a searchable database. And when you ask them for a copy of the data underneath the website, they say no. Why? They say they don't have to give you the data because they've already given you the data, never mind that they haven't given to you in a form you can actually load into R with ease. Lucky for us, there's a way for us to write code to get data even when an agency hasn't made it easy: webscraping.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Why would we ever write code to grab a single table? There's several reasons: 1. Our methods are transparent. If a colleague wants to run our code from scratch to factcheck our work, they don't need to repeat the manual steps, which are harder to document than writing code. 2. Let's suppose we wanted to grab the same table every day, to monitor for changes. Writing a script once, and pressing a single button every day is going to be much more efficient than doing this manually every day. 3. If we're doing it manually, we're more likely to make a mistake, like maybe failing to copy every row from the whole table. 4. It's good practice to prepare us to do more complex scraping jobs.
themes: reproducibility; transparency; automation; web scraping; error reduction

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The degree of difficulty here goes from 'Easy' to 'So Hard You Want To Throw Your Laptop Out A Window.' And the curve between the two can be steep. You can learn how to scrape 'Easy' in a day. The hard ones take a little more time, but it's often well worth the effort because it lets you get stories you couldn't get without it.
themes: web scraping; difficulty curve; skill investment; data access
relevance_rationale: Web scraping difficulty curve is flattening as LLMs can generate scraping code from page descriptions

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: In programming, there's a principle called 'DRY' which stands for 'Don't Repeat Yourself.' If you find yourself copying the same code over and over again, with minor changes, it's better to find a way to avoid that.
themes: DRY principle; code efficiency; for loops; iteration; programming
relevance_rationale: DRY principle endures but LLMs generate functions and loops from natural language, lowering the barrier

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The bad news is that it's more complicated because it's more like survey data with a large sample. That means there's margins of error and confidence intervals to worry about.
themes: ACS; margin of error; confidence intervals; survey data; Census
relevance_rationale: Understanding margin of error in survey data like ACS is statistical literacy that persists
notes: ACS estimates require attention to uncertainty, unlike decennial census hard counts

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: by examining the number of hospital beds per 100,000 people in each county, what regional geographic patterns can we identify? ... Each hospital exists inside of a county, so we're going to first calculate the total number of beds in each county.
themes: per-capita normalisation; geographic analysis; aggregation
relevance_rationale: Per-capita geographic analysis endures conceptually but LLMs can now generate the normalization and mapping code
notes: Demonstrates the standard move of normalising raw counts by population to make geographic comparisons meaningful

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: But there's a problem. The column in our population data that has county names is called 'NAME,' and it has the full name of the county spelled out in title case ... If they're going to join properly, we need to clean one of them up to make it match the other.
themes: data cleaning; join preparation; string normalisation
relevance_rationale: String matching for joins across datasets is increasingly handled by LLM fuzzy-matching capabilities
notes: Key pre-join cleaning pattern: standardise case, remove extraneous text, trim whitespace so keys match across datasets

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: We have two NAs after we join, for Queen Anne's County and Caroline County. That's not an error. There are no General Acute Care hospitals in those counties, according to our data ... So let's convert those values to 0 using replace_na().
themes: missing data; join diagnostics; NA handling
relevance_rationale: Understanding that NAs after joins may reflect real-world absence not errors requires domain judgment
notes: Post-join verification: explain why NAs appear and decide whether they represent missing data or true zeros

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Baltimore City is still up there at the top, even when measuring by beds per 100k. But there are some surprising ones at the top, some of Maryland's smallest counties\! Allegany, Talbot, Somerset may not have that many beds, but they also don't have a lot of people.
themes: per-capita normalisation; small-denominator effect; outlier interpretation
relevance_rationale: Interpreting geographic patterns and contextualizing surprises in data is editorial judgment
notes: Illustrates how per-capita measures can surface surprising results driven by small populations

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: So let's change the color scale to a 'log' scale, which will help us see those differences a bit more clearly.
themes: choropleth; log scale; colour scale; visualisation
relevance_rationale: Choosing log scales for visualization is a decision LLMs can now recommend and implement automatically
notes: Log scales compress wide-ranging data so geographic patterns in the middle range become visible

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Many of the data analyses that you do will be largely one-off efforts -- you're going to do the analysis and write the story and be done. ... But what happens when you have a long-running story, where you're going to update it every day, or every week? What changes when you're writing that code? 1. How will this run again without changing anything? 2. What questions do you have that have to be answered each time? 3. What changes when you have to repeat questions to changing data?
themes: automation; reproducibility; recurring analysis; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Automating recurring analysis is now much easier with LLM-generated scheduled scripts and pipelines
notes: Three design questions that distinguish a repeatable pipeline from a one-off analysis

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: You could look at a county or state website, copy and paste some information into a file and then do some calculations, but there's a better way. Instead, let's build a system that goes out each day to retrieve the data, makes it easier to analyze and then calculates the percentage change in the number of people getting fully vaccinated between the latest date and a week earlier. And makes us a chart showing that.
themes: automation; data pipeline; live data; percent change
relevance_rationale: Manual copy-paste vs coded retrieval tradeoff shifts further toward automation with LLM assistance
notes: End-to-end automated pipeline: fetch, clean, compute, visualise -- replaces manual copy-paste

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The dashboard is a series of HTML pages placed on the main page via iframe ... But if you view the source on that URL, there's ... no data. It's being pulled in from another URL. If we want to automate getting this data, we'll need to find out where it lives. One big clue is in the composition of that URL: 'github.io.' Are these files (and maybe the data) already on GitHub?
themes: data sourcing; web scraping; URL inspection; government data
relevance_rationale: Reverse-engineering dashboard data sources is now assistable by LLMs analyzing page source
notes: Technique for tracing dashboard data back to its raw source by inspecting URLs and following hosting clues

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: when we read in that data, the vaccination_date column is formatted as a <chr> column, not as a date. Let's fix that using lubridate's handy mdy_hms function, which matches the format in the dataframe.
themes: date parsing; data types; type coercion
relevance_rationale: Date column type fixing is routine work LLMs handle automatically during data profiling
notes: Always check and fix date column types on import; lubridate functions match the source format

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Let's see if there's anything unusual in the data by counting the number of rows for each county ... There are totals for all of Maryland's jurisdictions, plus one for 'Unknown' and one that's NA. All but the last one have roughly the same number of records. What's going on with the NA records?
themes: data quality; row counts; exploratory checks; anomaly detection
relevance_rationale: Exploratory counting and anomaly detection can be accelerated by LLM-assisted data profiling
notes: Count rows per group as a quick data-quality check; unexpected categories (Unknown, NA) signal issues to investigate

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: They don't seem to pertain to any county (or no county), and it's not clear what these records represent. Let's remove them from our dataset
themes: data cleaning; NA removal; filtering
relevance_rationale: Deciding what ambiguous records mean and whether to exclude them requires editorial judgment
notes: Remove unexplainable NA rows only after investigating and confirming they add no analytical value

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Let's look at the most recent date, and that's something that takes on different meaning when we're talking about updating data. We need to make this generic so that every time we pull this up and run it, it's the most recent date at the top.
themes: automation; dynamic filtering; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Checking data currency and understanding update cadence requires human contextual awareness
notes: Use dynamic date references (not hardcoded) so repeated runs always surface the latest data

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: now is when we need to start asking ourselves -- what are the questions that are going to come up day after day? What about how this most current date compares to the previous day, or the previous week or month?
themes: recurring questions; comparison; temporal analysis
relevance_rationale: Identifying recurring newsworthy questions to ask of updating data is editorial judgment
notes: Identifying the recurring editorial questions that drive the design of an automated analysis

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: What if we just ranked them? Where does this date rank? For that, we'll create a new column called Rank using mutate and we'll use a function called min_rank to rank them.
themes: ranking; contextualisation; temporal comparison
relevance_rationale: Ranking and mutate operations are easily generated by LLMs from natural language descriptions
notes: Ranking the current day against all prior days to contextualise a single data point

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: How about the percent change between the latest date and one week ago? To do that, we need to find the latest date ... Luckily, if we have one date we can calculate another one by adding or subtracting days
themes: percent change; week-over-week; temporal comparison
relevance_rationale: Percent change calculations across time periods are routine operations LLMs generate easily
notes: Standard percent-change calculation using date arithmetic to locate the comparison point

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The first layer will be all the bars. The second layer will just be the latest, and we'll make that bar red. Then we'll add a point to the top of that line to really draw attention to it. Then we'll use ggrepel to label it. Then I'm going to add a smoothing line. That'll illustrate the trend clearly. The rest is labeling and adjusting the text to make it look more like a news graphic.
themes: layered charts; annotation; trend line; news graphic
relevance_rationale: Multi-layer chart construction with highlighting is increasingly LLM-generatable from descriptions
notes: Build up chart layers: all data, highlight latest, label, add trend line, style for publication

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: One thing we are missing? An automated summary. What if we programmatically wrote the description for this chart using the percent change calculation we did before? ... we're going to use a function called paste to merge some text together. We're going to paste together a sentence fragment, the percent change number and another sentence fragment together to form a sentence.
themes: automated text; chart subtitle; programmatic writing; template
relevance_rationale: Programmatic chart descriptions are now a natural LLM capability, extending this idea significantly
notes: Generate chart subtitle text programmatically so the narrative updates automatically with the data

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: This is going to be a story for months, if not years. So repeating this analysis is a must for a reporter covering health care in Maryland. We've set ourselves up to do this every week when the data comes out. We just open our notebook, go to Run > Restart R and Run All Chunks and sit back and watch as it does it all again. Then we go report.
themes: reproducibility; automation; beat reporting; workflow
relevance_rationale: Automating recurring analysis for ongoing stories is now much more accessible via LLM pipelines
notes: The payoff of automation: one-click re-run frees the reporter to do actual reporting

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: when you have counties with a large number of cases, you'd use a different kind of scale called a log scale. It's a way of representing a very wide range of data in a more compact way
themes: log scale; visualisation; wide-range data
relevance_rationale: Understanding when to use log scales for skewed data is statistical reasoning independent of tools
notes: Log scales make line charts readable when some series dwarf others

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: for a map, we can't have multiple days. We need a single day. Ideally, it would be the most recent date. We can get it using the max function.
themes: latest date; dynamic filtering; map automation
relevance_rationale: Filtering to most recent date for maps is routine data wrangling LLMs handle from natural language
notes: Use max(date) to programmatically select latest snapshot for map, avoiding hardcoded dates

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The story didn't present any evidence of political meddling. Instead, it offered the results of several lines of data analysis that attempted to answer this central question: did red states get a bigger slice of the PPP pie than blue states? Mostly, it used basic descriptive statistics, calculating rates, ranking states and computing averages. But the data set it used also presents an opportunity to use two slightly more advanced statistical analysis methods to look for patterns: linear regression, to examine relationships, and a t.test, to confirm the statistical validity of an average between two groups.
chapter: Basic Stats: Linear Regression and The T-Test
themes: statistical methods; regression; t-test; descriptive statistics; analytical strategy
relevance_rationale: Using multiple lines of data analysis to build a case without claiming causation is enduring methodology
notes: Progression from descriptive stats to inferential: rates and rankings first, then correlation and significance testing

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: We can be a bit more precise by calculating a statistic called the correlation coefficient, also called 'r.' r is a value between 1 and -1. An r of 1 indicates a strong positive correlation. ... A correlation of 0 indicates no relationship. All r values will fall somewhere on this scale, and how to interpret them isn't always straightforward. They're best used to give general guidance when exploring patterns.
themes: correlation coefficient; r value; pattern detection; interpretation
relevance_rationale: Understanding correlation coefficients is core statistical literacy unchanged by LLMs
notes: r is a guide, not a verdict; interpretation requires context

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Is this finding statistically valid? We can get a general sense of that by calculating the p-value of this correlation, a test of statistical significance. For that, we can use the cor.test function.
themes: p-value; statistical significance; correlation test
relevance_rationale: P-value calculation mechanics are automated by LLMs but understanding what p-values mean still matters
notes: After computing r, always test statistical significance with cor.test to check p-value

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: p < .05 is accepted in many scientific disciplines -- and by many data journalists -- as the cutoff for statistical significance. But there's heated debate about that level, and some academics question whether p-values should be relied on so heavily.
themes: p-value; statistical significance; threshold debate
relevance_rationale: Understanding p < .05 conventions and their limitations is statistical literacy independent of tools
notes: Acknowledges the contested nature of the .05 threshold; caution against mechanical reliance

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: And to be clear, a low p-value does not prove that we've found what we set out to find. There's nothing on this graph or in the regression model output that proves that Trump's administration tipped the scales in favor of states that voted for it. It's entirely possible that there's some other variable -- or variables -- not considered here that explain this pattern. All we know is that we've identified a potentially promising pattern, worthy of additional reporting and analysis to flesh out.
themes: correlation vs causation; confounders; statistical humility; reporting
relevance_rationale: Low p-value not proving causation is a fundamental statistical reasoning principle
notes: Critical epistemological point: statistical significance does not equal causal proof; patterns require further reporting

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: A t-test can help us answer that question. It can tell us where there's a statistically significant difference between the means of two groups. Have we found a real difference, or have we chanced upon a statistical aberration?
themes: t-test; group comparison; statistical significance
relevance_rationale: Running t-tests is easily automated by LLMs but knowing when to apply them still requires understanding
notes: Use t-test when comparing means of exactly two groups to confirm the difference is not random

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: This is a bit more promising. T is much stronger -- about 3 -- and the p-value is .002. Both of these should give us assurance that we've found something statistically meaningful. Again, this doesn't prove that Trump is stacking the deck for states. It just suggests there's a pattern worth following up on.
themes: t-test interpretation; statistical significance; pattern vs proof
relevance_rationale: Interpreting t-statistics and p-values in context is human judgment work
notes: Reinforces that even a strong t-test result suggests a pattern, not proof of causation

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Turning unstructured text into data to analyze is a whole course in and of itself -- and one worth taking if you've got the credit hours -- but some simple stuff is in the grasp of basic data analysis.
themes: text analysis; unstructured data; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Text analysis as a field has been transformed by LLMs which handle unstructured text far more capably
notes: Frames text analysis as an extension of data analysis skills, not a separate discipline

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: What we want to do is to make the content column easier to analyze. ... We'll probably want to remove URLs from the text of the tweets since they aren't actual words.
themes: text cleaning; URL removal; preprocessing
relevance_rationale: Manual text preprocessing like URL removal and tokenization is handled automatically by LLMs
notes: Strip URLs before tokenising text; they add noise to word frequency counts

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: If you are trying to create a list of unique words, R will treat differences in capitalization as unique and also will include punctuation by default ... Fortunately, this is a solved problem with tidytext, which has a function called unnest_tokens that will convert the text to lowercase and remove all punctuation.
themes: tokenisation; case normalisation; punctuation removal; text preprocessing
relevance_rationale: Case normalization and punctuation handling for text analysis is obsoleted by LLM text understanding
notes: unnest_tokens handles lowercase conversion and punctuation removal in one step

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Well, that's a bit underwhelming - a lot of very common (and short) words. This also is a solved problem in working with text data, and words like 'a' and 'the' are known as 'stop words.' In most cases you'll want to remove them from your analysis since they are so common.
themes: stop words; text preprocessing; word frequency
relevance_rationale: Stop-word removal as a manual step is obsoleted by LLMs that inherently understand word significance
notes: Remove stop words to surface meaningful content words in frequency analysis

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The next step in text analysis is using ngrams. An ngram is any combination of words that you specify. Two word ngrams are called bigrams (bi-grams). Three would be trigrams.
themes: ngrams; bigrams; text analysis; multi-word phrases
relevance_rationale: N-gram frequency analysis is largely superseded by LLM semantic understanding of text
notes: Move from single-word to multi-word analysis to capture phrases and richer patterns

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: There are some differences here, too, but also some potential challenges to doing an analysis. For one, there are variations of words like 'vaccine' that could probably be standardized - maybe using OpenRefine - that would give us cleaner results. There might be some words among our list of stop words that actually are meaningful in this context.
themes: lemmatisation; domain stop words; text cleaning; context sensitivity
relevance_rationale: Entity variation challenges in text analysis are better handled by LLMs but awareness of the problem endures
notes: Generic stop-word lists and raw token forms may not suit every domain; consider standardisation and custom stop words

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Gauging the sentiment of a word can be heavily dependent on the context, and as with other types of text analysis sometimes larger patterns are more meaningful than individual results. But the potential with text analysis is vast: knowing what words and phrases that public officials employ can be a way to evaluate their priorities, cohesiveness and tactics for persuading voters and their colleagues. And those words and phrases are data.
themes: sentiment analysis; context dependency; text as data; accountability
relevance_rationale: Context-dependence of sentiment is a real limitation that LLMs handle better than lexicons but imperfectly
notes: Sentiment lexicons are blunt instruments; value lies in aggregate patterns, not individual word scores

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The number one sin of all early career data journalist is to get really, really, really attached to the analysis you've done and include every number you find. Don't do that. Numbers tell you what. Numbers rarely tell you why. What question has driven most people since they were three years old? Why. The very first thing to do is realize that is the purpose of reporting. You've done the analysis to determine the what. Now go do the reporting to do the why.
themes: writing; restraint; numbers as starting point; reporting
relevance_rationale: Not falling in love with your analysis and letting the story lead is editorial discipline unchanged by AI
notes: Data analysis finds the what; reporting explains the why. Do not overload the reader with numbers.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The trick to writing a numbers story is to frame your story around people. Sometimes, your lead can be a number, if that number is compelling. Often, your lead is a person, a person who is one of the numbers you are writing about. Tell their story. Briefly. Then, let us hear from them. Let them speak about what it is you are writing about. Then come the numbers.
themes: writing; narrative structure; humanisation; story framing
relevance_rationale: Framing data stories around people rather than numbers is a storytelling principle independent of tools
notes: Lead with a person, then pivot to numbers; the human story frames the data

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Using ratios instead of percents. Often, it's better to put it in counts of 10. 6 of 10, 4 of 10. It's easy to translate that from a percentage to a ratio. But be careful when your number is 45 percent. Is that 4 in 10 or 5 in 10? If a ratio doesn't make sense, round. There's 287,401 people in Lincoln, according to the Census Bureau. It's easier, and no less accurate, to say there's more than 287,000 people in Lincoln.
chapter: How to write about numbers without overwhelming with numbers
themes: ratios; rounding; simplification; writing with numbers
relevance_rationale: Making numbers relatable through ratios and rounding is a communication skill unchanged by LLMs
notes: Practical hierarchy: prefer ratios over percentages, round when appropriate, only use exact numbers when precision matters

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: A critical question your writing should answer: As compared to what? How does this compare to the average? The state? The nation? The top? The bottom?
chapter: How to write about numbers without overwhelming with numbers
themes: comparison; context; benchmarking; writing
relevance_rationale: Providing comparison context for numbers is core journalistic framing independent of tooling
notes: Every number in a story needs a comparison point to be meaningful

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Sometimes ratios and rounding are not appropriate. ... Case counts are where an exact number is called for. You don't say that there are more than 70 cases in Lancaster County on the day this was written. You specify. It's 75. You don't say almost 30 deaths. It's 28. Where this also comes into play is any time there are deaths: Do not round bodies.
themes: precision; death counts; exact numbers; writing ethics
relevance_rationale: Knowing when exact numbers matter vs when rounding is appropriate is editorial judgment
notes: Hard rule: never round death counts or case counts. Precision signals respect and accountability.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Then notice how they take what is a complex analysis using geographic information systems, raster analysis, the merging of multiple different datasets together and show that it's quite simple -- the averaging together of pixels on a 1-5 scale. ... Simplify, round, ratios: simple metrics, powerful results.
themes: simplification; writing; complex methods; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Translating complex analysis into plain language is a storytelling skill LLMs assist but do not replace
notes: Present complex methodology in its simplest accurate form; readers need the result, not the machinery

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The complexity comes when you realize the data you are dealing with represent real people's lives.
themes: ethics; human impact; data responsibility
relevance_rationale: Recognizing that data represents real people is an ethical principle amplified not diminished by AI
notes: Framing principle: data represents people, and publication has consequences

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Because we were going to put this information in front of a big audience, Google was going to find it. That meant if we used our normal open door policy for the Googlebot, someone's mug shot was going to be the first record in Google for their name, most likely. ... And that record in Google would exist as long as the URL did. ... That was a problem because here are the things we could not know: Was this person wrongly arrested? Was this person innocent? Were the charges dropped against this person?
themes: SEO ethics; search indexing; privacy; arrest records; unintended consequences
relevance_rationale: Considering searchability and downstream harm of published data is more urgent in the AI era
notes: Publication at scale changes the nature of public records; what was findable-in-principle becomes findable-in-practice

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: The life of your data matters. You have to ask yourself, Is it useful forever? Does it become harmful after a set time? ... So, at the 60 day mark, we deleted the data. We had no way of knowing if someone was guilty or innocent, so all of them had to go.
themes: data expiry; data lifecycle; deletion policy; ethics
relevance_rationale: Data lifetime considerations and when to remove published data are amplified by AI scraping
notes: Design a data retention policy that reflects what you can and cannot verify about the records

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: We couldn't stop people from posting a mug on Facebook, but we didn't have to make it easy and we didn't have to put that mug front and center. So we blocked Facebook from using the mug as the thumbnail image on a shared link. And, after 60 days, the URL to the mug will throw a 404 page not found error. Because it's gone.
themes: social sharing; friction design; link expiry; privacy engineering
relevance_rationale: Minimizing harm by not publishing mugshots or harmful imagery is ethical judgment unchanged by AI
notes: Design friction into sharing: block thumbnails, expire URLs. Cannot prevent sharing but can limit amplification.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: What we didn't realize when we launched, and what we figured out after Pick the Perp, was that we had become data provider, in a sense. We had done the hard work of getting the data out of a website and we put it into neat, semantic, easily digestible HTML. If you were after a stream of mugshots, why go through all the hassle of scraping four different sheriff's office's horrible HTML when you could just come get ours easily?
themes: unintended data provider; downstream use; scraping risk; data architecture
relevance_rationale: Recognizing journalists as data providers with downstream consequences is amplified by AI reuse
notes: Any well-structured public data app becomes a de facto API; plan for downstream reuse

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: What I want you to think about, before you write a line of code, is what does it mean to put your data on the internet? What could happen, good and bad? What should you do to be responsible about it?
themes: ethics; pre-publication checklist; responsible data; risk assessment
relevance_rationale: Thinking ethically before coding is even more critical when LLMs make publishing data trivially easy
notes: Ethics must be addressed at the design stage, before any code is written

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: Because you can put data on the web does not mean you should put data on the web. And there's a difference between a record being 'public' and 'in front of a large audience.'
themes: public records; amplification; privacy; publication ethics
relevance_rationale: Distinction between public records and publishing structured databases is amplified by AI indexing
notes: Crucial distinction: legal availability vs mass exposure. Scale changes the ethical calculus.

source_title: Data Journalism: A Quick & Practical Guide
author: Rob Wells
extract: So before you write the first line of code, ask these questions: This data is public, but is it widely available? And does making it widely available and easy to use change anything? Should this data be searchable in a search engine? Does this data expose information someone has a reasonable expectation that it would remain at least semi-private? Does this data change over time? Does this data expire? What is my strategy to update or delete data? How easy should it be to share this data on social media? How should I deal with other people who want this data? API? Bulk download?
themes: ethics checklist; data publication; privacy; data lifecycle; responsible design
relevance_rationale: Ethical pre-publication checklist for data is more important given LLM-era scraping and aggregation
notes: Comprehensive pre-publication ethics checklist covering discoverability, privacy, expiry, sharing, and downstream access

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Data journalism is gathering, cleaning, organizing, analyzing, visualizing, and publishing data to support the creation of acts of journalism. In its most elemental forms, data journalism combines: 1) the treatment of data as a source to be gathered and validated, 2) the application of statistics to interrogate it, 3) and visualizations to present it.
themes: data-as-source; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: Core definition still valid but now LLMs can assist with several of these steps, especially cleaning and organizing

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Some proponents of open data journalism hold that there should be four components, where data journalists archive and publish the underlying raw data behind their investigations, along with the methodology and code used in the analyses that led to their published conclusions.
themes: transparency; reproducibility; show-your-work
relevance_rationale: Transparency through publishing raw data, methodology, and code remains essential and arguably more important when LLMs are part of the workflow

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Data is socially constructed. In order to understand a data set, it is helpful to start with understanding the people who created the data set—think about what they were trying to do, or what they were trying to discover. Once you think about those people, and their goals, you're already beginning to tell a story.
themes: data-as-source; critical-thinking; social-construction
relevance_rationale: Understanding data as socially constructed and interrogating its provenance is a fundamentally human editorial judgment that LLMs cannot replace
notes: Quote from Meredith Broussard

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Publishers will need to continue to evolve toward a multidisciplinary approach to delivering the news, where reporters, developers, designers, editors, and community managers collaborate on storytelling, instead of being segregated by departments or buildings.
themes: collaboration; newsroom-structure; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: Multidisciplinary collaboration endures but team composition now includes AI-augmented roles and prompt-skilled journalists

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: As data journalists grow in skill and craft, they move from using basic statistics in their reporting to working in spreadsheets, to more complex data analysis and visualization, finally arriving at computational journalism, the command line, and programming.
themes: skill-progression; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: The skill progression from spreadsheets to programming is compressed by LLMs that can generate code and assist analysis at every level
notes: Describes a learning trajectory for data journalists

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: By automating common reporting tasks, for instance, or creating custom alerts, one data journalist can increase the capacity of the people with whom she works, building out databases that may be used for future reporting.
themes: automation; efficiency; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Automation of reporting tasks endures as a goal but LLMs have dramatically expanded what a single journalist can automate

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Collaboration across newsrooms and a focus on publishing data and code that show your work differentiate the best of today's data journalism from the CAR of decades ago.
themes: transparency; collaboration; show-your-work
relevance_rationale: Show-your-work ethic and cross-newsroom collaboration remain defining best practices regardless of tools used

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Journalists still have to confirm their sources, whether they're people or data sets, and present them in context. Using data as a source won't eliminate the need for fact-checking, adding context, or reporting that confirms the ground truth. Just the opposite, in fact.
themes: verification; data-as-source; ground-truth
relevance_rationale: The need for human fact-checking, context, and ground truth verification is even more critical when LLMs can generate plausible but wrong analysis

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: We interviewed experts to understand which comparisons would be most meaningful in the data. We looked for top-line numbers that could serve as easy benchmarks people could understand quickly. What sets our app apart is that it allows you to compare your doctor to others in the same specialty and state.
themes: benchmarking; comparison; user-centric-design
relevance_rationale: Consulting domain experts and designing meaningful comparisons requires editorial judgment that LLMs cannot substitute
notes: Quote from Charles Ornstein on ProPublica Treatment Tracker

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: We don't start with the data, or the technology. Everything we make starts with a user-centered design process. We talk about the users we want to speak to and the needs they have. Only then do we talk about what to make, and then we figure out how we're going to do it.
themes: user-centric-design; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: User-centered design thinking as the starting point for journalism projects remains essential regardless of AI tooling
notes: Quote from Brian Boyer, NPR Visuals

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: If we report on data, and we open data while reporting, then others can reuse and build another layer of knowledge on top of it. This is not only a data revolution. It is an open innovation revolution around knowledge.
themes: open-data; transparency; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Open data journalism as knowledge commons is a philosophical commitment that transcends any particular tool era
notes: Quote from Momi Peralta, La Nacion

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Not all data sets are synonymous with facts. They must be treated with skepticism, from origin to quality to hidden biases. Data does not intrinsically imply truth. Yes, we can find truth in data, through a process of honest inference, but we can also find and argue multiple truths or even outright falsehoods from data.
themes: critical-thinking; data-quality; bias
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward data and awareness of hidden biases is more important than ever when LLMs can amplify biased datasets
notes: Paraphrases Nick Diakopoulos

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: A managing editor might float an assertion or hypothesis about what lies behind news, and then assign an investigative journalist to go find out whether it's true or not. That reporter then must go collect data, evidence, and knowledge about it. To prove conclusions are sound, the journalist may need to show his or her work, from the sources of the data to the process used to transform and present them. That also means embracing skepticism, avoiding confirmation bias, and not jumping to conclusions about observed correlations.
themes: hypothesis-testing; show-your-work; critical-thinking; confirmation-bias
relevance_rationale: Hypothesis-driven investigation requiring evidence, skepticism, and avoiding confirmation bias is core journalism unchanged by LLMs

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Data-driven journalists could share their code and data on open source repositories like GitHub for others to inspect, replicate, and extend. Organizations that practice data-driven journalism should invite and provide empirical critiques of their analyses and findings. Making well-documented data available is extremely time-intensive, but if you're going to publish original empirical research, you should accept and respond to legitimate critiques.
themes: reproducibility; peer-review; transparency; show-your-work
relevance_rationale: Publishing code and data for peer critique remains a gold standard transparency practice regardless of whether code is human- or LLM-written
notes: Paraphrasing Brian Keegan proposals

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: To do this kind of work well, journalists need a firm understanding of public records laws, a grasp of programs such as Excel or Access, contacts with statisticians, and a comfort level in creating data sets where none exist.
themes: skill-requirements; data-creation; public-records
relevance_rationale: FOIA knowledge and statistician contacts endure but tool-specific references to Excel/Access are dated; LLMs now assist with dataset creation
notes: Quote from Charles Ornstein

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Data-driven investigative techniques enable beat reporters to look beyond anecdotes or a rotating cast of sources to find hidden trends or scoops. A body of empirical evidence, based upon rigorously vetted data, can give editors and reporters the ability to move away from 'he said, she said' journalism that leaves readers wondering where the truth lies.
themes: evidence-based; critical-thinking; verification
relevance_rationale: Using empirical evidence to move beyond he-said-she-said journalism is a timeless epistemological principle

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Using inbound search-engine analytics for reporting: Homicide Watch used clues in site search queries to ID a homicide victim. Organizing beats around databases may well hold similar importance in investigative projects.
themes: analytics; beat-reporting; data-as-source
relevance_rationale: Using analytics and organizing beats around databases still valid but LLM-powered search and monitoring tools have superseded early newsbot approaches
notes: Homicide Watch case study

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: We should make decisions about when to publish based upon data. Who will the audience be for a particular piece of content? Who are they? What do they read? That will lead to a very different approach to being a publishing enterprise. We will go from a 'publish' to a 'launch.'
themes: audience-analytics; publishing-strategy
relevance_rationale: Data-driven publishing decisions endure but now include AI-powered audience analytics and personalization capabilities
notes: Quote from Aron Pilhofer, NYT

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: We embedded open data strategists and evangelists into the newsrooms, backed up by an external development team at a civic tech lab. They're structuring the data that's available, such as turning old microfiche rolls into digital information, cleaning it up, and building a data disk. They're building news APIs and pushing the idea that rather than building websites, design an API specifically for third-party repurposing of your content.
themes: data-infrastructure; collaboration; open-data
relevance_rationale: Embedding data strategists and building APIs endures conceptually but the specific infrastructure approach is now augmented by LLM-powered data pipelines
notes: Quote from Justin Arenstein, African Media Initiative

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Newsbots may be most useful as early alert systems for beat reporters and editors, finding signal in the noise that journalists can then use as digital tips to assign, investigate, and confirm. If a member of Congress is at risk, you see a spike in 'legal services,' a standard variation above the mean. That should send a notification to congressional reporters.
themes: automation; monitoring; algorithmic-detection
relevance_rationale: Alert systems for beat reporters endure as concept but LLMs now provide far more sophisticated signal-detection than rule-based newsbots described here
notes: Paraphrases Aron Pilhofer on automated alerts

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: ProPublica wrote dozens of data scrapers to cross-reference their database of payments with state medical board and medical school listings. For the initial story, they teamed up with five other newsrooms including NPR and the Boston Globe, which required programmatically creating a system in which we could coordinate data and research. With all the data we had, this wasn't a project that would've succeeded by just sending Excel files back and forth.
themes: scraping; cross-referencing; collaboration; data-infrastructure
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing databases with scrapers still relevant but LLMs can now write scrapers and do entity resolution much faster
notes: Dollars for Docs project

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: ProPublica created a software application to enable volunteers to translate unsearchable PDF files into structured data and sort the files by market, amount, candidate, and political group. Nearly 1,000 people pored over the files, logging detailed ad spending data to create a public database that otherwise wouldn't exist.
themes: crowdsourcing; data-creation; PDF-extraction
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing PDF-to-structured-data conversion has been largely replaced by LLM-powered document extraction and OCR
notes: Free the Files project

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: WNYC's Cicada Tracker project turned public radio listeners into sensor journalists: 800 people making trackers produced 8,000 cicada sightings and 1,500 collected temperature readings. The project made crowdsourcing data collection through sensor journalism and a distributed listening audience a reality.
themes: crowdsourcing; sensor-journalism; citizen-science; data-creation
relevance_rationale: Citizen sensor journalism and crowdsourced data collection address a physical-world gap that LLMs cannot fill

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The site demonstrated two important practices that deserve to become standard in data journalism: explaining the methodology behind its analysis, including source notes, and publishing the data behind the investigation.
themes: transparency; methodology; show-your-work
relevance_rationale: Publishing methodology, source notes, and underlying data are transparency standards that become more important with AI-assisted analysis
notes: Oakland Police Beat case study

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The L.A. Times found that poor recordkeeping is holding back state government efforts to upgrade California's 911 system. As with any database project, beware of garbage in, garbage out. Poor collection practices and aging IT will derail any institutional efforts to use data analysis to improve performance.
themes: data-quality; verification; GIGO
relevance_rationale: Garbage-in-garbage-out and poor recordkeeping problems remain regardless of whether analysis is done by humans or LLMs

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The projects I'm most proud of have taken large, ugly data sets and refined them into something worth knowing: a nut graf in an investigative story or a data-driven app that gives the reader some new insight into the world around them.
themes: data-quality; data-cleaning; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: Refining ugly datasets is still valuable but LLMs have dramatically accelerated the cleaning and structuring process
notes: Quote from Ben Welsh, LA Times

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The Upshot made editorial decisions to share how reporters found the income data, link to the data set, and share both the methodology behind the forecasting model and the code for it on Github. That is precisely the model for open data journalism that embodies the best of the craft.
themes: transparency; reproducibility; show-your-work; open-data
relevance_rationale: Sharing methodology, data, and code on GitHub as open data journalism best practice endures regardless of AI tools

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Doing original research on data is hard: It's the core of scientific analysis, and that's why academics have to go through peer-review to get their figures, methods, and approaches double-checked. Journalism is meant to be about transparency, and so should hold itself to this standard—at the very least.
themes: peer-review; transparency; scientific-method
relevance_rationale: The rigor of peer-review-style transparency for journalism doing original data research is even more vital when LLMs assist analysis
notes: Quote from James Ball, Guardian

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The International Consortium of Investigative Journalists analyzed 260 gigabytes of leaked corporate data, constituting text, PDF, images, spreadsheets, and images to reveal how government officials were offshoring money. More than 80 journalists from 40 different countries worked together.
themes: collaboration; cross-border; document-analysis; scale
relevance_rationale: Large-scale cross-border data investigations endure but LLMs now dramatically accelerate document processing, entity extraction, and translation
notes: Offshore Leaks / ICIJ

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Reuters Connected China structured inherently qualitative relationships—the connections between people (family, mentorship, rivalry, alliances), the importance of particular job roles, the power dynamic between institutions. By quantifying and categorizing these complex relationships, we break from the constraints of long-form text and allow new ways of communicating and interpreting acquired knowledge.
themes: network-analysis; structured-data; relationship-mapping
relevance_rationale: Structuring qualitative relationships into network data endures as method but LLMs can now assist with entity extraction and relationship mapping from text
notes: Quote from Irene Jay Liu

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: In countries without FOIA, Tola and her team aggregated or created the rest of the data used in the project, from scraping and processing PDFs of spending data from regional government websites, then adding geolocation in cooperation with a local developer.
themes: scraping; PDF-extraction; data-creation; geolocation
relevance_rationale: Scraping PDFs and adding geolocation in low-FOIA contexts still needed but LLMs can now assist with PDF extraction and geocoding
notes: Wired Italy school earthquake risk project

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Irene Choge, a Kenyan journalist, started mining the available data and public records. She first looked at medical records to see if cholera was involved. Then, she examined water records and physical infrastructure. It was there that she found a key correlation: The schools that saw the worst drop-offs in academic performance by teenage girls were the ones that didn't have sanitation facilities.
themes: hypothesis-testing; cross-referencing; public-records
relevance_rationale: Mining public records to find unexpected correlations requires investigative intuition and domain knowledge that LLMs cannot replace
notes: Kenya data boot camp example

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Journalism programs need to step up and understand that we live in a data-rich society, and math skills and basic data analysis skills are highly relevant to journalism. Journalism is one of the few professions that not only tolerates general innumeracy, but celebrates it. There is still far too much tolerance for anecdotal evidence as the foundation for news stories.
themes: numeracy; skill-requirements; education
relevance_rationale: The critique of innumeracy in journalism and need for data literacy in curricula remains urgent regardless of AI assistance
notes: Quote from Aron Pilhofer

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: People with the right mindset, who feel valued for their editorial judgment and creativity, and who are given real responsibility over their work, will learn whatever they need to learn in order to get a project done. There's no magic threshold you have to pass between programmer and not-programmer. Making tools to automate tasks is what a programmer does.
themes: skill-development; newsroom-culture; automation
relevance_rationale: Growth mindset, editorial judgment, and learning-by-doing culture are human organizational qualities unaffected by LLM availability
notes: Quote from Scott Klein

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Journalism schools still teach journalism as a very hierarchical, often solitary pursuit. That's not the way it works in data journalism. We need a pairing model across newsrooms, not just in the nerd corner.
themes: collaboration; peer-learning; newsroom-culture
relevance_rationale: Collaborative non-hierarchical journalism endures but pair-programming model now extends to human-AI pairing as well
notes: Quote from Tasneem Raja

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Introduce data journalism within the curriculum, then provide a venue for students to tinker and explore. Ideally, someone on faculty should know enough to guide them. The school should show an interest in data journalism work on par with more traditional storytelling. And they should require more math classes.
themes: education; numeracy; skill-development
relevance_rationale: Introducing data journalism in curriculum endures but now must include AI literacy alongside math classes
notes: Quote from Chase Davis

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: We read Joel Best's Damned Lies and Statistics and talk about how data comes into being. Then, we go on to data analysis and we practice different ways of representing data. I focus on teaching the students how to use technological tools in the service of doing a story.
themes: education; social-construction; data-literacy
relevance_rationale: Teaching how data comes into being and using tools in service of story are pedagogy principles that transcend any specific tool era
notes: Quote from Meredith Broussard on Temple curriculum

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Cleaning data is often the most time consuming part of the data journalism process. A crucial part of what's needed to practice data journalism has little to do with tools and technology and everything to do with perspective and critical thinking. You need a mindset which is about putting this in the context of the story and spotting stories.
themes: data-cleaning; critical-thinking; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: Data cleaning as time-consuming process still true but LLMs have dramatically reduced the manual effort while the critical thinking aspect endures
notes: Paraphrases Jonathan Stray and Emily Bell

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The canonical suite of tools for computer-assisted reporting: spreadsheets, databases, text editors, and statistics software. Today, data journalists also leverage Web-based tools like Open Refine, Google Fusion Tables, and Tableau, and modern programming languages like Python, Ruby, and Javascript, as well as d3.
themes: tool-ecosystem; workflow-design
relevance_rationale: The canonical tool list (Excel, Access, Fusion Tables, etc.) is thoroughly outdated; LLMs plus modern tools have replaced most of this specific stack

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
