extract: Stories just as likely come from the unstructured information that comes from documents, audio and video, tweets, other social media—from government and non-government sources. The value that journalists can add has shifted from finding information to making sense of what's actually happening, processing, analyzing and vetting data, and finding signal in the digital noise.
themes: unstructured-data; sensemaking; data-as-source
relevance_rationale: Finding signal in noise from unstructured sources endures as core value-add but LLMs now assist massively with processing unstructured text, audio, video
notes: Paraphrases Sarah Cohen

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The data stuck on your hard drive is sad data. Knowledge management isn't a sexy problem to solve, but it's a real business problem. People could be doing better reporting if they knew what was available. Data should be visible internally.
themes: knowledge-management; data-infrastructure; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Internal knowledge management endures as problem but LLM-powered search and retrieval systems now offer solutions
notes: Quote from Brian Boyer on PANDA project

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: For data journalists, showing your work means sharing your source data, methodology, and code, not just a notebook. Members of newsrooms that compete on beats are working together on code.
themes: show-your-work; open-source; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Show-your-work transparency including source data, methodology, and code is a timeless standard that gains urgency with AI-assisted workflows

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Open government data means that more people can access and reuse official information. This in itself is not enough. Journalists need to know what official data means, what it says, and what it leaves out. Only by equipping journalists with the skills to use data more effectively can we break the current asymmetry, where our understanding of information that matters is mediated by governments, companies, and other experts.
themes: data-literacy; critical-thinking; open-data; power-asymmetry
relevance_rationale: Critical assessment of government open data quality and completeness requires human editorial judgment regardless of AI tools
notes: Quote from Liliana Bounegru

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The demand for structured, nicely scrubbed data for the purpose of building apps can result in fake records rather than real records being released. We get the lowest common denominator of information. When data gets pushed out as open government data, it often gets scrubbed of important details and then we have a harder time getting them under FOIA. We should push harder for actual records and get better at using records in whatever form they exist.
themes: data-quality; public-records; FOIA; verification
relevance_rationale: Warning about sanitized open data replacing real records is a political accountability insight unaffected by LLM tools
notes: Quote from Sarah Cohen on limitations of sanitized open data

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Data journalism doesn't have to be the product. Data journalism can also be the route that you follow to get to a final story. It doesn't have to produce an infographic or a map.
themes: workflow-design; investigation
relevance_rationale: Data journalism as a process not just a product is a framing insight that remains valid regardless of tools
notes: Quote from Justin Arenstein

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Don't assume data is inherently accurate, fair, and objective. Don't mistake your access to data or your right to publish it as a legitimate rationale for doing so. Think critically about the public good and potential harm, the context surrounding the data, and its relevance to your other reporting. Then decide whether your data publishing is journalism.
themes: ethics; critical-thinking; harm-assessment
relevance_rationale: Ethical judgment about whether to publish data, considering harm and public good, is irreducibly human deliberation
notes: Quote from Jeff Sonderman, Poynter

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Journalists must consider when it's appropriate to scrape data, access data, store it—or not. Does the story require storing personal information? If so, such sensitive data will need to be protected with the same vigor that journalists have protected confidential sources.
themes: ethics; data-security; privacy
relevance_rationale: Data ethics around scraping, storage, and protecting personal information are legal and ethical questions unaffected by LLM capabilities

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Choose the right stories: a well-written review of the scholarly literature may better inform public debate. Embrace complexity: no interesting causal relationship involves only two variables. Use statistics intelligently: a scatterplot with a least-squares regression line is not 'doing statistics.' Bad statistics is worse than no statistics. Be modest: if you have so many caveats as to completely undermine any conclusion, then don't offer a conclusion.
themes: statistical-rigor; critical-thinking; complexity; humility
relevance_rationale: Editorial wisdom about choosing stories, embracing complexity, using statistics responsibly, and being modest are timeless principles
notes: Andrew Whitby's four principles

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Social scientists know that the sources for data and conditions under which it is collected will shape and bias any subsequent research conclusions. Data journalists have to go beyond acquiring and cleaning data to understanding its provenance and source. Then, they'll need to make sure that its presentation doesn't tell a different story than the data itself allows.
themes: provenance; bias; social-construction; data-quality
relevance_rationale: Understanding data provenance and ensuring presentation matches what data actually supports is critical thinking LLMs cannot substitute

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The antidote to data-ism is for journalists to interrogate data just as they would human sources, checking facts and assumptions, comparing results, and documenting the process and results of their investigations as a social scientist or biologist would.
themes: critical-thinking; scientific-method; verification; documentation
relevance_rationale: Interrogating data like a human source is a fundamental journalism principle that becomes more important with LLM-generated analysis

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: First, searching. Using advanced search techniques, in countries like ours, you find there is more on the Deep Web than in the surface. Then scraping, converting data from PDFs, structuring datasets, and analyzing data. Then, learning to publish in open data formats.
themes: data acquisition; scraping; open data; data pipeline
relevance_rationale: Deep web searching, scraping, and PDF conversion still relevant but LLMs have automated much of the structuring and conversion work
notes: La Nacion interview - Momi Peralta on foundational data journalism skills sequence

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: If there are language barriers, translate. If there is no open data, start by doing it yourself. If technology is expensive, check first for free versions. Most are enough to do everything you need.
themes: resourcefulness; open data; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Resourcefulness ethos endures but the specific advice to find free tools is transformed by LLMs that replace many specialized tools entirely
notes: La Nacion - creative problem solving for data access barriers

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: It's our job to make sure everybody in the newsroom can tell a story by any means necessary. That is, reporters should know how to map, the mapmaking pros on my team should know how to factcheck, the fact checkers should know when to use a column chart versus a bar chart, and so on. We don't believe in siloed skills.
themes: cross-training; newsroom collaboration; skill integration
relevance_rationale: Cross-functional skill sharing across newsroom roles is an organizational principle that endures regardless of AI tools
notes: Tasneem Raja at Mother Jones on cross-functional team skills

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Never build something you can only use once. Instead, think both in terms of the specific needs of the project in front of you, and the broader needs of a similar project you might not even know of till next year.
themes: reusability; abstraction; project management
relevance_rationale: Building reusable tools and thinking about future projects is sound engineering practice that applies equally to AI-assisted workflows
notes: Josh Williams advice via Tasneem Raja on building reusable tools

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Everything is data. Words are data. Gifs are data. If it can be sorted, tracked, counted, merged, filtered: it's probably data. I'd say half the project my team does is more qualitative than quantitative.
themes: data definition; qualitative data; framing
relevance_rationale: Expansive definition of data including qualitative information is a mindset shift that remains relevant and is reinforced by LLM capabilities
notes: Raja on expansive definition of data beyond quantitative

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Pick a project you genuinely want to do, and then hack, Google, and plead for help in forums, and read books, until you get it working.
themes: learning; project-based learning; self-teaching
relevance_rationale: Learning by doing endures but the advice to Google and plead in forums is now supplemented by LLM-assisted coding and debugging
notes: Raja on best approach for learning data journalism skills

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: We don't have a pair programming model on the editorial side of the newsroom, and we need one. Journalism schools still teach journalism as a very hierarchical, often solitary pursuit. That's not the way it works in data journalism, and the best learning is still gonna be on the job. That requires cross-pollination between folks with different skill sets.
themes: pair programming; pedagogy; newsroom collaboration; cross-training
relevance_rationale: Cross-pollination between skill sets endures but now includes AI fluency and human-AI collaboration models
notes: Raja on need for collaborative coding model in newsrooms

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: At ProPublica, no one has a specific responsibility like design, backend development, data analysis, etc. Instead, people on the team tend to do the whole stack from beginning to end. When we need help, or don't understand something, we ask our teammates.
themes: full-stack journalism; team structure; newsroom collaboration
relevance_rationale: Full-stack journalism endures as ideal but LLMs now enable individuals to cover more of the stack without deep expertise in each area
notes: Sisi Wei on ProPublica full-stack team model

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: An insatiable appetite to get to the bottom of something, and the willingness to learn any tool to help you find the answers you're looking for. In that process, you'll by necessity learn programming skills, or data analysis skills. But without knowing what questions to ask, or what you're trying to accomplish, neither of those skills will help you.
themes: question-driven inquiry; curiosity; skill acquisition
relevance_rationale: Curiosity and knowing what questions to ask as prerequisites before tools is a timeless insight reinforced by the prompt-engineering era
notes: Sisi Wei on questions driving tool selection, not vice versa

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: In terms of programming, just pick a project, make it simple, make it happen and then finish it.
themes: project-based learning; prototyping
relevance_rationale: Project-based learning endures but the simplicity of starting now includes LLM-assisted prototyping
notes: Wei on learn-by-doing approach

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Data journalism allows journalists to point to the raw data and ask questions, as well as question the very conclusions we are given. It allows us to use social science techniques to illuminate stories that might otherwise be hidden in plain sight.
themes: social science methods; accountability; verification; evidence-based reporting
relevance_rationale: Using social science techniques to illuminate hidden stories is an epistemological foundation that transcends tool changes
notes: Wei on data journalism as social science method for journalism

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: The same core skills you need to practice any kind of journalism: curiosity, skepticism, an eye for detail and a sense of a good story. Also numeracy, or at least conceptual mathematical literacy, which is still unfortunately too rare. Also important are databases and spreadsheets, statistics, and some kind of programming language.
themes: numeracy; foundational skills; skepticism; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Core skills of curiosity and skepticism endure but the specific tool requirements (databases, spreadsheets, programming) are now augmented by LLMs
notes: Chase Davis on foundational data journalism skills including numeracy

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: I think a data journalist needs news judgment and attention to detail in order to identify the newsworthiness and limitations of datasets. Statistics can help explain a dataset's strengths and weaknesses.
themes: data limitations; news judgment; statistical literacy; verification
relevance_rationale: News judgment and understanding dataset limitations are editorial skills that remain essential regardless of AI assistance
notes: Dan Hill on identifying dataset limitations

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Our team adheres to modified scrum principles. We have a daily scrum at 10:00am for all of the members of our Visuals team. We work on projects in one-week sprints. At the end of each sprint we have an iteration review and a 'ticket shuffle' where we decide what tickets each of us should work on in the next sprint.
themes: agile methodology; project management; team structure; newsroom workflow
relevance_rationale: Agile/scrum project management for newsroom teams is an organizational methodology independent of AI tools
notes: Jeremy Bowers on NPR Visuals team agile/scrum workflow

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Our projects involve minimally four people: One developer, one designer, one project manager and one stakeholder. Some projects add more designers or more developers as necessary.
themes: team structure; project management; newsroom collaboration
relevance_rationale: Minimum team composition may shift as LLMs enable smaller teams to do more, reducing need for separate developer/designer/PM roles
notes: Bowers on minimum viable team composition at NPR Visuals

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: If we report on data, and we open data while reporting, then others can reuse and build another layer of knowledge on top of it.
themes: open data; transparency; reproducibility; public service
relevance_rationale: Open data journalism as knowledge commons is a philosophical commitment that transcends any particular tool era
notes: La Nacion on publishing data alongside reporting

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Instead of a person, you're using data as a source. Over time, I shifted from being a reporter who does CAR to being a specialist at the Center for Public Integrity to a CAR editor at the New York Times and then started this team.
themes: data as source; CAR; evidence-based reporting
relevance_rationale: The evolution from reporter-who-uses-data to data journalism specialist reflects career development patterns still relevant today
notes: Pilhofer on treating data as a journalistic source

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: I feel strongly, and now there is a growing consensus, that we should make decisions like that based upon data. Who will the audience be for a particular piece of content? Who are they? What do they read? That will lead to a very different approach to being a publishing enterprise.
themes: audience analytics; data-driven publishing; distribution strategy
relevance_rationale: Data-driven publishing decisions endure but now include AI-powered audience analytics and recommendation systems
notes: Pilhofer on data-driven publishing decisions at NYT

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: There is still far too much tolerance for anecdotal evidence as the foundation for news stories.
themes: evidence-based reporting; numeracy; rigor
relevance_rationale: Critique of over-reliance on anecdotal evidence in journalism is a timeless epistemological point
notes: Pilhofer on cultural resistance to data-driven evidence

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: People with the right mindset, who feel valued for their editorial judgment and creativity, and who are given real responsibility over their work, will learn whatever they need to learn in order to get a project done. The people on my team focus on telling great journalistic stories and don't let not knowing how to do something stop them from doing so.
themes: culture; learning mindset; team management; autonomy
relevance_rationale: Growth mindset and not letting tool gaps stop you from pursuing stories is a human quality unaffected by AI tools
notes: Scott Klein on culture enabling skill acquisition at ProPublica

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: There are ways to design data so that more important numbers are bigger and more prominent than less important details. People know to scroll down a Web page for more fine-grained details. At ProPublica, we design things to move readers through levels of abstraction from the most general, national case to the most local example.
themes: information hierarchy; data visualization; user experience; presentation
relevance_rationale: Information design principles endure but now must account for AI-generated visualizations and interactive formats
notes: Klein on ProPublica information design from general to local

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: Can you imagine a sports reporter who doesn't know what an on-base percentage is? Or doesn't know how to calculate it himself? You can now ask a version of that question for almost every beat.
themes: numeracy; data literacy; beat reporting
relevance_rationale: Numeracy as essential for every journalism beat is arguably more important when journalists must evaluate LLM-generated statistics
notes: Klein on data literacy as essential for every beat

source_title: The Art and Science of Data-Driven Journalism
author: Alexander Howard
extract: It makes lots of sense for us to make the effort of typing, building datasets, cleaning, converting and sharing data in open formats, even organizing our own 'datafest' to expose data to experts.
themes: data cleaning; open data; dataset building; community engagement
relevance_rationale: Building and sharing datasets in open formats endures but the manual typing and conversion effort is now largely automated by LLMs
notes: La Nacion on investing in dataset creation and sharing

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Data journalism is 'gathering, cleaning, organizing, analyzing, visualizing, and publishing data to support the creation of acts of journalism.' A more succinct definition might be simply the application of data science to journalism, where data science is defined as the study of the extraction of knowledge from data.
themes: data journalism definition; pipeline sequence
relevance_rationale: Definition of data journalism as extraction of knowledge from data applied to journalism remains valid
notes: Quoting Alexander Howard 2014 Tow Center report

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Data reporting includes: deploying computer-assisted reporting for writing journalistic stories; practicing precision journalism including social science research methods; visualizing data for exploration and analysis; programming to obtain and analyze data for stories.
chapter: Four Key Areas of Data Journalism
themes: CAR; precision journalism; data analysis; visualization
relevance_rationale: CAR taxonomy of practices endures but LLMs now assist with social science methods, visualization, and data analysis
notes: Taxonomy of data reporting practices

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Data reporting techniques include: invoking public records law to negotiate for data; using web scraping tools and techniques; using relational database software; understanding statistical concepts and software; using mapping and visualization tools.
chapter: Four Key Areas of Data Journalism
themes: FOI; web scraping; databases; statistics; mapping
relevance_rationale: FOI, scraping, databases, statistics, and mapping remain relevant but LLMs automate significant portions of scraping and data wrangling
notes: Practical technique inventory for data reporting

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Computational journalism uses frameworks and applications like Jupyter that enable journalists to mix code and prose as they perform analysis and show the steps in their work. Platforms like Overview facilitate the use of complicated computational processes like natural language processing and topic modeling.
chapter: Four Key Areas of Data Journalism
themes: reproducibility; computational journalism; NLP; topic modeling
relevance_rationale: Jupyter and NLP tools endure but LLMs have dramatically simplified topic modeling and text analysis, reducing barrier to entry
notes: Jupyter for transparent analysis; Overview for NLP

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Philip Meyer believed social science methodologies, including statistical tests and surveys, could have great impact in journalism. His survey of Detroit riot participants contradicted earlier theories about who participated and why. 'The tools of sampling, computer analysis, and statistical inference increased the traditional power of the reporter without changing the nature of his or her mission: to find the facts, to understand them, and to explain them without wasting time.'
chapter: A Brief History of Computers and Journalists
themes: precision journalism; social science methods; statistical analysis; evidence
relevance_rationale: Social science methodologies in journalism as foundational epistemological commitment is unchanged by LLMs
notes: Foundational argument for quantitative methods in journalism

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Elliot Jaspin used databases to match felons and bad driving records to school bus drivers. Bill Dedman used data from a 9-track tape to show banks were redlining African Americans on loans. Steve Doig used data analysis and mapping to show that weakened building requirements were the reason Hurricane Andrew had so devastated certain parts of Miami.
chapter: A Brief History of Computers and Journalists
themes: record linkage; mapping; investigative journalism; database analysis
relevance_rationale: Record linkage, mapping, and database analysis as investigative techniques remain foundational CAR practices
notes: Three canonical CAR investigations showing data matching and spatial analysis

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: A key tenet of the boot camp is practical, hands-on training, using data sets that journalists routinely report on, such as school test scores. 'Learning by doing' is the model. Boot camp graduates have gone on to robust data journalism careers and integrated those teaching techniques into their classes.
chapter: A Brief History of Computers and Journalists
themes: pedagogy; hands-on training; boot camps; learning by doing
relevance_rationale: Hands-on learning-by-doing pedagogy with real datasets is a timeless training model
notes: NICAR boot camp model as pedagogical standard

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: As journalism programs began to offer classes, they focused on the basics: negotiating for data, cleaning it, and using spreadsheets and relational databases, mapping, and statistics to find stories.
chapter: A Brief History of Computers and Journalists
themes: data negotiation; data cleaning; spreadsheets; databases; statistics
relevance_rationale: Core CAR curriculum of data negotiation, cleaning, and spreadsheet analysis endures but LLMs automate much of the cleaning step
notes: Core CAR curriculum sequence: negotiate, clean, analyze

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: It is important for classes to be designed so that they teach data and computation as fundamental styles of inquiry. Students can learn enough about the concepts behind a technique to be able to more easily learn new tools that address the technique—as opposed to focusing on the discrete tools used from time to time.
chapter: The Task at Hand: Causes for Concern and Reasons for Hope
themes: computational thinking; transferable skills; conceptual learning
relevance_rationale: Teaching concepts over tools is even more important when LLMs make tools change faster
notes: Teach concepts not tools; tools change but inquiry methods persist

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: It is not the purpose of a program in data journalism to compete with other disciplines, but to develop a curriculum that is intrinsically journalistic—one that reflects a mission to find and tell stories in the public interest—as well as develop partnerships and collaborations with other disciplines.
chapter: The Task at Hand: Causes for Concern and Reasons for Hope
themes: public interest; interdisciplinary; journalism mission
relevance_rationale: Data journalism distinguished by public interest mission remains the core differentiator from data science
notes: Data journalism distinguished by public interest mission

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Journalists do not often work with samples, but they do work with entire data sets. A more useful statistics class might be the type of instruction Meyer provided, using social science to address journalistic challenges. Accommodating both techniques in a research or statistics class could foster collaboration instead of silos.
chapter: The Task at Hand: Causes for Concern and Reasons for Hope
themes: statistics; population data; social science methods; journalism pedagogy
relevance_rationale: Key distinction that journalism uses whole populations not samples persists and matters for LLM-assisted analysis
notes: Key distinction: journalism uses whole populations not samples

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: We first collected the course offerings of 113 programs. We scraped what we could from journalism program websites and hand-entered the remainder. To verify the data, we emailed or called programs that had listed either no classes or very few. We then revisited every program website for all 113 programs and double-checked the data.
themes: web scraping; data collection; verification; mixed methods
relevance_rationale: Scrape-then-verify methodology endures but LLMs now assist with both web scraping and cross-verification steps
notes: Authors' own methodology: scrape then verify by phone/email

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: A key tenet of this field is that using data to report and tell stories can result in a more powerful story. As LaFleur described it: 'understand the data, interview the data, report the data.'
themes: data interviewing; data-driven reporting
relevance_rationale: Treating data like a human source -- understand, interview, report -- is a permanent analytical stance
notes: Core CAR maxim: treat data like a human source

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: The core data syllabus should consist of negotiating for data, thinking critically about data, and using spreadsheets to analyze data. Students are taught critical thinking and developing a 'data frame of mind'—being able to question data in a disciplined way, make sense of discrepancies, and find the underlying patterns and outliers that are important to the analysis.
chapter: Teaching Data Fundamentals: Rows and Columns
themes: critical thinking; data frame of mind; patterns; outliers; spreadsheets
relevance_rationale: Core data syllabus of negotiation, critical thinking, spreadsheets endures but spreadsheet analysis is partially displaced by LLM-assisted code
notes: Doig's three-part core: negotiate, think critically, spreadsheet analyze

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Hard skills for CAR courses: searching for and finding documents and data including public requests, deep research, and scraping; understanding data structures and how to clean and standardize data; analyzing data using spreadsheets, databases, mapping, and visualization; learning advanced statistical methods. Guiding concepts: finding what no one has known before; developing data-driven storytelling techniques; thinking of data as an asset in the reporting process.
chapter: Teaching Data Fundamentals: Rows and Columns
themes: FOI; data cleaning; standardization; storytelling; statistical methods
relevance_rationale: Hard skills taxonomy endures but FOI research, scraping, and cleaning can be substantially LLM-assisted
notes: Comprehensive CAR skills taxonomy: hard skills plus guiding concepts

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Data journalists need to know how to do basic exploratory visual analysis. Even journalists who practice data visualization need to start with the exploratory analysis. They need to know—just like the CAR specialists—how to 'interview' the data.
chapter: Teaching Advanced Data Skills: Visualization and Programming
themes: exploratory data analysis; visualization; data interviewing
relevance_rationale: Exploratory visual analysis endures as concept but LLMs can now generate exploratory charts from natural language commands
notes: Cairo: visualization first as analysis tool, not presentation

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: There is a key difference between building a new website or multimedia presentation and building something like ProPublica's 'Dollars for Docs,' which enabled readers to drill into the story of pharmaceutical industry payments to doctors and also made it possible for other journalists to find and tell other stories.
chapter: Teaching Advanced Data Skills: Visualization and Programming
themes: news apps; public interest; reusable data; ProPublica
relevance_rationale: Distinction between presentation websites and generative news apps that enable further journalism is permanently valuable
notes: Distinction: presentation vs. generative news apps that enable further journalism

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: There is a consensus when it comes to the foundations of data journalism curricula: critical thinking, mastery of key data skills, and teaching programming concepts so that students will be able to learn new tools as needed.
themes: critical thinking; programming literacy; adaptability
relevance_rationale: Consensus on critical thinking, data skills, and programming concepts as DJ foundations remains valid
notes: Consensus from 50 interviews on DJ foundations

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: In a data journalism class, having that critical thinking skill means that the students learn to treat data in an ethical way, so that rather than bending the data to represent a particular view, the goal is toward truth and accuracy.
themes: ethics; accuracy; critical thinking; bias
relevance_rationale: Ethical treatment of data toward truth and accuracy rather than bending data to fit views is a permanent principle
notes: Ethical framing of data analysis: truth over advocacy

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: In the beginning, professors provide data to students to analyze. By the middle of a course, students often have to obtain their own data, submitting public records requests. The students then move on to data that require more complex analysis. By doing this, professors teach the critical thinking that goes with negotiating for information and understanding the bounds of that information.
themes: scaffolding; FOI; data acquisition; progressive complexity
relevance_rationale: Scaffolded pedagogy from provided data to self-sourced data to complex analysis is a timeless teaching sequence
notes: Pedagogical sequence: provided data -> self-sourced data -> complex analysis

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: 'I would always err on the teaching of critical thinking skills. That is the harder skill to ingrain in people. You can learn how to click things and write a line of code.'
themes: critical thinking; tool agnosticism
relevance_rationale: Critical thinking as harder and more important than tool skills is even more true when LLMs handle tool skills
notes: LaFleur: critical thinking harder and more important than tool skills

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Students need to develop a sense of when data could be problematic, what are signs of that, and what is each student's best practice for examining the data. The information should not always be presented to students up front—give them a chance to come up against obstacles.
themes: data quality; skepticism; problem detection; pedagogy
relevance_rationale: Developing intuition for problematic data and encountering obstacles firsthand is irreplaceable experiential learning
notes: Chinoy: let students encounter data problems firsthand

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: To do more advanced data journalism, practitioners need, at a minimum, to understand how programming works. The ability to work with data and think in terms of computation is a skill broader and more necessary than any specific tool or programming language. It is vital that we don't confuse the two.
themes: computational thinking; programming literacy; tool agnosticism
relevance_rationale: Understanding how programming works endures but LLMs now enable computational thinking without writing code directly
notes: Computational thinking vs. coding as distinct but related skills

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: By using programming, journalists can think beyond rows and columns as they search for answers in data of all forms, whether structured or unstructured.
themes: computational journalism; unstructured data; programming
relevance_rationale: Programming to think beyond rows and columns endures conceptually but LLMs now handle unstructured data without manual coding
notes: Hansen: programming expands what counts as data

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: It's all about working with data in a principled way. Diakopoulos ties this to CAR and Philip Meyer's crusade to bring the scientific way of thinking into journalism: thinking methodically, thinking about how to frame an experiment, gather data, and use rigorous methods to build evidence of some finding of journalistic importance.
themes: scientific method; evidence; rigor; precision journalism
relevance_rationale: Working with data in a principled way using scientific thinking and rigorous evidence methods is permanently essential
notes: Diakopoulos: data journalism as principled empirical inquiry

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Data needs to be vetted and be expressed in a way that gets into the public's brain easily. From the investigative side, you are looking for evidence in the data.
themes: verification; public communication; evidence; storytelling
relevance_rationale: Data must be both vetted and accessible to the public -- dual requirement is permanent
notes: Donald: data must be both vetted and accessible

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Students should begin to develop a frame of mind in which they approach every story looking for data possibilities. They should understand how to use basic methods using spreadsheets and relational databases, get a primer on statistical concepts, learn how to take data findings and locate the people who illustrate those findings, and convert their data analysis into a pitch for a journalistic story.
themes: data frame of mind; spreadsheets; statistics; humanizing data; story pitch
relevance_rationale: Data frame of mind and humanizing data findings endure but spreadsheet and database skills are partially displaced by LLM code generation
notes: Model curriculum: from data analysis to story pitch pipeline

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Students will learn how to find data online, how to maintain personal records as they report stories, and how to use simple visualization methods to find new information: how keeping a timeline can help reveal discrepancies and how cross-checking sources of information may lead to new avenues of inquiry.
themes: data discovery; record keeping; timelines; cross-checking; discrepancies
relevance_rationale: Finding data online and record-keeping endure but timeline and cross-checking can now be LLM-assisted
notes: Practical data skills: timelines reveal discrepancies, cross-checks open leads

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Central concerns include: spreadsheet training, how to find data, clean it, look for patterns and outliers, and question the biases and omissions in how it was gathered. Students should also learn how to critically assess claims surrounding data. Reporting on data may go astray when it presumes this information is complete and accurate. Reporters should be trained to look for problems in data.
themes: data cleaning; bias detection; completeness; accuracy; outliers
relevance_rationale: Critical assessment of data claims, looking for problems, biases, and omissions is permanent verification skill
notes: Core DJ skills: clean, find patterns, question biases and completeness

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Two skills that should emerge from this class are to look for trends and to identify outliers. Every data point is a possible source or anecdote.
themes: trends; outliers; human sources; anecdotes
relevance_rationale: Identifying trends and outliers with every data point as potential source or anecdote is a permanent analytical skill
notes: Dual analytical skill: trends for patterns, outliers for stories

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Instruction should include discussion of the ways that different visualization methods can be misleading. The goal is not to design a graphic for publication, but to graph for the sake of understanding the data. Students may think of this as a research method or a sketchpad for further reporting.
themes: visualization as analysis; misleading graphics; exploratory methods
relevance_rationale: Visualization as research method endures but LLM-generated misleading graphics add new literacy demands
notes: Visualization as research method, not publication design

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Data journalism must be taught as a foundational method in introductory classes, a distinct theme in media law and ethics, a reporting method suitable to any specialized reporting course, and a subject in which interested students can pursue advanced coursework or a concentration.
themes: curriculum integration; data across curriculum; pedagogy
relevance_rationale: Integrating data journalism across foundations, ethics, beats, and specialization is a permanent curriculum model
notes: Four-level integration model: foundation, ethics, beats, specialization

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Legal considerations form one of the core concerns of data journalists: making public records requests can be one of the most fruitful avenues for reporting, but also one of the most frustrating. Students should also address the common misconception that data is sterile, objective, or in some sense detached from human experience. On the contrary, all data exists because someone has chosen to gather it, and the use of data has social and ethical consequences.
themes: FOI; data subjectivity; ethics; public records; data provenance
relevance_rationale: Data as never objective, always shaped by collection choices, plus FOI and ethical considerations are permanent
notes: Data is never objective: always shaped by collection choices

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Courses should include material on the verification of photos through metadata or crowdsourcing, and ethical considerations surrounding leaked or sensitive data, as well as source protection and digital security in conditions of pervasive surveillance.
themes: photo verification; metadata; digital security; source protection; leaked data
relevance_rationale: Photo verification via metadata endures but LLMs and AI-generated imagery add new verification challenges beyond metadata
notes: Verification and security as linked ethical-technical concerns

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Use Overview or DocumentCloud to explore a large cache of documents, such as the Congressional Record, Wikipedia, or a recent leak. Organize reporting for a long-form narrative piece by placing sources, quotes, and chronologies in a spreadsheet.
chapter: Model 2: Narrative Reporting and Feature Writing
themes: document analysis; Overview; DocumentCloud; spreadsheet organization
relevance_rationale: Overview and DocumentCloud for document analysis are partially displaced by LLMs that can directly analyze and summarize document caches
notes: Tools for document-driven narrative: computational doc analysis + spreadsheet chronologies

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Students should emerge from science reporting class with an understanding of the scientific method, randomized controlled experiments, statistical significance, and other factors they will encounter while reporting on topics in science and the environment.
themes: scientific method; statistical significance; science reporting; evidence evaluation
relevance_rationale: Understanding scientific method, statistical significance, and experimental design for science reporting is permanent
notes: Science literacy as prerequisite for data-driven science/environment reporting

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Methods of Collecting Data course skills: web scraping, APIs, cron jobs, bash scripting, digitizing paper documents, regular expressions, parsing text and data, fuzzy string matching, record linkage, content analysis. Tools: Python, Beautiful Soup, Mechanize, Scrapy, Tabula, SQL, MongoDB, data formats (CSV, JSON), Tesseract OCR.
chapter: Model 3: Concentration in Data and Computation
themes: web scraping; APIs; OCR; record linkage; fuzzy matching
relevance_rationale: Data collection toolkit of scraping, APIs, OCR, regex endures but LLMs substantially automate scraping, parsing, and OCR tasks
notes: Comprehensive data collection toolkit: scrape, parse, digitize, match, automate

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Advanced Data Analysis and Journalistic Algorithms course skills: Python for machine learning, clustering, classifying documents, standardizing and matching algorithms. Tools include clustering algorithms (k-means, k-nearest neighbor), topic modeling algorithms (LDA or NMF). Example: record linkage for data cleaning using machine learning to regularize names in Federal Election Commission data.
chapter: Model 3: Concentration in Data and Computation
themes: machine learning; clustering; topic modeling; record linkage; classification
relevance_rationale: ML for journalism (clustering, topic modeling, record linkage) endures conceptually but LLMs provide these capabilities with far less setup
notes: ML for journalism: clustering outliers, topic modeling documents, entity resolution

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Advanced Journalistic Text Mining: Text is data. The purpose of this class is to teach journalists to gather, analyze, and present stories using large amounts of textual data. Skills: web scraping, analyzing large bodies of text, sentiment analysis, topic modeling. Tools: Overview, DocumentCloud, Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) or Stanford NLP.
chapter: Model 3: Concentration in Data and Computation
themes: text mining; NLP; sentiment analysis; topic modeling; unstructured data
relevance_rationale: Text mining with NLP tools like NLTK/Overview is substantially displaced by LLMs that handle sentiment analysis and topic modeling natively
notes: Text-as-data pipeline: scrape, analyze with NLP, present findings

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Journalism schools should build collaborative partnerships with other disciplines. Practitioners of data-driven and computational journalism have thrived by embracing interdisciplinarity. Several journalism schools have begun to build bridges with computer science departments by opening research centers, co-teaching and cross-listing classes, and developing joint degree programs.
themes: interdisciplinary collaboration; computer science; research centers; joint degrees
relevance_rationale: Interdisciplinary collaboration between journalism and computer science departments is a permanent institutional model
notes: Structural models for cross-disciplinary DJ programs

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: There should not be a single faculty member juggling all the classes in data and computational skills, nor should guest lectures from that faculty member suffice. Journalism schools must commit to the idea that they cannot train information professionals to work in an increasingly complicated world of information without developing these crucial literacies. It must be integrated across the board.
themes: curriculum integration; faculty development; institutional commitment
relevance_rationale: Systemic integration of data skills across curriculum rather than siloed instruction is a permanent institutional requirement
notes: Systemic integration, not siloed specialist instruction

author: Charles Berret, Cheryl Phillips
extract: Given the expense of proprietary software and its inevitable obsolescence, there are few advantages to using these applications in data and computation classes instead of free and open-source ones. F/OSS software is often more secure because it can be openly vetted by security researchers.
themes: open source; free software; tool selection; security
relevance_rationale: Preference for free and open-source software in DJ education for cost, longevity, and security reasons remains valid
notes: F/OSS preferred for DJ education: cost, longevity, security

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Avoid 'tool soup, which is trying to put a pinch of everything into a story, for the sake of seeming thorough or well-rounded. Often one single tool alone is enough to tell a story at a higher plane. It is easy to go overboard and overwhelm the reader.' Put the story first and foremost, then try to find the tools to tell it.
chapter: A Cautionary Tale: It's About the Journalism, Not Just the Tools
themes: tool selection; presentation design
relevance_rationale: Story-first over tool-first thinking applies equally to AI tools as to traditional digital tools

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Pick-Stick-Dig-Dump framework for evaluating tools: (1) Pick an app that fills a specific need after checking reviews and privacy settings, (2) Stick with it by using it in daily coverage, (3) Dig into it for two to three months asking whether it saves time or enhances work, (4) Dump it after three months if it does not prove its value.
themes: tool selection; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Framework is sound but needs to account for AI tools that combine multiple capabilities and evolve faster than 3-month cycles

author: Mike Reilley
extract: When evaluating tools, assess four core attributes: Quality (does it help produce better or different work?), Time (does it save time or reduce friction?), Cost (can the newsroom and audience afford it?), and Reliability (is the tool likely to persist and is its team committed?).
themes: tool selection; workflow design; resource constraints
relevance_rationale: Quality-time-cost-access evaluation still valid but LLMs collapse multiple tool categories and change the cost calculus
notes: Caplan framework

author: Mike Reilley
extract: AI will transform journalism in five areas: Organizing (natural language queries over gathered information), Converting (translating and summarizing across languages and formats), Monitoring (tracking subtle changes in government and corporate data over time), Analyzing (identifying outliers and patterns in complex datasets), and Presenting (generating charts, infographics, audio/video versions of stories).
themes: AI adoption; automation; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Five areas are correct but understates how deeply LLMs already perform these tasks natively rather than as separate tools
notes: Caplan framework

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Teach journalism concepts, not tools. Tools are a vehicle for reporting, not a means to an end. Combine old-school shoe-leather reporting with cutting-edge digital techniques to produce data-driven and multimedia stories. Sprinkle ethics, editing and journalism into the software lessons rather than preaching ethics in long standalone slideshows.
chapter: Teaching Digital Concepts, Not Just Tools
themes: pedagogy; ethics; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Teaching concepts over tools is even more important when AI tools change rapidly

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Be a reporter first -- this is the skill from which all else flows. Be a reader second -- channel your own reading behaviors to inform what you produce. Tell one story at a time or make one comparison (X vs. Y axis) at a time. If you have to explain what your tool is telling the reader, you have made it more complicated than you need. Quality over quantity: you do not need endless maps, charts and graphs on a single story.
themes: presentation design; data visualization; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Being a reporter first and reader second are foundational human skills unaffected by LLMs
notes: Warren framework

author: Mike Reilley
extract: There is no magic to setting up a good reporting workflow. Research before you go, and then research some more. Ask basic questions: What am I writing and why? What do I want or need to get from this source and when and how are the best ways to do that?
chapter: Getting Organized: Establish a Digital Workflow
themes: workflow design; reporting methodology
relevance_rationale: Research-before-you-go and asking basic questions remain core reporting discipline regardless of AI
notes: Graves

author: Mike Reilley
extract: For longer stories, keep a high-level timeline and short bios of key players for quick reference. Maintain a spreadsheet of sources with title, phone number, email, contact dates and follow-ups. Build separate folders for images, maps, data, and records so they can be accessed quickly as both reporting resources and story embeds.
chapter: Getting Organized: Establish a Digital Workflow
themes: project management; data management; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Timeline and bio-tracking are now easily automated by LLMs that can maintain and query structured notes
notes: Graves

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Use a Google Drive file-naming system that makes sense: Assignment_(Name)_Date_StorySlugNOTES or Assignment_Names_(Dates)_Story slug. Check with editors for a specific naming system.
themes: data management; workflow design
relevance_rationale: File naming conventions still matter but LLM-powered search reduces dependence on rigid naming schemes
notes: Graves

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Nine rules for writing for the web: (1) Get the most newsworthy information in the first paragraph in 25-30 words, (2) Link to sources whenever you reference information, (3) Hotlink phrases not full URLs at four to five words maximum, (4) Indent quotes using blockquote, (5) Use brief paragraphs with 1 idea, 2-3 sentences, 4-5 typed lines, (6) Write literal SEO headlines under 75 characters in subject-verb-object structure, (7) Split articles with subheadings, (8) End with a call to action or what happens next, (9) Embed linked media such as tweets, videos, audio or images.
chapter: Get the News in the Lead
themes: web writing; SEO; presentation design; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Web writing fundamentals endure but LLMs can now generate and optimize headlines, chunking, and linking
notes: Bradshaw nine rules

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Great reporting drives great writing. If you cannot research, pull public records, interview sources and gather information, then your story will not work. It does not matter how talented a writer you are. You cannot write what you do not know, what you have not heard, what you have not seen.
themes: reporting methodology; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Reporting skill as prerequisite for good writing is a human judgment that AI cannot replace
notes: Graves and Pagel/Streckfuss

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Covering a major story over several years requires next-level organization. Keep a spreadsheet of who is testifying in order, with ages, key details of testimony and background, linked to recorded testimony files. At end of each day, capture audio files with transcripts and link them to the testimony spreadsheet. Keep key prior stories in a Google Doc for quick reference.
themes: project management; data management; long-form reporting
relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet tracking for long investigations is now supplemented by LLM-powered knowledge management tools
notes: Graves Pike County method

author: Mike Reilley
extract: When filing FOI requests, be specific about the type of record, what dates the records cover and what the topic is. Simply asking for 'all of the mayor's email correspondence' is too broad. Requesting the mayor's emails over the past three years discussing the public funding of a new bridge with the city's chief financial officer is more specific and gives the agency a better roadmap to find the records. This typically cuts down on denials.
themes: public records; FOI; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: FOI request specificity and strategy remain essential human-driven legal processes

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Triangulating journalistic sources: data, documents and human sources work in concert with one another when reporting a story. Reporters use public records, data analysis and interviews together to strengthen stories.
themes: triangulation; reporting methodology; verification
relevance_rationale: Triangulating data, documents, and human sources is a core epistemological principle unaffected by AI

author: Mike Reilley
extract: It is particularly important to use a diverse group of experts in your stories regardless of topic. Many newsrooms now track diversity in their coverage -- cross-sections of the community, how they are quoted, where they appear, and that includes expert sources. Always vet expert sources: read beyond their bio, search their name for trouble, check if they are being paid by a company to shape what they tell you, and search Google Scholar for their publications.
themes: source diversity; verification; expert sourcing
relevance_rationale: Diverse sourcing is an ethical imperative that AI cannot automate away

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Best advice for reporters using transcription tools: proceed at your own risk. The tools are fine for routine interviews and public meetings. But think twice before using them with sensitive stories and interview subjects because cloud-based services share data with third parties and respond to law enforcement requests, creating privacy and security vulnerabilities for confidential sources. For sensitive information, use a non-cloud recorder and transcribe manually.
chapter: Using Transcription Tools to Speed Reporting
themes: source protection; digital security; transcription; ethics
relevance_rationale: LLM transcription is now far more accurate than earlier tools but caution for sensitive quotes still applies

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Use transcription tools only to check quotes and for context when writing. Also take handwritten notes. Taking physical notes signals to sources that you are working. In advanced courses, require students to upload the audio file, the transcript and their handwritten notes with full source name and contact info. Spot check these files and use them to fact-check work when grading.
chapter: Using Transcription Tools to Speed Reporting
themes: verification; transcription; fact-checking; pedagogy
relevance_rationale: LLM transcription quality now rivals hand notes but maintaining parallel note-taking habit still valuable
notes: Graves method

author: Mike Reilley
extract: An old reporter's trick: do not transcribe the entire interview. As you record, keep track of the time code on the recorder. In your notebook, list the times when an interesting quote starts or ends, and work forward or backward from there. This saves many hours of time with transcriptions.
themes: efficiency; transcription; workflow design
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now transcribe full interviews cheaply and instantly, making selective time-code transcription unnecessary

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Self-editing process: step away from the story, clear your head, then read start to finish without editing as the reader would see it. Then make an editing pass for grammar, AP Style, punctuation, usage, spelling. Make a second pass for fact-checking names, data and hotlinks. Run grammar and spellcheck. Write the headline last. For longer stories, printing and editing by hand catches mistakes that backlit screens make eyes lazy to.
chapter: Editing Your Work and Writing Headlines
themes: editing; fact-checking; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Stepping away and reading as a reader is a cognitive editing practice no AI replaces

author: Mike Reilley
extract: You must fact-check any AI-generated content for plagiarism, falsehoods, outdated information and biased or made-up information. Think of AI as making suggestions: you can accept or reject them the same way you would grammar check or spell check. AI should work for you, not vice versa.
chapter: Editing Your Work and Writing Headlines
themes: AI adoption; fact-checking; verification; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Fact-checking AI output for plagiarism, falsehoods, and bias is foundational to responsible AI use

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Write SEO headlines in present tense and subject-verb-object structure (who-did-what) at 8-10 words or around 75 characters maximum containing keywords from the story. Do not use puns and metaphors because search engines are literal. Do not write the headline as a title or label. Use Google Trends to test which keywords are searched more to choose the better headline term.
themes: SEO; web writing; presentation design
relevance_rationale: SEO headline craft persists but LLMs can now draft and A/B test headlines at scale

author: Mike Reilley
extract: The general rule when searching for a person, a story, or just about anything, is to start specific and work your way out from there. Type the exact term using quotes to denote an exact phrase and see what comes up. More often you will need to cut out a word or search for a more umbrella term, and then skim or read whatever comes up.
themes: search techniques; research methodology
relevance_rationale: Start-specific-go-broad search strategy remains sound methodology for any search tool including AI
notes: Sunne

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Use 'filetype:' search operator to find specific data formats (xlsx, pdf, doc, ppt) on the open web. Use 'site:' operator to search within a specific website, which is particularly helpful with government websites that have poor built-in search engines. Use the AROUND operator in all caps to find two terms within a specified number of words of each other.
themes: search techniques; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Filetype and site operators still work but LLM-powered search and document retrieval now offer alternative discovery paths

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Set up Google Alerts with search operators to monitor specific agencies. Example: site:lla.la.gov investigative ('new orleans' OR 'orleans parish') pings you when the Louisiana Legislative Auditor posts a new investigative audit mentioning New Orleans. Google Alerts arrive within a day of a new result -- not instantaneous but good for staying on top of specific files, agencies or news topics.
themes: monitoring; search techniques; automation
relevance_rationale: Google Alerts still useful but LLM agents can now monitor and summarize broader source sets
notes: Sunne

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Google Dataset Search lets you search millions of datasets posted by academic researchers, nonprofits, companies and government agencies. Filter results by when data was posted, format and whether you must pay. Google does not fact-check the datasets -- it merely guides you to them and provides information so you can vet the data yourself. It is an incredibly efficient tool for finding clean datasets on deadline.
themes: data acquisition; data discovery; verification
relevance_rationale: Google Dataset Search remains relevant but LLMs can now also discover and interpret datasets conversationally

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Be careful not to draw assumptions from Google Trends surges. Search the dates in Google News thoroughly before using them in a story. Explain the Trends chart y-axis to readers -- the 0 to 100 represents a normalization, not actual traffic numbers. When attributing results, use 'According to an analysis of Google search data.' Do not draw gross generalizations: just because candidates are searched more does not mean they are more popular.
themes: data analysis; verification; data visualization; Google Trends
relevance_rationale: Caution about misinterpreting Google Trends is an analytical reasoning skill unaffected by AI

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Use the Wayback Machine 'Changes' tool to see when a web page was updated. Select two captures and click Compare to see side-by-side views. This is helpful for seeing if an organization is changing or hiding something on its website.
themes: verification; web archiving; accountability
relevance_rationale: Wayback Machine comparison for tracking web changes remains a unique archival verification method
notes: Silverman

author: Mike Reilley
extract: When searching an online database, you can sometimes get it to return all of the records by leaving the search bar blank. You can also try entering wildcards like '*' and '%%'. These tricks do not work with every database, but they are always worth trying.
themes: data acquisition; search techniques
relevance_rationale: Database query tricks persist but LLM-powered scraping and API access offer more systematic alternatives
notes: Sunne

author: Mike Reilley
extract: To find older datasets on government sites that only show current data, hack the web address. Agencies have consistent URLs for naming files -- simply change the ID, date or section in a URL to pull up archived documents. Example: changing '20190430' to '20180430' in a DOD report URL retrieves the same location's report from a year earlier.
chapter: Hacking a Web Address to Find Archived Data
themes: data acquisition; search techniques; web scraping
relevance_rationale: URL hacking for older datasets still works but LLMs can now automate pattern detection in government URL structures
notes: Sunne

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Steps to debunk fake news: (1) Think before sharing and read beyond the headline, (2) Search if other outlets are reporting it, (3) Check fact-checking sites like Snopes and PolitiFact and Google Fact Check Explorer, (4) Check the outlet and author's credibility and social media, (5) Look at the publish date and time, (6) Examine links and sources used -- are they attributed and current?, (7) Look for questionable quotes and AI-generated image tells like disproportionate eyes/ears/hands, (8) Beware of confirmation bias.
themes: fact-checking; verification; misinformation; media literacy
relevance_rationale: Critical thinking steps for debunking fake news are human judgment skills that AI assists but cannot replace

author: Mike Reilley
extract: When fact-checking images, assess five dimensions: Provenance (is it the original content? does it look altered?), Source (who captured it? use reverse image search), Date (when was it captured?), Location (where was it captured? use Google Earth), and Motivation (why was it captured? consider the context and reputation of who is sharing it).
themes: fact-checking; verification; image forensics; visual journalism
relevance_rationale: Five-dimension image verification framework endures but AI vision models now automate several of these checks
notes: First Draft News framework

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Use reverse image search in Google Images and TinEye interchangeably as they run different algorithms and provide sometimes different results. Scroll through results to find the earliest posts and see where the photo first appeared. Use the Tools button to filter by date. If a photo may be doctored, use FotoForensics to check metadata and see if the photo has been layered and flattened/compressed.
chapter: How to Do a Reverse Image Search
themes: fact-checking; verification; image forensics
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search still valuable but AI vision models and multimodal search now provide additional verification paths

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Solutions for building trust with audiences: Be Right (explain how stories developed and changed over time), Be Your Community (be knowledgeable about local community issues), Be Real (be authentic and own up to mistakes), Be Neutral (leave personal opinions out of news coverage). If just one journalist from a local news source is not trusted, audiences are overwhelmingly less likely to use the source overall.
themes: trust; audience engagement; ethics; transparency
relevance_rationale: Trust-building strategies with audiences are relationship and transparency practices unaffected by AI tools
notes: RTDNA/Magid

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Apply a scientific method to building audience campaigns: (1) Outline a specific audience goal at the beginning, (2) Formulate hypotheses to test, (3) Track as much data as you can using distinct bit.ly URLs to measure click-through rates, (4) At campaign end, revisit original hypotheses -- moments when hypotheses are wrong are often most useful, (5) Document findings immediately, including what you would do differently.
chapter: Developing Social Audiences at Large Newsrooms
themes: audience engagement; analytics; experimentation; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Scientific method for audience engagement is a strategic framework independent of AI tools
notes: Johnson/Guardian

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Steps to verify information found on social media: (1) Be skeptical -- always read past the headline, (2) Review the original source's social media accounts for reliability, (3) Cross-check with other sources and contact directly, (4) Check the geo-location of the post, (5) Seek corroboration on social media via image search and other eyewitness accounts, (6) Seek official corroboration by calling authorities, (7) If an image seems suspicious, reverse image search it, (8) Use Google Earth to verify photo locations.
themes: verification; fact-checking; social media; crowdsourcing
relevance_rationale: Skepticism and multi-step social media verification remain essential human editorial judgment

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Twitter threads are an effective way to roll out investigative projects. Keep a 'data diary' of the reporting, analysis and writing/editing process, then explain that reporting process to the reader through the thread. This makes your reporting transparent and builds trust with the reader. Incorporating graphics, video and photos into threads helps readers notice them in feeds and conveys data and background.
themes: transparency; trust; social media; data journalism; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Twitter threads as rollout strategy needs updating for platform changes and LLM-assisted content distribution

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Use analytics to look at what you can change or improve in coverage, design or presentation rather than what can be cut. Track social analytics over several weeks and months before making brash decisions about adjusting coverage and resources. Export Twitter analytics as spreadsheets and sort by impressions, engagements, replies and URL clicks to find patterns in which topics, days and times perform best.
themes: analytics; audience engagement; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Analytics for editorial improvement endures but LLMs now enable more sophisticated audience analysis

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Crowdsourcing is where mainstream media ask for or search for eyewitness accounts from breaking stories they are not staffing or have not arrived at yet. It is best practice to ask the person posting who shot the photo and get that person's permission to use it, though you can embed tweets rights-free.
chapter: Social Media: Past, Present and Future
themes: crowdsourcing; source acquisition; ethics; user-generated content
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing concept endures but LLMs can now help filter, verify, and synthesize UGC at scale

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Do not speculate on social media -- write only what you observe or know. Never delete tweets, but correct errors. Alert your audience when you will be live-tweeting. Include observations and try to put the reader into the scene. Live-tweeting is the best on-deadline writing: there is little time to overwrite or overthink, which produces the most powerful inverted pyramid leads.
chapter: More Social Media Tips from Chris Graves for Covering Trials and Breaking News
themes: social media; live reporting; accuracy; trust
relevance_rationale: Do not speculate and correct errors publicly are ethical principles unaffected by AI
notes: Graves

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Fact-checkers need automated tools from platforms that match fact-check articles to misinformation that is being repeated to enable them to work faster. Politicians often repeat the same false claims several times, so building a library of already-debunked claims enables near-real-time fact-checking of future speeches.
chapter: Fact-Checking and the Rise of Fake News
themes: fact-checking; automation; misinformation; workflow design
relevance_rationale: Automated fact-check matching is now more feasible with LLMs performing semantic matching at scale
notes: Adhikari/Nicholson

author: Mike Reilley
extract: For publications of any size, think about what the primary audience objective is with any platform and then make sure your work there supports that goal. When approaching existing online communities like Reddit, bring added value to a discussion within your coverage areas -- host an AMA with reporters who can provide expertise or comment on trending threads with additional context from previous reporting.
chapter: Developing Social Audiences at Large Newsrooms
themes: audience engagement; social media; distribution strategy
relevance_rationale: Platform strategy based on audience objectives is editorial judgment independent of AI
notes: Johnson/Guardian

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Reporters have many options for finding datasets. They can file a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request, like Ramos did for the Chicago police reports for his story. They can build the dataset themselves, like Ramos did when he merged the stolen cars dataset with the police report data that included the cars' make and model.
themes: FOIA; dataset building; data merging; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Dataset discovery methods still valid but LLMs can now assist with FOIA drafting and data portal navigation
notes: Two pathways for data: FOIA requests and self-built datasets through merging

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Data scraping, also known as web scraping, is the process of importing information from a website, a PDF or other document into a spreadsheet or local file on a computer. It's an efficient way to get data from the web beyond a download button on a page. A scraping formula creates a link between the web page being scraped and the spreadsheet. Then, when the web page updates, so does the spreadsheet. This saves time, is more efficient and produces cleaner results than cut-and-paste.
themes: scraping; data acquisition; automation; efficiency
relevance_rationale: Web scraping concept endures but LLMs can now write scraping code, parse PDFs, and extract structured data conversationally
notes: Definition and rationale for web scraping as linked live data pipeline

author: Mike Reilley
extract: In two sentences, Eyre encapsulated the issue using three key data points, the last of which he calculated by dividing the 780 million pills by the state's population. More importantly, the 433 pain pills per resident brought the story into a crystal-clear focus and scaled the issue in an alarming way.
themes: data storytelling; scaling; per-capita calculation; narrative
relevance_rationale: Using key data points to encapsulate a story is narrative craft that AI assists but does not replace
notes: Per-capita calculation as technique for making data relatable to readers

author: Mike Reilley
extract: When writing for the web, specifically mobile, a paragraph should never be longer than three sentences in order to maintain clarity and readability on those devices. Spreading the data throughout the story and balancing it with human-centered anecdotes, quotes and context helps increase reader understanding of the issue.
themes: data storytelling; mobile writing; readability; humanizing data
relevance_rationale: Short paragraphs for mobile readability is a presentation principle that applies regardless of who writes the text
notes: Heather Cherone on mobile-first data writing and balancing numbers with human stories

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Cherone favors keeping one or two statistics per paragraph and keeping the stories focused on the people impacted by the numbers, not just the statistics themselves. She and many other data journalists are the first to tell you: Great data stories don't read like data stories. They are stories about how issues impact human beings.
themes: data storytelling; humanizing data; narrative; reader engagement
relevance_rationale: Limiting statistics per paragraph and centering people is storytelling judgment AI does not replace
notes: Principle: great data stories center people not numbers

author: Mike Reilley
extract: The number 29,912 can be rounded up: Nearly 30,000. Round off percentages, too: 62.2% is 62%; 79.9% is 80%; 75% is three-fourths or three in four. The percentages 12.55% of women and 25% of men can be 1 in 8 women and 1 in 4 men. Instead of a 100% increase, say it doubled. It's easier for the reader to understand. Break data into bullet-point lists and pullout boxes.
themes: data storytelling; simplification; readability; numeracy
relevance_rationale: Rounding numbers for readability is a presentation principle that persists regardless of AI
notes: LaFleur and Hacker NICAR 2022 tips for simplifying numbers in stories

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Could the data be better presented in a chart, infographic or database? Does it need to be written?
themes: presentation format; data visualization; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Deciding between chart and text is now augmented by LLMs that can suggest and generate visualizations
notes: LaFleur and Hacker - question whether text is best format for data

author: Mike Reilley
extract: You always, always, always work off a COPY of your dataset in case you mess something up.
themes: data integrity; workflow; data management
relevance_rationale: Working on a copy of the dataset is a fundamental data integrity practice that applies to any workflow
notes: Pro tip on always working from a copy of original data

author: Mike Reilley
extract: A data dictionary provides a detailed description of the dataset, which includes the meaning of each column, the type of the data and any other relevant information.
themes: data dictionary; documentation; data management; metadata
relevance_rationale: Data dictionaries remain essential but LLMs can now auto-generate and interpret them from raw datasets
notes: Exercise introducing data dictionaries as standard practice

author: Mike Reilley
extract: As with any story, data-driven pieces need diverse sources and voices. This goes beyond interviewing people impacted by the issue. It also involves actively seeking diverse expert sources in stories on any subject.
chapter: Diversity, Equity and Inclusion in Data Journalism
themes: diversity; sourcing; equity; inclusion; framing
relevance_rationale: Diverse sourcing beyond data subjects is an ethical reporting principle unaffected by AI
notes: Principle that diverse sourcing applies to data stories not just traditional reporting

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Mojo kind of flips the script on you and says: No Picture, No Story. So we start out by learning how to report in pictures as a photojournalist, then working with shots as a filmmaker, and then learning how to write with those pictures like an editor, and then writing to those pictures like a scriptwriter.
themes: visual storytelling; mobile journalism; workflow; multimedia
