relevance_rationale: Visual-first mobile journalism storytelling is a narrative principle independent of AI tools
notes: Montgomery on visual-first reporting workflow for mobile journalism

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Zoom with your feet, not the lens. Move closer to your subject and frame the shot. You'll get better-quality video and audio. Use the lens zoom on your phone only if you're in a restricted area and can't get closer to the subject. Remember, you're after sequences: wide, medium and close, so you'll want to move around a lot and shoot a variety of distances and angles.
themes: video technique; mobile journalism; multimedia; sequences
relevance_rationale: Physical proximity to subjects for better video is a craft skill AI cannot replace
notes: Physical zoom over digital zoom; sequence composition 50% closeups, 25% medium, 25% wide

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Be careful with editing too tightly. Quotes can be edited down so tightly that it changes the meaning of what the person was saying. Remember to edit to the full context of what was being said, even if you have to paraphrase in a voice-over.
themes: editing ethics; context; accuracy; video editing
relevance_rationale: Caution against over-editing quotes is an ethical principle regardless of whether AI or humans edit
notes: Commandment 4: context over soundbites in video editing

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Focus Thy Story Into Three Words. 'Who Did What?' or Noun-Verb-Object. It's a simple way to start the editing process. My students are sometimes overwhelmed by all of the video they have. But editing an hour of content into a three-minute mobile video can be daunting. Start with 'who did what,' then decide how you want to organize it: narrative, timeline, process, chapters, etc. Once you have that organizational structure, editing becomes much easier.
themes: story structure; editing workflow; narrative; simplification
relevance_rationale: Three-word story focus (noun-verb-object) is a narrative discipline that transcends tools
notes: Noun-Verb-Object framework for organizing video editing

author: Mike Reilley
extract: When you pitch a story idea to editors, think in terms of these types of videos to have a plan going in. Think of a 'shot sheet' of sequences you can shoot. But also be flexible; many times my students returned from shooting a video and shifted to a new type of video format during the shoot. Let the content dictate the format, not vice versa.
themes: planning; flexibility; story format; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Pre-planning video types before shooting is a production planning skill unaffected by AI
notes: Pre-production planning with shot sheets but letting content drive format

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Journalists should understand what the native camera can do and set it up for their needs. For example, Apple users should go to Settings > Camera and explore the list of options. Another tip, while exploring Camera Settings, is to set video frame rates to match the country's electricity hertz cycle. This will avoid lights flickering in the video.
themes: camera settings; mobile journalism; video technique
relevance_rationale: Native camera setup advice needs updating for AI-enhanced camera features and computational photography
notes: Rob Layton on configuring native camera including frame rate to match local Hz

author: Mike Reilley
extract: It's also important to measure your screen time. Journalists also should organize their apps in a way that makes sense to them, so they can find them quickly without having to search for them. If you have a lot of apps, like I do, you should organize them into folders by type of tool.
themes: tool management; workflow; mobile journalism; productivity
relevance_rationale: Managing screen time and app organization is personal productivity discipline independent of AI
notes: Reilley on organizing mobile tools for fast access during breaking news

author: Mike Reilley
extract: They pulled public records of sidewalk complaints and tracked how many were repaired ... and which ones have not. The records weren't hard to find. They're posted on the City of Chicago Data Portal.
themes: public records; data portals; accountability; verification
relevance_rationale: Using public records to track government responsiveness is a reporting methodology AI does not replace
notes: Using open data portals to verify government responsiveness to complaints

author: Mike Reilley
extract: The concept was simple: Students would use their smartphones to shoot photos of poor sidewalk conditions and geolocate them. They would upload the photos to Tumblr from their phones and write a short description of the conditions and the precise location of the issue.
themes: crowdsourcing; geolocation; mobile journalism; data collection
relevance_rationale: Smartphone geolocated photo collection now augmented by AI image analysis and automated geolocation
notes: Chicago Stumbler as model for mobile crowd-sourced data collection with geolocation

author: Mike Reilley
extract: If something goes wrong with your digital security, GIJN recommends reaching out to nonprofit groups dedicated to detecting and tracking attacks and training users.
themes: digital security; journalist safety; support networks
relevance_rationale: Digital security incident response through nonprofit groups is an organizational resource unrelated to AI

author: Mike Reilley
extract: You can scrape data from the FDIC Failed Banks List (or any other site with a table of data) with this basic scraping formula.
themes: data scraping; spreadsheets; data collection
relevance_rationale: Basic table scraping formulas are now trivially handled by LLMs that can scrape, parse, and structure any web table

author: Mike Reilley
extract: The tool allows you to upload documents and analyze a document up to 75,000 words. You can upload multiple documents.
themes: AI tools; document analysis; reporting workflow
relevance_rationale: Specific tool for document upload and analysis is superseded by LLMs that natively analyze documents up to millions of tokens

author: Mike Reilley
extract: She pulled data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics CPI Average Price Database, a one-screen data search where you can look up product average prices by U.S. cities, regions, etc.
chapter: Exercise 8: Canva Exercise -- How Much Does That Greasy Cheeseburger Really Cost With Inflation?
themes: government data; data collection; data visualization
relevance_rationale: BLS data retrieval process still valid but LLMs can now query and compute from such databases directly

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Instead of outlining a step-by-step process, we're just offering you tips on how to build the map. It's best to base the map on the deaths per 10k rather than total deaths. The former factors in population.
chapter: Exercise 7: Flourish.studio -- Death by Heart Disease by State Map (by Rate)
themes: data visualization; normalization; rate calculation
relevance_rationale: Map-building tips endure conceptually but AI now automates geocoding, base map selection, and layer generation

author: Mike Reilley
extract: Business reporters use this to track companies and their stock prices across multiple markets.
chapter: Exercise 2: Scraping Data from Google Finance
themes: data scraping; financial data; monitoring; spreadsheets
relevance_rationale: Specific stock-tracking tool reference is superseded by LLM agents that can query financial data in real time

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If your government, region or city has an open data portal, that should have regular updates. If there's an office of national statistics, sign up for updates. FOI requests are often a good source of data. It's a good idea to have data regularly come to you — either by email or RSS.
chapter: The scouting mission: where's the data?
themes: data acquisition; open data; FOI; beat monitoring
relevance_rationale: Monitoring open data portals, FOI requests, and setting up data feeds is a beat-building practice that endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you are reporting on a specific field like crime, health, education, welfare or the environment, try to find bodies (local, national and international) that publish data regularly in that area.
chapter: The scouting mission: where's the data?
themes: beat monitoring; data acquisition; domain expertise
relevance_rationale: Building domain expertise by identifying bodies that publish data regularly is a journalistic practice unaffected by LLMs

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Given the choice, always avoid PDFs.
themes: file formats; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Avoiding PDFs was critical when extraction was hard; LLMs now handle PDF extraction well, though structured formats remain preferable

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: What the data contains doesn't really matter here: the point is the exercises you'll be going through in looking at it. You can apply the same process to most regular public datasets, whether it's employment data, environmental information, or weather.
themes: transferable skills; analytical process; replicability
relevance_rationale: Transferability of analytical processes across datasets is an enduring epistemological principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Building contacts is no less important in data journalism than other forms of journalism: these are the people who can alert you to the existence of data, or even leak it to you.
themes: source relationships; data acquisition; human sources
relevance_rationale: Building human source contacts for data tips and leaks is a relational practice AI cannot replace

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Every dataset will have stories it can tell. Here are just some typical stories often found in data: top of the pops/flops, holding up a mirror, a freak occurrence, a claim debunked or supported, trend, postcode lottery, juking the stats, victims/winners/losers, things happen together, it's not working.
themes: story types; analytical frameworks; newsworthiness
relevance_rationale: Taxonomy of data story types (trends, anomalies, comparisons, etc.) is a framework for editorial judgment that endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Spend five minutes looking at the fields (column labels) in your dataset and five minutes going through that list and writing down potential data stories. The dataset alone may not answer your questions — but it may set you on the journey to doing so.
themes: story discovery; hypothesis generation; exploratory analysis
relevance_rationale: Column-label scanning for story ideas endures but LLMs can now rapidly surface potential stories from dataset structures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You will often find yourself distracted by many different possible stories in the data — but if you're up against a deadline then you'll need to be focused. Data has so many potential stories that it can be easy to get distracted following multiple avenues of enquiry.
themes: editorial judgment; time management; focus
relevance_rationale: Focus and editorial judgment under deadline pressure is a human editorial skill unaffected by AI

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The quickest way to crack open our data and get a good overview of it is by using a pivot table. A pivot table can aggregate figures so we can combine all payments by company to see how much they got in total. Pivot tables can also count things.
chapter: The crowbar of data journalism: pivot tables
themes: pivot tables; aggregation; spreadsheets; exploratory analysis
relevance_rationale: Pivot tables as analytical tool endure but LLMs can now perform aggregations and counts conversationally without spreadsheet expertise

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you have a dataset it's a good idea to look through the fields and think of possible stories to tell about the subjects. We've narrowed our field to two strong candidates for the 'who' part of our story. The 'what' of the story needs to go into the Values field.
chapter: Who might your story be about?
themes: story structure; who-what framework; analytical process
relevance_rationale: Who-what framework for structuring data stories is an editorial thinking pattern that endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: In most cases adding columns to a pivot table will complicate the table to the point where any clarity is lost. Instead, it's generally better to create a new pivot table or use the report filter. Keep things simple wherever you can.
themes: simplicity; pivot tables; analytical clarity
relevance_rationale: Simplicity principle for pivot tables endures but the specific spreadsheet techniques are now supplemented by LLM-assisted analysis

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You'll notice that many of these stories don't require any further work on the data — so at this point it's time to make a clean exit, and start speaking to people.
chapter: Bringing the cream to the top
themes: human sources; verification; reporting
relevance_rationale: Knowing when to leave the data and start talking to people is an editorial judgment that endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Advanced filters allow you to use wildcards to find payments that have something in common. Wildcards allow us to be much more open in our criteria. Look for patterns in your data when filtering — for example, adding a space to exclude false matches like CAR matching CARE.
themes: filtering; wildcards; spreadsheets; pattern matching
relevance_rationale: Wildcard filtering in spreadsheets endures conceptually but LLMs can now handle pattern matching and fuzzy text queries directly

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Searching for cost codes using Google search operators like filetype:xls, filetype:pdf, or site:birmingham.gov.uk can help narrow the search. Sometimes you will find a spreadsheet or document which lists all the codes in your data and adds extra context.
chapter: Finding related data - search techniques of the professionals
themes: search operators; data acquisition; contextual data
relevance_rationale: Google search operators for finding related data endure but LLMs now offer additional ways to discover contextual datasets

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When making an FOI request relating to data: request digital spreadsheet format (CSV or XLS); ask if different parts of codes have particular meanings; try requesting the data dictionary; anticipate any objections that may be made under exemptions; remember you can request documents as well as data.
chapter: Finding related data - search techniques of the professionals
themes: FOI; data acquisition; data dictionaries; file formats
relevance_rationale: FOI request best practices like requesting CSV format and data dictionaries are procedural knowledge independent of AI

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: As FOI requests often take weeks or even months, it's worth making these requests even before you plan to chase any stories, so you have basic contextual data available when the next datasets come out.
chapter: Finding related data - search techniques of the professionals
themes: FOI; time management; workflow planning
relevance_rationale: Pre-emptive FOI requests before stories arise is a workflow planning practice unaffected by LLMs

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Remember to treat data as a source like any other. It is there to be questioned, followed-up and cross-referenced. Data is compiled by humans and published by humans. It is humans who decide when to gather it and how to classify it. All of these decisions influence what the final data looks like.
themes: verification; data skepticism; source criticism; data quality
relevance_rationale: Treating data as a source to be questioned and cross-referenced is a foundational verification principle

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You mustn't take replies at face value: it's just another source. Nor should you report it as 'he said, she said'. Look for evidence, revisit your data, speak to an independent expert, or whatever will help you be confident which party is correct, and no one is getting hurt unjustifiably.
themes: verification; triangulation; editorial ethics; harm minimisation
relevance_rationale: Triangulation and avoiding he-said-she-said reporting by seeking evidence is a core journalistic standard

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Once you have your story lead, there's no need to waste more time in the data. Turning that lead into a story means adding context, colour, and people. The five Ws and a H are useful starting points for reaction and explanation.
themes: storytelling; human sources; reporting; context
relevance_rationale: Adding context, colour, and people to data leads is a storytelling imperative that endures

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Before you visualise any data, ask yourself whether you are telling a story about comparison, composition, distribution, or relationships. The key to clear data visualisation is to strip the data down to bare essentials — try to avoid having more than four or five data points. Use one colour for your focal point and another for everything else.
themes: data visualisation; chart selection; simplicity; design
relevance_rationale: Chart selection principles endure but LLMs can now suggest appropriate visualizations and generate them from data descriptions

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Your story lead may have come from data, but the story itself doesn't have to have numbers — and it should certainly have people. What makes us care about it is reading about the woman who has been unemployed for a year or the couple who had to sell their house. Never forget that any data you work with is a means to an end, not the end itself.
chapter: Why does it matter - why do I care?
themes: storytelling; humanisation; editorial purpose; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Humanizing data stories with real people is a journalistic imperative that no AI can replace

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A good story answers two questions in its first few pars: why does this story matter, and why do I care? The impact is the key piece of information here.
chapter: Why does it matter - why do I care?
themes: storytelling; newsworthiness; impact; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Story impact and audience relevance as editorial criteria are human judgments that endure

author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Consider building relationships with FOI officers, data analysts, statisticians, academics and others who work with data directly and can explain the problems with it.
themes: source relationships; collaboration; data literacy
relevance_rationale: Building relationships with FOI officers, data analysts, and academics is a human networking practice

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Data journalism involves investigative, in-depth, precision, computer-assisted and analytical journalism, to which three fruits of technological innovation are added: large volumes of data, interactive visualization, and incorporation of the programmer into the journalistic team.
themes: definition; interdisciplinarity; team composition; data journalism identity
relevance_rationale: The definition incorporating programmers on teams needs updating since LLMs now serve as programming intermediaries for journalists

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: There are at least four different products that arise from data journalism: data-driven articles, interactive visualizations, open datasets, and news apps.
themes: outputs; news apps; visualisation; open data
relevance_rationale: The four DJ product types -- articles, interactive visualizations, open datasets, news apps -- remain valid output categories

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: In countries where there are no public information access laws or these don't work as they should, media outlets and organizations can devote themselves to the search, extraction and opening of public data. Since there are data that governments do not allow access to, media outlets can venture into that territory.
themes: open data; media role; access to information; civic function
relevance_rationale: Media outlets role in extracting and opening data where FOI laws fail is a civic function that endures regardless of AI tools

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: For data journalism one can do without many things, since there are free digital tools, but what we cannot do without is data. It is a necessary condition without which we cannot even begin to formulate any journalistic hypothesis.
themes: data as prerequisite; hypothesis formation; open data
relevance_rationale: Data as the sine qua non prerequisite for DJ -- you cannot begin without it -- remains true regardless of AI capabilities

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Visualizing data is not the same as accessing it. For data to be accessible it must be shown in a reusable format. An official document uploaded to Flickr as an image is not accessible — one would have to resort to OCR to extract the data, and these programs don't always work efficiently.
chapter: Visualizing data is not the same as accessing it
themes: data access; file formats; reusability; open data
relevance_rationale: LLMs with vision capabilities can now extract data from images and PDFs far more reliably than the OCR tools described here

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: When a CSV data file is downloaded, the columns must be restructured using Text to Columns in the Data tab, selecting Delimited with Tab and Comma separators.
chapter: Visualizing data is not the same as accessing it
themes: CSV; spreadsheets; data cleaning; file formats
relevance_rationale: Manual CSV restructuring with Text to Columns is now handled automatically by LLMs and modern data tools that auto-detect delimiters

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Short chronicles are suggested, with forceful conclusions and an important, dominant visualization in the article, when telling a story. Without an appropriate visualization accompanying the analysis of that data, it would be impossible to simply visualize the variables under study.
chapter: Short chronicles, with forceful conclusions and a strong visualization
themes: storytelling; visualisation; brevity; narrative structure
relevance_rationale: Short narratives with forceful conclusions and dominant visualizations remain sound storytelling principles

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Comparison is perhaps one of the most used practices in journalism, but when a large amount of numerical information must be processed, the matter becomes complicated. Therefore, different ways of visualizing the dataset are used; in this way, we can better analyze certain processes and changes not detected at a glance.
chapter: Short chronicles, with forceful conclusions and a strong visualization
themes: comparison; visualisation; pattern detection; analytical method
relevance_rationale: Using visualization to detect patterns not visible in raw numbers is an analytical principle that endures even with AI-assisted analysis

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Stating that data journalism requires a specialized visualization team as a sine qua non condition would establish an excessively large and dangerous gap between some and others. For those who have not received formal instruction, simpler ready-to-use resources remain.
chapter: For visualization beginners: ChartGo and, afterward, Many Eyes
themes: accessibility; skills gap; resource constraints; democratisation
relevance_rationale: LLMs and AI-powered chart tools have dramatically lowered the visualization skills gap, making specialized teams less of a prerequisite

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: A GIS is software that allows a better visual representation of numerical data, as these are distributed geographically and spatially. Census data, for example, tend to be boring; although, managed through GIS programs, they can offer a clear view of the variables under study.
chapter: A GIS allows a better visual representation of numerical data
themes: GIS; mapping; spatial analysis; visualisation
relevance_rationale: GIS for spatial analysis and geographic data visualization remains a valid technique; AI enhances but does not replace spatial reasoning

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Mashups allow the combination of data that exists in different sources, such as YouTube, Flickr, Google Maps and Google Earth. The scenario in which mashups are developed is incredibly versatile and new resources appear every day.
chapter: Mashups allow combining data from different sources
themes: mashups; data integration; hybrid applications; web tools
relevance_rationale: Mashup tools like Flickr/Google Maps integration described here have been superseded by modern APIs and LLM-powered data integration

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Google Earth is very useful when investigating companies or cases involving properties acquired by public officials, or the ecological impact of pollution. Google's advanced search allows finding places, people, companies by selecting the KMZ/KML format that returns Google Earth views.
chapter: Mashups allow combining data from different sources
themes: Google Earth; investigation; geolocation; verification
relevance_rationale: Google Earth for investigating properties endures but satellite imagery AI and geospatial LLM tools have expanded these capabilities significantly

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Data journalism requires permanent training and knowledge of subjects generally far from the preferences of the conventional journalist. One must understand how the Public Administration works, know how to interpret laws, decrees and ordinances. If a journalist does not understand how the institutions of their country function, they will find it difficult to embark on the path successfully.
themes: training; institutional knowledge; skills development; domain expertise
relevance_rationale: Understanding public administration, interpreting laws, and institutional knowledge are domain expertise requirements no LLM replaces

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Skills developed in web searching: using Google in the traditional way is not enough. One must learn to get more out of advanced searches, by format, by domain and by date; but also, in the absence of a national open data initiative, monitor the possible official sites where there is data.
themes: search skills; advanced search; data acquisition; monitoring
relevance_rationale: Advanced Google search techniques by format, domain, and date have been largely supplanted by LLM-powered search and data discovery tools

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Have basic mathematics and statistics knowledge, both descriptive and inferential: a journalist who is innumerate will not have sufficient capacity to face the process known as the interview with the numbers. This is of particular interest when it comes to analysis of public budgets, taxes, crime and environmental contamination.
themes: numeracy; statistics; quantitative literacy; analytical skills
relevance_rationale: Basic numeracy endures as essential but LLMs can now scaffold the interview-with-numbers process for journalists with weaker math backgrounds

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: The data journalist must always consider the possibility that, on occasion, there are no stories behind the data. Data journalism involves hard work — one must access dozens, if not hundreds or thousands of spreadsheets. Sometimes the routine is disappointing, because you won't always find what you're looking for.
themes: expectations; persistence; null results; work ethic
relevance_rationale: Accepting that data may yield no story and that DJ requires persistence through disappointing results is a mindset principle that endures

author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: Often, the volume of data is so large that it is impossible to find a story unless an application is designed that allows us to group and analyze variables, for example, by geographic location, by date, by company name. News apps are the fruit of the joint work of journalists and computer programmers.
themes: news apps; programmer collaboration; scale; team composition
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now group and analyze large-volume variables without custom apps, reducing the dependency on programmer-journalist collaboration for this step

source_title: Manual de Periodismo de Datos Iberoamericano
author: Felipe Perry, Miguel Paz (eds.)
extract: The work of collecting, processing, and delivering data in adverse circumstances has produced a form of learning that should keep us alert so as not to fall into the trap of easy shortcuts when processing data and creating visualization formats. Rigor, an obsession with details, and the determination to tackle issues of social impact are elements that will enable the generation of relevant, well-told, and well-documented stories.
themes: rigor; data quality; social impact; verification
relevance_rationale: Rigor, detail obsession, and social-impact framing are timeless editorial principles unaffected by LLMs
notes: From editors' introduction; framed around Latin American context of limited data access

source_title: Manual de Periodismo de Datos Iberoamericano
author: Felipe Perry, Miguel Paz (eds.)
extract: Philip Meyer, tired of newspapers being filled with politician interviews and official press releases, decided he would create his own source by building a database. He investigated campaign financing of board members and demonstrated that 65 percent of the campaign contributions originated from insurance companies. This marks the origins of precision journalism and its sociological research methods as the precursor to data journalism.
themes: precision journalism; database construction; investigative methods
relevance_rationale: Historical epistemological grounding of precision journalism remains relevant as foundational context
notes: Historical precedent from Philip Meyer at Miami Herald (1959)

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Computer scientists know how to handle data, but journalists are the ones who know how to ask questions so that the data tell a story. It is journalists who, in contact with political and economic power, use their instinct to organize data and uncover information that the powerful perhaps prefer to keep hidden among trillions of numbers and that should not surface as a journalistic headline. And it is also journalists who know how to turn cold numbers into a compelling story that captures readers.
chapter: Big data and virtual public opinion
themes: data analysis; storytelling; journalist role; investigative instinct
relevance_rationale: Journalists knowing how to ask questions and turn numbers into compelling stories is a human editorial function LLMs cannot replace

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Working with numerical data, whether in large or small quantities, remains subjective and can in no way aspire to become the truth. The way of obtaining data, of quantifying what is human, is always subjective and depends on what we measure and how we measure it. Big data only reinforces the idea that we must know very well the measure of that datum and its source. Moreover, the algorithms for cross-referencing or searching data are also subjective and depend on what the algorithm's creator thinks.
chapter: Ethical and critical questions about big data
themes: data quality; subjectivity; measurement; algorithms; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Subjectivity in data measurement and the need to understand methodology and source critically endures and applies equally to LLM outputs

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Big data is prone to apophenia, which is the pernicious tendency to see patterns, connections, or both in random events and meaningless data. Leinweber (2007) demonstrated that by using data mining techniques we can prove a spurious correlation between butter production in Bangladesh and changes in the Standard & Poor's 500 Index.
chapter: Ethical and critical questions about big data
themes: data quality; correlation; patterns; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Warning about apophenia and spurious correlations in big data is even more relevant with LLMs that can pattern-match at scale

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: When one analyzes, for example, a Facebook friends list or a Twitter follower list, one finds an agglomeration of entities ranging from friends, work colleagues, and family members to politicians, celebrities, and companies. The results of such analyses of large datasets must be handled with great care, because one might conclude that a famous person interests us more than a family member, when reality contradicts that assertion.
chapter: Ethical and critical questions about big data
themes: context; data quality; social media; interpretation
relevance_rationale: Careful interpretation of social media data and avoiding misleading conclusions from aggregated digital traces endures

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: All of these aspects must be analyzed in depth before undertaking an investigation or a report using mass data techniques obtained from the Internet. A journalist or social researcher must understand that there is a difference between what is legal and what is moral.
chapter: Ethical and critical questions about big data
themes: ethics; privacy; legal; data collection
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing legal from moral in data ethics is a fundamental principle that endures

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Investigative journalism does not consist of summarizing or piecing together the discoveries or data of others, but of carrying out original research, often using raw materials. It can be conducted through a series of interviews, or by cross-checking data and figures. On many occasions, the fruits and originality of the investigation are due to the discovery of patterns or connections that no one had previously observed.
themes: investigative journalism; cross-referencing; patterns; original research
relevance_rationale: Original research through cross-checking data to discover unseen patterns is an investigative principle that endures
notes: Attributed to David Randall

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: The five phases are: 1. The lead: the starting point of an investigative piece. 2. The inquiry: consulting sources and documents to support or refute the lead. 3. The publication: loose ends must be avoided, as they can undermine the entire investigative work. It is better to hold back a story if there are doubts. 4. The pressure: from the very first moment a piece of investigative journalism is published, the journalist and the outlet where they publish will face pressure from those affected. 5. The prison: in many cases those in power go after the investigative journalist.
themes: investigative journalism; workflow; verification; sources
relevance_rationale: Five phases of investigative journalism from lead to publication pressure are an enduring workflow model
notes: De Pablos Coello 5Ps model

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: The journalist's role is fundamental and generally requires four things: 1. Without being a mathematician, it is useful to have an interest in numbers, statistics, large quantities of numerical data (data mining), and in general in everything that can be expressed through numerical variables. 2. Without having to be a computer engineer, it is highly advisable to have a special interest in computer programming and to be capable, if not of writing code, at least of knowing what to ask of an engineer. 3. A passion for journalistic narrative. 4. An enthusiasm for artistic expression: sometimes it is simply not enough to build a story from numbers.
chapter: From big data to data journalism
themes: skills; numeracy; programming; storytelling; visualization
relevance_rationale: Four journalist competencies endure conceptually but LLMs now allow journalists to code, analyze stats, and visualize without deep specialist knowledge

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Meyer proposes the opposite: he measures a great deal of data and seeks an explanation for it. Taking into account a very important principle of philosophical logic — which states that correlation does not imply causation — it becomes necessary to develop a theory capable of finding the real cause from a data correlation. This theory is called Grounded Theory.
chapter: From big data to data journalism
themes: grounded theory; correlation; causation; hypothesis; methodology
relevance_rationale: Correlation does not imply causation and the need for theory to explain data patterns is a foundational epistemological principle
notes: Philip Meyer's approach

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: The journalistic project was designed as a social science research project: each researcher received a guide of topics they were to ask the respondents. The questions were neutral and leading questions were not permitted. They lasted 45 minutes and space was left for respondents to provide first-person accounts of their experiences. Demographic data were collected from the interviewees, including where they lived, their age, ethnic origin, educational qualifications, and even their criminal history.
chapter: From big data to data journalism
themes: survey design; data collection; research methods; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Structured interview protocols with neutral questions and demographic data collection is a research design principle that endures
notes: Reading the Riots project

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: The steps for applying this theory to a phenomenon from which data can be obtained are the following: 1. Theoretical sample: individuals, situations, and events idealized for the process of analysis. 2. Data collection or familiarization: compiling data and designing databases. In this phase, memos begin to be constructed. 3. Coding or categorization: this is the process of naming or labeling things, categories, and properties. It begins with open coding, then axial coding, and finally selective coding, where the initial categories are reduced and the core variable or central category is sought.
chapter: From big data to data journalism
themes: grounded theory; coding; categorization; methodology; data analysis
relevance_rationale: Grounded theory coding methodology endures but LLMs can now assist with open, axial, and selective coding processes

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Meyer considers that his success as a journalist in discarding the first two sociological theories did not lie in the use of early computers but in applying the scientific method to the explanation and presentation of the facts. That is, the current change does not lie in the use of computers but in the application that newspaper made of grounded theory to construct a journalistic narrative. The Guardian's historical contribution lies in the method, not in the machinery.
chapter: From big data to data journalism
themes: scientific method; methodology; grounded theory; narrative
relevance_rationale: Primacy of scientific method over machinery in data journalism is a foundational insight that endures in the LLM era

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Finding the right data is just as difficult as locating the best source to interview for a report. Professional standards do not change. The traditional journalist must always be wary of statements and interested sources offered to them by communications offices. The same applies to data: one must check who compiled it, under what methodology, and above all whether the information extracted from it is valid.
chapter: What data journalism is and how it works
themes: verification; data quality; sources; methodology
relevance_rationale: Verifying who compiled data, under what methodology, and whether conclusions are valid is a core verification principle

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Data journalism: 80% routine work, 10% great idea, and 10% production. Data journalism is laborious and highly routine: 80% of the time is spent reformatting PDF files or converting datasets into spreadsheets. The data journalist is the bridge between those who generate millions of data points and the public, which has no time to filter them but has the right to know what those data say.
chapter: What data journalism is and how it works
themes: workflow; data cleaning; time management; routine
relevance_rationale: The 80% routine cleaning work is now substantially reduced by LLMs, fundamentally changing the time allocation in data journalism

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Data journalism can only begin in two ways: either you have a question that requires data, or you have a dataset that needs to be questioned. Compilation is the true heart of data journalism. This data can be found through methods ranging from advanced computing techniques — even hacking — government websites to scraping hidden online databases using tools such as OutWit Hub, Scraperwiki. Sometimes data is provided by an organization, but many other times surveys or online forms are used: this is called crowdsourcing.
chapter: Steps to follow in the practice of data journalism
themes: data collection; scraping; crowdsourcing; question-driven
relevance_rationale: Two entry points to DJ and data compilation methods endure but scraping tools mentioned are outdated; LLMs now assist with all collection methods
notes: Attributed to Paul Bradshaw

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Raw data normally arrive duplicated, in incorrect codes, and in formats that those in power — political, economic, scientific, etc. — introduce to make them difficult to read. On the other hand, there is a lot of information irrelevant for journalistic purposes. Detecting and extracting the important data is the hardest part for the traditional journalist. In this regard, it is recommended to receive training in spreadsheet design or statistics and data mining programs.
chapter: Steps to follow in the practice of data journalism
themes: data cleaning; skills; spreadsheets; training
relevance_rationale: Data cleaning challenges endure but LLM-assisted cleaning dramatically reduces the difficulty for traditional journalists

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: The point is not to publish data, but to draw a conclusion, which becomes the headline. What matters is knowing how to cross-reference data from different sources to obtain new information: for example, we can cross-reference data on illnesses in Madrid's neighborhoods with data on air pollution. Within numerical data series, it is very important to detect patterns and trends and, above all, atypical behaviors. Anomaly has always been a news value since the birth of journalism.
chapter: Steps to follow in the practice of data journalism
themes: cross-referencing; patterns; anomaly detection; data analysis
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing data sources to find patterns and anomalies as news is a core analytical practice that endures

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: It is not just about verifying the source that compiles the data, but above all the data themselves. Darrel Huff pointed out that if you want to prove something absurd, take a lot of data, torture them until they say what you want to prove and call the resulting confession statistics.
chapter: Steps to follow in the practice of data journalism
themes: verification; statistics; data quality; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Warning against torturing data to confess desired conclusions is a critical thinking principle that endures

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: One of the most common fallacies that can occur is inferring that two events are related because they occur together. From the beginnings of Western thought, great philosophers such as Aristotle, Hume, and Kant have warned us that correlation does not imply causation. The temptation to link data and, motu proprio, draw causal conclusions — that is, produce a good headline — can be very dangerous if what we seek is the truth, journalism's ultimate objective.
chapter: Steps to follow in the practice of data journalism
themes: correlation; causation; fallacies; critical thinking; verification
relevance_rationale: Correlation vs causation warning and temptation to produce false headlines from data is an enduring epistemological principle

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: In journalism, data are useless if they do not tell a story. Moreover, the story cannot be fiction — it has to be true. The journalist's obligation is not that of the artist. The journalist must first obtain — above all else — the truth, and only then produce an appropriate visualization. Not the reverse: attracting audiences with appealing visualizations that do not describe the truth, or that mask it.
chapter: Steps to follow in the practice of data journalism
themes: visualization; truth; storytelling; ethics
relevance_rationale: Truth must precede visualization; attractive graphics must not mask falsehood is a core journalistic ethics principle

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Good design still matters. And these turn out much better — more informative — if the visualization design is developed ad hoc for a specific story. That is, there is no comparison if the visualization is produced by human journalists who understand the specific problems they want to visualize, because they have in mind the story to tell, versus when the data are fed into a machine with a standard algorithm.
chapter: What data journalism is and how it works
themes: visualization; design; storytelling; journalist role
relevance_rationale: Custom ad-hoc visualization design by journalists who understand the story remains ideal but LLMs now enable non-designers to create story-specific charts
notes: Attributed to Simon Rogers

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Digital journalism establishes the primacy of immediacy over reflection and interpretation and, very especially, to the detriment of quality.
chapter: Future of journalism profession in the network society
themes: quality; speed vs. depth; digital journalism
relevance_rationale: Tension between immediacy and quality in digital journalism is an enduring structural challenge
notes: Quoting Maria Teresa Sandoval

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: If journalism wants to survive, it will have to take in young people and offer them good working conditions so that they can train in peace. But also, if it wants to survive, it cannot do without veteran journalists, who are the only ones capable of transmitting to the young the idiosyncrasies of the craft.
chapter: Tension between expert bloggers and craft journalism
themes: training; craft knowledge; mentorship; newsroom sustainability
relevance_rationale: Need for mentorship between veteran and young journalists to transmit craft knowledge endures regardless of AI tools

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: An economist --- or a physicist or a sociologist --- may be able to write a good economic --- or scientific --- analysis that is comprehensible to the general public, but it would be altogether ridiculous for them to publish a report in which they interview colleagues in the hallway or question themselves. It would be very difficult for them to write a vibrant chronicle of how a budget is debated in a parliament or how a new exoplanet is discovered.
chapter: Tension between expert bloggers and craft journalism
themes: craft knowledge; genre expertise; reporting vs. analysis; journalism identity
relevance_rationale: Domain experts cannot replicate journalistic craft skills like interviews, chronicles, and accountability reporting

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: Journalistic genres --- especially the chronicle and the feature report --- are far more attractive for achieving large audiences or goals of persuasion of public opinion than simple analyses or opinion columns by experts.
chapter: Future of journalism profession in the network society
themes: genre; audience engagement; storytelling; journalism identity
relevance_rationale: Journalistic genres being more effective for audience engagement than expert analysis is a communication principle that endures

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: The best weapon of mass persuasion is beauty; and journalists have always tried to use art --- whether literary or visual art through the image --- not only to recount and publish the truth, but to bring it closer to the segment of the public most reluctant to become aware of real problems.
chapter: Tension between expert bloggers and craft journalism
themes: storytelling; visual journalism; audience engagement; craft knowledge
relevance_rationale: Beauty and art as tools for making truth accessible to reluctant audiences is an enduring principle of communication

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: One would need to analyze why someone, motu proprio and without pay, begins to publish information.
chapter: Future of journalism profession in the network society
themes: source evaluation; motivation analysis; credibility
relevance_rationale: Analyzing motivations of unpaid information publishers is a source evaluation principle that endures and applies to AI-generated content

source_title: Big Data y periodismo en la sociedad red
author: Carlos Elías
extract: A physicist or an economist working at a university does not contemplate denouncing corruption, uncovering a Watergate or a GAL scandal; rather, they will use the media to proselytize their point of view and sell more books, have more cited articles, or attract more funding for their research. It is not necessarily a social service, any more than the magazine published by an energy multinational or a tobacco company need be. Communication and journalism must not be confused.
chapter: Tension between expert bloggers and craft journalism
themes: journalism identity; accountability; source evaluation; independence
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing journalism from communication and noting experts have their own agendas is an independence principle that endures

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Identify the problem that motivates your journalistic work and define what purpose guides you: why it is of interest, how it affects citizens and how it does so in different geographic contexts over different chronological stages. Doing so helps you focus on a specific dataset, discarding numerical noise.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: problem definition; research design; scoping
relevance_rationale: Defining the problem, purpose, and public interest before touching data is a journalistic reasoning skill no LLM replaces
notes: Citing protocol from Dougherty and Ilyankou (Hands on Data Visualization)

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Question the data: what aspects remain unclear, which ones are uncertain, what are the causes that originate the phenomenon described by the data and in what context they occur. Always consider original ideas and unexpected angles, questioning accepted truths and approaching the familiar as if it were something new.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: critical thinking; questioning data; hypothesis formation
relevance_rationale: Questioning data, seeking unexpected angles, and challenging accepted truths require human editorial judgment that LLMs cannot autonomously exercise

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Datasets become 'subjects' that must be interrogated to discover their history, to understand their potentiality as sources. Once useful databases are located, ask who, how and why the data was collected, what the reliability of the catalog is, and what interests lie behind the publication.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: source evaluation; data provenance; critical assessment
relevance_rationale: Interrogating dataset provenance, collection motives, and reliability is a critical-thinking skill that endures regardless of tooling
notes: Quoting Hassel Fallas (La Data Cuenta)

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Prioritize access to raw data rather than those that have already been aggregated, since this will introduce constraints and biases. Raw data allow broadening the perspectives from which we view the phenomena analyzed to discover trends that might have gone unnoticed.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: raw data; aggregation bias; data collection
relevance_rationale: Prioritizing raw over aggregated data to avoid embedded bias remains essential even when LLMs can summarize aggregated data

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Ensure that the requested data are returned in a reusable format and clean them before setting to work. The most common tools among data journalists are spreadsheet programs and OpenRefine, an open-source tool designed to clean and transform messy data: remove spaces and non-printable characters, round decimals, reformat dates, clean misspelled words, remove duplicate rows or columns, convert non-tabular formats.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: data cleaning; formats; OpenRefine; spreadsheets
relevance_rationale: Core cleaning tasks like deduplication and reformatting are now largely automatable by LLMs, though the principle of demanding reusable formats endures

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Always be transparent in your methods and open your data, as organizations such as CIVIO open the data they have used and indicate the methodology they followed to carry out their journalistic work.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: transparency; methodology disclosure; open data; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Transparency in methods and open data are accountability principles that become more important, not less, when LLMs are part of the workflow

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Bear in mind what your potential audience may know about the topic and frame your work within this level of knowledge. Audiences are never uniform: people have different expectations, concerns and worries. What someone understands when they look at the data depends on what they already believe.
chapter: Finding Data and Preparing It to Convert into Journalistic Information
themes: audience awareness; framing; knowledge gaps
relevance_rationale: Audience awareness and framing around reader knowledge levels is editorial judgment that LLMs cannot substitute for

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: A breaking news event can prompt us to search for data to generate context. We must ask: is it a common occurrence? Has it happened before? How many times? The goal is to make the event evolve from what happened to the broader phenomenon in which it is embedded. The window of opportunity is brief, requiring the story to be published in less than 24-48 hours.
themes: news judgment; contextualization; timeliness
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now rapidly contextualize breaking events against historical data, accelerating the 24-48hr window but not replacing news judgment

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Add credibility: present information without adding biases, recognizing what is important and what is not. Sometimes the only data you have comes from an unidentified source. Always verify with another expert and do not publish biased data unless you have substantial evidence to corroborate them. Do not forget the ethical impact of the story and the care that must be applied when collecting data, the necessary protection of privacy and the transparency of the methodological process.
themes: credibility; verification; ethics; privacy; bias
relevance_rationale: Verification with experts, recognizing bias, protecting privacy, and ethical judgment are human responsibilities undiminished by AI tools
notes: Refs Chaparro Dominguez and Diaz-Campos on ethical best practices

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Humanize your data by telling the stories of the people affected. After the data, shoe leather: you have to go to places and see what the data is saying, because if you don't, you are not telling anything. Find the people who identify and help you understand what the large chunk of data you are covering means.
themes: humanization; field reporting; shoe-leather reporting; empathy
relevance_rationale: Humanizing data through field reporting and finding affected people requires physical presence and empathy that no LLM can provide
notes: Quoting Ana Tudela of Datadista

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: The visualization must meet three basic principles: Accuracy (clarity and integrity to not distort information), Usefulness (design must facilitate user exploration and interaction), and Adaptability (to consumption environments and users' needs in terms of depth, complexity and data modality). Resist the temptation to make spectacular decorative visualizations because the story told by the data may be lost. Functionality takes precedence over aesthetics.
themes: visualization; design principles; accuracy; usability
relevance_rationale: Visualization principles of accuracy, usefulness, and adaptability are design fundamentals that remain valid regardless of AI-generated charts
notes: Citing Material Design principles

source_title: Guía de Buenas Prácticas para Periodistas de Datos
author: Lorena R. Romero-Domínguez
extract: Label your data directly rather than including legends or keys, since this allows readers to see the data and the label at the same time. Labels should be as close as possible to the points of interest, to prevent readers from constantly moving their eyes. Use annotations to include tips and explanations that highlight outliers and historical milestones. Prepare users for intensive visualizations by including one or two introductory paragraphs before the visualization.
themes: visualization; labeling; annotation; readability
relevance_rationale: Direct labeling, proximity of annotations, and preparing readers for complex visuals are perceptual design principles that endure

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: A change in the technical capacities and operational thinking of the investigative journalist is required. The classic metaphor of the craft would have to shift from having a toolbox to managing a laboratory kit. Journalists don't need to learn to program, but they do need to develop a big data mindset, so that they understand that data contains stories that may go untold.
themes: big data mindset; skills evolution; data literacy
relevance_rationale: The claim journalists dont need to program but need a data mindset needs updating since LLMs now serve as the programming intermediary

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: Journalistic work with databases comprises five stages: collection, cleaning, analysis, verification and presentation of findings. At the collection stage, know the formats and tools for collecting data; big data can be obtained through scraping. At the cleaning stage, identify duplicate records, incomplete fields, misspelled words, invisible characters or blank spaces. At the cross-referencing stage, the value of findings depends on the quality of the questions and on combining two or more records to find revealing coincidences.
themes: pipeline stages; data collection; cleaning; cross-referencing; methodology
relevance_rationale: The five-stage pipeline endures conceptually but LLMs can now assist with scraping, cleaning, and cross-referencing steps directly
notes: Based on Paul Bradshaw's five-stage framework

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: The investigative journalist must apply traditional methodology at the verification stage: going to the necessary places, interviewing the people involved, reviewing new documents to detect the strengths and weaknesses of the database. Ask: Do the data reflect the real condition of the people? What expert can I consult to validate the cross-referencing methodology? Is it possible that the finding is correct but admits more than one interpretation?
themes: verification; field reporting; expert consultation; interpretation
relevance_rationale: Field verification, interviewing sources, and detecting database weaknesses through traditional methods remain irreplaceable by AI

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: You can become a coding virtuoso if you wish, but the main task is to think about data as a journalist rather than as an analyst. No digital tool will replace the exercise of asking what relevant information the data can provide, or what would happen if one mixed one database with another.
themes: journalistic thinking; analytical mindset; questioning data
relevance_rationale: Thinking about data as a journalist rather than an analyst -- asking what stories data can tell -- is editorial judgment that endures
notes: Quoting Simon Rogers (Facts are Sacred)

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: The basic skills of investigative journalists are similar to those of the most qualified prosecutors and police, field anthropologists and private investigators: the use of primary sources, verification of evidence, first-hand witness interviews, and following the trails of people, documents, and money. Spreadsheets must be seen as forensic records of reality: they offer details, but the truth requires work.
chapter: The dimension of what is investigated
themes: investigative skills; primary sources; verification; forensic approach
relevance_rationale: Primary-source verification, following money trails, and treating spreadsheets as forensic records remain core investigative skills
notes: Quoting David Kaplan (GIJN); distinguishing leaks journalism from investigative journalism

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: The ICIJ performed data cleaning and organization using OpenRefine. The program dtSearch helped track names among 260 gigabytes of data. Nuix software obtained connections of keywords in email attachments without opening documents. Talend Open Studio integrated and organized data in relationship graphs. The programmers reconstructed the software system of offshore service companies, paving the way for journalists to navigate structured files and identify who was behind the companies.
themes: OpenRefine; data cleaning; cross-referencing; collaborative investigation; leaked data
relevance_rationale: Specific tools like dtSearch and Nuix are now largely supplanted by LLM-powered search and entity extraction across large document sets
notes: From Offshore Leaks case study (ICIJ, 2013)

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: For Swiss Leaks, the first step was to reconstruct the HSBC customer database from available flat Excel files. Next, relationships were drawn between names and countries. Talend software transferred the database to Neo4j graph database for organizing relationships. Linkurious facilitated visualization and analysis. The database gave rise to a diagram of 275,000 nodes with 400,000 relationships. Reporters communicated through the Voyager platform for sharing files and interview transcripts — the closest thing to a global newsroom.
themes: graph databases; Neo4j; collaboration platforms; data reconstruction; network analysis
relevance_rationale: Graph database workflows like Neo4j plus Linkurious endure conceptually but LLMs now offer faster entity extraction and relationship mapping
notes: Swiss Leaks case (ICIJ, 2015); 154 journalists, 47 countries

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: When I design a spreadsheet for a story I think about the goals: what I want to know and what the possible patterns in the data are. I never begin a process without developing a plan. I use my own matrix, which helps us aim at specific questions.
themes: planning; research design; hypothesis-driven analysis; spreadsheet methodology
relevance_rationale: Planning analysis goals and developing a question matrix before touching data is a research design discipline no AI replaces
notes: Quoting Ginna Morelo (El Tiempo, Colombia) and Lise Olsen (Houston Chronicle)

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: The seven pharmaceutical companies had published information on their websites but in formats difficult to analyze (PDF and JPG). Two journalists and a developer downloaded and organized it in Excel, then used OpenRefine to clean and standardize doctor names. They cross-referenced with public databases of medical licenses and disciplinary records. ProPublica presented the investigation as a journalistic application where anyone can search their doctor's name.
themes: format conversion; data cleaning; cross-referencing; public accountability apps
relevance_rationale: PDF-to-data conversion and name standardization with OpenRefine are now largely automatable by LLMs, though the cross-referencing logic endures
notes: Dollars for Docs (ProPublica, 2010)

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: Journalists manually created an Excel database from 59 price resolutions. The components of the price formula were broken down to determine how operating costs were included in fuel tariffs. With the help of specialists, cross-subsidies were found. The team had to build five versions of the database before finding the correct one.
themes: manual database construction; iteration; expert consultation; formula analysis
relevance_rationale: Iterative database building requiring five versions and expert consultation reflects the messy reality of investigative work that AI cannot shortcut
notes: Aresep fuel pricing investigation (La Nacion Costa Rica, Hassel Fallas, 2014)

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: The starting point of Cuidados Intensivos was the design of a fundamental database structure for understanding the sector and identifying all State institutions that held necessary information. The main challenge was verifying data update dates. When taking random verification samples, they detected that the Medical Association was not regularly updating accredited specialties. The problem had to be corrected by hand, with specific searches of specialists' records.
themes: database design; data freshness; sampling; manual correction; verification
relevance_rationale: Verifying data freshness through random sampling and correcting institutional records by hand requires human persistence and judgment
notes: OjoPúblico Cuidados Intensivos project

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: Official information is contradictory. When we compared physical documents with what was on Indecopi's website, we found 30 penalty resolutions against clinics that did not appear online. The application processed more resolutions than the government body itself. Always insist on obtaining physical documents in addition to digital records, as bodies may have more information than they have processed for online publication.
themes: FOI requests; official data gaps; document verification; persistence
relevance_rationale: Insisting on physical documents to catch gaps in digital records is an investigative persistence principle no AI can replace
notes: OjoPúblico lesson from Cuidados Intensivos

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: Technical terms hide revelations. During the investigation, we had to immerse ourselves in technical terms used by the State. Without mastery of this specialized jargon, we would have missed relevant data. When reports stated a clinic had an 'Operational risk level: 6%,' what was actually revealed was that the establishment was failing to comply with 94% of care standards. Journalists turned to experts who helped explain terminology in simple language for users.
themes: domain knowledge; technical literacy; expert consultation; reframing
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now help decode technical jargon and reframe specialist terminology, though domain immersion and expert consultation still matter
notes: OjoPúblico lesson from Cuidados Intensivos

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: If the database doesn't exist, there are always ways to build it. Peru lacks an official record of sanctioned medical negligence. We resolved to build an initial database from complaints reported in the media that had been formalized with the justice system. We reviewed archives of three newspapers from 1991 through mid-2015, transferred information to a spreadsheet, then cross-referenced doctors and establishments with records from the Prosecutor's Office and the Judiciary. Only formalized cases were included.
themes: database construction; archival research; cross-referencing; data gaps
relevance_rationale: Building databases from scratch when none exist -- mining archives, cross-referencing records -- is investigative resourcefulness that endures
notes: OjoPúblico Cuidados Intensivos — building data from scratch

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: Liz Mineo combined traditional journalistic techniques with incipient data-thinking. She built a database of 293 works in 21 departments totaling 100 million soles by 61 companies from an 80-page leaked file. The first filter revealed works concentrated in non-vulnerable areas. The second identified shell companies owned by associates of the agency chief. The third stage was verification: visiting investment sites to discover unfinished or non-existent works. Without that field visit, findings might not have been as conclusive.
themes: database construction; pattern detection; field verification; corruption investigation
relevance_rationale: Combining database analysis with field verification to discover non-existent works remains essential investigative methodology
notes: Pioneering Peruvian case from 1997 (Liz Mineo / El Comercio)

author: David Hidalgo, Fabiola Torres (Ojo Público)
extract: The collaborative spirit among experts from different fields is the defining feature of Big Data era journalism. The symbiosis between code writers and journalists has taken this profession into uncharted territory in the exploration of documents previously impossible for any outlet to analyze. Investigative journalists can no longer work in isolation as lone wolves.
themes: collaboration; interdisciplinary teams; hackers and journalists; newsroom structure
relevance_rationale: LLMs now serve as the code-writing intermediary, partially replacing the need for dedicated programmers on teams, though collaboration principles endure
notes: Recurring theme across foreword, HacksHackers, OjoPublico cases

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: Faced with the provisional nature of the situation, I began to observe that, when drawing on public databases to write reports, journalists needed, for example, to expose the limitations of those records. They needed to explain that the numbers were not detailed or that there could be delays in entering information into public systems, which in turn would generate a temporary distortion in analyses.
themes: transparency; data limitations; uncertainty; methodology
relevance_rationale: Exposing data limitations and explaining record-entry delays to readers is a permanent transparency obligation

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: In some cases, when certain statistics could not be used, data journalists would indicate, in the stories, why they decided to exclude one or another state from the analysis, or why such information should be observed with caution by the reader. They were, then, explaining their working method, informing what sources were consulted, how reliable those sources were, and how one or another result was reached.
themes: transparency; methodology; sources; data quality; accountability
relevance_rationale: Explaining methodological choices, source reliability, and exclusion decisions in published stories is timeless transparency practice

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: Transparency in journalism carries the meaning of openness, of strategically managed visibility and of reduction of information asymmetry, since the journalist has first-hand access to data that the audience does not possess; thus, authors such as Kovach and Rosenstiel advocate that ways be found for journalism to compensate for this aspect, by revealing information about sources and reporting methods.
themes: transparency; accountability; sources; methodology; trust
relevance_rationale: Transparency as reduction of information asymmetry by revealing sources and methods is a foundational journalism value

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: In Data-Driven Journalism, this includes, for example, providing hyperlinks to original databases and, when a programming language is employed, the source code that drove the collection, analysis, and results.
themes: transparency; open source; reproducibility; hyperlinks
relevance_rationale: Linking to databases and source code endures but now extends to sharing LLM prompts and AI-assisted analysis pipelines

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: Volunteers work with a shared methodology to follow the same data collection process; moreover, there is a constant information verification process — including a kind of independent double-check before updating the shared system with new data — in order to avoid errors. All methodology and source code are available on GitHub.
chapter: Public transparency and open data on Covid-19
themes: verification; collaboration; open source; methodology; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Shared methodology, independent double-checking, and open-source code for verification are permanent best practices
notes: Brasil.io Covid-19 data collection

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: One of the first and most important lessons I drew from my experience with open data and the Covid-19 pandemic was precisely the precariousness in the collection and opening of information. Journalists were required to look skeptically at the statistics, understanding their contexts of production and availability.
themes: data quality; skepticism; open data; context; verification
relevance_rationale: Looking skeptically at statistics and understanding contexts of data production is a permanent verification stance

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: Data-Driven Journalism is defined by Träsel as the application of computing and social science knowledge in the collection, processing, interpretation, and presentation of data, with the aim of expanding journalism's function as a defender of the public interest. I reiterate the definition that it is a set of practices and knowledge whose origins are associated with scientific knowledge, through truth and methodological objectivity, capable of offering high degrees of transparency and collaboration, producing enduring content.
themes: definition; scientific method; transparency; collaboration; public interest
relevance_rationale: Defining DDJ through scientific method, transparency, collaboration, and public interest is timeless

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: The expression raw data is paradoxical. As the authors remind us, data do not exist by themselves in the world, but need to be collected, compiled, generated, and processed. Rosenberg explains that data derives from the verb dare, or to give, and is thus related to the production of an argument — that is, it presupposes an interpretive layer. Facts are ontological, evidence is epistemological, data is rhetorical.
themes: data epistemology; context; critical thinking; interpretation
relevance_rationale: The insight that raw data is paradoxical and data is rhetorical, not ontological, is a permanent epistemological truth

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
extract: Data-Driven Journalism has as one of its differentiators its proximity to the scientific method. Therefore it bets, for example, on the use of hypotheses in place of the conventional construction of a story pitch. This hypothesis will serve as the starting point for the investigation, which has its own method.
chapter: Ways of working: method and workflows
themes: hypothesis; scientific method; workflow; investigation
relevance_rationale: Hypothesis-driven investigation as DDJ differentiator from conventional pitching is a permanent methodological principle

source_title: Os Elementos de Transparência no Jornalismo Guiado por Dados
author: academic
