author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: SQLite stores dates as text, unlike some other relational databases. To treat them as dates, use the date() function. The strftime function will return NULL if you attempt to parse an invalid date. You can use it to run some limited integrity checks on columns with date values.
themes: SQL; dates; data integrity
relevance_rationale: SQLite date handling still relevant but LLMs can now generate these queries from natural language, reducing need to memorize syntax
notes: strftime-as-validator pattern: SELECT *, strftime('%Y', open_date) AS date_test FROM inspections WHERE date_test IS NULL

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Use julianday() to convert date strings into numbers so you can subtract them to find the difference between dates. To convert to years, divide by 365.25.
themes: SQL; dates; calculation
relevance_rationale: Date arithmetic in SQL remains useful but LLMs can write these formulas on demand from plain-English descriptions
notes: Pattern: julianday(close_case_date) - julianday(open_date) / 365.25 AS years_open

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: When writing nested queries, it's helpful to write the inner query (subquery) first, then build the outer query on top of that. You must give the inner query a name using AS and set it off using parentheses. Use tabs to set off lines of your query so that you can easily read them.
themes: SQL; subqueries; code readability
relevance_rationale: Subquery logic still matters but LLMs can generate nested SQL from natural language, reducing manual query-building effort

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: AP DataKit is an open-source command-line tool designed to better structure and manage projects. It makes it easier to standardize and share work among members of your team, and to keep your past projects organized and easily accessible for future reference. Keep all parts of a project cleanly separated: data, code, configuration, documentation.
themes: project management; reproducibility; file organization
relevance_rationale: Project organization principles like separating data, code, config, and docs remain essential regardless of tooling

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: When default table-finding in pdfplumber doesn't work on complex PDFs, use explicit_vertical_lines and explicit_horizontal_lines to tell pdfplumber exactly where table boundaries are. Identify header regions first, extract column separators from header cells, then find row separators by locating key entities (like payee names) in a specific column. Feed those coordinates back as explicit lines.
themes: PDF extraction; pdfplumber; table parsing; messy data
relevance_rationale: PDF extraction with explicit lines remains relevant for complex layouts but LLM-based document understanding can now handle many cases automatically
notes: VERIFY on every page, not just one sample page - 3000-page PDFs are not trivial

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: To rotate each page 90 degrees before extraction, use pdftk: pdftk file.pdf cat 1-endeast output file_rotated.pdf. Start with pdfplumber docs. Use debug_tablefinder() to visually inspect what pdfplumber detects. Crop the page to isolate regions of interest before attempting table extraction.
themes: PDF extraction; pdfplumber; pdftk; visual debugging
relevance_rationale: Iterative PDF debugging workflow endures in principle but LLM-based PDF extraction tools reduce the manual tuning cycle
notes: Iterative approach: visualize -> crop -> adjust settings -> re-visualize

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

themes: PDF extraction; verification; scalability
relevance_rationale: Verification at scale is a timeless principle -- extraction settings must be validated across all pages regardless of the tool used

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The editor's role in data projects: Be clear, consistent about goals. Ask what kinds of data are available. Discuss known/suspected problems with the data. author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: 10 questions every editor should ask: Does the data answer our questions? Does it tell us something we didn't think to ask? Where did you find the data? How did you vet/clean the data? How did you calculate those numbers? Are you keeping a data diary? Did you replicate your data work? Could someone else? Have you consulted experts or done a scientific lit review? Do we need a white paper? Can you write a nerd graf/story now? What is the significance of the data?

relevance_rationale: The 10 editorial questions checklist for data stories is a timeless verification framework

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Data checks: What is the source of the data? How many records should you have/do you have? Is data missing? Is the data within reasonable ranges? Is everything included? Are there consistency issues? Are totals within reasonable bounds? Have you done internal checks? Are there duplicates? Gut check.
themes: data quality; verification checklist; completeness; duplicates
relevance_rationale: Data quality checks for completeness, range, duplicates, and consistency remain essential regardless of tooling

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Do my findings make sense? Compared to what? Company XX has done XX terrible thing, but what about other companies in the same industry? Should you report a rate or a raw number? Is your data on the same scale?

relevance_rationale: Contextualizing findings through comparison, rates vs counts, and appropriate scale is fundamental analytical reasoning

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Other considerations: Are there codes that you don't understand or questions you need answered? Can you go look at one? Ground truth. Is the data a sample with a margin of error? Are changes over time reasonable? How else could you look at the data? Are there lurking variables? With maps -- did you use the right projection? Are you matching data sets? Beware data portals.

relevance_rationale: Ground truth verification, lurking variables, and proper map projections are analytical considerations no AI eliminates

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Read the methodology. Know the sample size and margin of error. Know the questions. Know who funded the research. If the source won't provide the methodology, that's a red flag. Don't report numbers for which you haven't checked the methodology.
themes: polling data; methodology review; funding transparency; red flags
relevance_rationale: Evaluating methodology, sample size, funding transparency of research data is core journalistic verification

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Best practices: Look at your data. Maintain a consistent universe of cases. Keep a data diary or detailed markdown file. Backup your data (Never work from original). Check against reports. Make sure you're using the right tool. Check with experts from different sides of the issue. Show findings to the targets and experts. Duplicate your work - don't just rerun the same script that could have created an error. Have another person duplicate your work.
themes: best practices; data diary; reproducibility; independent verification; expert review
relevance_rationale: Independent duplication of analysis by another person goes beyond what automated tools can validate
notes: Distinction: replicating via same script is not sufficient - need independent duplication

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Understand the methodology: It should be logical, make sense. Find similar stories and study what they did. Check with experts from different sides. Find a guide. Use the IRE Resource Center. Understand the process of how data was gathered.
themes: methodology review; editorial backstopping; prior art
relevance_rationale: Studying similar stories and consulting diverse experts to validate methodology remains essential

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Other backstops: Ask for a copy of the record layout. Ask for who else may have done similar research. Read that research. Read the documentation or data how-to file. Find the experts. relevance_rationale: Editorial backstopping processes like record layout review and second opinions are organizational practices that persist

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Data diary: For backstop. For story footnoting/lawyering. For staying on track. What you did. How you did it. File names. Copy of code/syntax. Should be clear so that your editor and colleagues can make sense of it. Later you can link back to page or number in fact check. Highlight everything that was derived from data (or docs) and go back and check.
themes: data diary; documentation; fact-checking; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: The data diary practice for reproducibility, fact-checking, and legal defense is unchanged by AI tools

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Gathering your own data - Pitfalls: Time and effort, Accuracy concerns, Consistency concerns. Benefits: Totally original data reporting, Control over all aspects of the data. Reminders: Leave time for follow up after collecting your data. If you've built a database, set a cut-off date. Use verification or double entry. Attempt to get ground truth.
themes: database building; original data; double entry; ground truth
relevance_rationale: Building original databases with verification and ground truth checks are investigative practices that persist

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

relevance_rationale: Having a shared workflow and project management system for team coordination is an organizational necessity

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Thesis, what is it, how does the data support it. Outline the project and the story/ies. Footnote story memos and drafts (increases accuracy and saves time). Write the nut grafs first. Structure is as important as prose. Avoid number overload - use your strongest finding from your data. Going from your data findings to your story is little different than going from notes/findings to your story.
themes: story writing; structure; nut graf; number discipline
relevance_rationale: Story structure, nut graf writing, and avoiding number overload are craft skills unaffected by AI

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Story Structure: Chronology (or reverse chronology) is the easiest to write and for a reader to follow. Warning: don't make the reader jump time. Thematic structure is more complicated to write, but can be effective for the right material. Even within a thematic structure, you can use internal chronology. Characters: Who are they and what themes/points do they illustrate? Data gives you an entire population to choose the best characters and scenes.
themes: story structure; chronology; thematic structure; character selection
relevance_rationale: Narrative structure choices and using data to select characters are storytelling principles independent of technology

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The Nerd box: Details of what you did, why, and how you did it. Keeps story flowing. Adds transparency. You don't always need one. Sometimes you need something more extensive. Write it before writing story.
themes: methodology box; transparency; story structure
relevance_rationale: The methodology box as transparency practice and story-planning device remains essential
notes: Write methodology before story so findings are clear in your mind

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Words matter: Making conclusions you can't back up. Rates versus raw numbers. The 'more' dilemma. Percent versus percentage points. False precision: 53.14 percent. Avoid number overload. Adjust money for inflation. When analyzing income or home values, use median rather than average. Keep numbers to graphics.
themes: precision; rates vs counts; inflation adjustment; median vs average; number literacy
relevance_rationale: Number literacy principles like rates vs counts, inflation adjustment, and median vs average are timeless

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Don't forget about the graphics/apps: They can't be an afterthought. Text needs to be edited. Data needs to be rechecked. Need a workflow - that's not just email.
themes: graphics; visualization; workflow; editorial process
relevance_rationale: Integrating graphics into editorial workflow rather than treating them as afterthought remains critical

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: One of the most common things journalists want to do with a date/time field is to calculate response times of local public safety units. Convert time into seconds by multiplying TIME(HOUR,MINUTE,SECOND) by 86400. To deal with calls that run across midnight, use an IF statement checking whether the receive date differs from the arrive date.
themes: Excel; response times; date/time calculation; public safety
relevance_rationale: Excel time calculations remain useful but LLMs can now generate these formulas from natural language descriptions

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Text-to-Columns is handy if the column you are trying to split is delimited. But sometimes it won't work and sometimes your data won't be neat and tidy. String functions (LEFT, MID, SEARCH, RIGHT) save the day when Text-to-Columns strips leading zeros or data isn't consistently delimited. These functions are also available in SQL, R and Python with slight syntax variations.
themes: Excel; string functions; data cleaning; leading zeros; text parsing
relevance_rationale: String function concepts endure but LLMs can write these data-cleaning formulas, reducing need to memorize syntax
notes: Text-to-Columns destroys leading zeros; string functions preserve them

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: When splitting names with middle initials: first assess whether you have a consistent pattern. Check whether periods are consistent. Use LEN to measure string length, then test whether the second-to-last byte is a space using MID and IF. Be sure to troll through your data and look for anomalies. Use the Filter feature or run a Pivot Table to look for problem records.
themes: Excel; name parsing; data cleaning; pattern detection; anomaly detection
relevance_rationale: Name parsing with Excel functions is increasingly handled by LLMs that can parse varied name formats contextually
notes: Check for suffixes (Jr, III) that break patterns; handle manually if few

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: IF statements are extremely powerful for assigning categories to data based on certain criteria and for data cleanup functions that require looking for patterns. Use IF functions to rearrange data that comes in report fashion or has problems that make sorting or PivotTables impossible. IF functions need a pattern in order to work.
themes: Excel; IF statements; data restructuring; categorization
relevance_rationale: IF-statement patterns for data cleanup endure conceptually but LLMs can generate these formulas from descriptions
notes: Key insight: IF needs a pattern - identify the repeating structure first

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Election data often comes with just raw vote totals and no indication of who won. Use a combination of IF, LARGE, MAX, INDEX, MATCH to determine winners and handle ties. LARGE finds the nth-largest value in an array; if the largest equals the second-largest, it's a tie. INDEX returns a value at a row/column intersection. MATCH finds which column holds the maximum.
themes: Excel; election data; INDEX-MATCH; winner determination
relevance_rationale: Election data winner-determination formulas are still useful but LLMs can produce these complex Excel formulas on demand
notes: Formula: =IF(LARGE(D3:i3,1)>LARGE(d3:i3,2), INDEX(header,1,MATCH(MAX(D3:i3),D3:i3,0)),Tie)

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: VLOOKUP requires that the field you're matching on is the farthest left column of the lookup table. If your table is set up differently, use a combination of INDEX and MATCH functions instead. MATCH finds the row number, INDEX returns the value at that position.
themes: Excel; VLOOKUP; INDEX-MATCH; data joining
relevance_rationale: INDEX-MATCH is being superseded by XLOOKUP in modern Excel, and LLMs can generate both approaches
notes: INDEX-MATCH is more flexible than VLOOKUP for non-standard table layouts

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Tableau Reshaper is a tool for normalizing data, whether you plan to use Tableau or not. Make sure the columns you want to convert are on the right side of your spreadsheet and the columns you want attached to each row are on the left side. Make sure headers are presented exactly as you want them in the final data.
themes: data normalization; wide to long; Tableau; Excel
relevance_rationale: Wide-to-long reshaping concept endures but specific tools like Tableau Reshaper are superseded by pandas/R and LLM-generated code
notes: Converts wide data to long format; exports to CSV if exceeds 1M rows

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You can use Weeknum and Weekday in conjunction with Pivot Tables to display data in a sort of calendar form. This would be useful if you're looking for patterns in your data based on the calendar. Put WeekNum in the Row, WeekDay in the Column and whatever field you want to count or sum in the Data box.
themes: Excel; pivot tables; temporal patterns; calendar analysis
relevance_rationale: Calendar pattern analysis concept endures but LLMs can generate pivot table configurations from plain-English requests

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You can build hyperlinks with a formula to launch documents directly from Excel. Use HYPERLINK() with concatenation to build paths from file name columns. Store documents in a sub-directory of the folder where the spreadsheet lives. This works for Word documents, other Excel files or PDFs.
themes: Excel; hyperlinks; document management; FOIA
relevance_rationale: Hyperlink formulas in Excel are tool-specific knowledge that LLMs can generate; the document management concept persists
notes: Pattern: =HYPERLINK(.\documents\ & E2, E2) to link spreadsheet rows to source documents

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: In a graph database, the data is either a node, a relationship, or an attribute. Use Cypher commands CREATE, LOAD CSV, and MERGE to build the graph. MERGE prevents duplicate nodes when loading CSV data. Attributes go in curly braces as property key-value pairs. Every query must start with a MATCH and end with a RETURN.
themes: graph databases; Neo4j; Cypher; network analysis
relevance_rationale: Graph database concepts endure but LLMs can generate Cypher queries and help design graph schemas from natural language
notes: Applied to political donation networks

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Use fuzzy matching in Cypher with regex: WHERE d.name =~ '.*Bamford.*' for partial match, WHERE d.name =~ '(?i).*Bamford.*' for case-insensitive. Filter relationships by properties like donation value: WHERE t.value >= 100000. Whenever you're writing Cypher, keep the Refcard up as a syntax reference.
themes: Cypher; fuzzy matching; regex; graph queries
relevance_rationale: Fuzzy matching in Cypher is increasingly replaceable by LLM-powered entity resolution and semantic matching

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: An easy place to track down APIs is a government open data portal. Many are built on Socrata which standardizes the API. The API Endpoint returns JSON data. If you aren't sure about JSON, you can convert API contents to CSV using online tools. Use Postman to visually explore and test API calls before writing code.
themes: APIs; open data; Socrata; Postman; JSON
relevance_rationale: API fundamentals persist but LLMs can now write API interaction code and even help discover undocumented endpoints

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: There are two kinds of API: a web API (talk to a URL) and a Python library API (a way of talking to Python that handles the URL for you behind the scenes). Sometimes it's just easier to write Python than trying to talk to a URL. Use Python requests to download data, then give it to pandas to turn it into a filterable dataframe.
themes: APIs; Python; requests; pandas; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Web vs library APIs remain relevant but LLMs can now auto-generate API client code and handle auth flows
notes: Example: response = requests.get(url); df = pd.DataFrame(response.json())

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: When you use some APIs, you need to prove who you are via API keys. API keys are sometimes in the URL, sometimes a parameter in the URL, sometimes separate keys combined using OAuth, or signed requests with encryption. If you run into OAuth or signed requests, it's best to switch to a library.
themes: APIs; authentication; API keys; OAuth
relevance_rationale: API key management endures but LLMs simplify OAuth flows and can generate auth boilerplate

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The best-kept secret of scraping: skipping scraping at all, and using the provider's unadvertised API. If you've ever tried to scrape a site and failed because the URLs didn't seem to work right, look for a secret website API that the site uses internally to load data.
themes: scraping; secret APIs; data acquisition; web inspection
relevance_rationale: Finding hidden APIs before scraping is a timeless investigative technique that predates and outlasts any tooling era

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Datedif is useful for calculating ages from birthdates. It gives you the difference between two dates in whatever unit of measure you specify. You can use the TODAY() function to refer to today's date. Example: =Datedif(b2,today(), 'y') gives years since birthdate.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Understanding Excel errors: #DIV/0\! means dividing by zero or blank cell - use IF to check before dividing. #N/A means formula couldn't return a legitimate result, often from VLOOKUP with no match. #NAME? means a typo in your formula. #REF\! means invalid cell reference. #VALUE\! means wrong data type in a function.
themes: Excel; error handling; debugging; data quality
relevance_rationale: Understanding Excel errors remains useful but LLMs can diagnose and fix formula errors conversationally

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The goal is to see if we can spot something specific in a video. This would be useful especially if you had hours of video you didn't have time to watch. Our plan will be: Download a computer-vision model pre-trained on 1.2 million images; Further train that model on images containing 'yes bikes' and 'no bikes'; Chop up our video into frames; Use the model to detect bikes in frames
chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Sorting Images with fast.ai
themes: machine learning; image classification; transfer learning; video analysis
relevance_rationale: Video classification workflow endures conceptually but multimodal LLMs can now classify video content directly without custom training

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Training a computer-vision mode from scratch to solve our problem would take thousands of images. Maybe more. Instead we take advantage of an existing model that was trained to detect objects -- from planes, to cars, to dogs, to birds -- by processing millions of images. This model is called 'resnet34.' With fast.ai we can infuse this model with our images (and their labels). This takes advantage of all of resnet34's 'knowledge' of image-detection and tacks on our particular problem. This technique is called 'transfer learning.'
chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Transfer Learning with ResNet
themes: transfer learning; neural networks; pre-trained models; image classification
relevance_rationale: Transfer learning concept endures but foundation models and zero-shot LLMs reduce need for fine-tuning on small datasets
notes: Core concept: reuse existing models rather than training from scratch

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: We can take a look to see where the model was most confused, and whether what the model predicted was reasonable or not. In this case the mistakes look reasonable (none of the mistakes seems obviously naive). This is an indicator that our classifier is working correctly. Furthermore, when we plot the confusion matrix, we can see how the mistakes are being made.
chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Evaluating Model Performance
themes: model evaluation; confusion matrix; error analysis; validation
relevance_rationale: Inspecting model confusion and validating predictions remains essential regardless of whether using fast.ai or LLMs

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Probably the trickiest part of machine learning image problems involve pairing the images with their proper labels. There are many ways to do this. Sometimes the labels are in the file names. Sometimes they're in a list. Ours are in two separate folders, or directories. I made these folders by sorting through about 300 maps of helicopter flight paths. Took me about 15 minutes.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: We'll add our new transforms, and we'll change the size of the training images. To the model, these will look like brand new images it has never seen before. That's because in the training process, our convolutional neural network looks at small groups of pixels at a time. If we use bigger versions of the image, it's still a bike to us, but those clusters of pixels will be completely new to the computer.
themes: progressive resizing; data augmentation; neural networks; training strategy

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

We'll then use transfer learning to further train wikitext-103 on our particular corpus: several thousand #txlege tweets. So we benefit from its training on both English-language articles and #txlege tweets. That will give us a language model that's good at detecting patterns in #txlege tweets.

extract: We developed this particular system to help support national politics reporters in figuring out where to go to talk to people. What it does is it data mines a national voter file, including every registered voter in the US to try to pull out interesting patterns in things like new registrations as well as other variables of interest. Counties get ranked on the degree of statistical outlier-y-ness as well as factors like the political relevance in terms of vote margins, if the district flipped recently, and if it has an open seat in 2020. A tip sheet is generated using natural language generation to convey the interesting bits from the statistical analysis.
themes: data mining; voter files; statistical analysis; NLG; story leads; Washington Post
relevance_rationale: Voter-file data mining for lead generation endures but LLMs can augment the analysis with richer demographic and sentiment patterns
notes: Diakopoulos Lead Locator: statistical outlier detection + NLG tip sheets for reporters

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Some experts estimate that only about 15% of a reporter's job and about 9% of an editor's job could be automated using current levels of AI technology. More often than not AI and automation technologies actually create new types of work, like the configuration, parameterization, knowledge management, data production, and template writing tasks involved with setting up, operating, and supervising automated systems. Instead of destroying jobs in journalism, AI appears to be creating them.

relevance_rationale: The 15%/9% automation estimates from pre-LLM era are outdated — LLMs have significantly expanded what can be automated in journalism
notes: Diakopoulos: AI creates new work types rather than replacing existing journalism roles

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: A regular expression is a common, compact way to represent some sort of pattern in text. You can use one to: Help you search within a document. Reformat or otherwise clean up an inconsistent or 'dirty' data set. Extract structured data from plain text. Many common programming languages, text editors and search tools support regular expressions, so you can use them in whatever environment you find most comfortable.
themes: regex; pattern matching; data cleaning; text extraction; cross-platform
relevance_rationale: Regex remains a powerful tool but LLMs can generate regex patterns from natural language and explain existing ones
notes: Regex as universal data-cleaning and extraction skill across environments

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Literals: Letters and numbers refer to themselves. Escaping: A ton of characters in regular expressions have special meanings; to override that meaning for a particular character, put a backslash before it. Character classes: You can specify a class of characters by surrounding them in square brackets. Groups: You can surround a part of a regular expression in parentheses to treat it as a unit. Quantifiers: Several symbols exist to let you specify how many times in a row to match a given character, class or group. Anchors: A caret matches the beginning of a line of text. A dollar sign similarly matches the end of a line.
themes: regex; pattern matching; syntax reference; text processing
relevance_rationale: Regex syntax details endure across tools but LLMs can generate and debug regex patterns conversationally
notes: Core regex building blocks: literals, escaping, classes, groups, quantifiers, anchors

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

You can scrape everything at a particular XPath using this Google Sheets formula: =importxml("url","XPath"). XPaths open up a whole new world: all bold text: //b. All large text (known as headers): //h1. All headers with the word 'Parish': //h1[contains(.,'parish')].

extract: Given a folder full of images, we want a list of descriptions of each image. One way to solve this problem would be to teach, or train a model. That is, use a pre-trained model -- one already trained by some other, kind people, who built it using millions of images and then shared it on the internet for others to use. Note that ResNet does not contain 1.2 million images\! It contains the complex mathematical formula -- the neural network and all of its structure and values -- that were fine-tuned by processing those images.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Frequency tables are dead-simple, but also quite powerful. For the dead-simple usage: Navigate to any column, and then press Shift-F. With just one keystroke, VisiData has already told us something useful about the dataset: That the 'operators' associated with 31% of the wildlife strikes are, according to the FAA, 'unknown.' We have also learned, from the 'bins' mini-report at the bottom-right of the screen, that there are 282 distinct values in the 'OPERATOR' column.
themes: VisiData; frequency tables; data profiling; exploratory analysis
relevance_rationale: Frequency tables as an analytical concept endure but LLMs can generate them from natural language without memorizing VisiData keystrokes
notes: Single-keystroke frequency table generation reveals distribution and cardinality instantly

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Sometimes you want to count how often combinations of columns occur. VisiData also makes this easy. First, turn the columns you want to count into 'key' columns, using the \! button. Then, type gF. With just a few more keystrokes, we've learned something else: Virtually all wildlife strikes with 'unknown' operators were identified based on carcasses found at the airport (rather than reports from pilots, for example).
themes: VisiData; cross-tabulation; multi-variable analysis; frequency tables
relevance_rationale: Multi-column frequency analysis endures conceptually but LLMs can produce cross-tabulations from plain-English requests

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: By default, frequency tables just count the number of times each value appears. But in VisiData, you can specify additional calculations by setting the column's 'aggregators'. VisiData's aggregators include min, max, mean, median, sum, distinct, and others. To add an aggregator to a column, navigate to that column and press +. When using aggregators, make sure that you've assigned the proper type (# for integer columns, etc.) to the columns of interest, so that VisiData knows how to calculate the aggregations correctly.
themes: VisiData; aggregation; summary statistics; data types
relevance_rationale: Aggregation concepts endure but LLMs can compute and explain aggregations without tool-specific keystroke knowledge
notes: Must set column types before aggregation; type assignment is a prerequisite for numerical operations

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: To get a bird's-eye view of your entire dataset, press Shift-I, which will provide summary statistics for each of your columns. In VisiData, this is called the 'Describe Sheet'. You'll notice that there are only min/max/median/etc. calculations for the columns we've given types. If we go back to the data sheet and tell VisiData that the HEIGHT, SPEED, and BIRDS_STRUCK fields are numbers, too, then pressing Shift-I will result in full summary statistics.
themes: VisiData; data profiling; summary statistics; Describe Sheet
relevance_rationale: Summary statistics as a first step endure but LLMs can generate descriptive stats and flag anomalies conversationally
notes: Describe Sheet gives instant dataset overview; numeric summaries require explicit type assignment

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Rather than guess at your column's data types, VisiData assumes that they're all plain-old text. If that column is a number or date, and you want to do any math-y operations on your column (e.g., sorting, summing, averaging, et cetera), you'll have to specify its type. To set a column's type, navigate over to that column and press one of the following keys: # for Integer, % for Float, $ for Currency, @ for Date, ~ for Text.
themes: VisiData; data types; type casting; data preparation
relevance_rationale: Column type setting is tool-specific; LLMs auto-infer data types and handle type coercion in generated code
notes: Explicit type assignment is essential before any numerical analysis in VisiData

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: VisiData provides several ways to filter your datasets: Row selection + double-quote, Frequency tables + Enter, Frequency tables + row selection + g + Enter. Pressing double-quote will create a copy of your current sheet but one that contains only selected rows. From any row in any frequency table, you can press Enter to create a new dataset containing only the rows that match that value.
themes: VisiData; filtering; subsetting; row selection
relevance_rationale: Row filtering concepts endure but LLMs can filter data from natural language descriptions without memorizing tool shortcuts
notes: Three complementary filtering approaches: select-then-copy, frequency-drill-down, multi-value frequency select

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: In VisiData, you can select rows by evaluating a given Python expression for every row in your dataset. These expressions can reference any column in your dataset. The two keystrokes for this are z| and z\. For instance: z| then OPERATOR == 'BUSINESS' and STATE == 'FL' then Enter selects 625 rows matching that compound condition.
themes: VisiData; expression filtering; Python expressions; compound conditions
relevance_rationale: Expression-based row selection endures conceptually but LLMs can generate filter expressions from plain English
notes: Python expressions enable complex multi-column filtering without writing scripts

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Expression columns evaluate a given Python expression for every row in your dataset. These expressions can reference any column in your dataset. You can create an expression column by pressing =. Let's say we want to identify wildlife strikes that were reported to have occurred at least 100 feet above ground. We could press = to bring up the 'new column expr=' prompt, type HEIGHT >= 100, and press Enter.
chapter: How to create an expression column
themes: VisiData; computed columns; derived data; Python expressions
relevance_rationale: Derived columns via expressions endure as a concept but LLMs generate these transformations from natural language

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You can split the text in any column in VisiData into two or more columns, based on a pattern (i.e., a 'regular expression') that you provide. To split a column, navigate to that column, and press :. Each value in the new column is a list of the pieces that resulted from the split. But you probably want each piece in its own column. To do that press (, which is the 'expand column' command.

themes: VisiData; column splitting; regex; data parsing; text processing

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Just like you can split a column by using :, you can extract part of a column into a new column by using ;. For instance, if you want to extract the first string of numbers from each aircraft type, (e.g., 28 from PA-28, 46 from PA-46 MALIBU, and 717 from B-717-200), press ; then type (\d+) (with one set of parentheses for each capture group), and then hit Enter.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: To join two or more sheets in VisiData, you'll do the following: 1. In the sheets you want to join, mark the shared columns as 'keys'. 2. In the Sheets Sheet, select the sheets you want to join. 3. Press & to open the join-choice prompt, type your desired join type and press Enter. VisiData supports seven types of joins: inner, outer, full, diff, append, extend, merge.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: VisiData's pivot tables are similar to pivot tables you might have created in various spreadsheet programs. Pivot tables create a cross-tabulation of two or more columns in a dataset. In VisiData, creating a pivot table involves the following steps: Use \! to designate the column(s) you want to serve as the pivot table's rows. Optional: Use + to specify additional metrics. Navigate to the column you want to serve as the pivot table's columns. Press Shift-W.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

Melting a dataset in VisiData involves the following steps: Optional: Use \! to designate the column(s) you want to keep unmelted. Optional: Use - to hide the columns you don't want to appear, at all, in the melted sheet. Press Shift+M.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: If you're only using VisiData to preview a dataset, consider loading just the beginning of the file. If you're working with a simple CSV file, you can accomplish this by using head on the command-line, combined with vd -f csv e.g.: head -n 1000 faa-wildlife-strikes.csv | vd -f csv. Alternatively, you can use a written-for-speed tool, such as xsv, to slice or filter the file before loading it into VisiData. You can also just press Control-c while the data is loading, which will halt the loading process. VisiData handles this gracefully.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: By default, VisiData considers the first row of a tabular dataset to be its columns' names. That's a safe assumption for many formats, but Excel spreadsheets often buck that trend, with titles, notes, or other cruft coming before the actual column names. You can auto-populate column names by navigating to the row that contains your desired column names, then type g^ to name all unnamed columns with the values in this row, or gz^ to name all columns.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: VisiData is fast: it opens in a blink of an eye, and can load multi-megabyte datasets nearly instantly. It's nimble: makes it easy to search, filter, sort, and join any table. It's great for getting a grasp of new datasets: With just a few keystrokes, you can summarize any column. It works with lots of data formats: CSV, Excel, JSON, and more. It's non-destructive: VisiData won't alter your raw data unless you explicitly tell it to.
themes: VisiData; tool selection; exploratory analysis; non-destructive analysis
relevance_rationale: VisiData speed and nimbleness for data exploration endure but LLM-powered tools offer similar rapid exploration conversationally
notes: Key advantage: non-destructive exploration of data without altering source files

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: VisiData isn't for every task. You might want to choose another tool for: Complex analysis -- you're probably better off using Python, R, or another programming language. 'Literate' analysis -- although VisiData lets you save and replay your analyses, it's not as legible/flexible as Jupyter notebooks, R Markdown, or other tools. Mission-critical analysis -- VisiData is well-engineered, but still a relatively young piece of software.
themes: tool selection; reproducibility; analytical complexity; limitations
relevance_rationale: Understanding tool limitations — when to use programming languages vs exploratory tools — is durable analytical judgment
notes: VisiData is for exploration, not production analysis; literate/reproducible workflows need different tools

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Dynamic columns (for instance, those created with the = command) are calculated 'lazily' -- i.e., only when they're needed. If you're running a lot of operations on a dynamic column -- perhaps calculating its average, median, and sum -- you can save some time by first 'caching' it. When you cache a dynamic column, VisiData calculates its current state and never recomputes it. To cache (or re-cache) a column, navigate to it in your sheet and type z'.
themes: VisiData; performance optimization; lazy evaluation; caching
relevance_rationale: Column caching for performance is VisiData-specific optimization knowledge that LLMs handle transparently
notes: Cache computed columns before running multiple aggregations to avoid repeated recalculation

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

Press | to raise the select-by-search prompt, type 'unknown', press Enter. Now, all the unknown species are selected. But we want the opposite of that: only known species selected. To do that, let's toggle the selection for all rows, by typing gt (mnemonic: 'global toggle'). Now press double-quote to create a new sheet containing only those rows.

Then create a frequency table for the combination of STATE and SPECIES columns using gF. Then join the two frequency tables using the three-step join process with an inner join. Now that we have the numerator and denominator in the same sheet, let's calculate the percentage with an expression column: count * 100 / state_total.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Generally, a web page consists of specifically formatted text files: .html (HyperText Markup Language) files give structure to a page, .css (Cascading Style Sheet) files determine how the page looks, .js (JavaScript) files add interactivity. Most HTML elements are represented by a pair of tags --- an opening tag and a closing tag. HTML elements can have any number of attributes, such as IDs, which uniquely identify elements, classes, which identify a type of element, and styles, which define how specific elements appear --- that will be useful to know about when we're scraping.
themes: HTML; web structure; scraping foundations; element identification
relevance_rationale: Understanding HTML/CSS/JS structure is foundational web literacy that persists for anyone working with web data
notes: Essential web literacy for identifying scrapeable elements

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You can inspect specific elements on the page by right-clicking on the page and selecting 'Inspect' or 'Inspect Element' from the context menu. Hover over elements in the 'Elements' tab to highlight them on the page. This can be helpful when you're trying to figure how to uniquely identify the element you want to scrape. To examine all of the source code that makes up a page, you can 'view source.' Try to locate the element(s) that you want to target (use Ctrl+F to find).
themes: browser devtools; element inspection; scraping preparation; HTML navigation
relevance_rationale: Browser inspection of page elements is a durable skill for understanding web data structure
notes: Browser DevTools as prerequisite step before writing scraping code

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: There are many ways to grab content from HTML, and every page you scrape data from will require a slightly different trick. At this stage, your job is to find a pattern or identifier in the code for the element you'd like to extract, which we will then give as instructions to our Python code. Inspect the table element or view the page source. How would you pick the table element out from all of the elements that make up the page? Is it the only table? If not, does it have any attributes, like a class or ID, that would allow you to target it?
themes: web scraping; pattern recognition; element targeting; HTML analysis
relevance_rationale: Finding patterns in HTML to target data extraction is a core scraping skill that endures even with LLM assistance
notes: Every scrape requires finding unique identifiers -- no universal approach

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Today, we'll use two third-party Python libraries to help us scrape: requests is the de facto standard for making HTTP requests, similar to what happens when you type a URL into a browser window and hit enter. bs4, or BeautifulSoup, is a popular library for parsing HTML into a data structure that Python can work with.
themes: Python; requests; BeautifulSoup; web scraping; libraries
relevance_rationale: requests and BeautifulSoup remain widely used but LLMs can generate complete scraping scripts from page descriptions
notes: Core Python scraping stack: requests + BeautifulSoup

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: If you want to make sure that your request was successful, you can check the status_code attribute of the Python object that was returned. A 200 code means all is well. 404 means the page wasn't found, etc.
themes: HTTP status codes; error checking; web requests; validation
relevance_rationale: HTTP status code checking for request validation is fundamental web literacy that persists
notes: Always verify HTTP response status before proceeding with parsing

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Before we start targeting and extracting pieces of data in the HTML, we need to turn that chunk of text into a data structure that Python can work with. That's where the BeautifulSoup (bs4) library comes in. We'll create a new instance of a BeautifulSoup object. We need to give it two things: The HTML we'd like to parse and a string with the name of the type of parser to use --- html.parser is the default and usually fine, but there are other options.
themes: BeautifulSoup; HTML parsing; data structures; web scraping
relevance_rationale: BeautifulSoup HTML parsing endures but LLMs can extract data from HTML without manual soup object manipulation
notes: Parse HTML string into navigable tree structure

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Our general strategy: 1. Find the main table with the data we want to grab. 2. Get a list of rows (the tr element) in that table. 3. Use a Python for loop to go through each table row and find the data inside it (td, or 'table data'). To accomplish this, we'll use two bs4 methods: find(), which returns the first element that matches whatever criteria you hand it, and find_all(), which returns a list of elements that match the criteria.
themes: BeautifulSoup; table extraction; web scraping; find; find_all
relevance_rationale: Table row iteration pattern endures but LLMs can generate complete table extraction code from a page description
notes: Standard table-scraping pattern: find table, find rows, loop and extract cells

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Notice that the first item that prints is the header row with the column labels. You are free to keep these headers if you want, but I typically skip that row and define my own list of column names. Another thing to consider: On better-constructed web pages, the cells in the header row will be represented by th ('table header') tags, not td ('table data') tags. We can skip the first row by using list slicing: rows[1:], which means, take everything in the rows list starting with the second item.
chapter: Loop through the rows and extract the data
themes: web scraping; table parsing; header handling; list slicing; Python
relevance_rationale: Header skipping and column naming in scraping endures but LLMs generate these patterns automatically
notes: Handle header rows separately; th vs td distinction matters

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: This is returning the entire Tag object --- we just want the contents inside it. You can access the .text attribute of the tag to get the text inside. It's also generally good practice to trim off external whitespace for each value, and you can use the Python built-in string method strip() to accomplish this as you march across the row.
chapter: Loop through the rows and extract the data
themes: text extraction; whitespace cleaning; strip; web scraping; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Text extraction and whitespace trimming endures but LLMs handle these cleaning steps in generated scraping code automatically
notes: Always strip whitespace when extracting text from HTML elements

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Now that we've targeted our lists of data for each row, we can use Python's built-in csv module to write each list to a CSV file. Define a list of headers to match the data. Using a with block, open a new CSV file to write to: Create a csv.writer object, Write out the list of headers using the writerow() method, Drop in the for loop and instead of just printing, create a list of items and use writerow() to write your list of data to file.
themes: CSV output; Python csv module; data export; web scraping
relevance_rationale: CSV writing with Python endures but LLMs generate complete file-output code from natural language
notes: Standard pattern for writing scraped data to CSV

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Remove internal whitespace: Python does not have a built-in string method to remove internal whitespace, but Googling around will yield a common strategy: Using the split() method to separate individual words in the string, then join()ing the resulting list on a single space. As a one-liner: my_text_clean = ' '.join(my_text.split()).
themes: whitespace cleaning; Python string methods; split-join pattern; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Manual internal whitespace removal via split/join is trivial for LLMs to handle in generated code without explicit instruction
notes: split-then-join idiom for normalizing internal whitespace

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Build a lookup table: Each numeric code in the 'WIA Code' column corresponds to a local area. See if you can figure out how to create a lookup dictionary that maps the numbers to their locations, then as you're looping over the data table, replace the numeric value in that column with the name of the local area instead.

relevance_rationale: Building lookup tables from code-to-name mappings endures conceptually but LLMs generate these dictionaries from descriptions

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Fix encoding errors: You might have noticed a few encoding problems --- e.g., Nestle is being rendered as NestlA. This is due to an encoding problem --- the warn_page.text is not encoded as utf-8. Using decode() and encode(), see if you can fix this. Hint: It looks like the state of Maryland is a big fan of latin-1.
themes: character encoding; utf-8; latin-1; data cleaning; web scraping

notes: Encoding mismatches common in government web scraping; detect and convert

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Dynamic columns (for instance, those created with the = command) are calculated 'lazily' --- i.e., only when they're needed. If you're running a lot of operations on a dynamic column --- perhaps calculating its average, median, and sum --- you can save some time by first 'caching' it. When you cache a dynamic column, VisiData calculates its current state and never recomputes it. To cache a column, navigate to it and type z'.
themes: VisiData; performance; lazy evaluation; caching; computed columns
relevance_rationale: Column caching for performance is VisiData-specific; LLMs handle performance optimization transparently in generated code
notes: Cache computed columns to avoid redundant recalculation

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Causal inference is the process of determining causality. The most important thing to remember is that the heart of causality is manipulation. You have to change something in order to measure the effect of it. This lack of manipulation is the reason why correlation is not causation.
themes: causal inference; correlation vs causation; analytical rigour
relevance_rationale: Causal inference principles and the distinction between correlation and causation are fundamental statistical reasoning that LLMs cannot replace
notes: Core principle: no causation without manipulation

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Good causal inference questions sound like experiments. If you can't think of an experiment that would help you answer a causal question, you probably can't answer it at all, even if you use a lot of advanced statistics.
themes: causal inference; question framing; research design
relevance_rationale: The experimental framing test for causal questions is an enduring analytical reasoning heuristic
notes: Practical litmus test for whether a causal question is answerable

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for causal inference. But in the real world, they are often too expensive, too time-consuming, or too unethical. If you can't run an experiment, the next best thing is to use a quasi-experimental method, which involves creating a treatment and control group from an existing dataset without designing and running an experiment yourself.
themes: causal inference; quasi-experimental methods; research design; hierarchy of evidence
relevance_rationale: The hierarchy of evidence from RCTs to quasi-experiments to regression is foundational research design knowledge
notes: Establishes a hierarchy: RCT > quasi-experiment > regression

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Difference in differences is useful when you have two groups, one treatment and one control, and two points in time, one before the intervention and another after. Card and Krueger used this approach to compare employment levels in New Jersey, which raised its minimum wage, to nearby areas of Pennsylvania, which did not.
themes: difference in differences; quasi-experimental methods; causal inference
relevance_rationale: Difference-in-differences as a quasi-experimental method is enduring statistical methodology
notes: Concrete example from economics applied to policy journalism

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Regression discontinuity design is useful when there is a cutoff in some continuous variable that results in very different outcomes for observations near the cutoff, even though all the observations around the cutoff are fairly similar. By comparing observations just above and just below the cutoff, we can measure a causal effect.
themes: regression discontinuity; quasi-experimental methods; causal inference
relevance_rationale: Regression discontinuity design is enduring statistical methodology for causal inference
notes: Illustrated with incumbency advantage example using vote share cutoff at 50%

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Regressions are often used to illustrate the relationship between two variables. But causality can't be measured without manipulation, so while a regression can help test a causal hypothesis by showing whether data is consistent with it, it can't on its own prove causality. Even when you're controlling for a lot of variables, you're really just doing a more advanced correlation.
themes: regression; correlation vs causation; analytical rigour; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Warning against over-interpreting regression as causal is critical statistical literacy that persists in the LLM era
notes: Key warning against over-interpreting regression results

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You should be extremely skeptical of drawing conclusions from correlations. You cannot make up for bad causal inference methods with more data, more code, more graphics, or more credentials.
themes: correlation vs causation; analytical rigour; skepticism; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward correlational claims and resistance to big-data solutionism are more important than ever with LLMs
notes: Concluding warning; the 'more data' point pushes back against big-data solutionism

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: When should you use a survey? The data doesn't exist or is really bad. There are no public records. You are looking to connect with sources. You want to collect personal stories.
themes: survey design; data gaps; source development
relevance_rationale: Decision framework for when to create your own data via surveys remains valid; LLMs do not fill data gaps where no records exist
notes: Decision framework for when to create your own data via surveys

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Talk to experts in this field: What information is already out there? What questions would the expert ask if they were writing the survey? What new information do you hope to learn? Identify your sources. Who is answering the survey? Collect and verify email addresses. How much of that population do you want to answer the survey? Do you want statistical significance and scientific rigor? Or a small sample? What questions are you trying to answer? Run the survey questions by at least one expert to find problems.
themes: survey design; expert consultation; methodology planning
relevance_rationale: Pre-survey expert consultation and population scoping are research design fundamentals that AI cannot replace
notes: Pre-survey planning checklist emphasizing expert review and population scoping

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Define your terms. Loose language gives sources room to hedge. Consider your format: Surveymonkey? Google form? Individual emails? Decide if the survey will be anonymous or not. If it is not anonymous, ask who is answering the survey and their title for attribution. Give sources a reasonable deadline, with time for you to follow up.
themes: survey design; precision; attribution; tool selection
relevance_rationale: Defining precise survey terms, choosing format, and managing attribution are research design decisions requiring human judgment
notes: Survey execution guidance: term definition, format choice, anonymity decisions

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Read responses as they come in and contact promising sources early. Did you give an early deadline? You can clarify unclear or non-responses. Talk to community leaders if something seems off (and to verify your info).
themes: survey follow-up; source verification; community validation
relevance_rationale: Real-time survey monitoring and community cross-checking of responses are human verification practices unaffected by AI
notes: Post-survey verification through community cross-checks

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Carefully define terms in your initial contact. For me I should have outlined exactly what a SANE certification means (dozens of hours of classroom/clinical time, passing exam). I would have added another question for the number of nurses who had some SANE training but not a full certification. Specifically ask for the name and title of survey respondent.
themes: survey design; term definition; lessons learned
relevance_rationale: Lesson that ambiguous terminology undermines survey quality is a research design principle independent of AI tools
notes: Retrospective lesson: ambiguous terminology undermines survey responses

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Filed records requests with 3 state agencies: None tracked SANE nurses. State nursing board also did not have reliable information. Searched any recent academic or legislative information on SANE numbers. Talked to experts in the space and ran survey questions by them. Collected and verified contact information for 130 hospitals and programs.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

Tested the survey among those we planned to interview. Paid homeless individuals to help us do the survey work (budget: $1,500). Made it anonymous. Google forms for data input.

extract: Better Questions: Mix of quantitative & qualitative. Space for 'why' & 'if-then'. Better tracking: Utilizing traditional survey tools. Using 'no response' to tell the story.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Use mean for numbers that vary little: sports stats, commute times, water levels, etc. Use median for numbers with potential outliers: salaries, income, wealth, home values, etc. The mode is the most frequently occurring number; it is rarely used in journalism.
themes: descriptive statistics; mean vs median; data interpretation
relevance_rationale: Decision rules for choosing mean vs median in journalism are core data literacy skills that LLMs do not replace
notes: Decision rules for choosing the right average in journalism

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: For this data set, the best average is the median. Because most people think 'average' can only be a mean, phrase it this way: The median, or midpoint, was $120,000.
themes: writing with numbers; audience literacy; descriptive statistics
relevance_rationale: Practical phrasing advice for explaining median to general audiences is a writing skill that remains human editorial work
notes: Practical phrasing advice for explaining median to general audience

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Statistics help us sift meaningful patterns from random chance. Random fluctuations in data are normal. Statisticians call this noise. For example, we want to know if data showing racial disparities are indicative of a meaningful pattern or just noise.
themes: statistical reasoning; signal vs noise; significance
relevance_rationale: Understanding that statistics separate meaningful patterns from random noise is foundational analytical literacy
notes: Foundational framing of statistics as pattern-vs-noise detection

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Statistics are not voodoo. They are based on empirical testing and numerical laws such as the central limit theorem. It is, after all, a science. On the other hand, statistics are not magic. They require interpretation to avoid false interpretations or ascribe undue importance.
themes: statistical literacy; interpretation; intellectual humility
relevance_rationale: Statistics require human interpretation to avoid false conclusions; this warning is amplified when AI makes analysis easier
notes: Dual warning: statistics are real science but require human judgment

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Correlation does not imply causation. Consider a plausible alternative explanation. An increase in antidepressants among older adults means lower suicide rates. However, that may be partly due to improvements in drugs that have reduced death risk from overdoses. Beware the law of small numbers. Rural U.S. states have both the highest and lowest rates of cancers. This is not a meaningful pattern but the result of the greater variance inherent when population numbers are relatively small.
themes: correlation vs causation; alternative explanations; small numbers; statistical pitfalls
relevance_rationale: Correlation-causation distinction, alternative explanations, and law of small numbers are timeless statistical reasoning principles
notes: Three core statistical interpretation principles for journalists

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Because human behavior is so complex, social science sets the level of probability (p) to separate patterns from noise at less than 5%, or p < .05. Colloquially, p < .05 means there was a less than 5% probability a relationship was due to chance. Although p < .05 is considered statistically significant, a more precise term would be statistically noticeable or statistically detectable (Jordan Ellenberg, p. 121). Whether that relationship is meaningful depends on human judgment – yes, you!
themes: p-values; statistical significance; interpretation; human judgment
relevance_rationale: Understanding p-values, statistical significance vs practical significance, and the role of human judgment is essential regardless of AI
notes: Reframing significance as detectability; judgment remains with the journalist

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Correlations range from 0.00 (no relationship) to 1.00 (perfect relationship). The correlation coefficient measures the strength of the relationship. A general rule of thumb (Cohen, 1988): 0.10 to 0.28 Small, 0.30 to 0.49 Moderate, 0.50 to 0.99 Large.
themes: correlation; effect size; benchmarks
relevance_rationale: Cohen benchmarks for interpreting correlation strength are reference knowledge journalists need to evaluate AI-generated analyses
notes: Cohen's benchmarks for interpreting correlation strength

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The strong correlation does not equal uniformity. Some cities are above the line (higher crime rates) and some are below. Also, do not treat this strong correlation as even remotely normal. In my research, 0.40 is about as strong as it gets.
themes: correlation; interpretation; variance; real-world expectations
relevance_rationale: Calibrating expectations for real-world correlation strength and understanding within-group variance is essential interpretive skill
notes: Calibrating expectations: 0.40 is typical ceiling for social science correlations

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: A third and more dominant variable may be at work: money. Schools that pay big salaries to coaches also can afford larger academic counseling staffs who keep players on track to graduate. The presence of a third, more influential variable, is reason to consider a plausible alternative explanation. The result may reflect a data definition that produces relative conformity. The NCAA created the GSR, or Graduation Success Rate, to be more generous than the federal data standard. The GSR allows universities to ignore athletes who transferred or who turned pro before graduation. Counting only players who stayed until the end of their athletic careers compresses potential variance.
themes: confounding variables; data definitions; institutional metrics; alternative explanations
relevance_rationale: Identifying confounding variables and questioning data definitions that manufacture patterns is core analytical thinking AI cannot replace
notes: How third variables and metric definitions can mask or manufacture patterns

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Despite the moderate strength of the relationship, unemployment was not a primary factor in vote share. Something else was. This statistic is an example of how statistics must be read carefully.
themes: spurious correlation; interpretation; critical reading of data

A school has 300 white and 120 Black students. Last year, 30 white students were suspended, and 20 Black students were suspended. Calculate the suspension rate for Black students: 20/120 = 0.17. author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

relevance_rationale: OpenRefine as no-code cleaning tool is still useful but LLMs now offer conversational data cleaning that is more accessible to non-coders

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: This will 'pivot' the data from individual columns for each year to putting all of the years into one column and creating a bunch more rows, essentially making it longer and thinner. Select Transpose and then Transpose Cells Across Columns into Rows. Make sure the box marked Fill down in other columns is checked.
themes: wide to long; pivot; data reshaping; OpenRefine
relevance_rationale: Wide-to-long data reshaping concepts endure but LLMs can now perform these transformations via natural language prompts

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: While I generally use simple R commands to pivot my data, I still will often run data through OpenRefine before I open it in RStudio because it is very smart at recognizing patterns in cells that might take me longer to address in an R script.
themes: tool selection; OpenRefine; R; pattern recognition; cleaning workflow
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine clustering for pattern recognition in messy text is being supplemented by LLM-based entity resolution and fuzzy matching
notes: Using OpenRefine for pattern-based cleaning even when R is available, because its clustering algorithms are faster for messy text.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Clustering is my favorite function within OpenRefine, because it can allow you to quickly clean up a big dataset by identifying similar text values without a bunch of fancy fuzzy matching or other complex processes. OpenRefine uses two overarching clustering categories. The first, key collision, creates alternate versions of strings that contain only the most meaningful parts (the key part), then buckets the strings together based on whether those keys match (the collision part). The second, nearest neighbor, compares all the different strings to each other and bins them when they are similar enough. But nearest neighbor methods take forever for big datasets because each pair needs to be compared to every other pair.
themes: clustering; key collision; nearest neighbor; fuzzy matching; entity resolution; OpenRefine
relevance_rationale: Clustering algorithms like key collision and nearest neighbor remain valid but LLMs offer more flexible entity resolution approaches
notes: Two clustering strategies: key collision (efficient, fewer errors) vs nearest neighbor (thorough but slow). Key collision preferred for large datasets.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The first, fingerprint, is the default key collision method, because it tends to make the fewest mistakes (fingerprint groups different strings together by removing whitespace, changing all characters to lowercase, removing punctuation and several other intuitive steps.) There's also metaphone3, a method that transforms strings into how they are typically pronounced. This is useful for errors that involve misspellings.
themes: fingerprint method; metaphone; string normalization; misspelling detection; OpenRefine
relevance_rationale: Fingerprint and metaphone methods are still valid algorithmic approaches but LLMs can handle misspelling detection more contextually
notes: Fingerprint (safest default) then metaphone (pronunciation-based) as sequential cleaning strategy.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Select Edit cells -> Common transforms -> Trim leading and trailing whitespace. This removes any spaces that are in the cells either before or after the city name. Now let's do one other easy transform, Edit cells -> Common transforms -> To titlecase.
themes: whitespace trimming; case normalization; text cleaning; OpenRefine
relevance_rationale: Basic text transforms like whitespace trimming and case normalization are now trivially handled by LLM-generated cleaning scripts
notes: Simple transforms (trim whitespace, titlecase) should be applied before clustering for quick wins.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: This Undo/Redo pane, with all of our steps listed in a linear fashion, is also helpful to use in the future, should we encounter similar data and want to clean it quickly. Click the Extract button and the window Extract Operation History should pop up. Check the tasks that you'd want to repeat on similar data. Open a TextEdit window and paste the code you generated into a new window and save it with a .json extension. Now if you want to apply the same commands to a similar data file, you can\! Just hit Apply, paste the .json script you saved into the window, and watch the replicable magic happen.
themes: reproducibility; operation history; reusable scripts; OpenRefine; JSON export
relevance_rationale: Exporting cleaning steps as reusable JSON scripts for reproducibility is a sound practice that generalizes to any pipeline tool
notes: Export cleaning steps as JSON for reuse on similar datasets. Key reproducibility feature of OpenRefine.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Some of the values have parentheses to indicate they are negative amounts, and some have commas while others do not. These characters and the dollar symbols are making the column get recognized as a text column rather than as numeric. You can click on Edit Cells and then click Replace. Type in an opening parenthesis into the Find box and then in the Replace box, type in a dash. Then get rid of the other three annoying characters by typing them into the Find box and leaving the Replace box blank. Once you do this, select Edit Cells, Common Transforms, To Number.
themes: type conversion; currency cleaning; string replacement; numeric parsing; OpenRefine
relevance_rationale: Manual string replacement for financial data cleaning is now easily automated by LLM-generated cleaning scripts
notes: Step-by-step: strip parentheses/commas/dollar signs then convert to numeric. Common pattern for financial data.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: A regular expression is a common, compact way to represent some sort of pattern in text. You can use one to: Help you search within a document. Reformat or otherwise clean up an inconsistent or 'dirty' data set. Extract structured data from plain text. Many common programming languages, text editors and search tools support regular expressions, so you can use them in whatever environment you find most comfortable.
themes: regex; pattern matching; text extraction; data cleaning; cross-platform
relevance_rationale: Regex as a cross-platform tool endures but LLMs can now generate regex patterns from natural language and handle many text extraction tasks directly
notes: Myers/AP. Three use cases for regex: search, clean, extract. Portable across languages and editors.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Another is to save those groups so you can refer to them later, such as in the replacement step of a find-and-replace operation. These often are referenced by their position; for example, in (\d\d\d)-(\d\d\d)-(\d\d\d\d), you might use \1 to refer to the first three digits, \2 to refer to the second three digits and \3 to refer to the last four digits.
themes: regex; capture groups; find-and-replace; data reformatting
relevance_rationale: Regex capture groups for reformatting remain useful but LLMs can now generate these patterns and perform complex text restructuring directly
notes: Myers/AP. Using regex capture groups for structured reformatting of text data.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Different programming languages, text editors and other tools handle regular expressions in their own ways. Some environments have different rules about when certain characters do and don't need to be escaped. Some languages such as R require backslashes themselves to be escaped, so you might have to type twice as many backslashes as you otherwise would think.
themes: regex; cross-platform differences; escaping; portability
relevance_rationale: Cross-platform regex differences and escaping rules are less burdensome when LLMs generate environment-specific patterns on demand
notes: Myers/AP. Regex syntax varies by environment; R double-escaping is a common gotcha.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Average/Mean - Most common (Total/# of items). Median - Middle value. Better to show typical value. Mode: Value that comes up the most.
themes: central tendency; mean; median; mode; descriptive statistics; Excel
relevance_rationale: Understanding mean, median, and mode and when to use each is foundational statistical literacy that LLMs do not replace
