author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Standard Deviation: How far numbers differ from the median. In a normal curve, 68% of values are within 1 standard deviation. 95% are within 2. And 99.7% are within three. So a difference of 2 standard deviations is usually significant. Outliers are generally beyond three standard deviations. Standard Error: How much numbers of a sample are likely to differ from the true value in the overall population. Bigger samples have lower standard errors.
themes: standard deviation; standard error; outliers; significance; dispersion; Excel
relevance_rationale: Understanding standard deviation, standard error, and outlier thresholds is core statistical literacy for data journalists
notes: Wallack/WBUR. Rule of thumb: 2 SD = significant, 3 SD = outlier. Standard error decreases with sample size.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Per capita rates are 'per person' or per unit. Number / Population. But you can convert to other rates like Per 1,000 or Per 100,000. Best to use a rate that allows you to express numbers more clearly. Like 15 homicides per 100,000 people. Instead of .00015 gun deaths per person or per capita. Experiment with the rate that works best.
themes: rates; per capita; normalization; data presentation; clarity
relevance_rationale: Choosing appropriate rate denominators for readability is a presentation judgment that requires human editorial sense
notes: Wallack/WBUR. Choose rate denominator for readability; avoid tiny decimals.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Correlation is between 0 (weak) and 1 (strong). Negative correlations mean when one value goes up, the other goes down.
themes: correlation; statistical analysis; Excel
relevance_rationale: Basic correlation interpretation is foundational statistical literacy that journalists must understand regardless of AI assistance

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Regression only works on numerical values, like salaries and height. If you want to convert a categorical variable to numerical, you need to turn it into a numerical value (often called a dummy variable). So for instance, if you have a gender column with categories like male, female and nonbinary, you might create a new column called 'female' that has 1 for female and 0 for not female.
themes: regression; dummy variables; categorical encoding; Excel
relevance_rationale: Understanding dummy variable encoding for regression is statistical knowledge journalists need to interpret AI-generated analysis
notes: Wallack/WBUR. Creating dummy variables for categorical data to enable regression analysis.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

extract: Errors are going to happen, so anticipate and measure them. Take a large handful, evaluate with a human. Out of 4 scored by a human as YES, our AI marked 3 as YES and one as MAYBE. Out of the 5 scored as YES by the AI, the human only agreed on 3 of them.

notes: Embeddings enable meaning-based search across multilingual document troves

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: These days everyone loves to search across documents. You shouldn't do it, but people love it.
themes: RAG; document QA; skepticism; reliability
relevance_rationale: Warning against naive RAG-based document search without understanding limitations remains crucial
notes: Skepticism toward RAG-based document QA for journalism — links to NYC chatbot failures

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Being able to use apps opens up a whole new world of testing, helping ensure all of the participants are comfortable with the use and quality of the tool. Whether AI is flawed or AI is a perfect angel, the more people who are participating with it the better! Technical people can get too obsessed with playing with their toys, and even normal folks can get into patterns and miss blind spots.
themes: Gradio; newsroom adoption; testing; inclusivity; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Using apps/interfaces for testing AI outputs ensures broader newsroom accessibility to quality assurance
notes: Lightweight app interfaces (Gradio) democratize AI tool evaluation across newsroom roles

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: AI can help us gather and present news more efficiently. But AI is frequently convincing but wrong -- sometimes in ways that only an expert, or at least an attentive reader, can detect. If we don't want AI to degrade the quality of our journalism, we need to make sure that it's doing a good job by quantitatively assessing the results of our AI methodologies.
themes: AI verification; quantitative assessment; journalism quality
relevance_rationale: Quantitative assessment of AI methodology is a foundational principle for responsible AI-assisted journalism

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Vibes are not enough. Use real examples. You need to formally define what's a right or wrong answer. You also need to check the model's performance against the true distribution of input data (not just examples you came up with off the top of your head).
chapter: Why quantitatively assess your AI methodology?
themes: ground truth; evaluation; vibes; formal testing; LLM
relevance_rationale: Vibes-based evaluation is insufficient; formal accuracy measurement is a timeless methodological requirement
notes: Anti-pattern: relying on informal impression of AI output quality

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You need to know your accuracy rate. It's usually okay if the accuracy rate isn't 100% (AI trades accuracy for speed/scale), but it's good to know how much accuracy you're giving up.
chapter: Why quantitatively assess your AI methodology?
themes: accuracy rate; tradeoffs; speed vs accuracy; LLM
relevance_rationale: Knowing your accuracy rate and accepting imperfect-but-known performance is fundamental to responsible AI use
notes: Explicit accuracy-speed tradeoff acknowledgment

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You might need MORE than an accuracy rate. Different kinds of errors are bad in different ways, so you should quantify those. If your AI methodology misses Trump saying 'Good morning' at the start of a segment, that's not a big problem. If quotes from others get misattributed to Trump, or are just made up, that is a big problem.
chapter: Why quantitatively assess your AI methodology?
themes: error taxonomy; false positives; false negatives; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing error types (false positives vs negatives) transcends any particular AI tool generation
notes: Not all errors are equal; classify error severity by journalistic impact

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Your prompt can usually be improved. You can probably spend 10 minutes fiddling with your prompt to make it better. If you have an accuracy rate, you can see if a change to a prompt makes the accuracy rate go up.
chapter: Why quantitatively assess your AI methodology?
themes: prompt engineering; iteration; accuracy measurement
relevance_rationale: Iterative prompt improvement through systematic testing is a durable practice for AI-assisted journalism
notes: Accuracy rate as feedback signal for prompt refinement

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Find ground truth. Select 50 random examples, put them in a spreadsheet, make a column for the right answer, then go through the examples and write the right answer. Why random? You want to estimate how well your model performs at the real task. If you select just a few examples -- or, worse, just try to make up a few -- your ground truth data might miss some of the complexities of your real data, causing your accuracy rate to be wrong. Why a few dozen? You want to roughly reflect the diversity of your data, and you'll learn about gaps in your methodology from seeing the kinds of errors it makes.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: ground truth; gold data; random sampling; annotation; evaluation
relevance_rationale: Ground-truth validation with random samples is a timeless evaluation methodology
notes: Step 1 of AI methodology assessment: create gold data from random sample of 50

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Extra credit: generate two ground truth datasets, one for preliminary development, and one for calculating your final accuracy rates.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: train-test split; gold data; evaluation methodology
relevance_rationale: Separate dev and test datasets to avoid overfitting is a universal machine learning best practice
notes: Dev/test split prevents overfitting prompt to evaluation set

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The accuracy rate is just the number of right answers divided by the total number of examples. You can also use metrics like Krippendorff's Alpha and Cohen's Kappa.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: accuracy; inter-rater reliability; Krippendorff Alpha; Cohen Kappa
relevance_rationale: Computing accuracy as correct/total is a basic metric unaffected by LLM advances
notes: Beyond simple accuracy: inter-annotator agreement metrics

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Stretch goal: classify answers as right, wrong, or okay. What if the model's guess doesn't match the right answer, but it's still an okay answer, perhaps because it's close enough or the right answer is ambiguous? You have to make this decision for yourself, based on your task.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: evaluation; ambiguity; partial credit; annotation guidelines
relevance_rationale: Three-tier classification (right/wrong/okay) for nuanced accuracy assessment is a lasting evaluation practice
notes: Three-tier evaluation (right/wrong/okay) for ambiguous classification tasks

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Consider false positives versus false negatives in establishing accuracy. You may try to balance the two (balancing precision and recall) if you're trying to establish estimates of the prevalence of classes within the dataset; or, you might want to bias your classifier towards generating fewer false positives in exchange for more false negatives, or vice versa. This is task specific; there's no right answer.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: precision; recall; false positives; false negatives; tradeoffs
relevance_rationale: False positive vs false negative trade-off analysis is fundamental to any classification task
notes: Task-specific precision/recall tradeoff decision

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: What's the baseline? If you're doing a classification task and one answer is more common than another, if you guessed the most common answer for everything, what would your accuracy rate be? The accuracy of your AI methodology should be substantially higher than this baseline.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: baseline; majority class; evaluation; classification
relevance_rationale: Establishing baselines before evaluating AI performance is a permanent methodological requirement
notes: Always compare AI accuracy against naive majority-class baseline

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Ask yourself, how good is good enough? If you're publishing AI-written text, unedited by humans, under your byline, 100% accuracy is probably the bar -- because errors mean corrections. On the other hand, if you're filtering a bunch of videos to find which ones talk about abortion so that a reporter can watch them and verify each one, the bar is probably much lower. For the latter kind of project, accuracy rates around 70% might be good enough. Consider publishing the accuracy rate in your methodology.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: accuracy threshold; human-in-the-loop; methodology transparency; risk
relevance_rationale: Defining acceptable accuracy thresholds based on use case and stakes is a durable editorial principle
notes: Accuracy bar depends on whether human verification follows; publish accuracy in methodology

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: If you're using AI to estimate the prevalence of something, use traditional sampling methodologies to calculate an estimate with a margin of error.
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: sampling; margin of error; prevalence estimation; statistical methods
relevance_rationale: Using sampling to estimate prevalence rather than trusting raw AI counts is a core statistical principle
notes: Apply traditional survey methodology when AI produces prevalence estimates

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Try variations of your original prompt and see if the accuracy rate is higher. Test a different LLM and see if the accuracy rate is higher. Some general guidance is that all else being equal, a bigger model will perform better on any task than a smaller model from the same provider. Conversely, a smaller model will almost always be faster and cheaper to run, so if you can get equivalent results with the smaller one, use that. Newer models tend to be better\!
chapter: How to quantitatively assess your AI methodology's success
themes: model selection; prompt iteration; cost optimization; LLM comparison
relevance_rationale: Systematic prompt variation testing to optimize accuracy is a durable AI evaluation practice
notes: Post-assessment optimization: vary prompt, model size, and provider

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: NHTSA investigation alerter evaluation: Get a spreadsheet of 100 recent investigations. Apply a proposed prompt to each investigation via the OpenAI API. Examine the investigations classified by AI as 'irrelevant' and code which ones are actually relevant. Then examine the ones classified as 'relevant' and code which ones are actually irrelevant. I find that the mental load is lower when you're looking for a single kind of error, rather than making a decision from scratch about each one. Calculate accuracy rate, false-positive rate and false-negative rate. Think about trade-offs between false positives (alert fatigue) and false negatives (possibly missing a story). Seeing too high of a false-positive rate, adjust the prompt and repeat.
themes: evaluation workflow; false positives; false negatives; alert fatigue; prompt iteration
relevance_rationale: Concrete evaluation workflow (100 samples, code ground truth, compare) is a reusable template for any AI task
notes: Concrete evaluation workflow: code errors by type separately to reduce cognitive load

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Florida private schools evaluation: Extract sample of 50 schools and research each to manually code them. Test simple prompt across the 50 school sample. Calculate accuracy rate. Tweak the prompt to decrease false-negative rate, aiming to maximize recall because all the flagged schools will be manually checked by reporters. Repeat multiple times, comparing new prompt answers against the already-generated gold data.
themes: evaluation workflow; recall; human-in-the-loop; gold data; iteration
relevance_rationale: Sample-based validation of AI extraction against manual research is a durable quality assurance method
notes: Maximize recall when human reporters will verify all positives anyway

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Podcast guests extraction evaluation: Generate an initial prompt and apply it to 100 episode descriptions. Quickly go through the 100 descriptions and extracted guest names, correcting the incorrect ones, and treat that as preliminary gold data. Calculate accuracy rate, incorrectly-included non-guest rate (false-positive rate) and missed-guest rate (false-negative rate). Look at misclassified episode summaries, adjust the preliminary gold data in case there are places where you made a mistake (which is really likely). Eventually, I added a second column -- not just the 'right answer' but also 'okay' answers that would be ignored for calculating accuracy rates.
themes: extraction; evaluation workflow; gold data revision; okay category; false positives
relevance_rationale: Iterative prompt refinement with ground truth comparison is fundamental to responsible AI journalism
notes: Iterative gold data refinement: annotator errors are common and should be corrected during prompt iteration

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: TikTok ad classification evaluation: Build a list of 50 TikTok videos already manually classified across all three groups. Test simple prompt across the 50 video list. Run the prompt again across the videos two more times with randomized ordering of the videos to again generate classifications. Calculate an accuracy rate for each round. Look at variability in accuracy across rounds to potentially adjust the prompts to generate more consistent results.
themes: evaluation workflow; consistency; ordering effects; multi-run; LLM
relevance_rationale: Using pre-classified examples to evaluate AI classification accuracy is a permanent evaluation technique
notes: Multi-run with randomized ordering tests for LLM consistency and order sensitivity

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You have to ruthlessly check this. It's good, but it is not perfect.
themes: LLM verification; data extraction; skepticism
relevance_rationale: Ruthless checking of AI outputs is a timeless principle regardless of how good models get
notes: Matt Waite on LLM-based data extraction quality

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Offload tasks we would do because it would be better, but aren't worth spending the time doing.
themes: AI use case; cost-benefit; newsroom workflow; task delegation
relevance_rationale: Using AI for tasks that are valuable but not worth full manual effort is a durable triage principle
notes: Matt Waite: LLMs best for tasks that improve quality but aren't worth manual time

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Traditionally, journalists would use formulas in Excel or Google Sheets to 'interview' the data to find trends, stories and data points. Instead of spreadsheet formulas, interview the data with prompts.
themes: data analysis; ChatGPT Data Analyst; prompting; spreadsheets
relevance_rationale: LLM-based spreadsheet interpretation is valid but tools have improved dramatically, changing what errors to expect
notes: LLM-based data analysis as alternative to spreadsheet formulas

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Learning point: It got the number right for Texas, but some read the first spreadsheet tab for the cell and others read the second spreadsheet for the cell. Will need to designate tab on future prompts for multi-tab sheets.
themes: ChatGPT Data Analyst; spreadsheet tabs; prompt specificity; verification
relevance_rationale: LLMs misreading spreadsheet structure is a persistent failure mode that requires human verification
notes: LLMs can read wrong tab; specify tab explicitly in prompts

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Be careful here as the data could be misread. I always check tabs in the sheet for a dictionary or go to the source/creator for clarity. Still, this could be a helpful guide/check.
themes: data dictionary; verification; source documentation; LLM
relevance_rationale: Checking tabs and verifying AI data reads against source spreadsheets is a permanent verification practice

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Use it as a tool to get to information, like footnotes in Wikipedia. Use it to interpret sample data (not full dataset itself). Use it to create a data dictionary. Search large PDFs for information. Writing code but check for bugs. Don't use it for: Final data analysis work. Personal data. Replacing work of team members. Processing entire datasets (smaller samples). Writing stories or public content. Check with bosses, policies, attorney before using it in a new way.
themes: newsroom AI policy; acceptable use; do/don't; governance
relevance_rationale: Using AI as a research starting point is sound but capabilities for source-finding have expanded significantly
notes: Newsroom do/don't list for LLM use: sample data only, code review, no final analysis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: IMPORTHTML formula for Google Sheets: =IMPORTHTML("URL","table", 0). One problem: If you go to type on the screen after you scrape, your data disappears because the spreadsheet is linked to the web page. To remedy, highlight all data, copy it, open new tab, and Paste Special > Paste values only.
themes: IMPORTHTML; Google Sheets; web scraping; data capture
relevance_rationale: IMPORTHTML is still useful but LLM-based scraping and dedicated tools have largely superseded it for complex cases
notes: Paste-values-only step critical to preserve scraped data

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You always, always, always work off a COPY of your dataset in case you mess something up.

notes: Fundamental data practice: never edit originals

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

Types of stories produced when and where. Used SpaCy's model when and where. Found TV more likely to focus on crime and newspapers sports and features.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: If you intend to push your responses into a CSV or database or require certain fields, you should really be using structured outputs with Pydantic. It's a way of demanding a certain output which started with OpenAI and has moved to a lot of the other providers.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The numerical result changes each time. It might be close to the same each time, but is that good enough? And is it even accurate? You might want to use this for qualitative work and maybe not for quantitative.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: We want to know who's in the image, but don't want to send it off to an AI tool in the cloud -- we're cheap\! We're private\! We're independent\! Instead, we're lucky that there are entire tools that can categorize images for us locally. This model is a zero-shot classifier, which means we don't need to teach it what we're looking for, it already knows what many things in the world are.
themes: CLIP; zero-shot classification; image analysis; local models; privacy
relevance_rationale: Privacy-conscious local image analysis to avoid sending sensitive images to cloud AI remains a valid concern
notes: Local zero-shot image classification (CLIP) for privacy-conscious newsrooms

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: It's good to test our model first, because it might actually not understand what you want it to identify. While most models are good at things like cats and dogs and boats, our specific use case might be outside of its knowledge.
themes: model testing; domain specificity; image classification
relevance_rationale: Testing model capabilities on known examples before deploying at scale is a permanent best practice
notes: Always test model on your specific use case before scaling up

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: We use PySceneDetect to split the video into scenes, then use a zero-shot image classifier (CLIP) on the middle frame of each scene. We set a 98% confidence threshold -- if the model isn't 98% sure, it labels the image as 'unknown'. This combines scene detection with per-scene classification to produce structured, reproducible data about video content.
themes: PySceneDetect; CLIP; video analysis; confidence threshold; pipeline
relevance_rationale: Scene detection plus zero-shot classification pipeline is valid but newer multimodal models can process video directly

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: These prompts won't produce perfect results every time. But use what you can, just as you wouldn't use everything from a Google search or other research, just use what you think works.

relevance_rationale: Using imperfect AI results as starting points and verifying manually is a durable workflow principle

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Role play with your prompts: Give GPT, Gemini or Claude a role to play as it executes the prompt. It helps tailor the content to that person's or audience's needs.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

relevance_rationale: Whisper model selection advice needs updating for v3, distil-whisper, and competing open-source speech models

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: ALWAYS TRY TO GET IT WITHOUT FOIA FIRST
themes: FOIA; records requests; strategy
relevance_rationale: Trying to obtain records without FOIA first is a timeless efficiency and access principle
notes: Core FOIA principle from Tisha Thompson, ESPN

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: THINK ABOUT HOW MANY RECORDS YOU MAY RECEIVE. 'SNOW' and 'ICE' = roughly 4,000 emails from one agency in a 24 HR period. Keyword search for 'SEAT' = 'Seatgeek', 'Superseats USA' & 'Supreme Seats'. But 'SEAT' is also part of the word 'Seattle'. In Washington state, there 6,000+ businesses containing the word 'Seattle'.
themes: FOIA; keyword search; precision; records requests
relevance_rationale: Estimating FOIA record volume before requesting is a fundamental records management practice
notes: Demonstrates how imprecise keyword searches in FOIA requests can produce overwhelming or irrelevant results

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: YOU NEED TO RESEARCH THE LAW BEFORE YOU SEND\! WHAT I DO EVERY SINGLE TIME BEFORE I SEND A REQUEST: I google 'Reporters Committee Open Government' for state requests and 'Reporters Committee FOIA' for federal requests.
themes: FOIA; legal research; RCFP; records requests
relevance_rationale: Researching applicable law before filing FOIA requests is a permanent legal literacy requirement
notes: Tisha Thompson's routine for legal preparation before filing records requests

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The hardest lesson I ever learned when the ambassadors denied my appeal for current records... I WILL NEVER AGAIN WRITE 'From January 1, 2003 until the present day.' I NOW WRITE 'From January 1, 2003 until this request is processed.'
themes: FOIA; date ranges; records requests; lesson learned
relevance_rationale: Persistence through FOIA denials and appeals is a human skill no AI tool replaces
notes: Hard-won lesson: use 'until processed' not 'until present day' to avoid records going stale during long fights

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Request Broad Data to Narrow In. Ask for metadata or summary-level data that doesn't require redaction. Provide technical instructions -- agencies often claim they 'can't extract' data when they can. Use legal precedent to assert your right to electronic records. Example: Instead of requesting full emails (which the agency said would take years to redact), I asked for email metadata. I provided case law for why they would need to give these records, AND I even gave them the PowerShell commands for extracting the metadata.
themes: FOIA; metadata; data extraction; technical assistance; records strategy
relevance_rationale: Requesting broad metadata to narrow scope is a strategic FOIA technique unaffected by LLMs
notes: Advanced FOIA strategy: request metadata instead of full records, provide technical instructions to agency

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Request System Manuals to Reveal Hidden Data. Agencies may claim they 'don't have' data simply because they don't know where it's stored. Request system documentation to uncover what data fields exist and how they're recorded. Example: 'I am requesting any contracts, instruction manuals, data dictionaries, glossaries of terms, record layouts or handbooks related to the Sheriff's Office's crime incident database or case management system.' Why this works: Exposes what data is actually collected. Helps refine follow-up requests. Prevents agencies from denying data exists.
themes: FOIA; data dictionaries; system manuals; hidden data; records strategy
relevance_rationale: Requesting system manuals to reveal hidden data structures is a document strategy no AI replaces
notes: Request system documentation before data itself to discover what fields exist

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Don't let records delays stall your reporting or writing. Let your editor help you map out what story you can do with or without the records. Share your system for tracking public records (a spreadsheet or some other database) with your editor with key information.
themes: FOIA; editors; story planning; project management
relevance_rationale: Not letting records delays stall reporting is an editorial management principle unaffected by technology
notes: Editor relationship management around FOIA delays

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: B.E.S.T. -- How Your Editor Can Back You Up. B - Boss Voice - Editors can call FOIA officers and demand answers. E - Escalation - They can push agencies, PIOs, or officials when you're being ignored. S - Strategic Planning - They can help figure out workarounds if records are denied. T - Taking Legal Action - They can help persuade your org to sue when necessary. If you're stuck, ask: 'Can my editor step in and push for this?'
themes: FOIA; editors; escalation; legal action; newsroom strategy
relevance_rationale: Editor advocacy for FOIA compliance is an organizational strategy independent of AI tools
notes: Framework for leveraging editor authority in FOIA disputes

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Track your requests with notiFOIA. It's a Google sheet that tracks your records requests and sends you an email reminder when you haven't heard from an agency. It can be a repository for your records so you can find them quickly. It's great if you file multiple records requests per week, are worried a request will get lost in the shuffle, want notifications without using a third-party service.
themes: FOIA; tracking; notiFOIA; Google Sheets; project management
relevance_rationale: FOIA tracking via Google Sheets is useful but AI-powered tracking and reminder tools now exist as alternatives
notes: Kate Martin's notiFOIA tool for tracking records requests via Google Sheets

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: A git scraper is a GitHub repository configured to use GitHub Actions to scrape websites or other online resources and record changes to them over time. Enter the URL of the website - or direct URL to a JSON file - in the description field when you create the repository. It will be configured to track changes to that page once per day, or any time you manually activate the scraper.
themes: git scraping; GitHub Actions; automation; data collection; Simon Willison
relevance_rationale: Git scraping via GitHub Actions for monitoring website changes is a durable infrastructure pattern
notes: Simon Willison's git-scraper-template for automated change tracking

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Websites are complicated beasts these days. Scraping just through parsing HTML often doesn't work - we need to load up the entire website in a real browser first. Some websites are resistant to scraping techniques. We're journalists, we have legitimate reasons to need that data\! Using JavaScript can solve both of these problems. We can extract any data that's visible on the page, and we can bypass all known forms of anti-scraping technology if we start by doing it in our own browsers.
themes: scraping; JavaScript; browser automation; anti-scraping
relevance_rationale: JavaScript-based in-browser scraping for complex dynamic sites remains a valid technique
notes: Rationale for in-browser JS scraping over traditional HTML parsing

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Web pages can get large with a lot of tokens. You can estimate the number of tokens before attempting a prompt using ttok. It's good practice to try different models and figure out the cheapest one that works for your particular problem. For this particular website the shot-scraper technique shown earlier is a better bet - it's much more reliable than LLMs.
themes: LLM scraping; cost management; token estimation; model selection; reliability
relevance_rationale: Token estimation for web page LLM processing is a practical cost and feasibility concern that persists
notes: Willison advises estimating token costs, testing cheapest models, and preferring deterministic scraping when possible

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Where LLM scraping gets really interesting is on harder content, such as PDFs. We'll feed the whole PDF file to Gemini 2.0 Pro with a schema. OpenAI models can't yet handle PDFs but they work very well with images. You can use the filepath directly rather than a URL, if you have the file saved locally.
themes: LLM extraction; PDF processing; Gemini; structured data; schema
relevance_rationale: LLM-based extraction from PDFs and complex documents is a growing and increasingly validated technique
notes: Different LLM providers have different capabilities: Gemini handles PDFs natively, OpenAI needs images

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: This is the ultimate hack for scraping data from a website that really, really doesn't want to be scraped. The Gemini models can accept video as input. The trick is simple: record a video of your screen, browse through a website clicking on different links and pausing for a few seconds each time, then get Gemini to turn that video into JSON data.
chapter: 4. Video scraping using Google AI Studio
themes: video scraping; Gemini; screen recording; anti-scraping bypass; structured extraction
relevance_rationale: Video scraping via Google AI Studio is tool-specific and multimodal video processing is rapidly evolving
notes: Novel technique: record screen while browsing, feed video to Gemini for data extraction

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: What can you get -- and get quick? Think about what you might need in a moment's notice: City salaries, including OT and 'other pay'. Vehicle crash data. Assessor data. Be a resource. You'll quickly be the go-to for all things data (that may and may not exist). Hold mini-sessions with staff to spread the knowledge (and so it's not all on you\!). One Sheets on topics that are guaranteed to come up: Crime, Wildfires, Extreme Heat.
themes: small teams; data readiness; beat reporting; knowledge sharing; TV journalism
relevance_rationale: Planning quick-turnaround data access for breaking news is a newsroom preparedness principle unaffected by AI
notes: Cody Lillich advice for solo data journalists in TV: stockpile common datasets, train colleagues

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Problem: CBP data portal only goes back to FY2022. Solution: Scrub monthly pdfs to get archival data to give a more accurate timeline along our sectors. Provides our producers with quick data and perspective that is updated once a month.
themes: PDF scraping; archival data; immigration; workarounds; data gaps
relevance_rationale: Scrubbing PDFs when portals lack historical data is still needed but LLMs now make PDF extraction much faster
notes: Workaround for incomplete government data portals by scraping archived PDFs

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Check your work. Always ask sources for datasets! Befriend the data stewards in your city. Design consistency. Communicate often, show drafts -- Save yourself from unnecessary revisions. Keep the data team (no matter how small) organized.
themes: verification; source relationships; data stewards; collaboration; small teams
relevance_rationale: Asking sources for datasets and befriending data stewards is a human relationship practice no AI replaces
notes: Junyao Yang (Mission Local) lessons for small data teams

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: When you get data: Make a copy. Create two folders: Original and working. You want to keep your original data pristine in case you mess up. Name files and versions in a reasonable way: Schools.final = not good. Schools.1.0 = better. Schools.2013-02-03 = best. Find a system that works for YOU\!
themes: data management; file naming; version control; data integrity
relevance_rationale: Original vs working copy data management is a fundamental practice regardless of what tools process the data
notes: Kate Martin's file management system for data projects

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Think of data as a source. You wouldn't run a one-source story on most things. Don't depend on data for the single source either. Data can: answer questions, raise questions, mislead you, and point you in the wrong direction. You can: misinterpret data, take it out of context, write an incorrect formula, find hidden gems, fact-check source statements, bring hidden issues to light.
themes: data as source; verification; skepticism; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Treating data as a source requiring corroboration is a core journalism principle unaffected by AI
notes: Kate Martin: data is a source that needs corroboration like any other

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Time management in small newsrooms. There is never enough time. Keep two running lists of story ideas: One list of stories you can do today to satisfy your boss. One list of project candidates that you can chip away at. Have five minutes? File records requests, look for documents and experts, write sections of the story. Write a story and don't turn it in. Work on your project story. When your boss asks for your daily story, then turn it in.
themes: time management; small newsrooms; project planning; workflow
relevance_rationale: Time management with running lists is valid but AI tools can now help prioritize and track tasks more efficiently
notes: Kate Martin's time management strategy for balancing daily and project work

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Check yourself before you wreck yourself. That story you thought was awesome, might not be. WARNING SIGN: If you are really excited about a story, double check your facts and formulas. You might have made a BIG error. Report it out a little before you tell your boss. When you know the story is solid, send your boss a memo with a few paragraphs. Underpromise and overdeliver. Have a minimum viable product if the story falls through. This is all about managing expectations.
themes: verification; confirmation bias; editorial process; minimum viable story
relevance_rationale: Self-checking compelling stories for errors before publication is a permanent editorial discipline
notes: Excitement about findings as a warning sign for errors; minimum viable product concept for stories

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Two guiding principles: Enable as much reuse as possible, within reason. Remember later WTF I've done. Python, in JupyterLab notebooks. DVC for backing up large files outside of GitHub. (But use whatever works in your specific context, seriously\!) Lots of GIS-related work. QGIS for experimenting. GeoPandas for posterity. Illustrator for publication.
themes: reproducibility; documentation; tool chain; GIS; workflow
relevance_rationale: Enabling code and workflow reuse while remaining audience-first is a durable engineering principle
notes: Justin Myers (Chicago Sun-Times): reusability and documentation as core workflow principles

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Know how to share intermediate results with people. Even simple annotated screenshots help a ton. How do you provide your colleagues with: Clarity? Empowerment? Inspiration? Communicate, communicate, communicate.
themes: communication; collaboration; intermediate results; newsroom workflow
relevance_rationale: Sharing intermediate results via annotated screenshots builds collaboration regardless of the analysis tool
notes: Justin Myers on sharing work-in-progress with non-data colleagues

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Pinpoint features: Create a searchable repository of PDFs. Automatically parses the documents and attempts to organize content. Extract structured data. Uses AI and machine learning. Works best with a collection of documents that follow a similar format. Uses an annotated template to 'learn'. Golden Document: One 'master' document to act as a template for the rest of the collection. How to choose your golden doc? Ideally the golden doc will be one with as many fields filled in order to let Pinpoint know what it should expect.
themes: Google Pinpoint; PDF extraction; template annotation; structured data; AI
relevance_rationale: Pinpoint features described here need updating as Google has added capabilities and competitors have emerged
notes: Rosmery Izaguirre (POLITICO) on using Pinpoint's golden document approach for standardized PDFs

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: How can we check and clean the results Pinpoint provided? Spot check. Programmatically count a repeating field and make sure it lines up. Put it through a different type of parser using OpenAI or other programmatical methods.
themes: verification; extraction validation; spot checking; cross-checking
relevance_rationale: Spot-checking and programmatic validation of AI document extraction is a permanent quality assurance practice
notes: Multiple verification methods for AI extraction results

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Do initial validation on a random sample. We ran our pipeline on ~50 precincts with different PDF formats and it scored nearly 100% vs. human review. Find a 'known' value you can check against (subtotals work great for this). We aggregated precincts to the county-level and compared to AP's county report. Programmatically flag values that are far off from what you'd expect. Check min, max, changes from previous years of data if possible.
themes: validation; sampling; known values; cross-reference; election data; outlier detection
relevance_rationale: Initial random-sample validation before full-scale AI extraction is a timeless methodological safeguard
notes: Sean McMinn (POLITICO): multi-layer validation strategy for AI-extracted election data

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Cross-check against multiple extraction methods -- Run the same document through pipeline more than once to compare results. Can use another AI pipeline to see how well the first one did.
themes: cross-validation; redundant extraction; AI verification; pipeline reliability
relevance_rationale: Cross-checking across multiple extraction methods to catch errors is a durable verification strategy
notes: Using multiple AI pipelines as cross-checks on each other

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: but you (really, truly) cannot trust LLMs\! LLMs are next-word prediction machines. just use pdfplumber\!\!\! ...but if you won't listen to me... Document Q&A models: Only uses content on the page\! Can be wrong, but won't hallucinate. Has a confidence score. Very speedy\!\!\! Use docTR, not tesseract. But Azure Form Recognizer and AWS Textract still beat it.
themes: LLM limitations; hallucination; pdfplumber; OCR; document QA; trust
relevance_rationale: The warning that LLMs are next-word predictors and cannot be trusted for factual extraction is a permanent caveat
notes: Jonathan Soma: strong preference for deterministic tools (pdfplumber) over LLMs; document QA models as safer middle ground

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Sometimes the LLM will get weird and return something you don't want. You can improve this by adding validation. The first one, which you should consider making routine, is to lower the 'temperature' of the model, which is a way to dial down its creativity and make it more consistent. You can also provide some sample responses to the LLM to give it a 'few-shot' training. You can also force the function to retry when there's an error. One way to manage that is to allow an 'other' category.
themes: LLM classification; validation; temperature; few-shot learning; retry logic; error handling
relevance_rationale: Handling weird LLM responses via structured output validation is sound but native JSON mode and tool use have improved
notes: Ben Welsh/Derek Willis: progressive strategies for controlling LLM output reliability

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Requesting answers one by one can take a long time. And it can end up costing you money. The solution is to submit your requests in batches and then get the answers back from the LLM as JSON you can parse.
themes: LLM classification; batching; cost management; efficiency
relevance_rationale: Batch prompting for efficiency is still valid but API batching, async calls, and provider batch endpoints have evolved
notes: Batch processing strategy for LLM classification at scale

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: You should take a random sample and code them with the correct answers. We use scikit-learn to evaluate how well the LLM does on the supervised sample. Check the ones that are wrong. Tweak your prompt to match.
themes: evaluation; ground truth; scikit-learn; classification report; prompt iteration
relevance_rationale: Random sampling and manual coding to create ground truth for AI evaluation is a timeless validation method
notes: Rigorous evaluation workflow: hand-code sample, split train/test, measure precision/recall, iterate

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

notes: Key finding: LLM classifiers outperform sklearn SVM with less code when improved with few-shot examples

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Matching up two sets of names that aren't quite the same, but refer to the same thing. Typically for investigative reporting that's names of people or names of companies. Of course it could also be names of places or other things too.
themes: fuzzy matching; entity resolution; record linkage; investigative reporting
relevance_rationale: Fuzzy matching concept endures but LLM-based entity resolution now offers a powerful alternative approach
notes: Max Harlow: definition and core use case for fuzzy matching in journalism

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: So there's a recipe here. A repeatable pattern we can use in investigations. There was a leak from a Jersey-based bank of 53,000 names of individuals who held accounts there. We wanted to find out which ones were notable public figures. To do this I scraped lists of 'interesting' people -- the Forbes rich list, data published on people who had given donations to UK political parties, and other sources.
themes: fuzzy matching; investigative pattern; leaked data; entity matching; public interest
relevance_rationale: The repeatable fuzzy matching recipe is valid but should now include LLM-based matching as an additional technique
notes: Repeatable investigative pattern: match leaked/obtained names against curated lists of notable people

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: False positives and false negatives. The first type are the ones we see in our data -- false positives, where the computer thinks two things are the same when they actually aren't. The other type are the records that we don't see in our data -- false negatives. The amount of false positives can also be called precision, the amount of false negatives is recall. On projects like those I've described, we probably want to bias towards having more false positives -- as we're going to manually go through the results anyway. And we can't go through results that aren't there.
themes: precision; recall; false positives; false negatives; fuzzy matching; tradeoffs
relevance_rationale: Understanding false positives vs false negatives in record matching is a permanent analytical framework
notes: For investigative matching, bias toward recall (more false positives) since manual review follows

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Now let's look at how we can actually improve matches like this -- by using multiple fields. This is the most powerful technique we're going to see today. That's because it can make your matches both more accurate and faster. If we have two people with the same name but we know one lives in Australia and the other lives in the UK, they shouldn't be a match. Using this data can also, perhaps counterintuitively, make matches faster too\!
themes: blocking; fuzzy matching; multi-field matching; performance optimization
relevance_rationale: Multi-field matching to improve accuracy is sound but LLMs can now consider multiple fields holistically
notes: Blocking: using additional fields (e.g. country) to both improve accuracy and reduce computation

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Know your data. A lot depends on the nature of your specific data, what kind of issues it has and how it was produced can guide your approach. For example, the donations data I showed you at the start -- this used to include lots of people with 'na' in their name -- this is how names were entered for people with no middle name, by putting 'not applicable' in that field. Knowing these kinds of details let you tackle them. That all said fuzzy matching itself can be a bit of a fuzzy science, and it's often worthwhile to try a few different approaches and see what works best.
themes: data quality; domain knowledge; fuzzy matching; data provenance; iteration
relevance_rationale: Understanding your specific data characteristics before matching is a permanent analytical requirement
notes: Understanding data provenance and quirks is essential before fuzzy matching

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Same name, same person. Keep in the back of your mind when matching up records like this that two people or two companies with the same name aren't necessarily the same\! Even if it's an exact match.
themes: entity resolution; false positives; verification; matching caveats
relevance_rationale: Same-name-different-person ambiguity in record matching is a domain knowledge issue no AI fully resolves
notes: Critical caveat: exact name match does not guarantee same entity

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: AI is getting better every day, and it's worth considering. But you don't know what it's really doing. It is also non-deterministic, and it can be slow and expensive. So it might be worth trying, and even more so in the future, but I would still recommend you start with the approaches I've discussed here.
themes: AI limitations; determinism; fuzzy matching; transparency; cost
relevance_rationale: AI matching is acknowledged as improving but the caveat about unknown internals needs updating for newer transparent models
notes: Max Harlow: prefer traditional fuzzy matching over AI for transparency and determinism

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Given the limitations of today's personal computers, it becomes difficult to work with datasets larger than a few gigabytes. Campaign contributions, voter registration files, consumer complaints, climate estimates, social media extracts and many other newsworthy sources often exceed tens of millions of records and surpass the capabilities of a typical laptop. Data journalists can turn to Athena to interrogate these datasets without having to invest in expensive hardware or learn a new programming language. SQL is a standard tool in the data journalist's toolbox, and Athena is built to work with it.
themes: Amazon Athena; big data; SQL; cloud computing; scalability
relevance_rationale: Hardware limitations for large datasets still exist but cloud compute and optimized libraries have expanded capabilities
notes: Amazon Athena as solution for datasets too large for local machines

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Telling it to extract data using the JSON schema. Handling edge cases. Troubleshooting when it didn't correctly parse what the precinct name should be. Troubleshooting when it was picking up on 'election day' or 'mail-in' votes. We want a prompt and schema that will handle any of those.
themes: JSON schema; prompt engineering; edge cases; election data; troubleshooting
relevance_rationale: JSON schema extraction with edge case handling is a durable structured data extraction pattern
notes: Iterative prompt+schema refinement to handle diverse document formats

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Think of Actions as renting a blank computer from GitHub. To use it, you will need to install the latest version of whatever language you are using, as well as any corresponding package managers and libraries. Because these Actions are used so often, GitHub has a marketplace where you can find pre-packaged steps for common tasks.
themes: GitHub Actions; automation; cloud computing; CI/CD
relevance_rationale: GitHub Actions as renting a blank computer is a useful conceptual model for understanding CI/CD infrastructure
notes: Mental model for GitHub Actions: renting a blank cloud computer

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: GitHub Actions provides two error-handling settings that will be helpful. One is called fail-fast. This flag controls whether the entire matrix job should fail if one job in the matrix fails. In our case, we want to mark this flag as false; even if one state's scraper fails, we still want the job to be completed. The second error-handling setting is called continue-on-error. When true, this allows the Action to continue running subsequent steps even if an earlier step fails.
themes: GitHub Actions; error handling; matrix strategy; resilience; automation
relevance_rationale: Error handling settings for parallel GitHub Actions jobs are platform-specific but the patterns are durable
notes: Error handling strategies for parallel scraping jobs in GitHub Actions

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: With multiple jobs running in parallel, we can no longer guarantee their order of completion. We'll solve that by splitting the action into two steps. First, we'll run all scrapers in parallel and save their outputs in temporary storage known as artifacts. Then, once every job finishes, we'll collect those Artifacts and make a single commit. This approach ensures that all parallel jobs contribute their changes without overwriting each other.
themes: GitHub Actions; artifacts; parallel processing; data pipeline; fan-in pattern
relevance_rationale: Race conditions and ordering in parallel workflows are fundamental distributed computing concerns
notes: Fan-in pattern: parallel scrape jobs save artifacts, then single commit job collects all

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: GitHub may automatically disable workflows if there's a period of inactivity. To get around this you can have your workflow commit an updated text file every time your Action runs.
themes: GitHub Actions; automation; workflow maintenance; inactivity
relevance_rationale: GitHub auto-disabling inactive workflows is a platform behavior that data pipeline maintainers must know
notes: Workaround for GitHub auto-disabling inactive workflows: commit a datestamp file

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: VLOOKUP is a function that allows you to 'join' data from one sheet to another. You can transfer one column of information over to another sheet, matching based on common values. IMPORTANT: the left-most column in this range needs to be the one you are matching/joining on. If you Google how to do a VLOOKUP, it won't include this first step I'm going to show you. But I can guarantee you, my approach saves a lot of headache and simplifies the ultimate formula. What we're going to do is assign a name to the 'range.'
themes: VLOOKUP; Google Sheets; data joining; named ranges; spreadsheets
relevance_rationale: VLOOKUP remains useful but LLMs can now write and explain these formulas, and modern tools offer alternatives
notes: MaryJo Webster: always use named ranges with VLOOKUP to simplify formulas

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Do a quick pivot table on the 'race' column and notice that we've got quite a few inconsistencies. When I was prepping this data, I took this column out of the pivot table and pasted it into the sheet that is called 'race_lookup.' Notice that it's the column called 'original.' And then I added a new column where I typed the value I wanted to replace each one with. We'll use a VLOOKUP to match those original values back in the dataset and create a new column where we'll drop the new values. This is a very easy data cleanup option when you have inconsistencies but it isn't hundreds of them.
themes: data cleaning; VLOOKUP; lookup tables; standardization; pivot tables
relevance_rationale: Using pivot tables to audit data quality before analysis is a permanent data cleaning practice
notes: Using pivot table to identify inconsistencies, then VLOOKUP with a lookup table for standardization

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Data journalists are very used to negotiating for records and turning a pile of record soup into structured data.
themes: data journalism identity; records negotiation; structuring data
relevance_rationale: Data journalists negotiating for records is enduring but LLMs can now help parse and structure the resulting document piles
notes: Michael Corey: data journalism as the craft of turning unstructured records into structured data

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Mapping Process: Digitized images of property records, usually from microfilm. OCR using Amazon Textract. Search for terms associated with racial covenants. Show each 'hit' to 5 volunteers on the Zooniverse crowdsourcing platform. They transcribe the text of the covenant, buyer, seller, date, and the property identifying information. Compare each of their responses to determine how confident we are in the answers. Finally we want to put those on a map.
themes: crowdsourcing; OCR; Zooniverse; historical records; digitization; pipeline
relevance_rationale: Manual property record digitization process could now be augmented with OCR and LLM-based extraction
notes: Full pipeline: microfilm -> OCR -> keyword search -> 5x crowdsource transcription -> confidence check -> mapping

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Why crowdsourcing? It's more accurate. We don't have 300 graduate assistants. We do have 11,000+ volunteers. Crowdsourcing is not just faster and more scalable than hiring a couple grad students, it's also more accurate. But the key thing is you have to be willing to do the community engagement to find crowdsourcing volunteers.
themes: crowdsourcing; accuracy; community engagement; volunteers; scalability
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing for accuracy is valid but AI-assisted transcription now competes with crowd accuracy for many tasks
notes: Crowdsourcing as both more scalable AND more accurate than small expert teams

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Property identification is hard (Pls slow your LLM roll). What is the lot, block, and addition? Many possible answers. Only one is correct, requires context and additional research. Each individual property really matters. Statistically good is not good enough. There are machine-learning based approaches that can surely get pretty good, statistically, at flagging and even transcribing racial covenants. But statistically good is not good enough.
themes: accuracy; AI limitations; individual records; statistical vs exact; crowdsourcing
relevance_rationale: Property identification complexity and the warning against naive LLM use for geocoding historical records persists
notes: When individual records matter (property mapping), statistical accuracy from ML is insufficient; human review required

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: How we map matters. How will this map be received? And what does that mean for how we map? We're talking about structural racism here. There are so many people who are motivated to not hear this information. So if we color in a lot on this map, we better be sure there was really a covenant there. Because what happens after we make a map, is that people want to discharge or disavow that covenant on their property. If someone goes through the effort of filing that paperwork, and then they find out nope, it was a mistake. It doesn't take many of those to kill all your goodwill.
themes: accuracy; mapping; consequences; trust; structural racism; presentation ethics
relevance_rationale: How maps are received and potential for harm is an ethical design consideration independent of production tools
notes: High-stakes accuracy: errors in mapping racial covenants undermine real-world remedial action and credibility

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Working with public historians and community. From a historian's perspective: You don't need to prove racism exists. Deep community and historian connections give your work credibility. Your level of trust is likely starting at 0. Journalism's traditional practices are what got you there. These should be your collaborators, not your sources. Crediting generously is free. But it's also time to start paying people. What is in it for your collaborators? Start by asking what they would like out of this work. Before you have decided what you will do.
themes: community collaboration; trust; historians; crediting; ethics; co-creation
relevance_rationale: Collaborating with community historians for context and accountability is a human-centered practice AI cannot replace
notes: Collaborative journalism principles: treat community as collaborators not sources, credit generously, ask what they want first

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

notes: Jonathan Soma: escalating tiers of scraping difficulty from tools to passive capture

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: Data journalism doesn't always involve fancy coding -- sometimes it's a lot of counting. Data journalism can be about conveying information in a novel way. A data story can be built around a single trend or observation. It's great for confirming/quantifying what you're hearing from human sources.

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: A common thing that trips you up with nested IF statements is not getting enough parentheses in there. Always count how many opening ones and make sure you have the same number of closing ones. If you get an error message, first thing to check is number of parentheses and whether they are all in the correct place.

relevance_rationale: Nested IF statement parenthesis debugging is a spreadsheet literacy detail that persists regardless of AI
notes: MaryJo Webster: common debugging tip for nested IF statements

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: The process is the product. When we started, we assumed that getting to the finished map was the goal. But our volunteers and other community members have taught us that just mapping covenants is not the real goal. The real goal and the secret to Mapping Prejudice's success is the experience of absorbing new information together, talking about that, and that is what opens people to new possibilities for reparative change.
themes: process vs product; community engagement; crowdsourcing; impact; civic change
relevance_rationale: The process being the product in community data projects reflects values of participation that transcend tools
notes: Key insight: the participatory process of data creation may matter more than the finished product

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis
extract: It's important to make a distinction. Essential - Does this align with our mission? Distinct - Are we uniquely positioned to tell this story? Impact - Could this lead to meaningful change? Can the problems described be rectified? Timing - Is this the right moment for the story? Obstacles - Are there legal, ethical, or reputational risks? What are the biggest hurdles? Result - What's the biggest vs. minimum viable version of the story? When will we know it's time to walk away?
themes: editorial framework; story assessment; mission alignment; impact; minimum viable story
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing essential vs nice-to-have FOIA requests based on editorial mission is a permanent triage principle
notes: EDITOR CHECK framework for evaluating whether to pursue a data story

author: IRE/NICAR, compiled by Sharon Machlis

source_title: Sandra Crucianelli — Periodismo de Base de Datos (blog/tutorials)
author: Sandra Crucianelli
extract: The reporter explains their methods, so that their work withstands critical review; this means that if a reader or another journalist wanted to retrace the same steps using the same documents, they would reach the same conclusion or a different one, should they be able to refute it or find other angles.

themes: methodology; reproducibility; transparency; critical review

source_title: Sandra Crucianelli — Periodismo de Base de Datos (blog/tutorials)
author: Sandra Crucianelli

source_title: Escola de Dados — Courses and Tutorials
author: Escola de Dados
extract: Data scraping is a technique in which a computer program extracts information from an interface designed for human reading. In journalism, this technique has been applied mainly to extract information from web pages and from documents that are not organized in a structured way, such as PDFs.
themes: scraping; data acquisition; PDFs; structured data
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now parse PDFs and generate scrapers directly, changing the technical landscape of data extraction

source_title: Escola de Dados — Courses and Tutorials
author: Escola de Dados
extract: Programming 'bots' that perform automated data collection from digital systems is a skill that any journalist of this century must develop. If before we needed to do good 'social scraping' to conduct our investigations (talking to sources and building lasting interpersonal relationships), today, in addition to that, we need to extend those skills to the digital realm using computing techniques.
themes: scraping; automation; digital skills; journalist-programmer
relevance_rationale: LLMs dramatically lower the barrier to writing scraping bots, so the skill emphasis shifts from coding to directing AI agents

source_title: Escola de Dados — Courses and Tutorials
author: Escola de Dados
extract: Extracting and compiling information scattered around the web into a structured format can make it possible to uncover potential irregularities, correlations, raise important questions, and open paths to producing useful services and tools.
themes: scraping; structured data; investigation; public interest
relevance_rationale: LLMs now excel at structuring scattered unstructured web data, so the principle endures but the technique description needs updating

source_title: Escola de Dados — Primeiros passos no jornalismo de dados
author: Escola de Dados
extract: Numbers alone are not relevant — you need to answer 'so what?': what do these numbers affect, who do they affect, and how?
themes: so-what test; critical thinking; audience; impact
relevance_rationale: The so-what test for numbers is fundamental editorial judgment that no LLM replaces

source_title: Escola de Dados — Primeiros passos no jornalismo de dados
author: Escola de Dados
extract: It is fundamental to read the notes on data-recording methodologies. The date a piece of data was recorded may differ from the date the recorded event actually occurred.

relevance_rationale: Reading metadata and data-recording methodology notes remains essential for data quality verification

source_title: Abraji Help Desk — Manual de Jornalismo de Dados
author: Abraji
extract: Start from scratch: investigate family, former spouses, whereabouts of children. Court records search: note names of parties and lawyers. Personalized biography: family, financial connections, 'who are they really?'
themes: investigative method; background research; public records; sources
relevance_rationale: Systematic investigative background research on people through public records is a timeless reporting methodology

source_title: Abraji Help Desk — Manual de Jornalismo de Dados
author: Abraji
extract: Website owners use the same Google AdSense (Pub-) and Analytics (UA-) IDs across multiple sites. Exploits the 'personal greed' of actors who sacrifice anonymity to maintain advertising revenue, revealing connections between apparently unrelated sites.
themes: digital forensics; network analysis; open-source intelligence
relevance_rationale: The OSINT principle of tracking shared ad/analytics IDs endures but tools and techniques have evolved beyond manual UA/Pub lookups

source_title: Abraji Help Desk — Manual de Jornalismo de Dados
author: Abraji
extract: Mass outreach: 30–40 messages; replies may come months later.
themes: source development; persistence; investigative method
relevance_rationale: Persistent mass outreach and patience with sources is a human relationship skill unaffected by automation

source_title: A dossier of data journalism teaching strategies
author: DataJournalism.com
extract: Thinking about data 'from the beginning reinforces for students that it is a core skill, just like interviewing or checking facts'.
chapter: Australia — Dr Kayt Davies, Edith Cowan University
