relevance_rationale: Treating data as a core journalism skill alongside interviewing and fact-checking is a durable pedagogical principle

source_title: A dossier of data journalism teaching strategies
author: DataJournalism.com
extract: Remember that the data is never the story. Many of the most powerful stories come from weaving together findings with stories of individuals and communities.
chapter: United States — Jeff Kelly Lowenstein, Grand Valley State University
themes: storytelling; human element; narrative
relevance_rationale: The principle that data alone is never the story and must be woven with human narratives is timeless editorial wisdom

source_title: A dossier of data journalism teaching strategies
author: DataJournalism.com
extract: IRE Contest entries are your friend — Reading these teaches students how to reverse engineer an investigation.
chapter: United States — Jeff Kelly Lowenstein, Grand Valley State University
themes: learning by example; reverse engineering; investigation
relevance_rationale: Learning to reverse-engineer investigations from exemplary work remains a valuable pedagogical method regardless of tools

source_title: A dossier of data journalism teaching strategies
author: DataJournalism.com
extract: Build incrementally — Start with smaller stories and projects that become the basis for larger, more ambitious ones.
chapter: United States — Jeff Kelly Lowenstein, Grand Valley State University
themes: scaffolding; incremental learning; pedagogy
relevance_rationale: Incremental scaffolding in data journalism education is a sound pedagogical approach independent of technology

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: investigative reporting requires skills and attitudes that most reporters never learn. Yet most reporters can indeed succeed at investigative work, if they acquire the appropriate methods and the desire to use them.
themes: method; skills; investigative identity
relevance_rationale: Core investigative identity and methods-based approach to journalism is independent of any technology era.
notes: From John Ullman's Reporter's Handbook argument

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: the various processes of investigative work – the conception, planning, researching, organization and composition of a story – were thought of as separate tasks which must be completed in a sequence of discrete steps. In 2009, the publication of Story-Based Inquiry demonstrated how they could be fused into a single continuous workstream. The benefits include economies of time – an explicit reply to the notion that investigative work is always slow and costly – as well as increased quality of information and storytelling.
chapter: Teaching economic and social aspects of investigative journalism
themes: story-based inquiry; workflow; efficiency
relevance_rationale: Story-based inquiry workflow concept endures but LLMs enable even faster integration of research/writing steps.
notes: Key claim about integrated vs sequential workflow

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: What the public wants from us is not a catalogue of information, but the meaning of the information that we have catalogued. That meaning is captured in a story. In other words, investigative reporting is not merely about finding information. It is about telling a story based on new and important information.
themes: narrative; storytelling; meaning-making
relevance_rationale: The principle that journalism is about meaning-making through narrative, not just cataloguing facts, is timeless.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: That is the key difference between facts and stories: We forget facts alone, but we remember facts that are embedded in a story
themes: narrative; cognition; storytelling
relevance_rationale: Cognitive principle about stories vs isolated facts is a permanent feature of human cognition.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Conventional journalism rarely involves stories that involve more than a few days to research and write. Investigations can last for weeks or months. They also involve far more information than is generated by research for most news stories. Thus a higher level of organization is required. The organizational work becomes ever more important in direct proportion to the amount of material that the reporter collects.
themes: information management; organization; scale
relevance_rationale: Information management in long investigations endures but LLMs transform how reporters organize and retrieve large volumes of material.
notes: Research: Going wide and deep subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: authentic investigative reporters do not accept something as true simply because someone, and in particular an official, gave them a report. Instead, they verify the information through other sources, and challenge information that cannot be verified. Nor do they consider someone more important because he has an official title. The importance of a source to an investigative reporter is based on the validity of the information that the source can provide.
themes: verification; source credibility; independence
relevance_rationale: Independent verification and challenging official claims is a permanent investigative principle regardless of tools.
notes: Source relations: from dependence to challenge subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The investigator's job is to arrive at a judgment – that something should never have happened, that it must never happen again, or conversely, that something worthwhile has been unjustly terminated.
themes: judgment; accountability; outcomes
relevance_rationale: The investigative judgment function — arriving at evidence-based conclusions about wrongdoing — is inherently human.
notes: Outcomes: Proving the need for change subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The most important question here is whether or not we exclude facts that conflict with our goals. An investigator takes all the facts that can be found into account. Though some facts are more meaningful than others, they cannot be eliminated simply because we don't like them.
themes: objectivity; bias; completeness
relevance_rationale: Intellectual honesty about not excluding inconvenient facts is a timeless epistemic principle.
notes: On practical objectivity vs ideological objectivity

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: the reporter earns the right and the confidence to make a judgment about the meaning of the facts, through an investigation.
themes: judgment; objectivity; evidence-based reasoning
relevance_rationale: Earning the right to judgment through rigorous investigation is a fundamental journalistic standard.
notes: Neutral toward facts, but earns right to judge their meaning

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The reporters collected internal documents of the Nixon campaign, including an organisational phone directory. The phone directory contained names and titles, and was thus a physical map of the organisation.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: documents; organizational mapping; Watergate
relevance_rationale: Using organizational documents to map power structures remains essential investigative technique.
notes: Woodward and Bernstein technique

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The reporters targeted people at lower managerial levels. Executives would lie to them or track them. Janitors would not see as much as hoped and have heard less. The people in the middle saw and heard a great deal and could do little to affect it.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: source selection; organizational hierarchy; interviewing
relevance_rationale: Targeting mid-level organizational insiders as sources is a timeless human intelligence technique.
notes: Woodward and Bernstein middle-management targeting

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: To keep track of these documents and of their interviews, they developed an elaborate filing system, modeled on the cross-referencing system used by the FBI. In this system, whenever a source mentioned events involving another source, copies of both their interviews were placed in each other's files.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: information management; cross-referencing; filing system
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing filing systems remain essential but LLMs and knowledge graphs can now automate the cross-linking.
notes: Woodward/Bernstein FBI-modeled filing technique

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: In their meetings with sources, they paid attention not only to information, but to emotion. In particular, they noticed that many of their sources were afraid. This, beyond the facts they initially collected, alerted them to the importance of the story.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: emotional intelligence; source reading; story significance
relevance_rationale: Reading emotional cues from sources to gauge story significance is an irreplaceable human skill.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Since Watergate, professional practice has been to use information from anonymous sources only when four of them agree on the same facts.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: anonymous sources; corroboration; verification threshold
relevance_rationale: Multi-source corroboration threshold for anonymous sources is a permanent verification standard.
notes: Four-source agreement rule post-Watergate

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: No one source is key to an investigation, and it is a fatal mistake to think that a 'deep throat' will tell you everything you need to know.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: source diversity; dependence avoidance; myth-busting
relevance_rationale: Source diversity principle and avoiding single-source dependency transcends technology eras.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: If a source really knows a secret, he or she has few reasons to share it with you, and the most common is to manipulate you. After all, if the information is secret, and you trust the source, the odds are good that you will publish the information without further verification.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: source motivation; manipulation risk; verification
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward source motivations and manipulation risk is a permanent epistemic safeguard.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: No reporter can prevail alone. There must be other actors, in the media and in society, who are willing to act on the information that the reporter provides.
chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
themes: coalition building; impact; social forces
relevance_rationale: Coalition-building for investigative impact is about social dynamics, not technology.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Davies suggests that investigative reports tend to begin with one of several categories of information. We refer to them as i) 'tips', ii) 'leftovers', or iii) observations and questions.
themes: story origins; tips; leftovers; observation
relevance_rationale: Story origin categories (tips, leftovers, observations) describe permanent patterns in how investigations begin.
notes: Nick Davies taxonomy

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: News media are full of leftovers – little stories that seem to shout, 'There's more to find here\!' but are usually left to rot. If you read your own newspaper you'll see all sorts of leads in the story that the original reporter just didn't have time to pursue. Remember that these leads will often appear as anomalies – facts that just seem strange, or out of place.
themes: leftovers; anomalies; story leads; news monitoring
relevance_rationale: Mining news leftovers for story leads endures but LLMs can now systematically scan and flag anomalies across large news archives.
notes: Walt Bogdanich quoted

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: We will notice something that seems strange, and we will imagine what might explain the mystery. Then we will see how we can verify our idea.
themes: anomaly detection; hypothesis generation; verification cycle
relevance_rationale: The observe-hypothesize-verify cycle is the core investigative logic regardless of tools used.
notes: Core investigative cycle in miniature

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: This is the principle: If something in the environment has suddenly changed, or appears to be out of place, there may be a story behind it.
themes: anomaly detection; observation; pattern recognition
relevance_rationale: Anomaly detection as story trigger is a timeless investigative instinct.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: There are always more stories available for investigation than we can ever finish. So the reporter must assess the time and resources that are necessary to the task, and measure them against the intrinsic value of the story.
themes: story selection; resource assessment; prioritization
relevance_rationale: Resource assessment and story prioritization are permanent editorial judgment tasks.
notes: Deborah Nelson story-evaluation framework follows

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It is worth noting that Allain is not looking to blame people: Her target is the system, not the individuals who can only fail to cope with an impossible situation. This is a classic dilemma of investigative reporting: Do we attack individuals, or seek to change the system that enables their corruption?
themes: systemic analysis; individual vs system; framing
relevance_rationale: Systemic vs individual framing dilemma is a permanent editorial and ethical question.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: She learned how to create and administer surveys, and to interpret the results.
themes: surveys; data collection; quantitative methods
relevance_rationale: Survey design and administration endures as a skill but LLMs can now assist with survey creation and analysis at scale.
notes: Allain investigation skill acquisition

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: She learned how to solicit sensitive information from individuals engaged in unethical or illegal activity. She did so, in essence, by asking officials if victims were telling the truth when they said that they were forced to pay bribes. Instead of accusing them, she asked, 'Why do you need this money?'
themes: interviewing; sensitive information; non-accusatory questioning
relevance_rationale: Non-accusatory questioning to elicit sensitive information is a permanent interpersonal interviewing technique.
notes: Indirect questioning technique from Allain

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Before undertaking an investigation, ask yourself if you want to become expert on the milieu where the story takes place. If students do not care about legal issues, they will never find the energy to investigate the justice system.
themes: domain expertise; motivation; story selection
relevance_rationale: Intrinsic motivation and domain interest as prerequisites for sustained investigation are timeless.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The most basic of those methods is the one that you are now going to teach your students: The use of hypotheses to frame and guide investigative research. We cannot investigate efficiently unless we know what we are investigating, and what we are looking for. Otherwise we will either pile up information until we drown in it, or wander around helplessly with no idea of where to find the information we need. Therefore, we need an idea of what a story might be, before we can investigate whether or not it really occurred that way, or in another way.
themes: hypothesis; story-based inquiry; research design; efficiency
relevance_rationale: Hypothesis-driven investigation as core method to avoid information drowning is more relevant than ever with LLM-era data abundance.
notes: Core method of GIJN since 2003

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: as investigative journalists we create a hypothesis – a story that we have reason to believe might be true - and then set out to verify that story. As we proceed, we will discover either that our initial idea was more or less accurate, or we will uncover evidence that points to another story.
themes: hypothesis; verification; iterative refinement
relevance_rationale: Iterative hypothesis testing and refinement is a permanent investigative logic.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: it is very, very hard to investigate a story by compiling an enormous number of facts, and then trying to make sense of them. The usual result is drowning in a chaotic sea of details. In order to investigate efficiently, we need a provisional idea of what we are investigating.
themes: hypothesis; information overload; research design
relevance_rationale: Warning against inductive-only fact gathering is even more critical when LLMs can generate vast information.
notes: Against inductive-only fact gathering

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: A hypothesis is not mere speculation. We do not invent facts to explain what we cannot see. We create a theory that accounts for what we already know and what we can see. Then we deduce the facts that might explain what we do not know, and seek to verify them.
themes: hypothesis; deduction; evidence-based reasoning
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing hypothesis from speculation via evidence-based deduction is a permanent epistemic standard.
notes: Standards for making hypotheses

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The hypothesis must contain assertions (or terms) that can be verified. No one of any intelligence volunteers information to a reporter simply because the reporter asks for it. However, many sources will confirm or deny information that is already in the reporter's possession. By creating a hypothesis, we see clearly what information we must acquire before we speak with anyone. These facts will make us more powerful when we proceed to interviews.
themes: hypothesis; verifiability; interview preparation; leverage
relevance_rationale: Verifiability as prerequisite for hypothesis terms, and using documents to gain leverage in interviews, is timeless.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: In its initial form, the hypothesis must be written in three sentences or less. A story combines facts into a sequence of events that leads to a meaning. It can, and must, be easily summarised. Otherwise your students do not understand what they are trying to prove.
themes: hypothesis; brevity; clarity; story structure
relevance_rationale: Three-sentence hypothesis constraint for clarity is a permanent pedagogical and investigative discipline.
notes: Three-sentence rule for hypothesis

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Encourage them also to start every answer with a human subject, followed by an active verb. We find this a useful trick to provide concrete answers - and concreteness is what we need, if we will ever be able to prove the story.
themes: hypothesis construction; concreteness; active voice; verifiability
relevance_rationale: Human subject plus active verb for concrete, provable claims is a permanent writing and investigation technique.
notes: Human subject + active verb = provable claim

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Encourage the students to start every question with 'How...' We think it is better to focus students on how things occur, than on why they occur. The 'how' points to concrete, verifiable actions, while the 'why' ends with unverifiable motives.
themes: question framing; how vs why; verifiability; concreteness
relevance_rationale: Preferring how over why questions for verifiability is an enduring investigative framing principle.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: every investigation becomes more difficult as it proceeds; if they cannot do the easy work first, they will never be able to do the hard work.
themes: difficulty progression; easy-first verification; research strategy
relevance_rationale: Easy-first verification sequencing is a permanent research strategy principle.
notes: Easiest verification points first

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Making a hypothesis seems time consuming. But with practice, it can be done in minutes. The time lost when you have no hypothesis is measured in days, weeks and months. The larger point is that news reporters are trained to do everything fast, and that is their default mode. Investigators must be less reactive, and more pro-active; they must take control of the story from the start.
themes: hypothesis; efficiency; proactive vs reactive; time investment
relevance_rationale: Hypothesis as time-saving device and proactive investigation stance is timeless methodology.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: An honest reporter constantly tests his or her hypothesis against the facts that emerge in the investigation.
themes: hypothesis testing; confirmation bias; intellectual honesty
relevance_rationale: Continuously testing hypothesis against emerging facts to guard against confirmation bias is permanent.
notes: Guard against hypothesis blinding

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: After the hypothesis, the timeline is the most important tool used by investigators, and probably the most widely used. It helps us to make sense of complex matters, by revealing causality and consequences of related events. It shows us what is missing from our story—that is, what might have happened before and after events that we have confirmed – and keeps the information we have gathered in a clear, strong order. A timeline also creates a foundation for the simplest and strongest narrative structures. In fact, the timeline enables us to begin creating our narrative while we are still conducting research.
themes: timeline; causality; narrative structure; information gaps
relevance_rationale: Timeline as core analytical tool endures but LLMs can now auto-generate and maintain timelines from document ingestion.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: They can easily identify the separate events by counting the verbs in the hypothesis: every verb is the expression of a different action.
themes: timeline; hypothesis decomposition; events; verbs
relevance_rationale: Decomposing hypothesis into discrete events via verb counting is a permanent analytical technique.
notes: Practical technique for breaking hypothesis into events

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It helps to begin every sentence with a human subject. It is easier to investigate human beings than abstract problems or systems. Moreover, at some point, students will need human sources to verify the events. In short, students should ask not 'What do I want to investigate?' but 'Who do I want to investigate?'
themes: concreteness; human subjects; source identification; framing
relevance_rationale: Framing events around human subjects for investigative concreteness is timeless.
notes: Formulate events concretely subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Make sure that the character in the event is engaged in an activity. Only activity leaves traces that we can follow up to prove that something happened. 'Landlords cheat' is less easy to prove than 'Landlords demand exorbitant rents and large security deposits.' 'Demanding' is a real action (the landlord is talking to the student), while 'cheating' is an abstract summary, and a subjective opinion at that.
themes: concreteness; verifiability; action traces; specificity
relevance_rationale: Insisting on concrete, verifiable actions over abstract characterizations is a permanent investigative standard.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: urge your students to stay clear of negatives and predictions. 'Landlords never return the money to students' states what does not happen. Things that don't happen are hard to prove, so let's change the sentence to state what does happen instead: 'Landlords refuse to return the money to the students.'
themes: framing; positive assertions; verifiability; concreteness
relevance_rationale: Framing assertions positively for verifiability is a timeless writing and investigation discipline.
notes: Avoid negatives and predictions in timeline events

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The hypothesis should be framed to reveal the most important story we can find here, without merely speculating. We need to know what the most important story may be, so that we can decide whether or not it is worth exploring. If the most important story we can hypothesize from the available facts is neither very interesting nor very easy, we abandon it.
themes: maximum story; story evaluation; go/no-go decision
relevance_rationale: Maximum vs minimum story evaluation for go/no-go decisions is a permanent editorial judgment.
notes: Identify maximum and minimum story subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: one of the common, and gravest, errors made by investigative reporters is to make accusations that their evidence cannot fully support, in order to make the story appear more dramatic.
themes: overreach; evidence sufficiency; credibility
relevance_rationale: Warning against overreach beyond evidence is a timeless credibility standard.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Journalists realized that instead of simply recording what official sources told them, they could conceive and document a story on their own. The focus of their work shifted from gaining access to powerful figures to confronting powerful figures with facts. Reporters were thereby forced to become expert at finding and documenting facts before, not after, they asked questions.
themes: document-based reporting; independence; confrontation with facts
relevance_rationale: Shift from access journalism to document-based confrontation with facts is a permanent methodological advance.
notes: Post-Watergate methodological shift

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Donald L. Bartlett and James B. Steele urged other journalists to develop 'a document state of mind' — to always think of what documents might be available to support their stories.
themes: document state of mind; evidence gathering; mindset
relevance_rationale: Document state of mind is a permanent investigative mindset regardless of whether documents are physical or digital.
notes: Bartlett and Steele at Philadelphia Inquirer

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Ordinary reporters ask their sources, 'What happened?' Investigative reporters can also ask their sources: 'Isn't this what happened?' The second question is much more powerful than the first. It shows the source that the reporter cannot easily be lied to. It also makes the source's task, of answering the question, much easier. Sources do not like to explain everything to reporters. Nor do they like to volunteer information that a reporter clearly does not possess. However, sources often confirm information that they would not volunteer.
themes: interviewing; confirmation technique; document-informed questioning
relevance_rationale: Confirmation-based questioning technique is a permanent interviewing skill for investigative reporters.
notes: From Lina Joudy Syria archaeology case

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: the reporter's documents clearly inform and guide her use of human sources. The documents are connected to institutions where her sources work. By knowing their contents, the reporter can engage in detailed and precise discussions with experts and officials.
themes: documents before interviews; preparation; source engagement
relevance_rationale: Documents informing and guiding use of human sources is a timeless investigative workflow principle.
notes: Documents as bridge to human sources

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It is a misunderstanding that journalism is about finding facts; instead, it is about finding reliable sources. Once you find the right documentary and human sources, the facts will emerge from them. However, hunting for sources consumes the largest share of a typical investigation. It is fair to say that 90% of journalists use only 10% of the available sources, precisely because finding new sources takes time.
themes: source finding; efficiency; source diversity
relevance_rationale: Finding reliable sources remains essential but LLMs dramatically reduce search costs and can surface sources journalists would otherwise miss.
notes: 90/10 source usage estimate

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: For investigators, it is usually far more efficient and fruitful to seek information from public documents first. The information they contain can confirm whether or not a story is plausible, before much more time is invested in it.
themes: documents first; plausibility test; efficiency
relevance_rationale: Documents-first strategy for plausibility testing before investing time remains a sound investigative principle.
notes: Putting documents first subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The fact that something is open to inspection does not mean that anyone inspects it. Often, publicly available documents contain answers to important questions, but no one has bothered to study the documents.
themes: open sources; public records; neglected data
relevance_rationale: Publicly available documents hiding in plain sight is a permanent investigative observation.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Open sources, in particular documentary sources, can be consulted without raising an alarm. Calling an official for an interview will attract attention, and doors may close.
themes: open sources; operational security; stealth research
relevance_rationale: Operational security advantage of consulting open sources before alerting targets remains essential.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Even if someone is willing to speak with you, he or she will provide better information if you are visibly capable of understanding it. The source will observe whether or not you understand the language and concerns of the people involved in your story. You must therefore do at least some documentary research in order to prepare for interviews.
themes: interview preparation; domain knowledge; source respect
relevance_rationale: Documentary preparation to demonstrate competence to sources is a permanent interpersonal dynamic.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: First we identify the people who are concerned by our story, and the open sources that may be related to them. Then we plan a route among these sources. If we know whom we expect or hope to meet, we have a better chance of being prepared when an opportunity appears. If we have a strategy to reach them, our chances of making contact increase.
themes: source mapping; research planning; route planning
relevance_rationale: Systematic source mapping and route planning is a permanent investigative planning technique.
notes: How to search for open sources subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Which of these characters will create a documentary record of his or her actions? Which of them will use a document to justify his or her actions?
themes: document trail; source identification; institutional records
relevance_rationale: Predicting which actors create documentary records is a timeless source-identification technique.
notes: Using timeline to identify open sources subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Wadih Al Absi's answer was 'to go back to the law, and look for gaps in the law.' No one could stop him from reading Yemen's laws.
themes: legal sources; access; gap analysis; open records
relevance_rationale: Using legal texts as always-accessible sources when officials refuse to speak is a permanent investigative fallback.
notes: When official sources refuse to speak, laws are always accessible

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: We can imagine whom these other characters may be by creating a 'source village'. Along with actors in the story, the 'village' contains various witnesses to events or their consequences. Besides helping us to think of new sources, a source village can help us to visualize the sources we already know, and their relations to each other. This is particularly useful when investigating business or criminal networks.
themes: source village; network mapping; visualization; source planning
relevance_rationale: Source village concept for network visualization endures but LLMs and graph tools can now automate network mapping from documents.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: journalists who focus on inside sources at the start of an investigation leave themselves with few options in the event that their targets refuse to speak. By identifying numerous opportunities to contact people, we ensure that at least some information will flow to us.
themes: source redundancy; risk mitigation; source diversity
relevance_rationale: Source redundancy and diversification as risk mitigation is a permanent investigative strategy.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: reporters must take great care not to become the toy of organisations or individuals that offer access. Sources who control information may decide to show only the parts that advance their interests. Thus once the reporter has entered the village, and learned useful information, he or she must proceed to others.
themes: source capture; access bias; independent verification
relevance_rationale: Warning against source capture and access bias is a timeless journalistic independence principle.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: We begin our interviews with the people who are most open to us.
themes: interview sequence; open sources first; low-hanging fruit
relevance_rationale: Starting with most accessible sources is a permanent interview sequencing principle.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The core strategy here is to put sources before information. Students can be trained, instead of looking for an answer on the Internet, to look for reliable sources, and then to look for the answer in these sources. The Internet is not a source. It is a container of sources, like a library.
themes: source evaluation; internet research; information literacy
relevance_rationale: Sources before information and treating the internet as container of sources, not a source itself, is even more relevant with LLMs.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Students should be advised that 'search costs' - the time and effort it takes to find the solution to a given problem - are a main reason that investigation is regarded by editors and publishers as slow and costly. By improving their efficiency, they make investigative reporting a viable enterprise.
themes: search costs; efficiency; sustainability; business case
relevance_rationale: Search cost reduction remains key for investigation viability but LLMs have fundamentally changed the economics of information retrieval.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Journalists have a dreadful habit of relying on secondary sources for scholarly and scientific articles - that is, a newspaper story about a discovery - instead of the primary source. Investigators need the primary sources, and they are not hard to find in specialised databases and search engines.
themes: primary sources; scholarly research; source quality
relevance_rationale: Insistence on primary over secondary scholarly sources is a timeless research quality standard.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: rather than try to discern the ideal or normative ethical position in a given situation, reporters may find it simpler and more effective to consider the risks involved. What dangers may arise, for the reporter and for others, from a given course of action? How can the reporter manage, reduce or eliminate those risks?
themes: ethics; risk management; practical ethics
relevance_rationale: Risk-based ethical framework for investigative decisions is a permanent practical ethics approach.
notes: Risk-based ethical framework rather than normative

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Investigative reporters must never be merely the servants of a cause. They must first seek to discover the truth of a given situation. Positive change cannot be founded on illusions, no matter how noble they may seem.
themes: independence; truth-seeking; advocacy vs investigation
relevance_rationale: Independence from causes and commitment to truth-seeking is a permanent investigative principle.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: in investigative work, as in all reporting, anyone who is implicated in a story as an actor or target must be given the opportunity to reply to any questions raised by the story, and to explain any facts concerning him or her.
themes: right of reply; fairness; ethical sourcing
relevance_rationale: Right of reply for implicated parties is a permanent ethical and legal standard.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Very frequently, reporters are offered 'scoops' that are essentially disinformation, based on the testimony of one or another parties to a scandal, or on a purportedly 'secret' document. In such cases the information should not be used unless the reporter can verify it independently.
themes: disinformation; independent verification; scoops; corroboration
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward scoops and requirement for independent verification is timeless and even more critical with AI-generated disinformation.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Corroboration serves that purpose: What other sources support the claims of the reporter's anonymous sources? They can be found through documenting the source's claims, or by confirming with three other human sources, anonymous or not.
themes: corroboration; anonymous sources; three-source rule; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Corroboration through documents or multiple human sources is a permanent verification standard.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: If a journalist is offered such documents, he or she is probably wise to accept them. However, the journalist will be even wiser to keep the documents in a safe place, outside his or her home or office. The journalist must also take care to ask the provider of the documents if he or she can be identified as their likely human source. If so, the journalist must never refer or allude to the documents. The information they contain must be documented by other means.
themes: source protection; operational security; confidential documents
relevance_rationale: Source protection and operational security for confidential documents is timeless and arguably more critical in the digital era.
notes: Put the source above the secret subsection

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It is fine to have an emotional reaction to what you see. But then you must confirm that what you have seen is true, and that your emotions are not misleading you.
themes: emotional observation; verification; bias awareness
relevance_rationale: Separating emotional reaction from verified facts is a permanent epistemic discipline.
notes: From Allain Divorced Women in Jordan case

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Never accept confidential information without asking the source how many other people know it, and if he or she can be identified as the source. Treat vulnerable sources as off the record. This means that you cannot use the information, or allude to it, unless you can find another source whom you can identify.
themes: source protection; verification; confidential sources
relevance_rationale: Source protection protocols for confidential information and vulnerable sources are timeless ethical obligations.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: To be legitimate undercover work must meet a double test: Is the story of great public importance, and is there no other way of doing it?
themes: undercover reporting; ethics; source access
relevance_rationale: Double test for undercover work legitimacy (public importance + no alternative) is a permanent ethical threshold.
notes: Double test as ethical threshold for clandestine methods

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The undercover work was supported by extensive documentary research concerning the companies that owned the schools, and by interviews with government officials and parents.
themes: undercover reporting; triangulation; documentary research
relevance_rationale: Triangulating undercover findings with documentary and interview evidence is a permanent verification principle.
notes: Zbiss Quranic kindergartens case; undercover as supplement not substitute for open-source work

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: By profession, journalists are opportunists. They grab what they can quickly take, and don't waste time longing for what seems out of reach. This attitude serves well in news reporting, where the reporter is always running after the action. But in investigative reporting, we usually need sources that other reporters don't care or know how to access, in order to verify a story.
themes: investigative method; source access; news vs investigation
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing investigative from news reporting by source strategy and persistence is a permanent identity distinction.
notes: Distinguishes investigative from news reporting by source strategy

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: One place to search for confidential sources is the story's timeline. Each event in the timeline concerns various participants and witnesses. If they have not been previously cited in news stories, there is a good chance that they have something to say, as well as good reasons for not saying it.
themes: source identification; timeline; source mapping
relevance_rationale: Using timeline events to identify uncited sources is a permanent source-discovery technique.
notes: Timeline as source-discovery tool

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: A reporter who already knows most or all of the facts that can be obtained from open sources will always be of interest to sources, including confidential sources, because the reporter is visibly capable of understanding what the source has to say.
themes: source trust; expertise; open sources
relevance_rationale: Reporter expertise as precondition for source trust and access is a permanent interpersonal dynamic.
notes: Reporter expertise as precondition for source access

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: He takes a collegial approach to investigating complex subjects, in which the reporter assumes that sources are acting in good faith, and continually builds expertise in their domain... From this reading he collected the names of actors in the story: While reading, you start laying a plan for interviewing. I highlight key people who are either authors or quoted in stories who indicate some level of familiarity with the topic, and write down a list with their names and apparent areas of expertise.
themes: source mapping; reading strategy; interview planning
relevance_rationale: Building source lists from document reading endures but LLMs can now extract and compile actor names from large document sets automatically.
notes: Schapiro method: building source list from document reading

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: I obtained press accreditation to attend trade-fairs of carbon project developers in which people engaged directly in the market gather with their peers – where I could speak with key players in a less formal and threatening fashion. These settings can be invaluable in having off and on the record conversations to deepen your understanding, and setting relationships into motion that can later lead to 'lucky' revelations.
themes: source access; informal settings; trade fairs; relationship building
relevance_rationale: Using informal settings like trade fairs for source access and relationship building is a permanent field technique.
notes: Schapiro method; 'lucky' in quotes signals revelations are product of preparation

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Aggression is not a substitute for research; most often, it is a sign of incompetence, or of fear. Dogs do not show their teeth because they are smart or brave. Before confrontation occurs, a reporter must acquire expert knowledge of the subject, the sources and their activities.
themes: source relations; interview technique; expertise
relevance_rationale: Research before confrontation and aggression as sign of incompetence is a timeless investigative principle.
notes: Research before confrontation; aggression as incompetence

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: In general, people find it easier to confirm information than to volunteer information. We can use our hypothesis for this purpose, especially if the reluctant source is someone we believe to be honest. We can say, for example: 'This is what I think happened. Is that accurate? Please tell me if I am wrong.'
themes: interview technique; hypothesis testing; reluctant sources
relevance_rationale: Using hypothesis to unlock reluctant sources via confirmation rather than volunteering is a permanent interview technique.
notes: Hypothesis as interviewing tool to unlock reluctant sources

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: A 'paper trail', in reporters' slang, is a chain of documentation that establishes causality or responsibility for a given event. There are two simple ways to imagine a paper trail that includes documents of interest to us.
themes: paper trail; documentation; causality
relevance_rationale: Paper trail concept and tracing chains of documentation for causality is a permanent investigative method.
notes: Definition of paper trail concept

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: We can deduce the existence of many documents that might have appeared at a moment in our timeline. For example, a meeting will be followed by a letter or memorandum that sums up participants' conclusions, or a business deal will be sealed by a contract. Many projects start with plans, followed by tender offers, tenders and contracts, then status reports when things go into motion, followed by evaluations.
themes: paper trail; timeline; document prediction
relevance_rationale: Deducing which documents should exist from timeline events is a permanent investigative reasoning technique.
notes: Using timeline to predict which documents should exist

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Most often, confidential documents are provided by human sources. We can predict who, among the actors in our story, is most likely to possess a given document by asking the following questions: Who asked for the document? Who created it? Who approved it? Who would receive a copy? Who shared it? Who responded to it?
themes: document sourcing; source mapping; confidential documents
relevance_rationale: Six questions to identify document holders is a permanent source-mapping technique.
notes: Six questions to identify document holders

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It is easier to find something when you are looking for it. That is why they need to imagine at least some documents in advance – so they can say, for example, 'Did you sign a contract? Do you have a copy? Please show it to me.'
themes: document discovery; anticipation; interview technique
relevance_rationale: Anticipating documents to make them findable is a permanent investigative preparation principle.
notes: Anticipating documents makes them findable

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: In most investigations, the first sources to speak are those who feel victimised. The second most talkative group, once they see our interest in the story, will usually be expert witnesses who can provide perspective on events. In general, those responsible for a bad situation are the last who wish to speak about it.
themes: interview sequence; source prioritization; victims; experts
relevance_rationale: Victim-first, perpetrator-last interview sequencing is a permanent investigative workflow.
notes: Victim-first, perpetrator-last interview sequence

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The ideal source in most investigations is someone in the middle ranks of an organisation, at operational or planning levels. These people have access to significant documents, but have very little influence on how policies are formed or implemented. They may thus consider themselves victims, obliged to carry out policies or actions in which they do not believe.
themes: source selection; organizational hierarchy; whistleblowers
relevance_rationale: Mid-rank organizational insiders as ideal sources due to access and motivation is a permanent human intelligence principle.
notes: Mid-rank sources as ideal: access to documents, motivation to speak

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: A general goal is to access the source's unique qualities. These include: insight, observations, experiences, judgments, or documents. When we have collected them, these unique qualities become our assets.
themes: interview goals; source value; information assets
relevance_rationale: Identifying unique qualities of each source (insight, observations, documents) is a timeless interview planning principle.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Before we interview someone, we should know, at a minimum, what role they probably played in our story. Are they victims, witnesses, or perpetrators? Which other actors or events may they have observed? We should also know any facts about them that we can collect from the Internet – a CV, interviews, news, etc.
themes: interview preparation; backgrounding; source research
relevance_rationale: Backgrounding interviewees endures but LLMs can now automate comprehensive background research from internet sources in minutes.
notes: Backgrounding the interviewee before contact

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Students can structure source conversations around the following types of questions: Key question: if you could ask only one question, what would it be? Objective questions: Is this what happened? Who was there? Where? When? How? Timeline: When did an event start? How? What happened next? Subjective questions: What do you think about the events? What did you feel? Resource questions: Who else will know about this? Whom should I speak with? Did anyone create a report, memorandum or other document about these events?
themes: interview technique; question taxonomy; structured interviewing
relevance_rationale: Five question types for structured investigative interviews is a permanent interviewing framework.
notes: Five question types for investigative interviews

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The goal of an interview is not to ask questions, but to listen! An investigator encourages people to talk, and observes how they speak – their emotion, body language or silence – as well as what they speak about.
themes: interview technique; listening; observation
relevance_rationale: Listening as primary interview goal and reading nonverbal cues is an irreplaceable human investigative skill.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Before ending an interview, professional reporters do the following: Quickly review their notes to see if anything needs clarifying; Ask: 'Is there anything I did not ask, but you would like to tell?'; Ask: 'Do you feel that you had a chance to tell everything you wanted?'; Ask whom the source believes they should speak with next; Ask for documents in possession of the interviewee.
themes: interview technique; closing questions; source chain
relevance_rationale: Five-step interview closing checklist is a permanent best practice for investigative conversations.
notes: Five-step interview closing checklist

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Journalists should not look at officials as though they are untouchable – they are public servants, there to serve us, not vice versa. Journalists have to be well prepared, knowing exactly why they are interviewing and what they want, and have enough ammunition to show the official they are expert.
themes: interview technique; officials; preparation; power dynamics
relevance_rationale: Treating officials as public servants and being well-prepared is a timeless power-dynamics principle.
notes: Rana Sabbagh on interviewing powerful sources

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: From the beginning you should be in touch with the target – maybe they will change your story. Maybe you are wrong. You can ask for general information. Or later you will say, 'You told me this, but this evidence says it isn't true. Give me answers.' From the beginning I am in touch, but in different ways. At first I seem naïve: 'Please give me all your information about my topic.' Then when I know I have all the evidence I can be more direct and stronger.
themes: interview strategy; target engagement; escalation; confrontation
relevance_rationale: Graduated-pressure interview approach from naive to confrontational is a permanent interviewing strategy.
notes: Sabbagh graduated-pressure approach: naïve first, confrontational later

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: The underlying principle remains the same: creating statistically valid analyses of events and phenomena that could otherwise not be accurately understood.
chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
themes: data journalism; precision journalism; statistical analysis
relevance_rationale: Statistical analysis of events and phenomena for investigative purposes is a permanent epistemological principle.
notes: Core epistemological claim about data journalism from Meyer tradition

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Data is not limited to numbers. Every time that reporters write down an idea, or interview someone, or save an article that they read on the Internet, in order to process and analyse it later, they are compiling data. The real issue is whether or not they compile data in a useful way. Strictly speaking, data are isolated facts kept or presented in a structure.
chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
themes: data definition; information management; structure
relevance_rationale: Broad definition of data as structured facts regardless of format is a permanent conceptual foundation.
notes: Broad definition of data; structure is what makes facts into data

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It is indisputable that every leading professional in this field organises data as an investigation proceeds, instead of waiting for the investigation to be over before organising. This is the justification for asking your students to learn at least one method.
chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
themes: information management; workflow; progressive organization
relevance_rationale: Organizing data progressively during investigation rather than after is a permanent best practice.
notes: Universal practice among top investigators: organize as you go

author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Some typical means of collaboration include: Sharing sources, such as lists of contacts, or data, or access to witnesses. Though investigative reporters must verify all of the information in their possession before using it, they can also obtain documentation, insights or data from others. Whistleblowers and NGOs are among the most common sources of such material.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: It is not enough to be right. A wise investigative journalist will ensure, as well, that he or she is not alone with the truth. In order to reduce this risk, the journalist should ensure: The facts of the story are not overstated or exaggerated... The facts, and a viable path to the facts, must be open to other journalists... Social actors can be engaged, as sources and as potential allies, before the story is published.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann

They are announced in advance, in particular to allies who will support them... Hostile response from targets of the investigation is anticipated and prepared for.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann

author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann

The former is appropriate for stories in which the passage of time is the key organizing factor, such as reconstructions of crimes, biographies or profiles, or stories about how policies go wrong. The picaresque structure is well-suited to stories in which related phenomena occur simultaneously in multiple locations.

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: 'Data' is not a synonym for 'numbers'. Data consists of facts that have been archived and organized, and that can be analyzed to uncover patterns and structures of fact. The facts may consist of numbers, but they may also consist of events, judgments, names, places, or any other category of information. Once they are organized so that they can be queried and interpreted, they become a database.
themes: data definition; database; patterns; structured information

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method

source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann
extract: Data journalism is a hybrid of social science inquiry and traditional journalism. It borrows scientific rigor from the former, and storytelling from the latter. And, as is the case in both fields, you cannot start a data journalism story without a specific question that leads to a hypothesis. Data is often a confusing, sometimes boring set of raw information. If you do not have a starting point, or an angle, through which you will examine and analyze documents or numbers, they will not mean much.

The gap between the two data sets becomes the story.

source_title: A Model for the 21st Century Newsroom

author: Sense about Science and RSS

author: Sense about Science and RSS

source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide

source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide
author: Robert Cribb, Dean Jobb, David McKie, Fred Vallance-Jones
extract: The Toronto Star analyzed the Toronto Police Service's own electronic database of arrests and made a compelling case for racial profiling. source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide
author: Robert Cribb, Dean Jobb, David McKie, Fred Vallance-Jones
extract: Investigative interviews are conducted only after careful preparation. You will want to research prospective interviewees, find out not only what they do and what they should know, but also what they are like and how they may react in an interview. You will examine documents, notes from previous interviews, and electronic records, drawing up a list of questions or talking points before you come close to conducting an interview.
