author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The biggest difference between open source investigation and traditional journalism is that everything is transparent: you can easily share it with online users, and nothing is hidden. Crowdsourcing and collaboration are often part of it: you can do so much more in a team, and with these types of techniques, it's even more important that you work with more people because they all find different things and you all need to monitor different things.
chapter: Open source intelligence: Using LinkedIn, reverse image search, people search, and other tools
themes: OSINT; transparency; collaboration; crowdsourcing
relevance_rationale: Transparency and collaboration principles of open source investigation endure regardless of AI tools
notes: Johanna Wild interview; OSI transparency principle

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Open source investigation often involves combining different methods---not only monitoring what's on social media but we might first identify a specific video and try to geolocate that to determine the exact location where it was taken; we might identify people that are interesting and find other ways to research them, like phone book apps to check who's behind a number---it's the combination of using all those techniques together in an investigative workflow.
chapter: Open source intelligence: Using LinkedIn, reverse image search, people search, and other tools
themes: OSINT; geolocation; investigative workflow; multi-method
relevance_rationale: OSINT combined-methods workflow endures conceptually but LLMs are transforming geolocation and cross-referencing capabilities
notes: Johanna Wild interview; combined-methods approach

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A reverse image search is a search based on an image rather than words: you upload an image and the search engine will show results of any webpages it knows of where the same, or similar, images appear. TinEye.com is one such search engine, and Bing Visual Search allows you to search by image too. Because people tend to use the same profile pictures in lots of different places, they can be especially useful in conducting reverse image searches to find other places where they have a profile.
chapter: Open source intelligence: Using LinkedIn, reverse image search, people search, and other tools
themes: reverse image search; verification; OSINT; TinEye
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search remains important but AI-powered visual search tools now go far beyond TinEye and basic image matching
notes: Technique for both finding and verifying sources

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Case studies are harder to find than experts: contact details for those affected by a particular issue aren't likely to be published by a single type of organisation like universities so you need to be more creative. You might think of the sorts of communities where those affected might gather online. ... Remember that with case studies in particular, it's not always about finding a way to contact the potential case study directly: often you're looking for a go-between, someone who knows the right person.
themes: case studies; community sourcing; go-betweens; human interest
relevance_rationale: Creative sourcing through community go-betweens requires human relationship skills LLMs cannot replace
notes: Go-between strategy: charities, community admins

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Note that some online communities may not be as diverse as the real-world communities they are supposed to reflect due to lower rates of internet access or literacy in certain groups within that community, or even the qualities of the online community itself. Be prepared to visit places in those communities physically in order to ensure that your sourcing doesn't suffer from blind spots.
themes: digital divide; source diversity; online community bias
relevance_rationale: Awareness of digital divide bias in online communities remains essential for equitable sourcing
notes: Cites Kurwa 2019 on digitally gated communities

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Any witnesses to a newsworthy event are likely to have many journalists trying to contact them, and your approach should be sensitive to that. You should also consider the physical safety of witnesses, and their emotional welfare, when communicating with them---indeed, their wellbeing and safety should be the first thing you ask about. Always be guided by respect for their needs and give them advice on where they can access help.
themes: ethics; witness welfare; sensitive sourcing; trauma
relevance_rationale: Ethical sensitivity when contacting witnesses is a human judgment skill unaffected by AI
notes: Cites First Draft guide to working with social sources

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Once you have introduced yourself, explained how you found them, and which organisation you work for, it is always better to speak to someone directly---face-to-face or on the phone---than via messages as early as possible, as this makes it easier to build trust and provide emotional support. It also makes it more likely that you can verify their account. Don't forget to tell them when they can expect to see the story---and update them if that changes.
themes: interviewing; trust building; witness contact; verification
relevance_rationale: Building trust through direct contact with witnesses is fundamental journalism unaffected by AI
notes: Move from messaging to direct contact ASAP

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Another approach may be to think about witnesses who may not have shared content on social media about the event. Think instead about who is likely to have been in the location and where to find those people online. ... Another tip is to use Google Street View to look at the location of the event and identify local businesses and shops whose employees may well have seen what happened---then find their phone numbers online and call them!
themes: witness sourcing; Google Street View; geolocation; creative sourcing
relevance_rationale: Creative lateral thinking to find witnesses beyond social media remains essential human reporting skill
notes: Lateral thinking: identify likely bystanders via Street View

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: For more recent changes, try conducting a relevant Google search and clicking on cache next to the relevant result. For older changes, the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine can give you snapshots going further back. Search for the URL and you will be presented with a calendar showing what dates it has saved that page.
chapter: Finding older versions of webpages and pages that have disappeared: Caches
themes: web archives; Wayback Machine; cache; verification
relevance_rationale: Google cache deprecated; Wayback Machine endures but AI tools now assist with web archival research
notes: Cummings blog post example: cache revealed backdated edit

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is important to remember that Google only indexes a small part of the web: most of it is invisible to search engines. The invisible web (sometimes called deep web) refers to millions of pages that cannot be found by search engines. The dark web refers to pages that have been intentionally hidden. Some research tasks are far better resolved by going straight to source, by finding interactive mediums or sources of expertise far beyond the crowd.
chapter: The invisible web and the dark web
themes: invisible web; dark web; search limitations; deep web
relevance_rationale: Deep web and dark web concepts remain essential; LLMs also cannot access most invisible web content
notes: Murray Dick quote; search engines cover small fraction of web

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: New Freedom of Information laws and a growing open data movement made it much easier for journalists to access data on everything from politics and the environment to education and culture---or for citizens to get hold of it and highlight stories that journalists should be digging into.
themes: FOI; open data; data access; civic data
relevance_rationale: FOI and open data as structural enablers of data journalism remain unchanged by AI
notes: FOI and open data as structural enablers of data journalism

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data journalism's distinctiveness from CAR lay in the way that it often connected to data directly on the internet; the growth of data visualisation, often abbreviated to datavis; and the way that user interactions and interactivity could be built into the storytelling.
themes: data journalism; CAR; data visualisation; interactivity
relevance_rationale: Historical distinction between CAR and data journalism remains relevant context for understanding field evolution
notes: Three features distinguishing data journalism from CAR

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Factchecking had historically been done before publication, both by reporters---when presented with claims by interviewees---and by subeditors, who checked journalists' copy before publication. But with sources able to publish their claims directly to audiences, and audiences able to spread misinformation without any checks in place, the focus shifted outwards.
chapter: Information wars: Fake news, sockpuppetry, and propaganda
themes: factchecking; verification; misinformation; post-publication
relevance_rationale: Shift from pre-publication to post-publication factchecking is even more relevant with AI-generated misinformation
notes: Shift from pre-publication to post-publication factchecking

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The need for explainers to help make sense of complex stories, and for more positive and empowering solutions journalism, were both identified as potential strategies for addressing news avoidance.
themes: solutions journalism; explainers; news avoidance; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Explainers and solutions journalism as responses to news avoidance remain essential editorial strategies
notes: Newman et al. 2022; response to selective avoidance

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Metadata about phones and internet communication revealed very detailed information about the movements of individuals, what webpages they visited, and who they communicated with. Data was collected even when phones were switched off. This made it possible to identify who journalists were making contact with, not only at the time but retrospectively too.
themes: metadata; surveillance; source protection; information security
relevance_rationale: Metadata surveillance of journalist-source relationships is even more critical with AI-enhanced surveillance capabilities
notes: Post-Snowden: metadata exposes journalist-source relationships

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Press releases raise important ethical issues for journalists. As Patricia A. Curtin puts it: Public relations practitioners provide information subsidies to the media on behalf of their clients to influence the media agenda and potentially affect public opinion. In other words, a news organisation's independence in choosing the most important stories and collecting the information to report them can be compromised by the availability of free content paid for by someone else.
chapter: Making leads come to you: updates and personal newswires
themes: press releases; information subsidies; editorial independence; PR
relevance_rationale: Information subsidy concept and PR influence on editorial independence remain fundamental; AI-generated PR amplifies the risk
notes: Curtin 2009; information subsidy concept

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Third-party analytics tools like Bitly and Buffer will give you a special URL for any link you want to share, and then track when people click on that link. This allows you to test what times most people click on your link, which social media platforms the links tend to perform best on, or what accompanying text is most effective in getting users to click.
themes: analytics; A/B testing; link tracking; social media optimisation
relevance_rationale: Bitly/Buffer link tracking tools still work but AI-driven analytics platforms now offer more sophisticated distribution testing
notes: Bitly, Buffer for distribution testing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is no longer enough to simply relay information from one part of society to another; journalists are expected to be expert curators and verifiers, data journalists, or multimedia producers. Our ability to protect our sources now relies not just on institutional support, but on technological savviness. And we must earn our readers' trust and engagement---we cannot take it for granted.
themes: journalist role; curation; verification; trust; multiskilling
relevance_rationale: Expanded journalist competencies (curation, verification, data, multimedia, source protection) remain essential in AI era
notes: Ch2 summary; expanded journalist competencies

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When you find images or other media online or on social media, bear in mind that the user still retains copyright over those, and you cannot usually publish a picture without permission from the copyright holder. If you find user-generated image content on Twitter, you can message the account holder to find out if they originated the image and would be prepared to allow you to use the image with a credit. It is important to establish that the person giving permission has the right to do so and ideally owns the photograph.
chapter: Do you have permission to use media?
themes: copyright; permissions; UGC; provenance; media law
relevance_rationale: Copyright and permission chains for UGC remain legally essential; AI-generated content adds new complexity
notes: Permission chain: identify originator, verify ownership, get consent

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Remember that you don't have editorial control over live video so it's important to consider any potential ethical issues if you intend to link to or use the material: could something distressing happen on the stream that may traumatise viewers? Could it put witnesses or law enforcement at risk? You may need to use only parts of the video, or blur elements, when publishing it.
themes: ethics; live video; editorial control; harm minimisation
relevance_rationale: Ethical risk assessment for live/UGC video remains essential editorial judgment
notes: Risk assessment for live/UGC video use

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Domingo et al. (2007) identified three more dimensions of convergence beyond physical changes in the newsroom: multiskilled professionals; multiplatform delivery; and active audience.
themes: convergence; multiskilling; multiplatform; audience participation
relevance_rationale: Convergence dimensions (multiskilling, multiplatform, active audience) remain foundational media theory
notes: Domingo et al. 2007 convergence framework

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Having a mobile-first strategy meant assuming that a typical reader would be accessing your stories on a mobile device. News website content management systems were increasingly designed to be responsive: to respond to the device being used and display content accordingly. Publishers also focused more on web performance optimisation (WPO): making sure pages loaded as quickly as possible.
themes: mobile-first; responsive design; web performance; CMS
relevance_rationale: Mobile-first design endures but responsive design and WPO techniques now include AI-driven personalization and app ecosystems
notes: Mobile-first as design and editorial strategy

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: WAN-IFRA recommended that publishers consider the corporate goals of each platform they are considering publishing on, and how that might impact on their own objectives---as well as each platform's core audience and their information consumption profiles. But it also warned publishers against locking themselves to Facebook, a related loss of branding and context, and relying too much on superficial metrics.
chapter: The rise of platform publishing and distributed content
themes: platform publishing; platform dependency; metrics; distribution strategy
relevance_rationale: Platform dependency warnings and evaluation framework remain critical; AI platforms add new dependency concerns
notes: WAN-IFRA platform evaluation guidance; Pfeiffer 2015

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sharing the data behind stories is another way to make your stories adaptable: with access to that data, users can make new charts, infographics, and tools from it, or find new stories. These in turn may be interesting, informative, or useful to your audience---and move the story forward.
themes: open data; reproducibility; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Sharing data behind stories for transparency and audience engagement remains essential; AI makes reuse even easier
notes: Frames data sharing as extending story impact, not just transparency

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Nowadays, teams will often use the website GitHub to share their data---and code---allowing others to reproduce the results and even improve the scripts. One of the features of GitHub is that you can easily make a copy of someone's project by 'forking' it to your own account, which means a reader can work on the same code and data that the journalist used.
themes: reproducibility; open source; GitHub; code sharing
relevance_rationale: GitHub for reproducibility and code-sharing in journalism remains standard practice
notes: Positions reproducibility as a publishing norm, not just internal practice

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Linking is a tool for providing transparency around the journalist's sources---and, by extension, trust. A 2021 Gallup/Knight Foundation survey of US news consumers found that 83% of 35-54-year-olds, and 65% of those aged 55 and older, said that linking was 'very important' in determining whether or not they trusted a news organisation, reinforcing earlier research that found hyperlinking to be the most highly rated tool in providing transparency.
themes: transparency; trust; source linking; verification
relevance_rationale: Linking as transparency and trust-building tool remains essential; evidence-backed claim about audience trust
notes: Evidence-backed claim that linking directly builds audience trust

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A journalist can communicate more succinctly through intelligent use of linking: the sentence 'police said in a Facebook post', for example, can be written as 'police said' instead, where 'said' is linked to the post that would otherwise need to be attributed. The link serves both as a clear signal that the information comes from an online source and information about where that source is.
themes: attribution; source linking; brevity; transparency
relevance_rationale: Using links for attribution and brevity remains core web writing practice
notes: Linking as both attribution method and space-saving device

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One reason is that the material may be deleted: in one survey of embeds in 2016, it was found that 10% had been 'modified or removed by their author since the article's publication'. If that happens then you will be left with a gap on your page where the embedded material should be---or worse, content that has been edited to misrepresent what was said. For this reason, some news organisations, or some stories, will instead use a screenshot of the social media post in order to ensure that there is a permanent record of the material.
themes: archiving; source preservation; embedded content; link rot
relevance_rationale: Link rot and embed deletion risks remain real; archiving embedded content is even more important as platforms evolve
notes: Practical warning about embeds as ephemeral evidence

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Passing along tweets as the official record grants authority and legitimacy to Twitter, not to sources, and certainly not to journalists. There is a risk of treating social media updates from officials as a form of 'convenient access' where journalists merely amplify what is said, rather than checking whether it is an accurate representation of what is happening, and where elite sources hide behind social media updates rather than engaging with journalists directly.
themes: verification; source authority; social media as source; amplification bias
relevance_rationale: Critique of amplifying official social media without verification is even more relevant with AI-generated content
notes: Molyneux and McGregor critique of embed-as-evidence

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: We should be attentive to the intended audience for a social media post, and whether vastly increasing that audience reveals an important story---or just shames or embarrasses a random person. We should not automatically or even typically comply with a poster's original intention---but we should be aware of it.
themes: ethics; privacy; public interest; social media as source
relevance_rationale: Privacy-as-continuum principle for social media content is even more relevant when AI can aggregate and amplify at scale
notes: BuzzFeed editorial guidelines on embedding private social media content

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Communities contain contributors who can lead you to a story or a key piece of information; communities contain keen moderators who can help maintain the standard of discussion; they even contain editors who will highlight mistakes, omissions, and new details to be incorporated into a story.
chapter: C is for community ... and conversation
themes: crowdsourcing; community; source finding; error correction
relevance_rationale: Community as distributed verification and sourcing layer is a fundamental journalism principle
notes: Community as distributed verification and sourcing layer

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Understanding what terms people are likely to use for something, then, is an important skill: when the Japanese volcano Mount Ontake erupted, most people outside Japan searched for 'Japan volcano', not 'Mount Ontake'. As a result, most news reporting used the former phrase over the latter.
themes: keyword research; audience analysis; discoverability; SEO
relevance_rationale: Keyword research principle endures but LLM-powered search changes how audiences find content beyond keyword matching
notes: Keyword selection requires understanding audience mental models

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Google Trends allows you to compare relative search volumes for different phrases. Check the country and time range matches your country and a recent timescale to get a more accurate result. Google's Keyword Planner will likewise suggest the most popular searches based on the word or phrase you type in.
themes: Google Trends; keyword research; audience analysis; SEO tools
relevance_rationale: Google Trends still useful but AI-driven audience insight tools expand beyond keyword volume comparison

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The best way to have lots of people linking to you is, of course, to write fantastic, compelling, unique content that others want to share and link to. No amount of SEO techniques can avoid the need for great journalism.
themes: quality; discoverability; SEO; editorial standards
relevance_rationale: Quality journalism as the ultimate discoverability strategy is timeless and platform-agnostic
notes: Quality journalism as the ultimate SEO strategy

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Publishing regularly, and persistently, for a long time on a core set of topics is a good strategy for improving your SEO. Search engines look for the same things we all do---evidence that you care.
themes: beat coverage; persistence; SEO; authority building
relevance_rationale: Persistent beat publishing for authority building remains sound editorial and discovery strategy
notes: Consistent beat reporting as both editorial and discovery strategy

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The golden rule is to have a sense of who you are writing for. And this becomes more important when you're writing more than one piece. Always know what you expect of a single individual reader and what you would like them to get through in an ideal situation. Don't fall into that trap of just 'coverage'.
themes: audience analysis; editorial strategy; reader-centred design
relevance_rationale: Audience-first editorial thinking is a timeless principle regardless of distribution technology
notes: Chris Moran (Guardian) on audience-first editorial thinking

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The same stories might be told in more engaging ways by using more socially successful formats such as debunking, explainers, or investigations that reveal a hidden truth.
chapter: Jonah Peretti and the Google penalty
themes: format choice; explainers; investigations; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Annalee Newitz's 'Valley of Ambiguity' -- accessible formats for complex stories

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Does the story involve a lot of facts or data? Consider an infographic or some sort of data visualisation. Have you secured an interview with a key character and a set of locations or items that tell their own story? An audio or video interview may be the most powerful choice of format.
themes: format choice; data visualisation; infographics; storytelling
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Format selection decision tree driven by story content type

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Such a strategy begins with the same principles as good journalism on any platform: knowing who you are writing for and why. If you know who your audience is, you have a good idea of what information is most valuable, what knowledge to take for granted, what style is appropriate, and where and when is best to reach them.
themes: content strategy; audience analysis; editorial planning
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Content strategy as the bridge between editorial judgement and publishing decisions

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Just because you can publish your story instantly does not mean that you should. News organisations often adopt publishing schedules online so that stories that are not time-sensitive can reach the biggest audience.
themes: publishing timing; editorial strategy; audience reach
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Strategic timing as organizational practice, not just individual choice

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One of the things that defines professional reporting is the fact that we are writing for a specific audience for purposes related to our job, rather than for our friends and our own purposes. That audience shapes what information we include, and what knowledge we take for granted.
chapter: Starting out: Creating a professional social media account
themes: audience analysis; professionalism; editorial judgement
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Audience-awareness as defining feature of professional journalism

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Before you retweet, like, or share any update from another user, take a moment to check if you are confident it is true. If there is a link in the update have you clicked on it and checked that it is what the update says it is? Is the link on a reliable site? Does the story sound 'too good to be true'? Is the account reliable? If an image is included, use Google's reverse image search feature to find out where that image has been used before.
chapter: Think before you retweet: The role of verification on social media
themes: verification; fact-checking; social media; reverse image search
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Verification checklist for social media sharing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Engage with the source and ask further questions first, for example, 'Where did you hear about this?' or 'Have you got a link to more details?' If they do not respond, assume that they cannot provide more details and be extra sceptical.
chapter: Think before you retweet: The role of verification on social media
themes: verification; source engagement; scepticism; social media
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Active verification through source dialogue rather than passive acceptance

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you do make a mistake, it's important to acknowledge that quickly and take steps to correct it. Don't delete the update---it can suggest that you are trying to cover up your mistake and prevent you from reaching the same people who saw or shared it. If you can edit the original, then write 'Correction' along with any clarification. Make it clear what wasn't correct in the original post.
chapter: What to do when you get it wrong
themes: corrections; transparency; error handling; accountability
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Correction protocol for social media errors

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Using your social media account to separate fact from fiction is one of the most valuable services you can perform as a journalist. You might find a claim by a politician or celebrity that is not supported by the facts---and make that part of your update. Adding context to a story is another way you can add value: if someone has made an important announcement, can you provide the background to that? The timeline leading up to it? A key statistic? If someone is saying more money is being spent, is that accounting for inflation?
chapter: Separating fact from fiction---or adding context
themes: fact-checking; context; inflation adjustment; claims verification
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Practical examples of adding analytical context to claims

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can invite others into your process. For example, if a politician is claiming that a protest was 'violent', you can ask people who were there whether that was their experience (you will still need to verify their claims, too). Or you might appeal to legal experts to clarify a point of law.
chapter: Separating fact from fiction---or adding context
themes: crowdsourced verification; expert sourcing; open journalism
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Verification as community process, with caveat about recursive verification need

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Experimentation and measurement are central to understanding what is effective in writing for your audience on social media. The measurement part is relatively easy. But experimentation is more important. Because while you can use measurement to find out which updates perform the best and worst, it requires experimentation to tell you why.
chapter: The importance of experimenting and measuring
themes: experimentation; measurement; audience analytics; evidence-based practice
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Measurement tells what; experimentation tells why

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Commonly you experiment by trying to rule out various factors that could influence the results: if you are testing timing then you might leave the text itself unchanged between each update. If you are testing different text you may try to keep timing as close together as possible, and so on.
chapter: The importance of experimenting and measuring
themes: experimental design; controlled testing; variable isolation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Describes basic controlled experiment methodology for content testing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One thing to beware of is analysing those that already follow you rather than those who you want to start following you (and might be online at a different time). It is important to analyse both your own account and those of direct competitors to see whether you could be doing anything differently.
themes: selection bias; audience analytics; survivorship bias
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Warns against survivorship bias in audience analysis

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Online, however, publishers can measure how long the user spends on the page, how far they scroll down, and even where their mouse travels and how they interact with the page.
themes: audience analytics; engagement metrics; user behaviour data
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Behavioural data as empirical basis for editorial decisions

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One analysis of over one million articles found that 'articles with an image once every 75-100 words got double the amount of shares of articles with fewer images'. Note, however, that you need to get the balance right: articles with even more frequent images got fewer shares.
themes: visual storytelling; engagement metrics; image density; evidence-based practice
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Data-driven finding with non-linear relationship warning

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Content that evokes high-arousal positive (awe) or negative (anger or anxiety) emotions is more viral. Content that evokes low-arousal, or deactivating, emotions (e.g. sadness) is less viral.
chapter: Jonah Peretti and the Google penalty
themes: virality; emotional valence; audience psychology; engagement
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Berger and Milkman research on emotional arousal and sharing behaviour

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The nature of the story influences the format you might use to report it. Does the event take place over a geographical area, and will users want to see the movement or focus on a particular location? Then a map might be most appropriate. Are things changing so fast that a traditional 'story' format is going to be inadequate? Then a liveblog may work better. Is there a wealth of material out there being produced by witnesses? A gallery, portal, or aggregator might all be good choices. Is there some complex science or backstory that needs to be understood? An explainer or backgrounder can work well.
themes: format choice; maps; liveblogs; explainers; story-driven design
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Decision tree matching story characteristics to format types

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The mistake that's often made here is to assume that something is either public or private. In reality, there is a sliding scale between the two. When in March 2014 one Twitter user asked her followers 'What were you wearing when you were sexually assaulted?' the answers were public to anyone who might search for them---but publishing those responses in an article generated a backlash. Why? Because tweeting to a few hundred followers is not the same as a tweet being published on BuzzFeed.
themes: privacy spectrum; ethics; consent; social media as source
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Public-private is a continuum, not a binary -- key ethical principle for data sourcing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Search engine optimisation (SEO) is the practice of optimising your content so that search engines can best understand it and are more likely to suggest it in response to a relevant search query. This does not mean that we are 'writing for search engines': we are writing for people. It is people's behaviour---clicking through to your articles, reading them, sharing them, and linking to them---that shapes how highly search engines rank your content.
themes: SEO; audience-centred design; discoverability
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Reframes SEO as human-centred rather than technical exercise

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Code is particularly important for images, video, and audio. Remember that search engines can't 'see' or 'hear'. Instead, they use a number of clues to try to understand multimedia. This includes: the filename (try to avoid using default names like 'IMG5634.jpg' and change it to something descriptive like 'LionelMessi.jpg'); captions (make sure these use keywords); if you are embedding media you've uploaded to services like YouTube, Vimeo, Soundcloud, make sure you've edited the title, description, category, and tags to include keywords.
themes: metadata; SEO; multimedia publishing; file naming
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Metadata hygiene for multimedia assets -- applies to data viz too

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Don't just spread information. Be careful. Be skeptical. Add context.
chapter: Think before you retweet: The role of verification on social media
themes: verification; scepticism; context; editorial judgement
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: NPR social media guidelines -- concise verification principle

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Look out for out-of-date material too. In the wake of terrorist attacks in Paris in November 2015, a seven-month-old story about attacks in Kenya became the top story on the BBC website with 7.5 million views coming from social media. Many---although not all---of these were people mistakenly believing the attack had just happened.
chapter: Think before you retweet: The role of verification on social media
themes: temporal verification; misinformation; social media; stale data
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Timeliness as verification dimension -- old data recycled as new

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Fairness involves making sure that important or significant facts are not left out; making sure that all information is relevant; being honest; and providing the opportunity for individuals or organisations 'to address assertions or claims about them made by others'.
chapter: Starting out: Creating a professional social media account
themes: fairness; right of reply; editorial standards; completeness
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Washington Post definition of fairness as editorial standard

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A clear summary that tells the story quickly. In other words, the 'inverted pyramid' structure recommended in traditional newswriting. Avoid 'setting the scene' unless you are writing a longform piece.
themes: inverted pyramid; story structure; scannability; web writing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Inverted pyramid persists as default online structure

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When it comes to news tweets, quotes and digits can be more effective than videos in leading to retweets. It may be that quotes indicate original information (rather than the reporter's second-hand understanding) and digits indicate facts (rather than opinion). Again, it's all about scarcity and standing out from the noise on social.
chapter: Use quotes and digits where you can
themes: numbers; quotes; social media; original information; scarcity
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Digits as trust signal -- implies data-backed claims have inherent social media advantage

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The Washington Post found that photo galleries (without sound) performed better during the day while people were at work, while video was watched more in the evening when users might not have to worry about colleagues hearing what they were watching.
themes: audience behaviour; format-timing interaction; publishing strategy
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Format effectiveness varies by time of day -- data-driven finding

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Among Republicans, compared with quotes, 'embedded tweets were unique in eliciting positive emotions which mediated higher ratings of Donald Trump's warmth and competence', potentially because of non-textual information such as the author's profile image; likes and retweets signalling community support; or elements that would not be part of a quote, such as hashtags. In other words, by embedding the tweet, you may be sacrificing editorial control over certain elements around how the words are presented.
themes: framing effects; editorial control; embedding; presentation bias
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Research evidence that presentation format (embed vs quote) changes audience perception

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can use these metrics to see if different approaches to subject lines, style, and structure make it more likely that subscribers open newsletters or click through to links. Some email platforms allow you to do A/B testing---sending two different versions of a newsletter ('A' and 'B') and seeing which one performs best.
themes: A/B testing; analytics; audience metrics; iteration
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Newsletter context but method generalises to any content experiment

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is essential that you differentiate your coverage in some way from any other liveblogs based on your audience. Differentiation is done partly through style and emphasis, but largely in where you focus your preparation: what is your expertise and how do you develop and deploy that live?
themes: audience; differentiation; editorial judgment; expertise
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Liveblogs are perceived as being more balanced or factual than traditional articles 'because of the range of opinions they present, the links they provide to sources and supporting documents, and their neutral tone'
themes: transparency; sourcing; credibility; audience trust
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Cites Thurman 2014

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Some ideas for different types of content you can switch between: Summarising and clarifying: what did all those words actually boil down to? Contextualising (politically): why are they making that statement? Contextualising (facts): what's the background to that statement? Factchecking: are they representing the facts accurately? Linking: is there a report that the statement is based on?
themes: fact-checking; contextualisation; summarisation; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Liveblog update taxonomy applicable to any real-time reporting

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The bottom of the 'value chain' in liveblogging is simply documenting what is happening. But if there are lots of other people doing this too, you may want to spend at least some of your time filtering and aggregating (curating) the most informative and insightful updates. Moving up the value chain, you should also consider adding some analysis of what is happening. You might decide to enrich coverage by adding audio, video, or other multimedia. Finally, if the event involves disputed facts or versions of events, you might seek to verify that information.
themes: value chain; curation; analysis; verification; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Four-level hierarchy: document > filter/aggregate > analyse > verify

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A good liveblog is well prepared---just as a good TV or radio programme is the result of days spent lining up guests, researching key talking points, and preparing key visuals or sounds. Information you should be seeking out for different types of events include: Agendas, schedules, and routes; Documents: presentations, transcripts, and reports are often published online by organisations in advance of meetings and conferences; Jargon; Systems---how do they work?; People and places.
themes: preparation; background research; documents; planning
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Preparation checklist for live reporting

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Contacts, for example, are key: knowing about people is one thing; knowing people is another. Make sure you have some good contacts in the organisations involved: ideally speak to them before the event so you have some quotes ready to go and they know who you are if you phone them during the event itself.
themes: source cultivation; contacts; preparation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Don't assume anything you read during live coverage is true. Try to go to the source of any facts or claims where possible, or speak to experts and other sources. Often liveblogs allow you to publish without going through an editor---so it's up to you to get things right the first time.
themes: verification; accuracy; editorial responsibility; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Your standards of accuracy should be the same whatever medium you report on. Liveblogs and mobile journalism place increased pressure on you as a journalist to 'hit the deadline', but you also have increased opportunities and resources to verify the information that you come across.
themes: accuracy; standards; verification; speed vs accuracy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you come across information that has not been verified, you might sometimes judge that your audience may be able to help with this. Acknowledging that a piece of information is circulating and putting that into context is important---especially when everyone else can see the same information too. You should continue to update on the status of that piece of information, and seek to check it yourself.
themes: transparency; crowdsourced verification; context; uncertainty
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Audience as verification resource; transparency about unverified claims

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Official statements should be treated with scepticism, too. Sometimes journalists should have known the statements were false. Sometimes the claims were potentially dangerous. Sometimes the wording may give a false impression, even while being strictly true: a politician will often say they are spending 'more than ever' on something, but they may still be spending less per person (because of there are more people than before) or less in real terms (because of inflation).
themes: scepticism; official sources; misleading statistics; inflation adjustment; per-capita
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Specific example of numeracy in verification: absolute vs per-capita vs real-terms

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The 'truth sandwich' approach to reporting what officials are saying. Suggested by linguist George Lakoff, it involves beginning your report with the truth before describing the false statement---then repeating the truth again after it. For example, you might report 'Smith falsely claims that her department brought the Olympic games to the city. The bid was actually won by her predecessor, Toni Jones'.
themes: framing; fact-checking; truth sandwich; misinformation; presentation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Lakoff's truth sandwich technique

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The internet gives us extra tools to verify information---certainly more than the phone ever did. Every piece of information, and every person, leaves a trail of data that you can use to build a picture of its reliability. Broadly speaking, there are three levels to verifying any information online: 1. The content, 2. The context, 3. And the code underpinning that.
themes: verification framework; content-context-code; digital trails; reliability
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Three-level verification framework: content, context, code

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Content verification considerations: Is it linkbait or clickbait? Recency: is it up to date? Frequency: does the source update regularly? Style and personality: does it match what you would expect from this source? For images, is there potential cloning or airbrushing?
themes: content verification; recency; frequency; style analysis; image manipulation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: First level of the content-context-code framework

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Context verification: What is the provenance of the content? Where did it come from? When was it first published? Can archives and caches provide information on how the page has changed---and is that suspicious? What pages are linking to the content? Does the site domain match what it purports to be? Can you correlate the information from other, independent, sources?
themes: provenance; context verification; cross-referencing; domain checking; archives
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Second level of the content-context-code framework

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Code verification: Does the source code provide any clues to the history of the page and its authors? Use WHOIS services to find out when the domain was registered and by who. For emails, see if the IP address can provide extra information. For wikis, check the history and discussion pages. For documents, check the metadata to see if that can offer further clues to the date of creation, author, and changes.
themes: code verification; WHOIS; metadata; document forensics; wiki history
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Third level of the content-context-code framework

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The more hoaxes you are familiar with, the more likely alarm bells are going to ring at the right time: the website Snopes.com keeps track of the most widely shared ones, and they often share similar qualities. The frequency and recency of information will give you a clue as to its veracity: the more recent the information, the more up to date it is likely to be (although it may be based on out-of-date information---trace it back to its source).
chapter: Questions to ask of content: Too good to be true?
themes: hoax detection; pattern recognition; recency; provenance tracing
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The Associated Press's Fergus Bell recommends separating the source and the content when verifying information. Steps: UNVERIFIED---Establish earliest instance (provenance). Then VERIFY THE SOURCE (review social history, make contact, ask questions, secure permission) and VERIFY THE CONTENT (translate text and audio, consult independent experts, seek separate confirmation, establish context). VERIFIED: ensure source and content consistent.
chapter: Questions to ask of context: Verifying social media accounts
themes: verification workflow; AP method; source-content separation; provenance
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Fergus Bell / Associated Press verification model

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Social media account verification questions: How long has the account existed? If it's only existed since a relevant story broke then it's likely to be opportunistic. Who did the person first follow or friend? Who first followed them? Who has spoken to them online? Who has spoken about them? Can you correlate this account with others?
chapter: Questions to ask of context: Verifying social media accounts
themes: social media verification; account age; network analysis; correlation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Use the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine (web.archive.org) to search for the URL of the page, see how long the page has existed, and look at older versions of the page in question, which might include giveaway information that has since been deleted.
chapter: Questions to ask of context: Verifying social media accounts
themes: Wayback Machine; web archives; provenance; deleted content
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The code behind a webpage can offer important clues. To see this, right-click on the page and select the option to view the source code. You can also use a WHOIS service to find out about the registration of the website. If you are asking for emails verifying a story, make sure you are forwarded the original email, and not a screengrab, and check the IP address of the email against who it's supposed to be from.
themes: source code inspection; WHOIS; email verification; IP address; digital forensics
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When the UK's Conservative Party sent a letter from 'more than 5,000 small business owners', one observer noted that the letter's metadata showed that it had originated from Conservative Central HQ. Once people realised this, they dug into the names on the letter and found dozens of problems including businesses that did not exist, duplicate names, people who were not business owners at all, and people who had never signed the letter.
themes: metadata forensics; document provenance; data quality; astroturfing
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Case study: metadata analysis leading to data-driven debunking

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The first thing you should ever do when you see an image is use a reverse search engine like Google Images or TinEye to find out if other versions of this image have appeared online. If you can't find any copies, look for cloning and airbrushing. Look for details that don't match up with the place or date that the video or photo was supposed to be of: you can search for weather reports in that place and time, or other images of that spot (using Google Street View).
themes: reverse image search; image verification; geolocation; weather cross-check; cloning detection
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can find out more about an image or video by checking the EXIF data: this is information about when the image was taken, on which camera, and with what settings. Jeffrey's Exif Viewer allows you to quickly see the EXIF data on any web-based image. That information can also be used when speaking to the person who claims to have taken the image, to check if they can tell you what time it was taken (although remember that EXIF data can be faked too).
themes: EXIF data; image metadata; verification tools; timestamp checking
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: Tools: Jeffrey's Exif Viewer, JpegSnoop, Forensically, Ghiro, Error Level Analysis

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you do post a liveblog update that later proves to be wrong: Capture the error---take a screenshot. Publicly acknowledge the error. Reference the error in the correction. Notify those who shared the error. Repeat the correction. Media outlets should correct online versions of their stories directly (with appropriate disclosures of how they were changed) rather than posting corrections at the end (which are likely to be ineffective).
chapter: What to do when you get it wrong: Correcting errors
themes: corrections; error handling; transparency; accountability
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Gibbs 2011 correction protocol + New America Foundation recommendation

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A typical mobile journalism workflow: 1. We need a scene picture immediately. 2. We need six to eight paragraphs of colour or scene-setting. 3. A 10-15 second video clip of the scene. 4. Five more pictures so we have enough for a gallery. 5. Then a constant stream of copy. 6. Then find out more information about the victim, speak to people who knew him, and ask for permission to use images they have of him.
themes: mobile journalism; field workflow; multimedia gathering; sequencing
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Jonny Greatrex's six-step mojo protocol

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Set out with a clear set of objectives---a shopping list---this will make it easier to edit: if you head out without a clear idea of what you want, you'll come back with far too much audio and risk missing the point of the piece. Strong audio clips featuring good quotes stand out a mile but make a note while conducting interviews to save time before the edit.
themes: audio gathering; planning; efficiency; note-taking
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Sian Grzeszczyk, BBC Radio

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The most common mistakes I see people make include trying to gather the material too quickly---which usually means going to the obvious sources---following what has already been written or covered on a story; and tending to overvalue material gathered early in the production process. In terms of planning, it's easy to undercalculate the amount of time needed post-gathering in terms of writing/mixing/reworking. And to not spend enough time on the writing element, which can make or break a project.
chapter: Hugh Levinson, head of longform audio
themes: source diversity; recency bias; time management; editorial process; obvious-source trap
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Hugh Levinson, BBC head of longform audio

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It's also important not to become fixated on one interpretation of a story---you need to be sufficiently self-questioning.
chapter: Hugh Levinson, head of longform audio
themes: confirmation bias; self-questioning; interpretation; reflexivity
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One of the most common mistakes made by new podcasters is to assume two friends chatting is enough to make a good podcast. To make your podcast stand apart, it needs to have unique content, that is, hard work on research. One of the easiest ways to make your podcast stand out is to have guests who have already done that research: experts and academics, people with interesting experiences or skills.
themes: research depth; expert sources; differentiation; content quality
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When editing, remember to: Cut with care---be fair and balanced. Don't cut the context of a quote. Select the introductory audio to give meaning to the piece, don't puzzle the listener. Don't cut the character of the piece---a pause or a stumble may be telling.
themes: editing ethics; context preservation; fairness; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Applies to any editing of source material, not just audio

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Many podcasts will publish a transcript of the show on their website and there's a good reason for this: it makes it much easier for search engines to understand your content and therefore makes your content easier for people to find. It also makes your content more accessible, and easier for people to share quotes if they want to.
themes: transcription; accessibility; SEO; discoverability
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sometimes the sound that travels furthest is audio that was made for another purpose. Calls to the police and hotlines, voicemails, and speeches are often widely shared, described as 'bits of audio that serve as evidence in a news story.'

relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Just because we can use video, doesn't mean we should. Knowing when to use video, why you're using it, and how to do it well is what this chapter is all about. Understanding why some broadcast techniques don't work online requires thinking about why those techniques were invented in the first place. Television has no choice in using video, so it has had to invent a number of ways to 'make the news visual'. These tricks are not needed online: if your story isn't visual, don't use video.
themes: medium selection; fitness for purpose; platform conventions; visual storytelling
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Video tends to play one of four roles online: Some video is used to illustrate a text-based story, much as a photo might do. Some video is used to add to a story, much as a link might do. Another way video might be used is to distil and tease a story, much as a headline does. The final type of video is one that simply tells the story---it stands alone.
chapter: Step 1: Coming up with online video ideas---and planning them
themes: video roles taxonomy; illustrate; supplement; tease; standalone; medium selection
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Four-role framework: illustrate, add to, distil/tease, tell

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Edited features and news packages need research and planning. Think about the people, the places, the action. Before setting out make a checklist: What's the story? What pictures will you need? Have you researched interviews in advance? Think about balance, right of reply, and visual libel. Think about third-party footage---the preponderance of cameras now means that you may find useful footage from other people---or even a CCTV camera.
chapter: Step 1: Coming up with online video ideas---and planning them

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: We will do a lot of what we call versioning: we might make a ten-minute film that is great on YouTube that we might promote on-site, but we might cut it down to three minutes for Facebook. Every project has seven different exports for different places. One of the things that I talk to my team about a lot is not just to think about the video itself but think about how the video lives on different platforms.

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When it comes to captions, I remember being told at university to write for the comprehension level of a nine-year-old child because of the way that people mentally digest when they're seeing and hearing a story. It's really important to be simple, so I have a two-line rule with text-on-screen videos. Think of each word as valuable real estate, and remember the language needs to be simple.
chapter: Samara Mackereth, HuffPost UK head of video
themes: captions; simplicity; readability; text-on-screen; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Samara Mackereth, HuffPost UK

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Shoot to edit. For example, you might be covering a protest. A shot of people chanting with placards only tells one part of the story. Think about the composition of visuals that will tell the story and give it context: footage of the school and its location, close-ups of individuals and their placard messages, interviews, a shot that shows how big the demonstration is, and the reaction of bystanders.
themes: shoot to edit; visual storytelling; composition; context gathering
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The first questions that anyone should ask before trying the platform are the same as with any new tool: What kind of users has the platform? Are they an audience we want to connect with? How is the feature going to help us get closer to our goals? Have other media outlets used the tool? How? What were the results? Does the tool need to be updated frequently? How does it fit into the newsroom's workflow?
themes: platform evaluation; tool assessment; audience fit; workflow integration
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Carla Pedret, Catalunya Radio. Generic tool-adoption framework.

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: NPR has used audio from Twitter Spaces to produce podcast episodes and as a source for new stories. 'To me, social audio is an in-real-time survey where we can gain feedback and hopefully reach new audience members.'
themes: audience feedback; source discovery; repurposing; social audio
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Matt Adams, NPR Engagement Editor. Via Scire 2022.

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: When approaching a longform audio project, you need to be able to discern whether it will sustain a longer piece. Asking questions like whether there is sufficient complexity in the story, a human angle, or a way of shaping/formatting the story to make it original. On top of that there should be a clear idea of what the piece will say, or at least what will make it distinctive: a budget, which will control the amount of time allotted to the project: and an early consideration of how to achieve extra impact.
chapter: Hugh Levinson, head of longform audio
themes: project scoping; complexity assessment; budget; impact planning; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Hugh Levinson, BBC

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Shifting the focus of coverage 'from what the politician is saying to what his government is doing.' The experience of misleading official statements has led many news organisations to begin adopting the 'truth sandwich' approach.
themes: framing; official sources; accountability journalism; actions over words
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Jay Rosen 2020 recommendation. Complements truth sandwich extract.

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data journalism can involve all sorts of skills and people, from those who are good at getting information in the first place to those who can pull stories out of it (analysis), those who can write the stories, tell those stories visually (data visualisation), or make them interactive. The key to data journalism is to start with simple projects that build different types of skills.
themes: skills; workflow; incremental learning
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Frames data journalism as multi-role pipeline; advocates learning by doing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data journalism ideas start in exactly the same way as every other journalism idea: You are either reacting to new information (in this case, data) or you have an idea---perhaps based on a lead or tip-off---and decide to seek out the information that allows you to write a story about that.
chapter: Coming up with ideas for data-driven journalism
themes: story ideas; reactive vs proactive; hypothesis
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Establishes reactive/proactive dichotomy for data story generation

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Having a hypothesis helps: be prepared to change your hypothesis as you start to look at the evidence. Sometimes that hypothesis can be as simple as 'things are working as they are supposed to'. Are things that are supposed to go down, going down? Are things that are supposed to be getting better doing just that? Do people have access to the services and opportunities they are supposed to?
chapter: Coming up with ideas for data-driven journalism
themes: hypothesis testing; accountability; falsification
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Hypothesis as a starting point that must yield to evidence

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Another form of a proactive story is to come up with an idea to follow up on a current news story, providing a data-driven angle on the issue.
