A national announcement about child poverty, for example, may give you the idea to follow up with an analysis of regional data to give readers a local picture of the situation. A sports team struggling may be the inspiration for a historical overview of their decline over time. A politician's claim about immigration may be factchecked against the reality.
chapter: Coming up with ideas for data-driven journalism
themes: follow-up; localization; fact-checking; timeliness
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Three follow-up patterns: localize, historicize, factcheck

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Look at lots of examples of data journalism as well: often you can adapt one idea in a number of different ways. If the story was done about one country or region, for example, why not repeat it for a different place? If it was focused on a particular category, see if you can repeat it for a different category; or see if you can adapt the idea for a different field entirely. You can also simply take an idea that was done a year or two ago and repeat it with newer figures. If an idea has worked before, there's a good chance it will work again.
chapter: Coming up with ideas for data-driven journalism
themes: replication; adaptation; story patterns
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Replication as a legitimate and productive idea-generation strategy

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data is regularly used to tell stories about how big or small a problem is; to identify whether things are getting better or worse; to identify unfair variation in people's experiences; and to identify people, places, or organisations that are doing particularly badly---or well. Scale stories, Change stories, Ranking stories, Variation stories tend to be the basis of news articles. Exploratory, Relationship, Needle in the haystack, and Bad data/No data/Get the data stories tend to be used more for features and in-depth reporting.
chapter: Eight types of stories you can tell
themes: story typology; angle selection; news values
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Eight-type taxonomy: Scale, Change, Ranking, Variation, Exploratory, Relationship, Needle-in-haystack, Bad/No/Get data

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One emerging branch of data journalism that has a particular focus on Variation stories is 'algorithmic accountability' reporting. ProPublica has been a leader in this field: its Machine Bias series looks at the power that algorithms have on people's lives, often leading to a variation of outcomes for people where you would want fairness: people being sentenced differently, or given different insurance quotes, despite no meaningful difference between them on the dimensions that matter.
chapter: Eight types of stories you can tell
themes: algorithmic accountability; variation; fairness; bias
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Algorithmic accountability as a variation story subtype

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Stories about problems with data quality can be incredibly important: power is exercised, money spent, and lives affected on the basis of data, so if the data is flawed then the exercise of power is likely to be flawed too. If a police force is underreporting crime, a hurricane-hit country is inaccurately reporting deaths, or tests sent are being counted as 'tests conducted', then there is the risk of those in power making false claims unchallenged. Story ideas can come from checking those claims, from hearing about someone who has been through the system and seen its gaps, or from simply scrutinising existing data for problematic features.
chapter: Eight types of stories you can tell
themes: data quality; accountability; power; bad data
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Data quality critique as journalism; flawed data enables flawed power

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sometimes there is no data at all---and instead of thinking that this is the end of your story, consider that it might be the beginning. Are there concerns being raised about the lack of information or transparency that you can report on? If it's not easy for people to get hold of important data, you might also work on a project to collect the data yourself and make that available to readers.
chapter: Eight types of stories you can tell
themes: data gaps; transparency; data collection; public interest
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Absence of data is itself newsworthy; journalists as data creators

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A great way to get data for exclusive stories is to use Right to Information (RTI) laws. When requesting data through an FOI request, it is always useful to specify the format that you wish the information to be supplied in---typically, a spreadsheet in electronic format if you want to avoid having to convert PDFs or Word documents. You should also request as much detail as possible: try asking for breakdowns by incident, day, week, or month rather than aggregate totals.
themes: FOI; data format; granularity; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Practical FOI advice: specify spreadsheet format; request granular breakdowns

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you are using a search engine to find the data you are looking for, it can help to specify the type of file you want (e.g. spreadsheet) and the type of domain it is on (e.g. government sites). You can do this by using the advanced search facility or you can use search operators such as filetype: or site:. Examples: 'School spending filetype:xls', 'disclosure log site:ac.uk', 'hate crime site:police.uk filetype:pdf'.
chapter: Using search engines to find data
themes: search operators; data discovery; advanced search
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Concrete search operator patterns for data discovery

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: There are two basic approaches you can take in gathering data: compiling it from other sources such as newspapers or other reports (secondary research) or collecting it yourself through methods such as observation or surveys (primary research). Whichever method you use, you should understand potential weaknesses in the methodology and seek to address these. The selection, size, and generalisability of the sample; the selection and phrasing of questions; the environment; the use of a control group; your own presence; and myriad other factors must be considered to ensure that your methods stand up to scrutiny.
themes: primary research; secondary research; methodology; sampling; validity
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Journalists must understand and address methodological weaknesses in data they gather

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sometimes news organisations use live data in their data journalism, particularly in sports and elections. This requires a certain amount of preparation and organisation: you'll need to identify where you will get the data from, and how you might filter it. If the live source is one that the public can contribute to themselves---such as a social media feed---you'll need to consider whether people might try to 'game' that data, intentionally skewing the results for political reasons, commercial gain, or simply for fun.
themes: live data; gaming; data integrity; planning
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Live data requires pre-planning and gaming-risk assessment

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sentiment analysis has a number of weaknesses: it is not always good at detecting irony or sarcasm. You should also be careful not to suggest that public sentiment on social media is the same as public opinion in general (social media users are not representative of the wider population---and stronger opinions are over-represented). Any coverage should make these weaknesses clear and be careful that results are not presented as representative of the general public.
themes: sentiment analysis; representativeness; social media bias; limitations
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Methodological caution about sentiment analysis and social media representativeness

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: 'Scraping' is the process of automatically gathering information from webpages or online documents. It can be especially useful in data journalism because it allows you to combine data from potentially thousands of webpages into one easy-to-analyse spreadsheet. To scrape information, it must be in a consistent format. So, for example, results from searching a jobs database can be scraped because each page of results has the same structure but results from different job listings websites cannot because each site will use its own way of presenting that information.
themes: scraping; automation; data collection; consistency
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Scraping requires structural consistency in source pages

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Sometimes data will need to be 'cleaned' in some way so that it can be properly or accurately analysed. Examples of issues that require cleaning include: misspellings or variations in spelling; numbers that are being treated as words (or vice versa); unnecessary columns or rows; duplicate entries; mistyped entries; mixed data that you want to separate. Finding out if your data is dirty might involve checking that the spreadsheet software understands things the same way you do: a computer will see 'New Town', 'Newtown', and 'newtown' as three separate towns when they may not be.
themes: data cleaning; dirty data; data quality; consistency
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Taxonomy of common data cleaning issues

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Some tips for cleaning your data include: Use a spellchecker to check for misspellings. Use 'find and replace' to remove double spaces and other common punctuation errors. Remove duplicate entries. Watch out for extra spaces at the beginning and end of text. Sort your data in numerical columns from largest to smallest: look out for unusually small or large numbers that may be errors. If you want to split an address or name into separate columns, try using the Text to Columns option under the Data menu in Excel.
themes: data cleaning; spreadsheet techniques; outlier detection; deduplication
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Practical cleaning checklist with specific tool techniques

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: For cleaning challenges that take up a lot of time, it is worth exploring a dedicated cleaning tool like Open Refine. Open Refine's import wizard allows you to easily merge heading rows, ignore rows before your headings, remove empty rows, and even merge sheets or files. Once imported, you can also perform 'common transforms' such as removing double spaces and inconsistent formatting across an entire dataset or use the 'cluster and edit' option to identify entries that are similar and mass-edit them.
themes: Open Refine; clustering; data cleaning; transformation
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: Open Refine recommended for complex cleaning; cluster-and-edit for fuzzy matching

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Once you have the data in front of you, it can be easy to be overwhelmed by the possibilities. So before you begin questioning your data, decide what the most important newsworthy aspect of the data is for your audience---and how much time you have to do the story. If your audience is local, check the scale of the issue for local organisations. If your audience is national, identifying broader trends may be more relevant, and have more impact. Make sure you stay focused on finishing one story before you move on to the others.
themes: prioritization; audience; newsworthiness; focus; time management
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Must prioritize analysis angles by audience and deadline before diving in

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can ask questions about data in a number of ways. Common techniques used to ask questions in a spreadsheet table include: Sorting the table by a particular column to bring the biggest or smallest values to the top; Filtering by one or more columns to focus on a particular category or area; Performing basic calculations, such as subtracting one figure from another to find out how much something has changed; Using 'wizards' such as a pivot table; Using spreadsheet functions to perform more advanced calculations such as averages.
themes: sorting; filtering; calculations; pivot tables; spreadsheet analysis
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Five core spreadsheet interrogation techniques ordered by complexity

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Simple calculations that can give you stories as a journalist include: Subtracting an older figure from a recent figure to find out how much something has changed; Dividing that change by the older figure to find out what percentage something has changed by; Dividing a part by the whole to find out what proportion it is of that whole.
themes: calculations; percentage change; proportion; spreadsheet formulas
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Three fundamental journalistic calculations: change, percent change, proportion

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One of the most powerful ways to use a spreadsheet to ask questions about data is to use a pivot table. This is useful when you are dealing with very detailed data---for example, with hundreds of rows representing lots of different events or organisations. A pivot table will take that detailed data and organise it by a particular piece of information in your data.
themes: pivot tables; aggregation; spreadsheet analysis
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: Pivot tables for aggregating detailed data by category

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One of the most important things to look for in your data is what isn't in it. Whenever you are dealing with a total number of events for each part of a country, you can expect that the most populated areas will generally have the most of anything. Those organisations and locations are likely to feature at the top of any ranking---so you should always look to find out how they rank per person instead (events 'per capita'). To do that you simply need to divide the number of events or items by the number of people.
themes: per capita; normalization; context; population; missing data
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Always normalize by population; look for what is absent from the dataset

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Always try to speak to experts to find out if there are other contextual factors that might be important as well. 'The ability for people to get in touch and explain to me the why is super important---I'm not saying I'm going to accept those anecdotes at face value but it gives me new lines of enquiry' (Mona Chalabi).
themes: expert sources; contextual factors; qualitative context; verification
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Chalabi: anecdotes from affected people generate new lines of inquiry

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Another important aspect of context is how the data was gathered. Data scientist Heather Krause recommends creating a data biography for data journalism projects that details where the data came from, who collected it, why they collected it, and how. The data biography covers: Funding (who paid for it), Motivation (why collected), Project design (sampling, classification, cleaning), Data collection and sourcing (methodologies), Analysis (methods and assumptions), Interpretation (discretion in focus or classification), Communication (selective emphasis, language, chart design).
themes: data biography; provenance; bias; methodology audit; data source evaluation
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Krause's 7-factor data biography framework for source evaluation

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Just because two things happen together, you cannot prove that they are related. During the first few months of the Covid-19 pandemic, there was a strong positive correlation between the wealth of a country and the number of deaths from the virus. The reason wasn't that wealthy countries were worse at fighting the virus---it was that wealthy countries were better at diagnosing people with the virus and collecting data on it. Normally to establish causation you need to be able to control various factors (called variables), and typically journalists cannot do that.
themes: correlation; causation; confounding variables; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Journalists typically cannot control variables needed to establish causation

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: There are actually three different types of average: the mean is what you get when you add up all values and divide by the number of people. But mean averages can be misleading: an average wage might be skewed by a few very big earners at the top. The median is a much better average when dealing with man-made figures: this is the mid-point at which half of values are higher and half are lower. Finally, there is the mode: the most commonly occurring value. If you are in any doubt about your figures, you should double-check your findings wherever possible with statisticians in the field you're covering.
themes: mean; median; mode; statistical literacy; averages
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Median preferred over mean for skewed distributions; verify with statisticians

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Margin of error indicates how accurate the data is: if someone says that the number of people unemployed in your town is 14,000 but the margin of error is plus/minus 2000, then the real figure could be anywhere from 12,000 to 16,000. Most data is based on samples: the larger the sample, the more accurate the results are going to be when generalised to the wider population. But it is still generalising, and that introduces room for error. In fact, even the margin of error is typically only the range within which the survey is expected to be right 19 times out of 20.
themes: margin of error; confidence interval; sampling; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Margin of error only 95% confidence; sample-based data always involves generalization

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: To merge data, the two datasets you wish to combine will need a common field---a column of data that they both share. If you have a choice, codes are generally better for matching than names. This is because names may be written differently in different datasets: a police force that might be referred to as Avon and Somerset in one could be called Avon and Somerset in the other. That slight difference will be enough to stop you from getting a match for that particular row.
themes: data merging; join keys; VLOOKUP; data matching; codes vs names
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Prefer codes over names as join keys to avoid matching failures from spelling variations

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The key thing when writing up a data story is to try to make the numbers relevant and meaningful---especially if they are big numbers. A story about parking charges generating 3.9 million for hospitals did not lead on that large but abstract figure---it ran under the headline Nurses charged almost 200 a month just to park their cars at work. And when the Evening Standard wrote about attacks by prisoners on wardens, they led on the five attacks every day rather than the 4000 attacks in the previous two years. Big numbers without context can be misleading.
chapter: Narrating: Writing a story that uses data
themes: writing; humanizing numbers; relevance; framing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Translate abstract big numbers into relatable everyday measures

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A good data story should also have more than just numbers. You should seek out quotes too: if it affects real people, it is important to hear about their experiences so the reader can understand why the figures matter. If it needs explaining further or contextualising, seek out experts to interview. If the story is critical or negative, you should seek a response to the figures from the organisations responsible. A spokesperson might suggest a mistake has been made in the calculations---but do not take their word for it! Check your calculations and consult an expert if you are not sure.
chapter: Narrating: Writing a story that uses data
themes: right of reply; expert quotes; verification; story structure
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Data stories need human voices and right-of-reply; never trust spokesperson rebuttals unchecked

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Many good data stories do not start with numbers---they start with people or, in the jargon of the newsroom, a case study. If your reporting leads you to victims of a particular issue, and their stories are compelling, then be prepared to put the data into the background and focus on them. Strong case studies can bring a data story to life in a way that the numbers will not. In short, the case study tells us why we should care, then the data tells us why this matters.
themes: case studies; humanizing; narrative structure; empathy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Case study = why we care; data = why it matters

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Personalisation is the process of making a story more personal to a reader by helping them to find the aspect of it that relates to them. There are ethical considerations in using personalisation: one of the roles that journalism plays in society is to form bridges between communities and help readers to be aware of the issues affecting communities other than our own. Without personalisation, some audiences may not read about the issue at all; but inviting a user to find out the impact on their own area also has the potential to keep them ignorant of how that issue affects others. Any use of personalisation should attempt to ensure that the resulting story still puts the personal dimension into a bigger context.
themes: personalisation; ethics; audience engagement; context; community
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Tension: personalisation drives engagement but risks parochialism; must contextualize

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Broadly speaking there are two typical reasons for visualising data: To find a story (or allow readers to find them) and To tell a story. The key to successful data visualisation is to be clear about what sort of story you are trying to tell: opting for fancy impressive chart types is pointless if the charts do not actually work. Broadly speaking there are four types of stories you may want to tell with charts: Stories about composition, Stories about comparison, Stories about relationships, And stories about distribution.
themes: data visualization; chart selection; story types; composition; comparison; distribution
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Four visualization story types: composition, comparison, relationship, distribution

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: In some cases, your visualisation may not be trying to tell a story, but instead helping the user to find their own story in the data. This type of chart is known as exploratory visualisation (as opposed to explanatory). The general principle when designing such visualisations is sometimes known as the Visual Information Seeking Mantra: Overview first, filter, then zoom (Schneiderman 1996), meaning that a good exploratory visualisation should provide an overview of the whole to begin with, but then the facility for a user to filter and zoom in to a particular point, which then reveals more details.
themes: exploratory visualization; explanatory visualization; Schneiderman mantra; overview-filter-zoom
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Exploratory vs explanatory distinction; Schneiderman mantra as design principle

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Double source data wherever possible---even the UN and WorldBank can make mistakes. Take information out---stories are about clear threads with extraneous information fuzzed out. Journalism is about telling stories. You can only truly do that when you mask out the irrelevant or minor data. Avoid standard abstract units---tons of carbon, billions of dollars---try to rework or process units down to everyday measures. All graphs, charts, and infographics should be self-sufficient: each should have a clear title, legend, source, labels. Show your workings---Google Drive makes it incredibly easy to share your data and thought processes with readers.
themes: McCandless tips; double-sourcing; simplification; transparency; self-sufficiency
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: David McCandless: 5 tips including double-source, simplify, everyday units, self-sufficiency, show workings

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Just because you have some geographical data does not mean you should map it. Maps are designed for a purpose. They tell particular types of stories well---but not all of them. If the story is not about the visible distribution or clustering of those points, but about comparison or composition, you are probably better using a bar chart or a pie chart. A map can actually give a misleading impression of comparison due to varying population density: a large area with fewer people will take up more space on your map, and be easier to see, than a smaller area with more people.
themes: maps; chart selection; population density; misleading visualization
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Maps mislead when area size does not correlate with population; use only for distribution/clustering stories

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The journalist must also consider whether to exclude any data points from the chart---or put another way, which elements to focus on---for the purposes of clarity. A bar chart with 100 bars, or a pie chart with 75 slices, is not going to be easy for someone to understand. Filter and simplify the data to deliver the essence of the data to your intended audience. It makes a distinction between the research stage when the journalist is gathering more data to conduct more in-depth analysis and the edit phase when it is important to assess whether all your extra information buries the main point of the story or enhances it.
themes: data filtering; simplification; chart editing; clarity; research vs edit phase
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: WSJ Guide: distinguish research phase (gather all) from edit phase (simplify to essence)

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Instead of having a rainbow of colours for data points, choose one eye-catching colour for your story element (the column, line, or slice that your story focuses on) and a less striking colour for all the other points. The reader's eye will naturally go to the red---the point of contrast---which is the point of the story. Before choosing a colour consider any meanings that colour may have: red can signify danger and negativity for Western audiences. Remember that colour's interpretations are culturally specific.
themes: colour; contrast; cultural sensitivity; chart design; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Use color for contrast not decoration; one highlight color plus neutral; consider cultural color meanings

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Start with the title: instead of writing a title that merely labels the data, write the title as you would a headline, telling us a story. Instead of Price of chocolate bars 2000-2020, write Chocolate bars double in price in 20 years. If it is an exploratory chart or map, use the title to invite the reader to explore. The footer is important: it is the place where you should indicate the source of the data, the time period covered, and what aspect of the data you focused on if you needed to leave data out.
chapter: Tips on editing chart and map text
themes: chart titles; headlines; source attribution; footer; labeling
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Chart title = headline that tells the story; footer for source, scope, caveats

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data journalists are increasingly using annotations to create a narrative across their visualisations: a simple line chart can be given a beginning, middle, and end by adding an annotation to peaks and troughs explaining what was happening at each point to cause those; a scatterplot might be made more inviting by labelling a few of the most newsworthy points. Most chart tools provide accessibility options including alternative descriptions: make sure you create one.
chapter: Tips on editing chart and map text
themes: annotations; narrative; accessibility; alt text; chart design
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Annotations create narrative arc on charts; always add alt text for accessibility

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: With bar charts, the y-axis should always have a baseline of zero: charts that start from other points can be misleading because they make differences between amounts look more pronounced than they actually are. Line charts do not have to start from zero---but starting a line chart from zero can be as misleading as not starting a bar chart from zero: showing changes in global temperatures on a zero-based scale can make those changes look minimal. Instead, try to pick a point that is meaningful and relevant.
themes: y-axis baseline; scale selection; misleading charts; bar charts; line charts
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Bar charts: always zero baseline. Line charts: choose meaningful baseline; zero can also mislead.

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: With time-based axes, consider if the timescale is an appropriate one: sometimes organisations will choose a timescale that starts from a high or a low in order to make change look worse than it is. Decide in advance what your timescale is going to be, based on what data is available: five years---sometimes ten years---is quite common to get a long-term view; three years is sometimes chosen to focus on more recent developments. You might choose a timescale that relates to the story being focused on: a period covering two different governments being in power, for example.
themes: timescale; x-axis; cherry-picking; temporal scope
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Cherry-picked timescales distort; choose scale that matches editorial purpose transparently

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Fancy 3D effects are considered bad practice in data circles. This is because they add extra visual information that distracts from the information being conveyed through the other dimensions of the chart. In fact, it can actually distort the chart by adding perspective that can make angles look narrower. Adding 3D is like adding overly long words to a news report: it might show off your skills---but it will make the story harder for a reader to understand.
themes: 3D effects; chart distortion; visual noise; best practices
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: 3D effects distort perception of chart values; avoid

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The best data visualisation does not just present data---it presents a narrative by using sequence to create a form of movement. These formats often adopt a narrative structure similar to Schneiderman's Information Seeking Mantra, Overview first, filter, then zoom: starting with a chart that shows the bigger picture and then following that with charts that drill down to specific aspects, highlighting or presenting key figures separately. A second common approach is to tour multiple sources of data instead of different facets of a single dataset. This more episodic structure provides a wider, more varied picture of an issue, sacrificing depth in order to achieve greater breadth.
themes: narrative sequence; overview-filter-zoom; drill-down; episodic structure
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Two narrative structures: drill-down (single dataset) vs episodic tour (multiple datasets)

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Infographics not only use charts and maps but also other graphical and typographical elements such as quotes, photos, and individual numbers in big type. The skill is to combine these using principles of contrast, repetition, alignment, and proximity (sometimes called the CRAP rule): Contrast---pick your most important element and make it much bigger than all the other elements. Repetition---try to make the other smaller elements the same size as each other. Alignment---align different elements with each other. Proximity---do not leave large gaps between elements.
themes: infographics; CRAP rule; layout; contrast; alignment; visual hierarchy
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: CRAP design principles for infographic layout: Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The key with the scrollytell is to ensure that the story and the map or chart work in tandem, both focusing on the same element at the same time, with the scrolling action helping to trigger a transition in the chart that helps the reader focus on the relevant data points. But do not use the scrollytelling format unless there is a good reason for it: if there is not enough detail in the chart to warrant this animation, use a simpler and less time-consuming format instead.
themes: scrollytelling; animation; text-visual alignment; format selection
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Scrollytelling requires text-visual synchronization; only use when data complexity warrants it

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Animation can be used to explain the visuals that the person is seeing by slowing or breaking them down for easier comprehension. The data documentary US Gun Killings In 2018, for example, animates a curved line for each person killed---but the first line is animated slowly, slowing and pausing at the point that the person was killed, in order to help the audience understand what is being shown. And animation can be used to convey uncertainty in the figures being communicated: the New York Times famously used a gauge with a jiggling needle to indicate the predicted gap between votes---the animation was designed to convey that the figures were uncertain, rather than fixed.
themes: animation; uncertainty; pacing; comprehension; visual storytelling
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Animation for: pacing comprehension, conveying uncertainty, showing scale

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Polls conducted via social media cannot tell you, or your audience, anything about the population as a whole---or even the readership of your publication. Research on online polls has found that women are less likely to participate, while polls can be hijacked by people who do not normally read your publication or can be artificially manipulated. It is important to ensure that readers understand the limitations of polls too: if 90 percent of respondents said they agreed with something, you must not report that 90 percent of people agreed with it.
themes: online polls; representativeness; manipulation; statistical literacy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Online polls are non-representative and gameable; never generalize results to population

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: To be reactive, you need to be regularly checking new sources of data, whether that is government data releases, company reports, or press releases. To be proactive, you need to be curious about the world around you, and issues that are already in the news. Identifying forthcoming data releases (most are public) is a good way to get started with simple data journalism techniques.
chapter: Coming up with ideas for data-driven journalism
themes: monitoring; data releases; proactive journalism; story generation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Systematic monitoring of data release calendars as story generation practice

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Needle in the haystack stories involve using datasets to find individual instances of behaviour that may be suspicious or unethical---in other words, a story lead. Often you are already interested in a particular organisation or individual and want to see what you can find out about their activities: there may be an unusual payment buried in the data or a pattern of behaviour that raises questions. The data provides the jumping-off point, and the authority, to begin asking questions---or to identify the key people, places, or companies that you need to start talking to.
chapter: Eight types of stories you can tell
themes: needle in haystack; anomaly detection; investigation; leads
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Data as lead-generation tool: find anomalies, then report traditionally

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Even simple changes to your data can make a big difference. Spreadsheet software assumes that your headings are in your first row: deleting unnecessary rows before those headings, and making sure the headings only fill one row, can help with analysis. Likewise, Excel will assume any empty row is the end of one table and will only sort, filter, or calculate up to that point. Removing empty rows solves that problem.
themes: spreadsheet structure; headers; empty rows; Excel behavior
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: Excel-specific structural cleaning: single header row, no empty rows

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Once you have the main story, you can always do further work on the data to provide other details that give context to that: you might lead on the fact that one particular school has a lot of police callouts, but also find out if callouts across all schools have gone up or down in order to mention that later on in the piece. You might also choose to explore more easily answered questions first in order to hit a tighter deadline, only tackling more challenging ones if you have the time.
themes: story structure; context; deadline management; prioritization
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Lead with main finding, then contextualize; triage analysis by deadline pressure

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Many of my stories---and I think the best---have not started with the data but with an idea, a hypothesis, a question, a curiosity, a similar story that I have read, a different angle for a story, an academic paper, a blog post from an activist, a chat with colleagues. I do not think these ways of coming up with data story ideas differ much from traditional story ideas. The data comes later when solving the questions or approaching the story from a data perspective.
themes: story generation; hypothesis; curiosity; data as method not starting point
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Carmen Aguilar Garcia: data is a method applied to questions, not the origin of questions

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: To map points, you will need as precise a description of locations as possible. Ideally, this should be a latitude and longitude---but if you have postcodes or zip codes you can find tools online that will convert those into latitude and longitude references, a process called geocoding. Bear in mind that these will not be exact, and you may need to clarify that they are only indicative of the general location. Beware of place names that also exist elsewhere: there is a Cambridge, Massachusetts, as well as a Cambridge, England. You may need to check the results and clean up the data to correct any places that have been incorrectly converted.
themes: geocoding; mapping; data quality; disambiguation; location data
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Geocoding introduces errors: ambiguous place names, approximate coordinates need verification

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Pie charts represent a particular challenge: as well as being the most commonly used chart, they are also the most commonly misused. Comparing the slices of a pie chart is much harder than comparing different columns in a column chart, so if you do use a pie chart stick to five or fewer slices by focusing on the top five, or grouping together the rest into a separate category.
themes: pie charts; chart misuse; simplification; best practices
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Limit pie charts to 5 or fewer slices; column charts often better for comparison

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you are working on campaigning journalism you might use data journalism techniques to build tools that empower readers to make informed decisions, find others with shared experiences, or hold power to account. Once the reader has found out how their local school is affected by budget cuts, you might provide a tool for them to email their local politician about it or find online groups campaigning for that school. You might build a tool that allows them to add to a shared dataset, send a Freedom of Information request for information, or categorise hundreds of documents.
chapter: Using data journalism to create tools for audiences
themes: tools for audiences; empowerment; crowdsourcing; campaigning; accountability
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Data journalism tools as civic infrastructure: empower reader action beyond consumption

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: For most journalists, an API can be hard work. If you come across an API while looking for data, see if there is an option to get all the data in a bulk download. If you cannot get that, look for a documentation page that explains how you can query the API for different bits of data and specify the format you get it in. In practical terms, querying an API means generating a URL: when you visit that URL, it will provide the data in a file, often in a format called JSON. JSON looks intimidating but you can convert it into a more traditional spreadsheet by using a free converter.
themes: APIs; JSON; bulk download; data formats; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: Prefer bulk download over API; JSON can be converted to CSV

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Because of the wide variety of skills involved, data journalism often involves collaboration. Look out for mailing lists and meetups where you can ask questions and share your work with others who might be able to build on it. Designers or developers might be interested in creating things with the data you have found, or you might be able to find data collected by others that you can work with.
themes: collaboration; community; skill sharing; interdisciplinary
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Data journalism as inherently collaborative; leverage community resources

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: A useful framework to draw on when thinking about how you approach user-generated content (UGC) is the POST process for social media strategy outlined by Forrester Research. This involves identifying: People: who is the community (or intended community) and where do they congregate online? Objectives: what do you want to achieve? Strategy: how are you going to achieve that? How will relationships with users change? Technology: only when you've explored the first three steps can you decide which technologies to use
chapter: Thinking about community management: The POST process
themes: community engagement; audience research; strategic planning
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Adapted from Forrester Research (Bernoff 2007); technology choices come last

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: One technique is to start with a few key individuals and trace what they do: who do they follow on social media and who follows them? What platforms do they use and what groups do they belong to (search for their username: people tend to use the same one)? What hashtags do they use? What lists are they included on and who else is on those? Who comments on and links to their blog?
themes: audience research; social network analysis; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Snowball-sampling approach applied to community mapping

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If the community is on your own site, Facebook page, or forum, then look at data on their activity: who is most active and when are people generally active? How active are they? What do users do? Do they lurk without contributing or do most people do more than that? Do they stay with the site or forum over a long period of time or is there a high level of 'churn' (people leaving and being replaced by other users)?
themes: audience analytics; community engagement; user behavior
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Data-driven approach to understanding community health

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Malmelin and Villi (2015) identify four different roles that journalists adopt when collaborating with the audience community: Observer, Developer, Facilitator, Curator. When acting as an observer, the journalist monitors the audience community's discussions to identify interests, needs, and concerns. As a developer, they help improve the online platform(s) and service so that users are more likely to contribute. Acting as a facilitator means helping to start and maintain online discussions and feed those back to the editorial team. And being a curator could involve highlighting the best work by members of the community, both online and in print.
themes: community engagement; journalist roles; audience collaboration
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Malmelin and Villi 2015 typology; all four roles relevant to data journalism sourcing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Communities represent a 'strategic resource' for publishers, giving them a competitive edge, generating both symbolic outcomes, such as contributors feeling more important, and 'instrumental' outcomes, in the form of stories.
themes: community engagement; newsroom strategy; audience value
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Lewis, Holton, and Coddington 2013; frames audience as resource not just recipient

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: You can categorise communities into five broad types: Communities of location (the smaller the 'patch', the easier a community can be identified); Professional communities, based on the industry or role that people work in; Communities of interest (hobbies, interests, or lifestyles); Communities of shared objectives (what changes are your audience trying to advance—or prevent?); Communities of issue (what problems or concerns do your audience share?)
themes: audience research; community typology; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Taxonomy useful for scoping data journalism projects around specific audiences

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: What motivates your community clearly links to the content that you might produce for it. Utility-driven communities might be interested in reading tutorials and tips, or interviews that explain how a person achieved success. Socially driven communities might want content that they can share with others, such as the latest gossip. Or they might want content that helps them express a particular identity. Change-driven communities might want to be kept up to date with possible opportunities or threats to that change. And emotionally driven communities may simply want amazing real-life stories, entertaining interviews, or articles that help them to experience new emotions.
themes: audience research; content strategy; community motivation
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Bacon 2009 motivation typology mapped to content formats

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Lewis, Holton, and Coddington distinguish between two useful concepts in community management: direct reciprocity and indirect reciprocity. Direct reciprocity is a simple relationship where two members both benefit from an exchange. Indirect reciprocity, in contrast, refers to acts where an exchange benefits not the giver, but another member of the social network. Direct reciprocity is considered a basic building block of online communities... But indirect reciprocity—one person helping another, who helps another—is the more productive, long-term approach: By working collectively, members expand their capacities to relay information more quickly and accurately, to connect one another with sources, to match resources with individual and community needs, and to build trust.
themes: community engagement; reciprocity; trust building; sourcing
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Lewis, Holton, and Coddington 2013; indirect reciprocity yields better long-term sourcing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The simplest way to find a place in a community is by solving problems. Listen for questions that people are asking or complaints that they make. A key skill of a journalist is to find the answers to questions or get responses to complaints—so that's likely to be one way you can contribute. Those answers and responses, of course, also make for good story ideas and content (which can help you attract other members of the community).
themes: community engagement; story discovery; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Problem-solving as dual-purpose: community building and lead generation

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Richard Millington distinguishes between four different stages in online communities. The inception stage is when a community is just starting. In the establishment stage, the community initiates at least half of the activity. In the maturity stage, 90–99% of activity comes from the community, and there is a strong sense of community with high levels of engagement. Finally, as the community grows too large it might move into the mitosis stage, splitting or growing into more specific groups. Millington suggests that the stage of development should determine where you focus your own activity as a community manager: in the inception stage, he recommends spending 40% of your time on growing that community.
chapter: What stage is your community at?
themes: community engagement; strategic planning; audience development
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Millington's four-stage model; time allocation shifts across stages

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Crowdsourcing is the process of inviting users to help you complete a task: normally finding, interrogating, or classifying information. In journalism it is often used in one of two ways: The 'wisdom of crowds' approach, whereby a news organisation seeks a diverse range of expertise to help solve a problem. And the 'mechanical turk' approach, where a news organisation needs a lot of users to perform a similar task.
themes: crowdsourcing; audience collaboration; data collection
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Two distinct crowdsourcing models for journalism; each suits different data needs

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The Florida News-Press used the 'wisdom of crowds' approach when it wanted to investigate high charges to connect newly constructed homes to the water supply. Short on in-house resources to investigate the leads, they asked their readers to help. Readers spontaneously organized their own investigations: Retired engineers analyzed blueprints, accountants pored over balance sheets, and an inside whistle-blower leaked documents showing evidence of bid-rigging.
themes: crowdsourcing; investigative journalism; audience collaboration; document analysis
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Howe 2006; readers brought domain expertise journalists lacked

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Users can be sceptical of crowdsourcing projects, seeing them as a way of getting users to do lazy journalists' work for them. For that reason, it is important to make sure that there is some benefit for the user, and that it is clear that the journalist is working hard too. You should always be one of the biggest contributors to any crowdsourcing project, leading by example. And always make it clear what will happen to the work of users, regularly providing updates on progress so that users can see the results of their work (or of others).
themes: crowdsourcing; transparency; audience trust; ethics
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Journalist must lead by example and maintain transparency about outcomes

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: The Guardian's MPs' expenses app is one of the most famous examples. This provided an interface for users to investigate MP expense claim forms that used many conventions of game design, including a 'progress bar', leaderboards, and button-based interfaces.
themes: crowdsourcing; gamification; document analysis; interface design
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: Guardian MPs expenses; game design conventions increase participation in data tasks

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Some news organisations have created dedicated roles for 'community content curators' or teams dedicated to sourcing, stimulating, and verifying UGC, while others outsource part of the job to dedicated organisations: Storyful, founded by the Irish journalist Mark Little in 2010, was one of the first to offer a service monitoring and verifying UGC for news organisations.
themes: UGC verification; curation; newsroom roles; outsourcing
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Specialised verification roles emerging; Storyful as pioneer of UGC verification services

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Research by the Engaging News Project found that nasty comments are reduced when journalists interact with comments. They recommended that journalists: Respond to questions; Ask questions; Provide additional information; Encourage and highlight good discussion. The research also found that the layout of comments made a difference, with commenters preferring a structure that allowed them to pick out different types of comments.
themes: comment moderation; audience engagement; quality control; interface design
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Steiner 2015; journalist participation in comments improves quality

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: At the New York Times, an audience development team monitors stories' social media performance not only to see what is being read most but also to pick up editorial leads. In one case, an article about college admissions was being intensely discussed on the Facebook page of conservative media personality Laura Ingraham: Recognizing her as an influencer, the social media team deployed the author of the piece to her page, where he answered the question Ingraham posed in her Facebook post about the story. The Times has set up its account so as to be alerted when influential social media accounts post a Times article.
themes: social media monitoring; editorial leads; audience analytics; newsroom workflow
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: McDermott 2015; NYT uses social monitoring for both analytics and active engagement

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: If you don't collect data you're probably reacting solely to the vocal minority in your community. Measuring the effectiveness of your work is crucial to community management. Try to be critical about what you measure: lots of users or registrations is not always a sign of health. Regular activity is a much better indication. Likewise, does it matter if lots of people read your material if most of them never finish and never return? All of these can help you identify where your attention is needed most: not necessarily where users are most vocal, but where they are quietest.
themes: metrics; audience analytics; data literacy; critical evaluation
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Millington 2012; warns against vanity metrics; silence may signal more than noise

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Quite often when you are asked to fill in these surveys you don't want to because it's being used commercially, but because this research is for editorial, people want to respond. The Lancashire Evening Post grew its reader interaction by creating a large survey on the environment that it promised to present strongly in its paper. A 40-question survey on local environmental issues gathered 1000 responses, a significant rise from the average hundred answers to the site's daily poll.
chapter: Live content: Chats, Q&As, and votes
themes: surveys; audience engagement; data collection; editorial purpose
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Oliver 2008; editorial framing increases survey participation vs commercial framing

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Having clear rules for acceptable and unacceptable behaviour is very important if you want to make sure people don't fall out and accuse you of favouritism. Codes of conduct or terms and conditions don't have to be complex, but they show that decision making isn't arbitrary and should make it easy for people to identify whether something is acceptable or not. T&Cs also help you legally: showing that you are a responsible publisher can help as part of a legal defence.
chapter: Codes of conduct: Terms and conditions
themes: community governance; ethics; legal protection; transparency
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Codes serve dual function: fairness signal and legal shield

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: It is useful to have policies in place regarding when comments are used on a story, when they are turned off, and what types of stories moderators should focus on. For legal reasons, comments are often turned off when a story relates to a crime where someone has not yet been convicted, while particularly controversial subjects such as immigration, abortion, and the Israel-Palestine conflict can lead to such heated (and generally unenlightening) comments that they are either turned off or pre-moderated (where comments only appear after they have been checked).
themes: comment moderation; editorial policy; legal risk; governance
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Pre-moderation as risk-management strategy for legally sensitive or inflammatory topics

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Remember that guest contributors will not necessarily be aware of journalistic conventions and values: research on news participation at community newspapers noted problems with plagiarism and copyright violations, accusations and attacks on individuals and businesses, and failing to present both sides of the story: 'ordinary citizens may not understand that there are many rules in journalism and that there is a process that news stories go through before being published'.
themes: UGC verification; editorial standards; audience collaboration; quality control
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Chung and Nah 2022; citizen contributions require editorial vetting

source_title: The Online Journalism Handbook: Skills to Survive and Thrive in the Digital Age
author: Paul Bradshaw
extract: Data protection laws generally stipulate that you should make it clear why any data is being collected (to be able to receive alerts, promotions, etc.) and you can only use any data collected for the purposes that were given: the Information Commissioner fined the Daily Telegraph £30,000 for misusing the data it had collected from its subscribers so that it could send hundreds of thousands of emails on the day of the general election urging readers to vote Conservative.
themes: data protection; ethics; legal compliance; audience data
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: UK ICO 2015 case; purpose limitation principle in data collection

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Journalists need to be data-savvy. These are the people whose jobs are to interpret what government is doing to the people. So it used to be that you would get stories by chatting to people in bars, and it still might be that you'll do it that way sometimes. But now it's also going to be about poring over data and equipping yourself with the tools to analyse it and picking out what's interesting.
themes: data literacy; skills; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Data literacy for journalists is even more essential in the LLM era, not less.
notes: Tim Berners-Lee quote via Arthur 2010

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalists can no longer stay away from data or just be 'porters' relaying experts' conclusions as in the traditional journalism era, but should be data knowledge producers with independent data collection, analysis, and visualization capabilities.
themes: roles; data literacy; independence
relevance_rationale: Journalists as independent data knowledge producers remains essential even with LLM assistance.

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalism should consider objectivity as a method rather than a goal. In addition, the significance of transparency for data journalism lies in the 'repeatability' of the data analysis process, which allows others to monitor the data journalism production process.
themes: objectivity; transparency; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Objectivity as method and reproducibility of analysis are foundational principles that persist.

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The authority of data journalism lies not in the name of 'data' but in a series of professional norms and ethical requirements in its production. The neglect of professional norms is gradually eroding the credibility and professional value of the data journalism field.
chapter: Exploring the Sustainability of Data Journalism
themes: credibility; professional norms; ethics
relevance_rationale: Professional norms and ethics as the basis of data journalism credibility are timeless.

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The objectivity, neutrality, and authority of data journalism can only be achieved by following the production standards.
chapter: The Fractured Perception of Data Journalism
themes: objectivity; standards; credibility
relevance_rationale: Following production standards for objectivity and authority endures regardless of tooling.

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The essence of data journalism is the construction of discourse. In a sense, data journalism 'constructs' reality with data visualization, rather than 'reflecting' it in full sincerity.
chapter: Importance of Critically Examining Data Journalism
themes: discourse; visualization; critical awareness; constructivism
relevance_rationale: Critical awareness that data journalism constructs rather than reflects reality is a lasting epistemological insight.

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalism is a news journalistic paradigm which uses data science methods to discover facts from various kinds of data and presents data through data visualization methods, serving news value and public interest.
themes: definition; data science; visualization; public interest
relevance_rationale: Definition holds but should now encompass LLM-assisted analysis and AI-generated visualizations.
notes: Author's own definition

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The basic form of data journalism must include three elements: the treatment of data as a source to be gathered and validated, the application of statistics to interrogate it, and visualizations to present it.
themes: definition; validation; statistics; visualization
relevance_rationale: Three elements endure but the methods for validation and statistics now include LLM-assisted approaches.
notes: Howard 2014, Tow Center

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalism means obtaining, reporting, curating, and publishing data in the public interest.
themes: definition; pipeline; public interest
relevance_rationale: Core definition of data journalism as obtaining, reporting, curating and publishing data remains stable.
notes: Jonathan Stray definition

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalism can be regarded as a process of refinement, in which the original data is transformed into meaningful content. When complex facts are refined into clear stories that are understood and remembered by the public, the value of data to the public increases. The production process of data journalism can be divided into four stages: data, filter, visualize, and story.
themes: pipeline; refinement; value chain
relevance_rationale: Pipeline concept of refining raw data into meaningful stories is a durable framework.
notes: Mirko Lorenz / European Journalism Centre 2010

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalism is 80% perspiration, 10% great idea, 10% output.
chapter: The Unclear Boundaries of Data Journalism
themes: effort allocation; process
relevance_rationale: LLMs dramatically shift the effort allocation — the 80% perspiration for data wrangling can now be reduced significantly.
notes: Simon Rogers 2011

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Regardless of whether data journalism is defined through its form or process, two elements stand out: (1) quantitative information should play a central role in the development or telling of the story, and (2) there should be some visual representation of the data in the story.
themes: definition; quantitative; visualization
relevance_rationale: Quantitative centrality and visual representation as defining elements of data journalism persist.
notes: Zamith 2019

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Bradshaw uses the inverted pyramid structure of data journalism to illustrate the production process: data compilation, data cleaning, understanding of data context, and data combination.
chapter: The Differences Between Data Journalism and Traditional Journalism
themes: pipeline; data cleaning; context; inverted pyramid
relevance_rationale: Inverted pyramid pipeline steps endure conceptually but data cleaning is increasingly LLM-assisted.
notes: Bradshaw 2016

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Computer-assisted reporting follows a hypothesis-driven path, while data journalism follows a data-driven path.
chapter: The Relationship Between Data Journalism and Precision Journalism, Computer-Assisted Reporting, and Computational Journalism
themes: epistemology; hypothesis-driven; data-driven
relevance_rationale: Distinction between hypothesis-driven and data-driven approaches remains a valid epistemological framework.
notes: Parasie 2019

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalism demands three different news skills: computer-assisted reporting, news application development, and data visualization.
chapter: The Relationship Between Data Journalism and Precision Journalism, Computer-Assisted Reporting, and Computational Journalism
themes: skills; visualization; programming; CAR
relevance_rationale: Core skill areas endure but now include prompt engineering and LLM orchestration alongside traditional CAR and programming.
notes: Zotto et al. 2015

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The application of open data and open-source software makes the production of data journalism more transparent. Although computational journalism is deeply influenced by the open-source movement, as algorithms are often regarded as commercial secrets, the production of computational journalism is not transparent.
chapter: The Relationship Between Data Journalism and Precision Journalism, Computer-Assisted Reporting, and Computational Journalism
themes: transparency; open source; open data; algorithms
relevance_rationale: Tension between open-source transparency and algorithmic opacity is even more acute with proprietary LLMs.
notes: Coddington 2015 comparison framework

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
