extract: Liliana Bounegru et al. summarize five models for narrating networks: (1) Exploring Associations Around Single Actors (ego networks), (2) Detecting Key Players (by connection count), (3) Mapping Alliances and Oppositions (groupings and gaps), (4) Exploring the Evolution of Associations Over Time, and (5) Revealing Hidden Ties (hidden paths between nodes).
chapter: Social Network Dimension: Revealing Relationships in Complex Networks
themes: network analysis; narrative models; ego networks; hidden ties
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Five models for network storytelling from Bounegru et al.

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The Texas Tribune's Schools Explorer presents the state's data on easy-to-follow charts, empowers users to create their own side-by-side comparisons, and uses an algorithm to generate summary sentences. Although The Texas Education Agency has already published such data on the web, the information on the government site is too complex, forcing parents to navigate a bewildering sea of acronyms, links, and PDFs.
themes: data apps; government data; user experience; automated summaries
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Texas Tribune model: simplify gov data with comparisons and auto-generated summaries

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The design of news gamification has three values: first, it caters to the participatory culture of social platforms; second, it enhances the fun and attractiveness of data products through interaction; and third, it changes the one-time consumption of data news and builds a 'strong relationship' with users through multiple participation of individual users and sharing with others.
themes: gamification; engagement; repeat use; participatory culture
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Three values of news gamification: participation, attractiveness, repeat engagement

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Whether data collection is based on proactive or passive 'consent' depends on the circumstances. If an organization publishes data on its own website, the data automatically becomes public information, and data journalists can access the data through various technical means (unless otherwise requested by the data owner), in which case passive consent applies to the data collection. However, if sensitive personal information is involved, proactive consent from the data subject is required.
themes: consent; personal data; ethics; data access
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Distinguishes proactive vs passive consent for data collection

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Bots have as much responsibility as their human creators; the very important limit between web scraping and hacking is the respect of the law.
themes: web scraping; ethics; legal boundaries
relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written
notes: Attributed to Cédric Sam, South China Morning Post

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: First, the reporters should always pay attention to the terms of service of the website they're accessing to understand whether the company prohibits scraping, even if its data is accessible. If they cannot determine what those terms are saying, they should reach out to an attorney. Second, if the terms of use prohibit data scraping, reporters should first seek alternate sources for the information. Third, if the information exists only on a company's site, it can be a good idea to contact the company and see if they will simply turn over the information. Fourth, whether the journalists contact the company or not, they should always diminish the risk of injury to the company or other individuals through whatever methods possible. For example, journalists should be careful not to build any tool that might overwhelm a company's servers. Additionally, if any sensitive data with privacy concerns are collected, journalists should be careful to redact and employ ethical journalistic standards. Fifth, journalists should be good citizens and ensure that any data collection is done in the public interest.
themes: web scraping; legal compliance; terms of service; public interest; harm minimization
relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs
notes: 5-step protocol from Victoria Baranetsky, Columbia TOW Center

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Over-extended use refers to the use of personal data by a data reporter for purposes other than those initially stated, which may violate the rights of individuals. Over-display refers to the display of personal data with little public interest, which is essentially 'display for display's sake.'
themes: personal data; privacy; proportionality; data display
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Taxonomy of data misuse: over-extended use vs over-display

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Just because content is publicly accessible does not mean that it was meant to be consumed by just anyone.
themes: public data; privacy; contextual integrity
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Attributed to Boyd & Crawford 2012

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalists must follow the principles of legality and proportionality in the use of personal data and need to collect data within the scope of the law, while data collection outside the law must be done only after obtaining the consent of the individuals concerned. In addition, the collected data may not be used for purposes other than those originally stated without the consent of the person concerned.
chapter: The Principles of Legality and Proportionality
themes: legality; proportionality; consent; data collection scope
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: If a regular user can't access it, journalists shouldn't try to get it.
chapter: The Principles of Legality and Proportionality
themes: data access; web scraping; legal boundaries
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Attributed to Shiab 2015; bright-line heuristic for scraping

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The collection and use of personal data in data journalism need to be based on the issue, not just the pursuit of large quantities. It is necessary to determine the appropriate scope of the data to be scraped. Otherwise, inappropriate and excessive mining of citizens' personal information by the media may lead to excessive 'display' of the data subjects' privacy, which may be harmful to them and cause adverse social effects.
chapter: The Principles of Legality and Proportionality
themes: data scope; proportionality; harm minimization; privacy
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Invokes data science concept of threshold value -- collect only what the question requires

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: A distinction should be made between the protection of sensitive private personal information and the use of general personal information -- thus achieving a balance of interests.
chapter: The Principles of Legality and Proportionality
themes: sensitive data; classification; privacy tiers
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: When providing data to the media, data holders need to indicate the existence of privacy-sensitive information, anonymize or delete such information, and classify the level of privacy of the data so that the media can determine how to use it.
chapter: The Principles of Legality and Proportionality
themes: data handoff; anonymization; privacy classification; data providers
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
notes: Guidance directed at data holders supplying media

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Journalists must weigh the benefits of open data against the risks of personal harm that may come with publication.
chapter: The Principles of Public Interest Priority and Minimizing Harm
themes: publication ethics; harm minimization; public interest
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Attributed to Lewis & Westlund 2015

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: In 2012, data journalist Nils Mulvad finally got the veterinary prescriptions data that he had been fighting for for seven years, but he decided not to publish the data when he realized that it was full of errors, because 'publishing it directly would have meant identifying specific doctors for doing bad things which they hadn't done.'
chapter: The Principles of Public Interest Priority and Minimizing Harm
themes: data quality; harm minimization; withholding publication; verification
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Case study: errors in data can cause false attribution of wrongdoing

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: When reusing personal data, journalists can achieve privacy 'anonymization' by removing the identifiable elements of personal information through codification or encryption. For example, after erasing an individual's identifying information (name, address, credit card number, date of birth, etc.) from a database, the remaining data is available for use and sharing.
chapter: The Principles of Public Interest Priority and Minimizing Harm
themes: anonymization; PII removal; data cleaning; privacy
relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: In the era of big data, anonymization of a single piece of information is not sufficient to avoid identification, and data subjects may still be identified through the combination of multiple anonymous single pieces of personal information. Data journalists need to comprehensively evaluate the potential risks of anonymous personal data and take corresponding measures to protect the legitimate rights and interests of data subjects.
chapter: The Principles of Public Interest Priority and Minimizing Harm
themes: re-identification risk; anonymization limits; big data; privacy
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Warns that combining anonymized datasets can re-identify individuals

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The journalists used linear regression, logistic regression, PCA (principal component analysis), K-means algorithm, and multiple expectation maximization algorithms to analyze the data. The study revealed the secrets of the $600 billion health care bill and eventually forced the U.S. government in April of that year to disclose for the first time important health care data that had been kept secret since 1979.
chapter: Status of Big Data News Production
themes: linear regression; PCA; K-means; machine learning; investigative journalism
relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives
notes: WSJ 'Medicare Unmasked' 2014; enumerates specific statistical methods

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The most significant feature of big data news is open production. For example, CCTV cooperates with professional institutions and companies in big data mining, analysis, and data visualization.
chapter: Status of Big Data News Production
themes: collaboration; outsourcing; big data; open production
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The media should not solely rely on the success of leaks and open government and thus make data-driven journalism remain a niche form of news reporting, but also develop its own internal database to provide various information services, such as public opinion monitoring, market analysis, and prospect prediction.
chapter: From Content to Service: Upgrading the Media Profit Model
themes: internal databases; self-sufficiency; data strategy; sustainability
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Media organizations can start by employing programmers and data analysts for user analysis or data news production, which can be seen as a preparatory phase of a big data strategy, followed by further planning based on the development of journalism and big data.
chapter: To Develop Big Data Strategies for Media Transformation
themes: hiring; phased strategy; capacity building; data teams
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Staged approach to building big-data capability

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The big data strategy of media is not equal to simply accumulating a large amount of data, but should mean using reasonable technology to collect data strategically and process data purposefully according to the business strategy of media, so that the data assets can create more economic benefits.
chapter: To Develop Big Data Strategies for Media Transformation
themes: data strategy; purposeful collection; business alignment
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data journalists can acquire data in three ways: creating datasets, combining datasets, and accumulating data in non-standard data formats.
chapter: To Accumulate Big Data Resources Actively
themes: data acquisition; dataset creation; data combination; non-standard formats
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Attributed to Financial Times 2017 report

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: The data that the media should accumulate include (1) government public data, (2) data from the media's own platforms, (3) social media data, (4) IoT data, and (5) other data related to public interests. Media can accumulate big data of a certain category or a certain field purposely according to their own development strategies.
chapter: To Accumulate Big Data Resources Actively
themes: data sources; government data; social media; IoT; platform data
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Five-category taxonomy of data sources for media

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: There are three stages in which media can enhance their big data processing capabilities: the stage of outsourcing big data production, the stage of equal cooperation, and the stage of independent production.
chapter: To Improve Big Data Processing Capability
themes: capacity building; outsourcing; collaboration; independence; maturity model
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools
notes: Three-stage maturity model for newsroom data capability

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: If the production of big data news is controlled by people outside the media, the truthfulness, objectivity, and fairness of news reporting will be at risk.
chapter: To Improve Big Data Processing Capability
themes: editorial independence; outsourcing risk; objectivity; trust
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: They tend to choose technology companies that have no conflict of interest with the news topic and have a good reputation, and will double-check the conclusions drawn from big data analysis through interviews and analyses of other data.
chapter: To Improve Big Data Processing Capability
themes: conflict of interest; triangulation; verification; partner selection
relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling
notes: Attributed to Guo Junyi, CCTV; practical safeguard when outsourcing analysis

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Data collection, analysis, narrative, and visualization are not neutral, natural, or transparent. For example, data collection may be incomplete, data analysis may be wrong, narrative may be biased, and data visualization may distort the facts ... Therefore, journalists need to follow the principles of objectivity and transparency.
themes: objectivity; transparency; bias; data skepticism; epistemology
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Summarizing claim from the book's conclusion

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Story-wise, I hope we're heading towards larger and more unstructured data sets. We will be looking for ways to conquer text; we haven't conquered text yet. In one way I wish that we had gotten the Panama Papers ten years from now because we would have been able to do much different stories than we do right now.
chapter: To Improve Big Data Processing Capability
themes: unstructured data; text analysis; NLP; capability gaps
relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
notes: Attributed to Helena Bengtsson, The Guardian

source_title: The Power of Data: Data Journalism Production and Ethics
author: Zhang Chao
extract: Whether as an application or as a data platform, big data news products have the ability to update and upgrade themselves, extending their 'shelf life' indefinitely, thus reaping both short-term and long-term benefits. Some important databases require only regular maintenance after they are built, so their operating costs can be greatly reduced.
chapter: From Content to Service: Upgrading the Media Profit Model
themes: data products; sustainability; databases; long shelf life
relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Develop human sources. Contact people, and talk to them. Be skeptical when something looks, sounds or seems too good to be true. Consult multiple, credible sources. Familiarize yourself with search and research methods, and new tools. Communicate and work together with other professionals — verification is a team sport.
chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
themes: verification fundamentals; source triangulation; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Developing human sources, skepticism, and collaborative verification are fundamental journalism principles unaffected by LLMs
notes: Six core principles carried from original Verification Handbook into investigative context

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: verification is a mix of three elements: Sources knowledge, reliability and honesty, and the number, variety and reliability of sources you can find and persuade to talk. Documentation.
chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
themes: verification framework; source evaluation; documentation
relevance_rationale: Buttry's three-element verification model (sources, documentation, knowledge) remains foundational regardless of tools
notes: Steve Buttry's three-element model; this Handbook focuses on the third element

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: just as we know that human memory is faulty and that sources lie, we must also remember that documents and data aren't always what they appear.
chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
themes: data skepticism; source reliability; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Documents and data still lie, but LLMs add a new category: AI-generated documents and synthetic data that require additional verification layers

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Channel 4 investigators took that previous Twitter handle and searched other social networks to see if they could find anyone using it. That led them to a Google+ account, and then to a Facebook page. There they found photos and other details about a man living in Bangalore who worked as a marketing executive for an Indian company.
chapter: UGC and open source information in investigations
themes: cross-platform search; identity resolution; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Cross-platform username tracing remains valid but LLMs can now automate OSINT correlation across social platforms far faster
notes: ShamiWitness case study: tracing a username across platforms to unmask identity

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The simple two-letter word 'is' reveals opinions and facts about your subject. To avoid clutter, include the company name of the person or any other detail you know, and tell Google that both words should not be that far from each other. The AROUND() operator MUST BE IN CAPITALS. It sets the maximum distance in words between the two terms.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: search operators; Google advanced search; background research
relevance_rationale: Manual Google search operator syntax for finding people is largely displaced by LLM-powered search and research agents
notes: Henk van Ess technique for building background profiles using AROUND() proximity operator

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: This search is asking Google to 'Show me PDF documents with the name of the CEO of Shell in it, but exclude documents from Shell.' This will find documents about your subject, but not from the company of the subject itself. This helps you to see what opponents, competitors or opinionated people say about your subject.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: search operators; exclusion filtering; competitive intelligence
relevance_rationale: PDF filetype searches on government sites still work but LLM search tools can handle this more naturally via conversational queries
notes: filetype:pdf combined with -site: to surface third-party perspectives on a subject

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Search for worldwide official documents about this person. It searches for gov.uk (United Kingdom) but also .gov.au (Australia), .gov.cn (China), .gov (U.S.) and other governmental websites in the world. If you don't have a .gov website in your country, use the local word for it with the site: operator.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: government records; site operator; public records
relevance_rationale: Manual site-specific government domain searching is displaced by LLM search tools that can query across jurisdictions conversationally
notes: Using site:gov.* variants to find official documents across jurisdictions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: With this formula you can find results that use different spellings of the name. You will receive documents with the word Shell, but not those that include 'Ben' as the first name. With this, you will find out that he is also referred to as Bernardus van Beurden. Now repeat steps a, b, c and d with this new name.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: name variations; iterative search; identity resolution
relevance_rationale: Boolean name-variant search formulas are displaced by LLMs that handle name variations, transliterations, and exclusions natively
notes: Iterative technique: discover name variants then re-run all previous search strategies with new spellings

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Most of the research you do is not based on today, but an earlier period. Always tell your search engine to go back in time.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: date-range search; temporal filtering; historical research
relevance_rationale: Temporal search filtering remains important but LLM tools handle date-range queries more intuitively than manual operator syntax
notes: Using date-restricted image/web search to find pre-event documentation

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Websites often cease to exist. There is a chance you can still view them by using archive.org. This tool can do its work only if you know the URL of the webpage you want to see. The problem is that often the page is gone and therefore you don't know it. So how do you find a seemingly disappeared URL? Step One: Find an index. Step Two: Put the index in the time machine. Step Three: Find the original website.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: web archives; archive.org; URL recovery; Wayback Machine
relevance_rationale: The Wayback Machine and web archiving remain essential for verification; the principle of preserving disappearing content is timeless
notes: Three-step method: find an index page that linked to the dead URL, look up that index in archive.org, extract the dead URL, then retrieve it

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Search engines like Google don't actually know what web pages are about. They do, however, know the words that are on the pages. So to get a search engine to behave itself, you need to work out which words are on your target pages. Choose your search terms wisely. Each word you add to the search focuses the results by eliminating results that don't include your chosen keywords. Some words are on every page you are after. Other words might or might not be on the target page. Avoid those subjective keywords, as they can eliminate useful pages from the results.
themes: search strategy; keyword selection; information retrieval
relevance_rationale: The epistemology of keyword-based search engines is being displaced by semantic search where LLMs understand page meaning, not just keywords
notes: Core principle: think about what words will appear on the target page, not what the page is about

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If you don't have definite keywords, you can still build in other possible keywords without damaging the results. Pages discussing heroin use in Texas might not include the word 'Texas'; they may just mention the names of different cities. You can build these into your search as optional keywords by separating them with the word OR (in capital letters).
themes: search operators; OR operator; keyword strategy
relevance_rationale: Manual synonym/location keyword expansion is displaced by LLMs that natively understand geographic and linguistic variants
notes: Using OR for optional keywords when target pages may use synonyms or specifics instead of general terms

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: You can focus your search on a particular site by using the search syntax 'site:' followed by the domain name. You can also use it to focus your search on municipal and academic sources, too. This is particularly effective when researching countries that use unique domain types for government and university sites.
themes: site operator; domain filtering; institutional sources
relevance_rationale: Site-specific search operator syntax is displaced by LLM search agents that handle domain filtering conversationally

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Some information comes in certain types of file formats. For instance, statistics, figures and data often appear in Excel spreadsheets. Professionally produced reports can often be found in PDF documents. You can specify a format in your search by using 'filetype:' followed by the desired data file extension.
themes: filetype operator; data formats; structured data
relevance_rationale: Filetype-based search operators are displaced by LLM tools that can locate and parse spreadsheets and PDFs directly
notes: Matching expected file format to data type: spreadsheets for stats, PDFs for reports

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Start by building a dossier on the person you're trying to locate or learn more about. This can include: The person's name, bearing in mind different variations, the spelling of foreign names in Roman letters, whether names changed when a person married, middle names or initials. The town the person lives in or was born in. The person's job and company. The person's phone number. Any of the person's usernames, as these are often constant across various social networks. The person's email address. A photograph, as this can help you find the right person if the name is common.
themes: dossier building; identity resolution; OSINT checklist
relevance_rationale: Building a person dossier with name variants is still essential but LLMs can automate transliteration, alias generation, and cross-referencing
notes: Comprehensive checklist of identifiers to collect before beginning a person search

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: People often use the same photo as a profile picture for different social networks. This being the case, reverse image search on sites like TinEye and Google Images, will help you identify linked accounts.
themes: reverse image search; cross-platform identification; profile matching
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search for linking accounts endures in principle but AI facial recognition and multi-modal search tools now vastly extend this capability

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Many journalists have been fooled by malicious websites. Since it's easy for anyone to buy an unclaimed .com, .net or .org site, we should not go on face value. A site that looks well produced and has an authentic-sounding domain name may still be a political hoax, false company or satirical prank. Some degree of quality control can be achieved by examining the domain name itself. Google it and visit any sites that offer the ability to perform a whois search. It will bring up the registration details given by the site owner when the domain name was purchased.
themes: domain verification; WHOIS; source authentication
relevance_rationale: Skepticism about domain ownership and professional-looking websites remains critical; anyone can still spin up convincing sites

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Whois is not a panacea for verification. People can often get away with lying on a domain registration form. Some people will use an anonymizing service like Domains by Proxy, but combining a whois search with other domain name and IP address tools forms a valuable weapon in the battle to provide useful material from authentic sources.
themes: WHOIS limitations; tool triangulation; source authentication
relevance_rationale: WHOIS limitations and anonymizing services still apply but domain investigation now also requires checking AI-generated content and deepfake sites
notes: Caveat: WHOIS alone is insufficient; must combine with other checks

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Everything has a paper trail, a lead that exposes the systemic underwire of a network, company, or person's illicit or illegal activities. The trick is to find it.
chapter: Records and publicly available material to investigate companies
themes: paper trail; corporate investigation; document-driven reporting
relevance_rationale: The principle that everything has a paper trail is a timeless investigative journalism axiom

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The most important aspects of any investigation are to dig, listen and ask pertinent questions. But asking questions requires context, and listening to the right sources means finding the core of the story. Data, free or otherwise, can never replace good investigative research. In order to do good investigative research, these days, one must become familiar with how and where knowledge can be found, and how best to access and develop it.
themes: investigative method; context-building; data limitations
relevance_rationale: The primacy of digging, listening, and asking pertinent questions in context remains the core of investigative journalism
notes: Data supplements but does not replace investigative research

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We gathered corporate brochures that listed financial receiving agents, brokers, auditors, physical offices and other details that detailed connections between seemingly independent companies.
themes: corporate records; network mapping; document collection
relevance_rationale: Gathering corporate brochures and documents to trace financial connections between companies remains essential fieldwork
notes: Using corporate promotional material as a source for mapping shell-company networks

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Our work made use of free public databases such as Duedil that allow for individual and corporate director searches. These enable users to identify the number of companies — current, dissolved, etc. — a director is involved in. It can also provide other important information: Shareholders, registered offices and a timeline of retired and current individuals involved. We also used LinkedIn to probe personal and corporate connections.
themes: corporate registries; Duedil; LinkedIn; director searches
relevance_rationale: Corporate database searches like DueDil still exist but LLMs can now automate director cross-referencing across multiple registries simultaneously
notes: Free tools for mapping corporate networks across jurisdictions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Along with databases, we used Whois Internet searches where possible to determine the date of creation and ownership information of websites that were connected to the network. We then cross-referenced the contact details of the websites with the information listed in corporate databases for brokers and shell entities. Using specific search phrases, we were able to draw out mentions of certain names, companies, products etc. from various files on the Internet.
themes: cross-referencing; WHOIS; corporate databases; triangulation
relevance_rationale: WHOIS cross-referencing with corporate records endures in principle but LLM agents can automate the cross-referencing pipeline
notes: Cross-referencing WHOIS data with corporate registries to confirm network links

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: As part of the investigation, we also created dummy profiles on social media to enable us to connect with relevant companies and individuals, and to engage in email communication. We posed as potential investors to gain firsthand access to the push and pull of the scam.
themes: undercover techniques; social engineering; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Undercover social media profiling for investigations raises identical ethical and methodological questions regardless of AI tools
notes: Ethical grey area: creating fake identities to gather evidence

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Unlike other jurisdictions, African countries often do not have centralized or electronically accessible data. To this end, we train and deploy in-country researchers to physically obtain not just the updated and accurate corporate, land, court and other data, but also to visit critical locations, conduct basic interviews and take relevant photos.
themes: in-country researchers; field verification; data gaps
relevance_rationale: Physical in-country data collection in jurisdictions without digital records remains irreplaceable by any technology
notes: Organizational solution to the problem of non-digitized records in some jurisdictions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We connected with Heinrich Böhmke, a South African prosecutor and an in-house expert at ANCIR, to 'cross-examine' our evidence. This is a process Böhmke took from the legal world and adapted for investigative journalism. We looked for bias, contradictions, consistency and probability within evidence, resources, interviews and sources.
themes: cross-examination; evidence testing; legal methods; bias detection
relevance_rationale: Cross-examining evidence with legal experts adapts courtroom adversarial methods for journalism; this human judgment process is not automatable
notes: Adapting legal cross-examination technique to stress-test journalistic evidence

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: By identifying the broad relations within, and between, people, companies, jurisdictions and numbers, we were able to visualize the network's structure. This told us how the scheme functioned and who was involved.
themes: network visualization; entity mapping; pattern analysis
relevance_rationale: Network visualization to map corporate structures endures but LLMs and graph tools can now automate entity extraction and relationship mapping

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It doesn't take a genius to develop a good investigation or to lift the corporate veil — it simply takes curiosity, technique and a commitment to read as much and as far into the issue as possible. Scour as many data sources as possible: Corporate, media, NGO, shipping, sanctions, courts… Look for what is not obvious, seems illogical, or that just plain sticks out to you. Follow your instinct. Ask as many questions as possible.
themes: investigative mindset; curiosity; exhaustive search; corporate investigations
relevance_rationale: Curiosity, technique, and commitment to reading deeply into corporate structures are enduring investigative virtues
notes: Followed by a checklist of questions to ask about corporate entities

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The most important lesson I've learned from two decades of using data for investigations is that data lies just as much as people, or even more so. Data, after all, is often created and maintained by people. Data is meant to be a representation of the reality of a particular moment of time. So, how do we verify that a data set corresponds to reality?
themes: data skepticism; data quality; representation vs reality
relevance_rationale: Data lying as much as people is even more true now that LLMs can generate plausible but fabricated datasets
notes: Giannina Segnini's core axiom from two decades of data investigation

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Two key verification tasks need to be performed during a data-driven investigation: An initial evaluation must occur immediately after getting the data; and findings must be verified at the end of the investigation or analysis phase.
themes: data verification workflow; two-phase verification; quality assurance
relevance_rationale: Two-phase data verification (initial evaluation plus final findings check) is a structural best practice that applies regardless of tools used
notes: Two-checkpoint model: verify on receipt and verify after analysis

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The first practice I recommend is to explore the extreme values (highest or lowest) for each variable in a dataset, and to then count how many records (rows) are listed within each of the possible values.
themes: data profiling; extreme values; completeness checks
relevance_rationale: Exploring extreme values and distributions is still essential but LLMs can now automate initial EDA and flag anomalies programmatically
notes: First step of initial verification: check extremes and value distributions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If we create a pivot table to count how many projects have a zero cost, in relation to the total records, we can see how more than half of those (53 percent) cost zero. This means that anyone who performs a calculation or analysis per country, region or year involving the cost of the projects would be wrong if they failed to account for all of the entries with no stated cost. Using the dataset as it's provided will lead to an inaccurate conclusion.
themes: missing values; zero values; data completeness; pivot tables
relevance_rationale: Pivot table analysis for zero-value detection is a valid technique but LLMs can automate this kind of data quality audit instantly
notes: World Bank example: 53% zero-cost entries would corrupt any cost-based analysis

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: A common mistake made when working with data is to fail to identify the existence of duplicate records. Whenever processing disaggregated data or information about people, companies, events or transactions, the first step is to search for a unique identification variable for each item.
themes: deduplication; unique identifiers; data quality
relevance_rationale: Duplicate detection is fundamental but LLMs now automate fuzzy matching and deduplication far beyond manual pivot table approaches

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: One of the best ways to assess a dataset's credibility is to choose a sample record and compare it against reality. It is always recommended to repeat this validation exercise on a significant sample of the records.
themes: ground-truth validation; spot-checking; data accuracy
relevance_rationale: Ground-truthing data against reality by sampling records is an irreplaceable verification step that no tool can automate
notes: Spot-check sample records against original documentation or real-world conditions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: From the moment it's first entered in a computer to the time when we access it, data goes through several input, storage, transmission and registry processes. At any stage, it may be manipulated by people and information systems. It is therefore very common that relations between tables or fields get lost or mixed up, or that some variables fail to get updated. This is why it's essential to perform integrity tests.
themes: data integrity; data lifecycle; relational consistency
relevance_rationale: Data corruption through pipeline stages is now compounded by AI-generated data entering pipelines, requiring additional provenance checks
notes: Data degrades through its lifecycle; integrity tests check that relationships between fields remain valid

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Open Refine is an excellent tool to quickly explore and evaluate the quality of databases. You can use Open Refine to run a numeric facet in the Amount field. A numeric facet groups numbers into numeric range bins. This enables you to select any range that spans a consecutive number of bins. You can generate a histogram with the values range included in the database. Records can then be filtered by values by moving the arrows inside the graph. The same can be done for dates and text values.
themes: OpenRefine; data profiling; faceted browsing; histograms
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine numeric faceting is now largely displaced by LLM-powered data profiling that can do richer quality assessment conversationally

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It is therefore mandatory to learn how to decrypt codes and to understand how they are used to better understand the logic behind databases and, more importantly, their relations. Each one of the 17 million cargo containers in the world has a unique identifier, and we can track them if we understand that the first four letters of the identifier are related to the identity of its owner.
themes: code systems; unique identifiers; database logic; domain knowledge
relevance_rationale: Understanding database codes and schemas endures but LLMs can now decode and explain proprietary coding systems given documentation or examples
notes: Decoding classification systems is both a data-quality practice and a story-finding technique

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: This example shows how decoding acronyms is not only a best practice for evaluating the quality of data, but, more important, to finding stories of public interest.
themes: code systems; story discovery; data quality
relevance_rationale: The insight that decoding acronyms leads to stories of public interest reflects investigative curiosity that transcends any tool
notes: World Bank structural adjustment codes contradicted official policy claims — decoding revealed discrepancy

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Before the analysis, we made sure to evaluate and clean all of the records, and to verify the relationships between each person and their assets.
themes: pre-analysis cleaning; relational verification; data preparation
relevance_rationale: The principle of evaluating and cleaning records before analysis and verifying relationships is a timeless workflow discipline
notes: La Nacion Avancemos subsidy investigation

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It was not until we went to visit their homes that we could prove what the data alone could have never told us: These kids lived in real poverty with their mothers because they had been abandoned by their fathers. Not even the best data analysis can replace on-the-ground journalism and field verification.
themes: field verification; ground-truthing; data limitations; reporting
relevance_rationale: Field visits to verify what data alone cannot reveal exemplifies the irreplaceable role of on-the-ground reporting
notes: Core lesson: data analysis must be validated by real-world reporting; the data showed fathers with assets but the reality was abandonment

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: I compared the position of the minaret, the dome and a nearby wall on the satellite map imagery to the video, and it was clear it was a perfect match. Now that the likely position of the camera in the town was established, I could watch the whole video comparing other details to what was visible on satellite map imagery. This further confirmed the locations matched.
themes: geolocation; satellite imagery; landmark matching; video verification
relevance_rationale: Satellite imagery geolocation matching endures but AI-powered geolocation tools now accelerate and partially automate the comparison process
notes: Higgins' Tiji video: matching mosque minaret/dome position between video and satellite imagery

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: As they walked through the streets, it was possible to map out the roads along the route they took, and then match that pattern to what was visible in satellite map imagery.
themes: geolocation; road pattern matching; satellite imagery
relevance_rationale: Road pattern matching from video to satellite imagery is still valid but AI vision models can now assist with automated feature matching
notes: When no large landmarks exist, map the route pattern from video and match to satellite imagery

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: While things like billboards and streetlights are small objects, the shadows they cast can actually indicate their presence. Shadows can also be used to reveal information about the comparative height of buildings, and the shape of those buildings. I used the website Sun Calc, which allows users to calculate the position of the sun throughout the day using a Google Maps based interface. It was then possible to establish the time of day as approximately 12:30 p.m. local time.
themes: shadow analysis; SunCalc; chronolocation; geolocation
relevance_rationale: Shadow analysis for geolocation endures in principle but AI tools can now compute sun angles and shadow lengths algorithmically
notes: Shadows reveal both presence of small objects and time of day via sun position calculation

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: By bringing together different sources, tools and techniques, it was possible to connect these individual pieces of information and establish critical facts about this incident.
themes: evidence synthesis; multi-source verification; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Synthesizing multiple evidence types to establish facts is a core analytical principle that transcends any specific tool
notes: MH17 Buk missile investigation: combining geolocation, chronolocation, social media, interviews

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: A key element of working with user-generated content in investigations is understanding how that content is shared. With Syria, a handful of opposition social media pages are the main sources of information from certain areas. This obviously limits the perspective on the conflict from different regions, but also means it's possible to collect, organize and systematically review those accounts for the latest information.
themes: UGC sourcing; information ecosystem mapping; selection bias
relevance_rationale: Understanding UGC information ecosystems and source networks in conflict zones remains essential contextual knowledge
notes: Understanding the distribution architecture of UGC is prerequisite to systematic collection

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The videos were uncovered by first geolocating the initial videos we found, then using that to predict the likely route those vehicles would have taken to get from each geolocated site. Then we would keyword search on various social media sites for the names of locations that were along the route the vehicle would have had to travel. We also searched for keywords such as 'convoy,' 'missile,' etc. that could be associated with sightings.
themes: route prediction; keyword search; geolocation chain; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Predictive route-based video searching is clever methodology but AI geolocation and video analysis tools now assist with this systematically
notes: Iterative method: geolocate > predict route > keyword search along route > geolocate new finds

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If there's one final piece of advice, it would be to give this work and approach a try in any investigation. It's remarkable what you can turn up when you approach UGC and open source information in a systematic way. You tend to learn quickly by just doing it. Even something as simple as double-checking the geolocation someone else has done can teach you a lot about comparing videos and photographs to satellite map imagery.
themes: learning by doing; OSINT; geolocation practice
relevance_rationale: The call to approach UGC and open source information systematically in any investigation is a timeless methodological principle

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: In a human rights investigation, we compare all facts gathered with relevant human rights norms and laws to make determinations of violations or abuses. Consequently, a single analyst who looks at UGC, such as myself, must be part of a team comprising relevant country, policy and legal experts.
themes: multidisciplinary teams; legal analysis; human rights methodology
relevance_rationale: Comparing evidence against legal norms with multi-disciplinary analysts is an organizational principle that requires human legal expertise
notes: UGC analyst must work within team of country, policy, and legal specialists

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Today we are achieving the best results when combining a variety of evidence, such as testimony, official documents, satellite imagery, and UGC. This requires the close collaboration of researchers who possess country expertise, trusted contacts on the ground, and highly specialized analysts who do not focus on a specific region or country, but are able to provide analysis based on satellite imagery or UGC.
themes: evidence triangulation; satellite imagery; UGC; specialization
relevance_rationale: Multi-evidence triangulation combining testimony, documents, satellite imagery and UGC requires collaborative human expertise
notes: Best results from combining testimony + documents + satellite + UGC across specialist roles

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: In some instances, one piece of evidence does not corroborate some of the information gathered during the investigation, such as when satellite imagery does not support eyewitness claims of a large mass grave. We then exercise caution and hold back on making statements of fact or determinations of violations.
themes: corroboration failure; evidentiary caution; conflicting evidence
relevance_rationale: Handling contradictory evidence and applying evidentiary standards despite gaps is core analytical judgment that requires human reasoning
notes: When evidence types contradict, withhold conclusions rather than privilege one source

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We first performed standard content analysis. This involved extracting the specifications of the roads, street lamps, buildings and vegetation, as well as details related to the people seen in the video such as clothes and military equipment. Reviewing the video frame by frame greatly aided with this process. The geographic features were then compared to satellite images of the area on Google Earth.
themes: content analysis; frame-by-frame review; geolocation; video forensics
relevance_rationale: Content analysis of video for roads, buildings, vegetation endures but AI vision models can now extract and match these features semi-automatically
notes: Amnesty International's standard procedure for video verification: extract visual features then match to satellite imagery

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Several photographs in our possession were actually geotagged. We discovered this by using a EXIF viewer to examine the metadata in the photo. This location data proved a perfect match to the street corner we identified as part of the content analysis of the initial video.
themes: EXIF metadata; geotags; photo verification; corroboration
relevance_rationale: EXIF metadata verification remains valid but needs updating for AI-generated images that may have synthetic or stripped metadata
notes: EXIF geotag data corroborated the location established through video content analysis

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We followed the video to perform a content analysis in order to identify distinctive markings on the soldiers and victims, or anything that could indicate location, time or date. This revealed two important details: a soldier wearing a black flak jacket stating 'Borno State. Operation Flush,' the name of the military operation in northeastern Nigeria; and, for a split second, an ID number on a rifle became visible.
themes: content analysis; identifying markings; unit identification; video forensics
relevance_rationale: Video content analysis for distinctive markings endures but AI object detection and uniform recognition tools now assist this process
notes: Frame-by-frame review to extract unit identifiers and operational markings from video

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: An AI researcher was also able to secure actual video files while on a field mission to the area. This allowed us to conduct metadata analysis. The metadata corroborated that the footage had been created March 14, 2014. Obtaining the original files had been possible only through well-established local contacts and networks, who might share content in person or via email (ideally encrypted).
themes: original file acquisition; metadata analysis; field collection; source networks
relevance_rationale: Securing original video files for metadata analysis through field missions remains essential physical evidence collection
notes: Original files preserve metadata stripped during upload; requires trusted local contacts

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Savvy news desk researchers and journalists who might be inclined to contact local sources via Twitter or other public platform should consider the risk implications for asking for such sensitive footage from contacts in insecure environments.
themes: source protection; operational security; ethics; risk assessment
relevance_rationale: Protecting sources from risk when requesting sensitive footage via public platforms is a timeless safety and ethics principle
notes: Warning against public-platform contact for sensitive material in conflict zones

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It's essential to determine what ethical standards will work for you and your audience, and what actions will allow you to establish and preserve a relationship with them. Our approach must be ethical so that it can be sustainable.
themes: ethics; sustainability; audience trust; UGC
relevance_rationale: Establishing ethical standards for UGC use and audience relationships is a foundational principle unaffected by AI tools
notes: Ethical UGC practices framed as prerequisite for sustainable source relationships

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The key takeaway here is to consider how the content creator sees their activity, rather than how the platform defines it. The main issue is likely to be how you identify yourself to and within that community.
themes: ethics; privacy expectations; platform norms; journalist identification
relevance_rationale: Considering how content creators view their activity and self-identifying ethically in private communities are timeless ethical principles
notes: Creator's perception of privacy matters more than platform's technical settings

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Seeking permission to use content from creators of UGC helps establish and maintain the reputation of your organization as one that gives fair treatment. Securing permission will also help you ensure you are using content from an original source. This means the act of asking for permission also opens up a potential source of additional information on the content that you otherwise wouldn't have had.
themes: permission seeking; source verification; UGC rights; relationship building
relevance_rationale: Seeking permission for UGC builds reputation and ensures accuracy; this ethical and practical principle is unaffected by AI
notes: Permission request doubles as verification step and source-development opportunity

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Does an assignment put someone at risk? Could an individual get too close to a dangerous event or to people who may cause them harm? What is your responsibility to a person who is harmed while carrying out an assignment set by you? How will you identify this person in the publication or broadcast? What impact does an assignment have on the honesty/authenticity of the content being produced versus something that was created unprompted?
themes: contributor safety; duty of care; assignment ethics; authenticity
relevance_rationale: Duty of care toward contributors who may face danger is a fundamental ethical obligation independent of technology
notes: Five questions to establish policy before soliciting audience contributions

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: When someone uploads a photograph or video to a social network, the copyright remains with them. So if you want to download the picture or video to use elsewhere, you must first seek permission. If you simply want to embed the material, using the embed code provided by all of the social networks, legally you don't need to seek permission. Ethically, however, it might be appropriate to contact the person who created the content to let them know how and where you intend to use it.
themes: copyright; embedding vs downloading; UGC rights; ethical practice
relevance_rationale: Copyright residing with content creators and the need to seek permission before use is a legal principle unaffected by AI
notes: Legal distinction between embedding (no permission needed) and downloading (permission required)

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: An important question to ask is whether the person actually captured the material him or herself. It is amazing how many people upload other people's content on their own channels. They will often 'give permission' for use even though they have no right to do so. You also want to ask basic questions about the location, and what else they could see, to help you authenticate what they claim to have witnessed.
themes: provenance verification; permission validation; UGC authentication
relevance_rationale: Verifying whether a person actually captured the material they uploaded is a timeless verification step
notes: Re-uploaders frequently grant permission they don't own; always confirm original capture

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If you do seek permission on the social network itself, make sure that you take a screenshot of the exchange. People will sometimes provide permission for use, and then, after negotiating an exclusive deal with another organization, they will delete any exchanges on social media to avoid showing them giving permission to others.
themes: permission documentation; screenshots; legal protection
relevance_rationale: Screenshotting permission exchanges as evidence against later disputes is a practical legal safeguard regardless of technology

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Best practice is to label any content with the information you can confirm, whether that's source, date or location. If you can confirm only two of three, add this information over the photo or video. The most important role for journalists is to provide the necessary context about the content that is being shared: debunk what is false, and provide crucial information about time, date or location, as well as showing how this content relates to other material.
themes: verification labeling; transparency; context provision; partial verification
relevance_rationale: Labeling content with confirmed source, date, and location information is a transparency standard that transcends tools
notes: Alternative to 'cannot be independently verified' caveat: label what you CAN confirm

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We must seek informed consent, not just consent, meaning: Does the uploader understand what they are giving permission for? And when it comes to crediting, you must talk to them about whether and how they would like credit.
themes: informed consent; crediting; ethical practice; UGC
relevance_rationale: Seeking informed consent and discussing crediting implications with uploaders is a timeless ethical obligation

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Be aware that embedded content will disappear from your site if it is removed from the social network by the original uploader. So you should ultimately try to procure the original file, especially if you are planning to run the content for a long time.
themes: content preservation; embedding fragility; original file acquisition
relevance_rationale: Procuring original files rather than relying on embeds that may disappear is a timeless archival best practice

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It is best practice to 'label' who has captured the content. Was it a UNHCR staff member? Was it a freelance journalist? Was it a citizen journalist? Was it a refugee? When someone unrelated to the newsroom takes a picture that is used by the newsroom, for reasons of transparency, any affiliation should be explained to the audience. Simply labeling this type of material as 'Amateur Footage' or something similar doesn't provide the necessary context.
themes: source labeling; transparency; attribution; audience trust
relevance_rationale: Labeling content creator type (staff, freelance, citizen, refugee) for transparency is an editorial standard unaffected by AI
notes: Specificity in labeling: identify the creator's role/affiliation, not generic 'amateur' label

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Spiegel has very specific and detailed guidelines for fact-checking. This process ensures we apply the same standard to all work, and helps ensure we do not overlook key facts or aspects of a story. Any fact that is to be published will be checked to see if it is correct on its own and in context. Every verifiable piece of information will be underlined. Standardized marks will be used to denote statements as correct, incorrect, not verifiable, etc. Correct facts and figures will be checked off. If corrections are necessary, they will be noted. The source of factual corrections and quotations must be given.
themes: fact-checking protocol; standardized workflow; systematic verification
relevance_rationale: Organizational fact-checking standards ensuring uniform rigor across all work are institutional practices that transcend technology
notes: Der Spiegel's documentation department: 70 specialist fact-checkers using standardized manuscript markings

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: When fact-checking a manuscript, other and if possible more accurate sources than the author's sources should be used. A statement is considered verified only if confirmed by reliable sources or experts. If a piece of research contradicts an author's statement, the author must be notified of the contradiction during the discussion of the manuscript. If a fact is unverifiable, the author must also be notified.
themes: independent sources; fact-checking; author notification; unverifiable claims
relevance_rationale: Using more accurate sources than the author's endures, but LLMs can now rapidly surface alternative sources and cross-check claims at scale
notes: Fact-checkers must use sources independent of the author's; unverifiable facts flagged to author

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Sometimes the limited time available means that priorities must be set. In such cases, facts that are the clear responsibility of the fact-checker must be checked first, particularly: Are the times and dates correct? Does the text contradict itself? Are the names and offices/jobs correct? Are the quotations correct (in wording and in context)? How current and trustworthy are the sources used?
themes: triage; priority checking; time constraints; essential facts
relevance_rationale: Prioritizing fact-checks under time pressure endures but LLMs can now assist with rapid triage of checkable claims
notes: Five-item priority checklist when time is limited: dates, internal consistency, names/titles, quotations, source currency

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Fact-checking starts with comparing a story draft with the research materials provided by the author. The fact-checker then seeks to verify the facts and assertions by gathering additional sources that are independent of each other. For crucial passages, the checker examines a wide variety of sources in order to examine what is commonly accepted and believed and what is a more subjective or biased point of view. They determine what is a matter of fact and what is controversial or, in some cases, a myth.
themes: source evaluation; independent verification; consensus vs controversy
relevance_rationale: Comparing drafts against research materials endures but LLMs can now automate claim extraction and source matching from manuscripts
notes: Three-layer process: compare to author sources, gather independent sources, distinguish consensus from bias

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Prefer original documents. If an academic study is quoted, obtain the original, full text. If company earnings are cited, obtain their financials. Do not rely on press summaries and press releases when the original document can be obtained. Prefer sources that delineate between facts and opinion, and that supply facts in their work. Prefer sources that clearly indicate the source of their information, as this enables you to verify their work. Beware of sources that make factual errors about basic facts, or that confuse basic concepts about a subject matter.
themes: source hierarchy; original documents; source quality indicators
relevance_rationale: Preferring original documents over press summaries is a timeless verification principle about primary sources
notes: Four guidelines for evaluating sources: prefer originals, prefer fact/opinion distinction, prefer cited sources, beware basic errors

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Accuracy is the basic prerequisite for good journalism and objective reportage. Journalists make mistakes, intended or not. Mistakes damage the most valuable asset of journalism: credibility. The method to reduce the probability of mistakes is verification; that is, checking facts before publication.
themes: accuracy; credibility; pre-publication verification
relevance_rationale: Accuracy as the prerequisite for credibility is the most fundamental journalism principle, wholly independent of technology
notes: Supported by data: 1,153 corrections found in a single Spiegel issue by Hamburg University thesis

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: In a frame-by-frame analysis of roughly three hours of an archived livestream of the protest (324,000 frames!) the defense team uncovered one frame of video that showed that the police vehicle Seixas was being accused of having set ablaze was the exact same vehicle that drove him away after he was detained. This was proven by comparing identifying characteristics of the vehicle in the video with the one that Seixas was transported in.
chapter: Video to help prove innocence of an activist in Rio
themes: frame-by-frame analysis; video evidence; exoneration; identifying characteristics
relevance_rationale: Frame-by-frame video analysis is valid but AI video analysis tools can now process 324K frames far faster than manual scrubbing
notes: WITNESS case: exhaustive frame-by-frame review of 324K frames to find exculpatory evidence

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: WITNESS helped the defense identify and prepare this evidence, both by assembling screenshots of these videos into a storyboard as well as by editing a 10-minute evidentiary submission video that was delivered to the judge, along with the accompanying documentation.
chapter: Video to help prove innocence of an activist in Rio
themes: evidence packaging; storyboard; evidentiary video; court submission
relevance_rationale: Assembling evidence storyboards from video endures as a practice but AI tools can now automate screenshot extraction and timeline assembly
notes: Packaging video evidence for legal use: storyboard of screenshots + edited submission video + documentation

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Reymond took a full two hours to verify its authenticity before posting it to his Twitter account. Reymond's process of verification included comparing the facial features of the man in the photo to the mugshot that later emerged, and cross-referencing other details that emerged as witnesses and officials shared details of the attack.
themes: image verification; facial comparison; cross-referencing; breaking news
relevance_rationale: Taking hours to verify photo authenticity through facial comparison and source triangulation reflects the care verification demands
notes: Two-hour verification before publishing: facial comparison + cross-referencing witness details

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The claim that the photo of Zehaf-Bibeau originated with an ISIS account is categorically false. The ISIS account that circulated the photo acquired it hours after it was originally posted to Twitter. The temptation to draw a connection between vivid photos like this one and our worst fears is enormous. Avoiding this temptation is one of the chief responsibilities of 21st century journalists.
themes: source attribution; confirmation bias; misinformation; provenance tracking
relevance_rationale: Tracing provenance chains to debunk false attribution of images is a timeless verification skill
notes: Ottawa shooting case: journalists attributed photo to ISIS when it actually came from a journalist; technical metadata proved provenance

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The first step is to determine which language to search for the information you need, and then to find the spelling in Arabic. However, keep in mind that the pronunciation of a single word can differ greatly among Arabic-speaking countries. Be aware that even within the same registry you should search using multiple spellings of the same word.
chapter: Searching for companies in the Middle East
themes: transliteration; multilingual search; Arabic registries; spelling variants
relevance_rationale: Multi-language and transliteration searching endures but LLMs now handle Arabic name variants and cross-script searching natively
notes: Company names may have multiple valid Arabic transliterations across countries; must search all variants

author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: These examples demonstrate how an understanding of cultures, languages and other factors can play a role in ensuring how effectively you can make use of public data and information during an investigation.
chapter: Searching for companies in the Middle East
themes: cultural competence; language barriers; investigative effectiveness
relevance_rationale: Cultural and linguistic understanding for effective use of public data is a human competency that LLMs support but cannot replace

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: When setting up your data team, it is absolutely ok to start small. The diversity of skills matters more than team size. However, avoid having a single data journalist and expecting that person to do everything.
themes: team composition; skill diversity; staffing
relevance_rationale: Starting small with diverse skills endures but team skill requirements shift when LLMs cover some coding and design tasks

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: The diversity of skills is more important than the size of the team. The key skills are Code, Design, and Journalism. Look for generalists with skills in at least two of the Code, Design and Journalism areas, and be willing to teach them the third.
themes: team composition; skill diversity; hiring
relevance_rationale: Code-Design-Journalism triad endures but LLMs blur boundaries as journalists can now code and design with AI assistance

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: All data team members, regardless of their discipline, are part of the news desk. They should be treated as journalists and sit within the desk to encourage communication and collaboration.
themes: team integration; newsroom culture; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Data team members as part of the news desk, not siloed, is a permanent organizational principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: The data team should not be seen as a 'service desk' that is called in to help solve a technical issue during a project or asked to create graphics at the end of the reporting process. It should be involved in the project as early as possible.
themes: team integration; workflow; early involvement
relevance_rationale: Data team as partner from project start rather than service desk is a permanent workflow principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Team members should be given self-learning time in their work schedule so they can expand or strengthen their skill-sets.
themes: professional development; skill building; team management
relevance_rationale: Allocating self-learning time for skill development is a permanent team management practice

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: For graphics, cut out those that are ineffective (dull), unnecessary (simply break up the text) or that add little or no value (don't help tell the story more effectively). Focus resources on improving the quality of essential visual elements, and optimize those graphics for platforms where your audience is most active.
themes: graphics strategy; resource allocation; audience
relevance_rationale: Cutting ineffective graphics and focusing resources on essential visual elements is permanent editorial discipline

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: A quick way to kick-start a data project is to pick an issue where data is readily available, especially in countries where government data is not public. Another consideration is to piggy-back on similar projects by media in other countries so your team can reproduce or innovate based on them. Issues like healthcare, government budget/spending and voting are some low-hanging fruit. Choose one with broad public appeal and a relatively long shelf life to maximize the impact.
themes: topic selection; story ideation; data availability; impact
relevance_rationale: Starting with available data on topics with public appeal and long shelf life is permanent project selection wisdom

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: When providing data journalism training for your newsroom, include managers and editors at least in the introduction or overview part. You want them to empower the journalists and be able to edit data stories.
chapter: Encouraging and Nurturing Data Culture in the Newsroom
themes: training; editorial buy-in; data literacy
relevance_rationale: Including managers and editors in data journalism training is a permanent organizational change management practice

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Customize training for your newsroom as every newsroom is different. Use participants' stories as examples or in exercises. Use real data relevant to topics covered by the participants. Start with tools/skills that have a low learning curve and will be used frequently.
chapter: Encouraging and Nurturing Data Culture in the Newsroom
themes: training; curriculum design; skill building
relevance_rationale: Customizing training with real data and participants own stories is timeless adult learning pedagogy

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Sit programmers, designers and journalists together. Proximity works. Use an instant communication tool like Slack for the whole newsroom. Have 15-minute daily stand-up meetings, where those in the newsroom share briefly what they did yesterday and what they plan to do today.
themes: collaboration; workflow; communication; agile methods
relevance_rationale: Proximity and Slack for collaboration endure but LLMs as team productivity tools change daily standup content and communication patterns

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Break down perceptions: i.e. developers and designers should not 'serve' journalists. They are an integral part in finding and presenting stories and designing how users interact with them.
themes: collaboration; team roles; newsroom culture
