relevance_rationale: Breaking down perception that developers serve journalists; all are integral to finding and presenting stories is permanent

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Involve everyone early in the project to build a shared sense of ownership. Start with projects on less time-sensitive issues to experience the collaborative process without rushing to meet a deadline.
themes: collaboration; project management; ownership
relevance_rationale: Involving everyone early to build shared ownership and starting with less time-sensitive projects is permanent project management wisdom

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: The key to producing such projects is story selection. Below are some characteristics that data teams should look for when conceiving projects: Evergreen or recurring topics, topics with broad and strong public interest, data that can be personalized.
themes: topic selection; story ideation; impact; personalization
relevance_rationale: Selecting evergreen topics with broad public interest and personalizable data is permanent story selection criteria

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: When such a need arises, the data team will ask developers to build what's required. Over time, the team will find some components or presentation formats are used repeatedly. This is when tools and templates should be built for journalists to create the components or plug in different types of content into the layout. The user interface should be easy enough for journalists to edit the content directly without touching any code.
themes: reusable tools; templates; efficiency; newsroom infrastructure
relevance_rationale: Building reusable templates endures but LLMs enable rapid generation of presentation components, reducing need for pre-built templates

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: If you can frame your question, hypothesis or reason in more specific terms, this helps make your reporting more efficient. Instead of 'Has crime risen or fallen in Gotham City?' use 'Has the intentional homicide rate risen or fallen in Gotham City despite the city tripling its crime-fighting spending in the last 3 years?'
themes: hypothesis framing; specificity; research questions
relevance_rationale: Framing specific hypotheses rather than broad questions to guide reporting is a permanent research design principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: When government data is not accessible or is unreliable, journalists should look into other data sources: international/regional data portals, research reports, academics and universities, professional bodies and trade associations, NGOs, crowdsourced data, and APIs.
themes: data sources; alternative sources; data access
relevance_rationale: Looking beyond government data to international portals, academics, NGOs, and APIs is permanently relevant for data access

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Asking your audience to provide you with data is usually a last resort on an issue your audience cares about but where no-one has collected any data. It can, though, be a challenge to verify crowdsourced data. However, the benefits of filling the data vacuum or complementing existing incomplete or flawed data can outweigh the shortcomings.
themes: crowdsourcing; data collection; verification; data gaps
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing as last resort with verification challenges but potential to fill data gaps remains relevant

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Crowdsourced data journalism is not just about asking users for data points. Some successful crowdsourced data journalism projects asked users to help on tasks that can't be computer-automated. The Guardian, for example, asked readers to sieve out important information from some 700,000 documents in the MP's Expenses project.
themes: crowdsourcing; distributed analysis; human computation
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing for tasks that cant be automated is partially displaced as LLMs can now handle many document review tasks

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Always question the quality of your data. Apply the same journalism ethics and standards - such as accuracy and accountability - to data-driven stories.
themes: data quality; accuracy; accountability; ethics
relevance_rationale: Questioning data quality and applying journalism ethics and standards to data-driven stories is permanent

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Your data might not be able to answer all these questions, but that doesn't mean you can't use it. The decision to use or not is an editorial one, just like whether you want to use the information provided by your sources. The circumstances vary, but what every data journalist can and should do is to be open and honest to their users. Let them know the weaknesses and limitations of your data. Explain your decision to use that data if necessary.
themes: transparency; data limitations; editorial judgment; honesty
relevance_rationale: Being transparent about data limitations and explaining editorial decisions about imperfect data is permanent

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: A good practice is to build a data biography - a document to record all the answers to the questions above.
themes: data biography; documentation; provenance
relevance_rationale: Building a data biography to document provenance and quality answers is a permanent best practice
notes: References Heather Krause data biography template

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Is the data accurate? Does it reflect the real world objects? Do you have enough data? Is all necessary data present? Did you get the data for the relevant time period? Are all data values clearly defined by the data provider? Does it come with a data dictionary/codebook? Is there another source that collects similar or parallel data? Who collects, processes, publishes and maintains the data? Why, when and how was the data collected? Did the collection method change over time? Does the data come with disclaimers, limitations or assumptions?
themes: data evaluation; data quality checklist; provenance; methodology
relevance_rationale: 11-question data quality checklist covering accuracy, provenance, methodology, and limitations is permanently essential
notes: 11-question checklist for evaluating data

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Before doing anything to the dataset, keep a copy of the original in case you make mistakes and lose access to the data source.
themes: data preservation; backup; original data
relevance_rationale: Keeping a copy of original data before any manipulation is a permanent best practice

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Data cleaning also involves transforming data: Split data into more than one data point - e.g. first and last name, street name and postal code. Transform data points into the same unit. Transform money values so they have a common time basis. Transform date and time into consistent formats. Transform the data structure in a spreadsheet to a proper layout e.g., only one row for header, one data point in each row.
themes: data transformation; normalization; standardization
relevance_rationale: Data cleaning transformations (splitting, normalizing, standardizing) endure but LLMs now automate much of this work

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: When you're about to start cleaning or processing your dataset, it is a recommended practice to log every step. This not only allows your colleagues or editors to 'bulletproof' your data and reporting, it also gives you a clear path to trace and reverse processes. It can be a useful reference when you deal with similar datasets in the future.
themes: audit trail; reproducibility; logging; documentation
relevance_rationale: Logging every cleaning step for audit trail, bulletproofing, and future reference is a permanent reproducibility practice

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: When dealing with a data sample, scrutinize its size, sampling method, margin of error and confidence level to avoid misleading your audience.
themes: sampling; statistical literacy; margin of error
relevance_rationale: Scrutinizing sample size, sampling method, margin of error, and confidence level is permanent statistical literacy

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Be extra careful in making cause and effect conclusions among data points. Correlation between two variables does not mean causation.
themes: correlation vs causation; statistical reasoning; analytical pitfalls
relevance_rationale: Correlation does not imply causation is a permanent analytical principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: In most cases you just need to perform basic statistical and mathematical procedures to find insight from the data. Know how to use spreadsheet program functions to find: Total, Maximum/minimum values, Averages: mean, median, mode, Percentage, Percent change, Rate: per capita, per cases, Distribution of data.
themes: basic statistics; spreadsheet analysis; descriptive statistics
relevance_rationale: Basic statistical procedures via spreadsheet functions endure conceptually but LLMs can compute these from natural language queries

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: As a rule of thumb, you should calculate both the mean and median. If they are close, stick with the 'mean', if they are far apart, because of extreme outliers, use the 'median'.
themes: mean vs median; statistical reasoning; skewed data
relevance_rationale: Mean vs median rule of thumb for handling skewed data is a permanent statistical reasoning principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Look out for: TREND - how the variables have changed over time or across groups. CONTRAST - the differences among comparable variables. OUTLIERS - data points that are far from the average (but double check to make sure they are not errors).
themes: analytical patterns; trend analysis; outlier detection; comparison
relevance_rationale: Looking for trends, contrasts, and outliers as analytical patterns is a permanent data analysis framework

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Analyzing a dataset can prompt more questions that require other datasets to answer. For example, when you compare the number of murder cases in two cities, you need their population numbers to calculate the number of cases per capita for a fair comparison.
themes: combining datasets; per capita rates; fair comparison
relevance_rationale: Combining datasets for fair comparison using per capita rates is a permanent analytical principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Many insights in the data are hidden from these 'interview' techniques. You need to convert the data into visual forms to see them. Relationships between variables, trends and insights within geospatial data are best recognized using visualization. The goal of visualization at this stage is exploratory data analysis rather than presentation.
themes: exploratory visualization; EDA; visual analysis; hidden patterns
relevance_rationale: Exploratory visualization to reveal hidden patterns endures but LLMs now generate exploratory charts from prompts

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: As in other forms of journalism, data journalism is vulnerable to mistake and bias. All the journalism ethics and standards apply to data-driven stories, but some steps are different from traditional reporting.
themes: ethics; bias; verification; standards
relevance_rationale: All journalism ethics and standards apply to data-driven stories is a permanent principle
notes: References ProPublica bulletproofing guide

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: It cannot be emphasized enough that the goal of data visualization is to communicate information effectively. A common mistake among designers is to go for a powerful graphic rather than powerful journalism. This results in fancy and decorative visualizations that are unclear, confusing or misleading.
themes: visualization purpose; clarity over decoration; communication
relevance_rationale: Goal of visualization is effective communication, not decorative complexity -- permanent design principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Data visualization expert Alberto Cairo has four principles for good visualization: Based on good data. Attract attention. Don't frustrate readers. Show the right amount of data.
themes: visualization principles; Alberto Cairo; design guidelines
relevance_rationale: Cairo four principles for good visualization based on good data, attention, clarity, and right amount of data are permanent

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Start by defining your goal. What is the single most important message you want your audience to take away from your visualization? Avoid trying to squeeze in too many messages. When you are clear with the message, select only enough data points to be visualized.
themes: message clarity; focus; data selection; user-centered design
relevance_rationale: Defining a single message goal before visualization design is permanent communication discipline

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Next, identify your targeted or potential users, and understand them. What is their data literacy level? What kind of charts do they understand better? These questions will help you decide which type of charts to use. Always use charts that users are most familiar with.
themes: audience analysis; data literacy; chart selection; user-centered design
relevance_rationale: Understanding audience data literacy for chart selection is permanent user-centered design practice

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Perform user-testing on your design, and redesign it based on user feedback. User-testing is not necessarily expensive or resource-intensive. Studies found that just 5 users can find 85% of the usability problems in your design. Don't ask your newsroom colleagues to be your test users unless journalists are your target users.
themes: user testing; iteration; usability; design process
relevance_rationale: User testing with 5 users finding 85 percent of usability problems is permanent UX research principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: The key to your design thinking should be knowing what device users use to access your product. Bear in mind a saying among developers at news organizations with a majority of users on mobile - if it doesn't work on mobile, it doesn't work.
themes: mobile-first; device awareness; responsive design
relevance_rationale: Mobile-first design thinking is a permanent platform consideration

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Avoid using pie charts to present your data as they are often less effective and widely misused. In most cases, a pie chart can be replaced by a bar chart.
themes: chart selection; pie charts; bar charts; visualization best practice
relevance_rationale: Avoiding pie charts in favor of bar charts is a permanent visualization best practice based on perceptual science

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: No 3D effects: These distort the data as the part closer to the viewer appears larger. Also avoid color gradients and drop shadows. These are unnecessary visual noise. Sort data from largest to smallest. Use only 2-3 colors in the same color in your graphic. Your headline should tell the story. Use direct labeling whenever possible. Don't let your audience jump back and forth between a graph and a legend. Make your message stand out from the rest: Use color tone, labeling or other visual effects to draw attention to the element that carries your message.
themes: visualization rules; chart design; headline writing; labeling
relevance_rationale: Six golden rules for chart design (no 3D, sort data, limited colors, story headlines, direct labeling, highlight message) are permanent
notes: 6 golden rules adapted from Eva Constantaras

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Although visualization plays a major role in most data-driven stories, it has to be complemented by other traditional storytelling elements such as text, image and video. In some cases data visualization is not the primary medium to communicate the data.
themes: storytelling; multimodal; narrative; complementary elements
relevance_rationale: Visualization complemented by text, image, and video for multimodal storytelling is a permanent communication principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: A major pitfall of data-driven stories is putting numbers above journalism. Ultimately, journalism should be about people, but data can sometimes turn human stories into hard cold numbers. It is imperative to humanize data by going to the ground and telling the stories of people whose lives are affected by the data.
themes: humanization; people-centered; storytelling; journalism values
relevance_rationale: Humanizing data by telling stories of affected people is a permanent journalism value

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Personalization brings users closer to the data, making it more engaging and relevant. In data-driven stories, personalization is often through interactive features or news apps that invite users to input their details before showing data based on those inputs.
themes: personalization; interactivity; engagement; news apps
relevance_rationale: Personalization through interactive features making data relevant to individual users is a permanent engagement strategy

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Another way to communicate your data is to build tools that allow other users to use your data based on their own needs.
themes: utilization; news apps; public tools; data access
relevance_rationale: Building public tools for users to explore data based on their own needs is a permanent service journalism model

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Don't hide your data. Open your data to public, including your competitors. Exposing data to experts and other users allows more findings and insights to be extracted. Sharing data, tools, technical know-how and newsroom workflow across news organizations has created a synergy that elevates the industry's capabilities as a whole. The wheel doesn't have to be reinvented by everyone every time.
themes: open data; sharing; collaboration; open source; transparency
relevance_rationale: Open data, sharing tools and workflows to elevate the entire industry is a permanent collaborative journalism principle

author: Kuang Keng Kuek Ser (MDIF)
extract: Everyone in the newsroom, including editors, should be equipped with basic spreadsheet knowledge as this is the foundation that serves as a 'gateway' to other forms of data journalism.
chapter: Encouraging and Nurturing Data Culture in the Newsroom
themes: data literacy; spreadsheet skills; newsroom culture; training
relevance_rationale: Basic spreadsheet knowledge as gateway to data journalism endures but LLMs now provide an alternative gateway through natural language data analysis
notes: Attributed to La Nacion

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Appropriately using data in journalism requires an apt understanding of data parameters and properties, such as data origins and sources, as well as the processes and practices of producing data. Being aware of power relations embodied in data, as well as potential social factors that influence data, is a precondition for journalists to be able to provide a proper interpretation of data for audiences.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Understanding data
themes: data literacy; power relations; interpretation; context
relevance_rationale: Critical thinking about data provenance, power relations, and contextual interpretation is a foundational literacy that no LLM can replace.
notes: Foundational claim about prerequisites for sound data journalism

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data provenance, collection, curation, access, and the context where all these happen are all under the influence of existing power relations. Therefore data and data systems are far from being objective or neutral but are deeply embedded and laden with social values and norms.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Data as power
themes: power relations; social construction; bias; provenance
relevance_rationale: Understanding that data embeds power relations and social values is a critical epistemological insight that persists regardless of tools used.
notes: Core epistemological claim: data is never neutral

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The material and power nature of data leads to the importance of metadata — data about certain data — and other related information that outlines data origin and context in relation to how the data was collected and produced, as this information can help users understand how data should be handled, analysed and interpreted.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Data as power
themes: metadata; provenance; interpretation; data quality
relevance_rationale: Metadata literacy and understanding data provenance remain essential even when LLMs can process data faster.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: By analysing data, journalists can well play their various roles as information providers, watchdogs or custodians of conscience. Data is an alternative, rich source of information from which journalists can find stories or even secrets that are important to the public interest.
chapter: Data as a source of information — The abundance of data and its benefits
themes: accountability; watchdog role; public interest; story discovery
relevance_rationale: The watchdog and accountability roles of data journalism are purpose-driven and persist regardless of tooling.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data stories do not have to be developed from large-scale data and involve long-time investigations but can come from small data with quick investigations. The Times' land banking story was developed from open data released by the land registry, and the whole reporting process was quick and efficient.
chapter: Data as a source of information — The abundance of data and its benefits
themes: open data; story discovery; efficiency; small data
relevance_rationale: The insight that impactful stories can come from small data with quick investigations remains true irrespective of AI capabilities.
notes: Counters assumption that data journalism requires big data

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Errors and inaccuracies mar data's quality. Any small mistakes occurring in the process of data production and curation may mean the inaccuracy of data, which makes verifying data incredibly hard, if not entirely impossible.
chapter: Data as a source of information — The uncertainty and messiness of data
themes: data quality; errors; verification limits
relevance_rationale: Data quality concerns and the difficulty of verification are fundamental challenges that LLMs do not eliminate.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Much data is unstructured and thus not machine-readable. Most (90%) of big data is unstructured data. Different origins of data and different requirements during different times when data is produced may mean data would end up in incompatible, incoherent, and unusable formats.
chapter: Data as a source of information — The uncertainty and messiness of data
themes: unstructured data; data formats; messiness; interoperability
relevance_rationale: LLMs have dramatically improved ability to process unstructured data, making the 90% unstructured barrier less formidable.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: In theory, data is repairable if specific problems in data are known and if the data's size is manageable. In practice, verifying and cleaning data is hard partly because it requires an enormous investment of resources and related technical skills which may not be available. The enormity of data with incomplete metadata would also make it unrealistic to correct and verify every single error in data.
chapter: Data as a source of information — The uncertainty and messiness of data
themes: data cleaning; resource constraints; verification; scalability
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now assist with data cleaning and verification at scale, reducing the resource investment described here.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: When it comes to interpreting the actual meanings of numbers provided by governments, we — including journalists — will need to consider what diagnostic methods each government is adopting, how cases and deaths are counted, and the levels of transparency in different countries. The discrepancies in standards for producing data might lead to data journalists and news media misinforming the public.
chapter: Data as a source of information — The uncertainty and messiness of data
themes: interpretation; comparability; methodology awareness; misinformation risk
relevance_rationale: Critical interpretation of government statistics and understanding methodological differences remains a human judgment task.
notes: Illustrated through COVID data cross-country comparability problem

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The GDPR means that datasets published by political authorities may not contain any detailed data indicating personal information. The lack of personal information in data protects privacy but creates obstacles to finding stories from data and putting a human face onto data stories. Only providing summary data greatly reduces the potential of developing news stories in and from data.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Privacy and national security concerns
themes: privacy; GDPR; summary data; story discovery; regulation
relevance_rationale: GDPR constraints on data granularity are regulatory realities unaffected by AI tooling.
notes: Tension between privacy regulation and journalistic potential

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Some unstructured data and semi-structured data can be extracted and converted into structured data by using techniques and tools such as Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and data extraction and mining skills.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Data as material
themes: OCR; data extraction; format conversion; unstructured data
relevance_rationale: OCR and data extraction tools are being rapidly superseded by LLM-based document understanding and multimodal parsing.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The EU data portal often hosts data in pdf format, which is unstructured data, and thus a considerable impediment to users' use of such data. That some data portals may require high technical skills to use makes the situation worse. Users need to know how to convert data in XML and JSON formats to Excel or CSV, or access data via an application programming interface (API).
chapter: Data as a source of information — The problems with ordinary users using open/big data effectively
themes: data portals; PDF; API; format conversion; technical skills
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now parse PDFs and interact with APIs more accessibly, lowering the technical skills barrier described here.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: There is an urgent need for data journalists to act as data intermediaries between data providers and citizens, translating data for citizens and ensuring government and business transparency and accountability. The absence of metadata increases the difficulty in using and interpreting data, as ordinary citizens may not have the interest or resources to find out about the context where data is produced, curated and shared.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Three implications of data's promise and problems
themes: data intermediary; translation; metadata; public understanding; accountability
relevance_rationale: The intermediary role of translating data for citizens and ensuring accountability is a journalistic mission that persists.
notes: Journalists as bridge between data and citizens

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Using data as a source of information would need journalists to develop more technical and data-related skills and knowledge in terms of collecting, cleaning, analysing and visualising data. It involves fewer interactions with news sources than using traditional journalistic methods. Yet, using data for reporting requires human interactions and skills at some points, such as accessing data via FOIs, seeking clarification and explanations for data under analysis, verifying data and looking for individual human stories to be used in data stories.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Comparing data with traditional sources
themes: skills; data pipeline; human sources; verification; traditional methods
relevance_rationale: While combining data and human sources endures, the specific technical skills pipeline needs updating for LLM-assisted workflows.
notes: Data journalism still requires traditional journalistic skills at key points

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The scientific, rigid, statistical appearance of data may create an illusion that data can offer a more objective and accurate account of reality. Deceived by such an appearance with insufficient understanding of the potential problems associated with data, journalists may be unable to produce news stories to the highest standards. The deceptively objective appearance of data may make journalists let their guard down.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Comparing data with traditional sources
themes: objectivity illusion; critical thinking; data literacy; bias
relevance_rationale: The warning against false objectivity from data is even more relevant when LLMs can generate convincing but misleading statistical narratives.
notes: Key warning about false confidence in quantitative evidence

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Putting data in context not only means journalists need to interpret data in appropriate contexts. It also means they need to understand the context in which data was generated, collected and curated to gain an appropriate understanding of data before carrying out data analysis.
chapter: Data as a source of information — Comparing data with traditional sources
themes: context; interpretation; provenance; data collection methods
relevance_rationale: Contextualising data within its production circumstances is a critical thinking skill undiminished by AI tools.
notes: Dual meaning of context: interpretive context and production context

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: For data journalists, accessing data is the first and foremost goal before finding a story in it. Open data is only a part of the whole picture. Data journalists may not even know whether the required data exists and where they can access it. Data journalists may also face technical difficulties in accessing data, which may require a high level of data collection and information retrieval skills.
themes: data access; discovery; technical skills; open data limits
relevance_rationale: The challenge of discovering whether needed data exists and how to access it remains a fundamentally human investigative task.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data stories are often developed from an analysis of public records data obtained from data providers in the public domain such as statistics offices or other government bodies. The data used in 545 data-driven news stories published between 2011 and 2019 were mostly retrieved from government bodies. Over two-thirds of news apps produced by US news organisations used publicly available data.
chapter: Access to data — Accessing open data in the public sector
themes: public records; open data; government data; source patterns
relevance_rationale: The prevalence of public records as primary data sources for journalism is a structural reality unchanged by AI.
notes: Empirical finding on dominant data sources

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The first reason for prevalence of public records data in news reporting is the conventional thinking of official datasets as trustworthy: 'I'm generally using like government statistics, or the NHS or the Office for National Statistics. They will put my confidence in them, and they will usually highlight if they think some things are reliable.'
chapter: Access to data — Accessing open data in the public sector
themes: trust; official sources; reliability; institutional authority
relevance_rationale: Trust in official data sources as a journalistic convention persists and requires the same critical evaluation regardless of tools.
notes: Reflects continuity with traditional reliance on official sources

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Judging which data to analyse and report on follows the same news values as traditional journalism: 'there is so much data out there, you couldn't potentially turn it all into a piece of writing, and at least knowing what people actually care about like public interest topics will help us select data for reporting, it's the same as in any other kind of journalism.'
chapter: Access to data — Accessing open data in the public sector
themes: news values; story selection; public interest; editorial judgement
relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment about which data stories serve public interest is a core journalistic value that AI cannot replace.
notes: Data selection follows same editorial logic as traditional journalism

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: There are cross-country differences in terms of the types and state of open data provided and how easily journalists can access it. In Sweden, a data-friendly country with one of the world's oldest FOIAs, a government agency extracted data from the database and printed out the Excel file, put the printed paper copies back into the copy machine and made PDFs out of them, and the PDFs are not machine-readable.
chapter: Access to data — Accessing open data in the public sector
themes: cross-country variation; data formats; government data practices; PDF
relevance_rationale: Cross-country variation in open data formats and FOI regimes is a structural reality unaffected by LLM tools.
notes: Even in transparent countries, data delivery formats can be hostile

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Sometimes providing data to journalists for reporting can be mutually beneficial. For journalists obtaining such data can help them cover the story, and for the commercial bodies concerned, being mentioned in news stories for the data they provide would be a good publicity opportunity. Companies that sell proprietary datasets sometimes provide them for free because 'we always mentioned in our report where we've got data from so that can be quite useful for them.'
chapter: Access to data — Collaborating with data holders in the private sector
themes: private sector data; collaboration; mutual benefit; data sourcing
relevance_rationale: Relationship-based data sourcing from private sector collaborations is a human negotiation skill that persists.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Economist journalists wrote code to retrieve data from metro stations and museums in 18 cities every hour from Google Maps. Aggregating 150,000 files retrieved, they revealed the changes in foot traffic around selected metro stations and the impact of the pandemic on major cities. On April 30, 2020, Google Health started publishing COVID-19 Community Mobility Reports, which was seen as a response to the Economist's foot traffic reports.
chapter: Access to data — Collaborating with data holders in the private sector
themes: scraping; API; tech platforms; code; data collection at scale
relevance_rationale: While the investigative logic endures, LLMs and AI can now automate much of the code-writing for API scraping and data aggregation.
notes: Journalist-driven data collection can push tech companies to publish open data

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: When using data automatically collected from users or generated by users' use of certain tools, such data may contain errors or fake data, which is very difficult to verify. While cleaning and verifying large-scale data, some dodgy data may be removed by detecting anomalies, yet due to the scale of data, it would be a mission impossible to verify every single data entry. This difficulty in data verification reinforces the importance of data journalists' role in making a judgement about and endorsing the reliability of their data.
chapter: Access to data — Collaborating with data holders in the private sector
themes: verification; anomaly detection; user-generated data; credibility; scale
relevance_rationale: LLMs can assist with anomaly detection in user-generated data at scale, though the verification challenge core insight endures.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data like user-generated data may be influenced by the existing digital divide among users as it would include the data collected from and created by users who are using the tools but exclude those non-users. Without acknowledging the exclusion of non-users, journalists would risk reinforcing the division between users and non-users. If they use the data to represent the actual reality without properly acknowledging and explaining the limitations, their news reports may distort the actual reality and mislead the audience.
chapter: Access to data — Collaborating with data holders in the private sector
themes: digital divide; representativeness; bias; limitations disclosure; audience
relevance_rationale: Digital divide biases in user-generated data are structural inequalities that AI tools do not address and may amplify.
notes: Obligation to disclose data limitations

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Sending FOI requests for public records data that is not published publicly is a common practice. Journalists should endeavour to obtain data that is not in the public domain, as it is 'problematic if the journalists have to rely on the publication of figures'. If the data needed is held by public authorities, sending FOIA requests to obtain that data would be the primary choice to get data and get around obstacles to data access.
chapter: Access to data — FOI practices and the undisclosed secrets
themes: FOI; data access; public records; proactive sourcing
relevance_rationale: FOI as a proactive data sourcing practice is a legal and investigative strategy unaffected by AI capabilities.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data-orientated FOIs often require a round-robin approach, sending the same FOI to dozens and perhaps hundreds of the same organisations. This is extremely time-consuming as different bodies may interpret the request differently, return the data in different formats or refuse to give us the data. The question of effort versus reward is particularly important.
chapter: Access to data — FOI practices and the undisclosed secrets
themes: FOI; round-robin requests; data harmonisation; resource constraints
relevance_rationale: LLMs could help draft, harmonise, and process round-robin FOI requests at scale, updating the time-consuming manual process.
notes: Pamela Duncan, The Guardian, on declining FOI use

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Common strategies for submitting a successful FOI request include being specific in the requests and using specific statute language. If the rejection is based on costs exceeding the allowed cost limits or that the costs which authorities ask requesters to pay are too high, then asking the authorities to explain and break down the costs would be a good idea.
chapter: Access to data — FOI practices and the undisclosed secrets
themes: FOI strategy; request drafting; cost challenges; specificity
relevance_rationale: LLMs can assist with drafting FOI requests using specific statutory language, though strategic thinking still requires human judgment.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: When open data is unavailable and FOI requests end in vain, journalists may use alternative ways to get data: using traditional journalistic methods, scraping data from the Internet, using Internet advanced research and search skills, and adopting social science methods. Interviews with experts such as prosecutors and finding alternative data sources such as court case files would also be a potential solution.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: alternative data sources; scraping; social science methods; interviews; court records
relevance_rationale: While alternative data sourcing strategies endure, LLMs enhance scraping, text analysis, and social science method execution.
notes: Four alternative pathways when official channels fail

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Digital techniques can create datasets that you wouldn't otherwise be able to obtain. A journalist scraped each individual page on the listings, enabling analysis of how many were actually breached. Scraping through tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands of webpages makes it much more efficient than manually gathering data over a year.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: web scraping; automation; efficiency; dataset creation
relevance_rationale: Web scraping for dataset creation is now substantially easier with LLM-assisted code generation and data extraction.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The biggest challenge with leaked data comes from the difficulties in verifying the data. What data journalists would usually do is to cross-check the data from other sources to do their best to confirm its genuineness though they cannot be 100% sure. The size of large-scale leaked data means it may be beyond the ability of data journalists to handle such data.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: leaked data; cross-checking; verification; scale challenges
relevance_rationale: Cross-checking leaked data against other sources is a verification principle that remains essential regardless of tools.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: For the Panama Papers, journalists did not have the techniques required to analyse the mostly unstructured data of that scale. Most of the stories were done with the 'fishing method': you have a fishing rod with a name, throw it into the big sea of documents, and pull it back — you found David Cameron's father. That's not really data analysis, that's just free text searching.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: Panama Papers; unstructured data; text search; scale limits; methodology
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now process unstructured documents at scale, fundamentally changing the fishing method approach used for Panama Papers.
notes: Honest admission that massive leaks often reduce to keyword search rather than systematic analysis

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: When the data does not exist or is scattered and hidden, data journalists may turn to traditional social science methods, journalistic methods, advanced Internet research skills or crowd-sourcing to gather data and build their own datasets. The Minnesota Star Tribune and others like the Washington Post and The Guardian started building their own datasets basically from media reports.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: dataset building; social science methods; crowd-sourcing; media reports
relevance_rationale: LLMs can accelerate dataset building from scattered sources via text extraction, classification, and entity resolution.
notes: Journalists as primary data producers when no dataset exists

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The Washington Post collected data about police fatal shootings from different open sources such as local news reports, law enforcement websites and social media, and independent databases such as Killed by Police and Fatal Encounters, on top of filing FOI requests. Based on the information collected and verified, they built a fatal shooting database shared on GitHub for other user groups to reuse.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: multi-source collection; GitHub; database building; verification; open sharing
relevance_rationale: Multi-source data collection and verification endure as principles, but LLMs can automate monitoring and cross-referencing workflows.
notes: Exemplary pipeline: multi-source collection, verification, then open publication

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The Star Tribune journalists found few sex assault investigations resulting in convictions, so they requested and verified data — case files — from government departments through FOI requests to build their own database. Apart from case files, they also collected and verified hundreds of pages of court records and police documents and interviewed more than 100 assault survivors, sex crimes investigators, jurists, women's advocates and academic researchers.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: FOI; dataset building; court records; interviews; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Building investigative datasets through FOI, court records, and interviews is a human-driven investigative process that persists.
notes: Mixed-methods approach combining FOI, documents and interviews

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: In both the Guardian's Beyond the Knife and the Bureau of Investigative Journalism's dying homeless projects, the non-existence of related data made data journalists build datasets from scratch. FOI requests, monitoring media reports and interviews are three main journalistic methods they used to collect and verify data and build datasets.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: dataset building; FOI; media monitoring; interviews; absence of data
relevance_rationale: Dataset building from scratch via media monitoring and FOI can now be significantly augmented by LLM-powered information extraction.
notes: Three-method approach for building data where none exists

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The Guardian's MPs' expense receipts were far too big for journalists to go through manually, and because they are unstructured data, it was difficult to analyse them automatically. Therefore The Guardian turned to crowd-sourcing, inviting ordinary readers to look at the receipts, point out the dodgy ones and provide the reasons and some extra information.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: crowd-sourcing; unstructured data; scale; reader participation
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now process unstructured documents like expense receipts at scale, potentially displacing the crowdsourcing approach.
notes: Crowd-sourcing as solution to unstructured data at scale

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Statistical departments decide what kind of data to collect and produce, and their decisions are influenced by their perceived priorities and resources. The ONS statistician explained that something has to rise on their priority list either because of government targets, because a government department has asked them, or because it's become an issue of great public concern in the media. Otherwise, it stays on the wish list.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: data production; institutional priorities; statistical agencies; data absence
relevance_rationale: Institutional decisions about what data to collect reflect power dynamics that are structural and unaffected by AI tools.
notes: Why some data does not exist — institutional priority-setting

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Through using data for journalism, data journalists and news media can expose the data incompetency of governments by revealing problems and errors associated with government-published data, which are potentially pressurised to produce data of better quality and to open up data. In the NJ Advance Media force reports project, reporters found plenty of problems with the data provided by state governments, including misspellings, duplications, misclassification of agencies and errors in the data standardisation process.
chapter: Access to data — Struggles and pushing for data of quality and openness
themes: data quality; government data; error detection; accountability; cleaning
relevance_rationale: Exposing government data incompetency through journalism is an accountability function that persists regardless of tooling.
notes: Verification as accountability function

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Receiving unstructured data that is not machine-readable from government bodies is a big headache for journalists. The EU data portal often contains data in PDF format, which is really difficult to analyse and read. In Sweden, despite high media freedom, the data handed to journalists is often in pre-digital formats such as print-outs. It would have been more helpful if government bodies would publish machine-readable data in the first place.
chapter: Access to data — Struggles and pushing for data of quality and openness
themes: PDF; machine-readable data; government data; format barriers
relevance_rationale: LLMs have substantially improved PDF parsing and conversion to machine-readable formats, reducing this barrier.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Checking the genuineness of the Telegraph's leaked expenses data was a considerable challenge which involved crosschecking and interviews to achieve it. Following the publication, the government decided to regularly publish all MPs' expenses claims. The data published annually by the IPSA provides an excellent opportunity for the news media to hold MPs accountable for what they spend.
chapter: Access to data — Struggles and pushing for data of quality and openness
themes: cross-checking; interviews; leaked data; accountability; transparency outcome
relevance_rationale: Cross-checking and interviewing to verify leaked data authenticity is a core investigative practice that persists.
notes: Verification of leaked data led to permanent open data publication

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Realising there was no data about the deaths of homeless people in the UK, journalists in the Bureau Local started to collect and verify the data by setting up a Google alert for related local news, interviewing people and sending FOI requests. They met with the Office for National Statistics who then published the data. Their work was cited in 800 different articles.
chapter: Access to data — Struggles and pushing for data of quality and openness
themes: Google alerts; media monitoring; FOI; dataset building; impact
relevance_rationale: Google alerts and media monitoring for dataset building can now be augmented by LLM-powered monitoring and extraction pipelines.
notes: Journalist-built datasets can prompt official statistical agencies to produce the data

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The Bureau Local's Sold from Under You project obtained data about land and property sold by local authorities between 2014 and 2018 via FOI requests made to 353 local councils in England. They made an interactive, searchable map using the data. They marked the refusals on the map and shared the raw data received through their FOI requests on Google Drive for free.
chapter: Access to data — Struggles and pushing for data of quality and openness
themes: FOI at scale; interactive maps; open data sharing; transparency
relevance_rationale: FOI at scale with data sharing via interactive maps is a civic journalism practice that persists regardless of AI tools.
notes: Publication includes both findings and raw data plus transparency about refusals

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Larger news organisations such as The New York Times have the resources to file FOIA lawsuits, which smaller, poorly resourced newsrooms may not be able to do. The NYT has a two-lawyer legal team that filed 31 FOIA lawsuits during the Obama years. Winning from time to time, they were able to force the government to release secret information. When the paper won the lawsuits, the government even financially compensated them for their time and endeavour.
chapter: Access to data — Struggles and pushing for data of quality and openness
themes: FOIA litigation; newsroom resources; inequality; legal strategy
relevance_rationale: Resource inequalities in FOIA litigation are structural organisational realities unaffected by AI capabilities.
notes: Resource inequality shapes which newsrooms can fight for data access

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Building datasets from scratch using FOI requests, media monitoring and interviews is more likely to be found in relatively large, resource-rich and better-off newsrooms in developed countries and international non-profit newsrooms. Journalists in small, resource-poor and cash-strapped newsrooms in developing countries may not be able to do the time-consuming work.
chapter: Access to data — Alternative ways of getting data
themes: resource inequality; newsroom capacity; developing countries; dataset building
relevance_rationale: LLMs may reduce resource barriers for dataset building, partially democratising capabilities previously limited to rich newsrooms.
notes: Digital inequality in data journalism capacity

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: collaboration was at the heart of their data journalism. The majority of the data stories with participants credited in the by-lines were the outcomes of collaborative work between non-data journalists and data journalists.
themes: collaboration; newsroom integration; bylines
relevance_rationale: Collaboration between data and non-data journalists is an organisational practice that persists and may become more important with AI.
notes: Based on analysis of 8 UK data journalists across 3 national newsrooms

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data journalists having little or no programming skills would rely on third-party tools and thus be limited by their design.
themes: tools; skills gap; third-party tools; tool dependency
relevance_rationale: LLMs dramatically lower the programming barrier, enabling journalists to generate code without deep technical skills.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: the former continued to mainly use out-of-the-box tools due to their shortage of resources, while the later was more inclined to turn to developing in-house tools
themes: tools; resources; in-house vs third-party; commercial vs public service
relevance_rationale: The in-house vs third-party tool distinction is shifting as LLMs enable rapid custom tool creation even in resource-poor newsrooms.
notes: Comparing commercial outlets vs public service media in Australia

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: data journalists at national newsrooms would have more resources to conduct long-term, time-consuming investigations than those at local newsrooms. In the UK, for example, data journalists at Reach plc are inclined to mostly use open data that is publicly available, while those at The Guardian may have more time and resources to collect and build up their own datasets for reporting.
themes: resources; national vs local; open data; dataset creation
relevance_rationale: LLMs may narrow the resource gap between national and local newsrooms for data analysis capabilities.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: the BBC's shared data unit creates 'story packs', consisting of data – mostly open public records data retrieved from the data.gov.uk or through FOI requests – and data analysis and share them with local newsrooms so that they can find and develop their own angles.
themes: collaboration; story packs; BBC shared data unit; local journalism; FOI
relevance_rationale: The BBC shared data unit model of distributing analysis to local newsrooms is an organisational innovation that persists.
notes: Model for centralised data unit supporting distributed local reporting

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The data teams in regional media companies such as Reach plc, JPI Media and Newsquest work like in-house 'data wire', supplying data analysis and bulletins to local newsrooms within organisations.
themes: data wire; regional media; centralised analysis; local newsrooms
relevance_rationale: Centralised data wire models for regional media are organisational structures that persist regardless of AI tools.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: data journalists in India seldom use RTI requests to obtain raw data or collect data by using methods like crowd-sourcing, which their Western counterparts undertake
themes: RTI; FOI; crowdsourcing; data collection methods; Global South
relevance_rationale: The gap in FOI and crowdsourcing practices between Western and Global South newsrooms reflects structural conditions unaffected by AI.
notes: Contrasts Indian vs Western data acquisition practices

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: for well-resourced newsrooms like The New York Times, data journalists not only have sufficient time and skills to conduct time-consuming data analysis and long-term investigative projects but also are part of a team of people such as developers and graphic designers working together supporting data journalists' work. For smaller newsrooms that do not have this luxury, data journalists may have to be 'unicorns' with all-around skills.
themes: unicorn journalist; skills; team composition; resources
relevance_rationale: LLMs blur the unicorn journalist ideal by enabling broader skill application without deep expertise in each area.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The reporters mixed traditional reporting skills such as interviews and field visits with data analysis skills and techniques.
chapter: What is data used for in journalism?
themes: mixed methods; interviews; field visits; data analysis
relevance_rationale: Mixing traditional reporting with data analysis is a methodological principle that persists and strengthens with AI assistance.
notes: Describing Korea Times data story on Seoul housing conditions

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Using the digital data – the viral video clip of the killing and related data from other sources such as Google Earth, Sentinel Hub and Bellingcat – journalists were able to probe into the truth and explore events in the foreign land of Cameroon.
chapter: What is data used for in journalism?
themes: OSINT; geolocation; digital forensics; verification; Bellingcat
relevance_rationale: OSINT and digital forensics methods are being augmented by AI-powered geolocation, image analysis, and verification tools.
notes: BBC Africa Eye Anatomy of a Killing example

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: It analysed data from multiple sources and developed a compelling story about the pollution in the river... This story, combining human stories and interactive data visualisations, focusses readers' attention on water pollution, its causes and consequences and the gravity and urgency of the issue.
chapter: What is data used for in journalism?
themes: multiple sources; data visualisation; human stories; narrative
relevance_rationale: Combining human stories with interactive data visualisation for narrative impact is a storytelling principle that persists.
notes: Reuters 'Race to Save the River Ganges' example

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Language barriers act as a considerable obstacle to global collaborations or reporting on global topics. Despite the de-territorial access, data may be published in diverse languages... Without speaking and knowing the language used in a dataset, journalists would find it extremely difficult to use the dataset for reporting.
chapter: What is data used for in journalism?
themes: language barriers; global data; accessibility; translation
relevance_rationale: LLM translation capabilities are rapidly reducing language barriers for accessing and analysing global data sources.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: data published by government authorities is unlikely to contain personal information. Data publishers may merely publish pivot tables containing summary data generated from their data analysis and aggregation. A direct consequence of this is the difficulty in accessing detailed information about individual cases that are crucial for story-telling.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: data access; aggregation; summary data; privacy; individual cases
relevance_rationale: The tension between privacy protections and journalistic access to individual-level data is a structural issue unaffected by AI.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Poorly structured and messy data means data journalists need to convert it to structured data or to clean (prepare) it so that the data can be analysed using a computer. A data journalist from the Financial Times obtained data from the EU data portal. However, all documents were in pdf format, and it took them a very long time to convert these documents to machine-readable data.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: data cleaning; PDF conversion; structured data; messy data
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now parse PDFs and clean messy data far more efficiently than the manual processes described here.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Not only does the data need to be cleaned, but it must also be verified. Even public records data published by government bodies is not guaranteed to be clean or 100% reliable and thus does not need to be verified. A Guardian data journalist commented: 'it's difficult (to verify the data they have got) because even if you get something under FOI from a government office, it doesn't mean it's right... and ...there is always an element of human error in everything.'
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: data verification; government data; reliability; human error; FOI
relevance_rationale: The principle that even official government data requires verification is a critical stance that persists regardless of tools.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Cross-checking, a traditional journalistic technique, is thought to be a good strategy to deal with the problem of data reliability. Cross-checking data against different sources and with people who are close to data is necessary to verify and understand data for reporting purposes.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: cross-checking; triangulation; data reliability; verification; sources
relevance_rationale: Cross-checking data against multiple sources and human experts is a verification principle that persists in the LLM era.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: that's where you have to try to figure out if there are any problems with the data. And that comes from understanding how the data was collected, and what's supposed to be in there. And then a lot of it comes down to sense when you look at your data, and then something looks weird. Usually, that's a sign that something might be wrong. In talking to other sources, you show them your findings. When they come back at you and say that doesn't quite make sense, and it isn't quite what I'm seeing elsewhere. That's another red flag that maybe something is wrong: the underlying data or you do your analysis wrong or something like that.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: data provenance; sense-checking; red flags; source consultation; analysis verification
relevance_rationale: Understanding data provenance and sense-checking through source consultation remain essential human verification steps.
notes: Minnesota Star Tribune data journalist interview

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: the meanings of data may be influenced by contextual factors such as the personal judgements of people who record or curate data, the criteria for producing data and the factors that delay the production of certain data.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: data provenance; context; construction of data; curation bias
relevance_rationale: Recognising that data meanings are shaped by contextual factors like personal judgments and criteria is an enduring epistemological insight.
notes: Example given: homicide index changes due to pending coroner reports

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Basic journalistic techniques such as interviews and cross-referencing, and checking are considered to be essential in collecting and creating data and data verification. In cases where no data exists, interviews with people who know the situation are an effective way to collect and generate data.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: interviews; cross-referencing; data creation; primary data collection
relevance_rationale: Interviews and cross-referencing as essential data verification techniques are core journalism practices that persist.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: when dealing with leaked data, the reliability of data needs to be handled with extra care in case it is fake. In 2015, The Sunday Times, for example, invited experts to verify the genuineness of leaked data about the athletics doping scandal. The involvement of experts can also help to 'endorse' the credibility of their reports.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: leaked data; expert verification; credibility; doping scandal
relevance_rationale: Expert verification of leaked data authenticity is a trust-building practice that remains essential and cannot be automated away.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Journalistic instinct is also thought to be important when it comes to verifying data. A data journalist commented, 'my journalistic instinct is sort of my most important tool'... Following journalistic instinct, data journalists find exclusives, great stories and appealing angles for their stories, and they can spot hooks and fake tip-offs.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: journalistic instinct; tacit knowledge; story selection; verification
relevance_rationale: Journalistic instinct and tacit knowledge for story selection and verification are human capacities that LLMs cannot replicate.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: you need to try to find people who know the data and who may be who've worked with it and get them to answer those questions for you, like, what is the best source here.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: domain experts; human sources; data provenance; source evaluation
relevance_rationale: Consulting domain experts who know the data is a human relationship skill essential for data interpretation that persists.
notes: Minnesota Star Tribune journalist comparing data sourcing to traditional human sourcing

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: (to verify leaked data) is much much harder. And that is a process that we more and more have to do... It's been usually raw data documents, so we have tried to see if it's a public document, can we find this document in the public domain. And if it's an event, we will find news stories that could be corresponding news stories of this event. So we do as much as possible, trying to check the things that we can check.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: leaked data verification; public domain cross-check; Panama Papers
relevance_rationale: Verifying leaked documents by cross-referencing with public domain sources is a core investigative practice that persists.
notes: Journalist who covered Panama Papers

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: COVID-19 case and death numbers are 'inaccurate', reflecting changing testing capabilities, diagnosis, tracing and reporting situations during different periods and in different areas. COVID-data is influenced by the actual data generation and collection practices of governments, such as how many people are tested and what death cases are counted as caused by COVID.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: data quality; COVID; measurement error; counting methodology; comparability
relevance_rationale: Understanding measurement methodology limitations in official data is a critical literacy skill that persists regardless of AI tools.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: some (but not all) of the British news media such as The Guardian, The Independent and the Financial Times have acknowledged such an impact. They also draw readers' attention to the potential influence of different data collection and publishing schedules on the data about case and death numbers, as well as the discrepancies between official data and excess death.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: caveats; transparency; data limitations; methodology disclosure
relevance_rationale: Disclosing data limitations and methodology caveats to audiences is an ethical transparency practice that persists.
notes: Best practice: disclosing data limitations to readers

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: the case and death numbers may not always reflect the gravity of the pandemic in these countries. The data about case and death numbers needs to be treated carefully with appropriate explanations. It would be more appropriate to put these figures in the context of population sizes and the actual testing situations in these countries.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: normalisation; per capita; contextualisation; data presentation
relevance_rationale: Normalising data per capita and contextualising numbers is a statistical literacy principle that persists regardless of AI.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: News coverage started using case or death rate (cases or deaths per unit/population) rather than merely the number and death cases. Data reporters and newsrooms are learning to deal with the problems associated with data.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: normalisation; rates vs counts; learning; iterative improvement
relevance_rationale: Using rates vs counts and iteratively improving data presentation are enduring data literacy principles.
notes: Evolution of COVID reporting practice over time

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The Independent heat map published in July 2020, for example, simply showed COVID case numbers in selected countries from around the world but did not include the information on population sizes in these countries. With the biggest case number, the US would be easily interpreted as the worst-hit country on the map. However, given the huge population in the US, this might not be true. In January 2021, the same author published another article, which presented case numbers per million people, indicating that the UK was the country worst hit by the pandemic at the time.
chapter: Handling the problems of data and potential issues in data reporting
themes: heat maps; normalisation; misleading visualisation; per capita; before/after
relevance_rationale: The lesson about misleading visualisations lacking normalisation is an enduring data presentation literacy insight.
notes: Concrete before/after example of improving data presentation

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Data reporting is time-consuming, particularly when it comes to long-term investigative data projects. Allowing sufficient time for data journalists to complete such projects would be crucial. As well as supporting the 24/7 daily reporting cycle, data journalists at big newsrooms can have the luxury of spending many weeks, months or even years working on a project.
themes: time investment; investigative projects; newsroom resources; daily vs longform
relevance_rationale: The time investment required for investigative data projects is a resource reality that persists even with AI assistance.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The need to collect, clean and analyse data expands data journalists' work responsibilities to include the role of data analysts. Data journalists are expected to take on such new responsibilities and learn the related new skills, which imply potential changes in the identity and job perception of data journalists.
themes: role expansion; data analyst; skills; professional identity
relevance_rationale: LLMs are reshaping the data analyst role expansion, as journalists can now perform analysis tasks with AI assistance rather than solo skill-building.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Only those who have good knowledge of data and are aware of the problems associated with data can appropriately handle, manage and present data. The problems of data, such as the messiness of data, suggest the ethical practices of data journalism are particularly important.
themes: data literacy; ethics; messiness; competence; quality
relevance_rationale: The requirement for deep data knowledge to handle data problems competently is an enduring quality standard.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: as data reporting may be seen as more accurate and objective than traditional reporting, the inability to ascertain and handle the problems about data, which are subtle and may not be perceivable, may lead to inaccurate reporting appearing to be deceptively accurate and thus misleading.
themes: false precision; objectivity; misleading accuracy; data ethics
relevance_rationale: The warning against false precision and misleading objectivity from data is amplified in the LLM era where AI can generate convincing statistical narratives.
notes: Key epistemological warning about data journalism

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: one way of producing data is to collect digital data with skills such as data scraping, clean it and store it in a database. Cleaning data means spotting and correcting errors in data, correcting incorrect formats of data and preparing the data for analysis.
chapter: Opening up data from news reporting
themes: data scraping; cleaning; error correction; data production
relevance_rationale: Data scraping and cleaning workflows are being substantially augmented by LLM-powered code generation and data processing.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
