extract: They may also produce new data by using social science and journalistic methods such as interviews, surveys, media coverage monitoring or filing FOIA requests. When they access ready-to-download datasets, they invest time to correct errors in them and verify them and merge different datasets from different sources, they are also producing data.
chapter: Opening up data from news reporting
themes: data production; social science methods; FOIA; dataset merging; surveys
relevance_rationale: While social science methods endure, LLMs can accelerate surveys, media monitoring, and dataset merging processes.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: participants believed data journalists should embrace transparency, provide hyperlinks to news sources and provide the details about methodology and open-source codes. They thought these elements to be central to data journalism's ethos to increase its credibility and fight against dis/misinformation. But participants did not consider sharing data as crucial.
themes: transparency; methodology disclosure; open source; credibility; misinformation
relevance_rationale: Transparency through methodology disclosure, hyperlinks, and open source code is an enduring credibility principle.
notes: 2019 survey of Brazilian data journalists

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: news organisations like the BBC's shared data unit, the Bureau (Investigative Journalism) Local, The Minnesota Star Tribune and The New York Times publish their code and data on their websites or servers like GitHub, a platform originally designed for version control and software development.
themes: GitHub; open source; code sharing; data publishing; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: GitHub and code sharing practices need updating to include LLM prompts, AI-assisted workflows, and reproducibility of AI-augmented analyses.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: although most of these projects directly mentioned the data sources, few of them (20%) provided access to the data used in the projects, and less than half of them (48.8%) published their methodology
chapter: Why choosing not to publish data
themes: transparency gap; methodology disclosure; data access; award projects
relevance_rationale: The transparency gap in methodology and data sharing among award-winning projects is a structural accountability concern that persists.
notes: Study of Data Journalism Awards 2019 and Sigma Awards 2020 candidates

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: The transparency of the data and analysis process may disadvantage news media by exposing them to criticism which may challenge their authority and legitimacy.
chapter: Why choosing not to publish data
themes: transparency risk; authority; legitimacy; criticism; open analysis
relevance_rationale: The tension between transparency and vulnerability to criticism is a perennial institutional concern unaffected by AI tools.
notes: Illustrated by ProPublica Machine Bias case where published data/methodology drew academic criticism

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: They looked at reports dating back to 2007, sent 506 public records requests, gathered 72,677 use-of-force documents covering from 2012 to 2016 and verified and cleaned such data, which was originally messy.
themes: public records requests; data collection at scale; cleaning; verification
relevance_rationale: Large-scale public records collection and cleaning can now be partially automated with LLM-assisted processing.
notes: NJ Advance Media Force Report project, cost ~K

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: I couldn't publish the data in the format that it came in, because it will link back to the people... and we don't want to personally identify these people... We also have GDPR considerations which we won't have a couple of years ago.
chapter: Why choosing not to publish data
themes: privacy; GDPR; anonymisation; data protection; personal data
relevance_rationale: GDPR compliance, anonymisation requirements, and privacy protections are legal obligations that persist regardless of AI capabilities.
notes: Guardian data journalist on privacy constraints

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: data journalists play four roles in the circulation of data: (1) 'data intermediaries' between data holders and audiences, (2) 'translators' of data for audiences, (3) 'endorsers' for data and (4) data holders and publishers themselves.
themes: roles; intermediary; translator; endorser; data holder; circulation
relevance_rationale: The four roles of data journalists in data circulation are fundamental professional functions that persist in the LLM era.
notes: Core theoretical framework of the book

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Only data transparency – which means making both data and metadata (data that explains the data) publicly accessible – can be achieved, data can be regularly and appropriately used.
themes: transparency; metadata; data documentation; accessibility
relevance_rationale: Data transparency through accessible data and metadata is an enduring principle that becomes more important with AI-generated content.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: Checking and cleaning data and talking to those who are close to data definitely can help to correct errors if there are any and contribute to contextualising and interpreting the data. In so doing, the credibility of news organisations and data journalists also contributes to boosting or endorsing the credibility of data.
themes: verification; cleaning; contextualisation; credibility endorsement
relevance_rationale: Verification through cleaning, contextualisation, and consulting data-adjacent sources is an enduring credibility practice.

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: its journalists tracked the details of each killing by monitoring data from multiple sources such as 'local news reports', 'law enforcement websites and social media' as well as 'independent databases such as Killed by Police and Fatal Encounters', collecting additional information about the fatal shooting by police and filing FOIA requests with government departments
themes: multi-source monitoring; database building; FOIA; social media; independent databases
relevance_rationale: Multi-source monitoring for database building can now be substantially augmented by LLM-powered information extraction and classification.
notes: Washington Post police shootings database methodology

source_title: Data for Journalism: Between Transparency and Accountability
author: Jingrong Tong
extract: data visualisations can offer them the opportunity to explore information. Through exploration, audiences can have the autonomy to interpret the data and story and connect the interpretation to their personal experiences.
themes: data visualisation; interactivity; audience autonomy; exploration
relevance_rationale: Audience autonomy through interactive data exploration is an enduring design principle for data journalism presentation.

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Investigating the digital means taking nothing at face value. It means understanding that things which appear to be quantifiable and data-driven — likes, shares, retweets, traffic, product reviews, advertising clicks — are easily and often manipulated.
themes: verification; default-distrust; digital-signals
relevance_rationale: Digital signals being manipulable is even more true now that LLMs can generate fake engagement at scale; needs AI-specific examples
notes: Core epistemological stance: all quantitative digital signals can be gamed

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Think like an adversary. Each new feature of a platform or digital service can be exploited in some way. It's critical to put yourself in the shoes of someone looking to manipulate the environment for ideological, political, financial or other reasons. When you look at digital content and messages, you should consider the motivations driving its creation and propagation.
themes: adversarial-thinking; motivation-analysis; platform-manipulation
relevance_rationale: Adversarial thinking now must account for LLM-powered manipulation campaigns that exploit platforms at unprecedented scale and sophistication

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Focus on actors, content, behavior and networks. The goal is to analyze the actors, content and behavior and how they are to document how they might be working in unison as a network.
themes: network-analysis; actors; behavior-analysis
relevance_rationale: Analyzing actors, content, behavior and networks endures but must now include AI-generated content and LLM-operated bot networks

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Monitor and collect. The best way to identify media manipulation and disinformation is to look for it all the time. Ongoing monitoring and tracking of known actors, topics and communities of interest is essential. Keep and organize what you find, whether in spreadsheets, screenshot folders or by using paid tools like Hunchly.
themes: monitoring; archiving; workflow; tools
relevance_rationale: Ongoing monitoring endures but must now incorporate AI-generated content detection and LLM-powered monitoring tools

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Be careful with attribution. It's sometimes impossible to say exactly who's behind a particular account, piece of content, or a larger information operation. One reason is that actors with different motives can behave in similar ways, and produce or amplify the same kind of content. Even the platforms themselves — which have far better access to data and more resources — make attribution mistakes. The most successful and compelling evidence usually combines digital proof with information from inside sources — an ideal mix of online and traditional investigative work.
themes: attribution; evidence-standards; mixed-methods
relevance_rationale: Caution about attribution remains critical; LLMs make it even easier for different actors to mimic each others signals
notes: Digital + traditional methods needed for reliable attribution

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: At First Draft, we talk about the concept of the tipping point. For journalists, reporting on falsehoods too early provides additional and potentially damaging oxygen to a rumor. Reporting too late means it has taken hold and there is little that can be done. Working out that tipping point is challenging. It differs by location, topic and platform.
themes: tipping-point; amplification; editorial-judgment
relevance_rationale: The tipping point concept for when to report on falsehoods is a timeless editorial judgment framework
notes: No universal rule for when to debunk

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The life cycle has five points of action, where the tactics of media manipulators can be documented using qualitative and quantitative methods. Note that most manipulation campaigns are not 'discovered' in this order. Instead when researching, look for any one of these points of action and then trace the campaign backward and forward through the life cycle.
themes: lifecycle-model; campaign-tracing; qualitative-methods; quantitative-methods
relevance_rationale: The lifecycle framework for media manipulation endures but must incorporate AI-generated content as a new vector at each lifecycle stage
notes: Five stages: planning, seeding, responses, ecosystem changes, adjustments

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Grappling with the content and the context of disinformation requires us all to forensically document with rigor how campaigns start, change and end. And to recognize that every perceived ending of a campaign may very well be a new beginning.
themes: documentation; lifecycle-model; rigor
relevance_rationale: Forensically documenting campaign lifecycles with rigor and recognizing campaigns never truly end are timeless analytical principles

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: First, I plug the username into Google. People — especially younger ones who eschew the larger social platforms — tend to leave a trail even in more unexpected places, including comment sections, reviews and forums, that can lead you to information and other accounts.
themes: username-search; OSINT; google-search
relevance_rationale: Username tracing across platforms endures but LLM-powered OSINT tools now automate cross-platform identity resolution

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: When I find a username or email I think might belong to my subject, I plug it into an online tool like namechk or namecheckr that looks for username availability across multiple platforms. These tools are designed to be an easy way for marketers to see if a given username they're planning to register is available across platforms. But they're also useful for checking whether a username you're investigating also exists elsewhere.
themes: username-search; cross-platform; namechk
relevance_rationale: Specific tools like namechk/namecheckr are displaced by LLM agents that can check username availability across platforms programmatically
notes: Username match across platforms does not prove same person

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Google's reverse image search is fine, but often other search engines — especially Russia's Yandex — may deliver better results. I use the Reveye Chrome extension, which allows me to right click on an image and search for its match across multiple platforms including Google, Bing, Yandex and Tineye.
themes: reverse-image-search; Yandex; verification-tools
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search across engines endures but must now include AI-powered visual search tools beyond Google and Yandex

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: I don't just trust my eyes. Identifying characteristics across photos like moles or facial hair or features is helpful; lately, I also like to check it with a facial recognition tool like Face++, which allows you to upload two photos and then gives a probability that those belong to the same person.
themes: facial-recognition; identity-verification; Face++
relevance_rationale: Using facial recognition tools like Face++ needs updating for newer AI models and ethical concerns about facial recognition technology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It's not just profile photos that can be signposts, however. As people become more aware of and concerned with their own privacy and that of their family, they're still inclined to share photos of things they're proud of. I've identified people by connecting photos of things like cars, homes or pets. In this sense, photos become a means to connect accounts and the people behind them to one another, enabling you to build out the network around your target.
themes: identity-verification; photo-matching; network-building
relevance_rationale: Using pets, possessions, and location clues to link accounts exploits human behavioral patterns that persist regardless of technology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: With a new investigation, I like to start at the beginning of someone's Twitter history and work forward in time. You can get there by hand, with an assist from an autoscroller chrome extension, or you can use Twitter's advanced search to limit the time frame to the first few months of an account's existence.
themes: Twitter; chronological-analysis; social-media-investigation
relevance_rationale: Chronological account review endures but LLMs can now analyze entire account histories instantly rather than manual scrolling

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Because of that truth, it's always illuminating to look at an account's earliest followers and friends. TweetBeaver is a good tool for investigating the connections between large accounts and for downloading things like timelines and favorites of smaller accounts. For larger datasets, I rely on a developer with API access.
themes: network-analysis; Twitter; early-followers; API
relevance_rationale: Analyzing earliest followers and connections is still valuable but specific tools like TweetBeaver may be defunct; LLMs enable network analysis at scale

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: To find recently deleted tweets, you can search Google's cache; older deleted tweets can also sometimes be accessed in the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine or another archive. The manual archive site archive.is turns up several deleted tweets.
themes: deleted-content; archiving; Wayback-Machine; cache
relevance_rationale: Searching caches and archives for deleted content is a timeless investigative technique; platforms change but the principle persists

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Confirming a social account with the subject, family and friends, law enforcement and/or social media PR are ways to protect yourself from being duped.
themes: verification; identity-confirmation; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Confirming accounts with subjects, family, and law enforcement is traditional verification that no tool replaces
notes: Applies especially in breaking news

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Whether it's tracking your steps in a Google doc or letting a paid tool like Hunchly monitor as you search — is the key to clarifying connections between people and the lives they lead online, and turning those conclusions into stories.
themes: documentation; workflow; audit-trail; Hunchly
relevance_rationale: Documentation of investigative steps endures but tools like Hunchly may be dated; LLM-assisted research logging is now available

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Our first step in investigating the set of accounts was to try to verify if they were connected to real people. This part required good old fashioned fact-checking and began with our creating spreadsheets to track details related to the accounts, including the personal details they listed, the pages they liked and other information.
themes: fact-checking; spreadsheets; sockpuppet-detection; structured-data
relevance_rationale: Combining old-fashioned fact-checking with spreadsheet-based systematic tracking of accounts is enduring methodology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Using reverse image search tools, we found that many of 26 accounts used profile photos of celebrities or personalities.
themes: reverse-image-search; sockpuppet-detection; identity-verification
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search to identify stolen profile photos endures but now must account for AI-generated profile photos that evade reverse search

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Fake accounts are often used to spam groups with links, and you can sometimes catch them reusing the same text when they do it.
themes: sockpuppet-detection; duplicate-content; coordination-signals
relevance_rationale: Detecting link spam patterns and reused text across fake accounts remains a valid investigative technique

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We also found that OKD2.com shared the same Google AdSense ID as other websites, such as askphilippines.com and why0why.com, that the 26 accounts were sharing. We identified the AdSense IDs on these sites by viewing the source code of pages on them and looking for a series of numbers that began with the letters 'ca-pub-.' Each Google AdSense account is given a unique ID that begins with 'ca-pub-,' and each page of a site that is linked to an account will have this code on it.
themes: AdSense-ID; source-code; network-mapping; website-investigation
relevance_rationale: Tracing shared ad/analytics IDs across websites to expose network connections is a durable forensic technique
notes: Key technique for connecting seemingly separate websites

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Developing sources at tech companies who are willing to tell you more information than the company will say on the record is becoming increasingly important as many stories cannot be uncovered purely using open source information as scammers and bad actors become more sophisticated.
chapter: Case Study: Fake Black Lives Matter page
themes: sourcing; tech-companies; open-source-limits
relevance_rationale: Developing human sources at tech companies is increasingly important as platforms become more opaque; no AI substitute exists

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: What was particularly notable about this story was the array of techniques that Massler and I used to get it over the line. We relied heavily on archive sites like the Wayback Machine that allowed us to see the look of websites the page had been linking to and the page itself before it came on our radar. This was particularly useful, as after Massler initially contacted MacKay the people behind the page began trying to cover some of their tracks.
chapter: Case Study: Fake Black Lives Matter page
themes: archiving; Wayback-Machine; evidence-preservation; counter-measures
relevance_rationale: Combining archive sites, source code analysis, and traditional reporting is an enduring multi-method investigative approach
notes: Subjects may destroy evidence once aware of investigation

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The simple act of picking up the phone to talk to MacKay and developing sources to provide information that would otherwise not be made public — traditional journalism techniques — were critical in exposing this scam.
chapter: Case Study: Fake Black Lives Matter page
themes: traditional-reporting; phone-calls; mixed-methods
relevance_rationale: Traditional journalism techniques like picking up the phone remain critical for exposing what digital-only methods cannot
notes: OSINT alone insufficient; traditional techniques remain essential

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: By default, you should distrust search engines from primary sources. They can give you a false impression of the actual content of the website and its associated databases. The proper way to search is to perform a 'primary source check.'
themes: search-engines; primary-sources; default-distrust
relevance_rationale: Distrusting search engine results from primary sources and checking actual database content is a timeless verification principle
notes: Site-internal search often returns incomplete results

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Go to a generic search engine. Start with the query and end with 'site:' followed directly with the URL (no spaces). This is the formula for finding out if an original source shows you everything.
themes: Google-search; site-operator; primary-sources
relevance_rationale: Site-specific search operator syntax is displaced by LLM search tools that handle domain-scoped queries conversationally
notes: Primary source check formula: query + site:domain

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Try to find the common denominator of any online invitation. It's often a PDF document. Search for just that with 'filetype:pdf' and you might find it.
themes: Google-search; filetype-operator; document-trail
relevance_rationale: Filetype-specific search operators are displaced by LLM agents that find and parse documents across formats conversationally
notes: Part of a 3-step document trail method: establish type, be neutral on wording, identify who is involved

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Generic search engines like Google often struggle to deliver quality results from the trillions of posts on Twitter, or on big platforms such as Facebook and Instagram. The answer for Twitter is to use its Advanced Search functionality and add keywords, username and time period.
themes: Twitter; advanced-search; platform-specific-search
relevance_rationale: Platform-specific search tools like TweetDeck advanced search are displaced by LLM-powered social media analysis tools
notes: Google site: operator unreliable for social media platforms

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Exclude the target website by adding -site:friendsofscience.org. This helps you find information from outside parties.
themes: Google-search; exclusion-operator; network-mapping
relevance_rationale: Site exclusion operators are basic search syntax now handled conversationally by LLM search agents
notes: Excluding target site reveals third-party references

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: In general, we suggest the following approach for identifying inauthentic and potentially automated behavior on social networks: Manually check the accounts for suspicious behavior. Combine this with the use of tools or more technical network analyses. Investigate their activity, content and network of other accounts they interact with. Combine this with traditional investigation techniques, such as trying to contact them or people they claim to know. Consult with outside experts who specialize in bots and inauthentic activity.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: bot-detection; inauthentic-behavior; mixed-methods; expert-consultation
relevance_rationale: Combined manual and automated bot detection endures but must now account for LLM-powered bots that evade traditional detection heuristics
notes: Four-step approach combining manual, technical, traditional, and expert methods

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Bot detection tools are a great starting point but they should not be your sole evidence. One reason for their varying degree of accuracy is there is simply no universal list of criteria for recognizing bots with 100% certainty.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: bot-detection; tool-limitations; evidence-standards
relevance_rationale: Bot detection tool limitations are even more pronounced now that LLM-operated accounts blur the line between human and automated behavior

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Journalists must avoid falsely labeling suspicious accounts as bots without proper evidence and analysis, as a mistaken accusation can undermine your credibility.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: bot-detection; attribution-caution; credibility
relevance_rationale: Avoiding false bot accusations that undermine credibility is a timeless ethical principle of responsible reporting

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: One way to deal with these different types of bots, cyborgs and hyperactive human accounts is to focus your investigation on monitoring all inauthentic or bot-like behavior, instead of trying to identify only one type of suspicious account.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: bot-detection; inauthentic-behavior; investigation-scope
relevance_rationale: Focusing on inauthentic behavior rather than bot identity endures but LLM-generated content blurs the behavior/identity distinction further

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If you come across such an account, analyze the account's tweet activity more deeply. Take the number of tweets located at the top of the page, and divide this by the number of days the account has been active.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: bot-detection; tweet-frequency; quantitative-analysis
relevance_rationale: Tweet-per-day activity analysis is still useful but LLM bots can now mimic realistic posting frequencies, requiring more sophisticated analysis
notes: Calculate tweets-per-day to gauge plausibility

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: To get a better understanding of the activity of a whole bot network, you can use a visualization platform like Gephi.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: Gephi; network-visualization; bot-networks
relevance_rationale: Gephi network visualization endures but LLM-powered graph analysis tools can now automate community detection and visualization

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: For now, a methodical analysis of account behavior, content, interactions and patterns remains the best approach for identifying inauthentic behavior.
chapter: Spotting bots, cyborgs and inauthentic activity
themes: bot-detection; methodical-analysis; behavior-patterns
relevance_rationale: Methodical behavior analysis remains best practice but must now account for LLM-generated content that defeats traditional behavioral signals

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We wanted to focus on pro-China accounts and content, since these were the ones Twitter had already found engaging in inauthentic activity. We tried keyword formulations like: 'Shame on Hong Kong' -police -government. This search yields results that contain the phrase but not the words police or government.
chapter: Case study: Hong Kong protests Twitter activity
themes: Twitter-search; Boolean-operators; keyword-refinement
relevance_rationale: Crafting targeted keyword formulations to isolate coordinated activity is enduring investigative craft
notes: Negative keywords to isolate pro-government content

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: You can filter by engagement simply by adding 'min_retweets:500' or 'min_faves:500' to your search query. This will get only tweets with at least 500 retweets or likes.
chapter: Case study: Hong Kong protests Twitter activity
themes: Twitter-search; engagement-filter; viral-content
relevance_rationale: Platform-specific engagement filter syntax like min_retweets is displaced by LLM tools that query social data conversationally

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The case study shows how it is possible to combine manual and automated techniques to quickly uncover a network of suspicious Twitter accounts. It also illustrates that it is useful to look for additional accounts and activity even after a platform announces a takedown of accounts.
chapter: Case study: Hong Kong protests Twitter activity
themes: network-analysis; manual-automated-mix; post-takedown-monitoring
relevance_rationale: Combining manual and automated techniques and seeking additional evidence beyond platform-provided data is enduring methodology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The key to monitoring and debunking during breaking news is to lay a strong foundation before it happens. This means having a solid grounding in basic verification skills, understanding how to monitor social networks and platforms, and knowing how to respond if you or your colleagues become targeted by bad actors.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: breaking-news; preparation; verification-skills; safety
relevance_rationale: Laying groundwork for verification before breaking news happens is enduring preparedness principle

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Before looking for hoaxes, set up a folder for your documents and start a spreadsheet for what you find. Immediately take a screenshot of each hoax and relevant piece of content you discover, and archive the page. Be sure to record the original and archived URLs of the content in your spreadsheet.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: archiving; workflow; spreadsheets; evidence-preservation
relevance_rationale: Archiving evidence immediately via screenshots and spreadsheets is enduring verification workflow

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: To avoid helping spread pages associated with dis- or misinformation, be sure to link to the archived URL in any articles or social media posts instead of the original. It is also a best practice to watermark your images with a clear label such as 'False' or 'Misleading' to ensure they are spread and indexed with the proper context. If you do write an article, focus your headline and copy on what is true, rather than primarily saying what is false. Studies have shown that repeating falsehoods can cause people to retain the incorrect information.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: debunking; responsible-reporting; amplification-avoidance; watermarking
relevance_rationale: Linking to archived URLs to avoid amplifying disinfo and watermarking screenshots are enduring practices
notes: Truth-first framing reduces retention of falsehoods

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: As the event unfolds, come up with a list of locations and relevant keywords. For location, take into account the city, state and country, and any relevant local terms such as the nickname for a city or affected neighborhood. Note that during stressful situations, people may misspell locations or names. Include common misspellings in your searches and try to identify possible autocorrect mistakes.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: keyword-lists; location-search; misspellings; monitoring
relevance_rationale: Keyword and location search techniques endure but LLMs can now generate and expand search terms dynamically

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: InVID is a free browser extension and the best platform for helping you analyze and verify videos. It allows for users to paste a URL into its engine, which will then extract thumbnails from the video.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: InVID; video-verification; browser-extensions
relevance_rationale: InVID still useful but AI-powered video verification tools are rapidly expanding capabilities

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The best way to search Twitter is by using TweetDeck, which allows you to create unique columns for searches and lists. Finding and duplicating relevant lists is key for staying abreast of a situation. You can use Google to search for Twitter lists using a simple formula: site:twitter.com/*/lists 'Alabama reporters'.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: TweetDeck; Twitter-lists; monitoring-setup; Google-search
relevance_rationale: TweetDeck deprecated; platform monitoring now requires updated tools and approaches for X/Twitter changes

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Has it spread outside of its initial network of sharers? Has it been shared by figures of authority? Has it generated significant engagement? There are no simple rules on when to debunk a falsehood, but some good questions to ask.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: debunking-criteria; tipping-point; editorial-judgment
relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment criteria for when to debunk are enduring newsroom decisions unaffected by AI
notes: Three questions to assess whether to debunk

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: After the dust settles, it is helpful to look back at your saved images and posts. While in the moment you want to highlight individual falsehoods by way of public service journalism, in the aftermath you should take stock of any themes or patterns that can be seen. A wrap-up story can help your readers fully grasp the purpose and methods of the disinformation's spread. It will also serve as a research tool for you and your newsroom.
chapter: Monitoring for fakes during breaking news
themes: post-event-analysis; pattern-recognition; wrap-up-story
relevance_rationale: Post-event pattern analysis and wrap-up reporting is enduring journalistic practice

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: To go beyond standard forms of image verification, we have combined methods from art history with questions designed specifically for mis- and disinformation content. Our framework, '20 Questions for Interrogating Social Media Images,' designed collaboratively with First Draft and journalists, is an additional tool journalists can use when investigating images.
themes: image-analysis; framework; art-history-methods; visual-literacy
relevance_rationale: Systematic image questioning frameworks combining art history and verification methods endure as analytical discipline
notes: Five core questions: What is it? What does it show? Who made it? What did it mean? What does it mean?

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Misinformation does not necessarily involve fake images or even the misidentification of what they show. Sometimes images can be used to support a message that misrepresents a wider situation. Some misinformation requires more than verification. There is a need to critically examine how real images are used to manipulate, and what such images do and mean.
themes: visual-disinformation; meaning-analysis; context-manipulation
relevance_rationale: Understanding that misinfo can use real images in misleading context is enduring media literacy insight
notes: Verification alone is insufficient; meaning analysis is needed

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Journalists should think carefully about bringing further attention to such emotionally fueled, controversial and potentially harmful disinformation by reporting on it, even with positive intentions.
themes: amplification-risk; editorial-judgment; harm-reduction
relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment about amplification risk and harm reduction in reporting on disinfo is enduring

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Technical approaches are useful until synthetic media techniques inevitably adapt to them. A perfect deepfake detection system will never exist.
themes: deepfakes; detection-limits; cat-and-mouse; synthetic-media
relevance_rationale: Deepfake detection cat-and-mouse intensified by LLMs; no perfect detection is even more true now

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Journalists and investigators need to establish a mentality of measured skepticism around photos, videos and audio. They must assume that these forms of media will be challenged more frequently as knowledge and fear of deepfakes increases. It is also essential to develop a strong familiarity with media forensics tools.
themes: deepfakes; skepticism; media-forensics; verification-mindset
relevance_rationale: Measured skepticism toward media now must account for far more sophisticated LLM-generated synthetic content

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: An approach to analyzing and verifying deepfakes and synthetic media manipulation should include: 1. Reviewing the content for synthetic media-derived telltale glitches or distortions. 2. Applying existing video verification and forensics approaches. 3. Utilizing emerging new AI-based approaches and emerging forensics approaches when available.
themes: deepfakes; verification-pipeline; forensics; three-step-approach
relevance_rationale: Three-step deepfake approach needs updating as LLM-era synthetic media may lack traditional glitches

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Since most deepfakes or modifications are currently not fully synthesized but instead rely on making changes in a source video, you can use frames from a video to look for other versions using a reverse image search. You can also check the video to see if the landscape and landmarks are consistent with images of the same location in Google Street View.
themes: deepfakes; reverse-image-search; geolocation; video-verification
relevance_rationale: Reverse image search for deepfakes still useful but fully synthesized content now more common than modified source video

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Given the nature of both media forensics and emerging deepfakes technologies, we have to accept that the absence of evidence that something was tampered with will not be conclusive proof that media has not been tampered with.
themes: deepfakes; evidence-absence; forensics-limits
relevance_rationale: Absence of tampering evidence not proving authenticity is an enduring epistemological truth amplified by AI
notes: Absence of tampering evidence is not proof of authenticity

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The best way to approach verification is using existing approaches to image/video, and complement these with forensics tools that can detect image manipulation. Trusting the human eye is not a robust strategy!
themes: deepfakes; human-perception-limits; tool-dependence; verification
relevance_rationale: Not trusting the human eye is even more critical now; AI detection tools are essential but also rapidly evolving

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Before trying this, you should talk to your editor or manager about how to answer these questions. Will you be truthful about who you are and why you are in the group? Is there a way to join by staying deliberately vague? If not, how can you justify that decision to hide your identity?
chapter: Monitoring and Reporting Inside Closed Groups and Messaging Apps
themes: ethics; closed-groups; identity-disclosure; editorial-process
relevance_rationale: Ethical deliberation about identity disclosure when entering closed groups is enduring editorial practice

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The other technique is to set up a tipline, where you encourage the public to send you content. The key to a tipline is having a simple, clear call to action, and that you explain how you intend to use that content.
chapter: Monitoring and Reporting Inside Closed Groups and Messaging Apps
themes: tiplines; audience-engagement; closed-groups; data-collection
relevance_rationale: Setting up tiplines with clear calls to action and transparency about data use is enduring engagement practice
notes: Tiplines can be gamed; transparency increases participation

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Reporting from and about closed messaging apps and groups is full of challenges, yet those sources will become increasingly important as spaces where information is shared. As a first step, think about the questions outlined in this chapter, talk to your colleagues and editors, and if you don't have guidelines in your newsroom about this type of reporting, start working on some.
chapter: Monitoring and Reporting Inside Closed Groups and Messaging Apps
themes: closed-groups; newsroom-guidelines; editorial-policy
relevance_rationale: Developing newsroom guidelines for reporting from closed messaging apps is enduring editorial need

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Unable to identify the authors of the recording, Comprova focused on a more conventional investigation, and requested the help of an expert report from Instituto Brasileiro de Pericia (the Brazilian Institute of Forensics). Experts compared the viral recording with Bolsonaro's voice in an April 2018 interview and concluded that the voice of the candidate was not the voice on the recording.
chapter: Case Study: Bolsonaro at the Hospital
themes: audio-forensics; expert-analysis; voice-comparison
relevance_rationale: Audio forensics via expert analysis endures but AI voice analysis tools now supplement traditional expert reports
notes: When digital provenance fails, use forensic analysis

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Remember that text, images or the entire site itself may disappear over time -- especially after you start contacting people and asking questions. A best practice is to use the Wayback Machine to save important pages on your target website as part of your workflow.
themes: archiving; Wayback-Machine; evidence-preservation; workflow
relevance_rationale: Archiving websites via Wayback Machine before contacting subjects is enduring investigative practice

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: This chapter will look at four categories to analyze when investigating a website: content, code, analytics, registration and connected elements.
themes: website-investigation; four-categories; methodology
relevance_rationale: Four-category framework for website investigation is enduring analytical methodology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: An important part of examining the content is to see if it is original. Has text from the site's About page or other general text been copied from elsewhere? Is the site spreading false or misleading information, or helping push a specific agenda?
themes: plagiarism-detection; content-analysis; website-investigation
relevance_rationale: Plagiarism detection on websites needs updating as LLMs can generate original-sounding text that evades traditional checks
notes: Copy-paste text into Google to check originality

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: This approach, first discovered by Lawrence Alexander, begins with viewing the source code of a webpage and then searching within it to see if you can locate a Google Analytics and/or Google AdSense code. These are hugely popular products from Google that enable a site owner to track stats or earn money from ads. If someone is running multiple sites, they often use the same Analytics or AdSense account to manage them. This provides an investigator with the opportunity to connect seemingly separate sites by finding the same ID in the source code.
themes: Google-Analytics; AdSense-ID; source-code; network-mapping
relevance_rationale: Google Analytics/AdSense ID tracing endures in principle but platform changes may alter technical specifics
notes: All Analytics IDs begin with ua-; AdSense IDs have pub-

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: One caveat is that it is relatively inexpensive to pay to have your personal information privacy protected when you register a domain. Even in those cases, a whois search will still tell us the date the domain was most recently registered, when it will expire and the IP address on the internet where the site is hosted.
themes: whois; domain-registration; privacy-protection; IP-address
relevance_rationale: WHOIS lookups and domain registration research remain relevant investigative techniques

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: As for IP addresses, beware that completely unconnected websites can be hosted on the same server. A general rule is that the fewer the websites hosted on the same server, the more likely they may be connected.
themes: IP-address; shared-hosting; false-positives
relevance_rationale: Caution about shared hosting false positives in IP analysis is enduring technical awareness
notes: Shared IP is weak signal; fewer co-hosted sites = stronger signal

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Ad libraries are nevertheless an important step toward more transparency and provide journalists and others with exciting new ways of investigating digital advertisements.
themes: ad-libraries; transparency; Facebook-ads; Google-ads
relevance_rationale: Ad libraries as transparency tools for investigating digital ads remain important regardless of AI
notes: Ad libraries available for Google, Facebook, Snapchat, LinkedIn

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Don't trust anything on 4chan/8chan. These websites are literally built to troll, spread innuendo and falsehoods about perceived enemies, push lies about marginalized people. Don't go into a thread asking for more details. Don't post anything, actually. You will be targeted by people with too much time on their hands.
themes: 4chan; 8chan; anonymous-platforms; source-reliability; safety
relevance_rationale: Distrust of anonymous platform content and safety awareness on 4chan/8chan is enduring source literacy

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Searching for '[city name] + [8chan or 4chan or incels.co]' or other extremist sites provides a useful template for any similar event.
themes: extremist-platforms; search-template; breaking-news
relevance_rationale: Search template for extremist platforms during breaking news is enduring investigative technique

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Right-clicking the timestamp and clicking 'inspect element' will bring up the site's source code, and it will highlight a section that starts with '<time unixtime=[number]>.' Copy and paste that number into an Epoch/Unix timestamp converter, and you'll get a to-the-second post timestamp in UTC time.
themes: timestamps; Unix-time; 8chan; verification
relevance_rationale: Unix timestamp inspection technique is platform-specific; concept endures but tools and platforms change
notes: Both 4chan and 8chan localize timestamps; inspect element reveals UTC

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It is again important to emphasize that the presence of a username across platforms does not guarantee the accounts were created by one person.
themes: username-matching; false-positives; attribution-caution
relevance_rationale: Caution that matching usernames across platforms does not prove same person is enduring attribution wisdom

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Changing Google's search parameters to filter by posts in the 'last hour,' then searching 'site:4chan.org polls' will give you a pretty good window into the polls 4chan users are trying to manipulate in real time.
themes: real-time-monitoring; Google-search; poll-manipulation; 4chan
relevance_rationale: Real-time Google search filtering for 4chan poll manipulation is tool-specific and needs platform updates

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: For an investigator, the goal is to find as much of the operation as possible before reporting it, because once the operation is reported, the operators can be expected to hide -- potentially by deleting or abandoning other assets.
themes: operational-security; pre-publication; evidence-preservation
relevance_rationale: Preserving evidence before reporting to prevent operator deletion is enduring investigative tradecraft

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: There is no single rule for identifying the first link in the chain by your own resources. The most effective strategy is to look for the incongruous. Taking the time to say 'That is weird: Why is it like that?' can be the first step in exposing a new operation.
themes: anomaly-detection; curiosity; investigation-start
relevance_rationale: Looking for the incongruous as investigation starting point is enduring curiosity-driven methodology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Three questions are crucial here, modeled on Camille Francois' Disinformation ABC: What information about the initial asset is available? How did the asset behave? What content did the asset produce?
themes: ABC-framework; assets; behavior; content; investigation-model
relevance_rationale: ABC framework (Assets, Behavior, Content) for network analysis is enduring investigative model
notes: Assets-Behavior-Content framework for information operations

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If an asset shares a text, it is worth searching where else that text appeared. Especially with longer texts, it is advisable to select a sentence or two from the third or fourth paragraphs, or lower, as deceptive operations have been known to edit the headlines and ledes of articles they have copied, but are less likely to take the time to edit the body of the text.
themes: text-matching; plagiarism-detection; network-mapping; search-technique
relevance_rationale: Text matching for network mapping endures in principle but LLMs can now paraphrase to avoid detection
notes: Search from body text, not headlines, for better matches

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Assets that post text with mistakes have particular value, as errors are, by their nature, more unusual than correctly spelled words. This made for a much more targeted Google search with far fewer results.
themes: spelling-errors; search-technique; unique-identifiers
relevance_rationale: Spelling error as unique search identifier may become less reliable as LLMs auto-correct disinfo text
notes: Misspellings narrow search results significantly

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: A key rule of analysis is to record them when you find them, because you may not get a second chance. Whatever the format, some pieces of information should always be recorded. These include how the asset was found, its name and URL, the date it was created, and the number of followers, follows, likes and/or views. They also include a basic description of the asset, to remind you what it was after looking at 500 other assets.
themes: evidence-recording; metadata; archiving; workflow
relevance_rationale: Recording evidence immediately with full metadata is enduring investigative workflow principle
notes: Record how each asset was found for reproducibility

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It is worth giving the asset a confidence score. The level of confidence (high, moderate or low) should be marked as a separate entry, and the reasons should be added to the notes.
themes: confidence-scoring; evidence-assessment; methodology
relevance_rationale: Confidence scoring for assets with explicit reasoning is enduring analytical methodology

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Ultimately, attribution is a question of self-restraint. The researcher has to imagine the question, 'How can you prove that this operation was run by the person you are accusing?' If they cannot answer that question with confidence to themselves, they should steer clear of making the accusation.
themes: attribution; self-restraint; evidence-standards; accountability
relevance_rationale: Attribution as self-restraint requiring provable evidence is enduring investigative ethics

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Contacting the relevant platforms is a vital part of investigative work. As the platforms have scaled up their internal investigative teams, they have become more willing to offer public attribution for information operations.
themes: platform-cooperation; attribution; sourcing
relevance_rationale: Contacting platforms for cooperation on attribution is enduring investigative practice

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Endless Mayfly illustrates how you can combine network and narrative analysis with external reporting to arrive at attribution. It also highlights the difficulty involved in attributing information operations to a specific actor, why multiple indicators are required, and how to use a confidence level to indicate your level of certitude for the attribution.
themes: attribution; triangulation; confidence-levels; mixed-methods
relevance_rationale: Combining network and narrative analysis for attribution with confidence levels is enduring methodology
notes: Three analysis types: narrative, network, external reporting

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Using content and discourse analysis on the 135 inauthentic articles collected in our investigation, we determined that the narratives being propagated were aligned with Iran's interests. Each article was coded into categories that were determined after an initial reading of all the articles. Two rounds of coding were conducted: The first round was executed independently by two researchers, and a second round was conducted together by the same researchers to resolve any discrepancies.
themes: content-analysis; coding; intercoder-reliability; narrative-analysis
relevance_rationale: Content/discourse analysis with intercoder reliability for narrative coding is enduring research methodology
notes: Standard qualitative coding methodology applied to disinfo

source_title: Verification Handbook 3: For Disinformation & Media Manipulation
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: To avoid hasty and poor attribution practices, it is important to consider multiple indicators, types of evidence and analyses, and to make use of a confidence level that considers alternative hypotheses and missing data.
themes: attribution; confidence-levels; alternative-hypotheses; evidence-standards
relevance_rationale: Using multiple indicators, confidence levels, and alternative hypotheses to avoid hasty attribution is enduring standard
notes: Uses intelligence-community framework: low/moderate/high confidence

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Just about any investigation today should incorporate the search, gathering and verification of open source information. This has become inseparable from the work of cultivating sources, securing confidential information and other investigative tactics that rely on hidden or less-public information.
themes: open source intelligence; investigation design; UGC
relevance_rationale: Integrating OSINT into investigations is a strategic principle that AI amplifies but does not replace

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Verification is a mix of three elements: Sources knowledge, reliability and honesty, and the number, variety and reliability of sources you can find and persuade to talk; Documentation.
themes: verification; sourcing; documentation
relevance_rationale: Three-element verification framework (sources, reliability, documentation) is epistemologically fundamental
notes: Attributed to Steve Buttry

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Just as we know that human memory is faulty and that sources lie, we must also remember that documents and data aren't always what they appear.
themes: verification; data skepticism; source reliability
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward documents and data is a core journalistic principle that applies to AI outputs too

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The simple two-letter word 'is' reveals opinions and facts about your subject. To avoid clutter, include the company name of the person or any other detail you know, and tell Google that both words should not be that far from each other. The AROUND() operator MUST BE IN CAPITALS. It sets the maximum distance in words between two terms.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: search operators; Google; background research
relevance_rationale: Boolean search operators still work but LLMs now enable natural-language queries that achieve similar filtering

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Search for PDF documents with the name of the CEO in it, but exclude documents from the company of the subject itself. This helps you to see what opponents, competitors or opinionated people say about your subject.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: search operators; background research; filetype search
relevance_rationale: Filetype PDF search for CEO documents still valid but LLMs can now search and summarize across document collections
notes: Using filetype:pdf and -site: operators

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Search for worldwide official documents about a person using site:gov.uk, site:gov.au, site:gov.cn, site:gov and other governmental websites. If you don't have a .gov website in your country, use the local word for it with the site: operator.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: search operators; government records; public records
relevance_rationale: Government site searches endure but LLMs can now aggregate and cross-reference across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: With a name variation formula you can find results that use different spellings of the name. You will receive documents with the word Shell, but not those that include 'Ben' as the first name. With this, you find out that he is also referred to as Bernardus van Beurden. Now repeat steps with this new name.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: search operators; name variations; iterative search
relevance_rationale: Name variation search formulas are now handled by LLMs that understand transliteration and spelling variants natively

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Most of the research you do is not based on today, but an earlier period. Always tell your search engine to go back in time.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: date search; temporal filtering; search strategy
relevance_rationale: Using date ranges in searches to find historical context is a methodological habit that remains important

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Websites often cease to exist. There is a chance you can still view them by using archive.org. This tool can do its work only if you know the URL of the webpage you want to see. The problem is that often the site is gone and therefore you don't know it. So find an index first — a source about the missing page such as its Wikipedia page — then put the index in the time machine, then find the original website.
chapter: Who, Where and When of a person
themes: web archive; archive.org; dead links; source recovery
relevance_rationale: Wayback Machine for retrieving defunct websites remains a unique archival resource AI cannot replace
notes: Three-step process for recovering disappeared websites

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Search engines like Google don't actually know what web pages are about. They do, however, know what words are on the pages. So to get a search engine to behave itself, you need to work out which words are on your target pages. Choose your search terms wisely. Each word you add to the search focuses the results by eliminating results that don't include your chosen keywords. Avoid subjective keywords, as they can eliminate useful pages from the results.
themes: search strategy; keyword selection; Google
relevance_rationale: Understanding search engine keyword mechanics still useful but LLM semantic search now transcends keyword matching

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If you don't have definite keywords, you can still build in other possible keywords without damaging the results. Pages discussing heroin use in Texas might not include the word 'Texas'; they may just mention the names of different cities. You can build these into your search as optional keywords by separating them with the word OR (in capital letters). You can use the same technique to search for different spellings of the name of an individual, company or organization.
themes: search operators; OR operator; keyword strategy
relevance_rationale: Building keyword alternatives with OR operators now supplemented by LLMs that infer related terms semantically

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: You can focus your search on a particular site by using the search syntax 'site:' followed by the domain name. You can also use it to focus your search on municipal and academic sources. This is particularly effective when researching countries that use unique domain types for government and university sites.
themes: search operators; site search; domain filtering
relevance_rationale: Site-specific search syntax endures but LLM agents can now crawl and index specific domains more flexibly

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Some information comes in certain types of file formats. For instance, statistics, figures and data often appear in Excel spreadsheets. Professionally produced reports can often be found in PDF documents. You can specify a format in your search by using 'filetype:' followed by the desired data file extension.
themes: search operators; filetype search; data formats
relevance_rationale: Filetype search for data formats still works but LLMs can now discover and parse spreadsheets across formats

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Start by building a dossier on the person you're trying to locate or learn more about. This can include: the person's name (bearing in mind different variations, spelling of foreign names, married names, middle names); the town the person lives in; the person's job and company; phone number; usernames (as these are often constant across various social networks); email address; and a photograph.
themes: people search; dossier building; identity research
relevance_rationale: Dossier building is still essential but LLMs can now automate assembly from multiple open sources simultaneously

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: People often use the same photo as a profile picture for different social networks. Reverse image search on sites like TinEye and Google Images will help you identify linked accounts.
themes: reverse image search; identity verification; cross-platform
relevance_rationale: Profile photo reverse search remains valid but AI face-matching and cross-platform identity tools now augment this

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Many journalists have been fooled by malicious websites. Since it's easy for anyone to buy an unclaimed .com, .net or .org site, we should not go on face value. A site that looks well produced and has an authentic-sounding domain name may still be a political hoax, false company or satirical prank. Some degree of quality control can be achieved by examining the domain name itself. A whois search will bring up the registration details given by the site owner when the domain name was purchased.
themes: domain verification; whois; source authentication
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward domain names and malicious websites is a verification mindset unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Whois is not a panacea for verification. People can often get away with lying on a domain registration form. Some people will use an anonymizing service like Domains by Proxy, but combining a whois search with other domain name and IP address tools forms a valuable weapon in the battle to provide useful material from authentic sources.
themes: domain verification; whois limitations; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Whois limitations and domain registration fraud awareness remain relevant regardless of AI tools

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Everything has a paper trail, a lead that exposes the systemic underwire of a network, company, or person's illicit or illegal activities. The trick is to find it.
chapter: Records and other publicly available material to investigate companies
themes: paper trail; corporate investigation; public records
relevance_rationale: Paper trail investigation principle is a foundational investigative mindset AI cannot replace

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The most important aspects of any investigation are to dig, listen and ask pertinent questions. But asking questions requires context, and listening to the right sources means finding the core of the story. Data, free or otherwise, can never replace good investigative research. In order to do good investigative research, these days, one must become familiar with how and where knowledge can be found, and how best to access and develop it.
themes: investigation design; data limitations; research strategy
relevance_rationale: Digging, listening, and asking pertinent questions are core human investigative skills

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We made use of free public databases such as Duedil that allow for individual and corporate director searches. These enable users to identify the number of companies — current, dissolved, etc. — a director is involved in. It can also provide other important information: Shareholders, registered offices and a timeline of retired and current individuals involved. We also used LinkedIn to probe personal and corporate connections.
themes: corporate registries; Duedil; LinkedIn; director search
relevance_rationale: Corporate database searches like Duedil still valid but LLMs can now aggregate data from multiple registries

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Along with databases, we used Whois Internet searches where possible to determine the date of creation and ownership information of websites that were connected to the network. We then cross-referenced the contact details of the websites with the information listed in corporate databases for brokers and shell entities. Using specific search phrases, we were able to draw out mentions of certain names, companies, products etc. from various files on the Internet.
themes: cross-referencing; whois; corporate investigation; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Whois and domain ownership checks persist but AI tools now automate cross-referencing of registration data

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We 'cross-examined' our evidence. This is a process taken from the legal world and adapted for investigative journalism. We looked for bias, contradictions, consistency and probability within evidence, resources, interviews and sources.
themes: cross-examination; evidence evaluation; legal methods
relevance_rationale: Cross-examining evidence against itself is a core investigative logic principle unaffected by AI
notes: Attributed to Heinrich Bohmke at ANCIR

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: By identifying the broad relations within, and between, people, companies, jurisdictions and sectors, we were able to visualize the network's structure. This told us how the scheme functioned and who was involved.
themes: network analysis; visualization; corporate investigation
relevance_rationale: Network visualization for corporate structures endures but LLMs can now auto-generate relationship graphs from documents

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It doesn't take a genius to develop a good investigation or to lift the corporate veil — it simply takes curiosity, technique and a commitment to read as much and as far into the issue as possible. Scour as many data sources as possible: Corporate, media, NGO, shipping, sanctions, court... Look for what is not obvious, seems illogical, or that just plain sticks out to you. Follow your instinct. Ask as many questions as possible.
themes: investigation mindset; corporate investigation; curiosity
relevance_rationale: Curiosity and tenacity as investigation drivers are human qualities AI cannot substitute

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The most important lesson I've learned from two decades of using data for investigations is that data lies just as much as people, or even more so. Data, after all, is often created and maintained by people. Data is meant to be a representation of the reality of a particular moment of time.
themes: data skepticism; data quality; verification
