relevance_rationale: Data lies as much as people is a skeptical epistemological stance that applies equally to AI-generated data
notes: Attributed to Giannina Segnini

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Two key verification tasks need to be performed during a data-driven investigation: An initial evaluation must occur immediately after getting the data; and findings must be verified at the end of the investigation or analysis phase.
themes: data verification; workflow; two-phase verification
relevance_rationale: Data verification tasks endure but LLMs can now automate initial evaluation and cross-referencing steps

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The first practice I recommend is to explore the extreme values (highest or lowest) for each variable in a dataset, and to then count how many records (rows) are listed within each of the possible values.
themes: data completeness; extreme values; data profiling
relevance_rationale: Exploring extreme values is sound practice now automatable by LLMs that can profile datasets instantly
notes: Is the data complete?

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: A common mistake made when working with data is to fail to identify the existence of duplicate records. Whenever processing disaggregated data or information about people, companies, events or transactions, the first step is to search for a unique identification variable for each item.
themes: deduplication; unique identifiers; data quality
relevance_rationale: Duplicate detection is now trivially automated by LLMs and data tools but awareness of the issue remains important

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: One of the best ways to assess a dataset's credibility is to choose a sample record and compare it against reality. It is always recommended to repeat this validation exercise on a significant sample of the records.
themes: ground-truthing; spot-checking; data accuracy
relevance_rationale: Spot-checking sample records against reality requires human field work AI cannot perform
notes: Are the data accurate?

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: From the moment it's first entered in a computer to the time when we access it, data goes through several input, storage, transmission and registry processes. At any stage, it may be manipulated by people and information systems. It is therefore very common that relations between tables or fields get lost or mixed up, or that some variables fail to get updated. This is why it's essential to perform integrity tests.
themes: data integrity; data lifecycle; systems thinking
relevance_rationale: Understanding data provenance through input-storage-transfer chains is a conceptual framework AI does not replace
notes: Assessing data integrity

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Open Refine is an excellent tool to quickly explore and evaluate the quality of databases. Open Refine can be used to run a numeric 'facet' which groups numbers into numeric range bins. This enables you to select any range that spans a consecutive number of bins. The same can be done for dates and text values.
themes: OpenRefine; data profiling; data quality
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine for data exploration is now largely superseded by LLMs that can profile and clean data conversationally

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It is mandatory to learn how to decrypt codes and to understand how they are used to better understand the logic behind databases and, more importantly, their relations. Decoding acronyms is not only a best practice for evaluating the quality of data, but, more important, to finding stories of public interest.
themes: codes; acronyms; data literacy; database logic
relevance_rationale: Understanding data codes remains important but LLMs can now decode and explain code systems from documentation
notes: Deciphering codes and acronyms

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Not even the best data analysis can replace on-the-ground journalism and field verification.
themes: field verification; ground-truthing; data limitations
relevance_rationale: On-the-ground journalism and field verification are irreplaceable human activities
notes: Core lesson from Segnini's Avancemos investigation

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: After obtaining the database of all beneficiary students, we added the names of their parents. Then we queried other databases related to properties, vehicles, salaries and companies in the country. This enabled us to create an exhaustive inventory of the families' assets. Before the analysis, we made sure to evaluate and clean all of the records, and to verify the relationships between each person and their assets.
themes: record linkage; data joining; cross-database analysis; cleaning
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing beneficiary databases is now accelerated by LLMs that can join and query multiple datasets
notes: La Nacion Costa Rica Avancemos investigation

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It was not until we went to visit their homes that we could prove what the data alone could have never told us: These kids lived in real poverty with their mothers because they had been abandoned by their fathers. No one ever asked about their fathers before granting the benefit.
themes: field verification; ground-truthing; data-reality gap
relevance_rationale: Field visits to prove what data alone cannot show is irreplaceable human verification
notes: Data showed wealthy fathers; field work revealed abandoned children

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: I used satellite maps to look for landmarks visible in videos to confirm where they had been filmed. I compared the position of the minaret, the dome and a nearby wall on the satellite map imagery to the video, and it was clear it was a perfect match. Now that the likely position of the camera in the town was established, I could watch the whole video comparing other details to what was visible on satellite map imagery.
themes: geolocation; satellite imagery; video verification; landmarks
relevance_rationale: Satellite geolocation of video landmarks now significantly augmented by AI geolocation and object recognition
notes: Tiji, Libya example

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: As they walked through the streets, it was possible to map out the roads along the route they took, and then match that pattern to what was visible in satellite map imagery.
themes: geolocation; road pattern matching; satellite imagery
relevance_rationale: Route mapping from video content now aided by AI-powered video analysis and map matching
notes: Brega, Libya example — hand-drawn road maps compared to satellite

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: While things like billboards and streetlights are small objects, the shadows they cast can actually indicate their presence. Shadows can also be used to reveal information about the comparative height of buildings, and the shape of those buildings. I used the website SunCalc, which allows users to calculate the position of the sun throughout the day using a Google Maps based interface. It was then possible to establish the time of day.
themes: shadow analysis; SunCalc; chronolocation; geolocation
relevance_rationale: Shadow analysis for object detection now augmented by AI computer vision and sun-position calculators
notes: MH17 Buk missile launcher example

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: By bringing together different sources, tools and techniques, it was possible to connect these individual pieces of information and establish critical facts about this incident. A key element of working with user-generated content in investigations is understanding how that content is shared.
themes: triangulation; UGC; information ecosystem; multi-source
relevance_rationale: Combining multiple evidence types for corroboration is a methodological principle independent of specific tools

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The videos were uncovered by first geolocating the initial videos we found, then using that to predict the likely route those vehicles would have taken to get from each geolocated site. Then we would keyword search on various social media sites for the names of locations that were along the route the vehicle would have to had to travel. We also searched for keywords such as 'convoy,' 'missile,' etc. that could be associated with sightings.
themes: geolocation; route prediction; keyword search; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Systematic approach to UGC geolocation now enhanced by AI that can predict routes and identify landmarks automatically
notes: MH17 Buk convoy tracking

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It's remarkable what you can turn up when you approach UGC and open source information in a systematic way. You tend to learn quickly by just doing it. Even something as simple as double-checking the geolocation someone else has done can teach you a lot about comparing videos and photographs to satellite map imagery.
themes: learning by doing; systematic approach; OSINT; geolocation
relevance_rationale: Systematic approach to open-source investigation yields results regardless of whether AI assists the process

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: In a human rights investigation, we compare all facts gathered with relevant human rights norms and laws to make determinations of violations or abuses. Consequently, a single analyst who looks at UGC must be part of a team comprising relevant country, policy and legal experts.
themes: human rights; interdisciplinary teams; legal framework
relevance_rationale: Comparing evidence against human rights norms and laws is legal-analytical judgment AI cannot replace

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Today we are achieving the best results when combining a variety of evidence, such as testimony, official documents, satellite imagery and UGC. This requires the close collaboration of researchers who possess country expertise, trusted contacts on the ground, and highly specialized analysts who do not focus on a specific region or country, but are able to provide analysis based on satellite imagery or UGC.
themes: multi-source evidence; collaboration; specialization; UGC
relevance_rationale: Multi-evidence convergence (testimony, documents, satellite, video) is a methodological principle unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: In some instances, one piece of evidence does not corroborate some of the information gathered during an investigation, such as when satellite imagery does not support eyewitness claims of a large massacre. We then exercise caution and hold back on making statements of fact or determinations of violations.
themes: corroboration; caution; conflicting evidence; epistemic humility
relevance_rationale: Handling contradictory evidence through further investigation is a core investigative logic principle

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We first performed standard content analysis. This involved extracting the specifications of the roads, street lamps, buildings and vegetation, as well as details related to the people seen in the video such as clothes and military equipment. Reviewing the video frame by frame greatly aided with this process. The geographic features were then compared to satellite images of the area on Google Earth.
themes: content analysis; frame-by-frame; video verification; geolocation
relevance_rationale: Content analysis of infrastructure in images now significantly aided by AI object recognition and measurement
notes: Nigeria Giwa barracks incident

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Several photographs in our possession were actually geotagged. We discovered this by using an EXIF viewer to examine the metadata in the photo. This location data proved a perfect match to the street corner we identified as part of the content analysis of the initial video.
themes: EXIF metadata; geolocation; photo verification; cross-validation
relevance_rationale: EXIF data extraction remains valid but AI tools now combine metadata analysis with visual content verification

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We followed the video to perform a content analysis in order to identify distinctive markings on the soldiers and victims, or anything that could indicate location, time or date. This revealed two important details: a soldier wearing a black flak jacket stating 'Borno State. Operation Flush,' the name of the military operation; and, for a split second, an ID number on a rifle became visible.
themes: content analysis; identifying markings; linkage evidence; attribution
relevance_rationale: Visual content analysis for identifying markings now augmented by AI pattern recognition and enhancement

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: An AI researcher was also able to secure the actual video files while on a field mission to the area. This allowed us to conduct metadata analysis. Social media platforms strip metadata during the upload process. Obtaining the original files was possible only through well-established local contacts and networks.
themes: metadata; original files; field collection; social media limitations
relevance_rationale: Video file forensic analysis now enhanced by AI metadata extraction and deepfake detection tools
notes: Social media strips metadata; must obtain originals

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Savvy news desk researchers and journalists who might be inclined to contact local sources via Twitter or other public platform should consider the risk implications for asking for such sensitive footage from contacts in insecure environments.
themes: source safety; operational security; ethics; sensitive content
relevance_rationale: Protecting sources from exposure via public social media contact is an ethical principle unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It's essential to determine what ethical standards will work for you and your audience, and what actions will allow you to establish and preserve a relationship with them. Our approach must be ethical so that it can be sustainable.
themes: ethics; sustainability; audience relationship; UGC
relevance_rationale: Establishing ethical standards for content use is a values-based editorial decision AI does not change

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: The key takeaway is to consider how the content creator sees their activity, rather than how the platform defines it. Private communities can be extremely fruitful for generating investigative leads. A less-obvious private community might be when an individual uses a YouTube page to share videos with friends and family. It's a public account, but the user assumes a level of privacy.
themes: privacy expectations; UGC ethics; private communities; consent
relevance_rationale: Considering how content creators view their activity is an ethical empathy principle that transcends technology

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Securing permission to use content from creators of UGC helps establish and maintain the reputation of your organization as one that gives fair treatment. Securing permission will also help you ensure you are using content from an original source. Communication with individuals is an important part of any verification process. The act of asking for permission also opens up a potential source of additional information on the content.
themes: permission; UGC ethics; verification; source relationships
relevance_rationale: Securing permission from UGC creators is a copyright and trust principle unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Does an assignment put someone at risk? Could an individual get too close to a dangerous event or to people who may cause them harm? What is your responsibility to a person who is harmed while carrying out an assignment set by you? What impact does an assignment have on the honesty/authenticity of the content being produced versus something that was created unprompted?
themes: contributor safety; duty of care; UGC ethics; assignments
relevance_rationale: Assessing risk to individuals from assignments is an ethical duty of care independent of technology

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: When someone uploads a photograph or video to a social network, the copyright remains with them. So if you want to download the picture or video to use elsewhere, you must first seek permission. If you simply want to embed the material, using the embed code provided by the social networks, legally you don't need to seek permission. Ethically, however, it might be appropriate to contact the person.
themes: copyright; UGC; embedding vs downloading; legal; ethics
relevance_rationale: Copyright remaining with content uploaders is a legal principle unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: An important question to ask is whether the person actually captured the material him or herself. It is amazing how many people upload other people's content on their own channels. They will often 'give permission' for use even though they have no right to do so. You also want to ask basic questions about location, and what else they could see, to help you authenticate what they claim to have witnessed.
themes: provenance; permission; authentication; original source
relevance_rationale: Verifying whether a person actually captured the material is a sourcing principle that applies regardless of AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If you do seek permission on the social network itself, make sure that you take a screenshot of the exchange. People will sometimes provide permission for use, and then, after negotiating an exclusive deal with another organization, they will delete any exchanges on social media to show them giving permission to others.
themes: permission; evidence preservation; screenshots; legal protection
relevance_rationale: Screenshotting permission exchanges for records is a documentation practice independent of AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: We must seek informed consent, not just consent, meaning: Does the uploader understand what you are giving permission for? And when it comes to crediting, you must talk to them about whether and how they would like credit.
themes: informed consent; crediting; UGC ethics; transparency
relevance_rationale: Informed consent for UGC use is an ethical-legal requirement unaffected by AI tools

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Best practice is to label any content with the information you can confirm, whether that's source, date or location. If you can confirm only two of three, add this information over the photo or video. The most important role for journalists is to provide the necessary context about the content that is being shared: debunk what is false, and provide crucial information about time, date or location, as well as showing how this content relates to other material.
themes: labeling; transparency; context; verification status
relevance_rationale: Labeling content with confirmed information is a transparency standard that persists regardless of AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Many news organizations will run pictures or videos with the caveat that 'this cannot be independently verified.' This is problematic, as the truth is that the newsroom may have run many verification checks, or relied on agencies to do these checks, before broadcasting or publishing. So this phrase is being used as an insurance policy. Repeating it undermines the verification processes that are being carried out.
themes: verification labeling; transparency; credibility; newsroom practices
relevance_rationale: Criticizing lazy cannot be independently verified caveats is a journalistic standards argument unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: It is best practice to 'label' who has captured the content. Was it a staff member? Was it a freelance journalist? Was it a citizen journalist? When someone unrelated to the newsroom takes a picture that is used by the newsroom, for reasons of transparency, any affiliation should be explained to the audience. Simply labeling this type of material as 'Amateur Footage' or something similar doesn't provide the necessary context.
themes: attribution; labeling; transparency; content provenance
relevance_rationale: Labeling content by who captured it (staff, freelance, citizen) is a transparency practice independent of AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Any fact that is to be published will be checked to see if it is correct on its own and in context. Every verifiable piece of information will be underlined. Standardized marks will be used to denote statements as correct, incorrect, not verifiable, etc. The source of factual corrections and quotations must be given. When fact-checking a manuscript, other and if possible more accurate sources than the author's sources should be used. A statement is considered verified only if confirmed by reliable sources or experts.
themes: fact-checking; verification protocol; Der Spiegel; standards
relevance_rationale: Checking every verifiable fact for correctness and context is a foundational accuracy principle

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: If a piece of research contradicts an author's statement, the author must be notified of the contradiction during the discussion of the manuscript. If a fact is unverifiable, the author must also be notified. When in doubt, it is better not to use a piece of information rather than to run the risk of an incorrect report.
themes: fact-checking workflow; author-checker relationship; editorial standards
relevance_rationale: Notifying authors when research contradicts their statements is a fairness process unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: When time is limited, facts that are the clear responsibility of the fact-checker must be checked first, particularly: Are the times and dates correct? Does the text contradict itself? Are the names and offices/jobs correct? Are the quotations correct (in wording and in context)? How current and trustworthy are the sources used?
themes: fact-checking priorities; triage; time constraints; checklist
relevance_rationale: Prioritizing fact-checks on claims carrying legal or reputational risk is editorial judgment AI does not replace

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Prefer original documents. If an academic study is quoted, obtain the original, full text. If company earnings are cited, obtain their financials. Do not rely on press summaries and press releases when the original document can be obtained. Prefer sources that delineate between facts and opinion, and that supply facts in their work. Prefer sources that clearly indicate the source of their information, as this enables you to verify their work. Beware of sources that make factual errors about basic facts, or that confuse basic concepts.
themes: source evaluation; original documents; source hierarchy
relevance_rationale: Preferring original documents over secondary reports is a core verification principle that applies to AI summaries too

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Accuracy is the basic prerequisite for good journalism and objective reportage. Journalists make mistakes, intended or not. Mistakes damage the most valuable asset of journalism: credibility. The method to reduce the probability of mistakes is verification; that is, checking facts before publication.
themes: accuracy; credibility; verification; journalism values
relevance_rationale: Accuracy as prerequisite for journalism is a foundational principle unaffected by AI

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Their search for exculpatory video involved looking on social networks, asking those who were at the event, and obtaining footage from the prosecution and courts. In a frame-by-frame analysis of roughly three hours of an archived livestream of the protest (324,000 frames!) the defense team uncovered a single frame of video that showed the police vehicle Seixas was being accused of having set ablaze was the exact same vehicle that drove him away after he was detained.
chapter: Videos help prove the innocence of an activist in Rio
themes: frame-by-frame analysis; video evidence; exculpatory evidence; livestream
relevance_rationale: Searching social networks for exculpatory video now augmented by AI-powered video search and analysis at scale
notes: WITNESS / Rio case

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: WITNESS helped the defense identify and prepare this evidence, both by assembling screenshots of these videos into a storyboard as well as by editing a 10-minute evidentiary submission video that was delivered to the judge, along with the accompanying documentation.
chapter: Videos help prove the innocence of an activist in Rio
themes: video evidence; storyboard; evidentiary submission; legal presentation
relevance_rationale: Assembling video evidence into storyboards now aided by AI video analysis and automated timeline creation

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Reymond took a full two hours to verify the photo's authenticity before posting it. Reymond's process of verification included comparing the facial features with details that emerged as witnesses and officials shared details of the attack. Along with the rifle, two other key pieces of evidence were the fact that the man in the photo was wearing a keffieh, which witnesses had described, and the fact that he was carrying an umbrella.
themes: photo verification; corroboration; witness details; responsible publishing
relevance_rationale: Taking two hours to verify before posting exemplifies verification discipline that remains essential with AI
notes: Ottawa shooting case study

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Despite its failure to substantiate this claim or provide appropriate credit, Canadian journalists seized upon @ArmedResearch's claim, reporting the photo was 'tweeted from an ISIS account,' with all the implications that accompany such an assertion. Technical data from the Twitter page, captured before the account was suspended, show that the ISIS account sourced the photo from Breaking3zero. The temptation to draw a connection between vivid photos like this one and our worst fears is enormous. Avoiding this temptation is one of the chief responsibilities of 21st century journalists.
themes: source attribution; misinformation; confirmation bias; provenance
relevance_rationale: Warning against seizing unverified claims from social media sources is a critical media literacy lesson
notes: False ISIS attribution of photo that actually came from journalist

source_title: Verification Handbook 2: A Definitive Guide to Verifying Digital Content for Emergency Coverage
author: Craig Silverman (ed.), EJC
extract: Searching for names of companies or people in the Middle East presents some special challenges. The first step is to determine which language to search for the information you need, and then to find the spelling in Arabic. Keep in mind that the pronunciation of a single word can differ greatly among Arabic-speaking countries. Be aware that even within the same registry you should search using multiple spellings of the same word.
chapter: Search for companies in the Middle East
themes: multilingual search; Arabic; transliteration; name variation
relevance_rationale: Middle Eastern name transliteration challenges now partially addressed by LLMs with multilingual capabilities

author: Tactical Tech
extract: An investigation is a process of collecting and analysing information from different sources and drawing conclusions that address an initial question, problem or assumption. This process allows you to build a body of knowledge about a person of interest, an organisation, a place, an event, a crime.
themes: investigation design; research questions; knowledge building
relevance_rationale: The definition of investigation as question-driven collection-to-analysis is a permanent framework
notes: Defines investigation as question-driven collection-to-analysis process

author: Tactical Tech
extract: The key to fulfilling that purpose is turning a body of knowledge into a body of evidence, an unbreakable proof, by interpreting and giving meaning to that knowledge. The process of setting your purpose, gathering information and extracting meaning is the central structure of any investigation.
themes: evidence; interpretation; investigation design
relevance_rationale: Turning knowledge into evidence through interpretation is a human judgment function LLMs assist but cannot replace
notes: Knowledge becomes evidence through interpretation; purpose-gather-meaning as core structure

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Investigations are often non linear, iterative processes. They involve a certain mindset to approach questions from different angles and with various tools and techniques. Be ready to rethink your strategies along the way if your current path reaches a dead end. You may need to consider alternative methods to interpret the same piece of information or search elsewhere for additional evidence that refines your understanding of the picture you're putting together.
themes: iteration; flexibility; investigation design
relevance_rationale: Non-linear iterative investigation requiring adaptability is an inherent feature of complex inquiry
notes: Non-linear process; requires adaptability and willingness to reinterpret

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Before diving in, be mindful of your own safety and the risks involved in whatever tools, information sources and techniques you want to use. Consider the technology and devices that are most suitable for each investigation you plan and be aware of the physical environment you live and work in. Just as you can find traces of events or individuals that you are investigating, your own research can leave traces that can put you, your collaborators and your sources at risk.
themes: risk assessment; operational security; safety
relevance_rationale: Operational security advice needs updating to account for AI-powered surveillance, LLM data leakage, and AI tool usage creating new digital traces
notes: Research leaves traces; risk awareness must precede investigation

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Good evidence: is first-hand - information you find out yourself. Is documented and preserved - you can record and demonstrate the process that led you to the evidence. Is timely - collected and verified close to the date of the event. Can be verified by others - someone else than you should be able to confirm its source and content accuracy. Connects other pieces of information together - helps you build the bigger picture. Includes metadata - more information beyond the content, such as details on its author, date, location. Doesn't expose human sources to risk - ensures vulnerable people are not harmed. May contradict you - it turns your initial assumptions or beliefs upside down.
themes: evidence quality; verification; documentation; metadata
relevance_rationale: The eight-point evidence quality checklist is a timeless verification framework
notes: Eight-point checklist for evaluating evidence quality

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Documentation means keeping track of every step you make, every piece of data and evidence you collect with details such as what it is, where, when, why and how it was collected. Preserving this data in its initial form and features is crucial for your investigation. The primary goal of documentation is to create a verifiable record of your investigation. Let's call it 'investigative hygiene' – regular maintenance of certain practices to ensure your investigation is healthy and can stand up to scrutiny and criticism if anyone tries to dismiss your evidence.
themes: documentation; reproducibility; investigative hygiene; provenance
relevance_rationale: Investigative hygiene through systematic documentation is more important than ever with AI-assisted workflows
notes: Introduces 'investigative hygiene' concept; documentation as verifiable record

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Your documentation habits are important if you intend to publish your findings, if you ever speak to law enforcement, if you pursue a case to court or offer your evidence to human rights defenders who represent cases of crime and abuse. Also, should personal or professional problems or changing socio-political conditions interrupt your work, it will be easier for someone else to interpret it and take on the rest of the investigation or use the evidence you managed to uncover.
themes: documentation; collaboration; handoff; reproducibility
relevance_rationale: Documentation for publication, legal use, and handoff to others is a permanent organizational need
notes: Documentation enables publication, legal use, and handoff to others

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Verification is an iterative process. You have to keep doing it over and over again. The process is not only related to one piece of evidence you uncover, but also to how all the pieces fit together. Any new evidence you find may cast reasonable doubt on old evidence that you may have already verified. That is why documenting your evidence is important; so that you can retrace your steps and verify again and again.
themes: verification; iteration; documentation; evidence
relevance_rationale: Iterative verification where new evidence may invalidate old is a timeless epistemological principle
notes: Verification is continuous, not one-time; new evidence may invalidate old

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Your assumptions about the credibility of your human sources can get in the way of proper verification if these assumptions are not true. It is essential not to take anything for granted since even the most trusted and reliable of sources can sometimes get it wrong, even if unintentionally.
themes: verification; source credibility; bias; assumptions
relevance_rationale: Not taking source credibility for granted and challenging assumptions is a permanent verification principle
notes: Even trusted sources can be wrong; challenge assumptions about credibility

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Another challenge is finding creative ways of verification – to be ready to use sources of information in ways they were not intended. For example, if you want to verify whether a public official was somewhere despite him denying so, you may not only want to visit his social media accounts, but also those of his relatives and friends to find clues of messages, comments and images that may confirm or deny that.
themes: verification; creativity; social media; lateral search
relevance_rationale: Creative verification via social media now includes AI-generated content detection; the lateral search principle endures but methods need updating
notes: Creative verification via indirect sources e.g. relatives' social media

author: Tactical Tech
extract: There are three main phases of verifying information: Verifying the source. Where you got the information and where it originated. Verifying the content. Whether it is exactly what it claims to be. Verifying its relevance. Whether it fits in to your investigation.
themes: verification; source verification; content verification; relevance
relevance_rationale: Three-phase verification framework (source, content, relevance) is a permanent methodological structure
notes: Three-phase verification framework: source, content, relevance

author: Tactical Tech
extract: In order to rigorously test your evidence and keep your biases in check, you can document your research process and your findings and present them to other investigators to confirm that it makes sense to someone else. However, always make sure you trust those you are sharing information with at any stage of an investigation and that you – and any collaborators – are aware of ways to stay digitally and physically safe as you communicate and share information.
themes: peer review; bias; collaboration; security
relevance_rationale: Peer review to check biases while maintaining security is an enduring collaborative principle
notes: Peer review as bias check; balance openness with operational security

author: Tactical Tech
extract: It's natural to become emotionally involved with a story, particularly as you speak to sources you sympathise with. Our unconscious biases make us more interested in hearing some stories, and less interested in others. You should accept that you cannot remain a completely detached observer. But you should still strive to be mindful of your biases and seek to act in a fair and honest way as much as possible. Your investigation may fail if you push a certain point of view at the expense of others, or neglect important context due to your personal bias.
themes: bias; objectivity; emotional management; fairness
relevance_rationale: Awareness of personal bias and striving for fairness is a permanent ethical principle for investigators
notes: Acknowledges pure detachment is impossible; bias awareness as practical necessity

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Google dorking (also known as Google hacking) is a technique used by newsrooms, researchers, investigative reporters and others to query search engines in order to find hidden information that might be available on public websites. The technique can strengthen your investigations by expanding your access to information that is of public interest but that is not, whether by design or by accident, readily available.
themes: dorking; search engines; hidden data; open source
relevance_rationale: Google dorking techniques are partially displaced by LLM-powered search and AI assistants, but advanced operators still have unique value
notes: Dorking as advanced search technique for finding non-obvious public information

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Dorking involves using search engines to their full potential to unearth results that are not visible with a regular search. It allows you to refine your searches and dive deeper, and with greater precision, into webpages and documents that are available online. Uncovering hidden files and security flaws by dorking does not require a great deal of technical knowledge. It's really about learning just a few search techniques and using them across a number of search engines.
themes: dorking; search technique; accessibility; precision
relevance_rationale: LLMs and AI search tools can now automate much of what dorking achieves, though the principle of precision search endures
notes: Low barrier to entry; cross-engine application recommended

author: Tactical Tech
extract: You can use dorking to protect your own data and to defend websites you are responsible for. We call this 'defensive dorking,' and it typically takes one of two forms: Checking for security vulnerabilities in an online service, such as a website or an FTP server, that you administer; or Looking for sensitive information about yourself - or about someone else, with their permission - that might be exposed unintentionally on a website, regardless of whether or not you administer that website.
themes: defensive dorking; operational security; vulnerability assessment
relevance_rationale: Defensive dorking now needs to include checking for AI-generated impersonation and LLM training data exposure alongside traditional checks
notes: Dorking applied defensively for personal/organizational data exposure checks

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Luckily, there are some easy techniques and digital archiving tools and services to help retrieve old content and deleted pages so you can still reference them in your investigation. Apart from this, digital archives often contain information that can help you identify other important data such as the owner of a website, useful names, contact details, documents and links to other sites.
chapter: Retrieving and Archiving Information from Websites
themes: web archiving; digital archives; evidence retrieval; deleted content
relevance_rationale: Web archiving and evidence retrieval from deleted pages is an enduring investigative need regardless of AI
notes: Archives useful not just for retrieval but for discovering ownership and connections

author: Tactical Tech
extract: While it is often a good idea to save HTML or PDF copies of important webpages to your own devices to make sure that you have multiple back-ups, archiving them with the Wayback Machine can add an element of neutrality and trust if you end up sharing those archives with others. It is also far more convenient, for most people, than maintaining an offline library of digital files.
chapter: Retrieving and Archiving Information from Websites
themes: web archiving; Wayback Machine; evidence preservation; neutrality
relevance_rationale: Third-party archiving for neutrality and trust is a permanent evidence preservation principle
notes: Third-party archiving adds credibility vs local copies alone

author: Tactical Tech
extract: When you archive a webpage with a service like the Wayback Machine or archive.today - especially if it has a long, complicated web address like an archived copy of a Google Cache entry - be sure to record that archived link somewhere in a file on your computer, in a secure cloud folder or elsewhere. Relying on your browser history to find such things is a recipe for disaster.
chapter: Retrieving and Archiving Information from Websites
themes: documentation; web archiving; link management; evidence preservation
relevance_rationale: Recording archived URLs separately is basic documentation hygiene unchanged by AI
notes: Practical advice: always record archived URLs separately

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Archive.today (archive.fo; formerly archive.is) archives web pages much like the Wayback Machine. It differs, however, by only storing individual pages, rather than entire websites, and it does so only at the request of its users, not automatically. Since it doesn't crawl sites, it doesn't have the breadth of information you can find on the Wayback Machine. It does provide three key features, however: First, unlike the Wayback Machine, it allows you to search the full text of its archives. Second, it ignores any restrictions that might be specified in the robots.txt files of the websites that it archives. As a result, it can save snapshots of some pages that the Wayback Machine cannot, such as public Facebook profiles and Twitter posts. Third, it also saves both a text copy and a graphical screenshot of the archived pages.
chapter: Retrieving and Archiving Information from Websites
themes: archive.today; web archiving; social media archiving; robots.txt
relevance_rationale: Archive.today as complement to Wayback Machine for social media and full-text search remains valid
notes: archive.today complements Wayback Machine: full-text search, ignores robots.txt, dual-format saves

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Another option to retrieve website contents and to stay updated if any changes occur is to use visual site monitors. These are services that can track and monitor visual changes in webpages, whether they happen in code, images, text etc. They can be very useful for researchers and help automate some of the work if you need to monitor many websites that are useful in your investigation.
chapter: Retrieving and Archiving Information from Websites
themes: monitoring; automation; change detection; web scraping
relevance_rationale: Visual site monitors now compete with AI-powered change detection tools; the monitoring principle endures but tool landscape has shifted
notes: Visual site monitors automate tracking of webpage changes over time

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Finding hidden content and connections is not an exact science but it requires a combination of acquired skills, a set of methods and tools and a good dose of perseverance.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: persistence; skills; methodology
relevance_rationale: Web investigation as craft requiring skills, methods, and perseverance is unchanged by AI
notes: Brief but important framing: web investigation as craft not formula

author: Tactical Tech
extract: When researching a website, one of the most useful sources of data can be found in its domain registration details. Over the course of your investigation, it might be relevant to know who registered or who owns a particular domain (whether it is an organisation or an individual registrant), when it was registered and by which registrar, as well as other details.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: WHOIS; domain research; ownership; web investigation
relevance_rationale: WHOIS domain research for identifying website ownership is still a core investigative technique
notes: Domain registration as key investigative data source

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Historic data can be a useful tool when investigating websites, because it can track the transfer of a domain's ownership. It can also help identify owners of websites who have not consistently chosen to obscure their registration data by using a WHOIS privacy service.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: WHOIS history; domain ownership; historical data; privacy circumvention
relevance_rationale: Historic WHOIS data for tracking ownership changes remains valuable investigative method
notes: Historical WHOIS records can reveal ownership changes and privacy gaps

author: Tactical Tech
extract: When you look up the domain names registered to a certain email address, phone number or name, it is called a 'reverse WHOIS look-up.' Several tools and services offer these kinds of searches.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: reverse WHOIS; entity resolution; network mapping
relevance_rationale: Reverse WHOIS lookups for entity resolution and network mapping remain a core OSINT technique
notes: Reverse lookup: from identifier to registered domains

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Websites are hosted on one or more servers, or computers running server applications that transmit the site's content to visitors. Because web hosting costs money, related websites will often share hosting. Analysing the other domains sharing the same hosting service can sometimes shine a light on the owner or administrator of the site you are investigating.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: shared hosting; IP analysis; network mapping; web investigation
relevance_rationale: Shared hosting analysis for ownership connections remains a valid investigative technique
notes: Shared hosting as proxy for ownership connections

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Some administrators may add sensitive web addresses to a robots.txt file in an attempt to keep them hidden. This approach can backfire, as the file itself is easy to access, usually by appending '/robots.txt' to the domain name. Be sure to check the robots.txt file of the websites you investigate, just in case they list files or directories that the sites' administrators want to hide.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: robots.txt; hidden content; web investigation; information leakage
relevance_rationale: Checking robots.txt for unintentional disclosure of sensitive paths is still a valid investigative technique
notes: robots.txt as unintentional disclosure of sensitive paths

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Sitemap files are sort of the opposite of robots.txt files. They are used by site administrators to inform search engines about pages on their site that are available for crawling. Websites often use sitemap files to list all of the parts of the site they want to be indexed, and how often they want search engine indexes to be updated. To access sitemaps, you need to add '/sitemap.xml' to the domain name. Not all sites will have an accessible sitemap.xml file. The result is sometimes URLs that typically do not show up in searches and you can explore those manually.
chapter: How to See What's Behind a Website
themes: sitemap.xml; web investigation; hidden URLs; crawling
relevance_rationale: Using sitemap.xml to discover hidden pages remains a practical web investigation technique
notes: Sitemaps can reveal pages not surfaced by regular search

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Maps and satellite imagery not only allow you to get an overview of an area but also help you make connections that would otherwise be difficult, if not impossible, to establish from ground level. You can see, for example, how a series of factories are arranged along the same railway or river, notice a pattern of illegal deforestation in a rainforest or assess environmental and infrastructural damage following a disaster.
chapter: Using Maps to See Beyond the Obvious
themes: geospatial analysis; satellite imagery; pattern recognition; mapping
relevance_rationale: Geospatial analysis via maps and satellite imagery for pattern recognition is enhanced by AI but the core principle endures
notes: Maps reveal spatial patterns invisible at ground level

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Adding further information to maps – showing land ownership or the habitat of protected animals, for example – can help you make connections that might be valuable to an investigation. Maps and satellite images can also enable you to see over walls and look at what is happening in places that are difficult to access because they are restricted or unsafe, or far from where you are based.
chapter: Using Maps to See Beyond the Obvious
themes: data layering; geospatial analysis; remote access; mapping
relevance_rationale: Data layering on maps for analytical connections and remote investigation is an enduring technique
notes: Layering data on maps creates analytical connections; enables remote investigation

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Generally, investigations have at least one geographic component. In most cases, the company you are investigating has physical or registered offices somewhere and probably also operates in a distinct location. The people you are investigating most likely leave physical or digital traces that can be linked back to specific places – images from their trips or social media posts, for example. While places may be more obviously important in some investigations than in others – for instance, deforestation has a clear link to a certain location – creating maps, using geodata and geolocating evidence can often help to verify, or call into question, other sources of information.
chapter: Using Maps to See Beyond the Obvious
themes: geolocation; geospatial analysis; verification; cross-referencing
relevance_rationale: Geolocation and geodata as verification tool for cross-referencing evidence endures as investigative method
notes: Geographic dimension present in most investigations; geodata as verification tool

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Carrying out a detailed survey of a place can be useful for some investigations. It can help you create an accurate record of earthquake damage to one or more buildings, for example, or document the location of bullet holes following a shooting. This approach is low-tech, but that doesn't mean it should be regarded as an inferior or amateur technique: many professionals, including architects and engineers, use it to gain the most accurate measurements.
chapter: Using Maps to See Beyond the Obvious
themes: field survey; low-tech methods; physical evidence; documentation
relevance_rationale: Low-tech physical surveying for accurate documentation is a permanent technique unaffected by AI
notes: Low-tech physical survey is professional-grade; not inferior to digital

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Field research is remarkably effective, since it often yields physical evidence such as film, video or sound recordings, which can make even the most controversial and complex issues easier to understand. This is usually accompanied by other investigative techniques and research strategies. In fact, investigators often exhaust their desktop research before considering going into the field.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: field research; desk research; evidence hierarchy; multi-method
relevance_rationale: Field research yielding physical evidence after exhausting desk research is a permanent investigative sequence
notes: Field research follows desk research; yields powerful physical evidence

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Most research, whether field or remote, follows a cycle of four phases: Plan - develop a good understanding of the objectives, logistics and risks of your field research activity. Act - the actual field research activity. Analyse - assess and reflect on research results. Reflect - compare your research results with the objectives of your investigation. More often than not, this leads to a new plan for continued research.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: research cycle; planning; iteration; field research
relevance_rationale: The Plan-Act-Analyse-Reflect research cycle is a timeless iterative methodology
notes: Four-phase research cycle: Plan-Act-Analyse-Reflect; cyclical not linear

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Your field research will be more efficient if you set clear goals and objectives for yourself beforehand. While each research trip is unique, there is a kind of formula for successfully fulfilling a research objective. A research objective is usually a concrete purpose that describes what you are trying to achieve. Ideally this purpose is SMART: Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Realistic, Timebound.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: SMART objectives; planning; research design; field research
relevance_rationale: SMART objectives for field research are a permanent planning framework
notes: SMART framework applied to investigative field research objectives

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Practice your research methodology - if you've never taken environmental samples, used drones or a hidden camera, for instance, start by practicing these techniques in places that are unrelated to your actual investigation, and in a low-risk environment.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: practice; preparation; field research; methodology testing
relevance_rationale: Practicing research methods in low-risk settings before deployment is timeless preparation advice
notes: Practice methods in low-risk settings before deploying in actual investigation

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Simulate what could go wrong - it's much harder to practice what could go wrong in your research, since you generally want to avoid such situations. The best way to do this is to create a risk assessment and management list and act out potential problem situations together with people you trust. You should never carry out field research without an emergency contact and without someone knowing what you are doing, so try to practice with that person in advance.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: risk assessment; simulation; safety; emergency planning
relevance_rationale: Risk simulation and emergency planning are permanent safety requirements for field research
notes: Role-play failure scenarios; never do field work without emergency contact

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Ensure legality - knowing what is legal and what isn't in the places where you go on field research is critical, and should be a priority in your risk management. Things like secretly recording a conversation with a person can be legal in one county and illegal in another. These laws can even vary from state to state within a country.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: legality; risk management; jurisdiction; recording consent
relevance_rationale: Knowing jurisdiction-specific legality of recording is a permanent legal requirement
notes: Legal frameworks vary by jurisdiction; recording laws differ state to state

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Evidence collection - photo, video and audio evidence is very powerful because it counts as objective proof, although it can also be manipulated. There is a wide range of imagery you might have to take during an investigation, including exteriors and interiors of buildings, events, people, animals, documents, your own activities to prove you have done what you claim you have, etc.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: evidence collection; multimedia; documentation; field research
relevance_rationale: Photo, video, and audio evidence collection methods are permanent investigative techniques
notes: Record your own activities as meta-evidence; wide range of visual documentation needed

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Witness statements - as a general rule, keep in mind that when interviewing witnesses in the field, either covertly or overtly, their statements can rarely be seen as completely impartial. Usually, you will need to corroborate their statements with other types of evidence from additional sources while in the field or later on.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: witness testimony; corroboration; impartiality; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Witness statements requiring corroboration is a timeless evidentiary principle
notes: Witness statements require corroboration; never treat as impartial

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Environmental samples - if collecting samples (water, soil etc.) your planning has to address relevant sampling protocols. Rules can be complicated and require a good deal of care and attention. If you do not follow protocol, your samples could be of poor quality, or, at worst, become contaminated and provide false results. Sample analysis should always be done in accredited laboratories.
chapter: Away From Your Screen, Out in the Field
themes: sampling protocols; environmental evidence; contamination; lab analysis
relevance_rationale: Environmental sampling protocols and accredited lab requirements are unchanged by AI
notes: Strict protocols required; accredited labs only; contamination invalidates results

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Human interactions are all about common sense and may seem fairly intuitive on the surface. But with interviews in particular, because you are dealing with people and people are often unpredictable, preparation is key. Interviewing is a much longer process than just a one-time conversation or a series of questionnaires to collect information. It requires background research, building profiles of people, establishing trust, anticipating risks, taking safety measures and more.
themes: interviewing; preparation; source profiling; trust building
relevance_rationale: Interview preparation including background research, profiling, and trust building is a permanent human skill
notes: Interviewing as extended process requiring background research and profile building

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Prioritise and organise interviews - if possible, start with interviews that provide background information about the investigation or about other potential interviewees. Then, you will be able to approach more evidence-focused interviews. Try to go from the easiest interviews to the most difficult ones so you can prepare better to address adversarial subjects at the end, when you have more knowledge on the topic.
themes: interview sequencing; strategy; adversarial sources; progressive difficulty
relevance_rationale: Sequencing interviews from background to evidence-focused to adversarial is timeless strategy
notes: Interview sequence: background first, evidence-focused later, adversarial last

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Diversify and gain multiple perspectives - any investigation is multi-layered. The more diverse sources you include, the richer your evidence becomes and the easier it is to avoid bias. Include primary and secondary sources and try to interview people across different ages, genders, castes, classes, beliefs or job statuses.
themes: source diversity; bias mitigation; multiple perspectives; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Diversifying sources across demographics for richer evidence and bias mitigation is a permanent principle
notes: Diverse sources strengthen evidence and counter investigator bias

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Obtain consent and permission - even if a person has agreed to talk to you, try to obtain written permission for recording and taking photos/videos and get their explicit agreement on how the information they are providing will be used, or if their name will be mentioned and how. This is especially important when interviewing minors, where an adult should always give consent on their behalf. Note that some of the evidence might not be admissible if you don't have legal consent - especially if information may end up used in courts or as proof by others (journalists, lawyers, campaigners, NGOs etc.).
themes: informed consent; ethics; recording permission; legal admissibility
relevance_rationale: Obtaining informed consent for recording and use of information is a permanent ethical and legal requirement
notes: Written consent critical for legal admissibility; special rules for minors

author: Tactical Tech
extract: It's important to do as much research as possible before your interview so you can speak confidently if a confrontation takes place. Most importantly, don't let your bias take over; even if you don't respect a hostile person or a suspected perpetrator, you have to approach the interview with empathy. As your time with the source progresses, you can begin to ask more pointed and tougher questions. Remember that getting verifiable evidence from the interview is your objective.
themes: adversarial interviews; empathy; preparation; progressive questioning
relevance_rationale: Adversarial interview technique with empathy and progressive questioning is a permanent human skill
notes: Start soft, escalate questions; empathy even for hostile subjects; evidence is the goal

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Checks and balances - avoid your personal biases and consider your interviewees' biases in order to gain a broader perspective from your interviews. The ways in which you display your evidence and the weight you give to every interview is important. Try to counterbalance statements accurately.
themes: bias; counterbalancing; evidence weighting; interview analysis
relevance_rationale: Dual-bias awareness in weighing interview evidence is a permanent analytical principle
notes: Dual-bias awareness: own and interviewees'; weight evidence fairly

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Giving right of reply - if the purpose of your investigation is to publish a story that accuses someone of wrongdoing, they have a right to respond to those allegations.
themes: right of reply; fairness; publication ethics
relevance_rationale: Right of reply before publication is a permanent journalistic ethics requirement
notes: Standard journalistic practice: accused parties get chance to respond before publication

author: Tactical Tech
extract: If you work (or plan to work) as an investigator, you need to create a network of sources that you can rely on for information over time. You can reach out to potential sources and build connections in various ways: by starting with an informal interaction, having a coffee or meeting them at their workplace. You can also try to turn your interviewees into sources by keeping in contact with them after you are done with a specific interview or investigation.
themes: source network; relationship building; long-term contacts
relevance_rationale: Building a network of sources over time is a permanent investigative practice
notes: Building source network as ongoing investment; interviewees can become sources

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Never take your sources for granted. Once you have established contact and started building a relationship, you need to show interest and maintain contact. Ideally, don't just call your sources when you need something. Rather, try applying the law of the three calls. Once in a while, send a message showing interest in how they are doing and make sure they feel that you are not looking for information. A second call may follow later on to check on them, ask how things are going with work and in their lives, and let them know that you are up for a coffee. This should be only for the purpose of catching up and not to extract any information. Then, when you really need them for the purpose of an investigation, the third call will happen naturally.
themes: source management; relationship maintenance; trust building
relevance_rationale: The law of three calls for source relationship maintenance is a timeless human relationship principle
notes: Law of three calls: two social contacts before one information request

author: Tactical Tech
extract: You need to preemptively check your source's credibility and reliability every time you meet them, and not just before the first meeting, depending on who they are, the context they are informing in, and other factors.
themes: source credibility; ongoing verification; trust
relevance_rationale: Continuous reassessment of source credibility is a permanent verification discipline
notes: Source reliability is not static; must be continuously reassessed

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Confidentiality is key for your sources. If you disclose their details to others, you compromise your sources and your relationship with them. Your interaction must be based on mutual trust and sources have to be sure that you will not deceive them under any circumstances. You have the right to protect them even when asked about them by a judge, though this might vary depending on the country you're in.
themes: source protection; confidentiality; legal obligations; ethics
relevance_rationale: Source protection and confidentiality is a permanent ethical and legal obligation
notes: Source protection as absolute principle; legal protections vary by jurisdiction

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Supply chain investigations focus on collecting evidence to link each stage of a product's journey, from the origin of the commodity to the final product, which can contain hundreds of different components.
themes: supply chain; evidence linking; investigation design
relevance_rationale: Supply chain investigation as evidence-linking across stages is a permanent investigative framework
notes: Supply chain investigation defined as linking stages through evidence

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Supply chains are often complicated networks or webs of actors, processes and movements. While it takes thousands of links in multiple supply chains to manufacture one laptop, investigations usually focus on very specific components of a supply chain, such as the source of one single element used in the manufacture of one part of the laptop, or a single factory where one stage of the production takes place. Maintaining such a focus makes the research more effective.
themes: focus; scoping; supply chain; research design
relevance_rationale: Narrow scoping of supply chain investigations for tractability is a permanent research design principle
notes: Narrow focus on specific supply chain links makes investigation tractable

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Verification at every step - as with any investigation, you should always question your initial assumptions and results to make sure that the information you collect is relevant to what you intended to prove. What at first appears likely may in fact have another explanation. For instance, you might have linked a company illegally clearing forests to a timber mill that sells to a furniture company, only to find out that the furniture company does not actually use the timber species harvested from the forest in question. You therefore cannot make a direct connection between the company and the illegal deforestation activity.
themes: verification; assumptions; false connections; supply chain
relevance_rationale: Verifying every assumed connection in supply chains with concrete examples of false links is a timeless warning
notes: Concrete example of how plausible links can be wrong; verify every connection

author: Tactical Tech
extract: The ability to look sideways by making previously unseen connections is a critical skill, as is being able to come up with innovative solutions to research problems.
themes: lateral thinking; creativity; problem solving; supply chain
relevance_rationale: Lateral thinking and creative problem-solving are inherently human investigative skills
notes: Lateral thinking as core investigative skill for supply chain work

author: Tactical Tech
extract: When thinking about supply chains, a useful first step is to understand the various actors – the participating companies or individuals – that operate along the way. While the actual chains are usually unique combinations, some of the actors can be categorised into groups, which are often connected by shipping or transport providers.
themes: actor mapping; supply chain; categorization; network analysis
relevance_rationale: Actor mapping and categorization as first analytical step in supply chains is a permanent framework
notes: Profile and categorize supply chain actors as first analytical step

author: Tactical Tech
extract: You can start your investigation at any point along the supply chain where you suspect unethical or illegal behaviour might be taking place. In most cases, investigators follow the trail downstream along the supply chain from this entry point. Once you identify an entry point, you can follow a sequential path along the supply chain to collect evidence connecting each step, be it along a production process or a product's transport path.
themes: entry point; downstream tracing; supply chain; sequential evidence
relevance_rationale: Choosing entry points and tracing downstream in supply chain investigations is a permanent methodology
notes: Pick entry point where abuse suspected; typically trace downstream to brand/retailer

author: Tactical Tech
extract: By doing this background research you create a profile of your main actors and their connections, build your foundation for further investigation, prepare yourself for interviews with relevant people (sources, witnesses, specialists etc.), establish connections and assess your potential risks.
themes: background research; profiling; risk assessment; preparation
relevance_rationale: Background research for profiling actors and assessing risks is a permanent investigative preparation step
notes: Company profiling serves multiple purposes: foundation, interview prep, risk assessment

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Staying safe is an integral part of any investigation. Your safety, and that of your data, your human sources and your collaborators should always be a priority. It's therefore vital to think of the risks associated with the type of research or investigation activity you are undertaking in any context, in order to mitigate those risks. This is part of a process called risk assessment.
themes: risk assessment; safety; operational security
relevance_rationale: Risk assessment as integral to investigation rather than add-on is a permanent safety principle
notes: Risk assessment as integral to investigation, not an add-on

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Create a more secure, compartmentalised email account. Establish a separate set of social media accounts to use with services that require your data, in order to compartmentalise (separate) your investigative work from your personal online identity. Create a single use 'identity' for a particular investigation, and dispose of it once research is done.
themes: compartmentalization; operational security; identity management; online safety
relevance_rationale: Compartmentalized identities now need to account for AI profiling, browser fingerprinting by LLM tools, and AI-assisted deanonymization
notes: Three levels of identity compartmentalization: separate email, separate social, disposable identity

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Risk is inherited. If you are someone with little to no risk (you may live and work in a safe area) but you are interviewing a person experiencing high risk (living in a dangerous area, being under pressure, working on controversial issues), you inherit that risk. Your risk level will be higher for a period of time before and after the interview.
themes: inherited risk; source safety; risk assessment; temporal risk
relevance_rationale: Inherited risk from high-risk sources is a permanent security concept unaffected by AI
notes: Concept of inherited risk: proximity to high-risk sources elevates investigator's risk

author: Tactical Tech
extract: Take care when deciding the order in which you collect information, the people that you share it with, when/where you arrange to meet them and where and how you store the information you've gathered. Start with background research and less risky interviews or field work first, advance as you gather more information and always reassess the risks.
themes: risk management; progressive disclosure; information sequencing; safety
relevance_rationale: Sequencing information collection from low to high risk with continuous reassessment is a permanent safety principle
notes: Sequence collection from low to high risk; continuous risk reassessment

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Investigative journalism focuses on one particular clear angle that can be proven or disproven through in-depth research and careful documentation. It focuses not on isolated cases but on systemic failures and recurrent models, in order to establish the underlying causes, win justice for victims, and put an end to violations.
themes: hypothesis; systemic focus; investigation scope
relevance_rationale: Defining investigative journalism as hypothesis-driven systemic inquiry is a foundational epistemology unchanged by LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When a source provides a journalist with information that may spark an investigation, they must ask for detailed information. How did this happen? Where? When? Why? Who are the main people involved? If the answers given are convincing and logical, there might be an idea there. If not, it is no more than a rumour.
themes: source evaluation; tip assessment; questioning
relevance_rationale: Evaluating human source tips through critical questioning is a core journalistic skill unaffected by LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Government documents are a treasure trove of information. They include sources as diverse as the official gazette, the company register, parliamentary minutes, stock market announcements, property and land registries, court judgements, tender announcements, and lists of those who have been granted or stripped of citizenship.
themes: open data; government records; public registries
