relevance_rationale: Government records remain vital but LLMs can now assist in rapidly searching, parsing, and cross-referencing public registries.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Civil society organisations' reports and databases provide a wealth of fresh, unpublished information that can produce excellent results. Such organisations tend to be very interested in cooperating with the media, with whom they enjoy a mutually beneficial relationship: the media makes use of their data, and they benefit from press coverage of their activities.
themes: civil society; data sources; media partnerships
relevance_rationale: Building partnerships with civil society for unpublished data is a relationship-driven practice LLMs cannot replace.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Through digital analysis of videos made publicly available, BBC Africa's investigative unit was able to establish the time and place of the killing, the weapons that were used, and the identity of the perpetrators, confirming that the Cameroonian army was responsible despite its repeated denials.
themes: OSINT; video analysis; geolocation; digital verification
relevance_rationale: OSINT video analysis principles endure but LLM-powered tools now augment geolocation, facial recognition, and metadata extraction.
notes: BBC Africa Cameroon case study

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When a journalist gets an idea for an investigation they need to answer: Is the idea clear and does it have a particular angle? Has it been investigated before? What is the new angle? How important is it to people? Are the sources publicly available? Is the idea risky? Will proving it be difficult? What budget, skills, and tools are needed?
themes: feasibility assessment; story selection; planning checklist
relevance_rationale: The feasibility assessment checklist for story ideas is an editorial judgment process that LLMs do not change.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: The journalist needs to make sure that the idea is specific rather than general and that it deals with a single issue. We have to think outside the box to get a narrower angle. Brainstorming questions can lead us to preliminary research that in turn allows us to develop a specific idea.
themes: specificity; narrowing focus; question generation
relevance_rationale: Narrowing an investigation to a specific angle requires editorial judgment that remains fundamentally human.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: During preliminary research, the journalist needs to find out whether this story has been covered before. If it has been done, then they rethink the angle or move on to other ideas.
themes: prior work; novelty check; background research
relevance_rationale: Checking prior coverage is still essential but LLMs can now rapidly surface and summarize existing reporting on a topic.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Every idea for an investigation involves some risk, be it legal, physical, or digital, and the journalist needs to be aware of what that risk is and how best to avoid it. If they conclude that they would be risking death by investigating, the best thing is to stay away. No story is worth dying for.
themes: risk assessment; safety; threat modelling
relevance_rationale: Physical, legal, and digital risk assessment for journalists is a safety imperative unchanged by LLM technology.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: The hypothesis is critical to how an investigation proceeds. It must have a solid grounding in carefully acquired facts and draw on preliminary research showing that there is a story worth writing. A hypothesis is made up of facts and assumptions. Facts means solid, corroborated and carefully documented information, while assumptions means information that is yet to be corroborated.
themes: hypothesis formation; facts vs assumptions; evidence grounding
relevance_rationale: The epistemology of hypothesis formation grounded in facts vs assumptions is a timeless investigative principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Investigative journalism, like science, is about coming up with hypotheses, testing them, and trying to prove them. The best examples of investigative journalism are rooted in a hypothesis that allows them to work out what happened, how it happened, and why it happened.
themes: scientific method; hypothesis testing; investigative epistemology
relevance_rationale: The scientific-method analogy for investigative journalism is a foundational epistemological principle unaffected by LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Hypotheses make it easier to collect data, gather and organise new facts and evidence, and analyse it. They help keep control of the investigation, test the best methodology, focus and be precise, establish the boundaries and goals, set budgets and keep a tighter hold on time and resources, and establish the sources of the investigation.
themes: project management; hypothesis utility; investigation structure
relevance_rationale: Using hypotheses to structure data collection, budgets, and timelines is an enduring project management principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: A hypothesis can be tested, is based on established facts as well as uncorroborated assumptions, is concise, is coherent, and deals with a single problem. Although the hypothesis is central to the investigative process, it can always be amended if new evidence and facts require. A good journalist should always be open to evidence that contradicts their hypothesis and work just as hard to disprove it as to prove it.
themes: falsifiability; intellectual honesty; hypothesis revision
relevance_rationale: Falsifiability and willingness to revise hypotheses based on evidence is a timeless epistemic commitment.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: After coming up with a hypothesis, a journalist can work out what information they have already and what information they want to get access to. The best way to do this is to produce a written research plan. A systematic plan requires systematic thinking: questions about the facts, sources, opinions and analysis, the background, and anticipated obstacles. The plan specifies the facts necessary, the questions that lead to those facts, the sources that answer these questions, the methodology, and acceptable standards for evidence.
themes: research plan; investigation design; methodology; evidence standards
relevance_rationale: Systematic research planning mapping hypotheses to questions and sources is an enduring investigative design practice.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: The investigation plan schema maps each point of the hypothesis to specific questions and the sources that can answer them. This helps the journalist predict results, decide where to start looking, map out prospective and confirmed sources, establish sufficient standards of proof, and decide on methodology, budget and timeline.
themes: investigation matrix; source mapping; evidence planning
relevance_rationale: The investigation plan schema mapping points to questions and sources is a durable structural methodology.
notes: The handbook provides a detailed worked example with a Points/Questions/Sources table

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When producing an investigation plan, we focus on proving that there has been logical, legal and ethical wrongdoing and establishing where this occurred, who is responsible and what their motives were (money, fear, coercion, maintaining the status quo, or neglect and incompetence).
themes: burden of proof; wrongdoing typology; accountability
relevance_rationale: Establishing burden of proof and accountability motives is a fundamental investigative journalism principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Reliable sources are the backbone of any investigation. They are the gateway to facts, to background and context. They feed into one another, lending one another credence. A good journalist makes their sources work for one another. The number of sources is proportional to the strength of an investigation: the more sources an investigation cites, the stronger it is. Diverse and numerous sources make an investigation more balanced and objective.
themes: source diversity; corroboration; investigation strength
relevance_rationale: Source diversity and corroboration as the backbone of investigation strength is a timeless epistemological principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: A good journalist begins their research with open source materials. They gather, analyse and assess information, perhaps creating a special database to facilitate new discoveries and conclusions that they can then try and corroborate using their own network or any of the other sources available to them.
themes: open source research; database building; research workflow
relevance_rationale: Starting with open sources and building databases endures but LLMs now dramatically accelerate the gathering and initial analysis phase.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Paper sources provide strong evidence or a historical or contextual background. They are entirely reliable so long as not forged and cannot be modified or refuted in court unlike human sources whose narrative may change. But they may be protected by privacy laws. Paper sources sometimes require expert analysis and explanation (budgetary or accounting documents, for example, or court judgements).
themes: documentary evidence; evidence hierarchy; legal constraints
relevance_rationale: The evidentiary hierarchy privileging documentary evidence over human testimony is an enduring epistemological principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Primary sources are people directly connected with the event: victims, eyewitnesses, people responsible, people who intervened or participated. All primary sources will have different stories despite being closest to the event. Every source has their own biases, their own personal, psychological and social makeup that affects their view of events. Some might lie, give unsubstantiated suggestions, exaggerate, miss out things, or conflate facts and assumptions.
themes: primary sources; source bias; witness reliability
relevance_rationale: Understanding primary source biases and witness unreliability is a timeless journalistic skill.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Journalists should never be afraid to ask: How do you know? Examining information carefully is our first responsibility.
themes: verification; critical questioning; journalistic duty
relevance_rationale: The imperative to always ask 'how do you know?' is a foundational verification principle unchanged by technology.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: The ideal human source in investigative journalism is the closest person to the story who can be relied upon and trusted, who is willing to stand by their statements publicly and who is easily and safely accessible. Journalists should look for human sources that meet all these criteria at the same time.
themes: source selection; source quality criteria; access
relevance_rationale: Criteria for ideal human sources -- proximity, reliability, willingness to go on record -- are timeless.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Experts suggest interviewing victims, those hurt by an event or enemies of the parties under investigation first, because they are most likely to talk. Sources that are the object of suspicion or accusation will talk to a journalist when they realise that everyone else involved in the event either has talked or might talk.
themes: interview sequence; source strategy; leverage
relevance_rationale: Strategic interview sequencing -- victims first, suspects last -- is an enduring investigative tactic.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: By communicating with sources, journalists aim to collect facts and get access to documents and supporting evidence. They should ask directly: do you have any documents that support your story? A meeting should also aim to acquire new sources: who knows about this other than you? Who was with you? Please give me their names and how to contact them. This is a central part of building a network of sources.
themes: source networking; document acquisition; snowball sourcing
relevance_rationale: Snowball sourcing and asking for documents during interviews is a relationship skill LLMs cannot perform.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: A good journalist knows that sources conflate facts and assumptions, and seeks the help of experts in analysing documents and interpreting events. Experts typically provide unbiased, in-depth and detailed information. A good journalist can also get sources to talk honestly. The approach taken when interviewing victims is totally different from the approach used when interviewing those responsible, and different again from experts.
themes: expert sources; interview technique; source-type adaptation
relevance_rationale: Adapting interview techniques to source type and using experts to interpret documents are enduring human skills.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Anonymous sources should only be used for a clear and justifiable reason, when there is no other option, and with the agreement of all parties. If you use an anonymous source, you must confirm the information using another source. Mark Hunter says that information should never be attributed to an anonymous source unless it is confirmed by at least four other sources.
themes: anonymous sources; corroboration threshold; editorial standards
relevance_rationale: Anonymous source protocols and corroboration thresholds are enduring editorial standards.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When sources request anonymity, journalists should establish whether they do not want their name or job title published, whether they do not want the information itself published, or both (off the record). You should ask who else is aware of the information. If there are several people, ask for their names and contact details without divulging the original source. You can then publish the information and note it has been confirmed by various sources.
themes: source protection; anonymity protocol; off the record
relevance_rationale: Establishing and managing different levels of source anonymity is a relationship protocol LLMs do not change.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Digital sources -- government and private databases, websites, video and audio libraries and social media -- constitute a vast archive that, with analysis, can help you get at the facts. They provide rapid, cross-border information. But digital sources produce soulless investigative reporting if they are not supplemented by human sources close to the issue. They also require careful verification, because they are easy to modify and decontextualise.
themes: digital sources; verification; human-digital balance
relevance_rationale: Digital source assessment endures but LLMs add both new verification capabilities and new risks of AI-generated disinformation.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In investigative journalism evidence must be proven beyond all doubt, because doubt will be interpreted to the detriment of the hypothesis. If an investigation presents possibilities rather than facts, then it is not yet fully ripe. A good journalist works to develop suspicion or possibility into certainty. Certainty requires the elimination of any doubt, and doubt can only be eliminated by marshalling solid, reliable evidence.
themes: burden of proof; certainty standard; evidence threshold
relevance_rationale: The standard that evidence must be proven beyond all doubt is a timeless investigative journalism principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: A good journalist looks for evidence refuting their hypothesis. This is the best way of avoiding guesswork. Contradictory evidence may demonstrate that the event did not happen as suggested in the hypothesis, and that the error was not deliberate. Evidence may prove that the error was the result not of collusion and bad intentions but dereliction of duty or neglect. This is a very different story, even if the ultimate result is the same.
themes: disconfirmation; intellectual honesty; alternative explanations
relevance_rationale: Actively seeking disconfirming evidence is a core epistemic virtue that becomes even more important in the LLM era.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Witness testimony must be provided by primary sources directly linked to the event, by several different and independent sources who cannot have decided on their story beforehand. For any piece of information to be acceptable, it requires corroboration by at least two known, independent sources. Several sources may depend on a single original source, and in this case they should all be taken as one and not treated as separate sources for corroboration.
themes: corroboration rule; source independence; two-source minimum
relevance_rationale: Two-source corroboration rule and source independence checks are timeless verification standards.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: An investigative journalist compares and inspects different accounts in order to work out where they intersect. They should test the credibility of each source throughout the interview, ask for evidence supporting their testimony, try to establish sources' motives, and weigh up information making sure it makes logical sense. A sceptical attitude is one of the best defences against being manipulated.
themes: cross-referencing; credibility testing; source motives; scepticism
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing accounts, testing credibility, and maintaining scepticism are enduring core investigative skills.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: An investigative journalist should work hard to get access to all the primary sources on both sides of the investigation -- for and against. They should have recourse to independent, neutral third parties familiar with the facts who can help them assess how accurate and trustworthy a narrative is.
themes: balance; both sides; neutral third parties
relevance_rationale: Engaging all primary sources on both sides and consulting neutral third parties is a timeless fairness principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Data does not only mean numbers. It means any raw material -- numbers, letters, symbols -- whether it has been processed or not, even if it is simply individual facts obtained in different contexts. To put together a database that will help produce a quality investigation, you need to be aware of where to find that data, whether it is descriptive or numerical, and how best to get at, develop, and verify it.
themes: data definition; database building; data literacy
relevance_rationale: Understanding data broadly as any raw material requiring careful sourcing and verification is a durable principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Much published or available data will fail to totally meet your requirements. You will need to put together a database tailored to your specific needs by using existing databases, collecting the missing data yourself or with the help of others, and conducting surveys, interviews and field visits.
themes: custom databases; data collection; field work; gap filling
relevance_rationale: Building custom databases from multiple sources endures but LLMs can now assist with data collection, structuring, and gap identification.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In the Jordanian House of Representatives investigation, the team found government contracts on official websites, tender data from the Public Procurement Bureau, an open database of companies and shareholders, and leaked Civil Status records. Using all this information, the team came up with a spreadsheet format appropriate to the investigation, with names, dates, company details, contract values, and other relevant information. The team proved the investigation by analysing, collating, indexing, and arranging data extracted from publicly available documents.
themes: spreadsheet methodology; data integration; public records; entity matching
relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet-based entity matching from public records endures as method but LLMs and AI tools can now automate much of the cross-referencing.
notes: ARIJ/amman.net Jordanian HoR investigation case study

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Data cannot take the place of traditional journalism, and even the best data analysis cannot replace fieldwork and investigation. The team submitted 27 freedom of information requests to various ministries, interviewed experts on the constitutional and legal aspects, and confronted representatives who had violated the constitution and the law.
themes: data limits; fieldwork; FOI requests; confrontation
relevance_rationale: The principle that data analysis cannot replace fieldwork, FOI requests, and confrontation interviews remains essential.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Verifying data protects journalists from misleading the public. Data is at least as dishonest as people: at the end of the day, it is collected and collated by human beings. Data cannot be trusted absolutely, because it is influenced by the biases of those who collect it. You should always verify your data and ask questions to those who collected it.
themes: data verification; data bias; data provenance
relevance_rationale: Critical awareness that data carries collector biases and requires provenance checking is even more relevant with AI-generated data.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: We advise journalists to ask government bodies for raw data, because analysis of raw data helps produce a successful investigation. It is also often necessary to access information that the government does not want to reveal. In this case you will have to negotiate with officials, work out how to classify it, digitalise it and enter it into a database. You will have to sift through it carefully and review it to guarantee it is free of mistakes.
themes: raw data; government data; data cleaning; digitisation
relevance_rationale: Requesting raw government data and digitizing it endures but LLMs can now assist with OCR, classification, and cleaning at scale.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Official documents used should confirm the incidents and acts that the journalist wants to prove. They should be related to the facts and the characters involved. Documents should not be used simply for decoration.
themes: documentary evidence; relevance; evidence purpose
relevance_rationale: The principle that documents must confirm specific facts rather than serve as decoration is a timeless evidence standard.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Leaks that do not come in document form but rather as copy-pasted or encoded text require particular care. Anyone whose name appears can easily dispute or deny their involvement, and journalists may be sued for publishing unsubstantiated information. When no original copy is provided, we have no way of knowing whether sentences or words have been lost or added, or whether figures, dates, or spelling of names is correct.
themes: leak verification; document authenticity; legal risk
relevance_rationale: Leak verification principles endure but LLMs introduce new challenges -- AI-generated fake documents and deepfakes require updated verification methods.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When power struggles take place between different elites or factions within the regime itself, many documents will be issued, some entirely valid and some less so. Journalists may find themselves being used as puppets in these conflicts. Be particularly careful in these circumstances: publishing a fake document may end your career.
themes: document manipulation; political instrumentalisation; source motives
relevance_rationale: Awareness that journalists can be instrumentalized in power struggles through planted documents is a timeless caution.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Personal experience means a journalist undertaking an entirely legal action, without concealing their identity, in order to test compliance to the law by others and uncover mistakes or violations. It is one of the most important sources of proof, particularly in investigations involving careful inspection of legal procedures or routine official practice. Eyewitnesses often do not pay enough attention to the detail of legally and procedurally complex processes.
themes: personal experience; compliance testing; process audit
relevance_rationale: Personal experience as compliance testing methodology is a physical fieldwork practice unaffected by LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When using personal experience: engage with the system as a normal citizen in an entirely legal way. Repeat the experience multiple times to prove the problem is systemic, not attributable to bad apples. Conduct in-depth research and familiarise yourself with the relevant regulations before engaging. Document everything: ask for receipts, cards and documents, take photos, record films, or write everything down in detail as soon as you finish.
themes: documentation; repeatability; systemic proof; field methodology
relevance_rationale: Documenting personal experience through repetition and meticulous records is a physical evidence practice LLMs cannot replace.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Journalists use lab analysis to confirm that violations have taken place that cannot be proven by other methods. Lab analysis constitutes very solid evidence, especially if produced by a neutral, specialised, recognised party. It requires a precise and solid methodology to rule out any doubt in the results. You should always stick to highly respected labs that enjoy technical and scientific accreditation.
themes: lab analysis; scientific evidence; accreditation; methodology
relevance_rationale: Using accredited lab analysis as evidence is a domain-specific verification method outside LLM capabilities.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Lab analysis results alone do not constitute an investigation. You also need to establish the background and context, work out what results mean from a scientific perspective, what effect they have on victims, and how the results connect to the victims and the event. The investigation may require testing multiple samples over an extended period to demonstrate that contamination is not an isolated case but part of a systematic and ongoing problem.
themes: contextualisation; repeated sampling; systemic proof; scientific interpretation
relevance_rationale: Contextualizing lab results within a broader narrative and demonstrating systemic patterns is an enduring analytical principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Investigations looking into the safety of food, medicine, water etc require taking samples correctly. You might seek the help of a technical expert in taking the samples and record the process on video. You should also record the payment as well as the process of taking samples to the laboratory and handing them over.
themes: sampling methodology; chain of custody; documentation; expert assistance
relevance_rationale: Proper sampling methodology and chain of custody documentation are physical-world standards unaffected by LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In many investigations a journalist will not be able to produce convincing proof on their own because of the technical nature of the issue. You should always choose the best experts -- the ones used by the courts or other official bodies. Get at least two or three independent experts to produce reports and compare results, so as to ensure they are neutral and independent. When finished, they should write a full report and, if possible, sign and stamp it.
themes: technical expertise; multiple experts; independence; expert reports
relevance_rationale: Using multiple independent technical experts and requiring signed reports is an enduring evidentiary standard.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Just as with lab analysis, technical expertise alone does not produce an investigation. It is part of the process of proof, and must be supported with context, background, source testimony, and, if possible, documentary evidence.
themes: evidence integration; contextualisation; multi-source proof
relevance_rationale: The principle that technical expertise alone does not make an investigation without context and testimony is timeless.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Going undercover should be a last resort used only after all other methods have failed and you have documented the failure. The information should be of great importance to the public interest. Document all your attempts to acquire information by professional and legitimate means. Always get your editor's approval. Always have a back-up plan in case you or your team are arrested. Consult a specialised media lawyer to establish legal problems that might arise.
themes: undercover journalism; last resort principle; editorial approval; legal preparation
relevance_rationale: Undercover journalism as a last resort with documented justification is an enduring ethical and legal principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: The point is for the journalist to show, precisely and objectively, the existence of a serious and systematic issue. Although there is a very thin line between going undercover and entrapment, journalism is not about setting people up to fail. By setting up an ambush you are indirectly encouraging them to make a mistake they would not have made otherwise.
themes: entrapment; ethics; objectivity; undercover limits
relevance_rationale: The ethical distinction between undercover journalism and entrapment is a timeless principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Always give the person or organisation affected by the investigation the chance to respond so that the investigation is fair and just. When producing the story, always make it clear to the public that you went undercover. Be transparent and honest and explain your decision to do so.
themes: right of reply; transparency; fairness; presentation ethics
relevance_rationale: Right of reply and transparency about undercover methods are enduring ethical obligations.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When using hidden cameras: the harm of allowing the information to remain hidden should be greater than the harm caused by using hidden cameras. You should have exhausted all other means. The recording should expose major and systematic failings. You should not record in homes or private spaces protected by law. The editor-in-chief should sign off. Consult a specialised media lawyer. Put in place an emergency plan. Always present affected parties with footage and provide a right of reply.
themes: hidden cameras; proportionality; editorial sign-off; legal safeguards; right of reply
relevance_rationale: Hidden camera proportionality test, editorial sign-off, and right of reply are enduring ethical and legal safeguards.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: A survey is a kind of research involving collecting and collating primary data from a particular community in accordance with a unified methodology, building on a particular hypothesis and set of questions, before analysing this data statistically using established scientific techniques. Since investigative journalism is a collaborative team activity, a team of journalists and researchers can help put together a survey methodology, gather data and analyse them using statistical software like SPSS.
themes: surveys; statistical analysis; team collaboration; SPSS; quantitative methods
relevance_rationale: Survey methodology endures but SPSS is dated; LLMs and modern tools like Python/R now handle statistical analysis more accessibly.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In Bangladesh, journalists combined survey methodology with in-depth interviews, pictures and documents. Rather than interviewing anyone willing, the sample was selected randomly, with 400 out of 7500 cases. The survey exposed massive corruption in a social health program, showing 96% of users were receiving support they were not entitled to. The investigation lasted twelve months and involved eighteen journalists, producing results that were almost indisputable.
themes: random sampling; mixed methods; survey-investigation hybrid; team scale
relevance_rationale: Combining random sampling surveys with in-depth investigation is an enduring mixed-methods approach.
notes: Bangladesh health program corruption case study

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In both investigations journalists successfully combined surveys with more conventional investigative techniques. Combining techniques bolsters an investigation and makes it more convincing and credible.
themes: mixed methods; methodological triangulation; credibility
relevance_rationale: Methodological triangulation strengthening investigation credibility is a timeless research principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Some journalists reach out directly to the public for information after publication, calling for input from their readership and the broader public. Data provided voluntarily by the public needs a great deal of work to verify and corroborate before it is incorporated into the investigation. Gathering testimony like this requires a solid relationship with the audience based on trust rooted in the journalist's independence and professionality.
themes: crowdsourcing; audience engagement; public testimony; verification burden
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing from the public with rigorous verification is an enduring practice that predates and survives LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Data constitutes the raw material that a journalist uses to cast light into darkness, clear up ambiguities and solve apparent contradictions. Investigative journalism and data-driven journalism both engage in in-depth research and sift through information to exclude any impurities (fake news or misleading data). You should be careful to distinguish data from facts: data does not necessarily mean fact. There may be biases in the way data is collected.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: data journalism; data vs facts; data bias; critical data literacy
relevance_rationale: Critical data literacy -- distinguishing data from facts and recognizing collection biases -- is even more vital in the LLM era.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: When you first start working on an investigation, look up the data already available -- whether official or unofficial -- to answer as many questions as possible before moving on to posing questions whose answers are unknown and coming up with hypotheses. Data will help make your story measurable, allowing you to render your hows as measurable how-muchs.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: data-first approach; quantification; measurement; research sequence
relevance_rationale: Data-first research approach endures but LLMs can now rapidly search and synthesize existing datasets before hypothesis formation.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: There are several database programs for handling data: Microsoft Excel (spreadsheets), OpenRefine (data refining), Fusion Tables (verification), MySQL (databases), SOLR and Access (databases). After collating and refining data, you can use descriptive analysis to answer what, who, how, where and when.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: data tools; spreadsheets; OpenRefine; databases; descriptive analysis
relevance_rationale: The specific tools listed (Fusion Tables discontinued, SOLR/Access dated) need updating; LLMs and modern Python/R workflows have superseded many.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Techniques from data-driven journalism that assist investigations include: analysing data taken from social media profiles of perpetrators or influential people to tease out lines of investigation, and accessing data that has been deleted using archiving tools like Internet Archive or the Wayback Machine.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: social media analysis; web archives; Wayback Machine; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Social media OSINT and Wayback Machine remain relevant but LLM-powered tools now augment profile analysis and pattern detection.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Fact-checking began with the investigation of fabricated photos or decontextualized video clips. But these techniques have got better and their use more sophisticated, creating a space for a new type of open source journalism. These modern techniques can be combined with traditional techniques to produce high-quality investigations.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: OSINT evolution; fact-checking; hybrid methodology; open source journalism
relevance_rationale: OSINT fact-checking evolution continues rapidly; LLMs and AI-generated deepfakes demand updated verification techniques.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: By comparing Google Maps with the crime scene, the BBC team were able to prove that not Boko Haram but government forces had carried out the executions, and that they had taken place not in Mali but in Cameroon. The video clip was verified and analysed scientifically: the armed men, the place, and the type and source of weapons were all identified.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: geolocation; video verification; Google Maps; digital forensics
relevance_rationale: Geolocation via satellite imagery endures but AI-powered tools now augment matching and analysis beyond manual Google Maps comparison.
notes: BBC Cameroon investigation case study

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: OpenCorporates contains information about more than 165 million companies and more than 200 million senior employees. You can conduct general and country-specific searches. Because the website uses specific filters, it is easier to establish connections between companies or work out which companies are run by the same CEO.
themes: OpenCorporates; company research; corporate networks; entity matching
relevance_rationale: OpenCorporates remains useful but LLMs can now assist with cross-referencing corporate networks and entity resolution at scale.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Investigative Dashboard (OCCRP) provides a massive database containing millions of records of previous investigations, links and public sources, and also makes it easy to contact experts in various fields from around the world.
themes: Investigative Dashboard; OCCRP; expert networks; cross-border
relevance_rationale: OCCRP Investigative Dashboard as a collaborative cross-border resource remains essential and is itself integrating AI tools.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: FlightRadar24 uses airport data and volunteer networks to show the movement of flights in real time. You can get information about flights, flight paths, planes' registration information and the companies they belong to. MarineTraffic is the maritime counterpart for following ship movements worldwide. Track-trace allows you to trace shipping containers around the world using an ID number.
themes: FlightRadar24; MarineTraffic; track-trace; transport monitoring; tracking
relevance_rationale: Flight and maritime tracking tools like FlightRadar24 and MarineTraffic are domain-specific platforms that endure alongside LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Amnesty International YouTube Data Viewer helps verify potentially fabricated video clips. All you need is the YouTube link. The tool culls data about the video like the original production date, and provides thumbnails that can be entered into Google reverse image search to identify potential original videos.
themes: Amnesty Data Viewer; video verification; reverse image search; YouTube
relevance_rationale: Amnesty YouTube Data Viewer concept endures but AI-powered deepfake detection and video forensics tools now supplement or replace it.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: MapChecking allows you to produce specially drawn maps to estimate crowd sizes -- how many people might fit in a particular area. This is useful for establishing how many people took part in a demonstration or celebration. Using the cursor, you delineate the area and the tool calculates the square kilometres and approximate number of people.
themes: MapChecking; crowd estimation; geospatial analysis; protests
relevance_rationale: Crowd estimation from mapped areas is now better served by AI-powered computer vision tools that can count from imagery directly.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: Given how many ways there are of getting information on others, journalists should be careful to protect themselves. OnionShare allows sending data over Tor's secure network using a one-time link. LastPass manages passwords. Signal should be a central part of any news outlet's communication strategy so that messages exchanged with third parties cannot be hacked or seen by others.
themes: cybersecurity; OnionShare; Tor; Signal; password management; source protection
relevance_rationale: Operational security tools like Signal, Tor, and secure file sharing are essential infrastructure that endures regardless of LLMs.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: For a journalist, a leaked document is the first clue in an in-depth investigation that will involve careful documentation of facts, marshalling primary sources and confronting those responsible -- as well as verifying the documents' authenticity and establishing the motive for the leak.
themes: leaks; document verification; leak motives; starting points
relevance_rationale: The principle that leaks are starting points requiring full verification and motive assessment is a timeless journalistic standard.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In 2016, a journalist surfing the Jordanian Constitutional Court website found the Prime Minister had repeatedly responded to court requests after the statutory time limit. Analysis of all judgements showed failures to submit documents on time in four constitutional appeals, that speakers of both chambers had ignored appeals, that two thirds of appeals failed, and 81% of judgements lacked consensus.
themes: government websites; open data; document analysis; pattern detection
relevance_rationale: Finding stories in government website data endures but LLMs can now automate pattern detection across large document sets.
notes: amman.net Constitutional Court case study

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In the case of the Panama Papers, the leaks came in the form of documents, passports, emails, signatures which helped confirm their veracity. But none of this means that journalists are exempted from engaging in their own traditional examination and verification of documents that they want to use in their investigations.
themes: document verification; Panama Papers; leaks; journalist duty
relevance_rationale: The duty to verify leaked documents regardless of their apparent authenticity is a timeless verification principle.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: The Panama Papers project drew on more than 11.5 million documents making up 2.6 terabytes of data dating from 1977 to 2015. The International Consortium of Investigative Journalists incorporated the data into a database that makes sifting through it and searching it much easier.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: Panama Papers; large-scale data; database construction; ICIJ; collaborative journalism
relevance_rationale: Building searchable databases from massive leaks endures but LLMs now dramatically accelerate parsing, indexing, and querying at terabyte scale.

author: Al Jazeera Media Institute
extract: In Sudan, BBC journalists were able to collect and review more than 300 videos shot by activists on the ground, allowing them to reconstruct a scene showing that the Rapid Response Forces had fired live ammunition on protesters. In both cases, journalists were able to dispense with traditional methods and with teams on the ground. Thousands of videos shared on social media, carefully verified, were the deciding factor.
chapter: Investigative journalism in the digital age
themes: UGC verification; social media evidence; remote investigation; video reconstruction
relevance_rationale: UGC video verification and remote investigation endure but AI tools now augment video analysis, authentication, and reconstruction at scale.
notes: BBC Sudan investigation case study

author: The Engine Room
extract: Determining the questions that you are trying to answer is the most important part of a data collection process. Data should always be gathered for a reason: namely, because you think the answer to your question lies within.
themes: research question; data collection; purpose
relevance_rationale: Starting with research questions before collecting data is a foundational principle unaffected by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Project design can translate seemingly fluffy principles into tangible, concrete steps and activities. It can help narrow down a possibly bewildering array of tools and techniques into those best suited for achieving responsible outcomes, and set the scene for how you and your colleagues and partners will interact with data.
themes: project design; responsible data; planning
relevance_rationale: Project design translating principles into concrete steps is organizational discipline independent of AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Don't design alone: think carefully about who needs to be in the room, and engage the various stakeholders or experts as soon as possible. It's usually a bad idea to bring specialists like techies or lawyers into planning processes after essential components have already been decided.
themes: collaboration; stakeholders; project design
relevance_rationale: Inclusive stakeholder engagement in design is a participatory principle unaffected by technology

author: The Engine Room
extract: Check yourself: give yourself opportunities for monitoring and calibrating, and don't wait for the project to be shut down before learning lessons and parsing failures. Periodically check whether you are staying true to the responsible data standards and ethical framework that you set for yourself up front.
themes: monitoring; ethics; iterative review
relevance_rationale: Monitoring and calibrating throughout a project is a management principle independent of AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Document the process: track both the small and big picture approach to have a clear, organisational baseline that can be relied upon as people come and go and the project evolves. This will also make it much easier for you to draw out lessons learned from the project.
themes: documentation; process tracking; institutional memory
relevance_rationale: Documenting process for organizational baseline is a governance practice that applies equally to AI workflows

author: The Engine Room
extract: Threat modelling is a type of risk assessment. To conduct a threat modelling exercise, identify: Assets (types of data the project will create or use), Risks (vulnerabilities in the data flow), Adversaries (individuals or groups interested in making threats real), Threats (potential harms grouped as loss, illegitimate access, or manipulation), Likelihood, and Mitigation Strategy.
chapter: Don't Panic, Plan: Assessing Risks and Threats
themes: threat modelling; risk assessment; security
relevance_rationale: Threat modeling framework endures but must now include AI-specific threats like model extraction and prompt injection

author: The Engine Room
extract: Feed your findings back into your project lifecycle diagram. Does this leave any critical gaps? Do you need additional resources? Should you reconsider the project in its entirety? Asking these questions will help you focus on finding the right responses.
chapter: Don't Panic, Plan: Assessing Risks and Threats
themes: risk assessment; project lifecycle; resource planning
relevance_rationale: Feeding risk findings back into project design is a governance loop independent of technology

author: The Engine Room
extract: Prevention is only half of what you can do. There will always be residual risk and threats you cannot foresee. This is why it is important to prepare for incidents to happen, so you can contain them or mitigate their impact. Encourage a culture of openness and learning from mistakes, not blame.
chapter: Don't Panic, Plan: Assessing Risks and Threats
themes: contingency planning; error culture; risk mitigation
relevance_rationale: Planning for incident response alongside prevention is a security principle that applies to AI systems too

author: The Engine Room
extract: Which technologies do you plan to use? Work out what you need before choosing a platform: be sure it has the functionality you need to grow along with your program and that you have budgeted for development, testing, maintenance and support. No technology is ever 100% secure, but technology that has been independently audited will have fewer vulnerabilities.
themes: technology choice; budgeting; security audit
relevance_rationale: Needs-first platform selection endures but must now evaluate AI-powered platforms and their data handling

author: The Engine Room
extract: Trust is important, but sometimes you will need to verify that best practices are being followed. At what stages in your project do you need to assure the quality of the work being done? This can happen at the data collection stage, during data migrations and verification, or when data is analysed.
themes: quality assurance; data integrity; workflow stages
relevance_rationale: Trust-but-verify for best practices compliance is a governance principle that extends to AI tool vendors

author: The Engine Room
extract: Data integrity includes aspects of both information security and information quality. Key questions: Is the data checked for changes at key points? Are changes flagged with version control? Is the data collection process well-documented through a codebook? Does the data include contextual metadata necessary to understand it?
themes: data integrity; version control; codebook; metadata
relevance_rationale: Data integrity checks endure but must now account for AI-generated or AI-processed data quality issues

author: The Engine Room
extract: Data storage mitigation strategies: only store the minimal amount of data necessary to complete the task; de-identify data by default; encrypt your data at all stages of collection, usage, transmission and storage; conduct regular audits and penetration tests of your security measures.
themes: data minimisation; encryption; security; storage
relevance_rationale: Data minimization and de-identification are privacy principles that apply equally when AI processes data

author: The Engine Room
extract: You can store some information separately from the rest. For example, if you have a database with case files, you can replace the names with codes and create a spreadsheet with the codes and their resolution to real names, then store this spreadsheet on another, highly-protected computer.
themes: data separation; pseudonymisation; security
relevance_rationale: Separating identifiers from case data is a privacy architecture principle independent of AI
notes: Book notes this alone will not achieve true anonymisation

author: The Engine Room
extract: A general security principle is to limit user access to only the minimum amount of data that they need to be able to do their work effectively, and only have access for as long as they need it. People should also only have the minimum permissions that they need to do their job.
themes: access control; least privilege; permissions
relevance_rationale: Least-privilege access to data is a security principle that becomes more important with AI tools

author: The Engine Room
extract: There are two main ways of getting data: using existing data (publicly available like a census, or non-released data via FOI requests, or scraping social media) and collecting new data (gathering data directly from subjects or generating it indirectly, e.g. logging GPS pings).
themes: data sources; existing data; new data; FOI; scraping
relevance_rationale: Two data acquisition paths still valid but now must include AI-generated synthetic data and LLM-extracted data

author: The Engine Room
extract: No matter where data come from, try your best to ensure that it is good data. The most basic aspects of data quality are accuracy, validity and timeliness.
themes: data quality; accuracy; validity; timeliness
relevance_rationale: Data quality fundamentals endure but LLMs can now automate accuracy, completeness, and timeliness checks

author: The Engine Room
extract: Designing the data collection process requires asking: What are you collecting and why? Who provides the data and did you get their consent? Who will collect the information—ensure they are appropriate in relation to participants' identities (gender, race, class, language)? How will you collect—establish a clear process.
themes: data collection design; consent; enumerator selection; methodology
relevance_rationale: Data collection design questions endure but must now address AI-mediated collection and consent for AI processing

author: The Engine Room
extract: Consider the impact of the data collection method on desired conversational outcomes. Conversations about sensitive topics shouldn't be interrupted by the data collection tool; do what you can to remove external factors that might act as inhibitors for respondents.
themes: survey design; sensitive topics; respondent experience
relevance_rationale: Considering data collection methods impact on sensitive conversations is a human-centered design principle

author: The Engine Room
extract: When deciding what data to collect, consider the minimum amount of data necessary to reach the project goals. The rationale for collection should be that it is necessary to achieve your goals, not just that it would make things easier or save you time.
themes: data minimisation; purpose limitation; collection scope
relevance_rationale: Data minimization principle is even more important when AI tools can easily combine and re-identify datasets

author: The Engine Room
extract: Metadata can reveal a lot of information that you were deliberately not collecting, or that you want to protect. If this is the case, make purging of metadata part of the collection process from the very beginning, or configure the tools you are using to not create it in the first place.
themes: metadata; privacy; data collection; operational security
relevance_rationale: Metadata exposure risks are amplified by AI that can extract and correlate metadata across sources at scale

author: The Engine Room
extract: In some cases, paper is a good option if using electronic devices will create suspicion or risk. But also consider that electronic devices can be password protected and encrypted to protect the data in ways that paper cannot.
themes: tool choice; paper vs digital; security tradeoffs
relevance_rationale: Choosing paper over electronic collection in hostile environments is a situational security judgment

author: The Engine Room
extract: When working with existing data, remember that it was collected by someone else for specific purposes that probably don't match yours. This can lead to biases and gaps. Try to gain insight into the context before using it: Who published/licensed the data? Who funded the process? What biases might it hold? What methods were used to collect it?
themes: secondary data; provenance; bias; context assessment
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward inherited data collected for other purposes is an epistemological principle that applies to AI training data too

author: The Engine Room
extract: Data should be usable as well as useful. Data will need to be machine readable for most types of analysis. Moving data from one format to another needs to be planned and executed carefully, so as not to lose information or inadvertently manipulate the data. Make sure to keep versions, and clearly document your steps.
themes: data formats; machine readability; version control; documentation
relevance_rationale: Data format conversion still essential but LLMs can now automate many format transformations

author: The Engine Room
extract: Even when data is de-identified, anonymized or aggregated so individuals are not immediately apparent, the possibility to re-identify individuals is always there, especially when combining that data with complementary sources. This is the Mosaic effect.
themes: re-identification; mosaic effect; anonymisation limits; privacy
relevance_rationale: Re-identification risk amplified by LLMs ability to cross-reference and infer identities from sparse data

author: The Engine Room
extract: It's worth spending time trying to identify types of people reflected by data, even when they are not readily apparent. Organize this as a brainstorming exercise with the project team, to try and work out as much as possible about the people behind a found data set, then use those intuitions to consider relevant responsibilities.
themes: hidden populations; data ethics; team exercise; re-identification risk
relevance_rationale: Brainstorming hidden populations in data is a human ethical exercise unchanged by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: When presenting existing data, it is critical to include information about the data's provenance and the context in which it was produced. Being explicit about the data's contextual unknowns is essential for enabling others to manage your project's data responsibly.
themes: provenance; transparency; contextual unknowns; data presentation
relevance_rationale: Presenting data provenance and contextual unknowns is a core transparency principle unaffected by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Informed consent is composed of three components: disclosure of research objectives and any risks of participating; capacity of individuals to understand implications of participating; and voluntariness of their participation.
themes: informed consent; disclosure; capacity; voluntariness
relevance_rationale: Informed consent components (disclosure, capacity, voluntariness) are foundational ethical principles

author: The Engine Room
extract: Consent should be linked to a clearly identifiable purpose rather than a vague, omnibus, open-ended one. Disclosure should include: the nature and objectives of the project, the purpose for which data is collected, how the data will be used, what risks the data and its use might pose, and what opportunities data subjects will have to review and revoke consent.
themes: consent policy; purpose limitation; disclosure checklist; data rights
relevance_rationale: Purpose-linked consent and disclosure checklists are enduring data ethics requirements

author: The Engine Room
extract: Research increasingly indicates that consent has its limitations. Due to bounded rationality, people cannot possibly understand what they are consenting to and what possible uses their data might be put to in the future. Projects should be wary of relying wholly on consent to legitimize any sort of practice or wide use of data.
themes: consent limitations; bounded rationality; privacy self-management
relevance_rationale: Consent limitations even more acute when LLMs can repurpose data in ways individuals cannot foresee
notes: Citing Daniel Solove on privacy self-management

author: The Engine Room
extract: Examine the raw data before doing anything to manipulate it. This includes making a complete backup of the original material, kept separate from any other activities. This can be vital for ensuring accuracy, for availability in case of accidental deletion, and for responding to accusations of manipulation, for example in a court situation.
themes: raw data backup; data integrity; chain of custody; verification
relevance_rationale: Backing up raw data before manipulation is fundamental data integrity practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: Go back and ask: reach out to a small sample of the surveyed population for more in-depth analysis. If the initial group was part of a small qualitative research project, you might scale up through a more quantitative questionnaire to support your first research.
themes: re-validation; sampling; mixed methods; follow-up
relevance_rationale: Re-validation through sampling and follow-up is a methodological principle independent of tools

author: The Engine Room
extract: Cross-checking: if there are other data available about the same subject, look at how they compare. With video footage, this can include examining extra metadata (time, location, camera type), matching location features with satellite photos or maps, and checking whether activity is validated by other information sources like reports, social media, or newspapers.
themes: cross-checking; triangulation; video verification; metadata
relevance_rationale: Cross-checking and triangulation are core verification principles that endure regardless of AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Messy data may hide harmful information. If we don't make sure that we can clearly name, describe and recognise all the information contained in data sets, we might make improper assumptions about the risks that data might pose. Gaps and assumptions come to the fore much more clearly after creating some order.
themes: data cleaning rationale; hidden risks; data transparency
relevance_rationale: Recognizing messy data may hide harmful information is an ethical imperative unchanged by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Cleaning data can mean: finding and removing unwanted bits of data in spreadsheets, formatting data correctly for the tools you are using, dealing with inconsistencies in the data, and structuring it so it can be used effectively.
themes: data cleaning; formatting; consistency; structure
relevance_rationale: Core cleaning tasks endure but LLMs can now automate much of the formatting and consistency work
notes: Sourced from School of Data

author: The Engine Room
extract: Filtering is usually the first step in checking data for consistency. It creates a menu-like list of data aggregates present in a column. Tools like Microsoft Excel, Libre Office Calc, Apple Numbers or Google Sheets all offer strong filtering functionalities. A first filtering round will already offer an overview of how comparable the data set is and where the main need for cleanup lies.
themes: filtering; spreadsheets; data exploration; consistency
relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet filtering still useful but LLMs offer conversational data exploration as alternative

author: The Engine Room
extract: For more advanced data cleanup, Open Refine offers powerful ways to combine, compare and reconcile your data. Open Refine lets you combine and aggregate slightly differing types of data using algorithms that assist with fuzzy comparisons of not-quite-equal information. It also lets you execute faceted search on your data.
themes: OpenRefine; fuzzy matching; reconciliation; data cleanup
relevance_rationale: OpenRefine fuzzy matching endures but LLMs now offer powerful entity resolution and reconciliation

author: The Engine Room
extract: A complementary aspect of data cleanup is making sure the information that has been collected is fully and exhaustively described. Describing information means documenting and collecting metadata (data about data). When preparing the data set for analysis, look into all the metadata you might have and make sure they are documented.
themes: metadata; documentation; data description; preparation
relevance_rationale: Documenting and collecting metadata is essential data stewardship regardless of AI tools

author: The Engine Room
extract: It is smart to adopt common standards and file formats for the data, so that they can be more easily shareable, more resilient (future-proof) and also comparable with other data sets—i.e. interoperable. One example is the CSV format. Having data live in a proprietary format like .xls might mean that one day the company that owns it stops supporting it, the data set becomes unusable, and the knowledge is lost.
themes: file formats; CSV; interoperability; future-proofing; open standards
relevance_rationale: Adopting open standards and interoperable formats is future-proofing wisdom independent of AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: All data, no matter how it is collected, will contain a certain amount of bias. It is your task to analyse what those biases might be, to minimise them to the extent possible, identify the ones that cannot be removed, and make sure that persistent biases are well known and explicitly flagged throughout the research.
themes: bias; transparency; limitations; research integrity
relevance_rationale: Acknowledging bias in all data and working to minimize it is a foundational epistemological principle

author: The Engine Room
extract: Bias is the tendency of results to favor a certain outcome, due to the implicit construction or logic of the collection or processing of the data, the way that the data was collected (setting, sequence of questions) and the way that the data is analysed. Being aware of sampling bias, how it impacts analysis and limits what we can say about the overall population is important.
themes: bias types; sampling bias; methodology; analysis limits
relevance_rationale: Understanding sampling bias, methodology limits, and bias typology is core analytical literacy

author: The Engine Room
extract: Take special care if you are working with comparative data: differences in collection techniques, or even differing definitions, might seriously skew your results. If you are collecting and comparing data on sexual abuse between several countries, terms like assault and rape might have very different definitions in different places.
themes: comparative data; definitional bias; cross-cultural analysis
relevance_rationale: Definitional bias in comparative data is a methodological challenge unchanged by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Consider testing for data collection bias in your results. Often results may be accidentally or deliberately manipulated by the people collecting the data. Participants may fear losing benefits if they give negative answers. The collector may have had trouble gaining access to the correct mix of participants. Check for this by re-validating a small sample.
themes: collection bias; re-validation; social desirability bias; sampling
relevance_rationale: Testing for collection bias and social desirability bias is enduring methodological practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: Every data set contains outliers—data points that are so different from all others that they really skew the results. These can be anomalies or may just be errors in data entry. Going through the data set carefully and removing these is part of the data analyst's standard toolkit. However, make sure you are only reducing data noise, not changing the data to fit your expected outcome.
themes: outliers; data noise; data entry errors; confirmation bias
relevance_rationale: Identifying outliers and guarding against confirmation bias are core analytical skills

author: The Engine Room
extract: Even if two variables might seem to be related, it doesn't mean that one caused the other. The classic example is the correlation between crime rates and ice cream consumption during summer months. The two variables are correlated, but neither causes the other—both are causally linked to temperature.
themes: correlation vs causation; statistical literacy; spurious correlation
relevance_rationale: Correlation vs causation is foundational statistical literacy that persists regardless of tools

author: The Engine Room
extract: Connect with experts in the field for opinion: experts working in the field you are doing research on might provide a valuable resource in knowing what your results actually mean, and whether there are gaps or blind spots in your research that you should address before starting the analysis.
themes: expert review; blind spots; peer validation
relevance_rationale: Seeking domain expert review to catch blind spots is essential human judgment practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: Once data is collected, it can be released at various levels: closed networks within an organisation, platform-dependent sharing between peer organisations, publishing with closed licenses, or publishing with fully open licenses. Carefully consider the various forms that sharing can take.
themes: sharing spectrum; data release; access levels; licensing
relevance_rationale: Data sharing spectrum from closed to open is a governance framework independent of AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Be aware of the blind spots that internal sharing often entails: you are probably seeking feedback from like-minded individuals who share your own biases and won't provide truly independent feedback. The wider you go, the more diverse feedback will be, but the less control you have.
themes: echo chamber; peer review; internal bias; feedback diversity
relevance_rationale: Echo chamber awareness in internal feedback is an organizational insight unchanged by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Data aggregation is a crucial point of risk assessment: sometimes standalone data deemed safe becomes harmful when combined with other data sets, or data that you thought was anonymised becomes easily discernible once combined with other data, using triangulation techniques.
themes: data aggregation; triangulation risk; re-identification; combined datasets
relevance_rationale: Data aggregation re-identification risk intensified by LLMs ability to combine and infer across datasets

author: The Engine Room
extract: Sharing your data with unusual suspects will provide you with opinions outside the echo chamber you are used to. You could share the data back with the people reflected in the data and see what they think of what it reveals.
themes: sanity check; participant feedback; external review
relevance_rationale: Seeking feedback from unusual suspects outside echo chambers is enduring organizational practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: Once the data set is shared publicly, the proverbial cat is out of the bag. Any personally identifiable information that hasn't been properly addressed will be impossible to mitigate because someone might have already made a copy. A final risk assessment of the data is more than warranted before publication.
themes: pre-publication review; risk assessment; irreversibility; PII
relevance_rationale: Irreversibility of public data release and PII risks are permanent concerns regardless of AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: There might be no such thing as perfect anonymisation. De-identified data sets can be combined with other supposedly anonymous data to re-identify individuals. This chapter uses the term de-identification rather than anonymisation because it doesn't have the magic bullet feeling and because the word itself implies that it can be undone.
themes: anonymisation limits; de-identification; terminology; mosaic effect
relevance_rationale: Anonymisation limits more acute now that LLMs can re-identify from minimal data points via inference

author: The Engine Room
extract: Anonymisation techniques: Data masking (supplying only part of a data set or deleting columns), Pseudonymisation (exchanging values for codes), Aggregation (rounding large numbers or providing only small samples), Derived data (providing the age of a person instead of the exact date of birth).
themes: anonymisation techniques; data masking; pseudonymisation; aggregation; derived data
relevance_rationale: Masking, pseudonymisation, aggregation, and derived data are enduring anonymisation techniques

author: The Engine Room
extract: Create an expert group of advisors who have experience with your project and with releasing data safely. Ideally some will be outsiders without biased connections to the project. Also seek advice from people with skills in de-anonymising, such as data scientists, open data experts and ethical hackers.
themes: pre-release review; adversarial testing; expert advisory; red team
relevance_rationale: Expert advisory and adversarial red-teaming before data release is enduring governance practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: No data is neutral, and presenting information can reveal even more biases. Use data as accurately as possible, and take care not to misrepresent topics or skew the data in order to suit your particular advocacy or campaigning need—it will make for an unreliable and easily discreditable campaign.
themes: data neutrality; misrepresentation; accuracy; advocacy ethics
relevance_rationale: Data is never neutral; accurate presentation without advocacy skew is core editorial ethics

author: The Engine Room
extract: When presenting data, ensure that marginalised groups are appropriately treated and they are not being omitted, trivialised, judged or romanticised. Be intentional in how they are represented and what this means for your participants and beyond.
themes: representation; marginalised groups; inclusion; editorial ethics
relevance_rationale: Responsible representation of marginalised groups in data presentation is enduring ethical principle

author: The Engine Room
extract: Data can never represent all people and all nuances of a cultural or social mosaic. It is vital to remember that data is inherently exclusive and rarely tells the whole story. When collecting or finding data, ask yourself: who does the data leave out? Who is not reflected and what impact might that have?
themes: inclusion; data gaps; representation; exclusion
relevance_rationale: Data being inherently exclusive and never telling the whole story is fundamental epistemology

author: The Engine Room
extract: Transparency calls for disclosure: keeping an open and easily accessible account of methodology, intent of data use, who will use it and for how long, as well as how the data, the analyses and the learnings will be shared. Privacy should be granted to those without power; transparency should be demanded of those with power.
themes: transparency; methodology disclosure; power asymmetry; privacy
relevance_rationale: Transparency in methodology disclosure and power asymmetry awareness are enduring principles

author: The Engine Room
extract: If you intend on using your data in legal cases, data management systems must: allow for an auditable chain of custody, ensure the integrity and authenticity of the data (e.g. through hashing and forensic examination), and ensure that data content itself is verifiable.
chapter: What Types of Laws and Procedures Apply to Your Data Project?
themes: chain of custody; legal evidence; hashing; forensic examination; authenticity
relevance_rationale: Chain of custody, hashing, and forensic integrity for legal evidence are enduring requirements

author: The Engine Room
extract: Legal systems often include a purpose limitation principle, in which data collected for one purpose cannot be used for any other purpose without the consent of the data subject. Data sharing can challenge this foundational principle, especially if the common principle of data minimisation was also ignored.
themes: purpose limitation; data minimisation; legal principles; repurposing
relevance_rationale: Purpose limitation and data minimisation are foundational legal principles unchanged by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Published data or online data is not necessarily open data. Data published as an Excel table within a PDF document, without an open license, is not open data because it can't be easily managed or re-used. Whether or not you want to open your data is an important consideration.
themes: open data; machine readability; licensing; access
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing open data from merely published data is an enduring definitional insight

author: The Engine Room
extract: If you don't license your data properly, others won't know if or how they can use it. Licensing makes it clear how you want your data to be used. Retaining attribution makes it easier to see how your data has been used by other parties and to maintain some level of accountability to the data owners.
themes: licensing; attribution; data tracking; accountability
relevance_rationale: Proper licensing and attribution for data accountability are enduring governance practices

author: The Engine Room
extract: Deciding what to do with the data after it has served its purpose is an integral part of the project design process and should be informed by the risk assessment. Map where the data is, ensure responsible data procedures are upheld after closing, and dispose of or archive appropriately.
themes: data lifecycle; project closure; archiving; disposal
relevance_rationale: Planning data disposal at project design stage is enduring lifecycle management practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: A good principle from the information security community is to only have the data you need for the time when you need it. If it is important to keep the data forever, you must plan for resources to continually update and support safe data management practices for the data.
themes: data retention; data minimisation; lifecycle management
relevance_rationale: Only keeping data you need for the time you need it is enduring data minimisation principle

author: The Engine Room
extract: Try to store data to be archived in popular, interoperable formats which are likely to be used in future. Over the course of a few years formats may go out of use and software systems become unable to use the data.
themes: archiving; file formats; interoperability; future-proofing
relevance_rationale: Storing archives in popular interoperable formats for future-proofing is enduring practice

author: The Engine Room
extract: Collecting data about someone creates an inherent power imbalance to the extent that the data collector effectively owns a commodity relating intimately to an individual. That data could have negative consequences for the data subject, such as whether or not they receive vital services.
chapter: The World of Data: Opportunities and Risks
themes: power imbalance; data ownership; data as commodity; harm
relevance_rationale: Power imbalance inherent in data collection is a foundational ethical insight unchanged by AI

author: The Engine Room
extract: Adopting a critical approach to avert a data for data's sake methodology will go a long way towards ensuring fairness and balance. Future-proofing is also an issue: what seems unproblematic data right now may turn out to be very sensitive in the future. Minimizing the data collected can in itself be a responsible measure.
chapter: The World of Data: Opportunities and Risks
themes: data for data's sake; future-proofing; data minimisation; critical approach
relevance_rationale: Critical approach against data-for-datas-sake and future-proofing concerns are enduring principles

author: The Engine Room
