extract: Removing all personally identifiable information from a data set isn't necessarily enough to protect identities. With increasing sophistication of analytical techniques and algorithms, de-identified data sets can be combined with other supposedly anonymous data to re-identify individuals. This phenomenon, known as the Mosaic Effect, is particularly challenging because evaluating the risk requires anticipating all data sets that might be combined with yours.
themes: mosaic effect; de-identification; re-identification risk; algorithmic analysis
relevance_rationale: Re-identification via algorithmic analysis is now dramatically easier with LLMs cross-referencing capability

author: The Engine Room
extract: The first step for live data processes is to clearly define who should have access to exactly what data at which point. Create a Roles-Responsibilities-Functionality Map to identify who should do what and when.
themes: access control; roles and responsibilities; live data; workflow
relevance_rationale: Role-based access control and workflow mapping for live data are enduring organizational practices

author: The Engine Room
extract: For sharing data with a limited set of actors, consider whether data sharing should be governed by explicit agreements such as MoUs or contracts. Entering into agreements about the conditions, limitations and ethical guidelines can impose some measure of control and establish shared expectations.
themes: data sharing agreements; MoU; contracts; governance
relevance_rationale: Data sharing agreements and governance through MoUs/contracts are enduring organizational practices

author: The Engine Room
extract: Managing data responsibly is not simply the task of one person: it's an overarching theme to be considered throughout (and after\!) the lifecycle of a project. Ensuring appropriate and responsible processes will require the input and engagement of researchers, communications focal points, technical staff, enumerators and local consultants.
themes: team responsibility; data lifecycle; cross-functional; project management
relevance_rationale: Cross-functional team responsibility for data lifecycle is enduring organizational principle

author: The Engine Room
extract: How you store your data should be measured against the particular risks you or your partners may be facing, and your personal priorities: are you more concerned about data loss, or surveillance? No method of storing data is 100% safe, so it is essential that you backup your data, no matter how or where it is stored.
themes: storage decisions; risk priorities; backup; data loss vs surveillance
relevance_rationale: Risk-based storage decisions balancing data loss vs surveillance are enduring security considerations

author: The Engine Room
extract: Deleting digital information is more than just clicking delete. As a general rule, everything that is on a hard drive needs to be overwritten several times. There are free tools to help you do this.
themes: data disposal; secure deletion; overwriting
relevance_rationale: Secure deletion principles endure but specific overwriting techniques need updating for cloud and AI-era storage

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: We can start to assess algorithmic power by thinking about the atomic decisions that algorithms make, including prioritization, classification, association, and filtering. Sometimes these decisions are chained in order to form higher-level decisions and information transformations.
themes: algorithmic accountability; analytical framework; taxonomy
relevance_rationale: Taxonomy of algorithmic decisions (prioritization, classification, association, filtering) is even more relevant with LLMs
notes: Core taxonomy for classifying algorithmic decisions — foundational for any investigation

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Embedded in every algorithm that seeks to prioritize are criteria, or metrics, which are computed and used to define the ranking through a sorting procedure. These criteria essentially embed a set of choices and value-propositions that determine what gets pushed to the top of the ranking.
themes: algorithmic accountability; ranking; bias; values
relevance_rationale: Ranking criteria as embedded value-propositions is a permanent analytical lens for any algorithm including LLMs
notes: Criteria as embedded value-propositions — key analytical lens

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Classification algorithms can be tuned to make fewer of either of those mistakes. However, as false positives are tuned down, false negatives will often increase, and vice versa. Tuned all the way toward false positives, the algorithm will mark a lot of fair use videos as infringing; tuned the other way it will miss a lot of infringing videos altogether. You get the sense that tuning one way or the other can privilege different stakeholders in a decision, implying an essential value judgment by the designer of such an algorithm.
themes: algorithmic accountability; classification; false positives; false negatives; bias; stakeholders
relevance_rationale: False positive/negative tradeoff as stakeholder value judgment applies equally to LLM classification decisions
notes: False-positive/negative tradeoff as stakeholder value judgment

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: To understand the power of classification algorithms we need to ask: Are there errors that may be acceptable to the algorithm creator, but do a disservice to the public? And if so, why was the algorithm tuned that way?
themes: algorithmic accountability; classification; public interest; investigative questions
relevance_rationale: Asking whether errors serve the creator but disserve the public is a permanent investigative question for any algorithm
notes: Diagnostic question for investigating classification algorithms

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: In general, there are two kinds of mistakes a classification algorithm can make—often referred to as false positives and false negatives. Suppose Google is trying to classify a video into one of two categories: 'infringing' or 'fair use.' A false positive is a video classified as 'infringing' when it is actually 'fair use.' A false negative, on the other hand, is a video classified as 'fair use' when it is in fact 'infringing.'
themes: algorithmic accountability; classification; error analysis; false positives; false negatives
relevance_rationale: Concrete false positive/negative framework applied to content moderation is directly applicable to LLM content filtering
notes: Concrete example of false-positive/negative framework applied to content moderation

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: What is the basis for a prioritization decision? Is it fair and just, or discriminatory? What are the criteria built into a ranking, classification, or association, and are they politicized or biased in some consequential way? What are the limits to measuring and operationalizing the criteria used by the algorithm?
themes: algorithmic accountability; investigative questions; bias; fairness
relevance_rationale: Master diagnostic questions about bias, fairness, and criteria operationalization apply to all algorithms including LLMs
notes: Master diagnostic questions for beginning any algorithm investigation

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: What are the limits of an algorithm and when is it known to break down or fail? For instance: What types of errors are made in classification? How has the algorithm been tuned to privilege false positive or false negative errors? Does that tuning benefit one set of stakeholders over another? What are thresholds used in classification decisions? What kind of uncertainty is there in the classifier?
themes: algorithmic accountability; investigative questions; error analysis; uncertainty
relevance_rationale: Diagnostic questions about failure modes, thresholds, and uncertainty apply with even greater force to LLMs
notes: Diagnostic questions focused on algorithm failure modes and uncertainty

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: What are the potential biases of the training data used in a classifying algorithm? How has the algorithm evolved with that data? What types of parameters or data were used to initiate the algorithm?
themes: algorithmic accountability; training data; bias; machine learning
relevance_rationale: Questions about training data bias and algorithm evolution are even more critical for LLMs trained on massive datasets
notes: Diagnostic questions about training data bias

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Any investigation must therefore consider algorithms as objects of human creation and take into account intent, including that of any group or institutional processes that may have influenced their design.
themes: algorithmic accountability; design intent; institutional analysis
relevance_rationale: Treating algorithms as human artifacts shaped by intent and institutional processes is permanently essential
notes: Algorithms as human artifacts — intent and institutional process always relevant

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: In the case of government, the federal Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) facilitates the public's right to relevant government data and documents. While in theory FOIA also applies to source code for algorithms, investigators may run into the trade secret issue here as well. Exemption 4 to FOIA covers trade secrets and allows the federal government to deny requests for transparency concerning any third-party software integrated into its systems.
themes: FOIA; trade secrets; algorithmic accountability; government data
relevance_rationale: FOIA for government algorithm source code and trade secret exemption obstacles remain active legal concerns
notes: FOIA Exemption 4 as obstacle to algorithm source code requests

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Oftentimes corporations limit how transparent they are, since exposing too many details of their proprietary systems (trade secrets) may undermine their competitive advantage, hurt their reputation and ability to do business, or leave the system open to gaming and manipulation. Trade secrets are a core impediment to understanding automated authority like algorithms since they, by definition, seek to hide information for competitive advantage.
themes: trade secrets; transparency; algorithmic accountability; corporate accountability
relevance_rationale: Trade secrets as core impediment to algorithmic transparency is even more relevant with proprietary LLMs
notes: Three reasons corporations resist transparency: competitive advantage, reputation, gaming

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Even when laws exist compelling government transparency, the lack of enforcement is an issue. Watchdogging from third parties is as important as ever.
themes: transparency; enforcement; watchdog journalism
relevance_rationale: Third-party watchdog journalism as essential complement to transparency laws is permanently needed
notes: Transparency laws without enforcement are insufficient — third-party watchdogging essential

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Reverse engineering is the process of articulating the specifications of a system through a rigorous examination drawing on domain knowledge, observation, and deduction to unearth a model of how that system works.
themes: reverse engineering; methodology; algorithmic accountability
relevance_rationale: Reverse engineering as rigorous deduction-based methodology for understanding systems is permanently valid
notes: Core definition of reverse engineering as investigative method

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: I would advocate that journalists engage in algorithmic accountability not just through reverse engineering but also by using reporting techniques, such as interviews or document reviews, and digging deep into the motives and design intentions behind algorithms.
themes: reverse engineering; traditional reporting; interviews; document review; design intent
relevance_rationale: Complementing reverse engineering with traditional reporting (interviews, documents) for design intent is permanently essential
notes: Reverse engineering must be complemented by traditional reporting — interviews and documents

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Algorithms are often described as black boxes, their complexity and technical opacity hiding and obfuscating their inner workings. At the same time, algorithms must always have an input and output; the black box actually has two little openings. We can take advantage of those inputs and outputs to reverse engineer what's going on inside. If you vary the inputs in enough ways and pay close attention to the outputs, you can start piecing together a theory, or at least a story, of how the algorithm works.
themes: reverse engineering; black box; input-output analysis; algorithmic accountability
relevance_rationale: Black box input-output analysis method is the primary approach for investigating proprietary LLMs
notes: Core method: vary inputs systematically, observe outputs, infer behavior

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: The first scenario corresponds to an ability to fully observe all of an algorithm's inputs and outputs. This is the case for algorithms accessible via an online API, which facilitates sending different inputs to the algorithm and directly recording the output.
themes: reverse engineering; API; data collection; input-output analysis
relevance_rationale: APIs as ideal access point for full observability reverse engineering is directly applicable to LLM API auditing
notes: Full observability scenario — APIs as ideal access point

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: The most common case that data journalists encounter: A large dataset is available but there is limited (or no) information about how that data was transformed algorithmically. Interviews and document investigation are especially important here in order to understand what was fed into the algorithm, in terms of data, parameters, and ways in which the algorithm is used.
themes: reverse engineering; partial observability; interviews; document analysis
relevance_rationale: Output-only scenario requiring reporting to understand inputs is the most common case for investigating proprietary LLMs
notes: Output-only scenario — most common; requires reporting to understand inputs

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: In such cases, partial observability (e.g., of outputs) through FOIA, Web-scraping, or something like crowdsourcing can still lead to some interesting results.
themes: FOIA; web scraping; crowdsourcing; partial observability; data collection
relevance_rationale: FOIA, web scraping, and crowdsourcing for partial observability remain key data-gathering tactics
notes: Three data-gathering tactics for partial observability: FOIA, scraping, crowdsourcing

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Some transparency by the services through their FAQ's and blogs suggest a hypothesis and tip as to what types of input the algorithm might be sensitive to. Moreover, the algorithms themselves, both their inputs and outputs, are observable and accessible through APIs, which make it relatively easy to quickly collect a wide range of observations about the input-output relationship.
themes: hypothesis generation; API; transparency; tip generation
relevance_rationale: Using company disclosures as hypothesis sources and APIs as data collection mechanisms is directly applicable to LLM investigation
notes: Company disclosures as hypothesis source; APIs as data collection mechanism

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Sometimes algorithms expose inputs and make it possible to record outputs, but those outputs are then further transformed and edited before they get to the user interface. What really matters in the end is not just the output of the algorithm, but how that output is made available to the user.
themes: user experience; API vs UI; verification; algorithmic accountability
relevance_rationale: Caveat that API output may differ from user-facing UI output is critical for LLM investigations where model behavior varies by interface
notes: Critical caveat: API output may differ from actual user-facing output

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: The lesson here for reverse engineering is that we need to be careful when using correlations to make claims about what inputs an algorithm is actually using. When we don't have access to the algorithm's inputs we can only make statistically informed guesses. Correlation does not imply causation, nor intent on the part of the designer. As much as algorithmic accountability can help us diagnose the existence of a problem, we have to go deeper and do reporting (when possible) to understand the motivations or intentions behind an algorithm.
themes: correlation vs causation; verification; reverse engineering; statistical inference; intent
relevance_rationale: Correlation does not imply causation or intent -- correlation-based claims need traditional reporting, even more so with LLMs
notes: ProPublica Message Machine lesson: correlation-based claims need traditional reporting backup

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: To get the story the WSJ had to simulate visiting the various sites from different computers and browsers in different geographies. This initially required using various proxy servers that made it appear like the website was being loaded from different geographies. The publication's staff also created different archetype users and built user profiles using cookies to see how those user profiles might impact the prices recorded.
themes: simulation; proxy servers; user profiles; web scraping; price discrimination
relevance_rationale: Simulating diverse users via proxies and constructed profiles for price discrimination investigation is a permanent technique
notes: WSJ technique: simulate diverse users via proxy servers and constructed user profiles

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: One of the issues is that sites like Staples might be using A/B testing to analyze whether or not subtle differences on their websites are useful to them. In other words, they're already running experiments on their sites, and to a reverse engineer it might look like noise, or just confusing irregularities. Algorithms may be unstable and change over time, or have randomness built in to them, which makes understanding patterns in their input-output relationship much more challenging.
themes: A/B testing; noise; temporal instability; reverse engineering; confounders
relevance_rationale: A/B testing creating confounding noise in reverse engineering is a permanent methodological challenge
notes: Major confounder: corporate A/B tests create noise that looks like algorithmic variation

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: If you suspect the algorithm may be extremely dynamic and time-sensitive you may need to initiate all of your inputs to the algorithm in parallel in order to minimize the impact of a changing and dynamic algorithm.
themes: parallel collection; temporal sensitivity; reverse engineering; methodology
relevance_rationale: Parallel data collection to control for algorithmic instability is a permanent practical methodology tip
notes: Practical tip: parallel data collection to control for algorithmic instability

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: In looking for algorithms that we want to hold accountable, we might ask several questions: What are the consequences and impact of that algorithm for the public, how significant are those consequences, and how many people might be affected by or perceive an effect by the algorithm? We might ask whether the algorithm has the potential for discrimination, or whether errors made by the algorithm may create risks that negatively impact the public.
themes: story selection; algorithmic accountability; public impact; discrimination; risk
relevance_rationale: Selection criteria for choosing which algorithms to investigate (impact, discrimination, risk) apply even more to LLMs
notes: Selection criteria for choosing which algorithms to investigate

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Essentially what we're looking to identify is an algorithm that's made a bad decision, that somehow breaks an expectation for how we think it ought to be operating. Is the algorithm's output consistent with what we think it should be? And if not, what's driving that inconsistency—a bug, an incidental programming decision, or a deep seated design intent? Observations, tips, and digging through data are all ways that we can identify interesting and significant algorithmic decisions that might warrant accountability reporting.
themes: story identification; expectation violation; algorithmic accountability; tips
relevance_rationale: Story trigger of algorithm output violating expectations then diagnosing cause is a permanently valid investigative approach
notes: Core story trigger: algorithm output violates expectations — then diagnose cause

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: When a classification decision has negative consequences, then looking for false positives, like Content ID's identifying fair-use content as infringing, can be a tip indicating a deeper story.
themes: false positives; tip generation; story identification; classification
relevance_rationale: False positives with negative consequences as story leads is directly applicable to LLM content moderation
notes: False positives with negative consequences as story leads

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: After choosing an algorithm on which to focus, the challenge then becomes how to sample the input-output relationship of the algorithm in some meaningful way. Figuring out how to observe or simulate those inputs is a key part of a practical investigation involving reverse engineering. Reporting techniques and talking to sources are two ways to try to understand what inputs are being fed into an algorithm, but when trade secrets obscure the process we're often reduced to guessing.
themes: sampling; input simulation; reverse engineering; sources; trade secrets
relevance_rationale: Sampling the input-output relationship and simulating inputs is the core method for auditing any algorithmic system
notes: Sampling challenge: observe or simulate inputs; sources help when trade secrets block

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Given a potentially infinite sampling space, we must define what is interesting and important for us to feed into an algorithm. For my story on search-engine autocompletions I wanted to know which sex-related words were blocked by Google and Bing, as well as whether adding 'child' to the query led to any difference in output. The sampling strategy followed from these questions.
themes: sampling strategy; research questions; reverse engineering; hypothesis-driven
relevance_rationale: Sampling strategy derived from specific research questions rather than open-ended exploration is permanently valid
notes: Sampling strategy must follow from specific research questions — not open-ended

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: You also have to make sure that the sample simulates the reality of importance to your audience. This was a key difficulty for Michael Keller's project on iPhone autocorrections, which eventually demanded he simulate the iPhone with scripts that mimic how a human uses the phone.
themes: ecological validity; simulation; user experience; sampling
relevance_rationale: Ecological validity of samples matching real user experience is essential for any algorithm audit including LLMs
notes: Sample must match real user experience — API results may not reflect UI behavior

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Data returned from the API that never appears in the UI is less significant since users will never encounter it in their daily usage—so I didn't report on it even though I had it collected.
themes: editorial judgment; user experience; API vs UI; relevance filtering
relevance_rationale: Excluding API-only data that never reaches users is a permanent editorial principle for algorithm reporting
notes: Exclude API-only data that never reaches users — practical editorial principle

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: I would argue that an experimental methodology is more powerful as it allows you to directly sample the input-output relationship in ways that let you assess particular questions you may have about how the algorithm is functioning. Indeed, there is a strong connection between the reverse engineering I'm espousing here and the scientific method.
themes: experimental method; scientific method; reverse engineering; hypothesis testing
relevance_rationale: Experimental methodology preferred over observational for reverse engineering is a permanent scientific principle
notes: Experimental approach preferred over observational when inputs are controllable

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Once you've got the input-output relationship of your black box mapped out, the next step is to search and filter for newsworthy insights. These expectations could be statistically based, built on an understanding of social and legal norms, or defined by comparing similar vendors of the technology like Google and Bing autocompletions, or iPhone and Android autocorrections.
themes: newsworthiness; statistical analysis; norms; comparative analysis
relevance_rationale: Three bases for newsworthiness filters (statistical, social/legal norms, vendor comparison) are permanently applicable
notes: Three bases for newsworthiness filters: statistical, social/legal norms, vendor comparison

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: At the WSJ the first filter used for narrowing-in on e-commerce sites was a statistical one: the variance of prices returned from a site for a given item across a variety of geographies. If any non-random variance was observed, the site was marked for a more rigorous and in-depth analysis.
themes: statistical filtering; variance analysis; price discrimination; triage
relevance_rationale: Variance as triage filter to identify targets for deeper investigation is a permanent analytical technique
notes: WSJ technique: variance as triage filter to identify sites for deeper investigation

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Rob Barry, who worked on the executive trading plans story for the WSJ, described a sophisticated data-mining technique involving clustering and Monte Carlo simulation to find newsworthy cases by trying to identify trading plans that fell outside of the norms of expectation.
themes: clustering; Monte Carlo simulation; outlier detection; data mining
relevance_rationale: Clustering and Monte Carlo simulation for outlier detection endure but LLMs can now assist with or automate such statistical analyses
notes: Clustering + Monte Carlo simulation for outlier detection in trading data

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Knowing where the algorithm violates the designers' expectations (e.g., it lets through child pornography when the stated intent is not to do so), or where it may have unintended side effects can both make for interesting stories.
themes: expectation violation; unintended consequences; story identification; design intent
relevance_rationale: Violations of designer expectations and unintended side effects as story archetypes are permanently applicable
notes: Two story archetypes: violating designer expectations, or revealing unintended side effects

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Since violence becomes a more interesting story if it's being suggested toward other people or living things I filtered my results against man-, woman-, person-, and animal-related word lists, essentially creating a newsworthiness filter. This sped up my ability to go through the results. Rather than reading through 14,000 results, I was reviewing fewer than 1,000.
themes: newsworthiness filter; data reduction; keyword filtering; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Newsworthiness word-list filter to reduce results is partially displaced by LLMs that can semantically filter large result sets
notes: Newsworthiness word-list filter to reduce 14K results to under 1K reviewable items

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Even with newsworthiness filters helping to identify possible stories, it's absolutely essential to have reporters in the loop digging deeper. For every site that was flagged as a statistical hit, Singer-Vine's team did a much more comprehensive analysis, writing custom code to analyze each.
themes: human-in-the-loop; verification; custom code; statistical hits
relevance_rationale: Human reporters essential for deeper verification beyond statistical flags is a permanent human-in-the-loop principle
notes: Statistical flags are starting points not endpoints — reporter verification essential

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Knowing what makes something a story is perhaps less about a filter for statistical, social, or legal deviance than it is about understanding the context of the phenomenon, including historical, cultural, and social expectations related to the issue—all things with which traditional reporting and investigation can help.
themes: context; traditional reporting; editorial judgment; newsworthiness
relevance_rationale: Contextual understanding from traditional reporting required beyond statistical deviance is permanently true
notes: Statistical deviance alone insufficient — contextual understanding from traditional reporting required

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Scott Klein, the news applications editor at ProPublica, considered using some computational means to detect gerrymandering, but quickly decided that, 'it [gerrymandering] is a motive, not a shape,' which ultimately made traditional reporting techniques much more effective for investigating the story.
themes: computational limits; traditional reporting; motive vs pattern; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Motive cannot be detected computationally, requiring traditional reporting -- a permanent limit of algorithmic analysis
notes: Key insight: some stories require understanding motive, which computation alone cannot reveal

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Developing the human resource to do algorithmic-accountability reporting will take dedicated efforts to teach the computational thinking, programming, and technical skills needed to make sense of algorithmic decisions. Teaming computationally literate reporters with tech-savvy computer scientists might be one method for doing more algorithmic accountability reporting.
themes: skills; computational journalism; interdisciplinary teams; training
relevance_rationale: Computational skills for algorithmic accountability are still needed but LLMs lower the programming barrier for journalists
notes: Two approaches: train journalists in computation, or pair reporters with computer scientists

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Legally speaking, the reverse engineering of commercial software does have some pitfalls. Other than the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA), there are no laws that directly prohibit or restrict reverse engineering, and even the DMCA has exemptions. Software vendors do typically add anti-reverse engineering clauses to End User License Agreements (EULAs), forcing the decision: Is it okay to breach such a contract if it gets you closer to the truth about the algorithm?
themes: legal risk; DMCA; EULA; reverse engineering; ethics
relevance_rationale: Legal risks of reverse engineering including DMCA and EULA considerations remain active legal constraints
notes: Legal landscape: DMCA has exemptions but EULAs pose contractual risk

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: We need to ask ourselves about the possible ramifications or negative consequences of publishing details of how certain algorithms work. By publishing details of how an algorithm functions, specifically information about what inputs it pays attention to, how it uses various criteria in a ranking, or what criteria it uses to censor, how might that allow the algorithm to be manipulated or circumvented? The ethical principal of beneficence offers guidance here: Try to maximize anticipated benefits while minimizing possible risks of harm to the public.
themes: ethics; beneficence; gaming risk; publication decisions; harm minimization
relevance_rationale: Weighing publication risks of exposing algorithm mechanics against beneficence is even more relevant with LLMs
notes: Ethical framework for publication: beneficence — balance public benefit vs gaming risk

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: In the same way that business journalists contextualize and help the public understand the information produced through financial transparency of companies, journalists will also be needed to frame, contextualize, and explain the transparency information about algorithms.
themes: contextualization; explanation; algorithmic literacy; journalism role
relevance_rationale: Journalists contextualizing algorithmic transparency information for the public is a permanently essential role
notes: Journalists as interpreters of algorithmic transparency — analogous to financial journalism

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: One of the main challenges to building newsroom algorithms is providing a window for the reporter into how a particular algorithmic decision was made. It remains to be seen how news organizations will incorporate the evidence that algorithms or simulations provide with an epistemology and ethic that demands full transparency.
themes: newsroom algorithms; transparency; epistemology; newsroom practice
relevance_rationale: Newsroom algorithms now include LLM-based tools; transparency and epistemology challenges have expanded significantly
notes: News orgs face same algorithmic transparency tensions they investigate in others

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: Next time you hear about software or an algorithm being used to help make a decision, you might get critical and start asking questions about how that software could be affecting outcomes. Try to FOIA it, try to understand whether you can reverse engineer it, and when you're finished, write up your method for how you got there. By method-sharing we'll expand our ability to replicate these types of stories.
themes: method sharing; FOIA; reverse engineering; replicability; workflow
relevance_rationale: Getting critical about algorithmic decisions, using FOIA, reverse engineering, and method sharing are permanent practices
notes: Three-step workflow summary: FOIA, reverse engineer, publish methodology for replication

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: ProPublica picked up the tip and started gathering hundreds and then thousands of these targeted emails, soliciting them from people who were willing to forward them on to the news organization. In addition to collecting the emails, ProPublica solicited the recipients to fill out a survey asking about basic demographic information, where they lived, and if they had donated or volunteered for the campaign before. These survey answers then served as the input to the algorithm they were trying to dissect.
themes: crowdsourcing; surveys; data collection; reverse engineering; proxy inputs
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing data from users and surveying demographics to reverse engineer targeting algorithms is a permanent technique
notes: ProPublica Message Machine: crowdsourced outputs + survey-based proxy inputs

author: Nicholas Diakopoulos, Tow Center / CJR
extract: This case underscores the challenge with trying to understand which inputs an algorithm pays attention to. There is a huge space of potential inputs, some of which are observable and some of which are not. Practically speaking you have to choose which inputs you want to investigate to see if they are relevant to the algorithm.
themes: input space; reverse engineering; selection bias; analytical scope
relevance_rationale: Challenge of vast unobservable input spaces when reverse engineering algorithms is a permanent methodological constraint
notes: Infinite input space requires pragmatic scoping — choose which inputs to test

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Critical journalistic scrutiny is essential for all sources (and statistics) concerning AI-related news – no matter whether sources are academics, regulators, big corporates or entrepreneurs. The same point applies to AI when reported as part of the operations of computer gaming companies, e-security services, vendors of Non-Fungible Tokens, crypto-currency exchanges, health care, agriculture, warfare, etc. There is a need to interrogate and deconstruct AI references, whatever the field of coverage.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: source criticism; verification; AI hype
relevance_rationale: Critical scrutiny of all AI-related sources is even more essential now that LLMs have accelerated AI hype across every sector

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Arguably, the most interesting thing about AI is often not the technicalities around the software, nor the hardware side. It is the inter-linked human dimension - including the vested interests always involved in defining and promoting AI, in developing software and control and access of hardware. And of course, the availability, ownership and conditionalities around data.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: story framing; human dimension; power dynamics; data ownership
relevance_rationale: Focusing on human dimensions, vested interests, and power behind AI remains the most important angle for journalism in the LLM era

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Each micro-story – eg. like a medical breakthrough using AI – will be part of a bigger systemic human story that provides many rich angles to reporting the specifics. To grasp the extent, requires that we recognize how the ensemble of technologies broadly known as AI are inextricably embedded in society and its power dynamics and challenges.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: systems thinking; story framing; contextualization
relevance_rationale: Embedding AI micro-stories in systemic human narratives about power dynamics is a framing principle that endures

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: This means knowing how to ask the questions about the existence (or not) and quality (or not) of ongoing human rights due diligence by AI developers, their institutions and customers and clients. Journalistic interviews should never neglect to enquire about pre- and post- impact assessments and resulting mitigation steps.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: interview technique; accountability; human rights; impact assessment
relevance_rationale: Asking about human rights due diligence, impact assessments, and mitigation is more urgent than ever with rapid LLM deployment

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: What ethical protocols have been used concerning data creation, storage, sales and usage, and what testing of deployments have been done? What harm incident reporting and tracking is in place (or not), and what can be learnt for improvements? Does a system stack up against the global standard that 'both technical and institutional designs should ensure auditability and traceability'?
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: data ethics; auditability; traceability; accountability questions
relevance_rationale: Questions about ethical protocols, data usage, harm tracking, and auditability apply directly to LLM systems
notes: Checklist of questions for AI-linked stories

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Without informed and independent journalism on AI, society at large will have huge deficits in the transparency and accountability which are absolutely essential for the governance of such an impactful technology. On the other hand, it is quality reporting that can delve into what packages of state regulation and self-regulation are optimum, and what potential exists for alternatives like co-regulation and multi-stakeholder regulation.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: transparency; accountability; regulation; public interest
relevance_rationale: The democratic necessity of informed independent journalism on AI for transparency and accountability is heightened by LLM proliferation

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Very seldom do journalists report on the technology itself, as a pure isolated entity, and very seldom do journalists thus need to know about the technical operations of neural networks or deep learning. Instead, more often journalists report on democratic processes, people's behaviour or, for example, structural injustices – AI integrated in all these, and all these influenced by AI technologies.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: story framing; contextualization; AI as societal topic
relevance_rationale: The claim that journalists rarely need to understand neural networks is less true now that LLMs are directly embedded in journalism workflows

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: AI constitutes an abstract object with global implications for journalistic inquiry that is more inclined to report on concrete occurrences that can be clearly limited and identified. It is ubiquitous, yet it may be difficult to identify; it renders diverse implications that may be hard to foresee. It requires expert knowledge to be profoundly understood, and developing full comprehension often demands triangulation, that is, integrating and compromising different perspectives.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: triangulation; expert sources; complexity; abstraction
relevance_rationale: AI as an abstract ubiquitous object requiring expert triangulation to understand remains an accurate characterization in the LLM era

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: What kind of relationship should journalists, as public gatekeepers and opinion makers, establish with AI? When participating in the journalistic coverage of AI-related topics, what kinds of things should be taken into consideration? Which sources should be drawn on?
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: source strategy; journalistic role; AI coverage
relevance_rationale: Questions about journalists relationship with AI as gatekeepers and what to consider in coverage are even more pressing with generative AI
notes: Framing questions for the handbook

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Our understanding of AI constitutes an interdisciplinary infrastructure, where expert sources need to be identified across different fields of knowledge. Academic research that can deliver an in-depth understanding of the phenomena involves theoretical development using very specific concepts to capture such aspects. Technological development that leans upon the innovative application of these technologies is not always very easy to access.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: interdisciplinary sourcing; expert identification; source access
relevance_rationale: Identifying interdisciplinary expert sources across fields for AI reporting remains essential and challenging

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The task of journalism is to balance these knowledge fields, bridging different aspects to gain insights into what is of public concern. In order to perform such a balancing act, a journalist needs to gain knowledge about these fields and be able to filter and synthetize information that may sometimes be contradictory and complicated.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: synthesis; knowledge bridging; public concern; contradictory evidence
relevance_rationale: Balancing knowledge fields and synthesizing contradictory information about AI is a core journalism skill unchanged by LLMs

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Journalists need to employ their strategies as with any other subject, including the journalistic processes of contextualization, data verification, consequence and risk analysis, relativization, the identification of normativity and bias and the creation of critical distances to the objects of inquiry.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: verification; bias detection; contextualization; risk analysis
relevance_rationale: Contextualization, verification, risk analysis, and identifying bias are foundational journalism processes that endure regardless of tools
notes: Core journalistic processes applied to AI coverage

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: A prerequisite for this area-specific literacy is the mapping of central actors and stakeholders, as well as infrastructures, and understanding how these cultural and political economies work. For example, concerning science, they need to know what scientific disciplines are at stake, how scientific knowledge production occurs, how scientific publications can be accessed and interpreted and how science works more generally.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: stakeholder mapping; source literacy; scientific publications; political economy
relevance_rationale: Mapping stakeholders, understanding political economies, and interpreting scientific knowledge production remain essential for AI beat reporters

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: A crucial step is taken when we understand that no technologies are neutral: there are always a number of interests underlying the technological structures and the modus operandi of different platforms.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: technology non-neutrality; platform interests; critical analysis
relevance_rationale: The principle that no technology is neutral and always embeds interests is a timeless critical insight directly applicable to LLMs

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The ICIJ collaborated with the AI studio at Quartz, which built a system that could 'read' all the documents. As phrased by Quartz, this was done to 'help journalists from Quartz, ICIJ and other partner organizations find the kinds of documents they expected in the cache of leaks – regardless of file format, spelling, transcription errors, phrasing, or even the language of the document'.
themes: NLP; document processing; investigative journalism; Luanda Leaks; ICIJ
relevance_rationale: ICIJ document reading via AI is a valid precedent but LLMs now offer far more powerful document analysis capabilities than described here
notes: Luanda Leaks: 715,000 documents processed with NLP

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: In Argentina, La Nacion used computer vision to analyze election ballots to confirm the suitability of the electoral process and to avoid fraud. They used computer vision to analyze analogue voting ballots, and then validated the output through crowdsourced human reviews. The publisher had a similar project in 2015, in which they manually analyzed 16,000 ballots. By adding computer vision to their 2021 edition, they could review 100,000 ballots.
themes: computer vision; election verification; crowdsourcing; scale
relevance_rationale: Computer vision for ballot analysis is still valid but the specific 2021 approach is now superseded by multimodal LLMs with higher accuracy
notes: Scale jump: 16k manual to 100k with CV + human review

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: True algorithmic literacy requires a different kind of toolkit that combines cultural knowledge and helpful metaphors with a basic understanding of both narrative and the technical abilities a new system might present. This is critical for journalism where future stories will heavily depend on an informed reporter who can convey this information to the general public.
chapter: Cultural Myths and Narratives about Artificial Intelligence
themes: algorithmic literacy; cultural knowledge; technical understanding; reporting toolkit
relevance_rationale: Algorithmic literacy combining cultural knowledge, metaphors, and technical understanding remains essential for journalists covering AI

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Understanding every interaction with an AI system as a kind of trade or two-way conversation can be a helpful starting place: most systems, especially those that offer free services, are also collecting personal data or observing our actions in other ways that may not be immediately obvious or explicit. Developing some understanding of the real terms of our relationships with intelligent systems is an important step in building transparent and mutually beneficial collaborations.
chapter: Cultural Myths and Narratives about Artificial Intelligence
themes: user-AI relationship; data collection; privacy; transparency
relevance_rationale: Understanding AI interactions as data trades and developing awareness of hidden data collection is directly applicable to LLM usage
notes: Part of Reading the Algorithm framework

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: AI algorithms depending on machine learning utilize large datasets with implicit bias or discrimination encoded into their data samples and the statistical correlations that are drawn from them. The choice of classical versus deep learning algorithms for an AI can have downstream effects on the performance, explainability and interpretability of the system.
chapter: Cultural Myths and Narratives about Artificial Intelligence
themes: data bias; algorithm choice; explainability; infrastructure analysis
relevance_rationale: Awareness of bias encoded in training data and downstream effects of algorithm choice is foundational AI literacy that endures
notes: Part of Reading the Algorithm: Infrastructure and System Design

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: An important consideration is the data-driven nature of many modern AI techniques, meaning that they find patterns and make recommendations based on collected, often historic data. This leads to risks, such as cementing historical injustices and making biased decisions based on non-representative data.
chapter: Policy Frameworks and Recommendations for Artificial Intelligence
themes: historical data bias; representativeness; injustice; risk assessment
relevance_rationale: Risks of cementing historical injustices through pattern-based AI decisions applies directly to LLM training data biases

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: A second consideration is the fact that most AI methods optimize some objective function. They are designed to achieve the highest possible score by changing their behaviour. Since typical objective functions could involve, for example, maximizing ad revenue or time spent in an app, they could lead to both undesired and unethical behaviour. There are important constraints that are never made explicit in the simplified objective functions used by AI systems. This leads to the so-called value alignment problem.
chapter: Policy Frameworks and Recommendations for Artificial Intelligence
themes: objective functions; value alignment; optimization bias; hidden constraints
relevance_rationale: The value alignment problem and risks of simplified objective functions apply directly to LLM reward modeling and RLHF

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: A third consideration is how to maintain meaningful human control over a system. AI systems are often much faster than humans, processing vastly larger volumes of data, and a single system can be used to determine thousands or millions of cases. Taken together, these systems work at a completely different scale than us and often use different methods than we do. This leads to the challenging question of how to maintain control of such a system.
chapter: Policy Frameworks and Recommendations for Artificial Intelligence
themes: human control; scale; automated decision-making; oversight
relevance_rationale: Maintaining meaningful human control over fast, high-volume AI systems is more pressing with LLMs processing millions of queries

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: One should never assume that an algorithm is a neutral gatekeeper. It is important to consider the political economy of algorithms (e.g. the ownership structure), which often directs the results an algorithm will produce. An algorithm can also be used to manipulate human behaviour.
themes: algorithm non-neutrality; political economy; ownership; manipulation
relevance_rationale: Never assuming algorithm neutrality and considering political economy of algorithms applies squarely to LLM platforms

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Journalism has the important duty of spreading relevant information, which is not a simple task; it requires a decent understanding of rapidly advancing technology, and an algorithm needs to be studied over a certain period of time. Moreover, organisations – not only platforms but also government bodies – that own algorithms rarely provide insight into how they work. In part, this is because of trade secrets, but it is increasingly because the systems are not designed to explain their decisions (also known as black box algorithms). Algorithms are often opaque, and technical complexity is a barrier to reporting.
themes: black box algorithms; opacity; access barriers; trade secrets; longitudinal study
relevance_rationale: Barrier of algorithmic opacity endures but LLMs have shifted the challenge -- they are more interactive yet their scale and training data remain opaque

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The composition of a structured toolbox for reporting on algorithms can help. Reporters can compile this themselves or use existing toolkits. A toolbox may consist of an extensive AI glossary and a repository of articles on AI reporting or scientific publications with which a journalist can become acquainted with the terminology; a contact list of AI experts and stakeholders for reflecting on the subject of a report (differentiating between technical, social/ethical and legal experts); a list of AI tools with which one can experiment and become accustomed to the technology; and links to professional communities that share new trends and insights.
themes: reporting toolkit; expert contacts; AI glossary; professional communities
relevance_rationale: Structured toolbox for reporting on algorithms endures as concept but now must include LLM-specific tools, prompt auditing, and generative AI terminology
notes: Structured toolbox for algorithm reporting

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: A second suggestion is to cultivate a critical attitude towards data, which several studies have shown is often lacking among journalists. Accuracy of data tends to be insufficiently questioned; this is important not only for interpreting press releases, research data or company results, but also for scrutinizing algorithms in times of big data. A good level of data awareness and data literacy is therefore an important prerequisite for being able to fulfil the mission of journalism.
themes: data literacy; data accuracy; critical attitude; press releases
relevance_rationale: Critical attitude toward data and data literacy as prerequisite for journalism mission is a timeless principle reinforced by LLM-era challenges
notes: Cites Simons et al. 2017; Stalph 2017; Kouts-Klemm 2019 on journalists lacking data criticism

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Journalistic opportunities do increase when one can evaluate algorithms through the lens of a data journalist. Courses for data journalists vary in content but often include components such as an elementary understanding of arithmetic and probability, advanced statistical techniques and data analysis, allowing journalists to dig deeper into the risks of algorithms and better assess what can go wrong in an investigation.
themes: data journalism skills; statistics; probability; algorithm evaluation
relevance_rationale: Algorithmic accountability through data journalism lens endures but must now include LLM-specific evaluation methods like prompt injection testing

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Another possibility is to learn algorithmic accountability reporting, a new branch of journalism that investigates the social power exercised by algorithms. Algorithmic accountability reporting, an umbrella term for various research methods, is a mechanism for investigating, clarifying and expressing the power structures, prejudices and influences that algorithms can exert in/on society. Effective algorithmic accountability reporting requires all the traditional skills of journalism in reporting, interviewing and domain knowledge and the ability to translate the results clearly and concisely. It also requires new skills, such as (at least) minimal knowledge of data journalism (e.g. scraping and cleaning data) and the use of advanced statistical techniques.
themes: algorithmic accountability; scraping; data cleaning; statistical techniques; power structures
relevance_rationale: Algorithmic accountability reporting endures as discipline but needs updating to include generative AI auditing, model evaluation, and LLM-specific methods
notes: Cites Diakopoulos 2014, 2021

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Familiarize yourself with the terminology: read research papers and journalistic articles on AI to get acquainted with the correct terms; however, translate technological and scientific terms into everyday language and expressions whenever possible. Complicate the narrative: the 'true' narrative may not be that simple; problematize oversimplified narratives on AI and triangulate with several experts and stakeholders. Beat the hype: examine technologies with a critical lense, avoid buzzwords and ask yourself: what does this mean in practice, or in the life of your audiences?
themes: terminology; translation; narrative complexity; triangulation; hype avoidance
relevance_rationale: Familiarizing with terminology, complicating narratives, and beating AI hype are timeless journalism practices amplified in the LLM era
notes: From Guidelines for Journalists Covering Algorithms (Sivadas & Argoub 2021), part 1 of 2

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Assess research results: when reporting on research on AI technologies, ask how the research was conducted, what data was used and how large the dataset was, what initial assumptions did the researchers have when approaching the study, and what consequences might come out of the technology. Test: if possible, try to acquire personal practical knowledge about the applications you are reporting about by testing new algorithms and systems by yourself. Follow professional online communities: national and international journalistic communities, both institutional projects and peer groups on social networking sites, provide journalists with up-to-date tools for thinking and action when reporting technologies and related issues.
themes: research assessment; hands-on testing; professional communities; methodology questions
relevance_rationale: Assessing research methods, testing systems personally, and following professional communities are enduring practices for tech reporters
notes: From Guidelines for Journalists Covering Algorithms (Sivadas & Argoub 2021), part 2 of 2

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: When covering malicious uses of AI, journalists are faced with the question of how to report on issues related to intentionally harmful contents and processes in a proper way. Journalists need to see the relevance, volume and expected consequences in realistic proportions. Moreover, the general journalistic ethics rules that are applied to crime and justice reporting are valid even in the coverage of AI; individuals should be protected, no one should be publicly suspected or prosecuted by journalists and facts should be validated, to avoid spreading rumours.
chapter: Reporting on Malicious Uses of AI Technologies
themes: proportionality; ethics; crime reporting norms; validation; harm avoidance
relevance_rationale: Ethics of reporting on malicious AI uses while protecting individuals and validating facts endures as a journalism principle

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: They defined AI as a 'dual-use area of technology', meaning that AI technologies can be used to both beneficial and harmful ends, and the celebratory aspects of AI should always be balanced against misuse-related considerations. AI is a double-edged sword.
chapter: Reporting on Malicious Uses of AI Technologies
themes: dual-use framing; balanced reporting; risk-benefit analysis
relevance_rationale: AI as dual-use technology requiring balanced coverage of benefits and risks is even more relevant with generative AI capabilities
notes: Brundage et al. 2018 Oxford workshop

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Deepfakes, as falsified content, counteract the historical conviction of images and visual materials as records of reality. Deepfakes easily go viral and, when depicting news events, may lead to mass panic among the audience, with fatal consequences. Deepfakes thus need active debunking measures from journalists.
chapter: Reporting on Malicious Uses of AI Technologies
themes: deepfake detection; debunking; visual verification; misinformation
relevance_rationale: Deepfake threats endure but have intensified dramatically with generative AI, requiring updated detection methods and reporting frameworks

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Several studies have discovered that in daily newspaper coverage, industry sources dominate and the discussion about AI is typically conducted by male stakeholders who can have financial gains from AI. In the study of AI coverage in the UK, the Reuters Institute report found that almost 60% of news articles across different outlets covered new industry products or initiatives. A third of unique sources across the articles were affiliated with industry, almost twice as many as those from academia, and six times as many as those from government.
chapter: Patterns of Storytelling about Artificial Intelligence
themes: source bias; industry dominance; gender bias; source diversity
relevance_rationale: Industry source dominance and gender bias in AI coverage is an empirical finding that likely persists and warrants continued monitoring
notes: Cites Brennen et al. 2018 Reuters Institute

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The discussion should obviously be more diverse and multivoiced in terms of sourcing, and journalists should consciously give space to actors other than major industry sources. Activists, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), and other civil-society representatives, as well as customers, should be included in reporting, as they are experiencing the consequences of AI in their everyday lives.
chapter: Patterns of Storytelling about Artificial Intelligence
themes: source diversity; civil society; NGOs; affected communities; inclusive sourcing
relevance_rationale: Diversifying sources beyond industry to include civil society, activists, and affected communities is a timeless journalism principle

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: While media should explore in detail the promise and pitfalls of AI, they would be well served to treat it less as a world-shaking revolution and more as a set of technologies in the process of being designed, as set of choices in the process of being made, and a set of problems in the process of being collectively solved.
chapter: Patterns of Storytelling about Artificial Intelligence
themes: framing; balanced reporting; de-sensationalizing; process orientation
relevance_rationale: Framing AI as choices being made rather than inevitable revolution is crucial counter-narrative in the generative AI hype cycle
notes: Brennen et al. 2018 p.10

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: AI is currently an area dominated by spot news events, but ideally it should be covered in more in-depth approaches following trends and long-term processes that affect society. If monitored in terms of individual events, which are typically product releases, investment decisions, or initiative launches by major industry organizations, reporting may relapse into following major players' agendas. As influential public actors, journalists can counteract AI-related representation of gender and race, as well as other biased structures, and contribute to increasing diversity in different fields.
chapter: Patterns of Storytelling about Artificial Intelligence
themes: trend analysis; long-term coverage; agenda independence; diversity; representation
relevance_rationale: Need for in-depth trend coverage rather than spot news about product launches applies directly to LLM coverage patterns

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: What kind of AI is the topic about? As AI is a very general term, broad in scope, it often needs to be specified. What is so special about this particular technology? What does it do, and possibly change? What is known about how the AI in question actually works? Is it clear to the audience how an app looks or how it is used in practice? Can the workings be illustrated in word or images somehow? Who are the stakeholders of the AI in question, and what are the financial interests in the background of this particular application? Who owns the product, and who has financed it?
chapter: Patterns of Storytelling about Artificial Intelligence
themes: AI specificity; stakeholder identification; ownership; financial interests; audience clarity
relevance_rationale: Checklist for specifying what kind of AI, who owns it, and financial interests is directly applicable to reporting on LLM products
notes: Checklist of questions when covering AI, part 1 of 2

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: How does the AI affect the lives of different groups of people? How do these people, as citizens and customers, perceive these changes? What consequences does the AI have for the target groups of your media? What kind of image does your reporting deliver of the phenomenon?
chapter: Patterns of Storytelling about Artificial Intelligence
themes: impact assessment; affected communities; audience relevance; self-reflection
relevance_rationale: Asking how AI affects different groups and centering audience impact is a timeless user-centered journalism principle
notes: Checklist of questions when covering AI, part 2 of 2

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Reuters' algorithm News Tracer automatically searches for breaking news on social media. By detecting clusters of similar information and reviewing the accounts that published the information, the robot can notify a journalist about a news item the moment it occurs.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: news discovery; social media monitoring; cluster detection; breaking news; Reuters
relevance_rationale: Reuters News Tracer social media monitoring is a specific tool example now superseded by more advanced LLM-powered news detection systems

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: BBC Juicer pipeline is watching RSS feeds of news outlets. When a new article is published on one of these RSS feeds, BBC Juicer scrapes the news article, both raw text and metadata (e.g., date, time, title, news source). In the next step, BBC Juicer identifies and tags concepts mentioned in the article text, making them searchable and therefore useful for trend analysis. By streamlining media workflows like that, AI enables journalists to focus on what they do best.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: news aggregation; RSS scraping; concept tagging; trend analysis; BBC
relevance_rationale: BBC Juicer RSS pipeline is a specific pre-LLM tool example; concept of automated content tagging endures but implementation has been superseded

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: They use AI and Natural Language Generation (NLG) to automatically produce publishable news texts from large datasets, including sports, property sales, traffic news, weather, the stock market, and local business registrations. Their robots analyse big datasets that are open and available to everyone and identify unusual events or patterns. The information can then be used to build extended articles or as alerts for the newsroom to follow up on.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: automated journalism; NLG; open data; pattern detection; United Robots
relevance_rationale: NLG for automated news from structured data is now dramatically more capable with LLMs; the limitations described are partly overcome
notes: United Robots example

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Thurman, Dörr, and Kunert suggest several aspects where the influence of algorithms and increasing use of digital data present ethical challenges. These include how journalists acquire, validate, and use digital data in news production and the risk of bias in the algorithms that power automation. The transparency of code and data is of course an issue, both from a professional and a democratic perspective. It is also a question of machine autonomy: whether algorithms can 'reason' and draw appropriate conclusions in all contexts.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: data validation; algorithm bias; code transparency; machine autonomy; ethics
relevance_rationale: Ethical challenges of algorithm bias, code transparency, and machine autonomy questions are enduring concerns amplified by LLMs
notes: Cites Thurman Dorr Kunert 2017

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Asking journalists about their perceptions of working with automation, Thurman, Dörr and Kunert identify several limitations: 1. Its reliance on single, isolated data streams. 2. The one-dimensional nature of the quantitative data feeds it relies on. 3. The difficulties of interrogating that data. 4. The lack of human angles in the texts generated. 5. The requirement to template stories – predicting 'top lines' – in advance. 6. The difficulty of working creatively with the data in the templating process.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: automation limitations; data interrogation; templating; creativity constraints; human angles
relevance_rationale: Limitations of pre-LLM automation (single data streams, lack of human angles, rigid templates) are partly addressed by LLMs but new limitations emerge
notes: Thurman et al. 2017 p.1254

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Create an understanding of what type of AI you need, what problem it should solve, and how it relates to other editorial strategies and policies. Which parts of the organization are affected and need to collaborate on this? Identify the obstacles that may arise, such as resources, culture, and management, and address these in a systematic way.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: AI strategy; newsroom collaboration; obstacle identification; editorial policy
relevance_rationale: Strategic planning for AI adoption including problem definition, organizational alignment, and obstacle identification endures as management advice
notes: Beckett 2019 advice for media companies

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Collaboration is an important factor for successful automation of any business, and journalism is no exception. Collaboration involves sharing resources and utilizing them where they are needed and leads to new ways of combining skills, abilities, and material assets. Novel contact surfaces lead to a better flow of information, new insights into problems, and the opportunity to learn from the mistakes of others.
chapter: Artificial Intelligence in Journalism and Journalistic Practice
themes: collaboration; interdisciplinary teams; resource sharing; newsroom innovation
relevance_rationale: Collaboration as key factor for successful AI adoption in newsrooms is an organizational principle that endures

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Journalists can profitably apply the same critical mind-set to the integration of AI systems into their own workplaces and newsroom practices. And, of course, as in other fields, for all stories that touch on AI, transparency is also a must when journalists or their outputs are sponsored in one way or another.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: self-reflexivity; transparency; sponsorship disclosure; newsroom AI
relevance_rationale: Applying critical mindset to AI integration in own newsroom and maintaining transparency about sponsorship is more relevant than ever

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Journalists should not be misled by the science-fiction fantasies, even though possessing an understanding of them is crucial. Journalists should be more curious about asking how intelligent systems affect us in versatile ways and looking for alternatives.
chapter: Introduction: Pedagogies of Teaching about AI Technologies
themes: sci-fi bias; narrative avoidance; alternative framing; everyday AI
relevance_rationale: Not being misled by science-fiction fantasies while understanding how AI affects us in everyday ways is directly applicable to LLM hype

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Several empirical studies based on survey data and tracking data have indicated that the use of gatekeepers, such as search engines and social media, is, in most cases, associated with more diverse news consumption, the opposite of the filter bubble hypothesis.
themes: filter bubbles; empirical evidence; nuance; algorithmic effects
relevance_rationale: Filter bubble research findings may need reassessment given LLM-powered recommendation systems that differ fundamentally from earlier search/social algorithms
