notes: Cites Ross Arguedas et al. 2022; complicates common filter bubble narrative

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The ubiquitous extraction (plus sale and use) of data from people to fuel AI is another narrative that needs continuous monitoring. Data are not just a resource that is somehow there for the taking – which is a key observation for journalists to dig into in regard to AI stories.
chapter: Foreword: Addressing the Diversity of Artificial Intelligence
themes: data extraction; surveillance; monitoring beat; data as resource
relevance_rationale: Data extraction from people to fuel AI as a narrative requiring monitoring is even more relevant with LLM training data controversies
notes: Cites Kate Crawford, Shoshana Zuboff

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Describing AI systems as taking actions independent of human supervision or implying that they may soon be able to do so is a pitfall. Rather than describing AI as a broad set of tools, such comparisons anthropomorphize AI tools and imply that AI tools have the potential to act as agents in the real world.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; anthropomorphism; framing; verification
relevance_rationale: Warning against anthropomorphizing AI endures but needs updating since LLMs exhibit more agent-like behaviors than earlier AI, complicating the framing
notes: Pitfall 1: Attributing agency to AI

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Images of humanoid robots are often used to illustrate articles about AI, even if the contents of the article have nothing to do with robots. This gives readers a false impression that AI tools are embodied, even when it is just software that learns patterns from data.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; visual framing; imagery; misleading representation
relevance_rationale: Critique of humanoid robot imagery for non-robot AI stories remains a valid media literacy point
notes: Pitfall 2: Suggestive imagery

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Articles often compare how well AI tools perform with human skills on a given task. This falsely implies that AI tools and humans compete on an equal footing—hiding the fact that AI tools only work in a narrow range of settings.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; false equivalence; human-machine comparison; framing
relevance_rationale: Warning against false human-AI competition framing endures but LLMs have genuinely expanded the range of tasks where comparison is relevant
notes: Pitfalls 3-4: Comparison with human intelligence and skills

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Describing AI systems as revolutionary or groundbreaking without concrete evidence of their performance gives a false impression of how useful they will be. This issue is amplified when the AI tool is deployed in a setting where they are known to have past failures—we should be skeptical about the effectiveness of AI tools in these settings.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; hyperbole; evidence; critical assessment
relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward claims of revolutionary AI without concrete performance evidence is a timeless journalistic principle
notes: Pitfall 5: Hyperbole

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Comparing AI tools with major historical transformations like the invention of electricity or the industrial revolution is a great marketing tactic. However, when news articles adopt these terms, they can convey a false sense of potential and progress—especially when these claims are not backed by real-world evidence.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; historical analogy; hype; critical assessment
relevance_rationale: Warning against comparing AI to electricity or industrial revolution without evidence is directly applicable to current LLM hype narratives
notes: Pitfall 6: Uncritical comparison with historical transformations

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Claims about how future developments in AI tools will affect an industry, when made without evidence, are mere speculation. Similarly, articles can include false claims about what an AI tool can do. News articles often cite academic studies to substantiate their claims, but there is sometimes a gap between the claims made based on an academic study and what the study reports.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; speculation; source verification; academic citation; fact-checking
relevance_rationale: Scrutinizing speculative future claims and checking academic studies against actual findings is a permanent verification standard
notes: Pitfalls 7-9: Unjustified future claims, false progress claims, incorrect study reporting

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Using deep-sounding phrases like 'the elemental act of next-word prediction' or 'the magic of AI' implies that an AI tool is doing something remarkable. It hides an understanding of how mundane the tasks are, and that AI tools are functioning exactly as expected.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; language; hype; demystification
relevance_rationale: Critique of mystifying language about AI applies directly to LLM marketing phrases like 'reasoning' and 'understanding'
notes: Pitfall 10: Deep-sounding terms for banal actions. Cites Emily Bender.

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: When an article only or primarily has quotes from company spokespeople or researchers who built an AI tool, it is likely to be over-optimistic about the potential benefits of the tool. News articles often re-use terms from companies' PR statements instead of describing how an AI tool works, which can lead to misleading wording that misrepresents the actual capabilities.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: sourcing; corporate PR; balance; AI coverage; critical sourcing
relevance_rationale: Warning about over-optimistic coverage when relying only on company sources and PR language is directly applicable to LLM product coverage
notes: Pitfalls 11-12: Treating spokespeople as neutral; repeating PR terms

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Limitations such as inadequate validation, bias, and potential for dual-use plague most AI tools. When these limitations are not discussed, readers get a skewed view. Even if an article discusses limitations and quotes experts, limitations are often downplayed in the structure of the article, for instance by positioning them at the end or giving them limited space. Limitations can also be caveated by positioning experts who explain them as skeptics who don't see the true potential of AI.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; limitations; story structure; balance; framing
relevance_rationale: Discussing limitations, bias, and dual-use rather than burying them is a critical reporting standard for all AI coverage
notes: Pitfalls 13-15: No discussion of limitations, limitations de-emphasized, skeptics framing

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: When discussing AI tools, articles often foreground the role of technical advances and downplay all the human labor that is necessary to build the system or keep it running. Downplaying human labor misleads readers into thinking that AI tools work autonomously, instead of clarifying that they require significant overhead in terms of human labor.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; invisible labor; automation narrative; human infrastructure
relevance_rationale: Foregrounding human labor behind AI systems is even more relevant with LLMs that depend on massive annotation, RLHF, and content moderation labor
notes: Pitfall 16: Downplaying human labor. Cites Ghost Work by Gray and Suri.

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Including numbers like '90% accuracy' in the body of the article without specifying the conditions under which these numbers are calculated can misinform readers about the efficacy of an AI tool, especially because AI tools are known to suffer performance degradations even under slight changes to the datasets they are evaluated on.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; performance metrics; uncertainty; data literacy; statistical reporting
relevance_rationale: Reporting AI accuracy claims without specifying conditions is a permanent verification pitfall amplified by LLM hype
notes: Pitfall 17: Performance numbers reported without uncertainty estimation or caveats

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Referring to AI tools as inscrutable black boxes is a category error. Instead of holding the developers of these tools accountable for their design choices, it shifts scrutiny to the technical aspects of the system. Journalists should hold developers accountable for the performance of AI tools rather than referring to these tools as black boxes and allowing developers to evade accountability.
chapter: A checklist of eighteen pitfalls in AI journalism
themes: AI coverage; accountability; black box; transparency; developer responsibility
relevance_rationale: Holding AI developers accountable rather than hiding behind black box rhetoric is even more essential with LLMs
notes: Pitfall 18: The fallacy of inscrutability

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Identify working strategies for the following procedures in terms of where to find open data, which software to use, and what to do with the software: finding the most frequent words, phrases, and topics in governmental documents (textual analysis, topic modelling); visualizing statistics on a map (data visualization); automatic collection of news topics from different sites (aggregation). Reflect on what kind of competence requirements the methodology creates.
themes: text analysis; topic modelling; data visualization; aggregation; open data; competence
relevance_rationale: LLMs now handle text analysis, topic modelling, and aggregation tasks directly, changing the software landscape for these procedures
notes: Assignment prompt; not direct method but outlines a practical pipeline for text/data work

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: To use AI for augmenting journalistic methods, reporters must learn more about the possibilities. Up to this point, responsibility for newsroom innovation is often located with IT departments. To ensure a professionally and financially sustainable development of journalism, editorial staff and management must get involved too.
themes: newsroom innovation; AI adoption; editorial leadership; organizational change
relevance_rationale: Editorial leadership involvement in AI adoption rather than leaving it to IT remains an essential organizational principle

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The core elements of journalism — story telling in different formats, critical thinking, verification, human values, ethics, autonomy and integrity — become even more important at a time when smart machines are entering every part of society and professional life. The professional identity of journalists is likely to change, but the development indicates an upvaluation of the tasks, traits, and functions that are not possible to automate.
themes: professional identity; automation; human skills; verification; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Core journalism skills becoming more valuable as automation handles routine tasks is a permanent professional identity truth
notes: Cites Lindén 2020

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: A critical understanding of AI requires tracing how system structures are based on decisions that veil economic, ethical or value-based political interests, which have consequences that maintain structures of power. Journalists play a pivotal role in mediating timely knowledge to policymakers, developers, researchers, educators, and civil society actors.
themes: critical AI literacy; power structures; journalism role; public interest
relevance_rationale: Tracing how AI system structures veil economic and political interests is permanently essential critical literacy

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The public discourse in news media on AI has certain biases, such as the dominance of industry sources, major transnational organizations and their new products or initiatives, as well as insider and expert views. The gender, racial and regional biases of AI systems may also easily be transferred into the journalistic discourse and coverage if journalists are not conscious of them.
themes: source bias; industry dominance; gender bias; racial bias; AI discourse
relevance_rationale: Bias in AI discourse from industry source dominance and gender/racial bias transfer is permanently relevant

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Technology-anchored ways of reporting — such as immersive journalism and mobile journalism, as well as the collaborative work of data journalists, journalist-programmers and hacks and hackers — have deepened journalism's relationship to data and computer systems. Approaches with a problem-solving and developing mindset, such as constructive journalism, solutions-based journalism, investigative journalism and fact-checking, have helped journalists analyze and validate information and co-think with audiences.
themes: computational journalism; data journalism; fact-checking; solutions journalism; audience engagement
relevance_rationale: LLMs have transformed the computational journalism landscape; collaborative hacks-and-hackers model needs updating for AI-native workflows

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Participatory, reciprocal and dialogical approaches in journalism invite citizens and consumers to investigate the everyday dimensions of narrow AI. Journalism can increase public understanding by diversifying the practices of presentation beyond mediation of information, engaging people through immersion, gamification and involvement.
themes: audience engagement; participatory journalism; immersion; gamification; AI literacy
relevance_rationale: Participatory approaches engaging citizens to investigate AI through immersion and gamification remain valid presentation strategies

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: The core competences foreseen as essential for future journalists include personal characteristics (curiosity, accuracy, ability to handle stress), personal competences (broad general knowledge, news judgment, criticality), abilities related to work and organisational behaviour (social skills, team player skills), and work practices and methods (information search and acquisition, interview techniques, analysis of information and data, sourcing, techniques of presentation and storytelling). Essential additions include numeracy skills — the ability to understand data, statistics and scale — and the ability to translate ideas from highly specialized terminology to everyday contexts.
themes: journalism competences; numeracy; data literacy; translation skills; education
relevance_rationale: Core journalism competences including numeracy, data literacy, and translation skills are permanently essential
notes: Cites Poynter industry report (Finberg & Klinger 2014)

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Algorithms can be used to work closely with news valuation standards and praxis within an organization. The public service algorithm News Values developed for Swedish Radio personalizes the news feed based on news value ratings made by the journalists, both to create a more relevant news feed and to streamline organizational news valuation standards. The system of news values stimulates a discussion about how we do our journalism.
chapter: AI's impact on journalistic professional identity and newsroom automation
themes: algorithms; news values; personalization; public service media; editorial standards
relevance_rationale: News value algorithms like Swedish Radio's need updating to account for LLM-based personalization and recommendation systems
notes: Swedish Radio example; Zachrisson 2021

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Robots can easily learn to detect deviation from a norm or expectation: a feature used to generate news about earthquakes over 3.0 on the Richter scale or to scan the Swedish criminal record for deviations in comparison with historical data and then notify a journalist.
chapter: AI's impact on journalistic professional identity and newsroom automation
themes: anomaly detection; automated alerts; deviation from norm; news generation
relevance_rationale: Anomaly detection and automated alerts now benefit from LLM pattern recognition, expanding what robots can detect beyond simple numeric thresholds
notes: Examples: earthquake alerts (LeCompte 2015), Swedish crime data (Magnusson et al. 2016)

source_title: UNESCO Reporting on Artificial Intelligence: Handbook for Journalism Educators
author: Maarit Jaakkola (ed.), UNESCO
extract: Go through the national ethical code for journalists and discuss how the use of a chosen AI technology or methodology in journalism may alter the ethics.
themes: ethics; AI methodology; professional codes; ethical review
relevance_rationale: Reviewing ethical codes against AI methodology use is a permanent professional obligation that intensifies with LLMs
notes: Assignment prompt; practical exercise for ethics audit of AI tools

author: Amnesty International
extract: A fact-finding mission to build a picture of: where and by whom algorithmic systems are being deployed, whether they are having detrimental impacts on people's lives, what information is available in the public domain, what further research avenues are possible, and options for challenging systems and seeking change.
themes: scoping; investigation-design; research-questions
relevance_rationale: Structured scoping as pre-investigation fact-finding about algorithmic systems is a design process independent of AI tools
notes: Scoping as structured pre-investigation fact-finding

author: Amnesty International
extract: Build relationships with local activists, journalists and organizations. Only proceed if your work is additive to local efforts.
chapter: Project Scoping — Considerations for Global Organizations
themes: collaboration; duplication-avoidance; community-engagement
relevance_rationale: Building relationships with local activists and ensuring additive work is a human collaborative principle
notes: Applies to any cross-jurisdictional investigation

author: Amnesty International
extract: Involve stakeholders, community members, and affected people as early as possible. Participatory research approaches include community-led data collection, collaborative workshops, and mapping exercises. Build a shared understanding of algorithmic harm and allow communities to shape the project and its goals.
themes: participatory-research; community-engagement; co-design
relevance_rationale: Participatory research with community co-design of goals is an ethical and epistemological commitment unaffected by AI
notes: Communities shape goals, not just provide testimony

author: Amnesty International
extract: Early steps: desk research (reviews of official government communications, strategy and procurement documents), FOIs (submit broadly to relevant public sector agencies — Lighthouse Reports submitted 100+ FOIs for their Suspicion Machine investigation), and speaking to impacted communities (engagement with individuals subjected to new technologies, obtaining copies of official decision communications).
chapter: Project Scoping — Knowledge of the System
themes: desk-research; FOI; source-development; document-acquisition
relevance_rationale: Three-pronged knowledge-building through documents, FOIs, and community engagement is a comprehensive sourcing strategy
notes: Three-pronged early knowledge-building: documents, FOIs, community engagement

author: Amnesty International
extract: Investigating potential human rights impacts requires both sociotechnical analysis and human rights-focused research. A holistic approach considers harms along the lifecycle of the technical system from inception to use. Analysis of civil society reports, news reports, and interviews with impacted communities can surface key human rights harms even if technical research cannot be undertaken.
chapter: Project Goals and Lifecycle — Holistic View
themes: sociotechnical-analysis; mixed-methods; workaround-strategies
relevance_rationale: Holistic sociotechnical analysis that uses non-technical evidence when code access is unavailable endures and is even more relevant with opaque LLM systems
notes: Non-technical evidence paths viable when code access unavailable

author: Amnesty International
extract: Discussions around change should precede any investigation. A theory of change incorporates goals, channels of influence, key messages, tactics and activities, with mechanisms for monitoring and evaluating success.
chapter: Project Goals and Lifecycle — Affecting Change
themes: theory-of-change; impact-planning; pre-investigation-design
relevance_rationale: Theory of change preceding investigation is an impact-planning framework independent of tooling
notes: Impact strategy precedes rather than follows investigation

author: Amnesty International
extract: Embed participatory approaches throughout a project's duration. After scoping, ensure regular consultation and reshape the project to align with community needs. Published research should be translated and made accessible (Easy to Read versions, explainer videos, presentations to communities).
chapter: Project Ethics and Principles — Community Participation
themes: accessibility; participatory-research; translation; community-engagement
relevance_rationale: Accessibility and translation of research outputs for communities is an ethical obligation that endures
notes: Accessibility as ethical obligation in output design

author: Amnesty International
extract: Embed racial justice and anti-discrimination within the strategy. Interview individuals with diverse identities. Intersectionality examines how different forms of discrimination overlap and interact — critical for analyzing how algorithmic systems compound harms against specific groups.
chapter: Project Ethics and Principles — Anti-Discrimination and Intersectional Lens
themes: intersectionality; anti-discrimination; sampling-strategy; analytical-framework
relevance_rationale: Intersectionality as analytical lens for algorithmic bias is more relevant than ever with LLM systems
notes: Intersectionality as analytical lens, not just ethical add-on

author: Amnesty International
extract: Prioritize safety over revelation of information. Invest in planning to assess risks and benefits for communities and the research team. Develop risk mitigation strategies in partnership with stakeholders. Do not proceed if the risk of harm is too high.
chapter: Project Ethics and Principles — Security Considerations
themes: safety; risk-assessment; ethics; harm-prevention
relevance_rationale: Prioritizing safety over revelation and risk assessment are ethical constraints that endure
notes: Safety trumps publication — a hard constraint

author: Amnesty International
extract: Where security allows, direct engagement with government officials should be the first port of call. Provides opportunities to interrogate the system and understand policy drivers.
chapter: Obtaining Access to Information — Direct Engagement with Government Officials
themes: source-development; government-engagement; interviews
relevance_rationale: Direct engagement with government officials as first port of call is a sourcing practice independent of AI

author: Amnesty International
extract: Freedom of Information requests are critical for circumventing lack of publicly available information. Template requests should cover: technical documentation (system architecture, algorithmic design), data collected/exploited, example model inputs and outputs, Data Protection Impact Assessments, equalities or human rights impact assessments, and bias or statistical assessments. Example model inputs/outputs and technical documentation are essential first ports of call.
chapter: Obtaining Access to Information — Freedom of Information Requests
themes: FOI; document-acquisition; template-strategy; algorithmic-transparency
relevance_rationale: FOI template strategy for algorithmic systems requesting technical docs and model I/O is directly applicable to LLM-era investigations
notes: Prioritizes model I/O and technical docs as first requests

author: Amnesty International
extract: Data protection laws grant people the right to access information organizations hold about them. Subject Access Requests can reveal system inputs and data processing when working with people subjected to algorithmic systems.
chapter: Obtaining Access to Information — Subject Access Requests
themes: SARs; data-protection; document-acquisition; individual-rights
relevance_rationale: Subject Access Requests to reveal algorithmic inputs and processing endure as a legal mechanism
notes: Requires working directly with affected individuals

author: Amnesty International
extract: Interview academics, analysts, practitioners, and front-line workers. Start by looking for relevant media and academic articles, reaching out to authors and quoted experts.
chapter: Obtaining Access to Information — Expert Interviews
themes: source-development; expert-interviews; literature-review
relevance_rationale: Using publication trails to find expert sources is a sourcing strategy independent of AI
notes: Publication trail as source-finding strategy

author: Amnesty International
extract: Map out the 'dream scenario' data and triangulate publicly available information with FOI requests. Public records, survey data, and quarterly/yearly reports from public sector agencies can be valuable.
chapter: Obtaining Access to Information — Alternative Data Sources
themes: triangulation; data-strategy; public-records; ideal-dataset
relevance_rationale: Dream scenario data mapping and triangulation with FOI is an investigation planning technique that endures
notes: Dream scenario mapping as planning technique

author: Amnesty International
extract: Map the supply chain from computing hardware to cloud providers to AI companies to government deployment. Useful sources: tender notices and RFPs, published government strategies, loan agreements or MoUs with international organizations, public company information, marketing materials and demonstrations, companies search databases and job advertisements, research letters requesting responses from private companies.
chapter: Obtaining Access to Information — Procurement Research and Supply Chain
themes: supply-chain-mapping; procurement; corporate-research; OSINT
relevance_rationale: Supply chain mapping from hardware to deployment using procurement docs is directly relevant to investigating LLM deployments
notes: Comprehensive source list for algorithmic supply chain investigation

author: Amnesty International
extract: Testimonies and focus groups are the backbone of evidence on algorithmic harms. Additional methods include legal analysis (international human rights law, UN reports, domestic law, court transcripts), discourse analysis (media reporting, government interviews, policy documents), and survey data (short surveys with people subject to the technology).
chapter: Human Rights Research — Methods and Data Sources
themes: testimony; mixed-methods; qualitative-data; survey-design
relevance_rationale: Testimonies as backbone of algorithmic harm evidence combined with legal and discourse analysis endures
notes: Testimony as primary evidence; quantitative as supplementary

author: Amnesty International
extract: Research solely focused on technical aspects without meaningful participation of impacted people can gesture towards false notions that issues can be solved through technical fixes alone.
themes: techno-solutionism; participation; framing-bias
relevance_rationale: Warning against techno-solutionism and purely technical framing is even more relevant in the LLM era
notes: Warns against purely technical framing of algorithmic harm

author: Amnesty International
extract: Looking at technology within the political, social, economic and cultural incentives and environmental factors. The word 'system' denotes that these tools are 'intricate, dynamic arrangements of people and code' that cannot be reduced to the technology component.
themes: sociotechnical-framing; systems-thinking; context
relevance_rationale: Sociotechnical framing of algorithmic systems as arrangements of people and code is fundamental and enduring
notes: Redefines 'system' beyond technology to include people and context

author: Amnesty International
extract: The core idea of statistical bias and fairness testing: compare accuracy of algorithmic decisions across demographic groups. Measuring fairness is not purely technical — proposed measures must be interrogated from legal, philosophical and sociological perspectives. Even when a specific definition of fairness is met, discrimination may still be present.
chapter: Algorithmic Auditing — Statistical Bias and Fairness Testing
themes: fairness-metrics; statistical-testing; bias-audit; limits-of-quantification
relevance_rationale: Fairness metrics as necessary but insufficient for detecting discrimination is a core insight for LLM-era auditing
notes: Fairness metrics are necessary but insufficient

author: Amnesty International
extract: Compile a checklist of what you can learn (inputs, processing, models, outputs). Interrogate design choices — these are not neutral decisions. Examine redactions (not always applied correctly). Use information for follow-up questions. Corroborate with testimonial evidence.
themes: document-review; verification; corroboration; redaction-analysis
relevance_rationale: Documentation review checklist for algorithmic systems including redaction analysis is directly applicable to LLM investigations
notes: Redactions themselves are analytically informative

author: Amnesty International
extract: Full access (White Box): Rotterdam Municipality's welfare algorithm — Lighthouse Reports obtained source code, variable lists, model evaluations, documentation, trained model file, and raw data, enabling direct stress-testing and bias analysis. Code access (Reverse Engineering): France's CNAF — researchers obtained redacted documentation and source code, reconstructed the system, ran tests using publicly available demographic data. Outcome data only (Black Box): Sweden's Social Insurance Agency — without access to source code or inputs, researchers requested data on outputs and outcomes for statistical bias and fairness testing.
themes: audit-methodology; white-box; black-box; reverse-engineering; access-levels
relevance_rationale: White-box, reverse-engineering, and black-box audit scenarios calibrated to access level are directly transferable to LLM auditing
notes: Three audit scenarios calibrated to level of data access

author: Amnesty International
extract: Center lived experiences of impacted people. Demystify the technology (simple language, graphics). Challenge false dichotomy between innovation and regulation. Present positive, rights-affirming vision of technology's role. Co-design key messages with partner organizations. Time publications strategically.
themes: narrative-strategy; accessibility; framing; publication-timing; co-design
relevance_rationale: Narrative strategy centering lived experiences and demystifying technology is a communication principle that endures
notes: Communication strategy as integral to investigation, not afterthought

author: Amnesty International
extract: Documenting evidence alone is not enough. Post-publication, researchers must continue to pursue accountability through advocacy, litigation and other means.
themes: impact; accountability; post-publication; advocacy
relevance_rationale: Post-publication accountability through advocacy and litigation is an impact principle independent of AI
notes: Publication as midpoint, not endpoint

author: Amnesty International
extract: AI systems can lead to discrimination through direct discrimination (explicit distinction between groups, e.g., algorithm includes protected characteristics) or indirect discrimination (neutral presentation but disproportionate impact). Proxies — inputs correlated with protected characteristics such as postcode correlating with socioeconomic status and race — are a key mechanism.
chapter: Human Rights Research — Right to Equality and Non-Discrimination
themes: proxy-variables; discrimination; algorithmic-bias; direct-vs-indirect
relevance_rationale: Understanding proxy variables and direct vs indirect discrimination in algorithms is essential for LLM-era bias analysis
notes: Proxy variable awareness essential for bias analysis

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: Data journalism is defined as a workflow that consists of: obtaining datasets via scraping or Freedom of Information Act Requests; cleaning (i.e. correcting errors and structuring data) and manipulating large datasets (i.e. using similar fields to combine relevant datasets); producing original data analysis using statistical concepts; designing graphics, visualizations, or multimedia accompaniments; using statistics to tell stories and illustrate concepts in an easy-to-understand way; creating media that has value for readers
themes: workflow; pipeline; data journalism definition
relevance_rationale: The six-step workflow definition endures conceptually but specific steps like scraping and cleaning are now substantially LLM-assisted
notes: 6-step workflow definition from Gray et al. Data Journalism Handbook

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: The unit on data acquisition focused on locating, examining and searching through government data sets. In-class exercises primarily used datasets obtained from HealthData.gov and FDA.gov. To provide practical knowledge about gaining data through public information requests, we had two guest speakers provide their expertise from drastically different viewpoints: the journalist submitting requests and the administrator at our institution who receives requests.
themes: government data; FOIA; public records; data acquisition
relevance_rationale: Teaching data acquisition through government portals and dual-perspective FOIA instruction remains essential civic and legal knowledge
notes: Dual-perspective FOIA teaching: requestor and administrator

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: Data sets are often dirty where it may be formatted incorrectly or inconvenient for data processing. In addition, data sets may contain erroneous data. Our data-cleaning unit focused on using Google Refine and advanced features of Microsoft Excel to spot questionable data and reformat data.
themes: dirty data; data quality; cleaning rationale
relevance_rationale: Understanding that data is dirty and recognizing formatting errors vs erroneous values is conceptual knowledge that endures even when AI cleans data
notes: Framing: dirty data = formatting errors + erroneous values

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: using conditional rules to highlight data that exceeds the expected range
themes: range checking; conditional formatting; outlier detection
relevance_rationale: Conditional formatting for range-checking is now supplemented by LLMs that can identify outliers and anomalies conversationally
notes: Excel conditional formatting as verification technique

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: using advanced Excel formulas including VLOOKUP, RIGHT, LEFT, SEARCH to combine and format data; using Excel formulas to change contents of a cell into more useful formats (e.g. convert a date in the format of 2/13/2014 into 4 columns with a weekday, day, month, and year)
themes: Excel; VLOOKUP; data transformation; date parsing
relevance_rationale: Specific Excel formulas like VLOOKUP, RIGHT, LEFT, SEARCH for data transformation are now handled more naturally by LLM-assisted coding
notes: Specific Excel formulas for restructuring data

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: identifying characteristics of suspicious and/or misleading data
themes: data integrity; suspicious data; verification heuristics
relevance_rationale: Identifying characteristics of suspicious and misleading data is a critical thinking skill that becomes more important when AI automates cleaning
notes: Listed as core cleaning competency but is fundamentally a verification skill

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: using Google Refine to correct spelling mistakes in hand-entered data
themes: Google Refine; spelling correction; data standardization
relevance_rationale: Google Refine for spelling corrections in hand-entered data is now largely handled by LLM-powered fuzzy matching and entity resolution

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course
author: Plaue and Cook
extract: the instructor taught user-centered design methods. As part of a motivation for the course, Martin Wattenberg's Baby Name Wizard was used as an example of an interactive visualization of a large, public data set. Students critiqued the Name Wizard along dimensions of usability, interaction design, and journalistic value.
chapter: Course Content — Data Reporting and Visualization Techniques
themes: user-centered design; visualization critique; usability; journalistic value
relevance_rationale: User-centered design methods and critiquing visualizations along usability, interaction, and journalistic value dimensions endure as design principles
notes: Three critique dimensions: usability, interaction design, journalistic value

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course

source_title: Data Journalism: Lessons Learned While Designing an Interdisciplinary Service Course

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du
extract: Another way to help students deal with the difficulties is to engage them in realistic, problem-centred activities. source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du
extract: It is unnecessary to bring all the knowledge and skills involved in the training of computer science and journalism into the training of DJ. In fact, using data for news storytelling requires only a very basic set of coding and programming skills. source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du
extract: To avoid the polarity problem, both discipline-based and interdisciplinary experiences should be taken as essential in the curriculum design. In terms of the discipline-based approach, students should be equipped with the necessary knowledge and skills of their own major, be it journalism, computer science, visual design or others. As for interdisciplinary experiences, it can be achieved through in-depth interdisciplinary interactions, such as practical case studies, team projects, field trips to the industry as well as exchange visits with other schools and departments.
themes: curriculum design; interdisciplinary balance; case studies; team projects
relevance_rationale: Balancing discipline-based depth with interdisciplinary integration through case studies and team projects endures as curriculum design
notes: Citing Jacobs 1989 polarity problem; balance domain depth with cross-domain integration activities

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study

source_title: Interdisciplinary Learning in Journalism: A Hong Kong Study
author: Zhu and Du

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: DJ has a tendency to be more exploratory than other kinds of investigative reporting, which usually begin with a hunch or tip-off and have a much clearer investigative trajectory. It can be a challenge to find data sets that will have currency at the semester's conclusion, will deliver a range of strong news leads and will sustain student engagement. In practice, this usually means choosing an area of research where the public interest value is high enough that if students begin with a broad, exploratory question their findings will be newsworthy, whatever the result.
themes: research design; topic selection; exploratory analysis; public interest
relevance_rationale: DJ as inherently exploratory, requiring high-public-interest topics where any finding is newsworthy, is editorial judgment that endures
notes: Contrasts DJ exploratory orientation with traditional tip-off investigative work

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: One of the most challenging elements of a data-driven investigation is conceptualizing a research strategy that will provide meaningful and reliable results and conclusions. This design process is more closely aligned with academic research strategies than journalistic practice.
themes: research design; methodology; academic methods
relevance_rationale: Conceptualizing a research strategy for meaningful and reliable results is a design skill closer to academic rigor than journalism tradition and endures
notes: Positions DJ research design as closer to academic than journalistic traditions

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Nguyen and Lugo-Ocando suggest that 'permanent determination to question data and a basic level of statistical reasoning' is a necessary starting point, as well as the ability to use logical, valid reasoning and journalistic scepticism to (a) find and acquire data, (b) explore and evaluate their real meaning, (c) investigate non-numerical factors shaping them and (d) report them in a balanced, fair, accurate, accessible and engaging manner.
themes: statistical reasoning; data scepticism; critical thinking; quantitative literacy
relevance_rationale: Permanent determination to question data and statistical reasoning as foundations for finding, evaluating, and reporting data endure as core competencies
notes: Four capabilities framework from Nguyen and Lugo-Ocando 2016

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: In a news conference format, the class considers some of the best ways to measure and evidence a phenomenon, and examines the potential gaps and weaknesses in the data. They begin to debate and ask questions: Does giving a lot of speeches make someone a better politician or just a louder one? Are some speeches more valid than others? Can we find out what each MP is talking about? What work might a politician do that isn't captured in the data?
themes: operationalization; measurement validity; research questions; collaborative design
relevance_rationale: News conference format for collaborative methodology design -- debating measurement validity and data gaps -- is active learning that endures
notes: News conference format used for collaborative methodology design

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The course would focus on DJ as the foundational research method. The data and analysis could guarantee strong, exclusive stories which students could then break into research areas and supplement with documents, interviews, analysis, visualization and storytelling, later in the process.
chapter: Towards a Project-based Approach: A Case Study
themes: data-first workflow; story development; exclusive data; research method
relevance_rationale: Data-first workflow producing exclusive stories supplemented by interviews and documents is a research strategy that endures
notes: Data-first approach: collect data first, then supplement with interviews and documents

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The most successful projects over the last six years have involved a mixture of hand-collected/coded data and open source or scraped data. Despite its potential to be monotonous, manual data coding activities are often identified by students as a highlight of the course and seeing the data at a micro-level helps the students understand more complex spreadsheets. Students approach data as an exercise in translation, and the act of translating information to data is perhaps more accessible than the reverse.
themes: manual data collection; data coding; data literacy; mixed methods
relevance_rationale: Manual data coding builds micro-level understanding and produces exclusive data; the pedagogical value of translation from information to data endures
notes: Counter-intuitive finding: manual coding valued despite available automated tools

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Even a simple, one-hour data collection exercise, if tied to the project outcomes, helps to explain the nature of data and to familiarize students with how it can be structured. As a bonus, hand-coded data is also exclusive data.
themes: data collection; exclusive data; data structure; hands-on learning
relevance_rationale: Short hands-on data collection exercises tied to project outcomes build structural understanding of data and produce exclusive material

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The cohort naturally treats the yet-to-be-collected data as they would any other source: they ask questions of it; consider its agendas, weaknesses and blind spots; are critical of it; examine its biases; contextualize it and cross-check it against other sources.
themes: data scepticism; source evaluation; bias; cross-checking; verification
relevance_rationale: Treating data with the same critical scrutiny as human sources -- questioning agendas, biases, blind spots -- is journalistic skepticism that endures
notes: Treats data with same critical scrutiny as human sources

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: This kind of critical engagement with the data takes place throughout the project: during the methodology design, data collection, data analysis, planning and researching the stories, visualizing the data and reporting findings.
themes: iterative verification; workflow; critical engagement
relevance_rationale: Continuous critical engagement throughout all pipeline stages rather than verification as a discrete step is a principle that endures
notes: Verification is continuous across all pipeline stages, not a discrete step

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Getting each student to choose a free, online tool and visualize an element of their research is a worthwhile exercise, not just because it exposes students to some techniques and frameworks for visualizations but also because it intensifies students' connection to the data. The visualization process often prompts students to ask deeper questions about the data, and come to different conclusions about their results.
themes: visualization; data exploration; analytical insight; free tools
relevance_rationale: Visualization as an analytical tool that deepens connection to data and prompts new questions endures beyond its presentation function
notes: Visualization as analytical tool, not just presentation

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Complex or technical analysis (for example, correlations) can still be achieved through enlisting an expert with access to specialist software; fortunately, a university is full of such experts. In this case, the students must still develop the questions that drive the specialist data analysis, even if they do not conduct the analysis themselves, to ensure they are active participants and collaborators in this facet of the process.
themes: collaboration; specialist expertise; question-driven analysis; interdisciplinary
relevance_rationale: LLMs now serve as the specialist collaborator for complex analysis, reducing dependence on university experts though question-formulation skill endures

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The major project is presented to students as an 'investigation', not as a 'data-driven investigation'. In the early weeks, all discussion is framed in the context of their topic. Students naturally self-identify a number of data-driven strategies for their investigation, well before anyone has mentioned statistics, maths or data. The project-driven approach allows the lecturer to normalize DJ as one of a range of journalistic tools a reporter has access to; the methodology does not define the students' investigation, the research questions do.
themes: dataphobia; framing; research questions; pedagogy; normalization
relevance_rationale: Framing DJ as investigation not data-driven investigation to overcome dataphobia is a pedagogical strategy that remains effective
notes: Guerrilla approach: frame around questions not methodology to overcome resistance

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: One data mining mission can provide multiple leads for stories. If you think there are no stories out there and the world seems a bit boring: dig, search, compile a series of stats and it can lead you to some ammunition for a killer story.
chapter: The Benefits and Challenges of the Project-based Approach
themes: story discovery; data mining; lead generation
relevance_rationale: One data mining exercise yielding multiple story leads is a discovery principle that endures and is amplified by AI-assisted exploration

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The most valuable investigative tool I have garnered from this exercise is the skill of being able to read data, in the sense that by changing the way pieces of information sit next to each other, you can have an entirely new outcome.
chapter: The Benefits and Challenges of the Project-based Approach
themes: data literacy; juxtaposition; analytical thinking; reframing
relevance_rationale: Reading data by juxtaposing information in new ways to reveal patterns is an analytical literacy skill that endures
notes: Student testimony; rearranging data reveals new patterns

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The emphasis on data logic, literacy, scepticism and research strategy is an approach that is likely to age well. While DJ technologies and skills are changing rapidly, the ability to problem-solve and collaborate through investigative roadblocks to find innovative solutions will retain its value.

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The early weeks of the semester are used to conceptualize and define the scope of the project. Students then collect and analyse data, contextualize it with interviews and other research, and write and visualize their findings. Although the project is collaborative, the students identify sub-topics and niches to specialize in. They share information and collaborate with students working on the same sub-topic, and must manage overlap between their research and stories.

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism

source_title: A DIY, Project-based Approach to Teaching Data Journalism
author: Caroline Graham

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Data journalism is defined broadly as reporting involving the collection, analysis, and presentation of quantitative datasets. To study code, we need to unpack the wider sociotechnical assemblages in which it is embedded to understand how programs work in the world.
themes: data journalism; definition; sociotechnical
relevance_rationale: Unpacking sociotechnical assemblages to understand how programs work in the world endures and is critical for understanding LLMs

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Interpreting code contextually requires considering the code in relation to other texts that define it, such as documentation, comments, program structure, and other ancillary material. We consider three interrelated texts—the code (and associated documentation), the data, and the published story—in order to highlight how programming code serves editorial purposes.
themes: critical code studies; contextual analysis; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Contextual code interpretation through documentation, comments, and program structure is essential for auditing any computational system including LLMs

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Many analyses of content, discourse, or practice in data journalism have focused on the uppermost tier of the field: award entries, projects or teams of leading newsrooms, high-profile hackathons, etc. This results in an unrepresentative picture that fails to capture general or everyday or quick turnaround data journalism that typically falls short of ideals espoused by data journalism evangelists.
themes: sampling bias; representativeness; everyday practice
relevance_rationale: Warning against sampling bias toward elite examples rather than everyday practice is a methodological insight that endures

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: We defined variables to capture various features of (1) data in the repository, (2) code in the repository, and (3) the published news story. We then used hierarchical clustering to triangulate across these characteristics and identify groups of stories for qualitative review.

relevance_rationale: Hierarchical clustering for content analysis is valid but LLMs can now assist with both quantitative classification and qualitative review

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: We classify data with respect to the repository as well as the published story. Journalists' reliance on government data has been understood as an information subsidy that both enables and influences news coverage. Government data may be released in response to Freedom of Information (FOIA) requests, or leaked. Datasets may also be scattered across disparate files or released in file formats inconvenient for data analysis, perhaps intentionally (e.g., tabular data in a PDF format).
themes: government data; FOIA; information subsidy; data sources
relevance_rationale: Government data as information subsidy that both enables and influences coverage, including deliberate format obstruction, endures
notes: Data often deliberately released in unusable formats

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Established practice in data journalism emphasizes making available the datasets that underlie analysis; scholars understand this as reflecting norms of transparency as well as civic engagement, where providing data may facilitate various forms of interactivity, engagement, participation and collaboration.
themes: transparency; open data; reproducibility; civic engagement
relevance_rationale: Transparency through publishing underlying datasets as a norm of civic engagement and reproducibility endures

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Scraping is understood as a technique not just to access but to create datasets, a distinction which becomes blurry in practice. For example, The New York Times' award-winning COVID-19 dataset relies almost exclusively on health data which was publicly available from various agencies—but which gained tremendous value when systematically cleaned and collated. In other cases, scraping specific items from a large number of sources can be used to create a new dataset.
themes: scraping; data creation; data wrangling; editorial labor
relevance_rationale: Scraping as data creation not just access, blurring lines between collection and editorial labor, is an insight that endures
notes: Scraping blurs line between data access and data creation

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Code may be used to generate quantitative data from qualitative observations (quantification) or to conduct mathematical operations on quantitative data once assembled (analysis). The variable quantification refers narrowly to cases where the repository includes code which converts textual data to numeric data, for example, measuring negative sentiment in a text.
themes: quantification; text analysis; sentiment analysis; data transformation
relevance_rationale: Code-based quantification of qualitative data like sentiment analysis endures but LLMs now perform this far more accessibly
notes: Distinguishes quantification from analysis as separate operations

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Data featured helps to identify cases where the news organization highlights the creation and publication of datasets as public-facing newswork in itself, and could potentially mobilize data publics which put that data to new uses. This is distinct from data present which noted whether the repository contains a dataset; this may or may not be highlighted in the news article.
themes: data publics; transparency; editorial framing; dataset as product
relevance_rationale: Distinguishing data-featured from data-present and the concept of data publics endures as an editorial framing insight
notes: Publishing data itself becomes a journalistic output

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Simply sharing datasets is a primary use of GitHub by data journalists. Sharing datasets via GitHub was considered a worthy goal in itself. Publishing underlying data bolsters the claims made in the news report, as anyone can see it for themselves. In some cases, the dataset becomes the stepping stone for other reportage and research.
chapter: Theme 1: Creating Databases as an End Goal of Data Journalism
themes: data sharing; transparency; reproducibility; GitHub
relevance_rationale: Publishing datasets as a worthy journalistic goal for transparency and enabling further research endures

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: GitHub provides version tracking, and therefore fosters transparency regarding how crowdsourcing was used to update/modify the dataset. GitHub permits automated updates to the dataset. GitHub brings together a programming community and provides publicly visible engagement metrics. A news report containing a weblink to GitHub might suggest to readers that the news organization is transparent and embraces an open source ethos—which is a performative aspect of transparency.
chapter: Theme 1: Creating Databases as an End Goal of Data Journalism
themes: GitHub; version control; transparency; crowdsourcing; automation
relevance_rationale: GitHub as transparency infrastructure with version tracking and public engagement metrics endures as a practice
notes: GitHub as infrastructure for journalistic transparency

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Databases can be used as crowdsourcing mechanisms to enrich existing data, although this method often proves unsuccessful. More than a hundred entries were updated in the dataset based on crowdsourced inputs.
chapter: Theme 1: Creating Databases as an End Goal of Data Journalism
themes: crowdsourcing; data enrichment; community participation
relevance_rationale: Crowdsourcing for data enrichment being often unsuccessful but sometimes valuable endures as practical knowledge
notes: Crowdsourcing often fails but can work for specific verification tasks

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Journalists use government data (sometimes in combination with other datasets) to perform statistical analysis that reveals a systemic issue of public concern. The authority vested in official data allows journalists to question those responsible—including public officials—while pointing to the results from the statistical analysis. In this model, data have no journalistic value on their own. The reporter has to find the hidden story in the data.
chapter: Theme 2: Government data as an Anchor of Investigative Data Journalism
themes: government data; statistical analysis; investigative journalism; accountability
relevance_rationale: Core epistemological model of using government data for statistical analysis to reveal systemic issues endures
notes: Core epistemological model of investigative data journalism

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: More complicated analyses can potentially invite counter-claims. Using sophisticated statistical techniques may invite criticisms of methodology. When using such techniques is necessary, journalists strengthen their claim and mitigate risks by relying on external expertise—and by having published datasets in a way that allows outsiders to replicate and confirm their analysis.
chapter: Theme 2: Government data as an Anchor of Investigative Data Journalism
themes: external expertise; reproducibility; methodological risk; peer review
relevance_rationale: Mitigating methodological risk through external expertise and published replicable datasets endures as a credibility strategy
notes: Strategy for defending complex statistical analyses

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: News organizations may take specific steps to avoid potential controversies when they undertake complex data analysis. A Los Angeles Times story outsourced the analysis to a data scientist at Harvard University, who performed specialized statistical modeling. The Washington Post used data from NOAA and a climate scientist provided the initial analysis at The Post's request. Comments in the analysis code justified the statistical methodology by stating that The New York Times had used the same approach.
chapter: Theme 2: Government data as an Anchor of Investigative Data Journalism
themes: external validation; academic partnerships; methodological justification; credibility
relevance_rationale: Outsourcing complex analysis to academics and citing precedent as credibility strategies endure
notes: Three strategies: outsource analysis, cite precedent, invite replication

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Access to government data can weaken the government's interpretive monopoly by allowing for alternative interpretations. Journalists' interpretation of data is often adversarial to the institutions which provide that data.
chapter: Theme 2: Government data as an Anchor of Investigative Data Journalism
themes: information subsidy; adversarial journalism; interpretive power; government data
relevance_rationale: Government data access weakening institutional interpretive monopoly through adversarial journalism endures
notes: Data access enables counter-narratives to official accounts

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: The labor of scraping, formatting and cleaning data includes assembling data scattered across different sites or sources, checking for missing or incorrectly recorded data, and converting data into formats compatible with analysis tools. These steps feature prominently in data journalism textbooks and tutorials, referred to as data scraping, data wrangling, preprocessing, data cleaning.
chapter: Theme 3: Data wrangling as Essential Work of Data Journalism
themes: data wrangling; cleaning; formatting; preprocessing
relevance_rationale: Data wrangling steps endure but LLMs substantially reduce the labor of formatting, cleaning, and assembling scattered data

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: We learned early that individual scrapers needed to be strategically fragile: We wanted them to break when the source website changed or failed to respond, to alert us so that our team could figure out whether the issue was with our code or the source website's changes. In the early days of the pandemic, a few notable sites changed several times in just a couple of weeks, which meant we had to repeatedly rewrite our code.
chapter: Theme 3: Data wrangling as Essential Work of Data Journalism
themes: scraping; fragility; monitoring; maintenance; automation
relevance_rationale: Strategic fragility in scraper design to detect source changes is a monitoring principle that endures for any automated data pipeline
notes: NYT quote on strategic fragility in scraper design

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Scraping PDFs should never—emphasis on NEVER—be a data journalist's first approach. In the ideal world, this information would have also been neatly available in a structured format, such as an API. The decision to dive into a PDF ocean should only be taken after it has been confirmed beyond doubt that is the only way to obtain the information contained in them.
chapter: Theme 3: Data wrangling as Essential Work of Data Journalism
themes: PDF scraping; data formats; APIs; last resort
relevance_rationale: PDF scraping as last resort endures as principle but LLMs have made PDF extraction dramatically easier, reducing the pain threshold
notes: Seattle Times quote; PDF scraping as method of last resort

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: In a second set of data wrangling cases, code generates clean, structured data primarily as an input for further analysis, rather than to publish a dataset. Data wrangling is ubiquitous and labor intensive but celebrated only rarely, even among the practitioners. It is more likely to be highlighted in news reports when the resulting database is itself newsworthy, but not in numerous other cases where the data is consumed within the analysis.
chapter: Theme 3: Data wrangling as Essential Work of Data Journalism
themes: data wrangling; invisible labor; editorial work
relevance_rationale: Data wrangling as undervalued editorial labor endures as insight but LLMs may further render this work invisible by automating it
notes: Wrangling is essential but undervalued editorial labor

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Web scraping provides a wider range of alternative sources of data, which helps in overcoming the topical saturation of open/FOI data. Scraping allows journalists to gather institutional data on their own terms, pushing against the dynamics of information subsidy; it is counterintuitively but unmistakably editorial effort.
chapter: Theme 3: Data wrangling as Essential Work of Data Journalism
themes: scraping; editorial labor; data independence; alternative sources
relevance_rationale: Scraping as editorial counterweight to institutional data dependency is an epistemological insight that endures
notes: Scraping as counterweight to institutional data dependency

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: An abundance of data has invited a new genre based on relatively low-effort data mining to uncover interesting sociopolitical trends. These stories mirror the trend in social sciences where the availability of social media data can shape lines of inquiry. Stories in the social analysis cluster fit the descriptions of a daily, quick turnaround, generally visualised, brief form of data journalism which is often editorialised and entertaining.
chapter: Theme 4: Abundance of Data Drives Quick Turnaround Data Journalism
themes: datafication; quick turnaround; social media; data abundance
relevance_rationale: Data abundance driving quick-turnaround journalism and shaping lines of inquiry is an observation that endures and intensifies with LLMs
notes: Data availability drives story selection, parallel to big data critiques in academia

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Data journalists are leveraging widespread datafication to collect their own data, going beyond traditional sources such as public data or FOIA requests. These include social media platforms, independent surveys, and miscellaneous online datasets.
chapter: Theme 4: Abundance of Data Drives Quick Turnaround Data Journalism
themes: data sources; social media; surveys; datafication; original data
relevance_rationale: Leveraging datafication to collect original data beyond traditional sources endures as a practice

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Projects in the tech reporting clusters tend to harvest data from platforms and other major tech firms using specialized methods, and use the created datasets to scrutinize the firms' policies and opaque algorithms. They share the epistemic model of reverse-engineering computational artifacts. To retrieve data needed to reverse-engineer the platform's algorithms, the outlet paid volunteers to run an application it created to monitor which groups and pages Facebook recommended to its users.
themes: algorithmic accountability; reverse engineering; platform scrutiny; data harvesting
relevance_rationale: Reverse-engineering platform algorithms through specialized data collection methods endures and is critical for LLM accountability
notes: Specialized data collection methods for investigating tech platforms

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: To uncover the blocklist, Markup located an undocumented and unpublicized YouTube API; systematically varying inputs to the API (random strings, hate speech, and uncontroversial words) revealed four classes of API outputs (no results, full results, blocked keywords, and partially blocked keywords).
themes: reverse engineering; API probing; systematic variation; algorithmic audit
relevance_rationale: Systematic API probing to map hidden categories is a technique directly applicable to investigating LLM APIs
notes: Specific technique: systematically varying API inputs to map hidden categories

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: The heavy labor of scraping, formatting and cleaning data is addressed in the data science field but seldom considered in journalism studies. Institutions often provide data which is effectively unusable, perhaps intentionally so. In these cases, journalists do the labor of making this data comprehensible and easily accessible, or provide interpretations of it.
themes: data wrangling; institutional obstruction; editorial labor; data science
relevance_rationale: Heavy labor of data wrangling and institutional format obstruction endure but LLMs significantly reduce the effort needed
notes: Institutions may deliberately release data in unusable formats

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: To hold tech companies accountable, journalists are adopting the methodology of reverse-engineering computational artifacts, which is distinct from traditional journalistic methods. Reverse-engineering is likely supplementary to traditional methods relying on whistleblowers and information from insiders.
themes: reverse engineering; algorithmic accountability; methodology; tech scrutiny
relevance_rationale: Reverse-engineering as a distinct journalistic method supplementing traditional whistleblower methods endures for LLM accountability
notes: Reverse-engineering as new journalistic method distinct from traditional approaches

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: Looking contextually at the assemblage of code, data, repository, and published news report can compensate for missing or inaccessible elements in journalistic code (such as a proprietary API) by providing a generalized picture of the work that code and data are doing. A contextual reading helps to focus the analysis of code on relevant sections when it is impractical to understand every line of code in a sizable sample.
themes: contextual analysis; assemblage; holistic reading; methodological strategy
relevance_rationale: Contextual reading of code-data-story assemblages to compensate for missing elements is a methodological strategy that endures
notes: Practical benefit: contextual reading compensates for incomplete code/data

source_title: Analyzing Code: What a Critical Code Studies Approach Reveals About the Epistemology of Data Journalism
author: Aman Abhishek, Lucas Graves
extract: GitHub appears to fulfill two infrastructural roles within data journalism. First is transparency by sharing of analysis material as a proof for the findings in the news report—ordinary users are not invited to make contributions because the analysis is supposed to be complete. Second is using GitHub to crowdsource databases and track changes to it—crowdsourcing provides missing data rather than changing the interpretations of the database or claims in the news report itself.
themes: GitHub; infrastructure; transparency; crowdsourcing; proof
relevance_rationale: Two distinct GitHub roles for transparency/proof and crowdsourcing/enrichment is an organizational insight that endures
notes: Two distinct GitHub roles: proof/transparency vs crowdsourcing/enrichment

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
