author: Caroline Graham
extract: The 2013 project methodology was thus developed around a data-driven research framework, which could transform the information gathering process for students so there would be: 'Less guessing, less looking for quotes; instead, a journalist can build a strong position supported by the data.' The first half of the semester would be dedicated to data collection; students would spend the remaining six weeks finding story angles, contextualizing the data findings with research and interviews and compiling their reports and visualizations for publication.
themes: research design; data-driven framework; structured workflow
relevance_rationale: Data-first research design with structured collection-then-contextualization workflow remains valid regardless of tools
notes: Explicit two-phase design: collect first, then contextualize with interviews

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Before the semester began, Crikey was approached to secure its interest in publishing a political accountability investigation that would attempt to quantify the level of political involvement of Australia's politicians. It was then necessary, in collaboration with Crikey's editorial team, to establish the scope of the investigation: It would be limited to Australia's House of Representatives (150 federal MPs) and political involvement would be quantified across three fields: committee work, speeches and questions (both questions without notice and questions in writing).
themes: scoping; publisher collaboration; operationalization
relevance_rationale: Collaborative scoping with publishers before data collection is a human editorial process unaffected by LLMs
notes: Scope defined collaboratively with publisher before data collection began

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Although information-gathering and fact-checking tasks can be distributed across a network of contributors, practices of sense-making invariably require a single analytical voice.
themes: networked journalism; analytical authority; distributed work
relevance_rationale: The need for a single analytical voice in sense-making endures even when LLMs assist with information gathering
notes: Citing van der Haak et al. (2012)

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: This 'brief' was then taken to the student cohort in a class run as a news conference. Students were encouraged to share their views on politics and political reporting, and to imagine what a data-driven approach might offer. They brainstormed ideas for the project and questions they had about the federal political process in Australia. These questions framed the research design, collection and analysis, and the process of asking them gave the students a greater sense of ownership of the project and an understanding of its direction and implications.
themes: question framing; collaborative planning; ownership
relevance_rationale: Collaborative question framing through news-conference format builds team ownership, a human organizational process
notes: News-conference format used to generate research questions from team

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Almost all data were collected from the Hansard, Australia's official online record of parliamentary debates. Information was also taken from MPs' biographies on the same website. From a research perspective, this ensured the information's credibility and consistency; from a teaching perspective, it also meant students became adept at searching the complicated site over the course of the semester.
themes: official sources; credibility; consistency
relevance_rationale: Official sources remain important but LLMs can now help navigate and extract from complex government sites
notes: Single authoritative source chosen to ensure data reliability

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The nature of the data collection was informed by traditional research methodologies—in this case, content analysis. As part of the research design, it was important to ensure the validity and reliability of the results. As coders, students were provided with a clear coding manual and coding response sheets, and reliability checks (10 per cent of coding cross-coded by another person) to ensure accuracy were conducted.
themes: content analysis; coding manual; inter-coder reliability; validity
relevance_rationale: Content analysis coding manuals and reliability checks endure but LLMs can now assist with inter-coder reliability and coding tasks
notes: 10% cross-coding threshold for reliability checks

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Each student was assigned six MPs to investigate. In addition, students were also assigned a seventh MP, selected from a group of four politicians who were coded as part of the research design testing process. This cross-coding was an important way to check for errors or inconsistencies. Students always coded the control-group politician first, and once they could demonstrate that they had done so correctly, moved on to their remaining (individual) coding.
themes: cross-coding; control group; error checking; quality assurance
relevance_rationale: Cross-coding and control groups remain valid QA but LLMs can supplement or accelerate consistency checks
notes: Control-group coding as gating mechanism before independent work

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: By far, the most important strategy used to facilitate the coding process was electronic code response sheets. For each of the three coding parts, students received an Excel or Word document to record results in; these documents used functions like drop-down responses and automated data-checking processes to save time and improve accuracy.
themes: data entry templates; constrained input; automation; accuracy
relevance_rationale: Drop-down templates and automated data-checking in Excel are largely superseded by LLM-assisted data entry and validation
notes: Drop-downs and automated checks embedded in collection instruments

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: When students were coding speeches and questions, each line in Excel represented one speech or question, which eliminated the risk of fabricated data. This also made analysis of the data in excel possible.
themes: data integrity; granular records; fabrication prevention
relevance_rationale: One-row-per-record structure as fabrication prevention is a data integrity principle independent of tooling
notes: One-row-per-record structure as built-in verification mechanism

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The data that were submitted required some cleaning in terms of format and consistency of presentation; however, there were very few errors found in the coding during this clean-up process and only one minor error found and corrected after publication.
themes: data cleaning; format standardization; error rate
relevance_rationale: Data cleaning for format consistency is now substantially accelerated by LLMs, though human review remains necessary
notes: Low error rate attributed to coding manual and electronic templates

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Continually pointing to the potential findings and placing the coding in the context of the overall investigative project was very important in maintaining enthusiasm for the project during the collection phase.
themes: motivation; tedium management; project context
relevance_rationale: Maintaining team motivation during tedious collection by pointing to findings is a human management challenge unaffected by AI
notes: Managing monotony of data collection through outcome visibility

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: While coding, students were encouraged to develop and share potential story angles. Where possible, coding questions could then be integrated to collect information for particular stories.
themes: story discovery; iterative collection; angle development
relevance_rationale: Iterative story discovery during data collection is an editorial judgment process that LLMs support but do not replace
notes: Collection and story-finding run in parallel, not strictly sequential

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Once the coding was complete, the data were analysed by a research assistant who was proficient in statistics. The findings were presented to students in the form of facts, lists, correlations and tables, including most and least active politicians; the average numbers of speeches and questions (by category); average length of committee service (against time served in parliament); the relationships between gender, age, experience, social media and performance; performance of backbenchers relative to those with a ministerial portfolio; party differences in performance; the extent to which country MPs are represented in parliament; the performance difference between marginal MPs and those in 'safe seats'; and the breakdown of speeches and questions by topic.
themes: statistical analysis; specialist roles; comparative analysis; correlation
relevance_rationale: Statistical analysis delegated to a specialist can now be partially handled by LLMs, though human statistical expertise remains important for interpretation
notes: Analysis delegated to statistics-proficient specialist; findings fed back to reporters

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The structure of the research process (conducting the data collection first) also seemed to have increased the knowledge base of the cohort who, by their own admission, initially had limited political knowledge. Working with the data, even at a micro level, had provided a framework for a broader understanding. The timing of the interviews (at the tail end of the research process) framed them as context or comment seeking (rather than idea or angle seeking) and meant that students approached their sources armed with a lead and the key pieces of information already in place.
chapter: Stage 3: Finding News Angles and Adding Context
themes: data-first interviewing; domain knowledge; source engagement
relevance_rationale: Data-first interviewing where immersion builds domain knowledge is an epistemological insight about learning that endures
notes: Data immersion builds domain knowledge; interviews become confirmatory rather than exploratory

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Students nominated an angle or area they were interested in investigating from the data and were allowed to work in pairs or individually on their stories. Those who chose to work alone were still allocated a 'hub' of students researching in a similar area so that they would have some support and feedback from other students and could share some research and resources.
chapter: Stage 3: Finding News Angles and Adding Context
themes: story selection; team structure; peer support
relevance_rationale: Hub structures for peer support among reporters working adjacent angles is organizational design unaffected by AI
notes: Hub structure for solo reporters working adjacent angles

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: As part of their completed package for Crikey, students were also required to create an infographic to visualize their findings. Students initially sketched out their ideas on paper, then recreated them electronically using 'Tableau', 'Piktochart', 'Infogr.am' and 'Easel.ly', which all provided intuitive drag-and-drop options, attractive templates and easy embedding within Crikey's web platform.
chapter: Stage 4: Writing and Visualizing the Stories
themes: visualization tools; infographics; sketch-first workflow; embedding
relevance_rationale: Sketch-first-then-digital visualization workflow endures but specific tools like Piktochart/Easel.ly are dated; LLMs can now generate charts directly
notes: Paper sketch before digital; free web-based tools chosen for accessibility

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: Students also reported that the process of creating the infographic helped them clarify and conceptualize their research, in turn making the writing process easier. However, it should be acknowledged that from a publication point of view, any errors in the infographics were difficult to subedit or change, and as a result, only some of the submitted graphics were published.
chapter: Stage 4: Writing and Visualizing the Stories
themes: visualization as sensemaking; editability; publication standards
relevance_rationale: Visualization as sensemaking tool and the editability problem for static graphics remain relevant regardless of AI
notes: Visualization aids understanding but static graphics create correction problems

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: A second research assistant was also commissioned to make a widget (web application) to allow readers to search the data by politician or electorate. In order to do this, the database results were simplified to 'rate' MPs in each of the three areas of quantifiable political activity.
chapter: Stage 4: Writing and Visualizing the Stories
themes: interactive search; data simplification; reader engagement
relevance_rationale: Building interactive search widgets required a specialist; LLMs now enable journalists to create simple data apps directly
notes: Raw data simplified into ratings for public-facing interactive tool

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: While manual collection appeared to help students understand the process and guaranteed the exclusivity of the results, combining some collection with existing open-source data or using technology-based collection techniques would likely increase student engagement.
themes: manual vs automated collection; engagement trade-offs; open data
relevance_rationale: Manual collection has pedagogical value but LLMs and scraping tools now make automated collection far more accessible
notes: Recommendation for future projects; manual collection has pedagogical value but monotony cost

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: One of the constraints of teaching journalism students within this area is perceptual (rather than actual) concerns about their ability and willingness to learn statistics or work with data.
themes: data anxiety; perceived barriers; numeracy
relevance_rationale: Data anxiety among journalism students persists but LLMs lower the barrier to working with numbers, potentially reducing this concern
notes: Citing Dunwoody and Griffin (2013); barrier is attitudinal not aptitudinal

source_title: By the Numbers: Data Journalism Projects as a Means of Teaching Political Investigative Reporting
author: Caroline Graham
extract: The process of designing the research framework (creating and testing the coding frameworks) and cleaning and analysing the data was enormously time consuming and a somewhat specialized process. For such initiatives to work, a small group of professionals need to invest time into content, coordination and training, and developing the underpinning frameworks for contributors.
themes: framework design cost; specialist investment; coordination overhead
relevance_rationale: Framework design and coordination overhead remain but LLMs can substantially reduce the specialist investment needed for coding manuals and templates
notes: Central challenge: networked collection requires heavy upfront framework design by specialists

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: When information was scarce, most of our efforts were devoted to hunting and gathering. Now that information is abundant, processing is more important. We process at two levels: (1) analysis to bring sense and structure out of the never-ending flow of data and (2) presentation to get what's important and relevant into the consumer's head. Like science, data journalism discloses its methods and presents its findings in a way that can be verified by replication.
themes: transparency; replicability; scientific method
relevance_rationale: Processing information at analysis and presentation levels remains essential regardless of LLM tools; transparency and replicability are timeless journalism values
notes: Meyer 2012 quote; frames DJ as two-level processing: analysis then presentation

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Information asymmetry—not the lack of information, but the inability to take in and process it with the speed and volume that it comes to us—is one of the most significant problems that citizens face in making choices about how to live their lives. Information taken in from print, visual and audio media influence citizen's choices and actions. Good data journalism helps to combat information asymmetry.
themes: information asymmetry; public interest; civic function
relevance_rationale: Combating information asymmetry is a permanent civic function of journalism that LLMs do not change
notes: Fries 2012 quote; frames DJ purpose as reducing information asymmetry

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: data journalists can work in multidisciplinary teams to tackle datasets, particularly large amounts of data, as scientists do, in order to achieve a coherent 'sense and structure' and bring it to the audience.
themes: collaboration; multidisciplinary teams; scientific method
relevance_rationale: Multidisciplinary teamwork endures but team composition changes when LLMs can substitute for some specialist roles

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: The processes by which the data is edited, interpreted, shaped and filtered alters it to a new form to evidence the claims made by the journalist.
themes: data transformation; interpretation; editorial judgment
relevance_rationale: Understanding data transformation as epistemic act remains fundamental regardless of whether LLMs assist the processing
notes: Data processing as epistemic transformation, not neutral relay

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: rather than focus on conducting research for information that has been published by reliable sources, data journalists also interpret, synthesize, analyse and evaluate data in a range of creative modes. Students need to learn to work mindfully with this source from the beginning of their studies, just as they do with primary and secondary sources.
themes: data literacy; core skills; interpretation
relevance_rationale: Interpreting, synthesizing, and evaluating data as core journalism skills persist even when LLMs assist with mechanics
notes: DJ as interpretation not just retrieval; treat data as core from day one

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: they need to understand that data journalism is likely to require them to work collaboratively, with other journalists, data scientists and information designers.
themes: collaboration; multidisciplinary teams; information design
relevance_rationale: Collaborative work endures but LLMs change the team composition; AI can partially fill data science or design gaps

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: students need to understand the limitations of data and become comfortable with manipulating it. Working with statistics is a good way to introduce students to the complexities of data and demonstrate that it is not value free.
themes: data literacy; bias; statistics; critical thinking
relevance_rationale: Understanding data limitations and that data is not value-free is a critical thinking skill no LLM replaces
notes: Data is not value-free; statistics as entry point to understanding limitations

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: data is a source that can be taken apart like any other.
themes: data literacy; source interrogation; core skills
relevance_rationale: Treating data as a source to interrogate is a foundational epistemological stance unchanged by LLMs
notes: Framing: data should be interrogated like any journalistic source

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Bad data, biased data, and flawed experiments can and will be used ignorantly or cynically to twist the truth, mislead, or misinform, even by journalists who wish to do the opposite. Even good data and solid research may be misrepresented or mistaken, a risk that will grow if journalists are pushed to create data visualizations or analyses without training in information design, statistics, and social science.
themes: bias; data quality; training; visualization risk
relevance_rationale: Risks of misrepresentation from untrained visualization persist and may increase with LLM-generated charts
notes: Howard 2014 quote; untrained visualization as a misinformation risk

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Journalists need to inspect data and identify the means by which it may be corrupted, made biased or how it is derived from flawed processes of experimentation. They know data is not value free. It has been created by people whose processes deserve investigation, verification and interrogation the same as any other journalistic source.
themes: verification; bias detection; source interrogation
relevance_rationale: Inspecting data provenance and verifying bias in data creation processes remains essential human judgment work
notes: Inspect provenance and process behind data creation

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Students need to learn to accurately interpret and represent data in summary form such as visualizations, and to draw on techniques and understandings that fall outside the traditional domain of media studies.
themes: visualization; interdisciplinary skills; data literacy
relevance_rationale: Accurate data representation in visualizations endures but LLMs now generate charts, requiring new literacy about AI-generated viz quality
notes: DJ requires skills beyond traditional media studies

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: the basics can be grouped into five key areas—data collection, cleaning, analysis, presentation and publishing
themes: pipeline model; workflow; core skills
relevance_rationale: Five-area pipeline model endures conceptually but LLMs automate parts of cleaning, analysis, and presentation steps
notes: Howard 2014 five-area framework; maps roughly to pipeline steps

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: data is not uniform, and there are no universal tools, applicable in all contexts. Furthermore, the tools professionals use often demand expert skills and knowledge, such as the creation of bespoke coding solutions using programming languages that respond to the challenges peculiar to individual datasets.
themes: tool selection; bespoke solutions; context specificity
relevance_rationale: No universal tools claim is weakened as LLMs provide general-purpose coding and analysis capabilities across contexts
notes: No universal tools; each dataset may require custom code

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Data journalists also work at expert levels of engagement over longer periods of time, and often create multimodal, interactive content in communication with multidisciplinary teams. These teams may include developers, designers, broadcast designers and project managers and processes that take large periods time.
themes: collaboration; multidisciplinary teams; long-form; interactive
relevance_rationale: Team-based multimodal production endures but LLMs reduce dependency on large specialist teams for some interactive content
notes: DJ as extended team-based production process

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: working with data is like interviewing a human source. Students must ask questions of the data and invite it to reveal the answers. But just as a source can only give answers about which he or she has information, a dataset can only answer questions for which it has the right records and the proper variables.
themes: question framing; data as source; limitations
relevance_rationale: Data-as-interview metaphor and understanding dataset scope limitations is timeless analytical wisdom
notes: Data-as-interview metaphor; dataset scope constrains possible answers

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Students must consider carefully what questions they want to answer even before they acquire their data, then look for data-evidenced statements to make in the story—gathering any information about the data needed to verify those statements.
themes: question framing; hypothesis; verification; evidence
relevance_rationale: Question-first workflow before data acquisition is a foundational research design principle unaffected by LLMs
notes: Question-first workflow: define questions before acquiring data

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: students need to think about the data from within a strong critical framework, and to interrogate the data based on questions that are meaningful.
themes: critical thinking; question framing; critical reflection
relevance_rationale: Critical framework and meaningful question formulation are human judgment tasks that LLMs cannot replace

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Students need to ask questions of the data source such as 'Is this data ethically framed?', 'What processes were used?', 'What processes am I using?' and 'Why?'.
themes: ethics; critical reflection; source interrogation; provenance
relevance_rationale: Ethical and process interrogation questions for data sources remain essential verification practice
notes: Concrete verification questions: ethics, process, reflexivity

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: data always comes from questions that are framed somewhere, somehow, as part of the human process of interpretation. Using the model of critical reflection, students first need to consider the analytic processes that yield the data.
themes: critical reflection; provenance; framing; interpretation
relevance_rationale: Recognizing that all data originates from human framing decisions is a permanent epistemological principle
notes: All data originates from human framing decisions; must interrogate those

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Often journalists start with a summary of data, but it is a good idea to provide students with an accessible set where they can see all the variables and records in the database rather than the subset that could answer the questions for the immediate story. Having access to the full data allows them to answer new questions that may come up in reporting, and even produce new ideas for follow-up stories.
themes: full dataset; exploration; follow-up stories
relevance_rationale: Obtaining full datasets rather than pre-filtered subsets remains best practice for thorough investigation
notes: Get the full dataset, not just the subset for the immediate question

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: They should also have access to the 'data dictionary' that explains any codes being used by particular variables.
themes: data dictionary; documentation; metadata
relevance_rationale: Always obtaining the data dictionary alongside the dataset is a permanent best practice
notes: Always obtain the data dictionary alongside the dataset

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: A good place for students to begin is by reflecting on the statistics for their own suburb in the Australian census dataset, or its equivalent wherever they are. It reminds them that census statistics are about people like them, and that we can all be reduced to numbers even though everyone has a story to tell.
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: personal connection; critical reflection; humanizing data
relevance_rationale: Starting with personally relevant data to build engagement and reflexivity is timeless pedagogy
notes: Start with personally relevant data subset to build engagement and reflexivity

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Asking students to create a basic visualization of part of the data, such as a pie chart, provides the opportunity to reflect on whether the visualization reveals something that the numbers did not. Ask them to reflect on their expectations and assumptions about the data. Usually there is a reason why they are drawn a particular data subset and documenting these initial thoughts help students to identify bias and reduce the risk of misinterpretation of the data.
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: visualization; bias detection; critical reflection; assumptions
relevance_rationale: Documenting assumptions and using early visualization to identify bias remains essential analytical practice
notes: Visualize early to reveal patterns; document assumptions to identify bias

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Students need to understand the basics in reading data, such as the difference between the mean and median. Students need to interview the data if you are to use it as a source of stories. Do the median figures tell the whole story?
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: statistics literacy; mean vs median; critical reading
relevance_rationale: Understanding mean vs median and what summary statistics hide is fundamental statistical literacy
notes: Basic statistical literacy: know what summary measures hide

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: The summary data about marital status in the dataset is a good example of why you do not take statistics at face value, and the importance of reflecting on them first. This datasets the parameter of 'aged 15 years and over' which could actually skew your reading of the data. As the age of consent in Australia is 16, it is likely that there are children in the 'never married category'. To know more, students would need to sort the data to remove the youngest age group.
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: parameter scrutiny; filtering; hidden assumptions; data cleaning
relevance_rationale: Scrutinizing parameter definitions and inclusion criteria to avoid interpretation skew is permanent verification skill
notes: Check parameter definitions; inclusion criteria can skew interpretation

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Can I trust this data? Why? Is it ethically collected? Are the parameters used clear? What assumptions are evident in the data? Is the dataset complete? Can you determine the mean scores in each category?
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: trust; ethics; completeness; parameters; verification checklist
relevance_rationale: Trust, ethics, completeness, and parameter verification checklist is universally applicable regardless of tools
notes: From Figure 1 guided-questions flowchart; first-gate trust check

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Would visualization help me to understand the data? What style of visualization works best for the data you are looking at? Why? Consider again how visualization can be used in interpreting data. Will it help you to see trends in the data?
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: visualization selection; exploratory analysis; trend detection
relevance_rationale: Visualization as analytical tool endures but LLMs now auto-generate exploratory charts, changing the workflow
notes: From Figure 1; visualization as analytical tool not just presentation

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Are the answers to all my questions in the data? What further information do you need from the larger dataset? What tools will you use to get this data?
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: gap analysis; data sufficiency; iterative inquiry
relevance_rationale: Gap analysis and checking data sufficiency against research questions is permanent analytical practice
notes: From Figure 1; check whether dataset answers your questions, seek more if not

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Is there a story of public interest in the data? What news values are you prioritizing in making this decision? Have you considered the interests of the audience?
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: news values; public interest; audience; story selection
relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment about public interest and news values in data is irreplaceable human decision-making
notes: From Figure 1; editorial judgment on whether data yields a public-interest story

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: If you cannot see a pattern or trend worth investigating, try visualizing new approaches to the data—you could try comparing trends over time, or creating subsets of data that are not simply mean figures.
chapter: A Model for Working with Data
themes: exploratory analysis; subsetting; trend comparison; visualization
relevance_rationale: Iterative exploratory analysis endures but LLMs can now rapidly generate alternative visualizations and subsets on demand
notes: From Figure 1; iterate with different visualizations and subsets if no story emerges

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Visualization is critical to data analysis. It provides a front line of attack, revealing intricate structure in data that cannot be absorbed in any other way. We discover unimagined effects, and we challenge imagined ones.
themes: visualization; exploratory analysis; pattern discovery
relevance_rationale: Visualization as primary analytical method revealing structure invisible in raw numbers is a permanent epistemological principle
notes: Aisch 2012 quote; visualization as primary analytical method, not just output

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: it is unrealistic to expect that data visualization tools and techniques will provide ready-made stories from datasets. It makes more sense to look for 'insights' which can become stories with further investigation.
themes: visualization; insight discovery; story development
relevance_rationale: Visualization yields insights not ready-made stories; further investigation is always required regardless of tools
notes: Viz yields insights not stories; further investigation still required

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: tables are very powerful when you are dealing with a relatively small number of data points. They are useful to demonstrate one-dimensional outliers (such as the top 10), but they are poor when it comes to comparing multiple dimensions at the same time. Charts, in general, allow mapping dimensions in data to visual properties of geometric shapes. Line charts are especially suited for showing temporal evolutions, while bar charts are perfect for comparing categorical data.
themes: chart selection; tables; line charts; bar charts; visualization types
relevance_rationale: Chart selection guidance (tables vs line vs bar) reflects perceptual science that remains valid
notes: Guidance on when to use tables vs line vs bar charts

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: like other forms of narrative journalism, data visualization can be effective for both breaking news—quickly imparting new information like the location of an accident and the number of casualties—and for feature stories, where it can go deeper into a topic and offer a new perspective.
themes: visualization; breaking news; feature stories; narrative
relevance_rationale: Data visualization serving both breaking news and feature stories is a permanent functional truth
notes: Viz serves both breaking-news speed and feature-story depth

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: Data is neither a primary source or a secondary one. It is 'live' in the sense you can ask it questions and arrive at answers, unlike secondary sources, which must be taken at face value. Data is also as significant and as valid as primary and secondary sources. But it is not value free. It has been created by people whose processes deserve investigation, verification and interrogation the same as any other journalistic source. Someone has set parameters on the information collected which in turn affects what is presented.
themes: data ontology; source interrogation; parameters; verification
relevance_rationale: Data as a third source type requiring interrogation of collection parameters is a foundational epistemological claim
notes: Data as third source type: live/queryable but shaped by collection parameters

source_title: First Things First: Teaching Data Journalism as a Core Skill
author: Lynette Sheridan Burns, Benjamin J. Matthews
extract: A capacity for effective collaboration with multidisciplinary teams and teams of other journalists in ways that move outside traditional industrial settings are also becoming more commonplace.
themes: collaboration; multidisciplinary teams; post-industrial journalism
relevance_rationale: Collaboration with multidisciplinary teams endures but LLMs change team composition and what counts as post-industrial journalism

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Data journalism is based on three fundamental pillars: the development of technology, the use of new journalistic practices (for example, mathematical knowledge, the ability to find data sets on the deep web and request data from government bodies, and competence in cleaning up data), and the participation and involvement of civil society, which is becoming increasingly aware of the importance of open data to governance.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: data acquisition; data cleaning; open data; skills
relevance_rationale: Three pillars of DJ endure but skills like finding data on deep web and cleaning are now substantially assisted by LLMs
notes: Martinisi 2013, Parasie and Dagiral 2013, Cohen et al. 2011 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Much of the time, government data is often 'dirty,' with missing metadata, incorrect fields, or gaps in collection. Journalists have to extract data from PDFs, validate it, and clean up data sets to make them usable in applications, report it out, and then present it in context.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: data cleaning; validation; government data; PDF extraction
relevance_rationale: PDF extraction and data validation remain necessary but LLMs have dramatically improved automated PDF handling
notes: Howard 2014 p.64 quoted

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: On May 9, 2016, the ICIJ decided to publish a searchable data set that strips away the secrecy of nearly 214,000 offshore entities. The consortium did not publish the totality of the leak to avoid disclosing raw documents or personal information en masse. The database contains a great deal of information about company owners, proxies and intermediaries in secrecy jurisdictions, but it doesn't disclose bank accounts, email exchanges and financial transactions contained in the documents. The principle that guided the selection of the information published was 'public interest.'
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: data publication; privacy; public interest; redaction; selective disclosure
relevance_rationale: Selective publication balancing public interest and privacy in leaked databases is an ethical principle that endures
notes: ICIJ decision on Panama Papers database release

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: In line with the basic principles of interpreting data, journalists should avoid creating trends and patterns that are not clearly inferred from data. Data are just statistics, and they may be interpreted in more than one way. In other words, journalists may have access to a data set, but that data set may not contain a pattern that supports a headline or a news item.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: data interpretation; false patterns; analytical integrity; bias
relevance_rationale: Avoiding false patterns and maintaining analytical integrity when interpreting data is a fundamental journalistic principle
notes: Elias 2015 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Together with their partners, Süddeutsche Zeitung checked and compared thousands of documents John Doe had delivered to us. We double cross-examined with other publications and documents or files from lawsuits. Not in a single case there were any doubts.
chapter: Ethical Debates on Sources: Journalism Stands for Public Interest
themes: source verification; cross-referencing; document verification; collaboration
relevance_rationale: Cross-examining leaked documents against external records for source verification is a foundational verification practice
notes: Wolfgang Krach, editor in chief of Süddeutsche Zeitung

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: They also agreed to make use of the source's documents, at all times interrogating the data he provided and comparing or contrasting them with information from other databases, such as Interpol's list of wanted persons and European and US sanctions orders.
chapter: Ethical Debates on Sources: Journalism Stands for Public Interest
themes: cross-referencing; external databases; sanctions lists; Interpol; triangulation
relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing with Interpol and sanctions lists for triangulation is a verification method independent of AI
notes: The Guardian, El Confidencial, La Nacion approach described

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Times editors believe that they owe it to their readers to do their own evaluation of the material. And that is happening now. We didn't have access to the documents, and that is a very big issue. This is a great trove of documents—certainly interesting and valuable—and it takes a while to know what to make of them.
chapter: Ethical Debates on Sources: Journalism Stands for Public Interest
themes: independent verification; editorial standards; source access; due diligence
relevance_rationale: Independent editorial evaluation of source material as professional standard endures
notes: Matt Purdy, NYT deputy exec editor; NYT was not part of ICIJ consortium

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: This was not a case of a single-fact story that we could simply confirm and go with. This was a case where hundreds of reporters had been working on it for a long time. However, reporters and editors worked on a follow-up article that was published the day after. Purdy stated that the newspaper's strategy was one of 'integrating it with our own reporting' and stressed the paper's unwavering commitment to comparing sources.
chapter: Ethical Debates on Sources: Journalism Stands for Public Interest
themes: integration; source comparison; follow-up reporting; editorial strategy
relevance_rationale: Integrating external reporting with own verification rather than accepting consortium findings at face value endures
notes: NYT strategy for handling Panama Papers without direct access to leak

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Not all news organizations are suited to the 'radical sharing' of information that these kinds of investigation require. Titles that participate in such international investigations cannot put aside any material to produce exclusives for themselves, and they need to agree on embargo dates.
chapter: Collaboration Between Media: The Importance of Sharing
themes: collaboration; radical sharing; embargo; international investigations; newsroom organization
relevance_rationale: Radical sharing requirements and embargo discipline in collaborative investigations are organizational principles unaffected by AI
notes: Marina Walker, ICIJ deputy director

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Early on, just as the first documents were becoming searchable through the I.C.I.J.'s sophisticated in-house system, it became apparent just how much we would have to relearn journalistic habits acquired over decades of reporting. We would have to learn how to share.
chapter: Collaboration Between Media: The Importance of Sharing
themes: collaboration; information sharing; journalistic habits; ICIJ platform; cultural change
relevance_rationale: Learning to share information across newsrooms in collaborative investigations is a cultural challenge independent of technology
notes: Michael Forsythe, NYT, reflecting on Paradise Papers after missing Panama Papers

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Publishing raw data online, as Wikileaks did, without professional filtering performed by journalists, can pose a threat to national security, and even to certain people. Journalism is still necessary to select, organize, and disseminate news stories contained in massive data, while also minimizing invasions of privacy and disclosures of information affecting states' security.
chapter: The Professionalization of Leaktivism and Social Change
themes: editorial filtering; privacy; national security; gatekeeping; responsible disclosure
relevance_rationale: Editorial filtering of raw leaks to protect privacy and security is a gatekeeping function that endures and gains relevance with LLM-scale data processing
notes: Contrasts WikiLeaks approach with ICIJ/Panama Papers approach

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: The newspapers involved in the Panama Papers investigation did not publish all the documents of the leak in unredacted form. Instead, they made an extra careful effort to avoid the release of personal information of nonpublic figures, playing a gatekeeper role.
chapter: Data Transparency and Journalists' Ability to Hold Power to Account
themes: redaction; privacy; gatekeeper role; selective publication; nonpublic figures
relevance_rationale: Redacting personal information of nonpublic figures in published leaked data is an ethical practice that endures
notes: NYT description of ICIJ consortium approach

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Charts are the only way of understanding complex ideas and connections. New visualization tools allowed journalists to develop multimedia pieces that made complex information more comprehensible and accessible. Journalists developed an interactive news game called 'Stairway to Tax Heaven,' in which players learn how to conceal their savings through offshore companies to avoid paying tax. And The Guardian published short explanatory videos and created a making-of podcast in which journalists who worked on the investigation talked about their experiences.
themes: visualization; interactivity; multimedia; news games; explainers; podcasts
relevance_rationale: Visualization and multimedia storytelling endure but LLMs add new capabilities for rapid interactive content creation
notes: Combines passages from lines 105-106 and Conclusions section

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: The best version of data journalism—that guided by ethical principles—can contribute to democracy in three ways. It can strengthen journalistic objectivity by ensuring that stories are supported by quantitative evidence; it can offer news media organizations new tools for enforcing government accountability; and it can increase citizens' participation by giving them, through their own access to the relevant data, the opportunity to decide if those data support the journalist's own conclusion.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: democratic function; objectivity; accountability; citizen participation; open data; quantitative evidence
relevance_rationale: Three democratic contributions of data journalism -- objectivity, accountability, citizen participation -- are fundamental values
notes: Parasie and Dagiral 2013 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: A large-scale implementation of data journalism in newsrooms may be restricted by a lack of resources such as time, tools, manpower, and the financial means and expertise required to appeal against denials of requests for data. Issues related to the organization of the newsroom, the behavior and structure of the market, and the power of government have curbed the development of data journalism. It is quite difficult to incorporate data journalism's routines into daily work, owing to the presence of established routines or a lack of time, people, and institutional or structural support. Under such circumstances, data journalism becomes reduced to the production of charts.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: resource constraints; newsroom organization; institutional barriers; routines; capacity
relevance_rationale: Resource constraints and institutional barriers to DJ adoption endure but LLMs substantially lower the time and expertise barriers described
notes: Fink and Anderson 2015, Solop and Wonders 2016, Manovich 2011, De Maeyer et al. 2015 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: There are three important areas of concern in relation to data journalism: data transparency and quality, social science research ethics, and the inscription of values into technological systems.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: data quality; transparency; research ethics; values in technology; critical data studies
relevance_rationale: Three ethical concerns of data transparency, research ethics, and values in technology are fundamental and endure
notes: Lewis 2015 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: The ability to work with data depends on both the digital tools available in each newsroom and the public institutions that offer open access to information.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: tooling; open data; institutional access; newsroom infrastructure
relevance_rationale: Dependency on newsroom tooling and open data access endures but LLMs provide new capabilities that reduce some tooling gaps
notes: Anderson 2012 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: As a rule, data sets cannot be released in raw form, because they contain personal or private information. Indeed, governments and public institutions sometimes make this point in order to avoid giving journalists the information they are looking for.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: privacy; raw data; government resistance; FOI; access barriers
relevance_rationale: Privacy as both genuine concern and government pretext for withholding data is a tension that endures
notes: LaFleur 2014 cited

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Journalists must weigh the benefits of open data against the risks of personal harm that may come with publication, particularly at a time when private information can be so easily shared and searched.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: privacy; harm assessment; open data; ethical balancing; publication decisions
relevance_rationale: Weighing open data benefits against personal harm risks is an ethical balancing act that becomes more important with AI
notes: Howard 2014 quoted in Lewis and Westlund 2015

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: In a world with so many political interests and economic dependency, where investigative journalism is expensive and unaffordable, where technology makes distances shorter and information travels all over the world at the speed of light, cooperation is becoming a key aspect for future journalism. Collaborative work may also be a way for newsrooms that have fewer and fewer journalists to avoid giving up on investigation work.
chapter: Collaboration Between Media: The Importance of Sharing
themes: collaboration; sustainability; resource pooling; international cooperation; newsroom economics
relevance_rationale: Collaboration as survival strategy for under-resourced newsrooms is an organizational reality that endures
notes: Nacho Cardero, editor in chief of El Confidencial

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Data journalism uses methods reinforced by values such as numeric infallibility, scientific rigor, computational neutrality, crowdsourced collaboration, intra- and extra newsroom cooperation and hyperlocal empathy, to generate exclusives that are generally perceived as more accurate and transparent.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: scientific rigor; neutrality; collaboration; accuracy; transparency; methodology
relevance_rationale: Values of scientific rigor, neutrality, and collaboration in data journalism methodology are fundamental principles
notes: Borges 2016 p.842 quoted

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Journalistic criteria—in other words, having reliable sources, comparing them, collaborating with other media outlets, and only publishing pieces on topics that are in public interest—can clarify professional journalism's stance vis-à-vis the grey area of leaktivism, and the application of them definitely represents a step forward in the establishment and survival of data journalism in today's newsrooms.
themes: source reliability; source comparison; collaboration; public interest; leaktivism; professional standards
relevance_rationale: Reliable sources, comparison, collaboration, and public interest as professional standards endure
notes: Authors' concluding synthesis

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: The New York Times was quick to grasp the importance of the leak and devoted an article to the insights of the investigation started by the German reporters Obermayer and Obermaier, detailing even the search program that they used to hunt for specific names or business: Blacklight.
chapter: Ethical Debates on Sources: Journalism Stands for Public Interest
themes: search tools; Blacklight; name matching; entity search; leaked documents
relevance_rationale: Search tools like Blacklight for name matching in leaked documents are now supplemented by LLM-powered entity extraction
notes: Only named tool in the chapter

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Despite the global nature of today's world, proximity is, paradoxically, still an important strength: each newspaper focused on its local issues, emphasizing different topics to make the whole picture more comprehensive.
themes: proximity; local angle; global-local; collaborative coverage; editorial focus
relevance_rationale: Proximity and local angle in global collaborative investigations is a journalistic principle independent of technology
notes: Authors' conclusion about division of labor in global investigations

source_title: Interrogating Data, Algorithms, and Automatization Through Journalism Ethics (Panama Papers chapter)
author: Helena Cortés, María Luengo
extract: Only the combination of powerful computers and the World Wide Web has offered the possibility of storing and analyzing (or generating by web robots) big databases in order to discover ideas for a story or to add facts and proofs to a story which has already been initially investigated by classical journalistic means.
chapter: How Ethical Approaches to Data Have Changed Newsrooms
themes: computational methods; story discovery; evidence; CAR; databases
relevance_rationale: Computers for storing and analyzing databases endure as a concept but LLMs vastly expand what is possible beyond traditional database querying
notes: Wormer 2007 quoted

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: FoI offers students a gateway to data journalism as it often involves datasets (typically numbers), the analysis of changes over time and pattern-detection. It introduces aspects of computational thinking, and many students have used FoI effectively in their data journalism.
themes: freedom-of-information; data-acquisition; computational-thinking
relevance_rationale: FOI as a gateway to data journalism introducing computational thinking and pattern detection endures as a legal and civic skill
notes: FoI as pipeline entry point to data journalism practice

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: finding stories in data; understanding its structure and context; gathering, cleaning and analysing it; and presenting data stories. Important, too, might be working collaboratively with other specialists, and exploring new tools, techniques and theoretical approaches.
themes: data-journalism-pipeline; collaboration; interdisciplinary
relevance_rationale: The full DJ competency list -- find, understand, gather, clean, analyse, present, collaborate -- remains a valid framework regardless of AI assistance
notes: Core competency list spanning the full DJ workflow

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: Mathematical, statistical and technological literacy -- and, indeed, high levels of competence -- would be essential, almost assumed.
themes: numeracy; statistical-literacy; skills-baseline
relevance_rationale: LLMs can now handle much statistical and technological work, shifting the baseline from assumed competence to assumed AI-assisted competence
notes: Prescriptive claim about prerequisite competences for DJ

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: The data journalism module has addressed this challenge by taking an approach akin to continued professional development, highlighting current practice. One assessment requires students to blog about their own progress as data journalists and encourages them to regard data journalism as, in effect, a beat for their reporting.
themes: reflective-practice; blogging; beat-reporting; experiential-learning
relevance_rationale: Reflective practice through blogging about DJ progress and treating DJ as a beat for continuous professional development endures
notes: Treating DJ as a beat to maintain currency; blogging as assessment

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: Typically, they include 'how to' posts; critical analysis of pieces of data journalism; interviews with practitioners; their own data journalism practice -- including experimentation -- and reflections on this; and the evaluation of relevant new tools or techniques.
themes: critical-analysis; tool-evaluation; experimentation; reflective-practice
relevance_rationale: Critical analysis of DJ work, practitioner interviews, tool evaluation, and experimentation remain valuable learning activities
notes: Taxonomy of student blogging outputs that double as professional development

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: by encouraging critical reflection, it aims to support professional development. The structure and activities help students to collaborate and operate effectively in the environment of modern networked journalism. They foster, too, an open approach and independent learning (important for future career development) that involve contact with practitioners and peers -- reflecting a key element in the practice and growth of data journalism
themes: networked-journalism; collaboration; professional-development; open-practice
relevance_rationale: Critical reflection, collaborative networked journalism, and open independent learning are professional development principles that endure
notes: Claim that networked, collaborative learning mirrors professional DJ practice

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: There is little by way of an established 'canon' or agreed body of knowledge, theory and practices on which to base a curriculum. It is a work in progress, with continuing fluidity, differing approaches and a relatively small corpus (compared to other aspects of journalism) of 'classic' examples of the kind that journalism educators tend to draw upon for case studies and other learning activities.
themes: canon-absence; curriculum-design; field-maturity
relevance_rationale: The absence of an established DJ canon complicating curriculum design persists and is arguably deepened by the rapid AI-driven evolution of practice
notes: Key claim: DJ lacks an established canon, complicating pedagogy

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: Problem-solving abilities of this kind are an important skill in data journalism and sought by employers
themes: problem-solving; software-troubleshooting; self-directed-learning
relevance_rationale: Software troubleshooting as a valued skill is partially displaced since LLMs can now resolve most installation and configuration issues
notes: Context: students resolving software installation/config issues (Tableau, Open Refine) themselves

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: Work primarily with live/real data in class
themes: authentic-data; pedagogy; hands-on
relevance_rationale: Working with live real data in class rather than sanitized datasets is a pedagogical principle that remains essential
notes: Quality document point; flagged as area for development, not yet positive

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: data journalism can be seen partly as an extension of the 'traditional' areas that journalism programmes have long taught: discovering relevant facts, verifying (and disputing) claims, reporting and analysis
themes: verification; traditional-journalism; fact-finding; reporting
relevance_rationale: Framing DJ as an extension of traditional journalism -- discovering facts, verifying claims, reporting -- is a conceptual anchor that endures
notes: Frames DJ as extension of core journalism: discover, verify, dispute, report, analyse

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: Greater emphasis on data would need some existing elements to be omitted from the curriculum, and further specialization. More on statistical analysis, visualization/design and programming are prime candidates.
themes: curriculum-design; specialization; statistics; visualization; programming
relevance_rationale: LLMs reduce the need for dedicated statistics and programming courses since they can scaffold these skills on demand, shifting curriculum priorities
notes: Identifies three areas DJ education should deepen: stats, viz, programming

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: students are taught by currently practising data journalists; internships of relevant data work alongside established professionals, ideally with interdisciplinary teams (e.g. web developers and designers); and students learning explicitly about new and changing practices, ideas and approaches. They would hear and learn from others working in data journalism (not only those formally teaching them) and in related fields (e.g. computing and open data).
themes: interdisciplinary; practitioner-involvement; socialization; team-composition
relevance_rationale: Interdisciplinary team composition and practitioner involvement endure but the specific roles needed shift when LLMs handle some developer tasks
notes: Prescriptive model for DJ socialization: practitioners, interdisciplinary teams, cross-field learning

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: the intensive, hands-on data work clearly required smaller groups
themes: class-size; hands-on; pedagogy
relevance_rationale: Small group sizes for hands-on data work is a pedagogical reality unaffected by AI tools
notes: Empirical finding: classes over 30 failed for DJ; small groups reinstated

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: an understanding of using Excel and being able to spot an angle in numbers is not as hard a climb as you may think at this stage. Data skills helped me get a front page splash
themes: spreadsheets; numeracy; story-finding; career-impact
relevance_rationale: Excel and angle-spotting remain accessible but LLMs now offer an even lower entry point for finding stories in numbers
notes: Alumni testimony; Excel + angle-spotting as accessible entry point to DJ impact

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: the development of data journalism education may be impacted more profoundly and/or immediately than other areas of journalism education because of its interaction with other fields -- notably statistics, computing, data science and visualization. For 'mainstream' journalism education, these may be an optional extra; for data journalism, they relate to its core functions.
themes: interdisciplinary; statistics; computing; data-science; visualization
relevance_rationale: DJ interaction with statistics, computing, and data science is being reshaped since LLMs blur disciplinary boundaries and lower technical entry barriers
notes: Key claim: DJ uniquely requires integration with stats/CS/viz; not optional

source_title: Learning to Teach Data Journalism: Innovation, Influence and Constraints
author: Jonathan Hewett
extract: where ethical issues related to data journalism are best addressed: in a module dedicated to data journalism, or one focused on ethical and professional standards? Similar questions arise in relation to the law, and even public administration (e.g. in relation to the data-related activities of public bodies).
themes: ethics; curriculum-design; law; public-administration
relevance_rationale: The question of where to locate DJ ethics education now must also encompass AI ethics, expanding beyond data privacy and law
notes: Open question about where to locate DJ ethics: specialized vs integrated

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: I ask the students to suggest stories that might arise from the data. At first, the students struggle with this and often do not suggest ideas directly related to the data. Instead, they suggest more conventional types of journalism. I encourage the students to think directly about the data: what stories can be found there?
themes: story-finding; data-driven-thinking; pedagogical-challenge
relevance_rationale: Teaching students to find stories directly in data rather than defaulting to conventional journalism frames is an enduring pedagogical challenge
notes: Highlights a common novice struggle: defaulting to conventional journalism frames rather than letting data suggest stories

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: I ask the students whether we need to do anything to make the data more manageable. The students are somewhat intimidated by the data's status as official statistics. Gradually I steer the conversation to the need to rationalize some of the data to make it easier to use -- for instance, removing subtotals and summing the data for China and Hong Kong. I then clean up the data in Excel and sort it from highest to lowest.
themes: data-cleaning; spreadsheets; official-statistics; rationalization
relevance_rationale: Manual data cleaning in Excel endures as pedagogy but should now also teach LLM-assisted cleaning approaches
notes: Students intimidated by official data; cleaning framed as making data 'manageable' rather than fixing errors

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: I ask the students how we can visually depict the data. I remind the students that data journalism usually has visually engaging and interactive graphics. I introduce the students to Datawrapper, a free online software tool that can produce visual displays of data with simple interactivity. I take the students through the process of inputting the data into a world map using a Datawrapper template.
themes: visualization; Datawrapper; interactive-maps; free-tools
relevance_rationale: Datawrapper remains relevant but LLMs can now generate visualizations from natural language, expanding the tool landscape for teaching
notes: Datawrapper chosen for accessibility and simplicity

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: Drawing on an earlier discussion on graphing data, I remind the students of the need for good statistical practice. The world map's title should clearly say what the chart is displaying and should state the source of the data.
themes: labeling; transparency; source-attribution; chart-standards
relevance_rationale: Clear titles, source attribution, and good statistical practice in visualizations are enduring presentation standards

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: To help the students grasp what data journalism is, I ask them to visit an excellent New Zealand data journalism site. I use a New Zealand site because it has direct applicability to the students, covering topics they identify with and showing them that New Zealand journalists do data journalism. Typically, this piques the students' interest.
themes: local-examples; motivation; relevance; exemplars
relevance_rationale: Using locally relevant published examples to motivate students is an enduring pedagogical principle
notes: Pedagogical principle: use locally relevant published data journalism to motivate and orient students

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: I ask them to suggest some possible data journalism topics, in particular thinking of how topics they have written articles about themselves could be developed as pieces of data journalism. I also ask them to suggest sources of data for such articles.
themes: story-ideation; data-sources; brainstorming; topic-development
relevance_rationale: Brainstorming DJ topics from existing beats and identifying data sources is an enduring story ideation practice
notes: Bridges conventional reporting experience to data journalism by reframing existing beats

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: To ensure the students understand how to undertake the data visualization, each student creates his/her own world map of the data. I inspect each map to make sure it has been done correctly.
themes: hands-on-practice; assessment; visualization; replication
relevance_rationale: Hands-on replication of demonstrated workflows as assessment is an enduring pedagogical method
notes: Verification through reproduction: students must individually replicate the demonstrated workflow

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: To teach journalism students data journalism and statistics generally, the lecturer must have a strong relevant background. I have a PhD in economics and before coming to Massey worked for many years as a financial journalist. I had to learn new skills to design and teach the data journalism module. Partly, I taught myself, including using the very helpful tutorials on the Datawrapper website.
themes: instructor-competence; self-teaching; technical-skills; prerequisites
relevance_rationale: Instructor technical competence requirements persist but the specific skills needed now include LLM literacy alongside quantitative and journalistic expertise
notes: Argues data journalism instruction requires both quantitative and journalistic expertise in the instructor

source_title: Teaching Data Journalism in New Zealand
author: Grant Hannis
extract: Stats NZ release most of their information as dense Excel spreadsheets. Since this module, I have gone through several spreadsheets while writing a story and been able to recognise and translate this information to readers.
themes: official-data; spreadsheets; translation; government-sources
relevance_rationale: Working with government Excel spreadsheets endures but LLMs can now assist with interpreting and translating dense official data
